First, the word is sometimes used, like applying the name of the fruit to its seed, to refer to the causes leading to a bodhisattva’s nonconceptual knowing.The idea behind naming the fruit (or result) after the seed (or cause) is like saying, after eating an omelet with many ingredients, that one has had “eggs” (a cause of that omelet) for breakfast. Second, the word is sometimes used, like applying the name of the result to its cause, to refer to what results from a bodhisattva’s nonconceptual knowing. To best approximate Ratnākaraśānti’s understanding of the term, we might translate it “the wisdom that completely transcends” itself, and in so doing transcends cyclic existence.

Thus it is that “transcendent state” of wisdom has been adopted here to capture, in a single translation of the term, both Haribhadra’s and Ratnākaraśānti’s explanations applied to different usages throughout their comments on the scripture. The word “transcendent” can be interpreted either as that state that results from having transcended (i.e., a buddha’s knowing) or that state that is in the process of transcending (i.e., a bodhisattva’s knowing). Here, even though later traditions emphasized the immanence of this “transcendent state of wisdom”‍—which might seem like a contradiction in terms for a transcendent state‍—their rhetoric was likely aimed at pointing out, through paradox, that this very transcendent wisdom sought outside oneself along the path turns out to have been one’s own most fundamental and immediate wisdom that from the beginning has been available, yet has remained unrecognized.

div.head: “Going About Life” (√car)
div.p: With regard to the immanence of the transcendent state of wisdom, it is telling that the Eight Thousand recommends bodhisattvas to “go about life in this transcendent state of wisdom.” Although some alternate translations of the Sanskrit verb √car take it to suggest that bodhisattvas should “practice this transcendent state of wisdom,” the English verb “to practice”‍—whether taken in the sense of practicing the piano or practicing medicine‍—wrongly implies that some mental effort or special focus or activity is always required to exercise the transcendent state of wisdom.Conze’s translation “they should course in perfect wisdom” gives the opposite impression from the verb (√car + locative), as if bodhisattvas must undertake some special mystical activity called “coursing” that no ordinary being understands how to do.

In fact, that bodhisattvas should “go about life” means that they should do whatever they do in life (i.e., sit, walk, eat, sleep, and so on) “in this transcendent state of wisdom.”That is not to say, of course, that the actions bodhisattvas choose to do are entirely uninfluenced by their commitment to the transcendent state of wisdom. See, for example, the comment by Ratnākaraśānti mentioned in n.1194.

div.head: “Mindset of Awakening” (bodhicitta)
div.p: The Sanskrit term bodhicitta‍—translated here as “mindset of awakening”‍—may not have been included in the early recensions of what eventually became the Eight Thousand. But the term may have first come into being and evolved in connection with this very scripture, enshrining as it does the term’s earliest usage before it was reinterpreted in the writings attributed to Nāgārjuna and Asaṅga. Although the Sanskrit word citta (in the compound bodhicitta) has both cognitive and affective aspects difficult to render accurately in a single English word, the term “mindset” has been adopted in the sense of a complex mental state constantly arising in each moment, shaped by particular past actions that affect the way one sees, thinks, feels, interprets, and acts moment to moment.Although Conze and others translate citta, respectively, as “thought” or as “mind,” “heart,” and so on, these terms exclude either the cognitive or affective aspect of the word, and are often forced to fit unfamiliar contexts that make no sense except in Buddhist-English jargon. For instance, can one “give rise” (utpāda; bskyed) to a mind or heart, except in some metaphorical sense? Yet a mindset, by its nature, is something that can arise, be given rise to, and be developed and expanded. One need only look at how French translates citta as esprit and German as geist to see how much less our words “mind” or “heart” are able to account for both the cognitive and affective aspects of the term. However, it should also be pointed out that in this scripture wherever the term citta appears alone (i.e., not in the compound bodhicitta), it may refer to the mindset of awakening, or to a proper mindset directly related to it, or to an improper mindset antithetical to it, or to the immediate content of any of these three senses of the word mindset.The scripture also uses the term “mindset” (citta) in the positive sense as a synonym of meditative concentration (samādhi). This usage was common especially in the context of the three trainings, which in early scriptures were listed as discipline, mindset, and wisdom instead of the more usual discipline, meditative concentration, and wisdom. For instance, when Subhūti makes this scripture’s most famous statement‍—namely “that mindset is free of a mindset” (acitta)‍—he is referring, specifically, to that mindset of awakening. That is to say, the term itself is not being used in a technical, Abhidharma sense relating purely to cognition or consciousness, but is framing the liberative value connected with specific cognitive and affective mental states. The subsequent ontological discussion about the existence or nonexistence of “that mindset” is another matter.

The use of the translation “mindset of awakening” also allows for divergent interpretations of the ambiguous term by Haribhadra and Ratnākaraśānti. In fact, the Sanskrit compound bodhicitta may well have been understood to implicitly criticize the soteriology of the Abhidharma, in which an ordinary sentient being’s mindset and its components (cittacaitta; sems dang sems ’byung) had to be brought to an end in order to reach the awakening (bodhi) of an arhat or a buddha. In other words, the term bodhicitta would likely have been received as an oxymoron that aimed to describe how a Mahāyāna practitioner could reach the freedom of nirvāṇa without entirely leaving behind cyclic existence‍—i.e., still be in the world, but not be of the world. Just as one might say something is “icy hot,” to speak of a bodhisattva’s mindset of awakening‍—a term that combines an ordinary person’s “mindset” with a buddha’s complete “awakening” free of that mindset‍—is an expression of the state of a bodhisattva’s knowing midway between an ordinary being’s and a buddha’s. In its time, it must also have evoked a particular experience that was comprehensible, but difficult to pinpoint except through combining such contradictory terms. For this very reason, some translators insist on leaving the term bodhicitta untranslated. Unfortunately, that approach misses the pedagogical value of the paradox at the heart of the term, which any Sanskrit speaker familiar with the difference between the viewpoints of the Disciple Vehicle and the Bodhisattva Vehicle might well have understood immediately. Probably, Mahāyāna Buddhists would recognize in this terminology that their very own mindset does not need to be reviled or eliminated but embraced as a means for attaining an awakening that surpasses that of arhats or pratyekabuddhas. The choice to translate the term into English aims at replicating this effect as far as it is possible.

Certainly, in their commentaries, both Haribhadra and Ratnākaraśānti rely upon this paradox in the term bodhicitta to interpret it in the light of their respective Madhyamaka and Yogācāra perspectives, which treat it, respectively, as a description of the relative and ultimate aspect of a buddha’s knowing (i.e., the goal) or as a description of the bodhisattvas’ aspiration and practical engagement that leads to their awakening for the sake of all beings (i.e., the path itself). However, rather than dispute the etymology of bodhicitta the two commentators dispute whether, in Abhidharma terms, the word bodhicitta highlights a difference in a bodhisattva’s general mindset or in that mindset’s particular components (cittacaitta; sems dang sems ’byung). To explain this complex issue briefly, the Abhidharma of the Disciple Vehicle claims that the three terms “consciousness” (vijñāna), “thinker” (manas), and “mindset” (citta) all share the same referent but that each term highlights one of its three different functions. But according to Yogācāra scholars‍—who are closer in spirit than the Ābhidharmikas to the usages in the Eight Thousand‍—“consciousness” (vijñāna), “thinker” (manas), and “mindset” (citta) refer to different things, i.e., respectively, to the six sensory consciousnesses, the afflicted ego-consciousness, and the receptacle (ālaya) consciousness. Since the receptacle consciousness in ordinary beings is said not to recognize its object as the dream world it projects, its appearances continue to change moment to moment‍—under the negative influence of ignorance, habitual fixation, and prior karmic deeds‍—until its very basis is transformed upon awakening, after which it remains permanently in the state of a buddha’s knowing.

However, although Haribhadra and Ratnākaraśānti generally agree about a buddha’s nondual knowing emerging from the transformation of consciousness, they disagree about the role that the nature of the mindset of awakening plays in causing that transformation. They also read this interpretive difference into the scripture, based on their divergent philosophical perspectives.

On the one hand, for Haribhadra, the term “mindset” (in mindset of awakening) can only describe a mindset, not the components of the mindset, because, like the transcendent state of wisdom, it must refer to a buddha’s nondual knowing, which has relative and ultimate aspects. In Haribhadra’s view, the mindset of awakening is only described, at the relative level, as an aspiration (praṇidhāna) to attain awakening‍—widely defined as the wish to attain awakening for the sake of all beings‍—and the engagement (prasthāna) in the six transcendent practices, such as generosity and so on. Thus, although Haribhadra does not explicitly state this, he reads the “mindset of awakening” in aspiration and engagement as mere figurative descriptions of how to repeatedly reset one’s mind on awakening and then proceed on that basis.

On the other hand, for Ratnākaraśānti, the “mindset of awakening” is a term, like the transcendent state of wisdom, that refers to a series of mindsets transformed along the path. But since the nature of that knowing is always sheer illumination throughout that path, the term bodhicitta can only refer to the conditioned components of a mindset, not to the mindset itself. Thus, in Ratnākaraśānti’s explanation, the “mindset of awakening” at the level of aspiration refers to an actual wish made at the time of formally taking a vow, during which one’s mind actually becomes set on awakening, whereas engagement refers to the effect of that initial wish on the mind once it has set out on the path to awakening. Although Ratnākaraśānti does not explicitly state it, he implies that the ultimate mindset of awakening, contra Haribhadra, is a figurative usage.

Whether or not the translation of key terms such as “mindset” actually captures the full cognitive and affective implications of the Sanskrit, an effort has been made in this translation to use terminology amenable to both Haribhadra’s and Ratnākaraśānti’s interpretations, as well as to the traditions of Nāgārjuna and Asaṅga on which they respectively rely.It is worth keeping in mind that Indian scholars, such as Haribhadra, Ratnākara, Prajñākaramati, Abhayākaragupta, and others, agree about the general usages of the term “mindset of awakening” (bodhicitta). But they disagree about what each usage’s precise referent is and whether it is a type of primary consciousness or a secondary mental state. For more on these different interpretations, see, for example, Padmakara Translation Group 2007, pp. 53–55. For more about the Sanskrit term citta, cf. Skorupski 2009, p. 165, n45, n59. For a taxonomy of various usages of the term bodhicitta, see Wangchuk 2007. The translation also attempts to dovetail more closely with the general spirit of the traditional definitions and the use of clever heuristic etymologies. For instance, one might heuristically answer the question “What is a mindset of awakening?” as follows: At the level of aspiration, the “mindset of awakening” is a mind set on awakening, i.e., the wish to attain complete awakening for the sake of all beings. At the level of engagement, the “mindset of awakening” is the mindset while awakening through the six transcendent states. At the ultimate level, the “mindset of awakening” is nondual wisdom inseparable from compassion.

div.head: “State of Complete Knowing” (sarvajñatā)
div.p: The Eight Thousand frequently uses the Sanskrit term sarvajñatā, which has been translated here as “the state of complete knowing.” To translate this term as “omniscience,” as has become accepted in some contexts, in the case of this text would entail several problems.

First, when the commentators explain the Eight Thousand, they distinguish three different possible referents of the term sarvajñatā in line with the first three topics of Maitreya’s Ornament, namely (a) the “complete knowing (of the constituents)” (sarvajñatā) as the final result of the Disciple Vehicle that knows the five constituents of experience to be empty of personal identity; (b) the “knowing of (all) the paths” (mārgajñatā) as a bodhisattva’s knowing that surpasses the complete knowing (of the constituents) in knowing all paths to awakening; and (c) the “knowing of all the aspects” (sarvākārajñatā) as the final result of the Bodhisattva Vehicle that includes knowing the 173 specific aspects explained in the fourth topic of the Ornament. Given the three different degrees and spheres of knowing, all three types of knowing could not all be called “omniscience” without rendering the word meaningless, and hence, the term “state of complete knowing” has been adopted as more amenable to the different referents.This problem is of far less prominence in the translation of other Prajñāpāramitā scriptures. The three Sanskrit terms used in the Ornament to distinguish the three kinds of sarvajñatā outlined here are hardly found at all in the text of the Eight Thousand. “Knowing of all the aspects” (sarvākārajñatā) occurs only once apart from in the heading of the first chapter (see n.65). “Knowing of the paths” (mārgajñatā) is not found at all. By contrast, in the long Prajñāpāramitā sūtras (the Hundred Thousand, Twenty-Five Thousand, and Eighteen Thousand) the differentiation into three is made much clearer by the very frequent use of distinct terms for them in the source texts. See the introduction to The Perfection of Wisdom in Twenty-Five Thousand Lines, i.95. In the translation of that text (and of the Hundred Thousand), the term sarvajñatā (thams cad shes pa) by itself has been rendered “knowledge of all the dharmas” whenever (as is mostly the case) it denotes what is here‍—in the translation of the present text‍—termed the “complete knowing” of the Disciple Vehicle. The differentiation into three terms, in the longer sūtras, of the Eight Thousand’s single term is sometimes taken as one example of evidence for the longer sūtra’s later evolution.

Second, although some Buddhist texts seem to describe the buddha’s knowing all the aspects of everything as something like a divine omniscience, many Mahāyāna Buddhist scholars sought to qualify this suggestion in different ways, such as saying that a buddha knows everything by knowing their single nature.For a discussion of some of the different Buddhist explanations of this term, see McClintock 2010. Here, too, one might ask what kind of knowing of the nature is entailed? Certainly, it is not merely intellectually knowing the nature of everything, but rather the uninterrupted knowing of that nature and all its possible manifestations. Granted, such knowing enables a buddha to “know” infinite things but not necessarily in an intentional way, as terms like “all-knowing” might imply.

Third, the three types of complete knowing are said to be synonymous with the transcendent state of wisdom of three types of noble beings‍—arhats, bodhisattvas, and buddhas, respectively. Hence, these terms must dovetail with one another in meaningful ways. Therefore, although the term “state of complete knowing” may be less than ideal, it has been adopted to allow the commentaries to differentiate usages and specify three different referents with very specific meanings.

Many translators have noted the difficulty of translating the word dharma because of its multiple meanings: thing, phenomenon, quality, truth, doctrine, teaching, ultimate reality, virtue, mental object, and so on. The Eight Thousand and other Prajñāpāramitā scriptures often play with these meanings. Indeed, the word dharma can have more than one meaning at the same time in this scripture, especially when it is used in proximity with related Sanskrit words like dharmakāya, dharmatā, and dharmadhātu. To allow for the etymological resonance and meaningful ambiguity in these passages, Harrison and others have opted to leave the term untranslated. Leaving readers to assess for themselves what each occurrence of the term may mean has both advantages and disadvantages.

In this translation, the untranslated, capitalized term Dharma‍—either alone or followed by the word “teachings”‍—is used to refer to the Buddha’s doctrine or specific teachings, because that has become a standard or convention. For other usages, however, the term dharma is rendered by the most appropriate term in each context, but with a note indicating the other meanings possibly intended. This approach has led to the term dharma being most often translated as a “thing”‍—in the generic sense of physical and mental things like forms, consciousness, and so on.Note, however, that “thing” has also been used in some passages to render the Tibetan dngos po, Sanskrit vastu. See n.391 and the end of n.472. Likewise, the compound word sarvadharmāḥ has been translated in the positive as “all things” or “everything” and in the negative as “nothing,” “anything”‍—or in many cases “any thing” (to point to the emphasis on “thing”). A less common usage of dharma in this scripture is translated as “awakened qualities”‍—when referring to the qualities possessed by a worthy one (arhat), a solitary realizer (pratyekabuddha), or a bodhisattva‍—or just as “qualities” where more appropriate.

As for compound terms with dharma in them, each is translated according to its most common usage, for example “truth body” (dharmakāya), “true nature of things” (dharmatā), and “ultimate realm of things” (dharmadhātu). However, all these compound terms can be interpreted variously in different contexts by the commentators from their differing philosophical perspectives. Ratnākaraśānti, for instance, explains the term dharmakāya in some contexts as “body of teaching” (Dharma=deśanā+kāya), in some as “body/embodiment of the true nature of things” (dharmatā+kāya), and in others as “body of awakened qualities” (dharmānām+kāya). For Haribhadra, the “true nature of things” (dharmatā) is often synonymous with emptiness and the “ultimate realm of things” (dharmadhātu). But depending on context, Ratnākaraśānti explains the “ultimate realm of things” (dharmadhātu) as the fundamental source (dhātu<hetu) of all things, or as the fundamental “source of all (awakened) qualities.”

div.head: Negation, Ambiguity, and Word Play
div.p: The Prajñāpāramitā literature is famous for its negations, especially those made by the privative a- or an-. Generally, when this ambiguous negation is prefixed to any word, it can be understood in either of two ways: (a) non-x, not x, or no x, or (b) x-less, lacking x, or not having x. When the scriptures were translated into Chinese, these negations were systematically translated the first way, but when they were translated into Tibetan, they were translated the second way.

The difference between these two interpretations is philosophically significant‍—generally, with the Yogācāra preferring the former and the Madhyamaka preferring the latter. So how they are translated into English is quite important. The approach taken in this translation is to use the English negation “free of” wherever possible, because, despite leaning toward the x-less interpretation, it can be interpreted in either way.

To see how this works both ways, take this scripture’s prime example: “That mindset of awakening is free of a mindset.” Here Haribhadra, as a Mādhyamika scholar, interprets “free of a mindset” to be an absolute, nonimplicative negation (prasajyapratiṣedha; med dgag), meaning that such a mindset appears but does not ultimately exist.Most Tibetans followed Haribhadra, so they translated the negation here as meaning “without” or “lacking” any mindset (sems med pa/ sems ma chis pa), i.e., as a nonimplicative negation (med dgag). But some Tibetans, inspired by Ratnākara perhaps, translated it as a “nonmindset” (sems ma yin pa)‍—i.e., something that is “not” a mindset‍—suggesting an exclusion or implicative negation (ma yin dgag). For an example see F.45.a.4 in the Tibetan translation of Ārya Vimuktisena’s Vṛtti (Toh 3787), where Ngok Lotsāwa translates acittam as “not a mind” (sems ma yin pa). Ratnākaraśānti, however, as a Yogācāra scholar, interprets “free of a mindset” to be an excluding, implicative negation (paryudāsa; ma yin dgag), meaning that such a mindset is a “mindset” only insofar as it is aware, but it is also “free of any mindset” insofar as it is entirely free of grasping. Thus readers, whether or not they prefer either one of these two interpretations, should keep in mind that this translation aims to allow both commentators the room to interpret the scripture, each with their own intended effect. The scripture’s ambiguity in Sanskrit could be said to have led to the development of two different Mahāyāna schools that are still practiced in some form today.

In Sanskrit philosophy, there are a variety of verbs for saying that something “does not exist.” But this scripture, despite its occasional philosophical undertones, does not read like philosophy. This translation therefore tends to interpret such verbs less existentially. For example, Subhūti’s first personal statements, “I do not find (avindan, na vedmi; ma ’tshal) any thing,” and so on, suggest that he is not so much making a general philosophical assertion as illuminating for others what it is like to go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. He uses the first person “I do not find” to express his experience at that moment. In response to Śāriputra, Subhūti then states that the thing is “not found” (na vidyate)‍—which can be philosophically interpreted to mean “the thing does not exist.” But in this case, to switch in the translation of the same verbal root √vid from “I do not find it” to “it does not exist” would distort the practical context, undermine the reader’s ability to see the logical flow, and suggest that Subhūti himself had jumped from a seemingly subjective experience to a seemingly objective conclusion about reality. Certainly, in the passage, his interlocutor Śāriputra represents the hardheaded philosopher trying to tease out the philosophical upshot of Subhūti’s statements. But this comes through still with the translation “not found” and allows for the practice-oriented interpretations of this passage by both commentators. Thus, although the Tibetan often translates phrases like na vidyate as “does not exist” (med), this translation seeks to preserve the nonphilosophical tone of these passages wherever possible, unless it would make a sentence unclear. Of course, readers are welcome to interpret these English translations as having philosophical implications, as the commentators themselves often do.

The tendency to prefer the logical and scientific over the magical and religious, and to seek clear, concise statements that can be easily understood and applied, is part of the modernist bias. But the Indian traditions out of which the Eight Thousand emerged seem to have preferred scriptures that were more mystical, poetic, and open to interpretation. It was up to the commentaries to provide a systematic analysis and to clarify ambiguities. The primary aim in the translation of this scripture has been to allow the Eight Thousand’s own interpretable but playful use of language to intrigue and inspire.

div.head: Summaries of the Text
Overall Synopsis of the Transcendent State of Wisdom
div.p: The transcendent state of wisdom can be defined as any instance of knowing, free from all conceptual proliferations, that directly sees the true nature of things and transcends cyclic existence. The term transcendent state of wisdom refers to both the spiritual knowing during meditative concentration and the pure worldly knowing during post-meditation periods for buddhas and noble bodhisattvas on the ten levels. But the term can also be used figuratively to refer to a text, a path, or a practice that leads to the realization of the way things truly are. The Eight Thousand scripture explains how going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivating it helps bodhisattvas, in both worldly and spiritual ways, along the path, as they seek to overcome even the subtlest ignorance and habitual fixation to realize the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of a buddha.

div.head: Synopsis of the Text According to the Ornament
div.p: The Eight Thousand is divided into thirty-two chapters focused on various themes connected with a bodhisattva’s journey to awakening; these are listed in the chapter-by-chapter synopsis below. Maitreya’s Ornament, however, is divided into eight topics of study that circumscribe the eight realizations that occur in stages within a bodhisattva’s mindstream on this journey. Haribhadra and Ratnākaraśānti show how the Eight Thousand unfolds in the order of the Ornament’s eight topics, namely: (I–III) the three objective goals to be realized, (IV–VII) the four ways of practicing to subjectively realize those objective goals, and (VIII) the single fruit with all the qualities that come from realizing those goals. The eight topics, which include seventy subtopics, correlate to the thirty-two chapters of the Eight Thousand as follows:
I. The state of knowing of all the aspects (sarvākārajñātā, rnam mkhyen)

The “state of knowing of all the aspects” refers to the realization of a buddha’s nondual, direct, nonconceptual knowing of the nature and varieties of all things at the same time. This is the highest of the three objective goals to be realized in awakening and is covered in chapter 1 of the Eight Thousand.

Its ten subtopics include (i) the mindset of awakening; (ii) the practical instructions for the Great Vehicle; (iii) the four stages of breaking through on the path of preliminary practice, namely, the warmth experience, the peak experience, the experience of acceptance, and the experience of the pinnacle of worldly things; (iv) the basis for progress on the path; (v) the reference points for progress on the path; (vi) the motivations for progress on the path; (vii) the armor-like practice; (viii) the practice of setting out; (ix) the practice of accumulating merit and knowing; and (x) the practice of going beyond cyclic existence.
II. Knowing of the paths (mārgajñatā, lam gyi rnam pa shes pa nyid)

The “knowing of (all) the paths” refers to the realization of a bodhisattva who knows all paths in the three vehicles to lack any inherent nature while simultaneously accomplishing the paths of learning through completing the accumulation of merit and wisdom, helping beings ripen, and purifying their perception. This is the middle of the three objective goals to be realized in awakening and is covered in chapters 2–7 of the Eight Thousand.

Its eleven subtopics include (i) the brilliant mindset at the root of knowing of the paths; (ii) the path of the disciples; (iii) the path of the solitary realizers; (iv) the bodhisattva’s path of seeing; (v) the activities on the bodhisattva’s path of cultivation; (vi) zealousness on the bodhisattva’s path of cultivation; (vii) praising, celebrating, and eulogizing zealousness on the bodhisattva’s path of cultivation; (viii) dedicating on the bodhisattva’s path of cultivation; (ix) rejoicing on the bodhisattva’s path of cultivation; (x) accomplishments on the bodhisattva’s path of cultivation; and (xi) absolute purity on the bodhisattva’s path of cultivation.
III. Complete knowing (sarvajñatā, thams cad shes pa nyid)

The “complete knowing” (i.e., of the constituents) refers to the realization of a disciple or solitary realizer whose knowledge of the emptiness of all things is limited to their lacking any personal identity. This is the most modest of the three objective goals to be realized in awakening and is covered in chapter 8 of the Eight Thousand.

Its nine subtopics include (i) not dwelling in cyclic existence because of wisdom, (ii) not dwelling in peacefulness because of compassion, (iii) being far without skill in means, (iv) being close with skill in means, (v) the counterproductive forces, (vi) their antidotes, (vii) the practice of these antidotes, (viii) the sameness of the two, and (ix) the disciple’s path of seeing.
IV. Realizing all the aspects (sarvākārābhisambodha, rnam kun mngon rdzogs rtogs pa)

“Realizing all the aspects” refers to the bodhisattva’s practice of distilling the essence of the above three types of knowing (I–III) into the unborn nature and cultivating all their aspects without conceptual proliferations. This is the initial way to apply oneself subjectively to realizing the three objective goals and is covered from chapter 9 to the first half of chapter 20 of the Eight Thousand.

Its eleven subtopics include (i) the aspects of the subjective perspectives that know the objective reality, (ii) the practice concerning those aspects, (iii) the positive qualities gained through practice, (iv) the faults overcome through practice, (v) the defining characteristics of practice, (vi) the factors conducive to liberation, (vii) the factors conducive to insight, (viii) the signs of bodhisattvas beyond regressing, (ix) the sameness of cyclic existence and peace, (x) the unsurpassed purity of the buddhafields, and (xi) the bodhisattva’s skill in means.
V. Realizing the culminations (mūrdhābhisamaya, rtse mor phyin pa’i mngon rtogs)

“Realizing the culminations” refers to the bodhisattva’s practice for causing the prior realization of all aspects (IV) to reach the culmination of mastery in each of the above three types of knowing (I–III). This is the next way to apply oneself subjectively to realizing the three objective goals and is covered from the second half of chapter 20 to the end of chapter 28 of the Eight Thousand.

Its eight subtopics include (i) the initial signs on the path of preparation when realizing its culmination, (ii) the increase and maturation of merit when realizing its culmination, (iii) familiarizing when realizing its culmination, (iv) stabilizing the mindset when realizing its culmination, (v) realizing the culmination of the path of seeing, (vi) realizing the culmination of the path of cultivation, (vii) realizing the culmination of meditative concentration without interruptions, and (viii) the misunderstandings to be eliminated.
VI. Realizing the aspects progressively (ānupūrvābhisamaya, mthar gyis pa’i mngon rtogs)

“Realizing the aspects progressively” refers to the bodhisattva’s practice for progressively cultivating the culminations in each of the above three types of knowing (I–III) in order to stabilize the realization that they all arise together. This is the next way to apply oneself subjectively to realizing the three objective goals and is covered in the first third of chapter 29 of the Eight Thousand.

Its thirteen subtopics include (i–vi) progressively realizing the six transcendent states of generosity, ethical discipline, tolerant acceptance, heroic perseverance, meditation, and wisdom; (vii–xii) progressively realizing the six recollections of the Buddha, the Dharma, the Saṅgha, ethical discipline, generosity, and the gods; and (xiii) progressively realizing things to have the inherent nature of not existing.
VII. Realizing the aspects in a single moment (ekakṣaṇābhisamaya, skad cig ma gcig gis mngon par rtogs pa)

“Realizing the aspects in a single moment” refers to the bodhisattva’s practice at the very conclusion of progressively cultivating the above three types of knowing (I–III). This is the next way to apply oneself subjectively to realizing the three objective goals and is covered from one third of the way through chapter 29 to halfway through the same chapter of the Eight Thousand.

Its four subtopics include (i) in a single moment realizing all things free of worldly influences, (ii) in a single moment realizing all beneficial things that arise through ripening, (iii) in a single moment realizing all things to lack defining characteristics, and (iv) in a single moment realizing the nondual nature of all things.
VIII. The fruit of the buddha’s body of qualities and awakened activity (dharmakāya, ’bras bu chos sku)
The “buddha’s body of qualities” refers to the state of a buddha who has all the undefiled qualities that result from cultivating the four practices (IV–VII).
This is the final realization of the three objective goals and is covered from halfway through chapter 29 through chapter 30 of the Eight Thousand.

Its four subtopics include the four aspects of the buddha’s body: (i) the natural body, (ii) the wisdom truth body, (iii) the enjoyment body, and (iv) the emanation body.These four bodies are based on Haribhadra’s explanation, which is more common in Tibetan lists of the seventy topics. Ratnākaraśānti divides these four topics differently, namely (1) natural body, (2) enjoyment body, (3) emanation body and (4) a buddha’s body of qualities and his activity.

div.head: Synopsis Chapter by Chapter
div.p: The Eight Thousand aims to illuminate the bodhisattva’s journey to awakening. Its thirty-two chapters address the eight main topics and subtopics described above. But this implicit structure will not be obvious to readers without much detailed analysis and help from the commentaries, because each chapter unfolds through a dialogue between interlocuters who do not provide any of the assumed path-related context for their discussion. Thus, although the outline of the Ornament above provides the precise context necessary for understanding the topics implied by these dialogues and their logical relationship, the various themes within the dialogues may appear disorganized without a knowledge of their precise context. As an aid for navigating the text, nonetheless, a more general thematic summary of the explicit topics discussed within each of the thirty-two chapters is provided here, with Roman numerals matching the topics and their subdivisions as outlined in the preceding section.
After each chapter description, we have added an overview of significant wordplay involved, and also a mention of the corresponding passages in the three long Transcendent State of Wisdom sūtras.

Generally speaking, one can understand the first half of the Eight Thousand, namely, chapters 1–16, to involve the bodhisattva from the initial stage of his or her mindset of awakening up to realization of the actual nature (tathatā) where the stage beyond regressing is attained. The second half, chapters 17–32, concerns similar topics but for a bodhisattva on the stage beyond regressing up to the final awakening as a tathāgata. In addition to the main content of each chapter, the summary will highlight the notable structural elements and stylistic differences, often centered around a play on words or a negation in the form xy is not y that expresses the seeming paradox of nonduality.In his dissertation, Shì Hùifēng explains the Eight Thousand to have the chiastic rhetorical structure “XY is ~Y” or “XY is not Y.” Shì’s conclusions are well considered and persuasive, but difficult to explain here and not directly relevant to summarizing the content. For ease, a simple rule of thumb for readers might be to regard the internal structure of each different chapter as involving a different rhetorical device, often with humorous undertones, such as its own setup, punchline, and so on, aimed at evoking various insight responses in the readers, rather than laughter, amusement, groans, or other responses.
1. The chapter “Going About Life in the Knowing of All Aspects”

list.item: I.i. The Blessed One causes Subhūti to illuminate for bodhisattvas the way to go beyond cyclic existence in the transcendent state of wisdom (1.3).
I.ii. Subhūti cannot find anything to illuminate for anyone, i.e., any transcendent state of wisdom to illuminate for any bodhisattva (1.6).

I.iii. He explains that remaining in a mindset undiscouraged while not finding anything is the guidance and instruction. That mindset is free of any mindset. It is the luminously clear, essential nature of a mindset to be realized (1.7). Thus, a bodhisattva does not go about life in designations, conventional signs, or conceptualizations. He or she sees all things to lack the inherent nature of things that ordinary, naive sentient beings imagine to exist. Likewise, a bodhisattva sees him- or herself and all beings to be like magical illusions.
I.iv. For this reason, the terms bodhisattva, great being, and Great Vehicle are free of referents (1.60).
I.v. But a bodhisattva is someone free of entanglements with any things, who aims to realize the objective of awakening (1.60).
I.vi. A bodhisattva is called a “great being” for several reasons, including his or her great goal of liberating all beings (1.62).
I.vii. With this unentangled mindset, a bodhisattva is equipped with the great armor, while knowing that he or she is not equipped with any armor (1.76).
I.viii. The Blessed One teaches what it means for a bodhisattva to set out on a great journey in the Great Vehicle (1.84).
I.ix. He says one should recognize the bodhisattva by means of his or her perfection of the six transcendent states (1.84).

I.x. But he or she goes beyond cyclic existence and comes to dwell in the state of complete knowing, even though no bodhisattva and no Great Vehicle can be found or mentally perceived (1.85) because all things are free of arising (1.98).

This chapter is framed by the central play on the Sanskrit word pratibhāna, which means both “illuminating” oneself by becoming clear about the transcendent state of wisdom and “illuminating” others by speaking in a manner that transmits wisdom to them.

The chapter corresponds in content to chapters 1–13 of the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and to chapters 1–21 of the Eighteen Thousand. Some points within it that correspond to where new chapters begin in the long sūtras are marked by notes in the text. However, while many of the themes within this chapter are discussed in similar ways in the longer sūtras, in the latter they contain additional themes and more extended discussions that cannot always be matched to this scripture.

div.p: 2. The chapter “For Śakra”

list.item: II.i. The brilliance of the Buddha’s miraculous power (anubhāva), majesty (tejas), and blessings (adhiṣṭhāna) outshines the splendor of the gods listening to the teaching, because they lack the mindset of awakening.

II.ii. Subhūti explains that a bodhisattva dwells in the transcendent state of wisdom by means of dwelling in emptiness, (2.6) i.e., by means of freedom from dwelling (2.25), because nirvāṇa and a magical illusion are free of duality and free from creating duality.

II.iii. Only those whose worldly influences have dissipated can accept the transcendent state of wisdom explicated in this way. In fact, there is no training in the transcendent state of wisdom, or any way to pursue it, because it is a perfection beyond measure and infinite just as sentient beings are beyond measure and infinite.
This chapter is framed by the central play on the word sthāna to mean “dwelling on” in a negative sense or “abiding in” in a positive sense.
The chapter corresponds to chapters 14–16 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and to chapters 22–25 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 3. The chapter “Paying Tribute to the Stūpas and the Transcendent States That Contain Immeasurable Benefits”

list.item: II.iv. The Blessed One teaches the many worldly benefits gained by a bodhisattva who keeps the Transcendent State of WisdomHere and in what follows, the “Transcendent State of Wisdom” (capitalized) refers to the scripture (or scriptures), while the “transcendent state of wisdom” (lower case) refers to the practice or state. in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, and so on. Beyond gaining these worldly benefits, being immune to Māra’s attacks, and protected by the gods, a bodhisattva is transformed by the transcendent state of wisdom.
II.v. By training in it as a magical incantation, a bodhisattva comes to attain the knowing of someone who has complete knowing.

II.vi. Furthermore, by preserving the Transcendent State of Wisdom and paying tribute (satkuryāt) to it with flowers, incense, and so on, a bodhisattva earns infinitely more merit than doing the same for the stūpas containing a buddha’s physical relics. Since the transcendent state of wisdom gives birth to the Tathāgata’s state of complete knowing, the Transcendent State of Wisdom scripture is the genuine relic and repository of his knowing, to be venerated with a wide variety of devotional offerings. At the very least, one should preserve the Transcendent State of Wisdom scripture in hopes of preserving it for future bodhisattvas. But after developing the mindset of awakening, for his or her own sake, a bodhisattva should have intense faith, trust, and confidence in the Transcendent State of Wisdom, and zealously listen to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. For the sake of others, a bodhisattva should elucidate it, unpack its meaning, mentally investigate it, and fully examine it. The Blessed One teaches that he praises the positive qualities of the transcendent state of wisdom (3.80) because it takes pride of place over the five other transcendent states. Those five are called “perfection” only because its wisdom transforms their roots of virtue through dedicating them to the state of complete knowing.
This chapter is framed by the central play on the Sanskrit word pariṇāmana, used to suggest both “transforming” worldly activities and “dedicating” them in the transcendent state of wisdom.
The chapter corresponds to chapters 17–21 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and to chapters 26–30 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 4. The chapter “Extolling Positive Qualities”

list.item: II.vi. (cont.) The Blessed One teaches that a bodhisattva should venerate the transcendent state of wisdom, because a buddha is not a physical body but a body of the truth that can only be seen by actualizing it oneself through the transcendent state of wisdom (4.2). The Blessed One teaches that he singles out the transcendent state of wisdom for praise, because whenever a bodhisattva is practicing generosity, ethical discipline, tolerant acceptance, heroic perseverance, meditative concentration, or looking insightfully into the nature of things, the transcendent state of wisdom alone takes the fore. But ultimately, there is no difference among the six transcendent states because they all are supported by skill in means and dedicated by the transcendent state of wisdom to the state of complete knowing (4.26).

This chapter is framed by the central play on the Sanskrit word pūrvaṃgamā, used to suggest that the transcendent state of wisdom both “takes the fore” in terms of leading and guiding other positive qualities and transcendent states, and also “takes precedence” in terms of importance and superiority.
The chapter corresponds to chapter 22 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and to chapter 31 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 5. The chapter “The Kinds of Merit”

list.item: II.vii. The Blessed One teaches that when a bodhisattva pays tribute to this Transcendent State of Wisdom with a wide variety of other devotional offerings and venerates it, he or she earns great merit. But when a bodhisattva, with faith, belief, conviction, and a clear mindset, shares the Transcendent State of Wisdom with others (5.1) and inspires them to fully engage it by helping them understand it, practice it, be enthusiastic about it, and rejoice in it (5.12), he or she earns infinitely more merit (5.85).

This chapter is framed by the central play on the fact that even though the Blessed One agrees each time when Śakra says it would not be easy to come up with any quantification or comparison for such masses of merit, the Blessed One continues to stretch the comparison further and further so that the difference in merit sinks in.
The chapter corresponds (more or less) to chapter 23 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and to chapter 32 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 6. The chapter “Rejoicing and Dedicating”

list.item: II.viii. The bodhisattva Maitreya explains that when a bodhisattva rejoices in others’ roots of virtue and dedicates them to unsurpassed, perfect awakening, the actual thing that produces the merit is called (ākhyāyate) the foremost (agram) and so on, when compared with any other things producing merit, such as generosity, ethical discipline, and so on (6.1). However, the bodhisattva does not turn those things into conventional signs but rather knows them to be nonexistent‍—a point not to be taught to beginning bodhisattvas because it might undermine their faith (śraddhā), appreciation (preman), trust (prasādaḥ), or devotion (gaurava) (6.15). That said, a bodhisattva can dedicate to perfect awakening the merit of rejoicing only if he or she has no conception or mental perception of a “mindset” that is rejoicing or that is dedicating (6.38).

II.ix. A bodhisattva knows all things to be unborn and manifested only by the imagination. Thus, he or she rejoices in the true nature of those things, knowing them all‍—i.e., the generosity, ethical discipline, and so on, up to the buddhas themselves‍—to be the same as liberation (6.96).
This chapter is framed by the central play on the Sanskrit word vastu to describe both “the thing” producing merit and the lack of any “things” on which dedicating and rejoicing are based.
The chapter corresponds to chapter 24 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and to chapter 33 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 7. The chapter “Hell”

list.item: II.x. The transcendent state of wisdom knows everything and actualizes the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. It brings an end to all habitual tendencies connected with the two obstructions of emotional defilements and objects of knowledge. It brings forth all the awakened qualities of a buddha. Hence, one should pay homage to the transcendent state of wisdom in exactly the same way that one pays homage to the Teacher. To accomplish the transcendent state of wisdom is not to accomplish the five constituents of experience. But, by not mentally constructing anything, one accomplishes the five constituents of experience. Of course, such an accomplishment confers nothing, and that is how it confers the state of complete knowing, because the transcendent state of wisdom is free of arising and cessation and it neither increases nor decreases the constituents of experience (7.22). The constituents underlying the clinging to existence are empty and void, free of duality (advayam), and free from creating duality. But for this reason, certain bodhisattvas might still be unable to have trust or firm conviction in this transcendent state of wisdom and may reject it (7.29). If they not only criticize the state of complete knowing, but also discourage (vicchandayiṣyanti) other persons, they will suffer terribly and end up wandering from hell to hell for a very long time (7.30).

This chapter is framed by the central play on the Sanskrit word nirhāra, which can mean either to “bring to an end” in the sense of removing something or “bring forth” in the sense of accomplishing something.

The chapter corresponds to chapter 25 and the first part of chapter 26 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (down to and including 26.24); to chapter 25 and the first part of chapter 26 (down to and including 26.28) in the Hundred Thousand; and to chapter 34 and the first part of chapter 35 (down to and including 35.22) in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 8. The chapter “Purity”

list.item: II.xi. The Blessed One teaches that it is difficult to have firm conviction in the purity of the transcendent state of wisdom for someone who is not committed to practice, is devoid of the roots of virtue, or has fallen into the hands of unvirtuous friends. But a bodhisattva knows that the five constituents of experience are neither fettered (abaddhaḥś) nor liberated (amuktaḥ) in the past, present, or future (8.5), because the purity of the five constituents of experience is the purity of the fruit, and the purity of the fruit is the purity of the five constituents of experience (8.7).

III.i-ii. Because it is absolutely pure, the transcendent state of wisdom dwells neither on this shore’s bank, nor on the opposite shore’s bank. Nor does it dwell split somewhere between the banks of the two shores (8.29).
III.iii-iv. A bodhisattva who conceives of such things distances him- or herself from this transcendent state of wisdom.
III.v. It is an attachment for a bodhisattva to conceive of any things, including his or her own first development of the mindset of awakening, and so on.

III.vi. Instead, a bodhisattva simply aims to help others practice the transcendent state of wisdom in accordance with reality for the sake of perfect awakening (8.34). As long as signs are present, his or her attachment is still present (8.36) because all things share a single defining characteristic, namely their lack of any defining characteristic (8.40).

III.vii. A bodhisattva does not go about life conceiving of anything, from physical forms up to a buddha’s complete knowing, as something with or without attachment. By transcending all such attachments, a bodhisattva goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom (8.60).

III.viii. Whether or not one praises its virtue, it does not get inflated or deflated. There is nothing in it that needs to be safeguarded, defended, or protected because no things are found or observed, like an echo (8.68).

The last part of this chapter is framed by the central play on the Sanskrit words sasaṅga and asaṅga. The translations “with attachment” and “without attachment” aim to capture the meaningful ambiguity in the Sanskrit that could mean either subjectively attached or unattached, or objectively impeding or not impeding. The ambiguity allows for cyclic existence and nirvāṇa to be seen as objectively impure or pure according to disciples, and as subjectively impure or pure according to bodhisattvas.

The chapter corresponds to the rest of chapter 26 (starting at 26.29), all of chapter 27, and the first part of chapter 28 (down to and including 28.41) in the Twenty-Five Thousand; to the rest of chapter 26 (starting at 26.2 5), all of chapter 27, and the first part of chapter 28 (down to and including 28.278) in the Hundred Thousand; and to the rest of chapter 35 (starting at 35.23), all of chapter 36, and the first part of chapter 37 (down to and including 37.36) in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 9. The chapter “Praise”

list.item: III.ix. The Blessed One and others generally explain the one hundred ten subjective aspects that know the objective reality at the level of worthy ones, bodhisattvas, and buddhas. To begin with, Subhūti explains that the transcendent state of wisdom and its name are not mentally perceived. The Blessed One teaches that upon awakening, Maitreya will see the five constituents of experience to be neither permanent nor impermanent, neither fettered nor liberated, because they are absolutely pure (9.2). The empty state, the state free of signs, and the state free of inclinations do not start or stop (9.13).

IV.i. Subhūti explains that a bodhisattva, who possesses the transcendent state of wisdom, does not awaken to any Dharma, will not set in motion a wheel of Dharma, nor reveal any Dharma, because no Dharma is mentally perceived, indicated, or set in motion. Absolutely no Dharma has come into being or will be caused to stop, because, from the beginning, no things (dharma) have come into being since they are void by nature. Thus, (a) from the perspective of the nonexistence of space, it is a perfection that is nonexistent, equal, void, indestructible, and so on, because physical forms and so on are nonexistent, equal, void, indestructible, and so on. (b) From the perspective of its freedom from a false nature, from conceptualizing, from mentally perceiving signs, and so on, it is a perfection free of attachment, free of origination, null, free of faults, free of defilements, and so on. (c) From the perspective of its inability to be harmed, its not being deflated, its lack of attachment or grudge, its having transcended calculation, and so on, it is a perfection of power, self-confidence, experiential knowledge, all the distinctive qualities of a buddha, and so on.
The second part of this chapter is framed by the central play on the Sanskrit word dharma. It refers to the Buddha’s teachings, to things in general, and, finally, to the qualities of a buddha.

The chapter corresponds to the rest of chapter 28 (from 28.42) and all of chapter 29 in the Twenty-Five Thousand; to the rest of chapter 28 (from 28.279) and all of chapter 29 in the Hundred Thousand; and to the rest of chapter 37 (from 37.37) and all of chapter 38 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 10. The chapter “Extolling the Positive Qualities of Recollection”

list.item: IV.ii. The Blessed One and others generally elaborate on the twenty-three practice perspectives concerning the aspects mentioned in the previous chapter. To begin with, Śakra explains that a bodhisattva who even so much as hears this transcendent state of wisdom must have performed a service to the former conquerors, planted the roots of virtue under numerous buddhas, and have been accepted by virtuous friends. So, what more needs to be said about those who take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, and so on? Śāriputra explains that any bodhisattva who does all this with intense faith and the mindset of awakening will become a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing (10.2). The Blessed One teaches that to engage in meditation in the transcendent state of wisdom, a bodhisattva neither dwells on physical forms and so on, nor the notion of physical forms and so on. Since a bodhisattva also does not dwell on the notion that physical forms and so on are profound and so on, he or she engages in meditation on physical forms and so on in the transcendent state of wisdom (10.9). A bodhisattva does not go about life in any things, and thus goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom (10.37).

IV.iii. The Blessed One teaches the fourteen benefits to be gained through practice. To begin with, it is only due to the miraculous power of the buddhas that a bodhisattva can even take in the Transcendent State of Wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it and so on (10.43). A bodhisattva borne in mind and supported by the buddhas will encounter no obstacles created by Māra (10.44). His or her mental exertion will be extremely effective, beneficial, fruitful, and rewarding, because the transcendent state of wisdom is intimately connected with the ultimate reality of everything, so sentient beings who attend to it gain insight into reality as it is. Furthermore, these scriptures associated with the six transcendent states will spread in the south, then spread from the south further to the east, and then from the east further to the north (10.49). This will cause many beings to practice the transcendent state of wisdom, see it accurately, be inspired by it, and become established in it (10.53). In all future lives, they will spontaneously encounter this and other scriptures connected to the transcendent state of wisdom.
The first part of the chapter revolves around the wordplay of negating both the things opposed to proper practice and the things in line with proper practice.
The chapter corresponds to chapter 30 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and to chapter 39 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 11. The chapter “Māra’s Activity”

list.item: IV.iv. The Blessed One teaches the faults to overcome through practice as the forty-six types of Māra’s activities that bodhisattvas will encounter as obstacles. For instance, bodhisattvas might take a long time to become illuminated by explanations about the transcendent state of wisdom. Or, as they are being illuminated, they will become distracted. Or, as they study, they will fail to be mindful about it, lack trust or enjoyment in it, and so on (11.4). Or, they will think that they should instead seek the collections of scriptures that recommend the stages of disciples and solitary realizers (11.8). Or, they leave behind the transcendent state of wisdom and distance themselves from it and so on (11.15). Any distraction or lack of the necessary conditions to study or practice the transcendent state of wisdom is the activity of Māra (11.30). Motivated by the fact that the transcendent state of wisdom causes sentient beings to abandon the defilements and to attain the state of complete knowing (11.45), Māra magically influences bodhisattvas not to take in the Transcendent State of Wisdom, or to keep it in mind, chant it, master it, and so on (11.50). A bodhisattva must recognize these obstacles as activities of Māra and abandon them.

The chapter is framed by the central play on the Sanskrit word karma, which can mean a “deed,” “by-product,” or “magic.” The word karma is consistently translated here as the “activity” of Māra, but it has these three meanings in different parts of the chapter.

The chapter corresponds to all of chapter 31 and to the first part of chapter 32 (down to 32.62) in the Twenty-Five Thousand; to all of chapter 31 and to the first part of chapter 32 in the Hundred Thousand; and to all of chapters 40 and 41 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 12. The chapter “Showing the World”

list.item: IV.v. The Blessed One teaches that the transcendent state of wisdom has the defining characteristic of a mother of noble beings, because she gives birth to them, undertakes difficult noble deeds for them, gives them a life, and shows them the world (12.1). To be specific, she shows them that the world, which consists in the five constituents of experience, has twenty-three characteristics in the context of a worthy one’s complete knowing, nine characteristics in the context of a bodhisattva’s knowing of the path, and twelve characteristics for a buddha’s knowing of all aspects. For instance, she shows them how the world does not perish or get destroyed insofar as it has the nature of emptiness, freedom from signs, and so on (12.9). Also, she shows them how the mindsets of innumerable sentient beings are distracted or not, emotionally defiled or not, stuck or not, and so on (12.17). Furthermore, she shows them what is accepted and denied in those mindsets, such as existence or nonexistence after death, an eternal self or world, and so on (12.44). She shows them the actual nature of the world, which is to say, of all things (12.51). These characteristics are not dependent upon anything (12.57). But the buddhas depend upon this transcendent state of wisdom (12.61). Since this transcendent state of wisdom causes the buddhas to realize all things to be uncreated (akṛtā) and so on, they are appreciative and grateful (12.62).

The whole chapter plays on the words saṃdarśana and saṃdarśayitrī, and later in the chapter it plays on the words kṛtajña and akṛtajña, and niśrita and upaniśritya. The play on the words saṃdarśana and saṃdarśayitrī is that she “shows” them the world but also “makes the world appear” empty, uncreated, and so on. The play on the words kṛtajña and akṛtajña is that the buddhas are both “appreciative” (kṛtajña) toward her for showing the world and “know it to be uncreated” (akṛtajña). The play on the words niśrita and upaniśritya is that they are “dependent” (upaniśritya) on her for showing the characteristics to be “not dependent” (aniśrita) on anything.

The chapter corresponds to the rest of chapter 32 (from 32.63) and all of chapter 33 in the Twenty-Five Thousand; to the rest of chapter 32 and all of chapter 33 in the Hundred Thousand; and to chapters 42 and 43 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 13. The chapter “The Inconceivable”

list.item: IV.v. (cont.) The Blessed One teaches that the transcendent state of wisdom assists with tasks that are inconceivable, incomparable, immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled, because the state of an awakened one has these distinctive characteristics (13.1). In that state, one also sees all things, just like space, to be inconceivable, incomparable, immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled (13.16). Upon hearing this, the mindsets of many beings become free of clinging and liberated from worldly influences (13.18). But the state of complete knowing also has no apprehending and no fixation (13.24). Thus, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound, difficult to see, and difficult to comprehend. If a bodhisattva has firm conviction in it, he or she must have performed services for former conquerors and have planted the roots of virtue over a long period.

This chapter plays upon slightly different usages of the Sanskrit word kṛtya. Understand the translation of the singular “task” to mean a “service” (kṛtya) being performed for others and the translation of the plural “tasks” to mean the “duties” (kṛtya) of others being performed.

The chapter corresponds to the first part of chapter 34 (down to 34.16) in the Twenty-Five Thousand; to the equivalent first part of chapter 34 in the Hundred Thousand; and to the first part of chapter 44 (down to 44.13) in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 14. The chapter “The Comparisons”

list.item: IV.v. (cont.) The Blessed One teaches that a bodhisattva with firm conviction in the transcendent state of wisdom, who follows a Dharma teacher like a newborn calf following its mother, will die and be reborn exclusively among humans (14.2). But a bodhisattva could also have been reborn in the human realm after having died in other buddhafields, where he or she served (paryupāsya) other buddhas and fully pursued his or her questions (14.4). Whatever the case, if that bodhisattva does not have faith, trust, or longing, then he or she does not gain the support of the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means, and instead gets stuck at the levels of disciples or solitary realizers (14.8). The Blessed One gives a series of comparisons here for the benefits of faith, trust, and longing: after a shipwreck, holding on to a floating piece of wood (14.7); carrying water in a clay pot that has been fired rather than unfired (14.8); launching a boat whose holes are caulked rather than uncaulked (14.10); and getting out of bed at the age of one hundred twenty years old with the support of two strong men rather than without that support (14.14).

The chapter’s comparisons hinge upon worldly and spiritual usages of the same Sanskrit words. For example, a drowning person can depend for support (adhyālambate) on floating wood or a bodhisattva can depend for support (adhyālambate) on the transcendent state of wisdom; a pot can be fired (paripakvatva) by a kiln or a bodhisattva can be matured (paripakvatva) by the transcendent state of wisdom; a sailor can do repairs to a boat (parikarmakṛtāṃ) or a bodhisattva can do preliminary practices (parikarmakṛtāṃ) for the transcendent state of wisdom; a feeble old man can be held up (parigṛhītaṃ) by strong men or a bodhisattva can be held up (parigṛhītaṃ) by the transcendent state of wisdom.

The chapter corresponds to the rest of chapter 34 (from 34.17) and all of chapter 35 in the Twenty-Five Thousand; to the rest of chapter 34 and all of chapter 35 in the Hundred Thousand; and to the rest of chapter 44 (from 44.14) and all of chapter 45 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 15. The chapter “The Gods”

list.item: IV.v. (cont.) The Blessed One teaches that a beginning bodhisattva who wishes to train in this transcendent state of wisdom should be devoted to virtuous friends, under whose guidance he or she should engage in meditation in the perfection of generosity, ethical discipline, tolerant acceptance, heroic perseverance, meditation, and wisdom (15.2). While practicing these, a bodhisattva does not hold fast to awakening as physical forms and so on, because the transcendent state of wisdom is not held fast (15.4). Having set out to realize awakening in this way, a bodhisattva performs difficult noble deeds for the sake of the world’s benefit and happiness. He or she aims to become a refuge, a shelter, a resting place, and so on for the world (15.11), because he or she will teach people the truth that physical forms rest upon space (15.23). A beginning bodhisattva, who will come to know the profound transcendent state of wisdom, must possess the inherent nature devoid of needing to withdraw, (15.26) because he or she is equipped with the great armor, namely, the armor that is not equipped with anything. Thus, there is nothing actualized in the transcendent state of wisdom, because cultivating the transcendent state of wisdom is like cultivating space (15.33).

This chapter plays upon the two Sanskrit words gati and gatikā‍—both of which can refer either to the going to a destination or to the destination itself (in the sense of either a destiny or a state of existence). Here, the word gati is translated consistently as “resting place” since it is used as a synonym for refuge, and the word gatikā as “rest upon” because it leads to that.

The chapter corresponds to all of chapters 36 and 37, and the first part of chapter 38 (down to 38.14) in the Twenty-Five Thousand; to all of chapters 36 and 37 and the first part of chapter 38 in the Hundred Thousand; and to all of chapters 46 and 47 and the first part of chapter 48 (down to 48.8) in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 16. The chapter “The Actual Nature”

list.item: IV.v. (cont.) Subhūti explains that the truth has an unobstructed nature because, insofar as it is like space, it is free from the mental perception of any places. Śakra says that Subhūti is born after the Blessed One, because whenever he teaches the truth he teaches it in the light of emptiness. Subhūti replies that, in being unborn, he is born after the Tathāgata and has followed after the Tathāgata’s actual nature. This is because all things, the Tathāgata, and Subhūti all share the same actual nature, which is unchanging, free of change, nonconceptual, and free of conceptualization (16.3). It is a nondual actual nature that is free of duality and free from creating duality. It is not anywhere different from the actual nature of everything (16.6). Subhūti has assimilated that actual nature that belongs to him, the Tathāgata, and all things (16.7). Then, the Blessed One teaches that the mindsets of bodhisattvas in the assembly have only just then become free of clinging and liberated from worldly influences because, despite having served prior buddhas and accumulated merit, those bodhisattvas had previously only actualized the pinnacle of reality at the level of disciples or solitary realizers, not at the level of a buddha, because they were not supported by the transcendent state of wisdom, lacked skill in means, and had no virtuous friend (16.19).

IV.vi. Subhūti explains that all things are empty, including anyone who could realize awakening and anything that could be realized. The Blessed One teaches that since awakening is a nonoccurrence, not really existent, nonconceptual, and unfabricated, it is something extremely difficult to attain (16.25). No one dwells in the true nature of things alone by way of being free from dwelling on anything because nothing is mentally perceived as a continuum (16.53).

IV.vii. But if any bodhisattva wishes to go beyond cyclic existence to awakening, then he or she should dwell impartially toward all beings (16.67). He or she should regard others with a benevolent mindset and so on, should practice generosity and so on, and should help others to take on these practices (16.69).

This chapter plays upon three similar-sounding Sanskrit words‍—“born after” (anujāta), “followed after” (anuyāta), and “assimilated to” (anugata)‍—as different aspects of taking after the buddha’s actualization of the actual nature. It also plays on forms of the Sanskrit verb √sthā: “dwells” (sthita), “free from dwelling” (asthāna), and “continuum” (sthiti), to express the paradoxical nature of awakening as a state of being.

The chapter corresponds to all the remaining content of chapter 38 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (beginning at 38.15); to the (equivalent) remaining content of chapter 38 in the Hundred Thousand; and to all the remaining content of chapter 48 in the Eighteen Thousand (beginning at 48.9).

div.p: 17. The chapter “The Attributes, Indications, and Signs of Those Beyond Regressing”

list.item: IV.viii. The Blessed One now explains the attributes, indications, and signs of bodhisattvas beyond regressing. A bodhisattva beyond regressing has firm conviction and immerses him- or herself in the fact that the actual nature is exactly the way it is. He or she does not look up to other renunciants or brahmins and does not pay homage to gods and so on (17.3). He or she practices the ten virtuous ways of acting and so on (17.4). He or she masters a teaching and gives a teaching for the benefit of all sentient beings and so on. He or she does not become worried and so on and does not have a wandering mindset and so on (17.5). He or she embraces mental purity more and more (17.8). He or she gives no weight to worldly concerns such as receiving donations, tributes, fame, and so on. He or she does not waver or get deterred by Māra’s many tricks (17.11). because, by achieving direct realization in the true nature of things, he or she cannot be led astray and so on (17.12). He or she is never preoccupied with any dependent arising of the constituents of experience, elements, and bases, and so on (17.22). He or she even gives him- or herself away and gives up his or her life for the sake of apprehending the genuine truth (17.29).
This chapter does not have central puns or wordplay, since it is essentially a list of attributes, indications, and signs.
The chapter corresponds to all of chapters 39 and 40 in the Twenty-Five Thousand; all of chapters 39 and 40 in the Hundred Thousand; and all of chapters 49 and 50 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 18. The chapter “Emptiness”

list.item: IV.viii. (cont.) The Blessed One outlines the extremely profound points connected with the transcendent state of wisdom. The Blessed One teaches that the term profound here refers to emptiness, and the freedom from signs, inclinations, and so on (18.4). The term profound refers to everything because physical forms and so on are as profound as their actual nature (18.5). If a bodhisattva engages in meditation on this even just for a single day, that bodhisattva performs a karmic deed so great that it causes that bodhisattva to turn his or her back on cyclic existence for many eons and instead to abide in this transcendent state of wisdom, train in it, progress in it, and so on (18.12). If a bodhisattva were to engage in meditation in this transcendent state of wisdom even just for a single day, he or she would earn more merit than serving, supporting, making donations, and so on to all the worthy ones and solitary realizers for eons equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges (18.13). If a bodhisattva were also to actually engage in meditation in retreat again and again while constantly supported by the transcendent state of wisdom, and when emerging from meditation, to give others the gift of the Dharma, then he or she would earn an even greater amount of merit than that. But a bodhisattva, going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom at this point on the path, knows the accumulation of merit simply to be “empty.” (18.19). Anything that is empty is also inexhaustible, and its emptiness is also its immeasurability. The terms immeasurable, incalculable, inexhaustible, emptiness, the freedom from signs, and so on are all synonyms. Subhūti explains that the Buddha teaches (deśitā) the true nature of all things, and yet the true nature of all things is inexpressible (18.27). But the Buddha clarifies that emptiness is not somehow an inexpressible thing at the core of the transcendent states that decreases or increases (18.31). The actual nature that neither increases nor decreases is unsurpassed, perfect awakening (18.37).

A key wordplay in this chapter centers around the Sanskrit word abhisaṃskāra, which, to make sense in English, has been translated as “accumulation” in relation to merit but translated as “cumulative construction” (that is, mental construct, conceptualization) in relation to physical forms and so on.
The chapter corresponds to chapter 41 in the Twenty-Five Thousand; chapter 41 in the Hundred Thousand; and chapter 51 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 19. The chapter “The Noble Lady Gaṅgadevī”

list.item: IV.viii. (cont.) The Blessed One teaches that a bodhisattva does not realize awakening as a result of either the first or last mindsets of awakening‍—and yet he or she does not realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening without them. He or she also does not awaken as a result of anything other than those mindsets‍—and yet a bodhisattva does realize awakening (19.5). Also, a bodhisattva does not go about life aiming to abandon conventional signs (nimitta) in this very life, like a disciple who does not accomplish the qualities of a buddha. Rather, a bodhisattva has the skill in means to know what a conventional sign is, while intensively cultivating the freedom from signs (19.28).

IV.ix. A bodhisattva‍—who arrives at the transcendent state of wisdom, goes about life in it day in and day out, and repeatedly practices it‍—does the same even in his or her dreams. And since he or she knows all things to be like dreams, such a bodhisattva does not incur or accrue any karmic deeds (19.32). A tathāgata is just like pure space, free of all thinking and conceptualizing. Hence, he produces no karmic deeds. He has no mindset, mental activity, or karmic deeds because the twelve dependently arisen objective bases do not arise for him (19.36). In response to the question of whether giving gifts and dedicating the merit in dreams is somehow real, Maitreya explains that these things cannot be physically encountered, or verbally described, or mentally focused upon because all things lack any inherent nature (19.42).

IV.x. The Blessed One teaches that a bodhisattva is not frightened by anything because he or she engages in meditation with the confidence that he or she possesses a reserve of strength necessary to become completely awakened (19.45). He or she is not frightened, for instance, by carnivorous animals, by thieves, by a lack of drinkable water or food, by disease, and so on (19.48). A bodhisattva would also never develop a mindset that thinks it will take “a long time” to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening‍—nor would he or she be frightened at such a thought (19.51). Furthermore, a bodhisattva would don the great armor, thinking to apply him- or herself with unwavering heroic perseverance until realizing awakening, praying that in his or her buddhafield, all sentient beings will be free of all such things too‍—i.e., carnivorous animals, thieves, a lack of drinkable water or food, disease, and so on. The Blessed One also foreordains the perfect awakening of the noble lady Gaṅgadevī, after she comes forward to say she is not frightened of those places (19.53). He then describes her journey and what her buddhafield will be like when she attains awakening (19.58).

The theme of this chapter, rather than wordplay or negations, is Gaṅgadevī, a member of the assembly, stepping forward in an unusual manner to provide a living example of the bodhisattvas about whom the Blessed One has been speaking.

The chapter corresponds to the remainder of chapter 41 (starting at 41.47) and all of chapters 42 and 43 in the Twenty-Five Thousand; the remainder of chapter 41 and all of chapters 42 and 43 in the Hundred Thousand; and the remainder of chapter 51 (starting at 51.53) and all of chapters 52 and 53 in the Eighteen Thousand.

div.p: 20. The chapter “Examining Skill in Means”

list.item: IV.xi. The Blessed One teaches how a bodhisattva should cultivate emptiness and engage in the meditative concentration on it by not identifying the true nature of things as the true nature of things, and not directly actualizing the pinnacle of reality (20.2) He or she does this through considering emptiness to be replete with all the excellent qualities to be ripened through abiding in the gateway to complete liberation, namely, the meditative concentration on emptiness and so on (20.4). The Blessed One teaches why a bodhisattva chooses not to actualize the pinnacle of reality directly, using the similes of an extremely able and courageous man who saves his family from dangerous attacks (20.5); of a large bird floating in midair without landing; and of a master archer able to prevent his arrow from falling to the earth (20.10). Likewise, a bodhisattva does not actualize the pinnacle of reality because he or she is supported by skills in various means and wishes to increase his or her pure qualities so that he or she can eventually teach the Dharma to all sentient beings and free them from their misconceptions and so on (20.13). The topic of the bodhisattva’s skill in means continues to 20.18.

V.i. The Blessed One then teaches how to recognize a bodhisattva beyond regressing. Among the signs are if a bodhisattva, even when dreaming, can perceive everything as a dream without actualizing awakening; does not develop a mindset to benefit him- or herself; sees him- or herself teaching the Dharma as a buddha; is not frightened by encounters with great danger or suffering; sees sentient beings in the realms of the hells, hungry ghosts, or animals and then regains mindfulness; or performs miraculous acts of truth to stop fires. It is also such a sign if a bodhisattva performs miraculous acts of truth while not dreaming.

The chapter corresponds to all of chapter 44 and the first part chapter 45 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (down to 45.8); all of chapter 44 and the first part chapter 45 in the Hundred Thousand; and all of chapter 54 and the first part of chapter 55 in the Eighteen Thousand (down to 55.9).

div.p: 21. The chapter “Detailing Māra’s Activities”
