Since I myself do not find, mentally perceive, or identify a bodhisattva or any thing called a ‘bodhisattva,’ and I also do not find, mentally perceive, or identify a ‘transcendent state of wisdom,’ what bodhisattva shall I instruct and guide, in what transcendent state of wisdom?According to Ratnākara (F.12.a–b), the repeated word “bodhisattva” in this sentence marks the transition to the subtopic “practical instructions” (the second subtopic of the first main topic in the Ornament, see introduction, “Synopsis of the Text According to the Ornament”). RK: de nas gdams ngag gi skabs la babs nas| gnas brtan rab ’byor gyis ji skad bshad pa bzhin sdud pa pos| de nas sangs rgyas zhes bya ba la sogs pa smras so| |byang chub sems dpa’ sems dpa’ zhes bya ba’i tshig gnyis ni de dang de yin zhes rtogs par byed pa. Nonetheless, O Blessed One, when this is being described, taught, and expounded in this way, if the mindset of a bodhisattva great being does not become deflated, depressed, dejected, or sunk in despondency, and his or her mental attitude does not become discouraged, shattered, frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror, then this very bodhisattva great being should be guided in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.14.a–b), (a) in the phrase “the mindset does not become deflated,” the “mindset”‍—in thinking about the meaning as it has just been described‍—is “not deflated,” i.e., not especially despondent, because he or she is reflecting upon that meaning (i.e., emptiness) alone; (b) “not depressed” means while subsequently applying him- or herself continuously (to that meaning), even then he or she is not deflated; (c) “not dejected” means when he or she recollects (the meaning) after recognizing that the mind has wandered elsewhere because it was naturally distracted, he or she is not upset; (d) “not sunk in despondency” means as his or her mindset has become very stable, when it gets increasingly subtle because of not proliferating into other coarse understandings, then he or she is not upset; (e) “his or her”‍—i.e., the bodhisattva’s‍—“mental attitude does not become discouraged” means with respect to that very state, he or she still pacifies the mind in order to remain neutral even while remaining in meditative concentration, because he or she sees the qualities of meditativate concentration; (f) “not shattered” means he or she does not get distracted by the lack of joy or inclination in the light of the (meaning), because of seeing the faults of distraction; (g) “not frightened” means even when covetousness or dissatisfaction arise, he or she does not get afraid and also overcomes that which arises, because he or she sees the faults of distraction. (h) “not terrified” means he or she is not frightened about the formation of any mental object that occurs of its own accord automatically in his or her mind that is free of faults, and hence he or she does that (i.e., declines to get involved in them); and (i) “not stricken with terror” means his or her mindset becomes perfectly (settled), and without any effort he or she is neither terrified nor totally frightened by any occurrence in the mind, because he or she perceives the occurrences in a natural and spontaneous way. This is the meaning. RK: (a) sems mi zhum pa zhes bya ba ni ji ltar thob pa de nyid la don la sems pa la sems khyad du mi gsod mi zhum ste| de nyid du nges par brtag pa’i phyir ro|| (b) kun tu zhum par mi bgyid ces bya ba ni sems kyi rgyun du ’jug pa la yang rjes su mi ’brang zhing zhum par mi bgyid do| (c) |mi ’gong zhes bya ba ni sems rang bzhin gyis g.yo ba’i phyir| gzhan du song ba la yang kun nas shes par byas nas sdud pa na skyo ba med pa yin no| (d) |mi gsod [F.14v] par mi ’gyur zhes bya ba ni sems shin tu brtan par gyur na yang rang bzhin gyis rtogs pa rags pa gzhan du mi ’phro ba’i phyir gong nas gong du phra mor byed pa na skyo ba med pa yin no| (e) |de’i zhes bya ba ni byang chub sems dpa’o| |yid phyir phyogs par mi bgyid ces bya ba ni ting nge ’dzin la gnas par byas pa yin yang bar mar gnas pa’i phyir te| de lta mod kyi de nyid du sems ’dul bar byed de| ting nge ’dzin gyi yon tan rnams mthong bas so| (f) |bcom zhing phyir phyogs par mi ’gyur zhes bya ba ni de la dga’ ba med pa dang| mos pa med pas rnam par g.yeng ba byed pa med par ’gyur te| rnam par g.yeng ba’i skyon rnams mthong ba’i phyir ro| (g) |mi skrag ces bya ba ni brnab sems dang| yid mi bde ba la sogs pa skyes pa na yang mi skrag pa yin te| ’on kyang de nyid ’joms pa ni yin te| g.yeng ba’i skyon mthong ba’i phyir ro| (h) |mi dngang zhes bya ba ni sems skyon med pa rang gis ngang gis ’byung ba nyid kyi don gang yin pa’i ’du byed de la mi dngang zhing de byed pa zhes bya ba’i don to| (i) |dngang bar mi ’gyur zhes bya ba ni sems yang dag par ’gyur te| ’bad pa med par rang gi ’byung bas dngang ba dang| yang dag par dngang ba de med pa yin te| lhun gyis grub pa nyid ngang gis ’byung ba ’dzin zhes bya ba’i don to. For edited Tibetan text of them, see Seton 2016, Aii, pp. 42–43. Note: Ratnākara is suggesting here that these nine mental states correspond to the nine stages of resting the mind in meditative concentration, which results in tranquility (śamatha). Below, he says that the last four of these nine stages are combined with the four stages of seeing clearly (vipaśyanā). Precisely this state should be understood as that bodhisattva great being’s transcendent state of wisdom. Precisely this state is the instruction in the transcendent state of wisdom. When a bodhisattva great being is abiding in such a mindset, that alone is his or her instruction and guidance.According to Ratnākara (F.14.b), he says this sentence in order to teach the ultimate bodhisattva and so on. “Precisely this state” refers to meditative concentration, which the bodhisattva has, because it is a particular type of mindset. “Precisely this state” refers to the transcendent state of wisdom that he or she alone is endowed with and “the instruction.” When someone with those two is “abiding in such a mindset”‍—i.e., when he or she is abiding in meditative concentration‍—then the “instruction” is not to abandon it and the “guidance” is connected to that. RK: don dam pa nyid kyi byang chub sems dpa’ la sogs pa bstan par bya ba’i phyir ’di nyid ces bya ba la sogs pa gsungs so| |ting nge ’dzin ’di nyid byang chub sems dpa’ yin te| sems kyi khyad par yin pa’i phyir ro| |’di nyid ces bya ba ni de dang ldan pa nyid shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa dang gdams pa yin no| |gang de gnyis dang yang dag par ldan pa ni de ltar gnas na zhes bya ba la ting nge ’dzin ’di nyid la gnas na ’di mi gtong ba yin te| de nyid gdams pa dang rjes su ’brel ba’i rjes su bstan pa’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 43. Note: Schmithausen (1977, pp. 46–47) postulates that these pronouns have problematic antecedents and were later additions to an earlier scripture. But RK treats them as pointing directly to something in a bodhisattva’s immediate experience.

“Furthermore, O Blessed One, by going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivating it,According to Haribhadra (37), he or she is “going about life” with unconcentrated knowing (jñāna), and “cultivating” (bhāvanā; sgom) with concentrated knowing (jñāna). Hari: caratety asamāhitena jñānena. bhāvayateti samāhitena. a bodhisattva great being should train in such a way that, while being trained, he or she would not develop conceits even about that mindset of awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.17.b–18.a), “furthermore” means that this paragraph relies (ltos pa) upon the practice instructions (explained in RK’s commentary on the Ornament). When “going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom,” the meditative reference point is the impermanence of the three noble truths and so on. His or her meditative perspective is nonattachment (anabhiniveśa; mngon par zhen pa med pa) and so on. So he or she is “going about life” in the freedom from reference points. When “cultivating” the transcendent state of wisdom‍—i.e., gradually cultivating it‍—his or her meditative reference point is the noble truth of the path included within the three former truths, which is the freedom from any reference point. Hence, the meditative perspective is the freedom from “conceit” that is to be known through cultivating that. He or she should “train in such a way that” means he or she should train without conceit and without being enmeshed. In the phrase “even that mindset of awakening,” that “mindset”‍—which is unequaled, because the disciples and solitary realizers do not share it‍—is a vast mindset because it is the source of benefiting sentient beings uninterruptedly. The term “mindset of awakening” (bodhicitta) should be understood here as the “mindset” whose nature consists in “awakening” (*bodhyātmakacittam). The word “even” is for emphasis. Therefore, the mindset of bodhisattvas is asserted to possess particular nobility compared to the mindset of all sentient beings. So if a bodhisattva’s mindset “would not develop conceits even about that,” then what need is there to speak of an ordinary mindset. RK: gzhan yang zhes smos pas ni gdams ngag la ltos pa’o| |shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa la spyod ces bya ba ni bden pa gsum dang ’brel ba’i mi rtag pa nyid la sogs pa ni dmigs pa’o| |mngon par zhen pa med pa la sogs pa ni rnam pa ste| mi dmigs par spyod pa’o| | shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa bsgom pas zhes bya ba smos pa ni rim gyis bsgom pa zhes bya ba’i don to| |bden pa gsum gyi ’og tu lam gyi bden pa’i dmigs pa ni dmigs pa med pa’o| |des rlom sems med pa ni rnam pa bsgoms pas shes par bya ba yin no| |de ltar bslab par bgyi’o zhes bya ba ni mngon par zhen pa med cing rlom sems med pa de ltar bslab par bya’o| |des kyang zhes bya bas ni gang de’i sems mnyam pa med pa yin te| nyan thos dang| rang sangs rgyas la sogs pa dang thun mong ma yin pa’i phyir ro| |sems rgya chen po rnam pa thams cad du dus chad pa med par sems can gyi don gyi bya ba rnams kyi ’byung gnas su gyur pa’i phyir ro| |byang chub sems zhes bya ba ni byang chub kyi bdag nyid kyi sems so| |kyang gi sgra ni lhag pa’i don to| |de’i phyir byang chub sems dpa’ rnams kyi sems ni sems can thams cad las khyad par du ’phags pa yod par ’dod de| des kyang rlom sems su mi byed na tha mal pa’i sems kyis lta smos kyang ci dgos zhes bya ba’i don to. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 52. Note: See the introduction to this translation for more on the term “mindset of awakening” (bodhicitta), its translation, and its interpretation in India (see introduction, “Mindset of Awakening”). Why is that? It is because that mindset is free of a mindset, which is to say, it is the luminously clear essential nature of a mindset.”According to Ratnākara (F.18.a), “why is that?” asks the question what is the meditative perspective due to which he or she does not develop conceits about that. “It is because” and so on expresses the answer. He says, “That mindset is free of a mindset” because it (de) does not appear as either (gnyis la) the grasped or the grasper. But one might think that the mindset does not exist, so he also states that it is “the luminously clear essential nature of a mindset.” One might ask, “How is that mind luminously clear?” The mind is primordially free of attachment and so on, because those are adventitious by nature. For precisely that reason, the subsequent (phyis) freedom from those (i.e., attachment and so on) does not exist. RK: |de ci’i slad du zhe na? zhes bya ba ni rnam pa gang gis de la rlom sems med pa yin zhe na? ’di ltar zhes bya ba la sogs pas lan smras so| |sems de ni sems ma mchis pa zhes bya ba ni gzung ba dang ’dzin pa gnyis la de mi snang ba’i phyir ro| |gal te sems med na ’on kyang de sems kyi rang bzhin ni ’od gsal ba zhes smos so| |ji ltar ’od gsal ba yin zhe na? gang gi phyir sems ni ’dod chags la sogs pa dang dang po nas mi ldan pa yin te| de dag ni rang bzhin gyis glo bur ba yin pa’i phyir ro| |de nyid kyi phyir phyis kyang bral ba med do. For edited Tibetan text of his broader comments, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 52. According to Hari (38–39), it is a nonimplicative negation. Hence, that (relative) “mindset” of awakening, which is not distinct from the perspective of the freedom from habitual fixation and so on, is ultimately “free of a mindset” since the luminously clear essential nature of a mindset is free of identity that is simple (i.e., partless) or complex (i.e., has parts). Note: In accordance with their respective philosophical positions of Yogācāra and Madhyamaka, Ratnākara and Hari disagree about whether the negation here is an exclusion/implicative negation (paryudāsa; ma yin dgag) or an absolute, nonimplicative negation (prasajyapratiṣedha; med dgag). In his Instructions on the Transcendent State of Wisdom, Ratnākara explains that the negation in this sentence is an exclusion (paryudāsa). Hence, that “mindset” of awakening is, on the one hand, “free of a mindset” insofar as there is no subject grasping or object grasped; on the other hand, it is “a mindset,” insofar as it consists in nothing but reflexively aware illumination.

Then the venerable Śāriputra asked the venerable Subhūti, “But, Venerable Subhūti, if that mindset is free of a mindset, does that mindset exist?”According to Ratnākara (F.18.a), “Then the venerable Śāriputra said …” thus, he knew, but asked the question to cause Subhūti to explain it. The next sentence is Subhūti’s reply to his question, in which he explains it. RK: |shA ri’i bus ’di skad ces smras so zhes bya ba ni de ltar shes kyang rab ’byor gyis ston par bya pa’i phyir dris so| |de skad ces smras pa zhes bya ba la sogs pas ni rab ’byor gyis dris pa’i lan ston to. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 52.
In response, the venerable Subhūti asked the venerable Śāriputra, “Well, Venerable Śāriputra, in the state free of a mindset, is an existence or a nonexistence found or mentally perceived?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” replied the venerable Śāriputra.

The venerable Subhūti continued, “O Venerable Śāriputra, if, in that state free of a mindset, an existence or a nonexistence is neither found nor mentally perceived, then is your questioning of my statement reasonable as you expressed it, saying, ‘If that mindset is free of a mindset, does that mindset exist?’ ”According to Ratnākara (F.18.a), “definitely not” means (an existence or nonexistence) is “not mentally perceived” because there is no proof of them, and also “not found” because there is disproof of them. Their negation perfectly transcends both existence (bhāva; dngos po) and nonexistence. “Does that mindset exist?” asks the question “does that nonexistence (nāstitā; med pa nyid) exist?” RK: |de ni med do zhes bya ba de ni mi dmigs pa ste| sgrub par byed pa med pa’i phyir ro| |de nyid pa yang ma yin te| gnod par byed pa yod pa’i phyir ro| |dngos po dang| dngos po med pa las yang dag par ’das pa ni gnod par byed pa yin no| |yod dam zhes bya ba ni ci de med pa nyid ces bya ba’i don to. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 52.

In response, the venerable Śāriputra asked the venerable Subhūti, “What then, Venerable Subhūti, is the state free of a mindset?”According to Ratnākara (F.18.a–b), even just based on Śāriputra’s question, Subhūti thought that Śāriputra was not clear, so to clarify, Śāriputra asks, “What is the state free of a mindset?” RK: de tsam gyis kyang rab ’byor gyis bsams pas gsal por ma gyur nas| gsal bar bya ba’i phyir dris pa ni de gang yin zhes bya’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 52.

The venerable Subhūti then said this: “O Venerable Śāriputra, the state free of a mindset is free of changing and free of conceptualization.”According to Ratnākara, Subhūti replies “the state free of a mindset is free of changing” because it is luminously clear by nature and untainted by changes. It is also “free of conceptualization” because all conceptual proliferations have subsided in it and it is not the content of any representational forms (ākāra; rnam pa). Hence, he or she “does not create conceits even about such a mindset” because, in that state, the mindset is simply nonexistent (sems med pa)‍—in other words, because that mindset is simply the emptiness of a mindset (sems stong pa nyid tsam las). Thus, the meditative reference point is “that mindset is free of a mindset (sems med pa), because it is naturally luminously clear.” The meditative perspective is the freedom from conceits. One should also understand here implicitly that just as the mindset is free of a mindset, so too physical form and so on are free of physical form and so on. RK: |rab ’byor gyi lan ni sems med pa de ni ’gyur ba med pa rnam par rtog pa med pa zhes smos te| sems rang bzhin gyis ’od gsal ba dang| ’gyur ba ma gos pa’i phyir ’gyur ba med pa’o | |spros pa thams cad nye bar zhi ba dang| rnam pa thams cad kyi spyod yul ma yin pa’i phyir rnam par rtog pa med pa’o | |de lta bas na de lta bu’i sems kyis kyang rlom sems su mi bya ste| de la yang sems med pa tsam gyi phyir te| sems stong pa nyid tsam las zhes bya ba’i don to| |de ltar na sems de yang sems med pa yin te rang bzhin gyis ’od gsal ba ni dmigs pa’o| |de la yang rlom sems med pa ni rnam pa’o| |ji ltar sems la sems med pa ste nye bar gzugs la sogs pa la yang gzugs la sogs pa med pa nyid du don gyis shes pa yin no. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, pp. 52–53. According to Hari (40), it is “free of changing” because the freedom from a mindset mentioned here (at the yogi’s conventional level) is a cause, step by step, for the actualization of freedom from change at the level of a buddha. It is “free of conceptualization” because it is a cause of a buddha’s actualization of freedom from error (at that level too).

The venerable Śāriputra then said to the venerable Subhūti, “Excellent, Venerable Subhūti, excellent. You are explaining just the way that the Blessed One indicated you would, that is to say, as the foremost among those who abide free of conflict!Śāriputra’s comment may include a broad notion of “conflict” (raṇa), i.e., Subhūti does not get caught in contradictions due to the debate challenges by Śāriputra, the wisest and most intelligent of the Buddha’s disciples. But the Tibetan translation interprets this conflict as “emotional defilement” (nyon mongs). For more on the designation of Subhūti as foremost among those free of conflict, see the Araṇavibhaṅgasutta in Majjhima Nikāya 3:231–237. See also AKBh 417 and MSABh 20–21.44.1 to 20–21.45.1, where freedom from conflict (araṇa) is explicitly associated with a lack of emotional defilements. Further details of Subhūti’s other designations as “foremost among…” can be found in the Introduction to The Perfection of Wisdom in Twenty-Five Thousand Lines (Toh 9), i.78–90. So, from this, we should understand a bodhisattva great being to be beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Also, we should know a bodhisattva great being never to be lacking the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara, “never to be lacking” the transcendent state of wisdom means increasing it even more and more. This statement corresponds to the particular meditative reference point and meditative perspective at the stage of initial warmth (in the four stages of insight on the path of preliminary practice). RK: shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa dang ma bral zhes bya ba des de’i gong nas gong du yang ’phel ba yin te| de ni dro ba chung ngu’i dmigs pa dang rnam pa’i khyad par dag go. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 53.

“Furthermore, anyone who wishes to train for the level of a disciple must listen to this very transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, and pass it on.According to Ratnākara (F.18.b–19.a), in this statement the phrase “wishes to train” means wishes to accomplish, and the word “for” indicates the factor motivating that training (nimitta); the phrase “listen to” means listen to texts; the phrase “take it in” means repeat it; the phrase “keep it in mind” means mentally retain it; the phrase “chant it” means say it aloud so that one does not forget it; the phrase “master it” means make oneself know everything together with the meaning; and the phrase “pass it on” means teach the words and the meaning to others so that by virtue of one’s own merit they may enter the path and come to be completely awakened. RK: |sa la zhes bya ba ni rgyu mtshan gyi don to| |slob par ’dod pas zhes bya ba ni sgrub par ’dod pas so| |mnyen par bya ba ni gzhung nyan pa’o| |gzung bar bya zhes bya ba ni bzlas par bya ba’o| |bcang bar bya zhes bya ba ni sems la gzhag par bya ba’o| |bklag par bya zhes bya ba ni mi brjed pa’i don du rjod par byed pa’o| |kun chub par bya’o zhes bya ba ni don dang ldan pas thams cad shes pa nyid du bya ba’o| |rab tu ston par bya pa’o | |zhes bya ba ni gzhan dag la don dang gzhung gi don dag| | bstan pas bdag nyid kyi bsod nams kyi stobs kyi gzhan dag rjes su gzung ba ni byang chub pa’i don du gnas pa yin no. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 55. According to Hari (42), the phrase “wishes to train” means wishes to do the practice; the phrase “listen to” means determining the words and meaning through both hearing consciousness and mental consciousness being drawn toward it; the phrase “take it in” means paying attention with a consciousness that is contemplating it; the phrase “keep it in mind” means perfecting it through practice with a consciousness that is cultivating it; the phrase “chant it” means vocalizing the text as a daily undertaking; the phrase “master it” means scripture and realization; and the phrase “pass it on” means elucidating it for others by explaining it and so on. Note: a “level” (bhūmi) refers here to the stable condition of a practitioner’s knowing achieved at a particular stage of the path, e.g., the ten bodhisattva levels leading to buddhahood. Also, instead of “keep it in mind” (dhārayitavyā), Schopen’s review of Edward Conze’s translation (p. 142) suggests the verb to mean he or she “carries around” (dhārayiṣyati) the scripture. But the commentators say something different here. He or she must train and engage in meditation in this very transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.19.a), the phrase “engage in meditation” (yogam āpattavyam; rnal ’byor du bya) means attain tranquility (śamatha) and extraordinary insight (vipaśyanā; lhag mthong). RK: |bslab par bya’o zhes bya ba ni sems dpas so| |rnal ’byor du bya’o zhes bya ba la rnal ’byor ni zhi gnas dang lhag mthong ngo| |de bya’o zhes bya ba ni thob par bya ba’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 55. According to Hari (42), one should “engage in meditation” because of one’s fundamental mindset bringing about a state of concentration (samādhi). Note: the same Sanskrit phrase yogam āpattavyam is translated by Conze (86) as “exert himself.”

“Furthermore, anyone who wishes to train for the level of a solitary realizer must listen to this transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, and pass it on. He or she must train and engage in meditation in this very transcendent state of wisdom.

“Furthermore, anyone who wishes to train for the level of a bodhisattva must listen to this very transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, and pass it on. Anyone endowed with skill in means must engage in meditation in this very transcendent state of wisdom in order to achieve all the bodhisattva qualities.According to Ratnākara (F.19.a), “for the level of a bodhisattva” means the ten levels from the first called “Delighted” up through the last called “Clouds of Dharma.” There are ten types of “skill in means” explained below in Chapter 20, at the end of section on realization of all aspects (the fourth main topic in the Ornament). “Endowed with” means possessing that. “To achieve” means to perfect. These are the distinctions of the causes alone. RK: |byang chub sems dpa’i sa la zhes bya ba ni rab tu dga’ ba nas chos kyi sprin gyi bar rnam pa bcu’o| |thabs mkhas pa ni ’og nas bshad pa’i rnam pa kun mngon par rdzogs par rtogs pa’i mjug nas ’byung ba’i rnam pa bcu’o| |de dang ldan pa ni yod pa’o| |yongs su bsgrubs pa ni kun nas mthar phyin pa’o| |de dag ni rgyu nyid kyi khyad par ro. Why is that? It is because all the bodhisattva qualities are expounded in detail in this very transcendent state of wisdom in which a bodhisattva must train and engage in meditation.

“Finally, anyone who wishes to train even in unsurpassed, perfect awakening must listen to this transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, and pass it on. Why is that? It is because all the buddha qualities are expounded in detail here in this very transcendent state of wisdom in which a bodhisattva must train and engage in meditation.”

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 5 of the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 8 of the Eighteen Thousand.The Venerable Subhūti then said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, I myself do not find, do not mentally perceive, and do not identify this very designation bodhisattva. Nor do I find, mentally perceive, or identify the transcendent state of wisdom. Given that I myself am not finding, not mentally perceiving, and not identifying the very designation bodhisattva, and given that I am also not finding, not mentally perceiving, and not identifying a transcendent state of wisdom, what bodhisattva am I supposed to instruct and guide in what transcendent state of wisdom? O Blessed One, it would certainly be unconscionable of me if I myself‍—while not finding, not mentally perceiving, and not identifying any actual thing‍—were to make one appear and disappear by means of the mere designation bodhisattva.According to Ratnākara (F.19.a–b), the word “unconscionable” here means that making something appear or disappear would only do something that does not fit the occasion. Here, to “appear” means to understand it through words, whereas to “disappear” means to cause the ascertainment of something else through words. The phrase “by means of the mere designation” means by words empty of any meaning. Here, the designation “bodhisattva” is either being put forth as an example of an empty word, or‍—in the case that something that appears or disappears is held, respectively, to be affirmed or denied by that word‍—it is not a so-called bodhisattva. RK: glo ba la gcags pa’i dngos po nyid ni yid la gcags pa ste| rigs pa ma yin pas byas pa nyid kyis so| |’du ba dang ’jig pa zhes bya ba la sgra las rtogs pa ni ’du ba’o| |sgra las gzhan gyi nges pa skyed pa ni ’jig pa’o| |ming tsam gyis zhes bya ba ni don gyis stong pa’i ming ste| ’di lta ste byang chub sems dpa’ zhes bya ba lta bu’o| gang dag ’du ba dang ’jig pa gal te bya ba dang| dgag pa yin par ’dod par gyur pa de’i tshe ni ’di lta ste |byang chub sems dpa’ byang chub sems dpa’ ma yin zhes bya bar gsungs bar ’gyur ro. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 56. According to Hari (44), he says it would be “unconscionable”‍—i.e., a regrettable thing‍—because he has a feeling connected with the fact that he does not perceive any existent referent of the term. This feeling is expressed by speaking of making one “appear,” i.e., affirming one, or “disappear,” i.e., denying one, in accord with what Dharmakīrti says (Pramāṇavarttika, IV.225), “A conventional designation (vyavahara) is just a sound, nothing else, except for its activity to affirm or deny …”. Since these two words‍—“appear” and “disappear”‍—include all its functions, a designation is taught here to be nothing more than this, i.e., sound. Hari: kaukṛtyam iti saṃvedananiṣṭhatvād viṣayasthiter anupalambhād etat kaukṛtyaṃ cetaso vipratisāritā. tad evāha yo ’ham ityādinā. āyavyayam iti vidhir āyaḥ. vyayo niṣedhaḥ. vidhānaṃ pratiṣedhaṃ ca muktvā śābdo ’sti nāparo vyavahāra iti kṛtvā padadvayenaiva sarvasaṃgrahād iha tāvanmātrakam uktaṃ. Furthermore, O Blessed One, that designation is neither something dwelling, nor something not dwelling, nor something free of dwelling, nor something not free of dwelling.This tetralemma about “dwelling” (sthita)‍—which effectively involves single, double, and triple negatives‍—sets up a play on the phrase “free of dwelling” (asthāna; mi gnas pa) below. According to (F.19.b), the designation “not dwelling” refers to it not having permanence, i.e., becoming a real particular or remaining in any condition. The words “nor not dwelling” mean it is not a dwelling in change or a dwelling in a permanent continuity. Since the basic idea is that nothing could appear or disappear, the last two phrases, “something free of dwelling” and “not something not free of dwelling,” have similar meanings. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 57. RK: (a) |mi gnas zhes bya ba ni khyad par du ’gro ba ste| ther zug tu gnas pas rtag pa med pa zhes pa’i don to| (b) |mi gnas pa la yang ma lags zhes bya ba ni ’gyur ba’i gnas pa dang| rgyun gyis rtag pas gnas pa ma yin zhes bya ba’i don to| | rigs pa ’dis ’du ba dang ’jig pa yod pa ma yin no zhes bya bar bsams so| |de bzhin du (c) gnas pa med pa dang| (d) gnas pa med pa ma yin pa’o. According to Hari (45), the designation is something “not dwelling” as an object, because it has the nature of a continuous series. Nor is the designation “not dwelling.” This accords with the following logical principle (in Jñānagarbha’s Distinguishing the Two Truths): “At the ultimate level, there clearly can be no negation because it has nothing to be negated, and the application of a negation would not be proper without a particular scope. Also, if a conceptualization were taken as the basis for negation, then that conceptualization is something relative, not at the ultimate level.” The designation is “something free of dwelling” because of being unlike a continuous series. And it is “not something not free of dwelling” because it is not something that exists. Hari: tad api nāmadheyam ālambanaṃ na sthitaṃ prabandharūpeṇa. niṣedhyābhāvataḥ spaṣṭaṃ na niṣedho ’sti tattvataḥ na ca nirviṣayaḥ sādhuḥ prayogo vidyate nañaḥ || vikalpāpāśrayatve vā sāṃvṛtaḥ syān na tāttvika iti nyāyān nāsthitaṃ. na viṣṭhitaṃ prabandhavisadṛśatvena. tathaiva nāviṣṭhitaṃ tat kasya hetoḥ ||avidyamānatvena tasya nāmadheyasya. Why is that? It is because that designation is not being found to exist. Thus, the designation is neither something dwelling, nor something not dwelling, nor something free of dwelling, nor something not free of dwelling.According to Ratnākara (F.19.b), this repeat of the above four possibilities can be understood slightly differently. In that sentence, the word “thus” means “given that words and their meaning are not two things.” Next, (a) the words “not dwelling” mean it is not something that remains in the actual nature. (b) The words “nor not dwelling” mean it is not just a state of nondwelling‍—here the idea is that insofar as it is an ascertainment of conventional daily dealings, it is not merely an imputation. (c) The words “nor something free of dwelling” means that the mere imputation is not something dwelling in physical form or any other manner of constituents of experience or elements‍—hence, the idea here is that it is not something inexpressible. (d) The words “nor something not free of dwelling” means it is not something absolutely free of dwelling insofar as it actually is something that can express conventional daily dealings. RK: de ltar zhes bya ba ni don dang ming gnyis med pas so| (a) |mi gnas shing zhes bya ba ni de kho na nyid la rnam par gnas pa ma yin pa’o| (b) |mi gnas pa yang ma lags | zhes bya ba ni gnas pa yang ma yin pa nyid tha snyad nges pa yin pa’i phyir de ni btags pa tsam ma yin| zhes bsam pa yin no| (c) |rnam par gnas pa yang ma lags | zhes bya ba la brags pa tsam de yang gzugs la rnam par gnas pa ma yin| phung po dang| khams la sogs pa’i ngo bo rnam pa sna tshogs su gnas pa ma yin no| |gang gi phyir des na brjod par bya ba ma yin no zhes bya bar bsams so| (d) |rnam par mi gnas pa yang ma yin te | zhes bya ba ni gtan du rnam par mi gnas pa ma yin te| gang zhig gis tha snyad tsam du brjod par nus pa’i phyir ro. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 57.

“O Blessed One, when this profound transcendent state of wisdom is being described, taught, and expounded in this way, if a bodhisattva great being’s mindset does not become deflated, depressed, dejected, or sunk in despondency, and his or her mental attitude does not become discouraged, shattered, frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror, then that bodhisattva great being has firm conviction with an altruistic disposition. Also, one should recognize him or her to be never lacking the transcendent state of wisdom, to dwell on the level of a bodhisattva beyond regressing, and to dwell with ease by means of freedom from dwelling.According to Ratnākara (F.20.a), the term “beyond regressing” indicates that the continuing progress of these bodhisattvas toward perfect, complete awakening has become absolutely certain. Having succeeded in this very state of not regressing, they become those with the necessary conditions for success. The term “level of a bodhisattva” refers to an interval of time, called a “level,” during which a bodhisattva possesses a certain set of qualities. The phrase “dwelling with ease,” etc. here means dwelling perfectly, and the phrase “by means of nondwelling” refers to acting without mentally perceiving the three (i.e., agent, action, and recipient). RK: phyir mi ldog pa zhes bya ba la sogs pa smos pa la phyir mi ldog ces bya ba’i phyir mi ldog pa ni yang dag par rdzogs pa’i byang chub las ldog pa med par nges pa’o| |de nyid la phan pas na phan pa’i rkyen can no| |byang chub sems dpa’i sa ni go skabs te ’di la yon tan gyi tshogs yod pas na sa ste| de la gnas pa’i legs pa zhes bya ba ni de nyid la yang dag par gnas pa’o| |ji ltar zhe na| gnas pa’i tshul gyis te| gsum mi dmigs pa’i sbyor bas so. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 58. According to Hari (46), he or she is “dwelling on” means he or she has attained and not abandoned “the level of a bodhisattva beyond regressing,” i.e., the eighth level, due to attaining the resultant state beyond regressing. We should recognize him or her “to dwell with ease” because, without attaining or abandoning anything, he or she gains the increasingly more subtle realizations to be developed in that state. And both of these ways of dwelling involve “dwelling by means of freedom from dwelling,” i.e., dwelling by means of freedom from habitual fixation (anabhiniveśa). Hari: sthitaḥ prāptāparihāṇyā avinivartanīyāyām aṣṭamyāṃ bodhisattvabhūmau tatkāryāvaivartika­tvalābhāt. susthitas tadutpādana­yogyatvenottarottara­viśeṣādhi­gamalābhād aprāptāparihāṇyā. etac ca dvayam anabhiniveśayogenety āha: asthānayogene ti.

“Furthermore, O Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being who goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivates the transcendent state of wisdom would not dwell on physical forms, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness.According to Ratnākara (F.20.b), “in the transcendent state of wisdom” means at the initial stage of peak (on the stages of insight on path of preliminary practice). The phrase “goes about life” means by studying and contemplating. The word “cultivates” means by meditative tranquility and extraordinary insight. This refers to his or her not dwelling on physical form and so on as described. RK: |shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa la zhes bya ba ni rtse mo chung ngu’i mtshan nyid la’o| |spyod cing zhes bya ba ni thos pa dang bsams pa dag gis so| |de la bsgoms pas zhes bya ba ni zhi gnas dang lhag mthong dag gis so| |gzugs la sogs pa la gnas par mi bgyi’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii: 60. Why is that? It is because, if he or she were to dwell on physical forms, he or she would go about life in the cumulative construction of physical forms.According to Ratnākara (F.20.b), the term “cumulative construction” (abhisaṃskāra) refers to “conceptualization” (vikalpa). So, he or she “would not go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom” because he or she would be going about in nothing but a conceptualization. RK: gzugs mngon par ’du byed pa zhes bya ba ni gzugs la rnam par rtog pa’o| |’di la spyod pa ma lags so zhes bya ba ni ’di la rnam par rtog pa nyid kyi phyir ro. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii: 60. According to Hari (48), a meditative perspective (ākāra) is just the mode of perceiving a meditative reference point (ālambanam). A meditative reference point to be perceived (grāhya) is just shaped by that meditative perspective, since the state of knowing is dwelling in that scope. Hence, if a bodhisattva dwells on physical form from a meditative perspective of impermanence and so on, then he or she goes about life in a “cumulative construction” of physical forms, i.e., in the “conceptualization” that applies to physical forms. Thus, the bodhisattva here would not be going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, but, chiefly, in the initial part of the peak stage (of the path of preliminary practice). Hari: ālambana­grahaṇaprakāra evākāraḥ. saṃvinniṣṭhā ca viṣayasthitir ity ākārānurūpam evālambanaṃ grāhyaṃ. ato yadi rūpe ’nityādibhir ākārais tiṣṭhati tadā rūpābhisaṃskāre rūpābhogavikalpe carati. evaṃ ca na carati prajñāpāramitāyāṃ mukhyato mṛdu mūrdhasvabhāvāyām. Note: the Sanskrit word abhisaṃskāra used here‍—which can mean “cumulative construction” or “accumulation”‍—is central to the wordplay in the eighteenth chapter that makes reference to this very passage: “Blessed One, the Blessed One previously taught that a cumulative construction (abhisaṃskāra) is a conceptualization (vikalpa). So why is the Blessed One now saying that a bodhisattva great being would ‘earn more merit’?” (see 18.18 and n.951). He or she would not go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Likewise, if he or she were to dwell on feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness, he or she would go about life in the cumulative construction of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness, and would not go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? It is because, while going about life cumulatively constructing these, he or she would not apprehend the transcendent state of wisdom, would not engage in meditation, and would not accomplish the transcendent state of wisdom. By not accomplishing the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would not go beyond cyclic existence in the state of complete knowing by means of apprehending the inapprehensible.According to Ratnākara (F.20.b), the state of complete knowing here is referring to the knowing of all things (vastu; dngos po). RK: thams cad mkhyen pa nyid la zhes bya ba ni dngos po thams cad mkhyen pa’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 60. Note: The term “state of complete knowing” (sarvajñatā; thams cad mkhyen pa ) in this scripture is interpreted differently in different contexts by commentators as referring to the fruitional state of the transcendent state of wisdom at the level of either a disciple (who knows all things), a bodhisattva (who knows all paths), or a buddha (who knows all aspects of everything). The above comment means that this “state of complete knowing” is referring to a “state of complete knowing” in which a disciple and solitary realizer go beyond cyclic existence. Why is that? It is because, in the transcendent state of wisdom, physical forms are not apprehended. Likewise, in the transcendent state of wisdom, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are not apprehended.

“Furthermore, the nonapprehending of physical forms is not the same as physical forms.According to Ratnākara (F.21.a), the word “nonapprehending” refers to a subject not mentally perceiving and the word “not apprehended” refers to an object not being mentally perceived. RK: | yongs su mi gzung ba zhes bya ba ni gzung bar bya ba ma yin pa’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 61. According to Hari (50), in this sentence the idea is that what is affirmed and denied belong to different (i.e., relative and absolute) dimensions of perception. Hari: yaś ca rūpasyāparigraho na tad rūpam ityādi. vidhipratiṣedhayor bhinnaviṣayatvād iti bhāvaḥ. Note: The English translation here follows Ratnākara and Hari’s glosses of the Sanskrit “nonapprehending of form” (rupasyāparigraha), which diverges slightly from the Tibetan “unapprehended form” (gzugs yongs su ma gzung ba). Likewise, the nonapprehending of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is not the same as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. And this transcendent state of wisdom is not something apprehended in addition to those.

“Therefore, it is in this transcendent state of wisdom that a bodhisattva great being should go about life. Also, the bodhisattva great being’s meditative concentration‍—called the meditative concentration free of anything apprehended‍—is vast, highly esteemed, focused on what is beyond measure, and unshared by the disciples and solitary realizers.According to Ratnākara (F.21.a), the meditative concentration is “vast” in terms of its vast scope; “highly esteemed” by all buddhas who praised it; and “focused on what is beyond measure” indicates its certainty with respect to buddhahood. RK: |dmigs pa yangs pas na yangs pa’o| |sangs rgyas thams cad kyis bsngags pas mdun* du bgyis pa’o| |tshad med pa ni sangs rgyas nyid do| |de la nges pa ni tshad med par nges pa’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 61. According to Hari (49), it is described as being “focused on what is beyond measure” because it is devoted to (viz. focused on) the complete knowing of everything or because it is determined to benefit (viz. focused on benefiting) infinite beings through vast compassion and wisdom. Hari: sarvākārajñatāpratiniyamāt urukaruṇāprajñāyogena vāpramāṇa sattvārthakaraṇe niyatatvād apramāṇaniyataḥ. Note: The Sanskrit word samādhi is often defined as the state of single-pointed focus on an object, but refers to something different in the cognitive and meditative contexts. In the cognitive context (of the Abhidharma discourse on mind), samādhi refers to “concentration” or “mental focus,” which is a mental factor (caitta; sems ’byung) conjoined with every momentary consciousness of an object. In the meditative context, the term implies a state of single-pointedness with any number of meditative objects. That is to say, although a meditator might have deliberately entered this state of concentration, the concentration‍—once it reaches the point of mental tranquility (śamatha)‍—effortlessly sustains itself for a certain duration of time, during which one becomes so mentally immersed or absorbed in the object of concentration that one may not even be aware that one is concentrating upon an object. With an eye toward the resultant experience, some translate samādhi as “absorption.” Perhaps originally to distinguish these two different usages, Tibetan developed two different translations of the single Sanskrit term, namely the heuristic translation “stable immersion” or “absorption” (ting nge ’dzin)‍—in the sense of being mentally absorbed in the experience of a particular object‍—and the semi-etymological translation “the mind being set in equipoise” (mnyam par ’jog pa). But interestingly, of these two Tibetan terms, the latter seems to be used exclusively in the meditative context where one deliberately concentrates upon an object, while the former is used both in cognitive and meditative contexts where one may be unaware of concentrating. Furthermore, this state of complete knowing is not apprehended, since it cannot be grasped through conventional signs.

“If it were something to be grasped through conventional signs, then the wandering ascetic ŚreṇikaThis alludes to the anecdote of a mendicant named Śreṇika Vatsagotra (Pali: Vacchagotta), not initially a follower of the Buddha, who despite his narrow and limited scope acquired faith in the Buddha. The anecdote is mentioned as significant not only in this text but in all the long Prajñāpāramitā sūtras in equivalent passages, i.e., in chapter 11 of the Ten Thousand (Toh 11, 11.31–11.33); chapter 8 of the Eighteen Thousand (Toh 10, 8.35–8.38); chapter 5 of the Twenty-Five Thousand (Toh 9, 5.52–5.54); and chapter 5 of the Hundred Thousand (Toh 8, 5.423–5.441). None of the Prajñāpāramitā sūtras themselves give further detail, and Śreṇika Vatsagotra’s questioning of the Buddha does not seem to appear in full in any canonical text in Tibetan translation. It is, however, related in a number of Pali texts and Chinese āgamas; see particularly Majjhimanikāya, 71–73, and Saṃyuktāgama, SA 962–964 and SA2 196–198. Nāgārjuna’s Mahā­prajñā­pāramitā­śāstra gives further details; see Lamotte 2010 vol. 1, p. 58 n119. A useful summary is set out in Conze’s introduction to the long sūtra; see Conze 1975, pp. 12–13. would never have gained faith in this teaching of the Buddha.According to Hari (50), the basic problem with grasping at signs is expressed in this sentence. That is to say, the wandering ascetic Śreṇika developed revulsion toward his own tradition because he did not foresee any liberation, even after a long period of practice, coming through a practice involved in grasping at conventional signs. So if there were something in the Buddhist teachings on the knowing of someone who has complete knowing that was to be grasped as a conventional sign, then Śreṇika would never have developed faith or trust in this teaching of the Buddha, because it would just have seemed equal to the teachings in his own tradition. Hari: yadi nimittato grahītavyā bhavet tadā svaśāsaṇe nimittagrahena dīrghakālaṃ muktim apaśyan nirviṇṇaḥ san naiv eha śāsane śreṇikasaṃjñakaḥ parivrājakas tapasvī animittayogena śraddhām abhisampratyayaṃ pratilabheta. nimittagrahasya tulyatvād iti bhāvaḥ. Note: Ratnākara explains (F.22.a) the locative pronoun “in this” to refer to the words “teaching of the Buddha.” These have been added to the main text to clarify. RK: ’di la zhes bya ba ni sangs rgyas kyi bstan pa’o). For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 61. Indeed, as someone who proceeded based on faith, the wandering ascetic Śreṇika had firm conviction in the knowing of someone who has complete knowing and he entered into it through a knowing limited in scope.According to Ratnākara (F.22.a), into what was Śreṇika able to enter? Into the knowing of someone who has complete knowing at the intermediate stage of the peak (on the path of preliminary practice). He entered into that as someone who proceeded based on faith and trust. How did he enter? Since he entered through “a knowing limited in scope” based on a universal (i.e., of the actual nature), the knowing limited in scope means (he entered) at the intermediate stage of the peak. RK: |kun tu rgyu sde can ji lta bu la zhugs she na| de yang thams cad mkhyen pa’i ye shes la ste| rtse mo’i ’bring la’o| |de ltar mos par byas nas de la yang dad pas rjes su ’brang ba yin no| |gang gis zhugs pa yin zhe na? spyi las phyogs gcig gis ’byung ba yin pas | phyogs gcig gis ye shes te rtse mo ’bring la’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 63. According to Hari (50–51), Śreṇika “had firm conviction”‍—which he maintained mentally‍—in this signless “knowing of someone who has complete knowing” as something that would lead to buddhahood. He is called “someone who proceeded based on faith” (śraddhānusārī) because, even though his capacity for learning the teachings intellectually was relatively weak, he was able to follow the path because of his faith. He “entered” into the path where he did “through a knowing limited in scope,” which is to say, his knowing was connected only with the lack of personal identity (i.e., not with the lack of identity of all things). Hari: atrānimitte tathāgata­tvāvāhakatvena sarvajñajñāne ’tannimittam adhimucyādhimokṣa­manaskāraṃ kṛtvā mṛdvindriyatvena śraddhayā mārgānusaraṇāc chraddhānusārī pradeśikena pudgala­nairātmya­jñānenāvatīrṇaḥ. Note: Ratnākara appears to disagree with Hari, saying that Śreṇika saw the actual nature, not just a person’s lack of identity. Also, the phrase “knowing limited in scope” (prādeśika) would be translated, based on the Tibetan rendering, as “knowing a single aspect of the whole” (phyogs gcig pa’i ye shes, *ekadeśajñāna). After entering it, he did not apprehend physical forms.According to Ratnākara (F.22.a), after entering the path because he had done what he was taught, the reason why he personally did not grasp physical form and so on was because he did not pay attention to any conventional signs. RK: |de zhugs nas gang byas pa de bstan pa’i phyir gzugs la sogs pa yongs su mi ’dzin zhes gsungs te| de’i mtshan ma yid la mi byed pa’i phyir ro. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 63. RK: de zhugs nas gang byas pa de bstan pa’i phyir gzugs la sogs pa yongs su mi ’dzin zhes gsungs te| de’i mtshan ma yid la mi byed pa’i phyir ro. According to Hari (51), the phrase “after entering it” indicates that it was some time later that he eventually became able not to pay attention to any conventional signs. Likewise, he did not apprehend feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. In that state, he did not even identify that knowing as delight or ease.According to Ratnākara (F.22.a), the phrase “delight or ease” here refers to that which is said to be born from meditative concentration (samādhi). Also, the phrase “he did not even identify” and so on refers to his not paying attention to any conventional signs (i.e., belonging to the constituents, such as physical form and so on). When the scripture says that his knowing was neither something “outside physical form” nor “outside consciousness,” it is because, even though he was not paying attention to the conventional signs of physical form and so on, they continue to appear (at least, at this level of practice). For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 63. RK: |ting nge ’dzin las skyes pa’i dga’ ba dang bde ba dang ldan pa shes pa de yang dag par rjes su mi mthong ngo| |shes pa de gzugs kyi nang na yang dag par rjes su mi mthong zhes bya ba la sogs pas kyang de’i mtshan ma yid la mi byed pa de nyid du bstan to| |gzugs las gzhan du yang ma yin| rnam par shes pa las gzhan du yang ma yin| de’i mtshan ma yid la mi byed kyang de dag snang ba’i phyir ro. According to Hari (51), Bhadanta Vimuktisena explains that, when cultivating that meditative state, he does not identify that knowing‍—which is cultivating the freedom from signs‍—through any conventional signs connected with the “delight or ease” that has arisen from it. Here, the word “delight” refers to the mental factor of mental well-being, whereas the word “ease” refers to the physical sense of relaxed flexibility. Both of these arise because, as someone free of worldly desire, he has entered the path based on either the first or second of the form meditations (where “delight or ease” are said to automatically occur). Hari: nāpi tatrānimittabhāvanāyām utpannena prītisukhena tad bhāvakaṃ jñānaṃ nimittataḥ samanupaśyati. tatra prītiḥ saumanasyaṃ caitasikaṃ. sukhaṃ prasrabdhiḥ kāyikī. sa hi laukikavītarāgaḥ prathama­dvitīya­dhyānānyatara­saṃniśrayeṇāvatīrṇa iti bhadantavimuktisenaḥ. Nor did he identify that knowing as inside physical forms. Nor did he identify that knowing as outside physical forms. Nor did he identify that knowing as both inside and outside physical forms. Nor did he identify that knowing as other than physical forms. Likewise, he did not identify that knowing as inside feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Nor did he identify that knowing as outside feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Nor did he identify that knowing as both inside and outside feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Nor did he identify that knowing as something other than feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness.

“The wandering ascetic Śreṇika had firm conviction in this way of proceeding.According to Hari (51–52), this section basically shows that Śreṇika was not someone who proceeds based on the truth (dharma) by understanding reality through his sharp intellect, but rather was someone who proceeds based on faith with firm conviction in this “way of proceeding,” i.e., at the particular stage of practice corresponding to the level of proceeding with firm conviction. Hari: atretyādi. atrāsmin padaparyāye yogasthānaviśeṣe ’dhimukti­caryābhūmau sthitaḥ śreṇiko ’dhimukta iti veditavyo nādhigata iti. yataḥ śreṇiko ’tra sarvatrānimitte sarvajñajñāne tattvānadhigamena śraddhānusārīty ucyate. tīkṣṇendriyatvena tattvādhigame tu dharmānusārī bhavatīty abhiprāyaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.21.b–22.a), the “way” (paryāya<rab tu dbye ba; prakāra) of “proceeding” (pada) here belongs to the peak, or, to be precise, the intermediate phase of the peak of the path of preliminary practice. RK: |tshig gi rnam grangs ’di la zhes bya ba la tshig ni rtse mo’i’o| |de’i rnam grangs ni rab tu dbye ba ste| rtse mo ’bring gi zhes bya ba’i tha tshig go. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 63. Note: The translation “way of proceeding” interprets Ratnākara’s gloss of pada to mean a “step” or “place” rather than “words” or “text” (tshig) as the Tibetan translator translated it. Cf. MW pada, “step”; PTSD pada, “place, path.” Conze translates this same Sanskrit compound as a “scripture passage.” Everywhere along this way, he was someone who proceeded based on faith in the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. Hence, it is said, ‘He took the true nature of Dharma to be authoritative in this way and had firm conviction in it.’The scripture here seems to be playing on the Sanskrit word dharma. Ratnākara and Hari disagree on whether to interpret the word dharma to mean “things” (dharma) or “teachings” (dharma). The translation “true nature of Dharma” (dharmatā; chos nyid) is a compromise that aims to allow for both interpretations. According to Ratnākara (F.22.a), the sentence basically means that Śreṇika took “the essence of the Buddha’s teachings” (dharmatā) to be authoritative. RK: chos nyid ces bya ba la sogs pa gsungs te| ston pa’i bstan pa tshad mar byas nas de ltar mos pa zhes bya’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 63. According to Hari (52), it was by force of Śreṇika’s former penetration of the truth that he took the “true nature of things” (dharmatā; chos nyid) and the transcendent state of wisdom as authoritative (pramānīkṛtya), i.e., as noncontradictory. For this reason, when he “had firm conviction,” it was free from conventional signs. Hari: dharmatām ity ādi. pūrvāvedhabalāt prajñā­pāramitādharmatām avisaṃvādena pramāṇīkṛtyaivam animitta­tvenādhimukte. For this reason, he neither apprehended nor mentally perceived any thing that he could grasp or let go of.According to Hari (52), the meaning here is that, because of the foregoing, he neither “apprehended”‍—i.e., falsely supposed‍—nor “mentally perceived” anything as existing. Hari: tasmāt tena na kaścid dharmo ’dhyāropataḥ parigṛhītaḥ nāpi vidyamāna­tvenopalabdhaḥ. Cf. Sparham 2006, p. 238. He did not develop conceits even about nirvāṇa.According to Hari (52), it is said that, as a doorway to the true nature of things, the meditative perspective does not create a (new) identity through negating inherent nature. In this regard, the story of Śreṇika is aimed at making known the way that someone who had emerged from a tīrthika (i.e., not Buddhist) concentrative state was able‍—only by force of his firm conviction‍—to directly witness the meaning of this teaching. Hari: etad uktaṃ: svabhāva­pratiṣedhenāsvīkāro rūpādīnāṃ dharmatāmukhenākāra iti. tatra śreṇikodāharaṇaṃ tasya tīrthika­sambandhena samādher vyutthita­syādhimokṣa­balenaiva tadartha­pratyakṣasākṣitva­jñāpanārthaṃ.

“O Blessed One, this too should be known as the transcendent state of wisdom of a bodhisattva great being, insofar as he or she does not apprehend physical forms, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Nor does a bodhisattva great being pass into nirvāṇa in the interval without having realized the ten powers of a tathāgata,The Dharmasaṃgraha (76) lists the ten powers as follows: (1) the power of knowing what occurs and what cannot occur; (2) the power of knowing the maturation of actions; (3) the power of knowing the various elements; (4) the power of knowing the various inclinations; (5) the power of knowing the superior and inferior capacities of beings; (6) the power of knowing the path to anywhere; (7) the power of knowing the reflections, liberations, meditative concentrations, meditative states, defilements, purifications, and arisings; (8) knowing the memories of former states of existence; (9) knowing death and rebirth; and (10) knowing the extinction of worldly influences. See also The Perfection of Wisdom in Twenty-Five Thousand Lines (Toh 9), 9.37; and Distinctly Ascertaining the Meanings (Toh 317), 1.84–90. There are slight variations among lists. the four grounds for self-confidence of a tathāgata,The Dharmasaṃgraha (77) lists the four grounds for self-confidence as follows: (1) the self-confidence of being perfectly awake with respect to all things, (2) the self-confidence that all worldly influences have been permanently destroyed, (3) the self-confidence of having accurately ascertained and explained those factors that impede awakening, and (4) the self-confidence concerning the way that the path leads beyond cyclic existence to all the accomplishments. See also The Perfection of Wisdom in Twenty-Five Thousand Lines (Toh 9), 9.38–41; and Distinctly Ascertaining the Meanings (Toh 317), 1.91–94. and the eighteen distinctive qualities of a buddha.The Dharmasaṃgraha (76) lists the eighteen distinctive qualities as follows: (1) never any shakiness in understanding, (2) no useless speech, (3) never robbed of memory, (4) never an unfocused mindset, (5) never conceiving of differences (among beings), (6) never an unconsidered indifference, (7) never losing interest in anyone or anything, (8) never giving up on heroic deeds, (9) never abandoning mindfulness, (10) never lacking in concentration, (11) never lacking in wisdom, (12) never lacking in liberation, (13–15) all his physical, verbal, or mental activities are accompanied and preceded by awareness, and (16–18) his seeing and being aware of the past, present, and future is unattached and unhindered. See also The Perfection of Wisdom in Twenty-Five Thousand Lines (Toh 9), 62.74; and Distinctly Ascertaining the Meanings (Toh 317), 1.96. There are slight variations among lists. Therefore, O Blessed One, this should also be known as the transcendent state of wisdom of a bodhisattva great being.

“Furthermore, a bodhisattva great being who goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivates it should reflect and meditate upon it in the following way: ‘What is that transcendent state of wisdom? To whom does the transcendent state of wisdom belong? Is that thing that is neither found nor mentally perceived the transcendent state of wisdom?’ If, reflecting and meditating upon it in this way, a bodhisattva great being’s mindset does not become deflated, depressed, dejected, or sunk in despondency, and if his or her mental attitude does not become discouraged, shattered, frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror, then such a bodhisattva great being should be known as a bodhisattva who never lacks the transcendent state of wisdom.”

Then the venerable Śāriputra asked the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, given that physical forms themselves lack the inherent nature of physical forms; given that feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness themselves lack the inherent nature of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness; given that the transcendent state of wisdom itself lacks the inherent nature of a transcendent state of wisdom; and given that the state of complete knowing itself lacks the inherent nature of a state of complete knowing, why should a bodhisattva great being be known as never lacking the transcendent state of wisdom?”

The venerable Subhūti replied to the venerable Śāriputra, “You are right, Venerable Śāriputra, you are right. Physical forms themselves lack the inherent nature of physical forms. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness themselves lack the inherent nature of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. The transcendent state of wisdom itself lacks the inherent nature of a transcendent state of wisdom. The state of complete knowing itself lacks the inherent nature of a state of complete knowing. Even the transcendent state of wisdom lacks the defining characteristic of a transcendent state of wisdom. Even a defining characteristic lacks the inherent nature of a defining characteristic. Even what is to be characterized [by a defining characteristic] lacks the inherent nature of being characterized. Even an inherent nature lacks the defining characteristic of an inherent nature.”
The venerable Śāriputra then asked the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, will a bodhisattva great being who trains in this go beyond cyclic existence to the state of complete knowing?”

The venerable Subhūti replied to the venerable Śāriputra, “Yes, that bodhisattva great being will go. Venerable Śāriputra, yes, that bodhisattva great being will go. A bodhisattva great being who trains in this will go beyond cyclic existence to the state of complete knowing. Why is that? Because, Venerable Śāriputra, all things are never even born and are not gone beyond.According to Hari (56), the overall idea here is that, as a rule, a bodhisattva approaches each particular realization one by one through increasingly subtle, particular meditative reference points and so on. In the sentence, the Sanskrit word hi has a restrictive sense, meaning things are “never even” born. Furthermore, since all things are in constant momentary flux (i.e., arising and ceasing are simultaneous), they cannot be “gone beyond” in the manner of a born thing ceasing‍—in other words, nothing (i.e., no one) reaches nirvāṇa. The precise meaning at this stage of practice is this: the meditative reference point, i.e., the physical form and so on, is distinguished as nonarising and nonceasing because it lacks any substantive inherent nature. Cf. Sparham 2006, p. 238. Hari: ajātā hītyādi. uttarottarālambana­viśeṣādibhir adhigamaviśeṣe niyamena yāsyatīti bhāvaḥ. ādyo hiśabdo ’vadhāraṇe. ajātā evety arthaḥ. kutaḥ. yasmāt kṣaṇikānityatayā jātidharmanirodhenāniryātā na nirvāṇaṃ gatāḥ sarvadharmā iti. prakṛtya­svabhāva­tvenānutpādānirodha­viśiṣṭaṃ rūpādy ālambanam iti yāvat. Note: Ratnākara does not comment on this particular sentence. The translation “not gone beyond” (aniryātā) follows Degé (nges par ’byung ba), Bendall Add 866, and Ratnākara’s and Hari’s lemmas, rather than Mitra’s Sanskrit edition, which has “not born after” (anirjātā) for “not gone beyond” (aniryātā). The scribal confusion of these two terms is rather common, because they look similar in many Sanskrit scripts. Hence, Venerable Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being, in going about life in such a manner, approaches the state of complete knowing.According to Hari (56), given the Sanskrit principle that all verbs of motion have the meaning of understanding, the phrase “going about life in such a manner” means cultivating as described before, and the word “approaches” means he or she is on the verge of attaining realization shortly thereafter. Hari: evam anantaroktanyāyena carato bhāvayataḥ, sarve gatyarthā bodhārthā iti kṛtvā, āsannībhavati śīghrādhigamaprāpter nikaṭavartinī syāt. In whatever manner he or she approaches the state of complete knowing, in that same manner he or she‍—for the sake of causing beings to mature‍—refines his or her body and mind, refines his or her particular appearance, refines his or her buddhafield, and meets with the buddhas.According to Hari (56–57), the phrase “for the sake of causing beings to mature” relies on the fact that the maturation of beings comes from one’s having directed positive qualities toward those beings. As for the other parts of this sentence, first, he or she “refines his or her body” and so on refers to the refinement of all aspects of the body and so on by virtue of understanding the inherent nature of everything. Hence, the body comes to be refined by way of the cause‍—i.e., repeatedly practicing with the meditative perspective of refining it by causal means. In the bodily refinement, the three types of physical actions are completely refined. In the mental refinement, the three types of mental actions are completely refined. Next, the complete refinement that manifests in the thirty-two principal characteristics of a great person (mahā­puruṣa­lakṣaṇāni) arises from accomplishing the qualities of being immoveable, well trained, and fulfilled. Finally, the refinement of the buddhafield refers to the world and its beings being refined. Hari: sattvaparipācanāyeti kuśala­dharmaniyojanāt sattvaparipākāya. kāyetyādi sarva­dharma­svarūpāvabodhena kāyādīnāṃ sarvākāraviśuddhir iti viśuddhihetutvād viśuddhyākāreṇa hetu­mukhenābhyasanāt kāyapariśuddhir bhavati. pariśuddhaṃ trividhaṃ kāya karma. cittapariśuddhiḥ pariśuddhaṃ trividhaṃ manaskarma. sthānasthottapta­pūrṇatvena niṣpatter dvātriṃśanmahā­puruṣa­lakṣaṇa­pariśuddhiḥ. sattva bhājanayoḥ pariśuddhatā buddha­kṣetra­pariśuddhiḥ. Thus, a bodhisattva great being who goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom is approaching the state of complete knowing.”

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 6 of the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 9 of the Eighteen Thousand.Moreover, the venerable Subhūti said the following about a bodhisattva great being: “If a bodhisattva great being goes about life in physical forms, then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the conventional sign of physical forms, then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the notion ‘physical forms are a conventional sign,’ then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the arising of physical forms, then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign.The use of the word “arising” (utpāda) in this scripture stems from a realist description of the world as ultimately consisting of irreducible atoms and irreducible moments of consciousness. In this realist description, a thing that is real first arises, i.e., comes into existence; then dwells, i.e., continues to exist; and finally ceases, i.e., goes out of existence. Mahāyāna Buddhists generally argue that this notion of arising, dwelling, and ceasing is arrived at through conceptualization, and does not describe the ultimate reality. Hence, to “go about life in the arising” means, in essence, to go about life in a conceptualization, not reality. If he or she goes about life in the cessation of physical forms, then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the dissolution of physical forms, then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the notion ‘physical forms are empty,’ then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the notion ‘I am going about my life,’ then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the notion ‘I am a bodhisattva,’ then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. This is because he or she is going about life in nothing but his or her mental perception of ‘I am a bodhisattva.’In Ratnākara’s comments (F.24.b), he glosses “mental perception” as a fanciful knowing (mithyājñāna). RK: dmigs pa zhes bya ba ni log pa’i shes pa’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p.75. According to Hari (58), it is said that as long as the bodhisattva goes about life based on grasping conventional signs through habitual fixation, he or she is afflicted by signs, and hence falls into the affliction of cyclic existence based on his or her own mental perception of things. Hari: evaṃ manyate: yāvad abhiniveśayogena nimittagrahāc carati tāvan nimittakleśā bodhisattvā iti vacanād upalambhamūle saṃsārakleśe patatīti.

“Likewise, if he or she goes about life in feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness, then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the conventional sign of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness, then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the arising of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness, then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the cessation of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness, then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the dissolution of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness, then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the notion ‘feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are empty,’ then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the notion ‘I am going about my life,’ then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. If he or she goes about life in the notion ‘I am a bodhisattva,’ then he or she is going about life in a conventional sign. This is because he or she is going about life in nothing but his or her mental perception of ‘I am a bodhisattva.’ Furthermore, if it occurs to him or her, ‘Those who go about life in this way are going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and are cultivating it,’ then he or she is going about life in nothing but a conventional sign. Such a bodhisattva should be known as someone who is not skilled in means.”According to Hari (58), what “not skilled in means” refers to here should be recognized from the introduction of its opposite (i.e., what “skilled in means” refers to). Hari: anupāyakuśalo viparyāsapravṛttatvād veditavyaḥ. The Dharmasaṃgraha (111) mentions three types of “means” (upāya) in which a bodhisattva is skilled: (1) helping all beings understand, (2) doing beneficial things for all beings, and (3) awakening happiness with ease. Dharmasaṃgraha: upāyas trividhaḥ, tadyathā – sarva­sattvāvabodhakaḥ, sattvārthābhāvakaḥ, kṣiprasukhābhisaṃbodhiś ceti. The Bodhisattvabhūmi (264.7–9) lists six “means” of benefiting beings, through (1) going along with (positive deeds), (2) standing as a hindrance (to negative deeds), (3) disposing oneself differently, (4) creating support, (5) counteracting what is done, and (6) being pure. Bodhisattvabhūmi: ānulomikaḥ, vibandhasthāyī, visabhāgāśayaḥ, avaṣṭambhajaḥ, kṛtaprakṛtikaḥ, viśuddhaś ca ṣaṣṭha upāyaḥ. For a translation of the section, see Engle 2016, pp. 439–47.
Then the venerable Śāriputra asked the venerable Subhūti, “If a bodhisattva great being were to go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, then how, Venerable Subhūti, would he or she do so?”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the venerable Śāriputra, “O Venerable Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being does not go about life in physical forms, does not go about life in the conventional sign of physical forms, does not go about life in the notion ‘physical forms are a sign,’ does not go about life in the arising of physical forms, does not go about life in the cessation of physical forms, does not go about life in the dissolution of physical forms, does not go about life in the notion ‘physical forms are empty,’ does not go about life in the notion ‘I am going about my life,’ and does not go about life in the notion ‘I am a bodhisattva.’

“Likewise, he or she does not go about life in feelings, conceptions, or conditioning factors. He or she does not go about life in consciousness, does not go about life in the conventional sign of consciousness, does not go about life in the notion ‘consciousness is a conventional sign,’ does not go about life in the arising of consciousness, does not go about life in the cessation of consciousness, does not go about life in the dissolution of consciousness, does not go about life in the notion ‘consciousness is empty,’ does not go about life in the notion ‘I am going about my life,’ and does not go about life in the notion ‘I am a bodhisattva.’ If he or she does not think, ‘Those who go about life in this way are going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and are cultivating the transcendent state of wisdom,’ then a bodhisattva great being who is going about life in this way is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. For he or she never goes close to the thoughts, ‘I am going about my life,’ ‘I am not going about my life,’ ‘I am going about my life and not going about my life,’ or ‘I am neither going about my life nor not going about my life.’There is a play on the words “not skilled in means” (anupāyakuśala) and the words “goes close to” (nopaiti), both of which are forms of Sanskrit verb upa+√ī. Etymologically speaking, the word “means” (in the phrase “not skilled in means”) refers to something that enables one to “go close” to the goal. So, just above, when he spoke about a bodhisattva “not being skilled in means” (anupāya), it meant someone without the skill to “go close to” (upāya) the goal. But here he speaks about a bodhisattva who is skilled in means, saying, effectively, that the bodhisattva does “not go close to” the thoughts, etc., that will be described. In essence, the wordplay is differentiating skill in means as “going close to” the right thing from the lack of skill in means, which is “going close to” the wrong things. Nor does he or she arrive at the thoughts, ‘I will be going about my life,’ ‘I will not be going about my life,’ ‘I will both be going about my life and not be going about my life,’ or ‘I will neither be going about my life nor not be going about my life.’ Why does he or she not come to think that? It is because no things are either arrived at or taken hold of. This meditative concentration of a bodhisattva great being‍—called the meditative concentration free of anything apprehended‍—is vast, highly esteemed, focused on what is beyond measure, and unshared by the disciples and solitary realizers. By abiding in this very meditative concentration, a bodhisattva great being quickly realizes unsurpassable, completely perfect awakening.”

Through the miraculous power of the Buddha, the elder, the venerable Subhūti, continued: “O Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being whom the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas have foreordained for unsurpassable, perfect awakening abides in precisely this meditative concentration.According to Hari (61), it is in the true nature of things that the previous buddhas formally “foreordain” a practitioner (by announcing his or her future awakening) when he or she has perfected meditative concentration. Hari: vyākṛto ’yam iti dharmataiṣā samyak­prati­panna­samādher yogino buddhair vyākaraṇaṃ kriyata. Note: This foreordaining by the buddhas occurs while the bodhisattva is still on the path but is certain to reach the goal. It often includes their saying when, where, and with what name that particular bodhisattva will attain buddhahood. Yet, he or she does not identify that meditative concentration and does not develop conceits about it, thinking, ‘I am concentrated,’ ‘I will attain the meditative concentration,’ ‘I am attaining the meditative concentration,’ or ‘I have attained the meditative concentration.’ Never are such thoughts as these experienced by that bodhisattva great being in any form or any manner.”The English translation “[not] experienced” (na saṃvidyate; ma mchis) is based on context and Sanskrit etymology‍—rather than on the Tibetan “do not exist”‍—because the text is speaking explicitly about meditative concentration, and only implicitly disproving existence in a philosophical sense.

At this, the venerable Śāriputra asked the venerable Subhūti, “O Venerable Subhūti, in what meditative concentration is a bodhisattva great being‍—whom the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas have foreordained for unsurpassable, perfect awakening‍—abiding? Is that meditative concentration something that can be demonstrated?”

“Definitely not, Venerable Śāriputra,” replied Subhūti. “Why is that? It is because even such a noble son or daughter does not perceive that meditative concentration, nor does he or she conceive of it.”
The venerable Śāriputra asked, “Venerable Subhūti, are you saying that neither do they perceive it nor do they conceive of it?”

The venerable Subhūti replied, “O Venerable Śāriputra, I am saying that neither do they perceive it nor do they conceive of it. Why not? Because, insofar as that meditative concentration is not found to exist, they neither perceive it nor do they conceive of it.”The translation here follows the extant Sanskrit, rather than the Tibetan, which repeats this final sentence.

Then the Blessed One commended the venerable Subhūti: “Excellent, Venerable Subhūti, excellent. It is exactly so, Subhūti, exactly so. Just as it is illuminating to you through the Tathāgata’s miraculous power and just as you are expounding it through the Tathāgata’s blessings, just so a bodhisattva great being should train in this meditative concentration. Why is that? It is because a bodhisattva great being who trains in this way trains in the transcendent state of wisdom.”

The venerable Śāriputra then asked the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, is it that a bodhisattva great being who trains in this way trains in the transcendent state of wisdom?”According to Hari (64), the idea behind this restatement is to ask, “What?” Hari: kim iti bhāvaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.24.a), this statement is expressed for Śāriputra to ask about the topic, in order to instigate the Blessed One to teach based on that very assertion he is asserting. RK: |de skad ces bka’ stsal pa dang zhes bya ba ni| gang bcom ldan ’das kyis bka’ stsal pa de nyid bcom ldan ’das kyi bka’ yin pas bstan par bya ba’i phyir skabs kyi shA ri’i bus gsol bar bya ba’i don du smras so. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 74.
At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Śāriputra, “Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being who trains in this way is training in the transcendent state of wisdom.”
At this, the venerable Śāriputra asked the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, in what thing does a bodhisattva great being train?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Śāriputra, “O Śāriputra, while training in this way a bodhisattva does not train in any things whatsoever.According to Ratnākara (F.24.a), the basic question here is this: What “thing” (dharma)‍—which can refer either to a generic term, a quality, a chief principle, a being, or the constituents of experience, and so on‍—should a bodhisattva mentally perceive and train in? The Blessed One responds that they should train in no thing because they should train in all things simply lacking identity.
