RK: chos gang la slob pa lags zhes bya ba la| spyi sgra dang| yon tan dang| gtso bo dang| skyes bu la sogs pa la zhes bya ba’am| ’on te phung po dang khams dang| skye mched la sogs pa’i chos la dmigs nas bslab par bya| zhes dgongs so| bcom ldan ’das kyis gang la yang mi slob bo zhes bka’ stsal te| chos thams cad bdag med pa nyid la slob pa’i phyir| zhes dgongs so. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 74. According to Hari (64–65), the bodhisattva “does not train in any thing” ultimately, because it would be a conceptualization founded upon a generic thing (vastumātra) and hence would be tied to erroneous understanding. Hari: evaṃśikṣamāṇaḥ śāriputra bodhisattvo mahāsattvo na kasmiṃścid dharme śikṣate| tatra vastumātrādhiṣṭhāna­vikalpena viparyāsānuṣaṅgāt paramārthato na kvacid ity āha bhagavān: na kasminścid dharme śikṣata iti. Why is that? It is because, Śāriputra, those things are not experienced by him or her in the same manner that they are fixated upon by immature, uninformed, ordinary persons.”This passage involves a play on words stemming from different meanings of the Sanskrit root verb √vid (i.e., to find, to encounter, to experience, to know, to be aware, to exist, etc.) and sam√vid (to know, to recognize, to exist, etc.). A few added words aim to allow the spirit and meaning to come through. But it should also be kept in mind that the translation “not experienced” (saṃvidyante) can be taken as a synonym of “do not exist.” According to Ratnākara (F.24.a), the question “why” asks how can he or she train but not in any things. In the answer that follows, the word “things” refers to substances, qualities, and functions. The correlative phrase “those things are not experienced” refers to the essence of things. In the relative phrase “in the same manner that they are fixated upon,” the words “in the same manner that” mean with whatever nature they are “fixated upon” as truly possessing. The term “ordinary persons” refers to the fact that they are not noble persons, which is also why they are called “immature.” Their lack of significant learning is why they are called “uninformed.” RK: |bcom ldan ’das kyis gang la yang mi slob bo zhes bka’ stsal te| chos thams cad bdag med pa nyid la slob pa’i phyir| zhes dgongs so| |de ci’i phyir zhe na | zhes bya ba ni de chos thams cad la mi slob pa ga las she na| lan ni shA ri’i bu zhes bya ba la sogs pa gsungs so| |chos de ni zhes bya ba ni rdzas dang| yon tan dang las la sogs pa’o| |de bzhin du zhes bya ba ni de’i ngo bo’o| ji ltar zhes bya ba ni ji lta bu’i ngo bos mngon par chags shing bden pa nyid du’o| |so so’i skye bo ni ’phags pa ma yin pa ste de yang byis pa’o| |gang gi phyir thos pa dang mi ldan pa ni thos pa mang po med pa’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 74. According to Hari (65), after this first statement comes the answer to the question “why,” i.e., how could he or she be training without training in any thing? In the answer, the word “immature” indicates their association with the things related to emotional defilement. The term “ordinary persons” refers to their nonassociation with things related to the purification of defilement. The word “uninformed” means that they have no great learning or firm conviction in the different types of teachings. Here is the thinking: things are not experienced/do not exist “in the same manner that they are fixated upon” by those persons on account of their ignorance and conceptualization, which fancifully mentally perceives (nonexistent) things as existent. Hence, those who are training in something relative are not training in reality. Hari: tat kasya hetoḥ | na hi te śāriputra dharmās tathā saṃvidyante yathā bālapṛthagjanā aśrutavanto ’bhiniviṣṭāḥ || śikṣamāṇo ’pi kathaṃ na śikṣata iti tat kasya hetor ity āsaṅkyāha : na hītyādi. sāṃkleśikadharmayogād bālāḥ. vaiyavadānika­dharma­virahāt pṛthagjanāḥ. ta eva caivaṃvidha­dharmādhimukti­bāhu­śrutyavigamād aśrutavantaḥ. evaṃ manyate : yathāvidyāvikalpabalād vidya­mānatvenopalambhād abhiniviṣṭāḥ tathā na saṃvidyante. ataḥ saṃvṛtyā śikṣamāṇās tattvato na śikṣanta iti.

“How then are those things experienced?”According to Hari (65), this question is asked because “are not experienced/do not exist” in that manner might imply that they are experienced/exist in another manner, and hence, a fault might arise from grasping another existent thing. Hari: yadi tathā na saṃvidyante ’rthāt tarhy anyathā vidyante. tathā ca bhāvagrahād doṣa ity abhiprāyeṇāha: kathaṃ tarhītyādi. asked the venerable Śāriputra.

The Blessed One replied, “O Śāriputra, insofar as those things are not experienced as existing, they are experienced. And, since those things are not known to exist as such by that bodhisattva great being, they are called ignorance.There is a play here on the Sanskrit word avidyā (ma rig pa), which, in order to capture the implications, might be translated as “ignorance,” “not knowing,” “something not to be known,” “something not to be found,” or “something nonexistent.” The basic idea is that “things” exist only in the minds of ignorant beings. It should be kept in mind that the same clause connective phrase “insofar as” (yathā; ci ltar)‍—which appeared in previous sentences in a slightly different sense‍—is understood here by Ratnākara and Hari, because of their philosophical differences, as having different meanings. According to Hari (65–66), after refuting the possible misunderstanding introduced in the previous sentence, the next sentence explains that even though things are described here as being nonexistent, they do not truly “exist” in that manner (i.e., as something truly “nonexistent”) either. So he says “insofar as” (yāthā)‍—here meaning “as a rule”‍—things are not experienced/do not exist, so they are experienced/do exist relatively, because they are only acceptable as such when unexamined. To make it clear: the fact that they are being experienced/existing is nothing but the fact that they are not being experienced/not existing. The overall meaning of the sentence is this: Things that are “not known as such” are called “ignorance”‍—i.e., not knowing or knowing incorrectly at the conventional level‍—based on a creative heuristic etymology, because those things “not known as such” arise because of it (i.e., ignorance). Hence, these words express the process of conceptualization based on ignorance. Hari: pravṛttaviparyāsanirākaraṇād yathā bhiniviṣṭāḥ tathā na saṃvidyanta iti yadi nāmaivam uktaṃ tathāpi tattvato na santīty āha: yathe ty ādi. yathā nyāyato na saṃvidyante tathā vicāraikaramya­tvena saṃvṛtyā vidyante. avidyamānataiva vidyamānateti yāvat. evam avidyamānās tenocyante avidye ti. evam ityādy upasaṃhāraḥ. evam avidyamānā yataḥ tena kāraṇena nairuktavidhānāt saṃvṛtyā viparītajñānam ajñānam avidyety ucyate. ity anenāvidyāvikalpo ’bhihitaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.24.a–b), “they are not experienced” refers to something existent, because there is contrary evidence that refutes the existence of things. “They are experienced/do not exist” refers to appearances, because they are nonexistent and false by nature. The phrase “insofar as” (yāthā), in this case, has the force of a reason. And the phrase “not being known (as such)” refers to something that is deceptive. The word “ignorance” (viz. “not knowing”) refers to a particular type of ignorance. RK: |ji ltar med pa zhes bya ba ni yod pa yin te| gnod par byed pa yod pa’i phyir ro| |de bzhin du yang dag par yod de zhes bya ba ni med pa’i phyir dang| brdzun pa’i ngo bos snang ba zhes bya ba’i don to| |de ltar zhes bya ba ni bshad pa’i rigs pas so| |med pa zhes bya ba ni brdzun pa’o| |ma rig pa zhes bya ba ni ma rig pa’i khyad par yin pa’i phyir ma rig pa zhes bya ba’i don to. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 74. Those things are habitually fixated upon by immature, uninformed, ordinary persons. But all things‍—insofar as they are not actually experienced‍—are just imagined by them.Ratnākara and Hari interpret these sentences as concerning the eighteen ways that the five constituents of experience are conceptualized to exist as either designated (prajñapti; btags) or substantive (vastu; rdzas) “things” that are either apprehending or to be apprehended. These are to be abandoned on the path of preliminary practice. Both commentators agree that these “things” do not actually exist as such. However, the commentators‍—who reconcile the Yogācāra and Madhyamaka systems differently‍—naturally differ on the ontological upshot of saying that things do not exist. In chapter 26, the related explanation begins of the thirty-six conceptualizations. Those are to be eliminated on the path of cultivation. After imagining them, those immature, ordinary beings become entangled in the two extremes, and neither perceive nor see those things.According to Hari (65), because they are “habitually fixated on the two extremes,” i.e., eternalistic or nihilistic notions, they do not “perceive” (jānanti) with the worldy knowing through hearing and so on that things, such as names and physical forms, are impermanent, at the relative level, because their conceptualization and habitual fixation on them lacks the causal efficacy of a permanent thing. Hari: śāśvatocchedayor antayor abhiniviṣṭās tān nāmarūpādīn dharmāṃs tadabhiniveśavikalpān nitya­syārtha­kriyāvirahāt saṃvṛtyānityā eveti laukikena śrutādimayena jñānena na jānanti. Hence, they imagine all the things not being experienced, and after imagining those, they become habitually fixated on the two extremes. After habitually fixating on those extremes, they resort to their mental perception as a foundation, and imagine things as past, things as future, and things as present.As mentioned previously, a “mental perception” (upalambha; dmigs pa) refers to a fanciful perception. RK: dmigs pa zhes bya ba ni log pa’i shes pa’o. Note: The same applies to this term throughout the scripture, not just here. After imagining those, they become fixated on names and physical forms.According to Ratnākara (F.24.b), “names and physical forms” refers to the constituents (skandha): “names” refers to the four nonphysical constituents and “physical forms” refers to the physical constituent. RK: ming dang gzugs la mngon par zhen pas zhes bya ba ni ming ni gzugs can ma yin pa’i phung po, gzugs ni gzugs kyi phung po yin te| de gnyis ka ni ming dang gzugs so. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 75. These things are also imagined by them. While they are imagining all these things not being experienced, they neither perceive nor do they see the path in accordance with reality. Since they are not perceiving or seeing the path in accordance with reality, they neither go beyond the three realms nor awaken to the pinnacle of reality.According to Ratnākara (F.25.b), the term “reality” (bhūta) here refers to the true nature (tattva) and the word “pinnacle” here refers to the supreme or highest point. So, in other words, this term is referring to emptiness. RK: |yang dag pa ni de kho na nyid do| |mtha’ ni mchog dang rtse mo ste| |yang dag pa’i mtha’ stong pa nyid ces bya ba’i don to. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 77. For this reason, those beings are considered immature. They do not place their trust in the true Dharma.According to Hari (68), the word “true” here is meant in terms of the unerroneous meaning and “Dharma” here has the characteristic of an antidote. Hari: aviparītārthena satyaṃ. dharmaṃ prati­pakṣalakṣaṇaṃ. On the other hand, Śāriputra, bodhisattva great beings do not fixate upon anything at all as the Dharma.”According to Hari (68), we should mentally supply a few words to understand the sentence as follows: a bodhisattva does not fixate on any thing as the Dharma that purifies by means of conceptualizing something to be grasped. Hari: kaṃcid dharmaṃ vaiyavadānikaṃ, grāhyavikalpeneti śeṣaḥ. Note: The translation “anything at all as the Dharma” is attempting to capture a slight play on words here.
At this, the venerable Śāriputra asked the Blessed One, “But, O Blessed One, does a bodhisattva great being who trains in this way train in the state of complete knowing?”

The Blessed One said, “O Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being who trains in this way does not train even in the state of complete knowing. Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being who trains in this way trains in all things. Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being who trains in the state of complete knowing in this way is approaching that state and will go beyond cyclic existence in that [knowing].”

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 7 of the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 10 of the Eighteen Thousand.Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, someone might ask me, ‘Is it the case that [a bodhisattva great being who is like an] illusory person would train in the state of complete knowing, approach that state, and go beyond cyclic existence in it?’ O Blessed One, if one were questioned in this way, how should one explicate the topic?”
In response, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “To explain, Subhūti, I will ask you some questions about this topic in return. Answer them however you please.”
The venerable Subhūti assented to the Blessed One by saying, “Very well, Blessed One.”

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, [regarding a magical, illusion-like bodhisattva great being,] what do you think? Are his or her physical forms one thing and the magical illusion a different thing? Are his or her feelings one thing and the magical illusion a different thing? Are his or her conceptions one thing and the magical illusion a different thing? Are his or her conditioning factors one thing and the magical illusion a different thing? Subhūti, is his or her consciousness one thing and the magical illusion a different thing?”

“Definitely not, Blessed One,” replied Subhūti. “For, O Blessed One, it is not that physical forms are one thing, and the magical illusion a different thing. Physical forms themselves, Blessed One, are the magical illusion, and the magical illusion itself is the physical forms. Likewise, O Blessed One, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are not one thing, and the magical illusion a different thing. O Blessed One, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness themselves are magical illusion, and the magical illusion is the feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.”

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Is this so-called ‘bodhisattva’ here a conception, a name, a designation, and a conventional expression for the constituents underlying the clinging to existence?”

In response, Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, yes. O Sugata, yes it is. O Blessed One, the reason is that a bodhisattva great being who trains in the transcendent state of wisdom is like an illusory person being trained in unsurpassable, perfect awakening. Why is that? It is because, O Blessed One, it should be kept in mind that a bodhisattva great being is him- or herself an illusory person, that is to say, the five constituents underlying the clinging to existence. Why is that? It is because, O Blessed One, that is how the Blessed One has taught: that physical forms are like a magical illusion, and what is so for physical forms is also so for the six sense organs and all five constituents. Likewise, it is because, O Blessed One, that is how the Blessed One has taught: that feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors are like a magical illusion, and likewise the Blessed One has taught that consciousness is like a magical illusion, and what is so for feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is also so for the six sense organs and all five constituents. O Blessed One, if those bodhisattva great beings have just begun to set out on the path and have heard this explication, would they not be frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, if those bodhisattva great beings who have just begun to set out on the path fall into the hands of unvirtuous friends, they will be frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror.According to Ratnākara (F.28.a), here not being in the hands of an unvirtuous friend‍—i.e., one who is ignoble, unlearned, and unskillful‍—is an implicit teaching, namely rejecting the unlearned and abandoning the unvirtuous friends as two things that do not support the path. RK: ji lta bu’i lag tu song na zhes na| sems dpa’ chen po sdig pa’i grogs po’i lag tu song na’o| |’dir ’am zhes bya ba’i sgra rtogs par bya ste| sdig pa’i grogs po’i lag tu song bar gyur pa’am zhes bya’o| |des pa ma yin pa dang| mi mkhas pa dang| thabs mkhas pa ma yin te| sems dpa’ chen po gang dag sems dpa’ chen po’i lag tu ma yin pa de la de skad ces bya ste don gyis de ltar bstan pa yin no| |thabs mi mkhas pa yongs su gtong ba dang| sdig pa’i grogs po yongs su spong ba rnams mi yongs su ’dzin pa rnam pa gnyis po dag go. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 84. But, Subhūti, if the bodhisattva great beings who have just begun to set out on the path fall into the hands of virtuous friends, they will not be frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror.”According to Ratnākara (F.28.a–b), this sentence is describing a being who has wisdom and skillfulness‍—i.e., one who teaches this way, is learned, and is virtuous friend‍—as the two things that support the path. A skillful friend is certain about and teaches all things to be essenceless, practices virtuous deeds with the knowing of someone who has complete knowing free of reference points, and dedicates these deeds to complete, perfect awakening. An unskillful friend is the opposite. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 84. RK: de bas na rab ’byor gal te zhes bya ba la sogs pa gsungs te| sems dpa’ mdzangs pa thabs mkhas pa yin te| de gang dag la chen po yod pa de dag la de skad ces bya ste| dge ba’i bshes gnyen gyi lag tu song ba’o| |’ dir yang ’am zhes bya ba ni sgra rtogs par ’gyur ro| |’di skad ston pa yin te| thabs mkhas pa dang| dge ba’i bshes gnyen ni yongs su ’dzin pa gzhan gnyis yin no| |de la gang chos thams cad ngo bo nyid med par nges shing bstan pa dang| rnam pa thams cad mkhyen pa nyid dang ldan pa’i mi dmigs pa’i yi shes kyis dge ba’i chos rnams spyod cing de dag yang dag par rdzogs pa’i byang chub tu yang dag par yongs su bsngo ba de ni thabs mkhas pa’o| |de la bzlog pa ni thabs la mi mkhas pa’o.
At this, Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “Who then, O Blessed One, should be known as virtuous friends for a bodhisattva great being?”

The Blessed One said, “Virtuous friends are those who instruct and guide a bodhisattva great being in the transcendent states and expound to him or her the activities of Māra like this: ‘The corruptions of Māra should be recognized as such; these are the corruptions of Māra. These activities of Māra should be recognized as such; these are the activities of Māra. Once you have recognized them, you should abandon them.’The proper name Māra means “death.” A māra is a maker of different types of obstacles for Buddhist practitioners on the path. In this scripture, Māra is personified as a single, king-like obstacle maker who commands hordes of obstacle makers trying to prevent spiritual progress or lead practitioners astray. According to Ratnākara (F.28.b), the instruction here is divided into Māra’s corruptions and his activities. The corruptions of Māra (out of which action arises) refer to his exertion of magical influence over a bodhisattva’s sense of what needs to be done physically and so on. In this way, he should be known as causing obstacles for a bodhisattva going about his or her life. The activities of Māra refers to Māra himself appearing in the guise of a buddha or another spiritual person, and leading a practitioner onto the wrong path. RK: lan ni dang de la zhes bya ba la sogs pa gsungs te| bdud kyi las nye bar ston pa ni ’dir bdud kyi nyes pa yang bdud kyi las yin te| bdud kyi bya ba las byung ba’i phyir dang las yin pa’i phyir ro| |ji ltar ston pa yin zhe na| ’di lta bur zhes bya ba la sogs pa gsungs so| |byang chub sems dpa’ rnams la bdud kyis byin gyis brlabs nas byung ba’i lus la sogs pa’i bya ba ni bdud kyi nyes pa’o| |de ltar de dag ni byang chub sems dpa’i spyod pa la bar chad byed pa nyid du rig par bya’o| |bdud kyi nyes pa ’di dag ces bya ba ni gang ngas khyod la bshad pa’o| |bdud kyi las shes bya ba ni bdud rang nyid sangs rgyas la sogs pa’i cha byad du byas nas lam log par ston par byed pa’o| |de bzhin du ston pa zhes bya ba de dag gis byang chub sems dpa’i spyod pa las dben pa nyid du rig par bya’o. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 85. Subhūti, such people should be known as the virtuous friends of a bodhisattva great being, who has donned the great armor, has set out on the great journey, and has embarked on the great vehicle.”According to Hari (74–75), the phrase “donned the great armor” means he or she is outfitted with the armor-like practice that will be explained (as taking a vow and practicing the six transcendent states before realizing the path of seeing). The phrase “set out on the great journey” means he or she is endowed with the practice moving forward in the very same way. The phrase “ascended in the great vehicle” means that by that very same means, he or she has driven the great vehicle up and away. To explain: it is held that he or she first sets out and later ascends in the Mahāyāna. Hari: mahāsaṃnāhasaṃnaddhasye ti saṃnāhapratipattyā vakṣyamāṇayā yuktasya. mahāyānasaṃprasthitasye ti prasthānapratipattyā tathaivānvitasya. mahā­yāna­samārūḍhasye ti tayaiva mahāyānam utkalitasya. tathā hy ādau tayā saṃprasthitaḥ paścād ārūḍha iṣyate.

At this, Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “What the Blessed One has said is as follows: ‘Such people should be known as the virtuous friends of a bodhisattva great being, who has donned the great armor, has set out on the great journey, and has embarked on the great vehicle.’

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 8 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 11 in the Eighteen Thousand.“And yet, O Blessed One, regarding that which you call ‘a bodhisattva great being,’ what is the referent of the word bodhisattva?”There is a play here on the word bodhisattva that cannot be represented in English. Generally, the word sattva/satva (within bodhisattva) can be interpreted to mean either a “being” or a “courageous one” (as the Tibetan translation sems dpa’ suggests), or even “spirit,” “mind,” “or consciousness.” If we artificially combine these different meanings into one, the word bodhisattva might give an overall sense of “a courageous being with a mindset aimed toward awakening.” In any event, the passage revolves around a Middle Indic play on words that depends on (a) the fact that the Middle Indic word satta could mean either “a courageous one” (sattva) or “an entanglement” (sakta) or other meanings (not applied in this particular pun), and (b) the application of Middle Indic spelling rules for internal coalescent links (sandhi) between the words “free of entanglement” (asatta) and “a courageous one” (satta), which produces an ambiguous result when compounded with the preceding word “awakening” (bodhi). The history of the terms played upon are discussed by different scholars. See, for instance, Orsborn 2012, pp. 158–68. Cf. Schmithausen 1977.

At this, the Blessed One said to Subhūti, “Subhūti, the referent of the word bodhisattva is free of any referent. Why is that? Subhūti, it is because a bodhisattva great being trains to be free of being entangled with any things. Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being is free of being entangled with the objective of comprehending all things, he or she realizes unsurpassable, perfect awakening. But, Subhūti, since he or she has the objective of awakening, he or she is called a bodhisattva great being.”According to Hari (78), the freedom from entanglement with the “objective of comprehending all things”‍—which serve as meditational objects‍—means being free from entanglement with (even) an accurate meditational object that does not go against the two truths. Because of that, when that freedom from entanglement is present, the bodhisattva (i.e., a being with the objective of awakening) “realizes unsurpassable, perfect awakening” by achieving the complete refinement of the meditative focus‍—and not by any other means. Hari: yasmāt sarvadharmāṇām evālambana­sthānīyānām anubodhanārthena samyagubhaya­satyānati­kramālambanena yāsaktatā tasyāṃ satyām ālambana­viśuddhi­gamanenānuttarāṃ samyaksambodhim abhisambudhyate nānyathā. Note: RK (F.29.b) explains that Subhūti’s question and the Blessed One’s answer mark the shift to the Ornament’s fourth subtopic, “the basis for practice.”
Subhūti said, “But the Blessed One has called him or her ‘a bodhisattva great being.’ For what reason, O Blessed One, is he or she called a bodhisattva that is a ‘great being’?”

The Blessed One said, “It is because he or she will cause a great number of beings and a great gathering of beings to achieve the greatest goal. Since he or she has this objective, he or she is called a ‘great being’ bodhisattva.”There is play here on the Sanskrit word mahāsattva, translated here as “great being.” The basic idea is that “great being” means he or she has the “great,” i.e., the greatest goal or highest point (agratā), as his or her “intention.” Another way to characterize the pun in English would be to say “great being” (mahāsattva) here means “being high-minded.”

This point in the chapter corresponds to points within Chapter 8 of the Twenty-Five Thousand (8.59) and Hundred Thousand (8.111), and to the beginning of Chapter 12 in the Eighteen Thousand.Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, I too will venture to illuminate the objective due to which a bodhisattva is called a ‘great being.’ ”
The Blessed One said, “O Śāriputra, please illuminate what you are thinking right now.”

The venerable Śāriputra said, “The objective due to which a bodhisattva is called a ‘great being’ is that he or she is going to teach the Dharma to eliminate the numerous erroneous beliefs‍—such as the erroneous beliefs in a self, the erroneous beliefs in a being, the erroneous beliefs in a soul, the erroneous beliefs in a person, the erroneous beliefs in existence, the erroneous beliefs in dissolution, the erroneous beliefs in nihilism, the erroneous beliefs in eternalism, and the erroneous beliefs in individuality‍—and so on.”There is another play here on Sanskrit word mahāsattva, translated here as “great” (mahā) + “being” (sattva). The play on the word is that it can also be interpreted as “great” (mahā) + “nonexistence” (asattva), i.e., the great eliminator of wrong views about the existence of things that do not exist, such as self, soul, being, etc. According to Ratnākara (F.31.a), the view of a self and so on have the same meaning, namely, all these views are fixated upon some real thing according to their particular tenet system. Among the erroneous beliefs, the word “existence” (in this passage above) refers to cyclic existence, and the word “dissolution” refers to nirvāṇa. These two are determined to be the main ones. The “erroneous beliefs in nihilism” refers to a nihilistic cutting off as attaining nirvāṇa. The “erroneous beliefs in eternalism” refers to the idea that a self remains forever. The “erroneous beliefs in individuality” refers to a habitual fixation on the categories that lack inherent nature‍—i.e., the constituents of experience, elements, gateways, and so on‍—as having an inherent nature. The additional unmentioned erroneous beliefs signified by the phrase “and so on” should be understood as the list of erroneous beliefs such as clinging to the supremacy of ethical discipline and vows, and so on (found in abhidharmic sources). RK: de nas bcom ldan ’das zhes bya ba la sogs pa la dus la zhes bya ba ni skabs so| |bdag tu lta ba la sogs pa la don gcig go| |de dag gi lta ba ni grub pa’i mtha’i bye brag gis de’i dngos por mngon par zhen pa’o| |’byung ba ni ’khor ba’o| |’jig pa ni mya ngan las ’das pa’o| |de gnyis kyi lta ba ni gtso bo nyid du nges pa’o| |mya ngan las ’das pa thob pa ni chad pa yin no zhes bya ba ni chad par lta ba’o| |bdag ni rtag par gnas pa yin no zhes bya ba ni rtag par lta ba’o |lus ni phung po dang| khams dang| skye mched la sogs pa’o| |de dag gi ngo bo nyid med pa la ngo bo nyid du mngon par zhen pa ni rang gi lus la lta ba’o | | ’di lta bu zhes bya ba ni ji skad du bstan pa’o| |sogs pa zhes bya ba ni tshul khrims dang brtul zhugs mchog tu ’dzin pa la sogs pa log pa’i lta ba rnams kyang rtogs par bya ba yin no. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 92. For this list of erroneous beliefs, see Pradhan’s AKBh (58).
Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, I too will venture to be illuminating about why a bodhisattva is called a ‘great being.’ ”
The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, please venture to illuminate what you are thinking right now.”

Subhūti then said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, he or she is called ‘a bodhisattva great being’ because he or she is neither entangled nor enmeshed in even that mindset‍—namely, that mindset of awakening, which is the mindset with complete knowing, the mindset free from worldly influences, the unequaled mindset, the mindset equal to the unequaled, and the mindset not shared by the disciples and solitary realizers.There is another play here on Sanskrit word mahāsattva, translated here as “great being” but which can also be translated as “great courageous one.” This passage is explaining the bodhisattva’s greatness by emphasizing the different ways that he or she is “great” (mahā-). This passage also (a) revolves around a play on the Middle Indic word satta, which in Sanskrit could mean either “a courageous one” (sattva), “an entanglement” (sakta), a living being (sattva), “existence” (sattva), “spirit,” “mindset,” or “consciousness” (sattva), and so on, and (b) the application of Middle Indic spelling rules for internal links (sandhi) between the words “free of entanglement” or “free of existence” (asatta) and “courageous one” (satta) and so on, which produces an ambiguous result when connected to the word “great” (mahā). See, for instance, Orsborn 2012, pp. 158–68. Cf. Schmithausen 1977. Why is that? It is because that mindset with complete knowing is free of worldly influence and free of enmeshment, and also because bodhisattva great beings are neither entangled nor enmeshed in even that mindset that is the mindset with complete knowing free of worldly influence and free of enmeshment. Since he or she has this objective, he or she is considered ‘a bodhisattva great being.’ ”
Then the venerable Śāriputra asked the venerable Subhūti, “Due to what reason, Venerable Subhūti, is he or she neither entangled nor enmeshed in even that mindset?”
Subhūti said, “Due to his or her being free of a mindset, Venerable Śāriputra, he or she is neither entangled nor enmeshed in even that mindset.”
Then the venerable Śāriputra asked the venerable Subhūti, “But then, Venerable Subhūti, if that mindset is free of a mindset, does that mindset exist?”
At this, the venerable Subhūti asked the venerable Śāriputra, “Well, Venerable Śāriputra, in the state free of a mindset, is an existence or a nonexistence found or mentally perceived?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” replied the venerable Śāriputra.

The venerable Subhūti continued, “O Venerable Śāriputra, if, in that state free of a mindset, an existence or a nonexistence is neither found nor mentally perceived, then is your questioning of my statement reasonable as expressed: ‘If that mindset is free of a mindset, does that mindset exist?’ ”

The venerable Śāriputra then said to the venerable Subhūti, “Excellent, excellent, Venerable Subhūti. You are explaining in just the way that the Blessed One indicated that you would, that is to say, as the foremost among those who abide free of conflict!”

This point in the chapter corresponds to points within Chapter 8 of the Twenty-Five Thousand (8.76) and Hundred Thousand (8.165), and to the beginning of Chapter 13 in the Eighteen Thousand.Then Pūrṇa, son of Maitrāyaṇī, said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, the one described in this context as a ‘great being’ is a sentient being who has donned the great armor, set out on the great journey, and mounted the great vehicle. It is for this reason that he or she is considered a ‘great being.’ ”
Then the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, to what extent has a bodhisattva great being‍—described in this context as having donned the great armor‍—donned the great armor?”

The Blessed One said, “Here, Subhūti, it occurs to a bodhisattva great being, ‘The sentient beings to be liberated by me are immeasurable. The sentient beings to be liberated by me are innumerable. And yet, there are no sentient beings to be liberated by anyone.’ With this understanding, he or she causes all those sentient beings to be liberated and yet no one is liberating and no one is liberated. Why is that? Subhūti, it is because, in light of the illusory nature of things, this is the true nature of things. Subhūti, it is just as if a skilled magical illusionist or a magical illusionist’s apprentice were to conjure up a great gathering of people at the intersection of four highways. Then, having conjured them up, if he or she were to make that very gathering of people disappear, Subhūti, what would you think in that case? Would anyone have killed anyone? Would anyone be dead or destroyed? Would anyone have made them disappear?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, in this very way, a bodhisattva great being liberates immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings, and yet no one is liberating and no one is liberated. If a bodhisattva great being hears this explication being given in this way and is not frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror, then, Subhūti, that bodhisattva great being should be known as having donned the great armor to that extent.”

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Indeed, O Blessed One, as far as I have understood the point of the Blessed One’s explication, the bodhisattva great being should be known as not having donned the great armor.”

The Blessed One said, “You are right. Subhūti, you are right. The bodhisattva great being should be known, indeed, as not having donned the great armor.According to Ratnākara (F.33.a), this interjection “indeed” arises from his being worthy of hearing the ultimate truth. RK: kye ma zhes bya ba’i sgra ni don dam pa mnyan pa’i ’os su ’gyur pa’i phyir ro. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 100. Why not? Because, Subhūti, the state of complete knowing is uncreated, untransformed, and unconditioned. Also, those sentient beings for whose sake he or she has donned the great armor are neither created, nor transformed, nor conditioned.”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “So it is, O Blessed One. So it is, Sugata. Why is that? O Blessed One, it is because physical forms are neither fettered nor liberated.There is a play on the word “unfettered” here. The Sanskrit term translated above as “donned” (saṃnaddha) literally means that a bodhisattva great being has “tied” or “fastened” (viz. bound or fettered) the armor onto him- or herself. But since he or she is not actually attached to or entangled with awakening (or anything else), he or she is neither bound nor fettered by this armor, and hence not liberated from (putting it on) either. This is the basic idea of the pun. Likewise, O Blessed One, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are neither fettered nor liberated. Also, the actual nature of physical forms is neither fettered nor liberated. Likewise, O Blessed One, the actual nature of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is neither fettered nor liberated.”According to Hari (618), in his explanation the master Vasubandhu says that the Sanskrit word tathatā (i.e., “actual nature”) derives from the Sanskrit suffix tā (which in this case) means the “nature” (bhāva) of being “as it is” (tathā, i.e., the way things actually are). The Sanskrit word tathatā has a shortened vowel ending (making it tatha + tā instead of tathā + tā‍—probably as an early adjectival form of the adverbial tathā). Hari: tathābhāvas tathate ty ata eva nirdeśād asya hrasvatvam ity ācāryavasubandhuḥ. Note: it is not clear in which explanation Vasubandhu says this, perhaps in his lost Paddhati? Hari makes this comment in chapter 16, not here.

Then the venerable Pūrṇa, son of Maitrāyaṇī, said to the venerable Subhūti, “O Venerable Subhūti, you say that physical forms are neither fettered nor liberated. Likewise, Venerable Subhūti, you say that feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are neither fettered nor liberated. Venerable Subhūti, you say that the actual nature of physical forms also is neither fettered nor liberated. Likewise, Venerable Subhūti, you say that the actual nature of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is neither fettered nor liberated.

“Well then, Venerable Subhūti, what physical forms are neither fettered nor liberated as you say? Likewise, Venerable Subhūti, what feelings, what conceptions, what conditioning factors, and what consciousness is neither fettered nor liberated as you say? Also, Venerable Subhūti, what actual nature of physical forms is neither fettered nor liberated as you say? Likewise, Venerable Subhūti, what actual nature of feelings, what actual nature of conceptions, what actual nature of conditioning factors, and what actual nature of consciousness is neither fettered nor liberated as you say?”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the venerable Pūrṇa, son of Maitrāyaṇī, “O Venerable Pūrṇa, the physical form of an illusory person is neither fettered nor liberated. Likewise, Venerable Pūrṇa, the feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness of an illusory person are neither fettered nor liberated. Venerable Pūrṇa, the actual nature of an illusory person’s physical form is neither fettered nor liberated. Likewise, the actual nature of an illusory person’s feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is neither fettered nor liberated. Why is that? Since the actual nature is not really existent, it is neither fettered nor liberated. Since it is void, it is neither fettered nor liberated. Since it has not arisen, it is neither fettered nor liberated.In a realist view, something that does not arise (anutpanna) because of causes and conditions is said to be either permanently existent or permanently nonexistent. However, here, the fact that the actual nature “has not arisen” implies that it is something immediate but beyond existing or not existing. For a bodhisattva great being who has donned the great armor, who has set out on the great journey, and who has mounted the great vehicle, this is the great armor free of any armor.”This section involves a play on the Sanskrit word yāna, occurring in the terms mahāyāna or niryāna derived from the verbal root √yā. The yāna in both terms can refer either to “a means of going” or to “the action of going.” If we consider them in connection with a courageous hero (i.e., a bodhisattva great being), they also can have the sense of marching forth (like on a military conquest). Finally, the prefix nir- (in niryāna), which is translated as “beyond,” here can also mean “free of,” and hence an additional pun (on top of others) is that the great going is free of going anywhere, i.e., going nowhere or disappearing. The passage also plays on the terms “setting out” (saṃprasthāna) and “stopping” (sthāna), both derived from the verbal root √sthā. In any event, when the word māhāyāna refers to a “means,” it is translated here, according to context, as either a “great vehicle” or a “great path.” But when it refers more to the “action” of going, it is translated as “great journey.” Furthermore, since the literature of the transcendent state of wisdom sometimes employs the metaphor of a military campaign to accumulate the bounty of merit and wisdom, these words can be fruitfully imagined as, respectively, “a great chariot,” “a great highway,” or “a procession” of all beings toward awakening.
At this, the venerable Pūrṇa, son of Maitrāyaṇī, became silent.

This point in the chapter corresponds to points within Chapter 8 of the Twenty-Five Thousand (8.215) and Hundred Thousand (8.377), and to the beginning of Chapter 15 in the Eighteen Thousand.Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, the bodhisattva great being who has donned the great armor in this way has set out on the great journey and has mounted the great vehicle. But what is that great vehicle? How should someone who has set out on that great journey be recognized? From where will that great path go beyond cyclic existence? By what means has that great vehicle set out? Where will the great vehicle come to dwell? Who will go beyond cyclic existence by means of this great vehicle?”According to Ratnākara’s explanation (F.34.a), we might understand the different meanings of “going” (yāna) in the six questions as follows: (a) Since going (yāna) is by means of it, it is a “vehicle” (yāna), and hence he asks the first question “What is the great vehicle?” (b) In the phrase “how someone has set out on it,” the Sanskrit term literally means “stand forth,” which has the meaning of begun to go (yāna) toward whatever there is. Going by some means involves a vehicle (yāna; theg pa), but the action of going is what is to be done (viz. “journey”). Hence, this question suggests that by whatever means it is to be done, by that means he or she will be going. (c) Insofar as “going” (yāna) implies “going beyond cyclic existence,” he asks the question “From where does he or she go beyond?” (d) Since that “going” implies the defining characteristic of someone, he asks the question “Who has set out?” (e) Since, after going and having arrived, he or she comes to be somewhere, he asks the question “Where does he or she come to dwell?” (f) Since those with weak disposition do not go beyond cyclic existence, he asks the question “Who will go beyond by means of this?” RK (with the variant Tibetan lemmas also marked in bold): ’di dag gi dri ba drug go| (a) |’dis ’gro bas na theg pa’o| |theg pa chen po de dag gang lags zhes bya ba ni dri ba dang po’i don to| (b) |de gang ltar yang dag par ’jug pa zhes bya ba ni rab tu gnas pa ste rab kyi sgra gang na yod na ’gro ba la ’jug pa yin no| |gang gis ’gro ba de ni theg pa ste| ’gro ba ni bya ba’o| |des na gang gis bya ba des ’gro bar ’gyur ro zhes bya ba’i don to | (c) |’gro ba ni nges par ’byung ba’i don to| |des na gang nas nges par ’byung bar ’gyur zhes bya bar dri ba yin no| (d) |’gro ba ni gang zhig gi mtshan nyid yin pas des na de gang gis ’jug par ’gyur zhes dris pa yin no| (e) |’gro zhing song nas la lar gnas pa’i don yin pas des na gang du gnas par ’gyur ba zhes dris pa yin no| (f) |’dis dman pa’i rigs nges par ’byung ba ma yin te| des na des gang zhig nges par ’byung bar ’gyur zhes dris pa yin no. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, pp. 101–2. According to Hari, the Mahāyāna is not perceived. Vimuktisena explains these six questions through six interrogatives, namely “what, how, from where, by whom, in what location, and who will go beyond cyclic existence.” In other words, (a) what the practice of wisdom and compassion is, (b) how to overcome counterproductive forces, (c) from where the antidote is transcended, (d) by whom one’s level is ascertained, (e) in what location the application of antidotes relaxes (praśrabdhi), and (f) who ultimately realizes this. Hari: tatra mahāyānaṃ na jñāyata iti (a) prajñākaruṇāprayogaṃ (b) tayor anyatarāvyatirekaṃ (c) vipakṣātikramam (d) avasthānaniścayaṃ (e) prati­pakṣa­prayoga­praśrabdhim (f) adhigantāraṃ cādhikṛtya yad yathā yato yena yatra yo niryāsyatīti katamad ityādikāpadeśena ṣaṭ praśnā ity āryavimuktisenaḥ.

At this, the Blessed One said to Subhūti, “The great vehicle, Subhūti, is the epithet of something immeasurable. Subhūti, it is immeasurable, because it is beyond measure. Subhūti, what you also asked is, ‘How can someone who has set out on the great journey be recognized? From where will that great path go beyond cyclic existence? Who has set out on the great journey? Where will the great vehicle come to dwell? Who will go beyond cyclic existence by means of this great vehicle?’According to Ratnākara (F.34.b), these six answers explain the six aspects of the Mahāyāna, namely (a) the greatness of the immeasurable, (b) the greatness of going forth, (c) the greatness of going beyond cyclic existence, (d) greatness as defining characteristic, (e) the greatness of attainment, and (f) the greatness of resting place. The first of these can also be split into two‍—namely “immeasurable” and “beyond measure”‍—thereby making a total of seven aspects. For edited Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Aii, p. 104. RK: lan drug po ’di dag gis theg pa chen po rnam pa drug tu smras so| (a) |dpag tu med pa’i chen po nyid dang| (b) yang dag par ’jug pa’i chen po nyid dang| (c) nges par ’byung ba’i chen po nyid dang| (d) mtshan nyid chen po nyid dang| (e) thob pa’i chen po nyid dang| (f) gnas kyi chen po nyid do| (g) |yang na rnam pa bdun te gzhal du med pa dang| dpag tu med pa’i dbye bas so. Note: Words have been added to the translation of the six statements to make more obvious how they address the corresponding questions above. For instance, the impersonal Sanskrit phrasing “by whom has the great journey been set out upon?” (kena vā tan mahāyānaṃ saṃprasthitam) has been made active in both question and answer. Cf. MW prasthitam + instrumental. Someone who has set out on the journey can be recognized by means of the transcendent states. From whatever belongs to the three realms, the great path will go beyond cyclic existence. He or she who is beyond the scope of perception has set out upon the journey. The great vehicle will come to dwell in the state of complete knowing. The bodhisattva great being will go beyond cyclic existence, but he or she will not go beyond cyclic existence from any location, nor will anyone even set out on a journey. He or she will not come to dwell in any state. But, by not dwelling anywhere, he or she will come to dwell in the state of complete knowing. By means of this great vehicle, no one has gone beyond cyclic existence, no one will go beyond cyclic existence, and no one is going beyond cyclic existence. Why is that? It is because those two things‍—that is, someone going beyond cyclic existence and some means by which to go beyond it‍—are neither found nor mentally perceived. Since these two are not to be found as such, what will go beyond cyclic existence by means of what?”In the extant Sanskrit versions, there are concluding sentences here that are lacking in all Tibetan sources. In Cambridge’s MS Bendall Add 866, it goes on to say, “It is in this very way that a bodhisattva great being will go beyond cyclic existence in the transcendent state of wisdom.” In Mitra’s edition, it goes on even further to say, “It is in this way that the bodhisattva great being, who has donned the great armor in this way, has set out on the great journey, and ascended in the great vehicle.”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, it is called a great vehicle because by means of it a bodhisattva great being will surpass the world with its gods, humans, and asuras, and hence will go beyond cyclic existence.According to Ratnākara’s comments (F.44.b), this sentence provides the reason for why it is called a great vehicle. Also, it should be understood here that his or her surpassing of the world is simultaneous with going beyond cycle existence, like closing one’s eyes while laughing. The reason why he or she surpasses the world is that they attain the greatness that the world has not attained, and furthermore, that attainment is the highest among the multitude of beings. In other words, the vehicle is called “great” because it is great in relation to all beings, in terms of its renunciation, and in terms of its realization. In this way, the sentence gives a basic overview of the way that the great vehicle goes beyond cyclic existence. What follows in the rest of this chapter explains different aspects of that process of going beyond cyclic existence. RK: mahāyānam ityādi| yasmād anena sadevamānuṣāsuraṃ lokam abhibhūya bodhisattvo niryāsyati tasmād idaṃ yānaṃ mahāyānam ity ucyate| abhibhūya niryāsyat īti samānakāle ktvāpratyayaḥ| saṃmīlya hasatītyādivat| kasmād asau sarvalokam abhibhavati ? tadalabhyasya | māhātmyasya lābhāt| kiṃ punas tat sattvarāśer agratāsaṃpādanam| sattvarāśimahattayā prahāṇamahattayā adhigamamahattayā ca mahat| itīdam uddeśaniryāṇam. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 142 and Aii, p. 104. It is a great vehicle that is equal to space. Just as there is room in space for immeasurable and innumerable beings, just so, O Blessed One, there is room in this vehicle for immeasurable and innumerable beings.There is a play on the word “space” (akāśa) formed from the Sanskrit verbal root √kāś. The wordplay about the vehicle being space means, essentially, that since there is nothing but space (viz. it is spacious), there is room for all beings in the vehicle. It is from this perspective that it is the great vehicle of the bodhisattva great beings. It is not seen coming. It is not seen going. It is not mentally perceived dwelling. Likewise, O Blessed One, the beginning point, the middle point, and the end point of this great path are not mentally perceived. O Blessed One, this path is the same in all three times. Therefore, it is called the great path.”

Then indeed the Blessed One commended Subhūti: “Excellent, excellent, Subhūti. It is as you say, Subhūti, it is as you say. In this way, it is the great path of bodhisattva great beings. Having trained in this, bodhisattva great beings have attained, are attaining, and will attain the state of complete knowing.”

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 12 of the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and to the beginning of Chapter 20 of the Eighteen Thousand.Then the venerable Pūrṇa, son of Maitrāyāṇi, said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, this elder Subhūti, who was asked about the transcendent state of wisdom, considers that the great path is the topic to be expounded.”

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, I was not speaking of the great path to the neglect of the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.45.b), Pūrṇa’s question here aims to remove others’ possible objection that talking here about the Mahāyāna is not relevant to the topic of the transcendent state of wisdom. RK: atha khalv ityādinā pūrṇasya praśnaḥ prajñāpāramitādhikāre mahāyānasya pareṣām aprastutatvāśaṅkānivṛttyarthaḥ. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 145.

The Blessed One said, “Absolutely not, Subhūti. You are expounding the great path in conformity with the transcendent state of wisdom, Subhūti.”According to Ratnākara (F.45.b), the idea here is that Subhūti is expounding the great path in conformity with the transcendent state of wisdom, because he teaches that all things on the great path lack inherent nature and because knowing that all things lack inherent nature is nothing but the transcendent state of wisdom. RK: anulomaṃ nirdiśasī ti tvaṃ hi sarvadharmān mahāyāne niḥsvabhāvān nirdiśasi| niḥsvabhāvatājñānaṃ ca teṣāṃ prajñāpāramitaiva| tasmād anulomaṃ nirdiśasīti bhāvaḥ. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 145.

At this, Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, it is through the Buddha’s miraculous power.Hari and Ratnākara naturally interpret the following paragraph from the perspective of their respective philosophical positions. According to Hari (109–11), it should generally be understood in terms of his perspective on the relative and absolute truths. According to Ratnākara (F.45.b–49.a), there are twelve statements that parallel a more expanded section in the Twenty-Five Thousand (12.9 et seq.) to communicate his interpretation of the three natures (trisvabhāva), i.e., (1–6) imagined nature, (7–8) dependent nature, and (9–12) actual nature. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, pp. 145–55 and Aii, pp. 137–50. Nevertheless, O Blessed One, a bodhisattva does not go close to either any beginning point, any end point, or any middle point. Why not? This is because, insofar as physical forms are limitless, a bodhisattva should be known as limitless.According to Ratnākara (F.46.a), physical forms and so on are, like space, limitless, on account of their emptiness. RK: rūpādaya ākāśasamāḥ rūpādiśūnyatām upādāya. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 146 and Aii, p. 140. Likewise, insofar as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are limitless, a bodhisattva should be known as limitless. He or she does not go close to the thought, ‘Bodhisattvas are physical forms.’ Even this thought is not found and not mentally perceived. Likewise, he or she does not go close to the thought, ‘Bodhisattvas are feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.’ Even this thought is not found and not mentally perceived. O Blessed One, since I am not mentally perceiving any such thing called a ‘bodhisattva’ anywhere at all or in any way at all, I do not identify this thing designated to be a ‘bodhisattva.’According to Ratnākara (F.46.a–b), a “thing designated to be a ‘bodhisattva’ ” refers here to the physical and mental constituents of experience called a bodhisattva. RK: bodhisattvadharmam iti bodhisattvākhyarūpādi. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 147 and Aii, p. 140. Nor do I identify or mentally perceive the transcendent state of wisdom. Nor do I identify or mentally perceive the state of complete knowing. O Blessed One, since I myself am neither mentally perceiving nor identifying that thing anywhere at all by any means at all, what thing should I instruct and guide in what thing by means of what thing? O Blessed One, this ‘buddha’ is a mere designation. O Blessed One, this ‘bodhisattva’ is a mere designation. O Blessed One, this ‘transcendent state of wisdom’ is a mere designation. But these designations have not truly come into being.According to Ratnākara (F.46.b–47.a), “a mere designation” means a name devoid of referent (artha). To explain: The name is not the nature (svabhāva) of a referent, because one sees the mere referent before the linguistic convention (saṅketaḥ). And, also, the referent is not the nature of the name, because one can ascertain (pratītiḥ) just a mere name whose referent is not found. And, since words are used even when there is no referent, a name has no referent. For this very reason, this designation has “not truly come into being” (anabhinirvṛttam), i.e., it is not established (siddham) as a designation (of a thing). RK: nāmadheyamātram iti arthaśūnyaṃ nāma| tathāhi nārthasya svabhāvo nāma, prāk saṅketād arthamātradarśanāt| nāpi nāmnaḥ svabhāvo ’rthaḥ, aviditārthasya nāmnaḥ kevalasyaiva pratīteḥ, asaty api cārthe śabdaprayogāt| nirarthakaṃ nāma| ata eva tac ca nāmadheyam anabhinirvṛttaṃ, nāmadheyatvenāsiddham. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 147 and Aii, p. 140. O Blessed One, it is just like the fact that the word self is said, but a self absolutely has not truly come into being.According to Ratnākara (F.47.a), “not truly coming into being” means not really existing, because that thing does not exist the way it appears. RK: anabhinirvṛtta ity asadbhūtaḥ| yathālakṣaṇam asattvāt. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 148 and Aii, p. 142. Similarly, all things have the inherent nature of lacking any state of being.This seventh statement‍—“all things have the inherent nature of lacking any state of being”‍—follows the Sanskrit suggested by Ratnākara, who explains, philologically, why some Sanskrit manuscripts‍—including those used by the Tibetan translation and by Mitra’s Sanskrit edition‍—have a different phrase due to a scribal error and an incorrect editorial correction. Without directly commenting on this seventh statement, Hari reads it as a relative clause that must be connected to the eighth statement: “Since all things lack inherent nature…” For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, pp. 148–52 and Aii, pp. 142–46. Cf. brgyad stong pa (F.15.a): de ltar chos thams cad ngo bo nyid ma mchis pa la. What are those physical forms that have not truly come into being and cannot be grasped? What are those feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness that have not truly come into being and cannot be grasped? The condition for all these things to be free of inherent nature in this way is their not truly coming into being, and yet those things’ not truly coming into being is not those things themselves. How then shall I instruct and guide someone who is not truly coming into being in the transcendent state of wisdom that also is not truly coming into being?According to Ratnākara (F.48.a), something “not truly coming into being” means it is nothing but emptiness. RK: tat kim iti tadā katham anabhinirvṛttim iti śūnyatāmātraṃ bodhisattvam anabhinirvṛttyām iti śūnyatāmātre prajñāpāramitāyām avavadiṣyāmi. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 153 and Aii, p. 152. O Blessed One, no things‍—whether they be things constituting buddhas, constituting bodhisattvas, or constituting anyone who might practice to awaken‍—are mentally perceived anywhere other than in their not truly coming into being.

“O Blessed One, when it is described in this way, taught in this way, and expounded in this way, if a bodhisattva great being’s mindset does not become deflated, depressed, dejected, or sunk in despondency, and if his or her mental attitude does not become discouraged, shattered, frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror, then in this way it should be known that this bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, this bodhisattva great being cultivates the transcendent state of wisdom, and this bodhisattva great being reflects upon the transcendent state of wisdom. The bodhisattva great being meditates upon the transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? It is because at the time when a bodhisattva investigates these things in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she neither goes close to physical forms, nor arrives at them, nor identifies their arising, nor identifies their cessation. Likewise, he or she neither goes close to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness, nor arrives at them, nor identifies their arising, nor identifies their cessation.According to Ratnākara (F.49.a), Subhūti says the first two phrases‍—i.e., “neither goes close to” and “nor arrives at”‍—to negate the conceptualization of an object’s and subject’s inherent nature. That is to say, a bodhisattva does not “go close to”‍—i.e., does not mentally perceive‍—these imagined things (from physical form to consciousness, and so on, up to the qualities of a buddha), because these things do not appear to him or her. He or she does not “arrive at” them‍—i.e., does not conceptualize them‍—because the transcendent state of wisdom is beyond conceptualization. Then, Subhūti says the next two phrases‍—i.e., “nor identifies their arising, nor their cessation”‍—to refute the conceptualization of their (the subject’s and object’s) two particular characteristics. That is to say, he or she does not identify any things as arising or having ceased, because he or she does not see any things‍—such as physical forms and so on‍—as having previously arisen and as now having ceased to exist. Indeed, since imagined things do not exist in the manner of their (imagined) characteristics, there is no (such thing as their) arising or ceasing to exist, and hence, a bodhisattva does not see either of these (i.e., the particular characteristics of arising or ceasing). For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, pp. 155–56 and Aii, pp. 150–51. RK: rūpaṃ kalpitaṃ yāvad vijñānaṃ yāvad buddhadharmān nopaitī ti nopalabhate, tadapratibhāsāt. nopagacchati na vikalpayati nirvikalpatvāt prajñāpāramitāyāḥ. iti svabhāvavikalpau pratiṣiddhau. viśeṣavikalpau pratiṣeddhum āha‍— na rūpā der utpādanirodh au| pūrvam utpanno rūpā dir idānīṃ niruddha iti na paśyati| kalpitasya hi yathālakṣaṇam asattvād utpādanirodhau na staḥ| tasmād ubhau na paśyati. According to Hari (113), these sentences explain how a bodhisattva could possibly investigate the transcendent state of wisdom through a perception of physical forms and so on. To explain: Just when he or she is investigating things, namely physical forms and so on, and their causes in the transcendent state of wisdom from the perspective of their not having an inherent nature, just then he or she does not “encounter”‍—i.e., mentally perceive‍—those things and does not “arrive at” ‍—i.e., assume‍—those things, because he or she does not identify an arising or ceasing. The basic reasoning here aims to negate the existence of any other (real) thing as being free of arising or ceasing. Hari: nanu rupādyupalambhena kathaṃ prajñāpāramitām upaparīkṣata iti tat kasya hetor ity āśankyāha: yasmim hī tyādi. yasmād yasmin kāle rūpādīn imān dharmān prajñāpāramitāyāṃ tannimittaṃ vyupaparīkṣate naiḥsvābhāvyākāreṇa. tasmāt tasmin kāle na rūpan upaiti na pratipadyate. ato nopagacchati na svīkaroti yato notpādanaṃ nirodhaṃ samanupaśyati. utpāda­vyaya­nirmuktasyānyasyāsattvād iti bhāvaḥ. Note: In his Instructions, Ratnākara explains that the actual nature of all things can be considered the single, universal characteristic that belongs to all things. However, conceiving of it as a characteristic to be mentally grasped prevents it being actualized directly in experience. So, this section is presenting a refutation of the way that realists might mistakenly conceptualize the actual nature of things to be examined on the path in terms of particular characteristics, such as arising, dwelling, and ceasing. Although the actual nature of things may be described in terms of what it is not‍—i.e., not arising, not ceasing, and so on‍—these particular characteristics are not to be considered as inhering in the actual nature. Why is that? To explain, the characteristic of not arising that belongs to physical forms is not the same as the physical forms.Based on Ratnākara’s suggestion (see previous note) that “not arising” is to be understood here as the particular characteristic (viśeṣa) of physical forms and so on, the relative-correlative construction (yad-tad; gang lags pa de) has been translated freely as “the characteristic of not arising…”‍—rather than (more literally) as “that which is the not arising”‍—throughout the passage. RK: viśeṣavikalpau pratiṣeddhum āha. The characteristic of not ceasing that belongs to physical forms is not the same as the physical forms.In order to clarify the meaning of nonduality, the former two sentences refute sameness or oneness. The following two sentences refute difference or duality. See subsequent notes for more information. This being said, the characteristic of not arising and the physical forms are something free of duality, and free from creating duality.According to Ratnākara (F.49), the question “why” indicates an objection (to the two previous sentences) that any physical forms‍—which do not exist in the same moment‍—cannot logically be established (side by side) as two different things. Response to this objection: The terms physical forms and so on here do not refer to their dependent nature. Rather, “something free of duality” refers to the actual nature (pariniṣpanna) because only that nature neither arises or ceases. Since the actual nature of things is characterized as the nonexistence of the imagined nature, its characteristics of not arising or ceasing can also be characterized as nonexistence. Hence, the idea that these three‍—physical forms, not arising, and not ceasing‍—are not different is due to their having the same single flavor. RK: nanu tadānīṃ rūpāder abhāvād advayībhāvo na yukta ity ata āha| yat punar ityādi| nātra paratantraṃ rūpādi gṛhyate| kiṃ tarhi yad advayam utpādavyayavirahāt pariniṣpannam ity arthaḥ| pariniṣpanno hi svabhāvo dharmāṇāṃ kalpitābhāva­lakṣaṇaḥ| anutpādāvyayāv api dharmāṇām abhāvalakṣaṇau| tata ekarasatvād eṣāṃ trayāṇām advayībhāva iti bhāvaḥ. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 158 and Aii, p. 152. According to Hari (114), the previous sentences could leave open the possible objection that the perception of physical forms just refers to the perception of their arising and ceasing, and hence the question means, “How could he or she not encounter physical forms?” In anticipation of this objection, Subhūti asks the rhetorical question “Why is that?” To this, he responds with the following statement. That which “neither arises nor ceases is not the same as the physical forms” since physical forms only appear at the conventional level, and hence things that remain only momentarily there in existence are actually ceasing, i.e., dissolving (also at the conventional level). Due to negating that (dissolution also), the text says next “not ceasing.” Hari: nanv aikātmye ’nutpādānirodhopalambha eva rūpopalambhaḥ. ataḥ kathaṃ na rūpam upaitīti tat kasya hetor ity āśaṅkyāha : tathā hītyādi. yasmād yāv anutpādāvyayau na tau rūpaṃ saṃvṛtyā rūpasyaiva pratibhāsanāt. tatra bhāva eva kṣaṇasthitidharmā. nāśo vyayaḥ. tatpratiṣedhād avyayaḥ. Note: The Yogācāra and Mādhyamika systems interpret nonduality differently and this affects Ratnākara’s and Hari’s interpretations of this passage. In brief, nonduality for Ratnākara means the direct knowing of sheer luminosity without any cognitive distinction between a grasping subject and an object grasped. For Hari, it means the absence of any conceptual distinction, including between ultimate emptiness and relative appearance. From these divergent perspectives, they make many comments throughout this section, most of which cannot be noted here. Also, the characteristic of not ceasing and the physical forms are something free of duality, and free from creating duality.According to Ratnākara (F.49.b), these first two sentences here refute the “nondifference” of things and their nature, whereas the next two sentences refute their “difference.” RK: evam abhedaṃ pratiṣidhya bhedapratiṣedhāyāha| ity anutpādāvyayau ca rūpādiś cādvayam etad advaidhīkāram iti. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 157, and Aii, pp. 151–52. Later in his comments on chapter 8, Ratnākara explains that these things are “something free of duality” due to their being the same, and “free from creating duality” due to their inseparability. Ratnākara glosses: advayaṃ tādātmyāt|advaidhīkāram avyabhicārāt. According to Hari (114), since diversity arises only at the conventional level, a particular physical phenomenon and its not arising or ceasing‍—the latter of which persists at the ultimate level‍—are “something free of duality.” “Here” (etad) refers to their single nature of not arising or ceasing, since it is “free from creating duality,” i.e., a valid cognition free from creating the two types of negated existence (existence and nonexistence). Hari: yataḥ saṃvṛtyā nānātvam iti tasmād anutpādāvyayau rūpaṃ ca paramārthato ’nutpādāvyayasyaiva sthiter advayam anutpādādyekarūpam etat. yato ’dvaidhīkāram apagatadvidhā bhāvakāraka pramāṇaṃ. Note: Yogācāra and Madhyamaka scholars interpret “nonduality” differently. Again, saying ‘physical forms’ is to quantify something free of duality.According to Hari (114), the expression “physical forms” is to quantify‍—i.e., designate‍—something free of duality as having the characteristic of not arising and so on, based on logic. Hari: advayasya nyāyenānutpādādirūpasyaiṣā rūpam ity udbhāvanāsaṃvṛtyā gaṇanā saṃjñā kṛtā saṃketitā. According to Ratnākara (F.50.a), in the phrase “is to quantify something free of duality,” that which is free of duality is a reference to the source of things (dharmadhātu). For that reason the statement “even a bodhisattva has not arisen” means that he or she is (not separate from) the source of things, on account of being free of arising. However, what is quantified (gaṇyate) here is not the nature that is dependent (i.e., consciousness of something else), since that dependent nature (of consciousness) is not the ultimate (reality, i.e., the unchanging and undeluded nature of sheer reflexive awareness), and since the ultimate is the topic being discussed (adhikāra) in this section. RK: tena hī ti yen ādvayasyaiṣā gaṇanā kṛtā| advayaś ca dharmadhātuḥ| tena kāraṇena bodhisattvo’ py anutpādaḥ| utpādavirahād dharmadhātur ity arthaḥ| paratantras tu svabhāvo neha gaṇyate| tasyāparamārthatvāt paramārthasya cehādhikārāt. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 160 and Aii, p. 155.

“Likewise, the characteristic of not arising that belongs to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is not the same as the so-called feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. The characteristic of not ceasing that belongs to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is not the same as the so-called consciousness. This being said, the characteristic of not arising and the feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are something free of duality, and free from creating duality. Also, the characteristic of not ceasing and the feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are something free of duality, and free from creating duality. Again, saying ‘feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness’ is to quantify something free of duality.

“Therefore, O Blessed One, at the time when a bodhisattva is deeply reflecting upon all things as all the aspects in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she does not go close to physical forms, arrive at physical forms, identify their arising, or identify their cessation. Likewise, he or she does not go close to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness, arrive at them, identify their arising, or identify their cessation. Why is that? To explain, physical forms’ nature of not arising is not the same as the so-called physical forms. Physical forms’ nature of not ceasing is not the same as the so-called physical forms. This being said, the characteristic of not arising and the physical forms are something free of duality, and free from creating duality. Also, the characteristic of not ceasing and the physical forms are something free of duality, and free from creating duality. Again, saying ‘physical forms’ is to quantify something free of duality.

“Likewise, the characteristic of not arising of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is not the same as the so-called feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. The characteristic of not ceasing of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is not the same as the so-called feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. This being said, the characteristic of not arising and the feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are something free of duality, and free from creating duality. Also, the characteristic of not ceasing and the feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are something free of duality, and free from creating duality. Again, saying ‘feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness’ is to quantify something free of duality.”

Then the venerable Śāriputra said this: “To explain the meaning of Venerable Subhūti’s teaching as I understand it, even bodhisattvas are free of arising. That said, Subhūti, if even bodhisattvas are free of arising, then how can they go about life performing difficult noble deeds? How are they able to subject themselves to those sufferings, to which they subject themselves for the sake of beings?”According to Ratnākara (F.50.a), the “sufferings” here refer to giving their hands, feet, heads, and bodies for the sake of sentient beings. RK: yāni duḥkhāni karacaraṇa­śiraḥśarīradānāni sattvānāṃ kṛtaśaḥ sattvānām arthāya tāni vā pratyanubhavituṃ kasmād utsahate. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 160 and Aii, p. 155.

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the venerable Śāriputra, “I do not hold that a bodhisattva great being is going about life performing difficult noble deeds, or that a bodhisattva great being is someone who goes about life with a conception of any noble deeds as difficult. Why not? To explain, it is not possible to bring about the benefit of immeasurable and innumerable beings after developing the conception of noble deeds as difficult. Rather, it is only after developing the conception of them as easy. That is to say, it is only after developing the conception of all beings‍—male or female‍—as mothers, fathers, daughters, or sons that a bodhisattva great being goes about life performing the noble deeds of a bodhisattva. Based on that, a bodhisattva great being should develop the conception of all beings as mothers, fathers, sons, and daughters until finally he or she develops the conception of them as his or her very own self, thinking, ‘Just as I must liberate myself from all sufferings by absolutely every means and in absolutely every way, just so I must liberate all sentient beings from all sufferings by absolutely every means and in absolutely every way. Likewise, I must develop this conception with respect to all sentient beings and I shall not desert any of them. I must liberate all these beings from immeasurable amounts of sufferings and I must not develop a mindset that is hostile toward them, even if I am being cut to pieces a hundred times over!’

“In precisely this way, a bodhisattva great being should develop the proper mindset. Furthermore, if he or she comes to abide with a mindset like this, then he or she will not abide with a conception of noble deeds as difficult.

“Furthermore, a bodhisattva great being should develop the proper mindset, thinking, ‘Just as a self is neither found nor mentally perceived anywhere by any means or in any manner, every thing is neither found nor mentally perceived anywhere by any means or in any manner.’ In this way, a bodhisattva should develop the conception of all internal and external things. If he or she goes about life with a mindset like this, then he or she will neither go about life conceiving of noble deeds as difficult, nor abide conceiving of noble deeds as difficult. As Venerable Śāriputra has also said, ‘A bodhisattva is free of arising.’ It is so, Venerable Śāriputra, it is so. A bodhisattva is free of arising.”From this point until the end of the chapter, the repartee reads like an extended tongue twister that playfully challenges the reader not to get lost. The Sanskrit text conspicuously even leaves off the names of the speakers, i.e., Subhūti and Śāriputra, seemingly in order to make it difficult to keep track of who is saying which line when. In accordance with the Tibetan, however, the translation here has included the names of each speaker to avoid increasing the confusion.

Śāriputra: “But, Venerable Subhūti, is just a bodhisattva free of arising, or are the qualities of a bodhisattva free of arising too?”This passage plays on the ambiguity of the multivalent Sanskrit word dharma and the elusive Sanskrit words anutpāda (“free of arising,” “not arising,” “nonarising quality,” “a thing that does not arise”) and anutpanna (“free of having arisen,” “not arisen,” “unarisen quality,” “a thing that has not arisen”). To get the gist of the play on words in this section, it helps to understand three intentional ambiguities in the passage. First, the passage plays on the word dharma, which can refer either to the real thing, the characteristics, or the qualities that collectively constitute something, such as a bodhisattva (in compounds like bodhisattvadharma). So, keep this in mind when the translation reads either “qualities” or “thing.” Second, in a realist framework, if a thing or quality is said not to arise (i.e., not to come into existence), then it must be either (a) an imaginary thing that is not observably real and never will be (e.g., the horns of a rabbit) or (b) a real thing that is permanent and unconditioned (e.g., space). But, in Ratnākara’s (F.51.a–b) extensive comments on this section (which will not be translated here), he suggests that when Subhūti says “free of arising” (anutpāda), he is referring to the actual nature (dharmatā) and source of things (dharmadhātu), which we might call a space of illumination in which and out of which all things‍—from an ordinary person’s physical form and its qualities up through a buddha’s state of complete knowing and its qualities‍—appear. In other words, Subhūti is implying something that does not fit within the above dichotomy of the realist framework. On the one hand, Subhūti uses “free of arising” to mean that all so-called things (conceived as real by ordinary beings) are actually imaginary. On the other hand, “free of arising” refers to their ultimate reality as the so-called nature or source of all things. Meanwhile, Śāriputra starts from the realist perspective and only step by step comes to understand the sense in which Subhūti uses the term “free of arising.” So, there is some humor intended because Śāriputra seems to be asking which of the two realist meanings Subhūti intends, but actually Subhūti’s answers are spoken from the perspective of what we might call a third meaning beyond the realist dichotomy. Third, since a realist might understand a bodhisattva and so on as not being ultimately real‍—even while they still consider the irreducible parts of the bodhisattva to be real things (dharma)‍—the distinction between the nonarising of a bodhisattva, and so on, and the nonarising of the qualities serves as a further point of misunderstanding in their dialogue, which the commentators Ratnākara and Hari must sort out blow by blow from the perspectives of their own philosophical schools.
Subhūti: “Venerable Śāriputra, the qualities of a bodhisattva too are free of arising.”
Śāriputra: “But, Venerable Subhūti, are just the qualities of a bodhisattva free of arising, or is the state of complete knowing free of arising too?”
Subhūti: “Venerable Śāriputra, the state of complete knowing too is free of arising.”
Śāriputra: “But, Venerable Subhūti, is just the state of complete knowing free of arising, or are the qualities of the state of complete knowing free of arising too?”
Subhūti: “Venerable Śāriputra, the qualities of the state of complete knowing too are free of arising.”
Śāriputra: “But, are just the qualities of the state of complete knowing free of arising, or is an ordinary person free of arising too?”
Subhūti: “Venerable Śāriputra, an ordinary person too is free of arising.”
Śāriputra: “But is just an ordinary person free of arising, or are the qualities of an ordinary person free of arising too?”
