Subhūti: “Venerable Śāriputra, the qualities of an ordinary person too are free of arising.”

At this, the venerable Śāriputra said to the venerable Subhūti, “O Venerable Subhūti, if even a bodhisattva is something free of arising, if even the qualities of a bodhisattva are free of arising, if even the state of complete knowing is free of arising, if even the qualities of the state of complete knowing are free of arising, and if even an ordinary person and the qualities of an ordinary person are free of arising, then certainly, Venerable Subhūti, a bodhisattva has already attained the state of complete knowing.”Here, the implication of Śāriptutra’s question is that if the transcendent state of wisdom entails seeing both ordinary beings and buddhas as “free of arising” equally, then there would be no difference between ordinary beings and buddhas, and the logical consequence would be that budding bodhisattvas (who are ordinary persons) would already be buddhas.

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the venerable Śāriputra, “O Venerable Śāriputra, I am asserting neither the attainment nor the realization of a thing that has not arisen. Furthermore, a thing that has not arisen does not attain an attainment that has not arisen.”

Śāriputra: “Then, Venerable Subhūti, is it the case that an attainment that has not arisen is attained by a thing that has not arisen? Or, is it the case that an attainment that has not arisen is attained by a thing that has arisen?”
Subhūti: “Venerable Śāriputra, has a thing that has not arisen, arisen? Or has a thing that has not arisen simply not arisen?”

Śāriputra: “But, Venerable Subhūti, is a thing that just arises free of arising? Or, is a thing free of arising something that arises?”According to Hari (122), Śāriptutra’s question intends to object as follows: Is your (i.e., Subhūti’s) cross-examination (of me, Śāriputra) about the different stages of something not arising based on the relative or absolute truth? Hari: nanu saṃvṛti­paramārtha­satyā­śrayeṇotpādānutpāda­vyavasthāyāṃ tavāpy eṣa paryanuyoga ity abhiprāyeṇāha : utpāda eva dharmo ’nutpāda utāḥo ’nutpādo dharma utpāda iti. According to Ratnākara (F.52.a), the two alternatives problematize the moment where the dependent nature of mind is transformed into the actual nature (pariniṣpanna) of mind, i.e., the complete knowing of everything. The first alternative asks, “Is that ‘thing that arises’ (utpāda)‍—referring here to the dependent nature of mind‍—subsequently ‘free of arising’ insofar as its not arising is because of the nonexistence of a cause (i.e., ignorance)?” The second alternative (pakṣa) asks, “Or, is a ‘thing that does not arise’‍—namely the state of complete knowing‍—‘something that arises’ right afterward?” As for the first alternative, how could there be an attainment of the state of knowing of all aspects that has not arisen? As for the second alternative, the cause of the state of complete knowing of all aspects would require further explanation, and the imagination of the unreal (viz. the dependent nature) would be permanent (śāśvata), since it would not be destroyed (in the awakening process)‍—this is the idea here. RK: āhe ti śāriputra āha| kiṃ punar ityādi| utpadyata ity utpādaḥ paratantraḥ svabhāvaḥ| kiṃ punaḥ sa paścād anutpādaḥ? kāraṇābhāvād anutpatter ity ekaḥ pakṣaḥ| utāho ’ nutpādaḥ sarvākārajñatākhyo dharmaḥ pūrvam anutpannatvād sa paścād utpadyata iti dvitīyapakṣaḥ| tatrādye pakṣe ’nutpannāyāḥ sarvākārajñatāyāḥ kutaḥ prāptiḥ? dvitīyapakṣe sarvākārajñatāyāḥ kāraṇaṃ vaktavyam| abhūtaparikalpaś ca śāśvato bhavet kṣayābhāvād iti bhāvaḥ. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 166 and Aii, p. 162. Note: The translation of the final phrase “something that arises” here follows the Skt manuscripts and commentaries by Hari and Ratnākara, rather than the negation “something that does not arise” (mi skye ba) found in the Tibetan versions.

Subhūti: “Venerable Śāriputra, it is not illuminating for you to utter, ‘Is a thing free of arising something that arises?’ ”According to Ratnākara (F.52.b), Subhūti is expressing an objection. Speaking of a distinction between the state of complete knowing as a thing arising and the imagination of the unreal as a thing free of arising is “not” being illuminating (pratibhāti), i.e., does not shed light (rocate). The state of complete knowing is nothing but the cessation of all errors in the stream of consciousness, when it has as its focus the emptiness of all things. Thus, the idea is that one does not need to specify the cause of that [state of knowing all aspects] nor is it the case that the imagination of the unreal would be permanent. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 166 and Aii, p. 162.
Śāriputra: “Venerable Subhūti, is it illuminating for you to speak of a thing that is even free of arising?”

Subhūti: “Venerable Śāriputra, the utterance is simply something free of arising. Venerable Śāriputra, precisely what is free of arising is illuminating. Venerable Śāriputra, the illumination is simply free of arising. In this way, it is absolutely illuminating.”These five sentences involve a subtle play on words. According to Ratnākara (F.52.b), in the first sentence, “an utterance” refers to words conceived in the mind. In the second, the thing expressed by this utterance is “clear.” In the third, “illumination” refers to an understanding along with its utterances. The idea is that these three are “simply not arising” in the state of complete knowing, because all things appear in it in the form of the true nature itself. The last sentence summarizes this with the words “absolutely illuminating.” RK: jalpa iti mānasaḥ śabdaḥ| pratibhātī ti jalpābhidheyaṃ vastu| pratibhānam iti sajalpā buddhiḥ| trayam etad anutpāda eva sarvākārajñatāyāṃ dharmatāśarīreṇaiva sarvadharmāṇāṃ tasyāṃ pratibhāsād iti bhāvaḥ| evam evātyantaṃ pratibhātī ty upasaṃhāra. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 166 and Aii, p. 162.

At this, Śāriputra said this: “The venerable Subhuti should be considered the foremost among Dharma teachers. Why so? Because the elder Venerable Subhuti comes through on every topic that one fully pursues one’s own questions about. And yet, he does not deviate from the true nature of things, nor does he go against that true nature of things.”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the venerable Śāriputra, “O Venerable Śāriputra, this is the true nature of things for the Blessed One’s disciples who are free of depending on things. On whatever topic they are cross-examined, they come out on top; they do not go against the true nature of things nor do they depart from the true nature of things. Why is that? Because, evidently, no things are depended upon.”

At this, the venerable Śāriputra said to the venerable Subhūti, “Excellent, Venerable Subhuti, excellent. Which of the bodhisattva great beings’ [six] transcendent states does not depend on any things?”

Subhuti replied, “O Venerable Śāriputra, only the transcendent state of wisdom, which is common to all the vehicles, is the transcendent state that does not depend on any things, because it is free from depending on any things.According to Ratnākara (F.53.a), the phrase “common to all vehicles” (Skt. sārvayānikī) means common to the three vehicles. RK: sārvayānikī ti yānatrayasādhāraṇī. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 168. According to Hari (124), the phrase “common to all vehicles” means that the transcendent state of wisdom is included in the three vehicles as the perfection of all the levels. Hari: yānatrayasaṃgṛhītā sārvayānikī sarvabhūmipāramitā. Note: The Tibetan version suggests the transcendent state of wisdom is “all vehicles” (theg pa thams cad) instead of being common to them all. This translation follows the commentators. Therefore, when this profound transcendent state of wisdom is being described, taught, and expounded in this way, a bodhisattva great being‍—whose mindset is not despondent, worried, confused, or sidetracked‍—should be known as a bodhisattva great being who is abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom and is never lacking that attentiveness.”

Śāriputra then asked the venerable Subhūti, “O Venerable Subhūti, how could a bodhisattva great being who is abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom never be lacking attentiveness? For if a bodhisattva great being were never lacking attentiveness, Venerable Subhūti, then he or she would be lacking the abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom. Conversely, Venerable Subhūti, if a bodhisattva great being were never lacking the abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom, then he or she would be lacking attentiveness. For, Venerable Subhūti, if it were the case that a bodhisattva great being both never lacked attentiveness and also never lacked the abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom, then no sentient beings would ever lack the abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom. Why? Because all sentient beings would abide without lacking attentiveness.”According to Ratnākara (F.53.a), from Śāriputra’s question‍—namely “O Venerable Subhūti, how could a bodhisattva…”‍—to the end of this paragraph is an objection to what he perceives Subhūti as asserting. It arises from Śāriputra considering “attentiveness” to be a conceptualization.

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the venerable Śāriputra, “Excellent, Venerable Śāriputra, excellent. But still, I must criticize you, Venerable Śāriputra, for having apprehended the topic just asserted as referring to a real thing. Why is that? Because, Venerable Śāriputra, it must be recognized that, insofar as a sentient being has no nature, attentiveness has no nature.According to Ratnākara (F.53.b), “sentient being” here refers to a self (ātman)‍—an example to illustrate the topic. “Attentiveness” here refers to a conceptualization‍—the topic that the example is illustrating here. RK: sattvam ātmā | sa iha dṛṣṭāntaḥ| manasikāro vikalpaḥ| sa dārṣṭāntikaḥ. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 169. Venerable Śāriputra, it must be recognized that, insofar as a sentient being has no real existence, attentiveness has no real existence. Venerable Śāriputra, it must be recognized that, insofar as a sentient being is void, attentiveness is void. Venerable Śāriputra, it must be recognized that insofar as a sentient being is not conceivable, attentiveness is not conceivable. Venerable Śāriputra, it must be recognized that, insofar as a sentient being is not an object of awakened understanding, attentiveness is not an object of awakened understanding.According to Ratnākara (F.53.b), “not an object of accurate understanding” means not the scope of perfect knowing. The “accurate understanding that an object is not in accordance with reality” (ayathābhūtārtha) is a phrase that refers to someone (i.e., a bodhisattva) who has perfect knowing of what is not in accordance with reality, i.e., that it is a false thing. RK: anabhisaṃbodhanatā samyagjñānāviṣayatā| ayathābhūtārtha tvena mithyāvastutvenābhisaṃbodhanaṃ saṃyagjñānam asye ti ayathābhutārthābhi­saṃbodhanatā. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 169. According to Hari (127), in the first sentence, “a sentient being is not an object of understanding” refers to the nonperception of a sentient being. In the next sentence, “a sentient being has the accurate understanding that an object is not in accordance with reality” means “accurate understanding” that a sentient being is “not in accord with reality” (i.e., it is false) Hari: sattvasyā pratipattir anabhisambodhanatā sattvānabhisambodhanatā. sattvo ’yathābhūtārtho ’līka ity abhisambodhanatā sattvā­yathābhūtārthābhi­sambodhanatā. Note: the translation of these two sentences follow the Sanskrit, Ratnākara, and Hari, which differ from the Tibetan’s opaque formulation: “Venerable Śāriputra, since a sentient being is not perfectly and completely awakened, attentiveness is not perfectly and completely awakened…” Tib: tshe dang ldan pa shA ri’i bu sems can mngon par rdzogs par byang ma chub pas na yid la byed pa mngon par rdzogs par byang ma chub par rig par bya’o… Venerable Śāriputra, it must be recognized that, insofar as the bodhisattva great being’s awakened understanding is that a sentient being is an object not in accordance with reality, his or her attentiveness involves the awakened understanding that an object is not in accordance with reality.Hari (126–27) and Ratnākara (F.53.b) give different, somewhat complicated commentaries about the logic of the back and forth between Śāriputra and Subhūti in these last three paragraphs about attentiveness. Their interesting explanations cannot be entirely conveyed here. But, in essence, they both seem to be suggesting that Śāriputra objects to Subhūti’s assertions about attentiveness, because he does not recognize that Subhūti is using the phrase “this attentiveness” to refer to a specific type of attentiveness, which differs from the usage of the term by common people and by schools connected with the Disciple Vehicle. In the back and forth of this passage, Śāriputra first assumes that attentiveness literally refers to being focused on a real thing separate from the mind and involves conceptualizing what to be attentive about. Then, based on Śāriputra’s narrow understanding of the term “attentiveness,” he asks a question designed to show that a bodhisattva’s transcendent state of wisdom‍—which was just described as not dependent on any things‍—cannot be described either as “never lacking attentiveness” (i.e., to real, conceptualized things), because these two descriptions are mutually exclusive. In response, Subhūti congratulates him for seeing that the transcendent state of wisdom and attentiveness (as Śāriputra construes the term) would indeed be mutually exclusive. However, Subhūti reproaches Śāriputra, essentially, for misunderstanding what Subhūti intended with the term “attentiveness” in this context. In brief, the particular kind of attentiveness, which Subhūti is asserting, is constantly attentive to the unreality of all things. Thus, in the common usage the word “attentiveness” assumed by Śāriputra, one would have to say that the bodhisattva is never attentive to things that are simply conceptualized (viz. imagined) to be real. But in Subhūti’s usage of the term “attentiveness” (i.e., attentive to things’ unreality), a bodhisattva in the transcendent state of wisdom is “never lacking attentiveness,” because the transcendent state of wisdom involves always seeing things as empty without being distracted from their unreality. So, Śāriptura’s objection is correct only insofar as common usage is concerned, but not with respect to the intended meaning of Subhūti’s words above. Interestingly, elsewhere, practicing this kind of “attentiveness” (manasikāra, i.e., to unreality) is also referred to as practicing “nonattentiveness” (amanaskāra or amanasikāra), in which one ceases to attend to whatever arises in a purely conceptual mindset. Cf. Hari (127) kathaṃ punar amanaskāra eva manaskāro na tu yathāruta…. So, Venerable Śāriputra, I am asserting that a bodhisattva great being abides in the state of this kind of attentiveness.”According to Ratnākara (F.53.b), “this kind of attentiveness” refers to the type that investigates what really exists as explained above. The word “state” (vihāra) here indicates that it is a form of tranquility meditation (śamatha). RK: anene ti yathoktena| evaṃrūpeṇe ti bhūtārthapratyavekṣakeṇa| vihāreṇe ti śamatharūpeṇa. For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 169.
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Going About Life in the Knowing of All Aspects,” the first chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 2: For ŚakraThe beginning of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 14 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 22 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: At that time, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, alighted in that very assembly and took his seat. He was flanked by his retinue of forty thousand gods from his Heaven of the Thirty-Three.Śakra (lit. “mighty”) is another title for Indra (lit. “powerful”), and the Buddha and others when directly addressing him use his personal name Kauśika. The Heaven of Thirty-Three is part of the desire realm. In this paragraph, the epithets have not been capitalized, and have been translated freely. He was further accompanied by the Four Guardians of the World together with the twenty thousand gods in the retinue of those Four Great Kings.Ratnākara (F.54, Jaini 21) and Hari (129, Sparham II–III, p. 71) add the names of the “Four Guardians of the World,” who are the same as the Four Great Kings and, as the lowest category of gods in the desire realm, are each associated with one of the four directions: Vaiśravaṇa (also called Kubera), Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Virūḍhaka, and Virūpākṣa. Note: Here, the number “forty thousand” (bzhi khri) is based on Tibetan, rather than on the Sanskrit “four hundred thousand” (catvāriṃśatā). RK: catvāro mahārājā vaiśravaṇadhṛtarāṣṭravirūḍhakavirūpākṣā caturādiṣu dikṣu| teṣāṃ kāyaḥ| tasmin bhavāḥ. Hari: kuveradhṛtarāṣṭravirūpākṣavirūḍhakāś catvāro lokapālāḥ. Also, Brahmā, the lord of this world system, alighted among that assembly and took his seat surrounded by his retinue of ten thousand gods and the five thousand gods of the Śuddhāvāsa heavens.According to Ratnākara (F.54), Brahmā is the lord (pati) over the billionfold world system (sahā) and the Śuddhāvāsa heavens (“Pure Abodes”) in the form realm have as foundation the fourth meditation (dhyāna). They are called “Pure Abodes” because only noble ones are born in their five heavens, which (in ascending order) are called Avṛha, Atapa, Sudṛśa, Sudarśana, and Akaniṣṭha. The “five thousand” gods in the form realm, which belong to five retinues in these heavens, are attracted by pervasiveness of light and so on. According to Hari (129, Sparham II–III, pp. 71–72), some say Brahmā pervades the fourth meditation, but others say he pervades only the third level of the first meditation. See Sparham II–III, 71–72, for the two different stories about how Brahmā conceitedly (and mistakenly) believes that he is the Creator God (īśvara) of those who are actually born in that realm as a result of their merit. RK: eṣa eva trisāhasro lokadhātuḥ sahā| tasyāḥ patiḥ| brahma ṇaḥ kāy as tasmin bhavāḥ| iha tūttarapadavṛddhiḥ| caturthasya dhyānasyāśrayaṃ ca śuddhāvāsāḥ| avṛhā atapāḥ sudṛśāḥ sudarśanā akaniṣṭhāś ceti| kutaḥ śuddhāvāsāḥ ? āryāṇām eva teṣūtpādāt| teṣāṃ pañcānāṃ nikāyānāṃ pañca iva sahastrāṇi (sic) | etāvanta eta rūpiṇo devanikāyā yathodhvam (sic) utkṛṣṭāḥ prabhāprabhāvādibhiḥ| Hari: sahe lokadhātāv ā samantāt patiḥ sahāpatir nāyako devaputraḥ brahmā pi caturthadhyāna­prabhavaḥ. saṃvartanyaḥ punas tisro bhavanty agnyambuvāyubhir | iti nyāyād yathākramaṃ prathamādi­dhyānatrayasya vināśenatasyaiva cirasthāyitayā patitvābhimānād ity eke. apare tv anyathānyatara­sattva­syābhāsvara­devanikāyāc cyutvaikākinaḥ śūnye brāhme vimāne saṃutpannasya aho vatānye ’pi sattvā ihopapadyerann iti praṇi­dhānānantaraṃ puṇyādikṣayād apareṣāṃ kākatālīyanyāyena tatra samutpāde ’ham eṣām īśvara ityādy abhimānāt pratha­madhyānatṛtīya­bhūmiko brahmā sahāpatir iti varṇayanti. Yet all their splendor, which arose from the maturation of the gods’ own former karmic deeds, was instantly outshone by the Buddha’s miraculous power, the Buddha’s majesty, and his blessings.According to Ratnākara (F.54, Jaini 21), the word “outshined” (abhibhūta) means that their light was “turned dark” (dhyāmikṛta). This happens at precisely the same time (as implied by the Sanskrit words in the sentence), since his manifestation of the proper mindset is capable of making those worldly benefits seem dark by comparison. The Blessed One is known by the epithet Outshiner of All, because of his manifestation of love in that mindset. The word “splendor” (avabhāsaḥ)‍—which refers to the halo born from the maturation of their own former actions‍—is something innate, not something they magically emanate (abhisaṃskārika). According to Hari (130, Sparham II–III, p. 72), “the maturation of their own former actions” means what emerges from the imprints of their own positive actions. The “splendor” is a light with beams. These gods are outshone “by his blessings” (adhiṣṭhāna), i.e., by the Buddha’s presence, and “by the Buddha’s majesty” (tejas), i.e., by means of the Blessed Buddha’s natural halo. Even though they themselves have great miraculous power, the idea is that all powers are bedazzled “by the Buddha’s miraculous power” (anubhāva). RK: yo ’pi teṣāṃ avabhāsaḥ so ’py abhibhūto dhyāmīkṛto ’bhūt| samakālam eveti yo ’pi so ’pi śabdārthaḥ| yogyatā hi cittotpādas tadarthadhyāmīkaraṇam| sarvābhibhūr bhagavān iti viditvā tadbhāve spṛhotpādanāt| avabhāsaḥ prabhā| svakarmaṇo vipaktir vipākaḥ| tasmāj jātaḥ sahajo nābhisaṃskārika ity arthaḥ| kena kāraṇenābhibhūtaḥ ? buddhādhiṣṭhānena buddhasannidhānena| kena kartrā? buddhatejasā buddhasya bhagavataḥ prakṛtiprabhayā| nanu te ’pi mahānubhāvāḥ kasmān na praticakrur ity ata āha buddhānubhāvene ti| buddhānubhāvena sarvānubhāvaḥ pratihanyataḥ iti bhāvaḥ. Hari: svakarmavipākajaḥ svaśubhakarmavāsananiryāto ’vabhāso raśmyālokaḥ.

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the elder, the venerable Subhūti, “Noble Subhūti, these many thousands of gods, who have alighted among this assembly and taken their seats in your presence, want to listen to the bodhisattva great beings’ transcendent state of wisdom‍—to the expounding of it, to the instruction in it, and to the guidance in it.According to Ratnākara (F.55.a, Jaini 21–22), the phrase “bodhisattva great beings’ transcendent state of wisdom” indicates the beginning of his explanation of the transcendent state of wisdom as the so-called “knowing of the paths” (mārgajñatā; lam mkhyen pa). The phrase “instruction in it” means the explanation of how to realize its goal, and the phrase “guidance in it” means the accompanying teaching on how to pursue that. RK: atha khalv ityādi| antikād iti sakāśāt| prajñāpāramitām iti bodhisatvānāṃ mahāsatvānām ity anena sambandhaḥ| mārgajñatām ity arthaḥ| upadiśyata iti upadeśaḥ| tasyā evārthasyāvagamāya vādo ’vavādaḥ tadanubandhāya śāsanī tatsahitā seti samāsaḥ. So, how should a bodhisattva great being dwell in that transcendent state of wisdom, train in it, and engage in meditation in it?”According to Ratnākara (F.55.a, Jaini 22), a bodhisattva great being should “dwell in” it through the wisdom of having learned it, “train in it” through the wisdom of reflecting upon it, and “engage in meditation in it” through the wisdom of cultivating it. RK: sthātavyaṃ śrutamayyā prajñayā| śikṣitavyaṃ cintāmayyā| yogam āpattavyaṃ bhāvanāmayyā.

The elder, the venerable Subhūti, said, “Kauśika, through the Buddha’s miraculous power, the Buddha’s majesty, and the Buddha’s blessings, I will expound it to you. Those gods who have not developed the proper mindset for supreme, perfect awakening can and should develop it. However, those who have already entered an irreversible state of perfection are not able to develop the proper mindset for supreme, perfect awakening.Subhūti’s remarks here apply, specifically, to the gods from the realms of the Pure Abode, who were born there because they had reached the stage of a non-returner in their last lifetime. That is to say, in the Disciple Vehicle, when a practitioner reaches any of the four irreversible stages that culminate in the complete cessation of rebirth, they are called having “entered an irreversible state of perfection” (samyaktvaniyāma) in the sense that they (now) have a fixed number of rebirths left before attaining the “irreversible state of perfection,” i.e., the nirvāṇa reached through the disciple path. In sequential order from lowest to highest, the four stages of disciple liberation: (1) a “stream enterer”‍—someone who is transformed beyond regressing from the liberation from cyclic existence‍—will be reborn in cyclic existence only seven more times (at most) before reaching nirvāṇa; (2) a “once-returner” will be reborn in cyclic existence one more time before reaching nirvāṇa; (3) a non-returner will never again be reborn in cyclic existence but will be born in one of the five heavenly Śuddhāvāsa form realms from where they will directly reach nirvāṇa; and (4) an arhat (“worthy one”)‍—etymologized in Sanskrit and Tibetan as someone who has completely conquered his enemy, i.e., overcome his defilements‍—will never be reborn anywhere and attains liberation instantly after death. So, Subhūti’s remark is addressing those among the gods who do not appear to qualify for Mahāyāna practice. Cf. Ratnākara (F.55.a, Jaini 22): yetv ityādi| hīnamārgalabhyaṃ nirvāṇaṃ samyaktvam tasmin niyamaḥ samyaktvaniyāmaḥ| taṃ avakrāntāḥ prāptāḥ. Why is that? It is because, insofar as they have closed themselves off from the stream of cyclic existence, they are unable to develop the proper mindset for supreme, perfect awakening, which leads them into cyclic existence again and again.According to Ratnākara (F.55.a–b, Jaini 22), those who “have closed themselves off” are bound by certain limitations (on the disciples’ path). For instance, they might be either a so-called stream enterer destined for arhathood in seven lifetimes or just go from birth to birth for a few more lifetimes. Either way, they are unable to develop the proper mindset because that mindset of awakening requires one to remain in cyclic existence to do the practices that cause one to reach supreme, perfect awakening. This paragraph explains the limitations of the scope of what is possible, because disciples on the so-called narrow path to peace never even wish for ultimate awakening, and hence are closed off from it. RK: baddhasīmāno baddhasetavaḥ| tadyathā śrotaāpanna­saptakṛdbhava­paramaḥ| dvitrijanmā kulaṃkulaḥ| anuttara­samyak­saṃbodhi­nimittaṃ yat punaḥ punaḥ saṃsaraṇaṃ tasmai cittam utpādāyitum abhavyāḥ| iti śamaikayānaiḥ śrāvakair uttamabodheḥ kadācid apy akāmanād yogyatāyā viṣayapratiniyamaḥ. But if even they were somehow to develop the proper mindset for supreme, perfect awakening, I would rejoice for them too, and I would never discount their root of virtue, since they too are able to acquire the most distinguished qualities among the distinguished qualities.”Both Ratnākara and Hari interpret the “the most distinguished qualities” to refer to the bodhisattva’s qualities of the mindset of awakening (bodhicitta) and so on. But Ratnākara, who holds the so-called “three-vehicle” (triyāna) theory, and Hari, who holds the so-called “single-vehicle” (ekayāna) theory, diverge in how they explain why Subhūti might seem to contradict himself about whether disciples, who are destined for complete cessation, can develop this mindset of awakening. For his part, Ratnākara’s “three-vehicle” theory generally follows the early Yogācāra position that the three distinct vehicles‍—i.e., the Disciple, Solitary Realizer, and Bodhisattva Vehicles‍—can only function to lead disciples to mutually exclusive terminal destinations. In this theory, Buddhist disciples are said to possess one of four types of dispositions or lineages (gotra; rigs), namely being either (a) fixed upon the state of a disciple, (b) fixed upon the state of a solitary realizer, (c) fixed upon buddhahood, or (d) not fixed in any one of the three types of awakening. Thus, Ratnākara (F.55.b) simply interprets this sentence, “But, if even they….I would not discount…,” as applying to the fourth type here, i.e., those disciples who are generally inclined toward awakening, but whose disposition is open to developing the proper mindset for supreme awakening through the bodhisattva path. RK: api tv ityādi utpādayerann ityetadantena vyāptiḥ| śrāvakair api bodhipariṇatikaiḥ samyaksambodhau cittasyotpādanāt| te hi svāṃ bodhim adhigamyāpi buddha­bodhi­sattvānāṃ lokottarāṃ vibhūtiṃ dṛṣṭvā vañcitam ivātmānaṃ manyamānās tathāgatair adhiṣṭhitās tad bodhau cittam utpādyācalyabhūmika­bodhi­sattvavan nirmitābhir upapattibhir bodhicaryāṃ caritvā parāṃ bodhim adhigacchantīti vyāptiḥ. For his part, Hari (132–35, Sparham II–III, pp. 73–78) interprets this passage as affirming his school’s “single-vehicle” (ekayāna) theory, i.e., that the Buddha taught three distinct vehicles as a provisional measure to attract disciples with different propensities, but in actuality he knew that they would all eventually end up entering the single definitive vehicle of the Mahāyāna. In his lengthy discourse here, Hari cites as support for his view other Mahāyāna scriptures, such as The White Lotus of the Good Dharma (Saddharmapuṇḍarīka, Toh 113), The Ten Bhūmis (Daśabhūmika, Toh 44-31), and The Sūtra on the Descent into Laṅka (Laṅkāvatāra, Toh 107). Hari: api tv ityādi… ity alaṃ prasaṅgena. Note: The translation “able to acquire” (adhyālambitavyāḥ) follows Ratnākara’s gloss (prāptavyaḥ), instead of the Tibetan, which suggests more “able to apprehend” (lhag par dmigs par bya).
Then the Blessed One said to the elder, the venerable Subhūti, “Excellent, Subhūti, excellent. You, Subhūti, are indeed excellent. You are inspiring bodhisattva great beings.”

Then the elder, the venerable Subhūti, said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, we must express gratitude for the kind deeds done on behalf of the Blessed One and not fail to express gratitude for them. What motivates me to say this? Previously, Blessed One, in the presence of former worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, the Blessed One had just become a bodhisattva and was going about his life engaged in pure spiritual practiceRatnākara (F.56.a) explains that the “pure” (brahma) refers here to nirvāṇa, so it basically means going about living life as a bodhisattva for the sake of awakening. RK: brahma nirvāṇaṃ tasmai caraṇaṃ brahmacaryam| taccaraṇaṃ bodhāye ti bodhaye| bodhicaryāṃ caratīti yāvat| yair iti vibhakti­pratirūpako nipāto yathārthaḥ. According to Hari (135), “engaged in pure spiritual practice “ means desisting from unchaste behavior. It means “having virtuous moral conduct, studying (the teachings), contemplating them, and (through meditation and other means) applying oneself toward cultivating them.” With these words, he is speaking about practicing the five lay precepts and so on as much as possible. Hari: brahmacaryam abrahmacaryād viratiḥ. vṛttasthaḥ śrutacintāvān bhāvanāyāṃ prayujyate | ity anena yathāsambhavaṃ pañca­śikṣāpadādikatvaṃ kathayati. in order to realize awakening for our sake.According to Hari (135), “for our sake” means done deliberately for us. The idea here is that it was based on the turning of the Dharma wheel that preceded the Disciple Vehicle. Hari: asmadarthe ’smākaṃ prayojanena kṛtenety arthaḥ. śrāvakayānapuraḥsarasya dharmacakrasya pravartanād iti matiḥ. Certain disciples instructed and guided him in the transcendent states, and by going about his life in those, the Blessed One developed the unsurpassed knowing. Similarly, Blessed One, it is also incumbent upon us disciples to lend help to bodhisattva great beings, to take care of them, to assist them, and to support them. Why is that? It is because those bodhisattva great beings whom we help, take care of, assist, and support will quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”

Then the elder, the venerable Subhūti, said to Śākra, powerful lord of the gods, “So, Kauśika, listen well and pay good attention. I will tell to you the way that a bodhisattva great being should dwell in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.56.a), to “dwell in the transcendent state of wisdom” means to be aware in the state of knowing it as the path. RK: prajñāpāramitāyāṃ sthātavyam iti mārgajñatāyāṃ veditavyam. Kauśika, a bodhisattva great being should dwell in the transcendent state of wisdom by means of dwelling in emptiness, since, Kauśika, a bodhisattva great being has donned that great armor.In the following, Subhūti demonstrates the way that “dwelling in the transcendent state of wisdom” effectively means “not dwelling” on any “thing.” Subhūti’s negation of each “thing” one by one (here and elsewhere) can be understood as performatively describing a certain kind of meditation on emptiness.
“That is, a bodhisattva great being should not dwell on physical forms. Likewise, he or she should not dwell on feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness.

“He or she should not dwell on the eyes. He or she should not dwell on visible forms. He or she should not dwell on visual consciousness. He or she should not dwell on contact with them through the eyes.Here, “contact” refers to the mental process whereby the mental faculty, object, and consciousness meet, as described in the Abhidharma. He or she should not dwell on the feelings born from that contact through the eyes. Likewise, he or she should not dwell on the other organs, namely, the ears, nose, mouth, body, or mental organ. Nor should he or she dwell on sounds, scents, tastes, tangibles, or [mental] phenomena. Nor should he or she dwell on aural consciousness up to mental consciousness, on contact through the mental organ, or on the feelings born from contact through the mental organ.
“He or she should not dwell on the earth element, the water element, the fire element, the wind element, the space element, or the element of consciousness.

“He or she should not dwell on the applications of mindfulness.The four “applications of mindfulness” refer to the means of attending to mindfulness of four meditative objects, namely body, feelings, mind, and phenomena. According to the Madhyāntavibhāgabhāṣya (50), cultivating these four objects causes one to penetrate the four noble truths, i.e., mindfulness of body reveals the truth of suffering, mindfulness of feelings reveals the truth of the cause of suffering, mindfulness of mind reveals the truth of the cessation, and mindfulness of phenomena reveals the truth of the path. Here, the translation “applications” (upasthāna) has been adopted because it is so widespread, but the Sanskrit term upasthāna (literally meaning “standing near”) can be interpreted as either (a) a means of “standing near” or “attendance” of mindfulness (e.g., explained by Yaśomitra’s AKBh-Vyākhyā (53): vigrahas tv evaṃ kartavyaḥ. upatiṣṭhate ’nenety upasthānaṃ. smṛter upasthānaṃ smṛtypasthānam iti), i.e., close application of mindfulness or (b) “the thing that is stood near” or “the thing attended to” (e.g., explained by Ratnākara), i.e., the nearby abode of mindfulness. According to Ratnākara (F.37.b), “the term smṛtyupasthāna refers to that which, because it is considered, brings about the standing near of mindfulness. There are ‘four’ based on the different kinds of meditative objects.” RK: gang gi rjes su lta bas dran pa nye bar gnas pa de ni dran pa nye bar gzhag pa’o| |dmigs pa’i bye brag gis ni bzhi ste. Note: In this latter sense, one might translate them as the “four immediate dimensions of mindfulness” because body, feelings, mind, and phenomena are all objects immediately available that one can stand near or tune into with mindfulness. Nor should he or she dwell on the correct exertions, the supernormal supports, the faculties, the strengths, the aspects of awakening, or the aspects of the path.This paragraph is referring to sublists within the thirty-seven factors in awakening (bodhipakṣyadharma) that encapsulate the path from first beginning to accumulate (saṃbhāra) merit and wisdom up until the point of buddhahood where training comes to an end (aśaikṣa). In those thirty-seven factors (4+4+4+5+5+7+8= 37), the four applications of mindfulness are as mentioned in the previous note; the four correct exertions are avoiding nonvirtue that has not yet arisen, abandoning nonvirtue that has arisen, adopting virtue that has not yet arisen, and maintaining virtue that has arisen; the four supernormal supports are devoted interest, diligence, attention, and discernment; the five faculties are faith, diligence, mindfulness, concentration, and wisdom; the five strengths are also faith, diligence, mindfulness, concentration, and wisdom, but at a different level; the seven aspects of awakening are mindfulness, wisdom, diligence, joy, relaxation, concentration, and equanimity; and the eight aspects of the path are accurate view, intention, speech, conduct, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, and concentration.

“He or she should not dwell on the fruition of a stream enterer, of a once-returner, of a non-returner, or of being a worthy one.These are the four technical terms that refer to the four stages beyond regressing that culminate in complete liberation according to the Disciple Vehicle. Nor should he or she dwell on being a solitary realizer. Nor should he or she dwell on being a buddha.These fruitions arise from the three vehicles for going beyond cyclic existence, namely, (1) the Disciple Vehicle, which leads to the state of a worthy one; (2) the Solitary Realizer Vehicle, which leads to the state of a solitary realizer; and (3) the Bodhisattva Vehicle, which leads to buddhahood.

“This is all said because he or she should not dwell on the notion of physical forms. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of any organ, and so forth, from the eye up to the feelings born from contact through the mental organ. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of any element, from the element of earth up to the element of consciousness.The three sentences in this paragraph reprise the three different ways of categorizing all the same observable things, namely, according to the constituents (skandha), elements (dhātu), or bases (āyatana).

“Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of the applications of mindfulness.In this paragraph, Subhūti uses the word “notion” to express the Buddhist idea of a path, as represented by the thirty-seven factors mentioned above. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of the correct exertions, the supernormal supports, the faculties, the strengths, the aspects of awakening, or the aspects of the path. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of the fruitions of a stream enterer, of a once-returner, of a non-returner, or of being a worthy one. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of being a solitary realizer. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of being a buddha.

“Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of physical forms as either permanent or impermanent.In the following, Subhūti uses the word “notion” to express the central Buddhist doctrinal ideas, known as either the three or the four marks (lakṣaṇa), on which one should not dwell. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness as either permanent or impermanent.

“Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of physical forms as either pleasant or painful. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness as either pleasant or painful.

“Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that physical forms are either empty or not empty. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness is either empty or not empty.

“Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that physical forms are either an identity or not an identity. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness is either an identity or not an identity.

“Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that physical forms are pure or impure. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness is either pure or impure.

“Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that physical forms are mentally perceived as empty.In the Sanskrit phrase rūpaṃ śūnyam upalabhyate veti na sthātavyam, the placement of “or” (vā) is unusual and requires interpretation. The Tibetan has made a double emendation as if reading the Sanskrit rūpaṃ śūnyam iti upalabhyate iti na sthātavyam. In this translation, we have merely eliminated the vā and interpreted it rūpaṃ śūnyam upalabhyate iti na sthātavyam. This translation basically agrees with Tibetan. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness is mentally perceived as empty.

“Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that the fruition of a stream enterer is made prominent by the unconditioned.The phrase “made prominent by the unconditioned” aims to capture the Sanskrit double meaning (hinted at in Hari’s comments below), namely (a) that the fruition is not a conditioned result, but noticeably comes to the fore when the conditioned obscurations cease, and (b) that this fruition, whose nature is not conditioned, is to be respected above all others. According to Ratnākara (F.58.a), the fruition is “made prominent by the unconditioned” because the dissipation of ignorance and defilements is something unconditioned. RK: asaṃskṛtaprabhāvitaṃ kleśāvidyākṣayasyāsaṃskṛtatvāt. According to Hari (143, Sparham II–III, p. 79), the idea behind being “made prominent by the unconditioned” is that, since its inherent nature has, ultimately, not come into existence, the path’s effect is a fruition born from the unconditioned. Subhūti teaches not dwelling on the various fruitions (being made prominent by the unconditioned) in order to counteract habitual fixation on the fruition in general, but the next sentence addresses one particular aspect of it. Hari: asaṃskṛtaprabhāvitam iti tattvato ’nutpāda­svabhāvatvān mārga­syāsaṃskṛtanirjātaṃ phalaṃ kāryam. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that the fruition of a once-returner is made prominent by the unconditioned, that the fruition of a non-returner is made prominent by the unconditioned, or that the state of a worthy one is made prominent by the unconditioned. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that the state of a solitary realizer is made prominent by the unconditioned. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that buddhahood is made prominent by the unconditioned.The last sentence of this paragraph appears in both the Tibetan and the Sanskrit (cf., e.g., the Cambridge MS Add.866, F.14.a.3). But it is incorrectly omitted in the modern Sanskrit editions and printings (Mitra, Wogihara, and Vaidya).

“Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that stream enterers are worthy recipients of meritorious offerings, or that those stream enterers are destined to be reborn no more than seven times.According to Hari (143, Sparham II–III, p. 79), in order to counter any habitual fixation on fruition in a particular context, Subhūti speaks of not dwelling on these beings as “worthy of offerings,” primarily because they are considered worthy of offerings insofar as they are distinguished fields of merit. Hari: viśiṣṭapuṇyakṣetratvena mukhyato dakṣaṇārhatvād (sic -dakṣiṇa Tucci p. 228:5) dakṣiṇīyaḥ. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that once-returners are worthy recipients of meritorious offerings, or that once-returners are not quite fully accomplished, in that they will return to this desire realm one more time before reaching the end of suffering. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that non-returners are worthy recipients of meritorious offerings, or that non-returners, after ceasing to return to this realm, attain nirvāṇa in another realm. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that the worthy ones are worthy recipients of meritorious offerings, or that worthy ones attain complete nirvāṇa within this very life in the nirvāṇa dimension without any constituents remaining.The nirvāṇa dimension is an unconditioned space (beyond the three conditioned realms of existence) in which worthy ones (arhat) are said to abide. The technical terms “with some constituents remaining” (sopadhiśeṣa) and “without any constituents remaining” (anupadhiśeṣa) refer to the two types of arhats, who are distinguished on the basis of whether or not any of the constituents of their experience (upadhi, i.e. skandha) still remain (śeṣa) after they attain nirvāṇa. Cf. Ratnākara (F.58.a): anupadhiśeṣa iti upadhayaḥ skandhāḥ, ta eva śeṣāḥ, sopadhiṣeśe tadviparyayād anupadhiśeṣaḥ. Note: Most early Buddhists suggest that this state “without any constituents remaining” is equivalent to a worthy one’s final liberation at death, but some do seem to suggest that it can refer to the state of freedom from craving in worthy ones who are technically still alive. Cf. BHSD parinirvāṇa, PTSD parinibbana. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that solitary realizers are worthy recipients of meritorious offerings, or that solitary realizers surpass the level of disciples, fail to reach the level of a buddha, and then attain complete nirvāṇa. Nor should he or she dwell on the notion that buddhas are worthy recipients of meritorious offerings, or even on the following notion: ‘A buddha surpasses the level of an ordinary person, surpasses the level of a disciple, and surpasses the level of a solitary realizer. He brings about the benefit of innumerable, immeasurable sentient beings. He causes innumerable quintillions of sentient beings to attain nirvāṇa. He definitively establishes innumerable, immeasurable sentient beings in the state of a disciple, the state of a solitary realizer, and perfect buddhahood. He dwells on the level of a buddha, does the deeds of a buddha, and then attains complete nirvāṇa through the complete nirvāṇa of a buddha while in the nirvāṇa dimension without any constituents remaining.’ ”According to Ratnākara (F.58.a), the “complete nirvāṇa of a buddha” is one which is fixed neither in cyclic existence nor in nirvāṇa, because, respectively, all his defiled qualities are completely destroyed and endless qualities free of worldly influences continue for the sake of beings infinitely. RK: buddha­parinirvāṇe ti| saṃsāranirvāṇayor apratiṣṭhite na| yathākramaṃ sarva­ṣāṃkleśika­dharma­parikṣayāt, anāśravāṇāṃ ca dharmāṇām anantānāṃ jagadarthāya yāvad ākāśam avasthānāt.

Then the venerable Śāriputra was ruminating to himself as follows: “Suppose that a bodhisattva great being does not dwell on this very notion, namely: ‘A buddha surpasses the level of an ordinary person, surpasses the level of a disciple, and surpasses the level of a solitary realizer. He brings about the benefit of innumerable, immeasurable sentient beings. He causes innumerable quintillions of sentient beings to attain nirvāṇa. He definitively establishes innumerable, immeasurable sentient beings in the state of a disciple, the state of a solitary realizer, and the state of a perfect buddha. He dwells on the level of a buddha, does the deeds of a buddha, and then attains complete nirvāṇa through the complete nirvāṇa of a buddha in the nirvāṇa dimension without any constituents remaining.’
“Nonetheless, how should a bodhisattva great being dwell? How should he or she train?”

Just then, through the miraculous power of the Buddha, the venerable Subhūti knew in his mind of the venerable Śāriputra’s mental ruminations and asked the venerable Śariputra, “Venerable Śāriputra, what do you think? Where does the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata dwell?”

The venerable Śariputra replied, “Subhūti, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata does not dwell anywhere. Why is that? Because the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata’s mind is never fixed in any dwelling.The translation “never fixed in any dwelling” (apratiṣṭhita) attempts to capture the slight play on the etymologically connected word “dwelling” (sthita). According to Hari (152, Sparham II–III, p. 100), after he realizes complete awakening, “his mind is never fixed in any dwelling”‍—given that, in absolute reality, things do not appear‍—and thus he does not dwell on anything by means of a mental perception (upalambha). Hari: apratiṣṭhitamānasa ityādi. yasmād bodhim abhisambudhya tattvato dharmāṇām anidarśanād apratiṣṭhitaṃ mana eva mānasam asyety apratiṣṭhita­mānasas tathāgataḥ. tasmān na kvacid upalambhayogena sthita iti pūrveṇa sambandhaḥ. Cf. Apte pratiṣṭhita, set up, fixed, placed, installed. He never dwells in the dimension of the conditioned, or in the dimension of the unconditioned, nor is he somewhere removed from these.”According to Hari (151 ff.), “conditioned” here refers to the realm of desire and so on, whereas “unconditioned” refers to the actual nature (tathatā) and so on. Hari: sa naive tyādi. saṃskṛta iti kāmādike. asaṃskṛta iti tathatādike. According to Ratnākara (F.58.a), “nor is he somewhere removed from these” means that he has not abandoned the attainment (which was just described as being like space). RK: na ca tato vyutthita iti prāptāparihāṇitaḥ.

The elder, the venerable Subhūti, said to the venerable Śāriputra, “Likewise, Venerable Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being should dwell and train in this very way. That is to say, he or she should train with the notion, ‘I will dwell just like the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata, who is neither someone dwelling, nor someone not dwelling, nor someone free of dwelling, nor someone not free of dwelling on anything.’According to Ratnākara (F.58.a), that the Tathāgata is neither (a) “someone dwelling” nor (b) “someone not dwelling” means that he is not fixed in any dwelling (apratiṣṭhita) because of either not existing (permanently) or not being removed (permanently from cyclic existence). Just as was explained before (when the same phrases appeared in the preceding chapter), the second pair of negations‍—i.e., that he is also neither (c) “someone free of dwelling” nor (d) “not free from dwelling”‍—are refuting the idea that the Tathāgata is somehow in a continuous flow-pattern. Cf. RK: na sthito nāsthita ity apratiṣṭhitamān asattvād avyutthānāc ca| na viṣṭhito naviṣṭhita iti pravāhayogena pūrvavat. A bodhisattva great being should train him- or herself in this, thinking, ‘I will dwell the way that the Tathāgata dwells and train myself in that.’ In other words, ‘Just as the Tathāgata dwells by means of freedom from dwelling, so too will I dwell and train myself.’ In such a way should a bodhisattva great being dwell and train, since, by training in this way, he or she abides in this transcendent state of wisdom and is never lacking in this attentiveness.”According to Hari (153), the meaning of the sentence is as follows: After realizing his awakening, a tathāgata does not perceive any things as ultimately real. and hence these things appear to have the nature of mere magical illusions. Hence, in order to realize the most sublime point of the Dharma‍—by cultivating the freedom from mental perception causally conducive to that authoritative person’s (i.e., the tathāgata’s) nonperception of things‍—one must not “abide” there out of being yoked to habitual fixation. That is to say, one must cultivate it like this extensively on the ten levels, such as being on the level of the path or fruit of a stream enterer and so on. Hari: tad ayaṃ vākyārthaḥ: yasmāt tathāgatena bodhim abhisambudhya tattvato na kecid dharmāḥ samupalabdhāḥ. tasmān māyāsvabhāvā evāmī bhāvāḥ pratibhāntīti pramāṇa­puruṣādarśana­kāraṇopapannānupalambha­bhāvanayāgra­dharmādhigamārthaṃ, vistareṇa srotāapatti­phala­prati­pannakatvādi­daśa­bhūmi­ṣvabhiniveśa­yogān na sthātavyam ity evaṃ bhāvanīyam iti. See also Sparham (II–III, p. 101) who offers an alternative interpretation of Hari’s comments.

Some gods in that assembly then had this thought: “When benevolent spirits are talking to other benevolent spirits, we can distinguish the language of the benevolent spirits‍—namely, the words of the benevolent spirits, their statements, and their overall point‍—just as it is spoken by those benevolent spirits. However, we do not distinguish this thing that Subhūti is speaking about, discussing, teaching, and expounding.”

Then, through the miraculous power of the Buddha, the venerable Subhūti knew in his mind that the gods were having this kind of mental rumination, and said to them, “Gods, it is not distinguished by you at all, because there is something about it not pointed out and there is something about it not heard.”This passage exploits Sanskrit ambiguity for the sake of humor and has been translated to preserve the two divergent ways to read the Sanskrit: (a) Subhūti is saying “…nothing (na kiṃcid) about it is pointed out…” because nothing about it can be pointed out insofar as it is beyond words, or (b) “a little (kiṃcid) something is (still) not (na) pointed out about it (by me)…” The gods misunderstand his meaning here to be the second, so they get excited that he is about to make some subtle point more clear.

But just then, the gods thought, “Aha! Subhūti will now make it clear. Aha! He will now spell it out. Noble Subhūti is about to get into the loftiest point among lofty points. He is about to get into, describe, and explain the most subtle point among the subtle points, the most profound point among the profound points.”According to Ratnākara, it is called the “loftiest point” because it is beyond the scope of anyone who is not a noble one, the “most subtle point” because it is beyond conceptual proliferations, and the “most profound point” because it is beyond measure. Compared to the disciple path, this knowing is more lofty, subtle, and profound. Cf. RK: anāryāṇām agocaratvena dūrāt | niṣprapañca­tvena sūkṣmāt | duravagāhatvena gambhīrāc chrāvakamārgād dūrataraṃ sūkṣmataraṃ gambhīrataraṃ jñānam| praviśati cetasā| deśayati granthataḥ| bhāṣate arthataḥ.

Then, through the miraculous power of the Buddha, the venerable Subhūti knew in his mind of the gods’ mental rumination and said to those gods, “So, gods, whether you wish to attain and dwell in the fruition of a stream enterer, a once-returner, a non-returner, a worthy one, a solitary realizer, or an unsurpassed buddha‍—there is no realizing it without developing acceptance of it.”
But then the gods wondered, “What sort of Dharma listeners would be acceptable to this noble Subhūti?”

Then, through the miraculous power of the Buddha, the venerable Subhūti knew in his mind of the gods’ mental rumination and said to those gods, “Gods, Dharma listeners who are like magical illusions or like magical emanations would be acceptable to me.According to Ratnākara (F.59.a), a “magical illusion” is a false person that appears by the force of mantras and so on, while a “magical emanation” is manifested through (a meditator’s) supernatural power (ṛddhi). RK: mantrādibalena pratibhāsamāno mithyāpuruṣo māyā| ṛddhisandarśito nirmitaḥ| tābhyāṃ sadṛśāḥ. Why is that? Because such listeners would never hear or actualize anything.”
Then those gods asked the venerable Subhūti, “But, noble Subhūti, those sentient beings who are like magical illusions‍—are they themselves not magical illusions?”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the gods, “Gods, those sentient beings are like a magical illusion. Gods, those sentient beings are like a dream.According to Ratnākara (F.59.a), the gods think that being a magical illusion is just being false. But Subhūti thinks that an illusion and a dream are particular types of falsity, which share only falsity in common. Therefore, both a sentient being and a magical illusion are free of duality, because they are indistinguishable insofar as they are both false. For that reason, the unreality of a person’s substance is said to be like an illusory person. RK: māyopamās te sattvā na māye ti| mithyātvam eva māyātvaṃ manyante| subhūtis tu manyate mithyāviśeṣo māyā ca svapnaṃ ca tābhyāṃ sādṛśyaṃ mithyātvam eva| tasmān māyā ca sattvāś cety advayaṃ mithyātvena nirviśeṣatvād iti| tad evaṃ māyāpuruṣavat puruṣadravyāṇām asattvam uktam. That is because sentient beings and a magical illusion are here something free of duality, and free from creating duality, and because sentient beings and a dream are here something free of duality, and free from creating duality. Also, gods, all things are like a magical illusion and like a dream.According to Ratnākara (F.59.a), “all things” ‍—i.e., all people and things‍—are like illusions, since, insofar as the substance of a person is not real, the things constituting persons are also not real. RK: sarvadharmā apī ti| puruṣadharmā api sarve māyopamāḥ| asati puruṣadravye taddharmāṇām apy abhāvāt. The fruition of a stream enterer is like a magical illusion and like a dream. In this way, both a once-returner and the fruition of a once-returner, both a non-returner and the fruition of a non-returner, and both a worthy one and the state of a worthy one are like a magical illusion and like a dream. Both a solitary realizer and the state of a solitary realizer are like a magical illusion and like a dream. Both a perfect buddha and the state of a perfect buddha are like a magical illusion and like a dream.”

The gods then asked the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, are you saying that even a perfect buddha is like a magical illusion and like a dream? Are you saying that even perfect buddhahood is like a magical illusion and like a dream?”
“Gods, I am saying that even nirvāṇa is like a magical illusion and like a dream,” replied Subhūti.
Then the gods said to the venerable Subhūti, “You are saying that even nirvāṇa is like a magical illusion and like a dream?”

The venerable Subhūti said, “Gods, I am saying that even if there were some other thing superior to nirvāṇa, that too would just be like a magical illusion and like a dream. Gods, it is because nirvāṇa and a magical illusion are here something free of duality, and free from creating duality. This is because nirvāṇa and a dream are here something free of duality, and free from creating duality.”

Then the venerable Śāripūtra, the venerable Pūrṇa, son of Maitrāyaṇī, the venerable Mahākoṣṭhila, the venerable Mahākātyāyana, the venerable Mahākāśyapa, and other great disciples together with a thousand bodhisattvas asked the venerable Subhūti, “Venerable Subhūti, who will accept the transcendent state of wisdom being explicated in this way?”

Then the venerable Ānanda said to the elders, “Venerable ones, those bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing, those persons blessed with correct views, or worthy ones whose worldly influences have dissipated will accept this transcendent state of wisdom being explicated in this way.”

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the other elders, “Venerable ones, no one will accept this transcendent state of wisdom being explicated in this way. Why is that? Because in this state, no Dharma is implied, no Dharma is elucidated, and no Dharma is made known. So, to the extent that no Dharma is implied in this state, no Dharma is elucidated in it, and no Dharma is made known in it, to that same extent no one will accept this transcendent state of wisdom explicated in this way.”
Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, then thought this to himself: “To venerate noble Subhūti’s skillful way of teaching the Dharma, I should emanate flowers and shower them down upon noble Subhūti.”
And at that moment, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, emanated flowers and showered them down upon the elder Subhūti.

In response to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, Subhūti thought to himself, “Flowers that I have never seen before are being showered down by Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, and they are falling among the gods in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three. These magically emanated flowers are not flowers produced by trees, bushes, or vines. These flowers showered down by Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, must be made by mind.”

Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, knew in his mind of Subhūti’s mental rumination and said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, these flowers are unproduced. Why is that? Because there are no flowers either produced by mind or produced by trees, bushes, or vines.”According to Ratnākara (F.60.a), since there is nothing to be grasped, trees and so on do not exist. Likewise, since the mind is not grasping them, it too does not exist, and hence the flowers are unproduced even by that mind. RK: anirjātānī ti| yathā hi grāhyatvād vṛkṣādayo na santīti na tebhyo nirjātāni tāni| tathā manopi nāsti grāhakatvād iti tato py anirjātāni.

Then the venerable Subhūti said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, what you are saying is that these flowers are unproduced, that is, neither produced by mind nor by trees, bushes, or vines. Yet, Kauśika, whatever is unproduced is not a flower.”

Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, thought to himself, “Ah! This profoundly wise noble Subhūti is explicating that designation of things with words and names. He is not contradicting that designation, but rather dissecting it and expounding it precisely.”According to Hari (166), he “is explicating” the designation of things‍—i.e., the designation of the constituents (skandha) such as consciousness and so on‍—on account of explaining the meaning of his previous words, as an aid to wisdom through hearing. By not denying the acceptance of conventional reality, he is “not denying” the true nature. He is “dissecting it” on account of making his explanation more firm through logical reasoning, as an aid to wisdom through contemplation. He is “expounding it” precisely, on account of expounding the implicit stages of meditative concentration, as an aid to wisdom through cultivation. Hari: nāmapadaprajñaptiṃ vijñānādi­skandha­prajñaptiṃ. śrutamayajñānārthaṃ granthārthakathanān nirdiśati. samvṛtyābhyupagama­syāvirodhād dharmatayā na virodhayati. cintāmayajñānārthaṃ yuktyā sthirīkaraṇād uttānīkaroti. bhāvanā­maya­jñānārthaṃ dhyānakramopadeśād upadiśati. According to Ratnākara (F.60.b), here he is explaining designation with words, as a mere manner of speech. He expounds the fact that physical form, which is a mere designation, is nothing but the true nature, which is itself a mere designation. This section corresponds to the stage of warmth for a bodhisattva on the path of preliminary practice. RK: nāmapada iḥ prajñaptiḥ vyavahāraḥ| tān nirdiśati| na ca virodhayati tāṃ cottānīkaroti| tām eva copadiśati| tathā hi prajñaptimātraṃ rūpaṃ prajñaptimātram eva dharmatety upadiśati ity ūṣmā. The Sanskrit compound noun nāmapadaprajñaptiṃ is interpreted in slightly different ways by Ratnākara, Hari, and the Tibetan ming dang tshig tu btags pa, and has been translated to allow all three interpretations here.

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to Subhūti, “Exactly so, noble Subhūti, exactly so. A bodhisattva great being should train in this state, in the exact way that the noble Subhūti is expounding it.”

Then Subhūti said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Yes, Kauśika, yes, a bodhisattva great being should train this way in this state. Kauśika, training in this way, a bodhisattva great being neither trains in the fruition of a stream enterer, nor in the fruition of a once-returner, nor in the fruition of a non-returner, nor in the state of a worthy one, nor in the state of a solitary realizer, nor in buddhahood.

“Yet that bodhisattva great being, who is not training on these levels, does train in buddhahood or in the state of complete knowing. That bodhisattva great being, who is training in buddhahood or in the state of complete knowing, does train in the qualities of a buddha that are innumerable, immeasurable. That bodhisattva great being, who is training in the innumerable, immeasurable qualities of a buddha, does not train to increase or decrease physical forms. Likewise, he or she does not train to increase or decrease feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness.

“That bodhisattva great being‍—who is not training to increase or decrease physical forms and who is not training to increase or decrease feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness‍—does not train to apprehend physical forms or eliminate them. Likewise, he or she does not train to apprehend or eliminate feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Nor does he or she train to apprehend any thing in order to develop it or cause it to vanish.

“That bodhisattva great being‍—who is not training to apprehend any thing in order to develop it or cause it to vanish‍—does not train to apprehend even the state of complete knowing in order to develop that thing or cause it to vanish. Yet, by training in this way, a bodhisattva great being trains in the state of complete knowing and will go beyond cyclic existence in the state of complete knowing.”

The venerable Śāriputra then said to the venerable Subhūti, “Venerable Subhūti, for bodhisattva great beings‍—who are not training to apprehend any thing and not training to develop it or cause it to vanish‍—even the state of complete knowing is not a thing that they train to apprehend in order to develop it or cause it to vanish. By training in this way, the bodhisattva great being trains in the state of complete knowing and will go beyond cyclic existence in the state of complete knowing.”

The venerable Subhūti said, “Yes, Venerable Śāriputra, that is exactly right. Venerable Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being‍—who is not training to apprehend any thing and not training to develop it or cause it to vanish‍—does not train to apprehend even the state of complete knowing and does not train to develop it or cause it to vanish. Nor does he or she train to apprehend even the qualities of a buddha in order to develop them or cause them to vanish. By training in this way, Venerable Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being trains in the state of complete knowing and goes beyond cyclic existence in the state of complete knowing.”
Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, then asked this of the venerable Śāriputra: “Venerable Śāriputra, on what basis should a bodhisattva great being pursue the transcendent state of wisdom?”
The venerable Śāriputra replied, “A bodhisattva great being should pursue the transcendent state of wisdom on the basis of Venerable Subhūti’s discourse.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the venerable Śāriputra, “Venerable Śāriputra, when noble Subhūti speaks of the transcendent state of wisdom, whose blessings should be recognized? Whose miraculous power should be recognized?”According to Hari (172), in some scriptural passages, based on the Tathāgata’s miraculous power or authority alone, someone other than the Tathāgata will speak to articulate a consequence of the discussion through examining it and so on. But in the expounding of the transcendent state of wisdom, the Blessed One has empowered, in particular, only Subhūti. So this motivates the question and response about Subhūti’s speaking about this topic. Hari: kuto gaveṣitavye ti kasya nirdeśāt pratipattavyā bhāvayitavyety arthaḥ. śāriputra āha | prajñāpāramitā kauśika bodhisattvena mahāsattvenāyuṣmataḥ subhūteḥ parivartād gaveṣitāvyā || prasaṅgāgata­vacanārthaṃ nirūpaṇādidvāreṇa tathāgatānubhāvād evānyaḥ kaścit kvacin nirdiśati. mukhyataḥ punar bhagavatā prajñā­pāramitopadeśe subhūtir evādhiṣṭhita ity abhiprāyavān āha: prajñāpāramitā kauśiketyā di. parivartād iti nirdeśāt.

The venerable Śāriputra replied, “Kauśika, it is the Tathāgata’s blessings that should be recognized and the Tathāgata’s miraculous power that should be recognized, when the noble Subhūti speaks of the transcendent state of wisdom.”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, you asked the following: ‘When noble Subhūti speaks of the transcendent state of wisdom, whose blessings should be recognized? Whose miraculous power should be recognized?’ When I speak of the transcendent state of wisdom, it is the Tathāgata’s blessings that should be recognized and the Tathāgata’s miraculous power that should be recognized. You also asked the following: ‘On what basis should a bodhisattva great being pursue the transcendent state of wisdom?’ Kauśika, a bodhisattva great being does not pursue the transcendent state of wisdom on the basis of physical forms, or on any basis other than physical forms. Likewise, he or she does not pursue the transcendent state of wisdom on the basis of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness, or on any basis other than feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Why is that? It is because physical forms are not the transcendent state of wisdom, nor is there a transcendent state of wisdom in anything other than physical forms. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are not the transcendent state of wisdom, nor is there a transcendent state of wisdom in anything other than feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, it is a great transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. It is a transcendent state beyond measure, this transcendent state of wisdom.Neither Ratnākara nor Hari comment here on the Sanskrit expression yad uta (’di lta ste) that is used repeatedly throughout this passage. But Ratnākara (F.226.a) does explain later in the commentary that the expression highlights the particular quality of something that has been pointed out only generally. So, although the expression is represented merely as the comma following “great transcendent state,” “transcendent state beyond measure,” and so on, we can understand “this transcendent state of wisdom” to indicate its distinctiveness from all other transcendent states in terms of its various particular qualities mentioned one by one below. RK: yaduta śabdaḥ sāmānyenoddiṣṭasya viśeṣaṃ dyotayati. It is an unlimited transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. It is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom.”

The venerable Subhūti said, “So it is, Kauśika. So it is. Kauśika, it is a great transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. It is a transcendent state beyond measure, this transcendent state of wisdom. It is an unlimited transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. It is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that?

“Kauśika, it is because, inasmuch as physical forms are great, it is a great transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. Likewise, Kauśika, inasmuch as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are great, it is a great transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom.

“Kauśika, inasmuch as physical forms are beyond measure, it is a transcendent state beyond measure, this transcendent state of wisdom. Likewise, Kauśika, inasmuch as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are beyond measure, it is a transcendent state beyond measure, this transcendent state of wisdom.

“Kauśika, inasmuch as physical forms are unlimited, it is an unlimited transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. Likewise, Kauśika, inasmuch as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are unlimited, it is an unlimited transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom.

“Kauśika, inasmuch as physical forms are infinite, it is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. Likewise, Kauśika, inasmuch as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are infinite, it is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom.

“Thus, Kauśika, one does not fixate on ‘a great transcendent state,’ ‘a transcendent state beyond measure,’ ‘an unlimited transcendent state,’ or ‘an infinite transcendent state.’ Because of that, Kauśika, this transcendent state of wisdom is a great transcendent state, a transcendent state beyond measure, an unlimited transcendent state, and an infinite transcendent state.

“Kauśika, since sense objects are infinite, it is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, insofar as sentient beings are infinite, it is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. But insofar as sense objects are infinite, how is it an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom? Because, Kauśika, the beginning, middle, or end of all things is not mentally perceived, and hence it is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. By the same account, Kauśika, since sense objects are infinite, it is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. Furthermore, Kauśika, insofar as all things are infinite and limitless, their beginning, middle, or end is never mentally perceived, and hence it is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, the beginning, middle, or end of physical forms is never mentally perceived. Likewise, Kauśika, the beginning, middle, or end of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness is never mentally perceived. Also, in this way, Kauśika, insofar as sense objects are infinite, it is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. Furthermore, Kauśika, all sentient beings are infinite and limitless. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, the beginning, middle, or end of any sentient being is never mentally perceived. Therefore, Kauśika, since sentient beings are infinite, it is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom.”
Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the venerable Subhūti, “Venerable Subhūti, how is it an infinite transcendent state due to sentient beings being infinite?”

The elder Subhūti said, “This transcendent state of wisdom is an infinite transcendent state since sentient beings are infinite, but not by way of counting them, or on account of their being great in number.”
Śakra asked, “Taking that into account now, Venerable Subhūti, how is it an infinite transcendent state due to sentient beings being infinite?”

“Kauśika, what do you think?” asked the elder Subhūti. “What thing possesses the name sentient being?”Ratnākara’s comments on the response to this question (in the next few notes) should all be understood in the light of what he explained in chapter 1 with regard to designations being devoid of referents (F.46.b–47.a, repeated here from n.180): “A name is not the nature (svabhāva) of a referent, because one sees the mere referent before the linguistic convention (saṅketaḥ). Also, the referent is not the nature of the name, because one can ascertain (pratītiḥ) just a mere name whose referent is not found. And, since words are used even when there is no referent, a name has no referent. For this very reason, this designation has not come into being (anabhinirvṛttam), i.e., it is not established (siddham) as a designation (of a thing).” For edited Sanskrit and Tibetan text, see Seton 2016, Ai, p. 147 and Aii, p. 140. RK: nāmadheyamātram iti arthaśūnyaṃ nāma| tathāhi nārthasya svabhāvo nāma, prāk saṅketād arthamātradarśanāt| nāpi nāmnaḥ svabhāvo ’rthaḥ, aviditārthasya nāmnaḥ kevalasyaiva pratīteḥ, asaty api cārthe śabdaprayogāt| nirarthakaṃ nāma| ata eva tac ca nāmadheyam anabhinirvṛttaṃ, nāmadheyatvenāsiddham.

Śakra said, “Noble Subhūti, there is neither a thing nor a nonthing that possesses this name sentient being.According to Ratnākara (F.62.a), the “thing” means the substance with the self (of a sentient being). That substance does not possess that “name” because such a thing (as a sentient being) does not exist. A nonthing (i.e., something lacking substance) does not possess a name, because (a name) expresses its own referent as a thing (that is heard). RK: na dharmādhivacanam iti dharma ātmadravyaṃ, na tasyādhivacanaṃ tasyābhāvāt| dharmatvena svārthābhidhānāt nādharmādhivacanam. Note: The Sanskrit word svārtha (translated as “its own referent”) in Ratnākara’s comment above is a multivalent part of the definition of a name. In essence, Ratnākara is suggesting here that the name “sentient being” cannot refer to a “nonthing” because the word “sentient being” communicates something that is neither a thing nor a nonthing. This name that has been bandied about is adventitious.According to Ratnākara (F.62.a), “bandied about” means employed in the scriptures. RK: prakṣiptam iti sūtraiḥ prayuktam. This name that has been bandied about has no substance.According to Ratnākara (F.62.a–b), it is “adventitious” because, when examined, it goes away. It “has no substance” because there is no substance belonging to the self (ātma). RK: | kutaḥ ? yata āgantukaṃ sati vicāre calatvāt| yato ’vastukaṃ ātmadravyābhāvāt. This name that has been bandied about is not characterized by a self.In the Sanskrit word anātmīya (translated here as “not characterized by a self”), the prefix an- clearly means “not” and the word ātmā clearly means “self,” but Ratnākara (F.62.b) suggests that the secondary nominal suffix -īya here can and should be interpreted in three different ways, making the term anātmīya mean (a) “not connected with a self” (b) “not possessed by a self” and (c) “not characterized by a self.” According to Ratnākara (F.62.b), each meaning can be understood in line with its own logic, namely, (a) “this name is not connected with a self,” because something (a sentient being) is called “a self” even based on an implied connection with a “self”‍—but since a “self” does not exist in absolutely any case, such a name (“sentient being”) would also not exist; (b) “this name is not something possessed by a self,” because the referent of the verbal expression “self” ‍—i.e., the constituents (skandhas) and so on‍—is what is possessed by a self, but that (referent of the name “sentient being”) is also absent; and (c) “this name is not characterized by a self” because‍—insofar as a name is an identification and what is characterized by a “self” is what is identified‍—that (which is characterized by the word “sentient being”) is absent. RK: ātmasambandhād apy ātmeti kiñcid ucyate| tad api nāsty ātmano ’tyantam asattvād ity anātmīyam| athavā ātma­prajñaptiviṣayaḥ skandhādir ātmīyas tasyāpy abhāvād anātmīyam| athavā nāma saṃjñā tasyātmīyaḥ saṃjñī| tadabhāvād anātmīyam. This name that has been bandied about lacks any sense object.”According to Ratnākara (F.62.b), “sense object” (anārambaṇam) means perceived object (viṣaya). Since a perceived object is absent, it is “lacking any sense object.” RK: ārambaṇaṃ viṣayas tadabhāvāt ānārambaṇam.

“Kauśika, what do you think?” asked the elder Subhūti. “Is there anything in this teaching that has made a so-called sentient being appear?”According to Ratnākara (F.62.a), he asks this in reference to the Buddha’s teaching so that he can then prove the absence of identity based also on the words of an authority. RK: atre ti śāstṛśāsane| etac cāptavacanād api nairātmyasiddhiṃ darśayitum āha.
“No, noble Subhūti,” replied Śakra.

The elder Subhūti asked, “Kauśika, what infinitude of sentient beings could exist in a place where no sentient beings are made to appear?According to Ratnākara (F.62.a), in this sentence, the “infinitude” is because they are beyond quantification. But “no sentient beings” are made to appear, because, when the lack of identity is proved through inference, self is also disproven based on these words of an authoritative person. To show this, he next says “suppose…” RK: kā satvānantate ti| gaṇanāti­krāntatvena na kācit, pramāṇasiddhe ’pi nairātmye āptavacanād api nātmasiddhir iti darśayitum āha| sacet yādi.
