Kauśika, suppose that the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata were to remain for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, and were to say the word ‘sentient being, sentient being’ with a clear, deep, resounding voice, announcing it infinitely. Would any sentient being have come to exist, be coming to exist, or come to exist, or would any sentient being have ceased, cease, or come to cease in that place?”

“No, noble Subhūti,” replied Śakra. “Why is that? Because a sentient being is primordially pure, primordially absolutely pure.”According to Ratnākara (F.62.b), “primordially pure” means nonexistent from the very beginning; “primordially absolutely pure” means nonexistent in every case. RK: ādiśuddhatvam ādita evāsattvam, pariśuddhatvaṃ sarvatrāsattvam.

The elder Subhūti said, “Thus, Kauśika, since sentient beings are infinite also in this manner, it is an infinite transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom. It is also for this very reason, Kauśika, that the infinitude of the transcendent state of wisdom should be understood by way of the infinitude of sentient beings.”According to Ratnākara (F.62.b), “by way of the infinitude of sentient beings” means because sentient beings lack the extremes of arising and ceasing. The infinitude is “for this very reason,” meaning it is not because sentient beings are beyond counting (as seemed to be suggested in the statements and questions above). RK: evaṃ sattvānantaye ti| satvānām utpāda­nirodhānta­virahāt sattvānantataye ti| evaṃ ce ty evam eva| na gaṇānati­krāntatveneti.

Then those gods who were with Indra, with Brahmā, and with Prajāpati, along with the multitude of men and women who were with the sages, proclaimed three times, “Oh the Dharma! Oh the Dharma! Oh the Dharma’s true nature!According to Ratnākara (F.63.a), in these three short exclamations, the word dharma has different meanings. The first dharma refers to the Dharma that is taught (by the Buddha). The second dharma refers to what is illuminated (by that teaching), i.e., the path of seeing (reality as it is). The third dharma refers to that very path of seeing, and the true nature (dharmatā), i.e., the original nature, of that path of insight, which is what gives rise to a tathāgata. RK: aho ityāścarye| dharma iti deśanādharmaḥ| punaḥ dharma iti dharmaprakāśito darśanamārgaḥ| tasyaiva dharmasya dharmatā prakṛtis tathāgatotpādanam. In this, the noble Subhūti is pointing out, teaching, clarifying, and revealing that which is the manifestation of the Tathāgata. Blessed One, from today onward, we will affirm that a bodhisattva great being who never lacks this transcendent state of wisdom and abides in it will be a tathāgata!”Ratnakāra here (F.63.a) includes an interesting gloss on the term tathāgata‍—usually said to be one who has realized (āgata or gata) the actual nature (tathā)‍—saying that one becomes a tathāgata because of clarifying the actual way things are. Here, the syllable ta (in gata) is based on creative etymology from the verb √gad (to “articulate” or “enumerate”) as in verbs such as √pac. RK (F.63.a): yathā dharmās tathaiva gadanāt tathāgataḥ| gadeḥ pacādyac| nairuktas takāraḥ.

The Blessed One then addressed the gods who were with Indra, with Brahmā, and with Prajāpati, along with the multitude of male and female sages: “So it is, gods, so it is. Gods, when I studied in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Dīpaṃkara in the royal palace of Dīpavati, I was never lacking in the transcendent state of wisdom, and at that time I was foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Dīpaṃkara with the following words:

“ ‘Brahmin son, after countless eons in the future, you will become a worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata, perfected in knowledge and appropriate conduct, a blissful one, a knower of the world, an unsurpassed guide for persons to be tamed, a teacher of gods and humans, a buddha, and a blessed one.’ ”

Then the gods said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, it is wonderful, Sugata. How supremely wonderful that the bodhisattva great beings have this transcendent state of wisdom that brings forth the complete knowing and unites them with it.”Here, Ratnākara (F.64.a) clarifies that “complete knowing” refers to the “complete knowing of all things.” RK: … sarvajñatāyāḥ sarvākārajñatāyā ity arthaḥ.
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “For Śakra,” the second chapter in “The Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 3: Paying Tribute to the Stūpas and the Transcendent States That Contain Immeasurable BenefitsThe title term guṇa (Tib. yon tan) often refers, in general Sanskrit, to personal “positive qualities” or a thing’s “virtues” or even “merits,” but, in Buddhist Sanskrit, it also means “benefits” or “advantages.” Since the term guṇa is translated either as “positive qualities” or “benefits” according to context, it may be helpful to keep this double meaning in mind while reading this chapter and the next one, because neither the text itself nor the two commentators ever see any need to explain how the various qualities, benefits, and degrees of merit that result from paying tribute and so on‍—which are described at length in this chapter and the next‍—are implicitly linked to each other and this chapter title. For instance, Ratnākara (F.64.b) simply states at the start of this chapter that it correlates to verse 2.13c in the Ornament: “benevolence and so on”‍—i.e., the four immeasurable states, namely benevolence, compassion, rejoicing, and equanimity. RK: ’maitryādi’ iti śāstram| maitrī­karuṇāmuditopekṣāś catvāryapramāṇāni maitryādi. Also, as will be clear in notes below, Hari (188) also connects the “immeasurable (aprameya) benefits” (which come from lacking faults) directly with “the four immeasurable states” (apramāṇa).The beginning of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 17 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 26 in the Eighteen Thousand. As the chapter continues, it also includes content corresponding to Chapters 18–21 of the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapters 27–30 in the Eighteen Thousand, starting respectively at the points below marked by the annotations n.269, n.302, n.309, and n.316.
div.p: The Blessed One was aware of all the gods who had alighted and taken their seats among that assembly. He also was aware of the four assemblies consisting of the monks, nuns, male lay devotees, and female lay devotees who had come and taken their seats. At that time, he made some gods of the desire and form realms‍—the gods in Brahmā’s retinue, and the gods of the Ābhāsvarā realm, the Parīttaśubha realm, and the Akaniṣṭha realm‍—bear direct witness as he addressed the assembly of the gods of the desire realm headed by the lord among gods, Śakra, the gods in Brahmā’s retinue headed by Brahmā the Great, and the gods of the Ābhāsvarā realm, as follows:Ratnākara and Hari contextualize this passage in the eighth of the sixteen moments of the path of insight (darśanamārga). According to Hari (187), to demonstrate the appearance of the fourth moment of (insight into) the origin (i.e., the fourth of the second set of four moments when realizing the second noble truth) as something experienced personally, the Blessed One made some of the gods of the desire realm and so on bear witness and addressed some others. Hari: samudaye caturthakṣaṇākāraṃ pratipādayituṃ pratyātmavedyatvena kāṃścit kāmāvacārādīn devaputrān sākṣiṇaḥ kṛtvā kāṃścid āmantritavān. According to Ratnākara (F.64.b), this very moment that is making them experience personally is aimed at causing the gods greater devotion as an antidote to their potentially falling into the disciples’ path (perspectives) in the eighth moment (of insight). Ratnākara (F.64.b): sākṣīkaraṇam aṣṭame kṣaṇe hīna­yāna­patana­prati­pakṣa­gauravātiśayotpādanāya tam eva kṣaṇam. “O gods, when a noble son or daughter takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, and passes it on,According to Ratnākara (F.64.b), the transcendent state of wisdom here refers to the moment of seeing due to the Blessed One’s introduction. This moment is called “transcendent state of wisdom” in the sense that it has arrived at the “other shore” (pāraṃitā<pāraṃgatā) that is the wisdom (prājñā) itself, i.e., the perfection is wisdom. What is it the other shore of? The ground from which there is (still the possibility of) falling into the disciples’ path. What is that wisdom? It is that seeing which consists in the four immeasurables. To explain, once bodhisattvas have attained that seeing, they see all beings as equal to themselves. Having benefited those lacking refuge, they must avoid falling at this point into the disciples’ path in which the benefit is only for one’s own self. Thus, the “transcendent state of wisdom” refers here to the insight whose nature is loving kindness, and so on. And, it is precisely the eighth moment. A bodhisattva “takes it in” through “mastering it” because of directly witnessing that transcendent state of wisdom. A bodhisattva “passes it on” because of it being illuminated to others who take it in themselves. RK: yo hīt yādinā prajñāpāramitām iti prakaraṇād darśanakṣaṇaḥ| prajñā saiva pāraṃgate ti pāramitā | kasya pāram ? hīnayāna­patanabhūmeḥ| kā cāsau ? yā catur(rṇāṃ) apramāṇātmakaṃ darśanam| tathāhi tatprāpto bodhisattvaḥ sarvasattveṣv ātmasamatādarśī tān aśaraṇān hitvā nātmanaḥ kevalasya hitam īha te yena hīnayāne nipatet| tasmān maitryādirūpaṃ darśanam atra prajñāpāramitā | saiva cāṣṭamaḥ kṣaṇaḥ| tāṃ yaḥ kaścid grahīṣyati yāvat paryavāpsyati sākṣātkaraṇāt| pravartayiṣyatī ti parebhyaḥ prakāśitāyās tasyās tair udgrahaṇāt. then Māra and his demon cohort, who prey on weakness and seek ways to attack, will never find any vulnerability in him or her.According to Hari (188), “Māra” here refers to the god Māra who (among the four types of Māras) is a god of desire. “His demon cohort” are the gods who are part of his faction. They “prey on weakness” because they see what weaknesses arise, and they “seek ways to attack” because they always look for future opportunities to attack. The word avatāra‍—which is translated differently each of the three times it occurs in this sentence‍—means “faults,” or, more precisely, “weaknesses” or “vulnerable points” (that can be attacked). Hari: tatra māro devaputramāraḥ kāmadevaḥ. tatpākṣikā devaputrā mārakāyikā devatāḥ. samutpannāva­tāra­darśanād avatāraprekṣiṇyaḥ. anāgatāvatāra­paryeṣaṇād avatāragaveṣiṇyaḥ. avatāro doṣaś chidram iti yāvat. Nor will any humans or nonhumans, who prey on weakness and seek ways to attack, find any vulnerability in him or her. Nor will any noble son or daughter ever die on account of failing to avoid some cause of an untimely death.According to Hari (188), the phrase “die on account of failing to avoid some cause of an untimely death” means to die, somehow, due to the coming together of circumstances, even though one’s natural lifespan (āyus) has not run out. The idea behind all these cases is that they never occur because his or her mindstream is utterly suffused with (the presence of the four immeasurables, namely) loving kindness, and so on. Hari: vidyamāne ’py āyuṣi kathaṃcit pratyayasāṃnidhyān maraṇaṃ viṣaṃāparihāreṇa kālakriyā. sarvaṃ caitan na bhavati. maitryādibhiḥ suparibhāvita­citta­saṃtatitvād iti matiḥ. For a gloss of this phrase, see Ratnākara (F.72.a); RK: viṣamo mṛtyuhetuḥ| tasyāparihāro viṣamāparihāraḥ.

“Moreover, O gods, those gods who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening‍—but who have not yet taken in this transcendent state of wisdom, not kept it in mind, not chanted it, not mastered it, and not passed it on‍—will wish to come near a noble son or daughter who has taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, and passed it on, and they will come into his or her presence in order to listen to this transcendent state of wisdom. After hearing it, those gods themselves will take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, and pass it on. Furthermore, any noble son or daughter who has taken in this transcendent state of wisdom, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, and passed it on will neither find themselves in danger nor come to face any obstruction while traveling, wandering, staying, sitting, or getting lost in the wilderness, at the base of a tree, in an empty house, in an unprotected area, on a road, on a highway, in a forest, or on the open sea.”

Then the Four Great Kings said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, it is wonderful that a noble son or daughter‍—who takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, and passes it on‍—guides sentient beings along the three paths and yet does not develop the conception ‘sentient being.’ We shall safeguard, defend, and protect any noble son or daughter who takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, and passes it on.”Ratnākara explains two possible interpretations of the Sanskrit compound of three words that describe what the Great Kings promise to provide. Since the Tibetan also follows the second interpretation, the translation here does likewise. According to Ratnākara (F.65.a), in the first interpretation, they will “provide the protection” associated with “safeguarding” and “defending.” Among those, “safeguarding” means with weapons (such as swords, amulets) and so on, “defending” means by making efforts to guard him or her, and “protection” arises from their supernatural control over that person when someone spontaneously attacks. In the second interpretation, they will “safeguard” by providing all sorts of physical well-being, “defend” by warding off external attacks, and “protect” by preventing internal diseases and so on. RK: rakṣāvaraṇa sahitā guptiḥ| tatra śastrādinā rakṣā| rakṣaniyogād āvaraṇam| svayam upasaṁkramya tasyādhiṣṭhānāt guptiḥ| athavā śarīrasya sarvasukhopasaṃharaṇaṁ rakṣā| bāhyopakramanivāraṇam āvaraṇam| ādhyātmikarogādinivāraṇaṁ guptiḥ. Cf. MW rakṣā, “amulet.” For yet another way of understanding the three words in the compound, see also DOP p. 57, gutti, which cites rakkhāvaraṇaguttin ti rakkhāsaṃkhata c’eva āvaraṇasaṃkhata ca guttim. Also, for saṃvidhā, see PTSD saṅvidahati.

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, I too shall safeguard, defend, and protect that noble son or daughter who takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, and passes it on.”

Also Brahmā, together with his retinue of gods, said to the Blessed One, “I too, O Blessed One, will safeguard, defend, and protect any noble son or daughter who takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, and passes it on.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, it is wonderful that any noble son or daughter‍—who takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, and passes it on‍—gains or receives such worldly benefits. But when the transcendent state of wisdom is taken in, are all six transcendent states taken in?”

In response, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Yes, Kauśika, yes. When the transcendent state of wisdom is taken in, all six transcendent states are taken in. Moreover, when a noble son or daughter has taken in the transcendent state of wisdom, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, and passed it on, then he or she receives worldly benefits. Kauśika, listen well and pay good attention, I will describe them all to you.”
Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, assented, saying, “Very well,” and listened to the Blessed One.

The Blessed One said, “Among those worldly benefits, Kauśika, when those inclined to attack, inclined to deny, or inclined to dispute my Dharma teachings wish to attack, deny, or dispute them, their repeatedly launched attacks, denials, and disputes will just fade away and collapse, over and over.According to Ratnākara (F.65.b), the word “attack” means by chastising, “deny” means by quarreling, and “dispute” means with mutual hatred. RK: vigrahītavyaṃ tāḍanena| vivaditavyaṃ kalahena| virodhayitavyaṃ parasparadveṣeṇa| te ’bhiprāyā iti vigrahādyabhiprāyāḥ. The Tibetan translation rtsod par ’dod pa dang rgol bar ’dod pa dang ’gal bar has a slightly different flavor, but Ratnākara’s commentary was followed here. Thus, the intentions of those inclined to reject, inclined to deny, or inclined to dispute will never be fulfilled. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, any such claims repeatedly launched against that noble son or daughter‍—who takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it‍—will just fade away and collapse, over and over. The intentions of those inclined to reject, inclined to deny, or inclined to dispute will never be fulfilled.

“Furthermore, Kauśika, that noble son or daughter who takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it receives this worldly benefit in a way that is just like the medicinal herb called maghi that neutralizes all poisons. To explain, suppose that a hungry viper that wants food and is actively seeking it notices some sort of prey nearby. Since the viper is attracted to its smell, that venomous snake might track that creature by its scent and pursue it with the wish to eat it. Now, suppose that the creature were to flee that snake to a place where there was the medicinal herb called maghi, and were to stay there once it arrived. In that case, the venomous snake would turn right back around because of the mere smell of the medicinal herb. Why is that? Because medicinal herbs like that have a curative benefit that overpowers the snake’s venom and, indeed, this maghi medicinal herb has such power. Similarly, Kauśika, any claims repeatedly launched as attacks, denials, or disputes against any noble son or daughter who comes to take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it will quickly subside because of this transcendent state of wisdom. Those claims will be quelled and fade from that place, rather than increase in energy, strength, or power, thanks to the inherent power of the transcendent state of wisdom.Ratnākara: balādhānene ti tadyogena sāmarthyādhānāt. In the very place that they are launched, they will be quelled, fade‍—not increase‍—and collapse. Why is that? It is because the transcendent state of wisdom is the queller‍—not the increaser‍—of attachment and so on, up to and including the clinging to nirvāṇa.The words “desire and so on” (rāgādi) refer to the Abhidharma list of so-called root afflictions (rtsa nyon drug), namely, attachment, anger, pride, ignorance, wrong views, and doubt. It is also because the Four Great Kings; Śakra, powerful lord of the gods; Brahmā, the lord of the world system; and all the blessed buddhas and bodhisattvas will safeguard, defend, and protect anyone who takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it. That is the worldly benefit that such a noble son or daughter will receive.

“Moreover, Kauśika, any noble son or daughter who comes to take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it will come to speak agreeably, gently, fittingly, and coherently, rather than being overcome with anger or with pride.According to Ratnākara (F.66.a), “to speak” means to instruct others in the transcendent state of wisdom and so on, and “agreeably” means in a way that is acceptable (to listeners) because one has been in their place oneself too. The word “gently” means kindly, because one speaks politely to them and out of generosity and so on. To speak “fittingly” is by saying what will only be of benefit. To speak “coherently” is by not speaking with a mixture of unbeneficial things in addition to beneficial ones. RK: prajñāpāramitādīnāṁ parebhya upadeśo vacanam| ādeyaṁ grāhyam| tadasya, svayam api teṣu sthitatvād ity ādeyavacanaḥ| mṛdu priyaṁ vacanam asyeti mṛduvacanaḥ| dānādīnāṃ teṣu ca sthitānāṃ varṇabhāṣaṇāt| mitavacano hitasyaiva vacanāt| aprakīrṇavacano hitasyāpy ahitamiśrasyāvacanāt. Why is that? It is because the transcendent state of wisdom tames and transforms him or her.According to Ratnākara (F.66.a), it “tames” by neutralizing anger. It “transforms” by destroying the egoic mind characterized by arrogance. RK: paridamayati krodhāsaṃśamanāt| pariṇāmayati unnatilakṣaṇasya mānasya kṣayanayanāt. Note: There is a slight play on the Sanskrit word pariṇāmayati, which is used here to mean “transforms” but will be later used in this passage to mean “dedicates.” It does not increase his or her anger or pride. He or she harbors neither hatred, nor malevolence, nor any negative propensity. Going about life in this way, the noble son or daughter develops mindfulness and benevolence, thinking as follows: ‘If I were to develop a grudge, my sense faculties would become shattered and the coloring of my face would become inflamed. In additon to that, it would be unfitting for me to do so, because I have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening and am training for that. So I should not let myself become controlled by anger.’ With this understanding, a son or daughter will quickly recover mindfulness. So any noble son or daughter who comes to take in the transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, promote it, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it also receives this worldly benefit.”

In response, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, it is wonderful the way that the transcendent state of wisdom serves to tame and transform a bodhisattva great being.”

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 18 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 27 in the Eighteen Thousand.The Blessed One said, “Furthermore, Kauśika, suppose that the noble son or daughter‍—who comes to take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it‍—were mountedThe verb “mounted” (samārūḍhaḥ) could mean on a horse or a chariot‍—it seems to have been elided in the Tibetan translation. at the battlefront during a war while he or she was taking it in, keeping it in mind, chanting it, mastering it, passing it on, teaching it, expounding it, explaining it, or reciting it. Alternatively, suppose that he or she were just entering into battle, had already entered into it, or were passing it by, standing in it, sitting in it, or going around in it, while taking in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeping it in mind, chanting it, mastering it, passing it on, teaching it, expounding it, explaining it, reciting it, or paying attention to it. Kauśika, there would be no possibility or chance for that noble son or daughter’s lifespan to be cut short. That is to say, no attack by another person could cut his or her life short. Even if that other person were to attack with a sword, a stick, a clod of earth, or something else, they would not be able to strike the noble son or daughter’s body. Why not? Because, Kauśika, it is a great magical incantation, this transcendent state of wisdom.Here begins a play on the word vidyā, which means both “knowledge” and “magical incantation” (vidyā; rig sngags), i.e., a particular type of knowledge. According to Ratnākara (F.67.a), the transcendent state of wisdom as a vidyā is “great” because its power is unobstructed in all times, in all places, and against all counterproductive forces, respectively. Cf. RK: tatkasya hetor iti tad apatanaṃ kutaḥ ? uttaraṃ mahāvidye tyādinā| sarvakālaṃ sarvatra deśe sarvavipakṣeṣu vāpratihataprabhāvatvād yathākramaṃ mahatī. Kauśika, it is a magical incantation beyond measure, this transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, it is an unlimited magical incantation, this transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, it is an unsurpassed magical incantation, this transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, it is an unequaled magical incantation, this transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, it is a magical incantation equal to the unequaled, this transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, while training in this magical incantation, a noble son or daughter has no thought to harm him- or herself, no thought to harm others, and no thought to harm both self and other. And, Kauśika, it is because of this, that, while training in this magical incantation, a bodhisattva great being comes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening and to attain the knowing of someone who knows completely. Also, after realizing perfect awakening by that means, he or she comes to behold the mindsets of all sentient beings.According to Ratnākara (F.67.a), he or she “gazes at” the mindsets of sentient beings in order to tame them and so on. RK: vyavalokayiṣyatī ti vinayanādyartham. Why is that?According to Ratnākara (F.67.a), “why is that” means why is that a knowing of someone who has complete knowing? RK: tatkasya hetor iti tat sarvajñajñānaṃ kutaḥ? Because, Kauśika, there is nothing that a bodhisattva great being training in this knowledge as a magical incantation will not come to attain, know, or actualize.The phrase “this knowledge as a magical incantation” translates the single word vidyā in order to capture the meaningful word play in which the word vidyā can be understood as referring to both its more general and limited meanings. According to Ratnākara (F.67.b), the logic of the three descriptions in this sentence is this: All the qualities of a buddha are to be attained, and these must be known reflexively (pratyātmavedya). For that reason, if a bodhisattva were not to attain those, then he or she would not become knower of everything. So it says “nothing that a bodhisattva will not come to attain.” And for that reason, a knower of everything knows everything to be attained and anything else. So there would also be nothing that he or she does not “come to know.” Since this includes here the most excellent actualized knowing, he also says there is nothing that a bodhisattva does not “come to actualize.” Thus, the word “or” here has the sense of interrelated options (that express three different aspects of the attainment of complete knowing). RK: na tat kiñcid asti yan na prāptam iti| sarve hi buddhadharmāḥ prāptavyāḥ| te ca pratyātmavedyāḥ| tato yady anena prāptā na syur nāyaṃ sarvavid bhavet| tasmān na tat kiñcid asti yad asya na prāptaṃ syāt| tataḥ prāpyam itarac ca sarvaṃ jānātīti na tat kiñcid asti yad asya na jñātaṃ syāt| prakarṣagataṃ ca sākṣātkārijñānam iha gṛhyate| tata āha| na kiñcid asti yad asākṣātkṛtaṃ syāt ? iti vā śabdāḥ parasparāpekṣayā vikalpārthāḥ. For this reason, it is called the knowing of someone who knows completely. Hence, that noble son or daughter‍—who comes to take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, promote it, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it‍—will also come to receive this type of worldly benefit.

“Furthermore, Kauśika, wherever sentient beings have written down this transcendent state of wisdom, made it into a book, and preserved it for veneration in an elevated position, no human or nonhuman,The term non-human (amanuṣya) means not human, and includes here demons, ghosts, gods, benevolent spirits, and malevolent spirits. who preys on weakness and seeks ways to attack, will be able to find any vulnerability in them, except as the maturation of their previous actions‍—even if those sentient beings were not to pay any tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom, not to take it in, not to keep it in mind, not to chant it, not to master it, not to pass it on, not to teach it, not to expound it, not to explain it, and not to recite it. Such a noble son or daughter will also receive this worldly benefit.

“Furthermore, Kauśika, it is exactly the same, for instance, as being either at the epicenter of awakening, within the perimeter around the epicenter of awakening, within the interior part of the epicenter of awakening, or right at the foot of the Bodhi tree, where no one‍—including humans, nonhumans, and animals‍—can be hurt, injured, or possessed by either humans or malevolent spirits, except as the maturation of their own previous actions. Why not? Because it is there that the past, present, and future worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas‍—who cultivate and manifest freedom from anxiety, hatred, and fear for the sake of sentient beingsAccording to Ratnākara (F.68.a), “freedom from anxiety, hatred, and fear” refers to a single immeasurable quality possessed by those who have great loving kindness. That the buddhas “nurture” them means they produce them. They “manifest” them because Māra and his army approach as obstacles. RK: abhayam avairam anuttrāsaṃ mahāmaitrīnām ekam apramāṇam| prabhāvayantī ti niṣpādayanti prakāśayantī ti sasainyasya mārasya vighnato pi kramaṇāt.‍—have previously awakened, are awakening, and will continue to awaken. In that very same way, wherever a noble son or daughter takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, or recites it, Kauśika, he or she cannot be hurt, injured, or possessed by humans or malevolent spirits, except as the maturation of a previous action. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, this transcendent state of wisdom makes that location, in effect, into a stūpa worthy of obeisance, worthy of respect, worthy of offering, worthy of honor, worthy of being tended to, worthy of tribute, and worthy of reverence.According to Ratnākara (F.68.a), “in effect” means that it is similar to a stūpa, which “should be tended to” because it is worthy of (being venerated with activities such as) the removal of left-over devotional offerings (nirmālya) and so on. RK: tadbhūtas tattādṛśaḥ| nirmālyāder akarṣārhatvāt| apacāyanīyaḥ. For all sentient beings who come to it for protection, it provides a shelter, a refuge, a sanctuary, and an ultimate resort. A noble son or daughter will also receive this worldly benefit.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, suppose that one noble son or daughter were to write this transcendent state of wisdom down, make it into a book, preserve it, and pay tribute to it‍—with heavenly flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it.According to Ratnākara (F.69.a), “write down” is done by himself and “make into a book” is done by someone else. The word “heavenly” means superior, because it excludes (inferior) rapidly deteriorating flowers and so on. “Flowers” means those with open blossoms; “wreaths” here are garlands of flowers; “perfumes” means scented woods; “fragrant oils” means made from the tamāla tree (whose fruit is used for marking the forehead). The “aromatic powders” are agarwood, sandalwood, and so on; “fabrics” means cloth; “devotional offerings” means food and so on; and a “wide variety” means from the different types of food, drinks, and so on. One should connect the verb “pay tribute” with the “flowers” and so on‍—it means one should make devotional offerings to venerate it. “Revere” means with devotion; “respect” means with affection; “venerate” means with hands reverently folded in añjalī, verses of praise, and so on; “honor” means circling it clockwise; and “tend to” means removing leftover devotional offerings. RK: likhitve ti svayam| pustakagatāṃ vā kṛtve ti pareṇa| divyābhir iti viśiṣṭābhiḥ| javā[bhisa]ktapuṣpādi­varjanāt| puṣpā ṇi muktakusumāni| mālyā ni srajaḥ| gandhā gandha dārūṇi| vilepanā ni sa (ta)māla bhavāni| cūrṇā agarucandanādīnām| cīvarā ṇi vastrāṇi| pūjā bhir iti nivedyādibhiḥ| bahuvidhā bhir iti bhakṣyalehyādibhedāt| puṣpādibhiḥ satkuryād iti sambandhaḥ, pūjayed ity arthaḥ| gurukuryād iti gauraveṇa| mānayed iti premnā(mṇā)| pūjayed iti praṇāmāñjali­stutyādibhiḥ| arcayed iti dakṣiṇāvartaiḥ| apacāyed iti nirmālyādyapanayanaiḥ. But now suppose that another noble son or daughter were to place in stūpas the physical relics of those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who had passed into complete nirvāṇa, enclose them, maintain them, pay tribute to them‍—with heavenly flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate them‍—revere them, respect them, venerate them, honor them, and tend to them. Which of these two noble sons or daughters would earn more merit?”

In response, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “To explain this, Kauśika, in return I will ask you yourself some questions about this topic. Answer them as you please. Kauśika, what do you think? By training in what path did the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata‍—that is to say, the physical body of the state of complete knowing manifested by the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata‍—attain the state of complete knowing and realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”This section of the chapter involves a play on the multivalent Sanskrit word śarīra (translated here as “body” or “physical relic”) and its synonym ātmabhāva (translated here as “physical body” or adjectivally “own unique physical”). According to Hari (209), here “the physical body of the state of complete knowing manifested” means the produced bodily nature that is appropriate for the realization of the state of complete knowing. Hari: sarva­jñatātmabhāvo ’bhinirvartāta iti sarva­jñajñānādhigama­yogyaśarīra­svabhāvo niṣpāditaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.69.a), that “physical body” that is “manifested” is nothing but “the state of complete knowing.” Cf. RK: yo ’yaṃ sarvajñatātmabhāva iti sarvajñata ivātmabhāvaḥ, sa nirvartitaḥ.

Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, replied to the Blessed One, “The worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata attained the state of complete knowing and realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening by training in precisely this transcendent state of wisdom.”Hari and Ratnākara explain the following passages differently, because of their disagreement about the fundamental relationship between the transcendent state of wisdom, the state of complete knowing, and a buddha who has complete knowing, as mentioned previously in the notes to chapter 1.

The Blessed One said, “Therefore, Kauśika, the Tathāgata is not considered the Tathāgata because of obtaining his own unique physical body.According to Ratnākara, the term “own unique physical body” (ātmabhāva) is used because it is designated as self, and as a “body” refers to his body with physical form (rūpakāya). Cf. RK: ātmabhāvaś cāsau ātmaprajñapteḥ śarīraṃ ca rūpakāyas. Rather, the Tathāgata is considered the Tathāgata because he obtained the state of complete knowing. This state of complete knowing is born out of the transcendent state of wisdom. Also, Kauśika, the Tathāgata’s obtaining of his own unique physical body‍—which is born from the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means‍—is, in effect, a repository for the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. In dependence upon this as a repository, the knowing of someone who has complete knowing is revealed, the body of the Buddha is revealed, the body of the Dharma is revealed, and the body of the Saṅgha is revealed.According to Hari (211), “this” refers to “the obtaining of his own physical body.” The word “revealed” means explained (by the Buddha). Here, “buddha” refers to that whose nature is the body with physical form (rūpakāya) and so on. Hari: enam ity ātma­bhāva­śarīra­pratilambham. prabhāvane ti prakāśanā. buddho rūpakāyādisvabhāvaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.69.b), the four being “revealed” means they are ascertained in the world. RK: loke pratītiḥ prabhāvanā. Thus, since the obtainment of his own unique physical body‍—which is caused by the knowing of someone who has complete knowing‍—is, in effect, a repository for the knowing of someone who has complete knowing, it serves all sentient beings as a stūpa, in effect, that is worthy of obeisance, worthy of tribute, worthy of respect, worthy of offering, worthy of honor, and worthy of being tended to. Because of this, after I have passed away into complete nirvāṇa, my physical relics should be venerated. Therefore, Kauśika, of those two noble sons or daughters, the one who would earn more merit is the one who would write this transcendent state of wisdom down or make it into a book, preserve it, and pay tribute to it‍—with heavenly flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it. Why is that? It is because that noble son or daughter would be making devotional offerings to venerate it as the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. For that reason, any noble son or daughter would earn more merit if he or she were to pay tribute to that transcendent state of wisdom that is written out or made into a book, revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, tend to it, and make various devotional offerings to venerate it. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, anyone who makes devotional offerings to venerate the transcendent state of wisdom makes devotional offerings to venerate the knowing of someone who has complete knowing.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, there are humans on this Jambu continent who do not write out the transcendent state of wisdom, do not take it in, do not keep it in mind, do not chant it, do not master it, do not pass it on, do not teach it, do not expound it, do not explain it, and do not recite it.According to Ratnākara (F.69.b), in this context the first set of activities from “write” it to “master” it are for one’s own benefit. The activities that follow are for the benefit of others. Among those benefiting others, “pass it on” is the overview. The next three verbs flush out the overview: “teach” according to its meaning; “expound it,” which means give detailed instructions; “explain” based on the text; and “recite,” which means the repeated practice of reciting. RK: likhiṣyanti yāvat paryavāpsyantī ti svārthaḥ| tata ūrdhva parārthaḥ| tatra pravartayiṣyantī ty uddeśaḥ| tribhir nirdeśaḥ| deśayiṣyantī ty arthataḥ| upadekṣyantī tity avadiṣyanti| uddekṣyantī ti granthataḥ| svādhyāsyantī ti svādhyāyam ity abhyāsaḥ. They also do not pay tribute to it‍—with heavenly flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, or a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—do not revere it, do not respect it, do not venerate it, do not honor it, and do not tend to it. But, O Blessed One, how could they not know that the Blessed One has taught that making devotional offerings to venerate the transcendent state of wisdom is extremely effective in this way? How could they not be aware that the Blessed One has taught that making devotional offerings to venerate the transcendent state of wisdom is extremely beneficial in this way, extremely fruitful in this way, and extremely rewarding in this way? Also, how could they not be made to know? Or if they do know, are aware, or are made to know, then how could they still not have faith in it?”According to Ratnākara (F.69.b), “not be aware” means they do not hear of it through words; “not know” means they do not come across a book; and “not be made to know” means they do not make themselves aware of the meaning. The words “or if” indicate a different angle on his question. RK: na jñāsyantīti na śroṣyanti śabdataḥ| na vetsyantī ti pustakaṃ na lapsyante| na vedayiṣyantī ti vedanaṃ vedaḥ arthajñānam| tat karotīti ṇic| arthaṃ na jñāsyantīty arthaḥ| ute ti prakārāntaram. Note: This reading follows the Skt. and Ratnākara’s comments, not the Tibetan, in which the differences between the parts of the question are not brought out.

In response, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, what do you think? How many humans on this Jambu continent have clear, knowledge-based trust in the Buddha; clear, knowledge-based trust in the Dharma; and clear, knowledge-based trust in the Saṅgha?”According to Ratnākara (F.69.b), the phrase “knowledge-based” here means based on having understood the four noble truths by means of their spiritual knowing. The words “clear trust” refer to the mental factor due to which the mind is made absolutely free of dirt, like water made free of dirt by a jewel that clarifies (prasāda) the water. RK: avetye ti lokottareṇa jñānena satyān jñātvā| prasādaḥ cittagato dharmaḥ| yena cittam atyantam akaluṣīkriyate| udakam iva dakaprasādena maṇinā. Cf DOP p. 259, avetyaprasādena, “trust founded on knowledge,” “intelligent trust”‍—citing almost verbatim this quote from Pali sources. Cf. PTSD avecca, “certainly”; -pasādo, “perfect faith.” Saṃyuttanikāya 229 (yo ariyasaccāni avecca passati).

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “There are very few humans on this Jambu continent who have clear, knowledge-based trust in the Buddha; clear, knowledge-based trust in the Dharma; and clear, knowledge-based trust in the Saṅgha.”

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “You are right, Kāuśika. You are right. There are extremely few humans on this Jambu continent who have clear, knowledge-based trust in the Buddha; clear, knowledge-based trust in the Dharma; and clear, knowledge-based trust in the Saṅgha. Kauśika, even fewer than these are those who reach the fruit of a stream enterer, a once-returner, or a non-returner. Even fewer than these are those who reach the state of a worthy one.According to Ratnākara (F.70.a), these are the four fruits (of the Disciple Vehicle): the first fruit is being the “stream enterer” is itself; the second and third fruit is being a “once-returner” and a “non-returner.” The fourth fruit is the state of a worthy one (arhat). RK: śrotasa āpattiḥ saiva phalam| sakṛdāgāmino ’nāgāminaś ca phalam| arhato bhāvo ’rhatvaṃ caturthaphalam. Even fewer than these are those who actualize a solitary realizer’s awakening. Even fewer than these are those who develop the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Even fewer than these are those who develop the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and then nourish that development of the proper mindset. Even fewer than these are those who develop the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening, then nourish that development of the proper mindset, and then continue to apply themselves with heroic perseverance.According to Ratnākara (F.70.a), “heroic perseverance” means enthusiastic effort in virtuous deeds. RK: vīryaṃ kuśale karmaṇy utsāhaḥ. Even fewer than these are those who engage in meditation. Even fewer than these are those who go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Even fewer than these are those who, in going about life and engaging in meditation in the transcendent state of wisdom, reside on the level of a bodhisattva beyond regressing. Even fewer than these are those who, by going about life and exerting themselves in the transcendent state of wisdom, will come to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Even fewer than these are those who, in going about life and exerting themselves in the transcendent state of wisdom, are presently realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Those bodhisattva great beings who reside on the level of a bodhisattva beyond regressing and then realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening come to expound the transcendent state of wisdom and instruct other noble sons and daughters with the altruistic disposition, exerting themselves and training in the transcendent state of wisdom. They also take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, pay tribute to it‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it. But, Kauśika, suppose that immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings were to develop the mindset of awakening. Or suppose that they were to develop the mindset of awakening and nourish it further. Or suppose that they were to nourish it further and go about life with the aim of awakening. Even if they were to do so, Kauśika, there would be but one or two among those immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings going about life with the aim of awakening who would actually come to reside on the level of a bodhisattva beyond regressing. Why is that? It is because the weak-willed who have slothful, lackluster perseverance, weak mindsets, faulty conceptions, insufficient conviction, and inferior wisdom have difficulty attaining unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.70.a), “weak-willed” means unsteady; “weak mindsets” means poor concentration; “faulty conceptions” means faulty mindfulness; “insufficient conviction” means little faith; and “inferior wisdom” means little investigation of reality. The feebleness of their (five) powers, such as faith and so on, is expressed by these words about their lackluster perseverance and so on. Among those, (a) “slothful, lackluster perseverance” pertains to their power of perseverance; (b) “weak mindsets” pertains to their the power of concentration; (c) “faulty conceptions” pertains to their power of mindfulness; (d) “insufficient conviction” pertains to their power of faith; and (e) “inferior wisdom” (hīnaprajñā) pertains to their power of wisdom. RK: sattvaṃ dhairyaḥ| cittaṃ samādhiḥ| saṃjñā smṛtiḥ| adhimuktiḥ śraddhā| tattvapravicayaḥ prajñā| śraddhādindriyāṇāṃ daurbalyaṃ hīnavīryādipadair ucyate| tatra hīnavīryaiḥ kusīdair iti viryendriyasya hīnasattvaiḥ| hīnacittair iti samādhīndriyasya| hīnasaṃjñair iti smṛtīndriyasya| hīnādhimuktikair iti śraddhendriyasya| hīnaprajñair iti prajñendriyasya. Therefore, Kauśika, a noble son or daughter who wishes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening would joyfully and constantly listen to this transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and fully pursue questions about it.According to Ratnākara (F.69.b), he or she “constantly” listens and so on, and fully pursues questions in order to cut through doubts. RK: abhīkṣṇaṃ śravaṇādi paripraśnaś ca saṃśayacchedārthaḥ. Why is that? It is because he or she would recognize, ‘The worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata previously practiced the bodhisattva’s way of going about life and trained in this transcendent state of wisdom. Thus, we too should train in it since he is our teacher.’ Kauśika, whether the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata is present or has passed into complete nirvāṇa, the bodhisattva great being should rely on the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Kauśika, then suppose that, in order to venerate a worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata who had passed into nirvāṇa, a noble son or daughter were to build tens of millions of stūpas out of the seven precious substances and enshrine the relics of the tathāgatas within them.According to Ratnākara (F.69.b), the “seven precious substances” are gold, silver, beryl (vaiḍūrya), coral, emerald, red pearl, and white pearl. RK: saptaratnāni tadyathā suvarṇa rūpyaṃ vaiḍūrya musāragalvaṃ aśmagarbhaḥ lohitikā muktā karketanaṃ ca. Also, suppose that, after building them, that noble son or daughter were to pay tribute to those stūpas‍—with heavenly flowers, heavenly incense, heavenly perfumes, heavenly wreaths, heavenly fragrant oils, heavenly aromatic powders, heavenly fabrics, heavenly parasols, heavenly banners, heavenly bells, heavenly flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate them‍—revere them, respect them, venerate them, honor them, and tend to them. Kauśika, what do you think? Based on that, would this noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit?”
“A great amount, O Blessed One. A great amount, O Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “In comparison, Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit would be earned by a noble son or daughter who did the following.The description that follows in the Sanskrit and Tibetan texts is all included in one extremely long sentence that carries on for over one full folio. It has been broken into several sentences here for the sake of readability. A few transition words have been added to help get the sense across. First, with intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening. Then, based on this altruistic disposition, he or she would listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it.According to Ratnākara (F.76.a–b), one should “listen to it” based on the text and the meaning; “take it in” means repeat it; “keep it in mind” means make it stay in one’s heart; “chant” means read aloud; and “master” means completely understand. Up to here is taught for one’s own benefit. The words “pass it on” mean transmit it to others‍—this is the overview. The next three verbs are the detailed explanation of this: One should “teach” based on speaking about the text; “expound” based on the meaning; and “explain” means instruct. Up to here is taught for the benefit of others. Finally, “recite it” means repeat it more and more because it brings benefit to oneself again and again. RK: tena śṛṇuyād granthato ’rthataś ca| udgṛhṇīyād āvartayet| dhārayed iti hṛdaye sthāpayet| vācayet pāṭhayet| paryavāpnuyād iti sarvathā avagaccheta| iyatā svārtho uktaḥ| pravartayed iti pareṣu sañcārayet| ayam uddeśaḥ| paro nirdeśaḥ| deśayed granthato bhāṣaṇāt| upadiśed arthataḥ| uddiśed ity avavadet| iyato parārthaḥ| svādhyāyed iti sutarām abhyaset| punaḥ punaḥ svārthakaraṇāt. Ratnākara’s comments made later in the text, from the beginning of chapter 5 where these exact same terms are repeated (see 5.1; and notes n.360–n.363), have been copied here (into this note and the three that follow) in order to give readers some context for understanding the terminology here, since neither Hari nor Ratnākara comment on them specifically in this first occurrence of the passage. Next, for the sake of others, he or she would elucidate it in detail, unpack its meaning, mentally investigate it, and fully examine the way it is with exceeding wisdom.According to Ratnākara’s creative etymologies annotated to the later parallel passage in chapter 5 (F.76.b, see 5.1; and n.361), “elucidate” (samprakāśayed) means consistently (sam-/santata) make clear (-prakāśayed) the transcendent state of wisdom based on the words in the text. The phrase “in detail” means doing so from multiple angles. The word “unpack” means expanding upon those words. The phrase “mentally investigate it” means one should learn it by heart, connect with it, and observe it, because these steps produce tranquility (śamatha). Next, one should “fully examine it,” because thinking analytically about it produces insight (vipāśyanā). Up to here, he explains what should be done. But if that is not possible, he explains another possibility, with the words “or…at the very least,” and so on. RK: parebhyaś ca samprakāśayed iti san tataṃ prakāśayed granthataḥ| vistareṇe ti bahubhyaḥ prakāśanāt| vivṛṇuyād iti vi stareṇeti vartate| manasā ’nvavekṣete ti cittenānubudhya saṃyojya paśyet| sa(śa)mathotpādanāt| parimīmāṃsām iti vicārāṇāṃ vipaśyanotpādanāt| iyatā caryoktā| asyā abhāve pakṣāntaram āha| pustake ty ādinā| ata eva antaśaḥ śabdaḥ. Or if not all that, then at the very least, this noble son or daughter would make the transcendent state of wisdom into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that these genuine teachings would last for a long time, while praying,According to Ratnākara (F.76.b, see n.362), one should “maintain” precisely this “book,” meaning that one should keep it from perishing (which in India would have been done with string and two slabs of wood to protect it). One should “preserve” this in one’s home and so on. RK: pustakagatām iti| mam eva dhārayed iti| avinaśvarī kuryāt| sthāpayed iti svagṛhādau. ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’According to Hari (218), the “guidance” refers to this very scripture that is the Mother Transcendent State of Wisdom and is the guidance of the buddhas. Since we cannot read, study, and so on without a book of the Transcendent State of Wisdom, he says may it “not be cut off.” Hari: mā buddhanetrī tyādi. buddhā nāṃ netrī nāyikā mātā prajñāpāramitaiva. asyāḥ pustakavaikalyena pāṭhasvādhyāyā dyasambhavāt samucchedo mā bhūt. According to Ratnākara (F.76.b–77.a, see n.363), the “buddhas” are the gurus of the world; we should show gratitude toward them. Their “guidance” is the wisdom that brings one to awakening. After wandering for three countless kalpas, they first developed it and then established it perfectly within their own tradition for the benefit of future beings. Since it has now reached us, we should not let “the buddhas’ guidance here be cut off”‍—meaning we should make sure it remains. To “provide assistance (anugrahopasaṃhāraḥ kṛta; phan gdags pa) to bodhisattva great beings” refers to what should be accomplished. And, it will be accomplished by “this guidance’s continued existence,” i.e., by its remaining in the world. RK: buddhāś ca lokaguravas teṣāṃ ca kṛtajñair asmābhir bhavitavyam| teṣām iyaṃ netrī bodheḥ prāpikā prajñā| tais trikalpāsaṃkhyeyā pariśrameṇotpādya samyag anāgatajanatārthāya svavaṃśe ’vasthāpitā| yāvad asmān upagatā| tato mā buddhanetrī samucchedo bhūd ity apīmāṃ sthāpayet|… bodhisattvānāṃ cānugrahopasaṃhāraḥ karaṇīyaḥ| sa ca kṛto bhaviṣyati| anayā sthāyitayā netryavaikalyena. Note: The Sanskrit word netrī means “guide rope” and can be translated more abstractly as “way” or even “guideline,” but it refers here more to the material teachings that help guide beings to awakening. Cf. DOP, Part II, pp. 639–640 s.v. netti.

“Also, he or she would pay tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it. Then, even by just doing this, this noble son or daughter would earn much more merit than the former one.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the tens of millions of stūpas built out of the seven precious substances to enshrine the relics of the tathāgatas within them. Suppose, Kauśika, that a noble son or daughter were instead to fill the entire Jambu continent with stūpas made out of the seven precious substances and enshrine the relics of the tathāgatas within them. Also, suppose that, throughout his or her life, that noble son or daughter were to pay tribute to them‍—with heavenly flowers, heavenly incense, heavenly perfumes, heavenly wreaths, heavenly fragrant oils, heavenly aromatic powders, heavenly fabrics, heavenly parasols, heavenly banners, heavenly bells, heavenly flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate them‍—revere them, respect them, venerate them, honor them, and tend to them. Kauśika, what do you think? Based on that, would this first noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit?”
“A great amount, O Blessed One. A great amount, O Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “In comparison, Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit would be earned by a noble son or daughter who did the following. First, with intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening.According to Hari (218), “a clear mindset that trusts” is a mindset that is well disposed because one sees the goodness (in the transcendent state of wisdom). That he or she would “develop the mindset of awakening” means that they would produce the particular mindset based on the bodhisattva’s vow that causes complete, perfect awakening. Hari: prasannacitta iti bhadratādarśanād abhimukhacittaḥ. bodhāya cittam utpādye ty anuttara­samyak­sambodhi­nimittaṃ bodhi­sattva­saṃvaragrahaṇād viśiṣṭaṃ cittaṃ kṛtvā. Then, based on this altruistic disposition, he or she would listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Next, for the sake of others, he or she would elucidate it in detail, unpack its meaning, mentally investigate it, and fully examine the way it is with exceeding wisdom. Or if not all that, then suppose that, at the very least, this noble son or daughter would make the transcendent state of wisdom into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that these genuine teachings would last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“Also, he or she would pay tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it. Then, even by just doing this, this noble son or daughter would earn much more merit than the former one.The Buddha’s comparison (between the benefits of venerating stūpas and the benefits of venerating the transcendent state of wisdom) repeats a number of times below with only slight variation before moving to a different topic. Although Conze paraphrases this passage and eliminates all repetitions, this repetition seems to be an integral part of the scripture and likely serves several purpose here, one of which seems aimed at helping readers exhaust their conceptual limits performatively.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind this Jambu continent being filled with stūpas made out of the seven precious substances and enshrining the relics of the tathāgatas within them. Kauśika, suppose instead that every single sentient being in the world system of four great continents were to build a single stūpa out of the seven precious substances and enshrine the relics of the tathāgatas within it. Also, suppose that, throughout their lives, they were to pay tribute to these stūpas‍—with heavenly flowers, heavenly incense, heavenly perfumes, heavenly wreaths, heavenly fragrant oils, heavenly aromatic powders, heavenly fabrics, heavenly parasols, heavenly banners, heavenly bells, heavenly flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate them‍—revere them, respect them, venerate them, honor them, and tend to them. Kauśika, what do you think in this case? Based on that, would all these beings earn a great amount of merit?”
“A great amount, O Blessed One. A great amount, O Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “In comparison, Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit would be earned by a noble son or daughter who did the following. First, with intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening. Then, based on this altruistic disposition, he or she would listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Next, for the sake of others, he or she would elucidate it in detail, unpack its meaning, mentally investigate it, and fully examine the way it is with exceeding wisdom. Or if not all that, then at the very least, this noble son or daughter would make the transcendent state of wisdom into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that these genuine teachings would last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“Also, he or she would pay tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it. Then, this noble son or daughter would earn much more merit than those sentient beings just mentioned.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind every single sentient being in the four-continent world system. Suppose instead that every single sentient being in a thousandfold, lesser order world system were to build a single stūpa out of the seven precious substances and enshrine the relics of the tathāgatas within it. Also, suppose that, throughout their lives, they were to pay tribute to them‍—with heavenly flowers, heavenly incense, heavenly perfumes, heavenly wreaths, heavenly fragrant oils, heavenly aromatic powders, heavenly fabrics, heavenly parasols, heavenly banners, heavenly bells, heavenly flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate them‍—revere them, respect them, venerate them, honor them, and tend to them. Kauśika, what would you think in this case? Based on that, would all these beings earn a great amount of merit?”
“A great amount, O Blessed One. A great amount, O Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “In comparison, Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit would be earned by a noble son or daughter who did the following. First, with intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening. Then, based on this altruistic disposition, he or she would listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Next, for the sake of others, he or she would elucidate it in detail, unpack its meaning, mentally investigate it, and fully examine the way it is with exceeding wisdom. Or if not all that, then at the very least, this noble son or daughter would make the transcendent state of wisdom into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that these genuine teachings would last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“Also, he or she would pay tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it. Then, even by just doing this, this noble son or daughter would earn much more merit than the former one.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind every single sentient being in a thousandfold, lesser order world system. Suppose instead that every single sentient being in a millionfold, medium order world system were to build a single stūpa out of the seven precious substances and enshrine the relics of the tathāgatas within it. Also, suppose that, throughout their lives, they were to pay tribute to them‍—with heavenly flowers, heavenly incense, heavenly perfumes, heavenly wreaths, heavenly fragrant oils, heavenly aromatic powders, heavenly fabrics, heavenly parasols, heavenly banners, heavenly bells, heavenly flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate them‍—revere them, respect them, venerate them, honor them, and tend to them. Then, Kauśika, what would you think in this case? Based on that, would all these beings earn a great amount of merit?”
“A great amount, O Blessed One. A great amount, O Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “In comparison, Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit would be earned by a noble son or daughter who did the following. First, with intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening. Then, based on this altruistic disposition, he or she would listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Next, for the sake of others, he or she would elucidate it in detail, unpack its meaning, mentally investigate it, and fully examine the way it is with exceeding wisdom. Or if not all that, then at the very least, this noble son or daughter would make the transcendent state of wisdom into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that these genuine teachings would last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“Also, he or she would pay tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it. Then, even by just doing this, this noble son or daughter would earn much more merit than those sentient beings.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind every single sentient being that there is in a millionfold, medium order world system. Suppose instead that every single sentient being in a billionfold, great order world system were to build a single stūpa out of the seven precious substances and enshrine the relics of the tathāgatas within it. Also, suppose that throughout their lives they were to pay tribute to them‍—with heavenly flowers, heavenly incense, heavenly perfumes, heavenly wreaths, heavenly fragrant oils, heavenly aromatic powders, heavenly fabrics, heavenly parasols, heavenly banners, heavenly bells, heavenly flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate them‍—revere them, respect them, venerate them, honor them, and tend to them. Kauśika, what would you think in this case? Based on that, would all these beings earn a great amount of merit?”
“A great amount, O Blessed One. A great amount, O Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “In comparison, Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit would be earned by a noble son or daughter who did the following. First, with intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening. Then, based on this altruistic disposition, he or she would listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Next, for the sake of others, he or she would elucidate it in detail, unpack its meaning, mentally investigate it, and fully examine the way it is with exceeding wisdom. Or if not all that, then at the very least, this noble son or daughter would make the transcendent state of wisdom into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that these genuine teachings would last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“Also, he or she would pay tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it. Then, even by just doing this, this noble son or daughter would earn much more merit than those sentient beings.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind every single sentient being in a billionfold, great order world system building a single stūpa out of the seven precious substances, enshrining the relics of the tathāgatas within them, and, throughout their lives, paying tribute to them‍—with heavenly flowers, heavenly incense, heavenly perfumes, heavenly wreaths, heavenly fragrant oils, heavenly aromatic powders, heavenly fabrics, heavenly parasols, heavenly banners, heavenly bells, heavenly flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate them‍—revering them, respecting them, venerating them, honoring them, and tending to them. Instead, Kauśika, imagine that all those sentient beings in a billionfold, great order world system also simultaneously obtained his or her own unique human body. Then, imagine that each human built a single stūpa out of the seven precious substances and enshrined the relics of the tathāgatas within them. Then, imagine that each human built the total number of all those stūpas put together. Also, imagine that after building and establishing them, throughout an entire eon or the remainder of an eon, each human paid tribute to them‍—with all the speeches, all the songs, all the dances, all the performances of musical and percussive instruments, all the flowers, all the heavenly incense, all the heavenly perfumes, all the heavenly wreaths, all the heavenly fragrant oils, all the heavenly aromatic powders, all the heavenly fabrics, all the heavenly parasols, all the heavenly banners, all the heavenly bells, all the heavenly flags, all the heavenly garlands of lights all around, and all the wide varieties of human and heavenly devotional offerings to venerate them‍—revered them, respected them, venerated them, honored them, and tended to them. Now, suppose that all these sentient beings were to establish immeasurable and innumerable stūpas with these kinds of meritorious activities and make these kinds of devotional offerings to venerate them. Kauśika, what would you think in this case? Based on that, would all these beings earn a great amount of merit?”
“A great amount, O Blessed One. A great amount, O Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “In comparison, Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit would be earned by a noble son or daughter who did the following. First, with intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening. Then, based on this altruistic disposition, he or she would listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Next, for the sake of others, he or she would elucidate it in detail, unpack its meaning, mentally investigate it, and fully examine the way it is with exceeding wisdom. Or if not all that, then at the very least, this noble son or daughter would make the transcendent state of wisdom into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that these genuine teachings would last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“Also, he or she would pay tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it. Then, this noble son or daughter would earn much more merit than all those sentient beings.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “So it is, Blessed One. So it is, Sugata. This is because, Blessed One, a son or daughter who pays tribute to the transcendent state of wisdom, reveres it, respects it, offers to it, honors it, and tends to it is absolutely paying tribute to the past, present, and future blessed buddhas in all the world systems‍—known through the Buddha’s knowing [of them]‍—revering them, respecting them, venerating them, honoring them, and tending to them.

“But, O Blessed One, never mind all those sentient beings in a billionfold, great order world system as you just described them. Suppose instead that all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, Blessed One, were each in turn to build a single stūpa out of the seven precious substances and to enshrine the relics of the tathāgatas within it, and then each sentient being individually were to build the total number of all those stūpas. Then, suppose that, after building and establishing them, they were, for an entire eon or more than an eon, to pay tribute to these stūpas‍—with all the words, all the songs, all the dances, all the performances of musical and percussive instruments, all the heavenly flowers, all the heavenly incense, all the heavenly perfumes, all the heavenly wreaths, all the heavenly fragrant oils, all the heavenly aromatic powders, all the heavenly fabrics, all the heavenly parasols, all the heavenly banners, all the heavenly bells, all the heavenly flags, all the garlands of lights all around, and with all the wide varieties of human and heavenly devotional offerings to venerate them‍—to revere them, to respect them, to venerate them, to honor them, and to tend to them.

“In comparison to all those sentient beings, Blessed One, this noble son or daughter would earn much more merit by doing the following. First, with intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening. Then, based on this altruistic disposition, he or she would listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Next, for the sake of others, he or she would elucidate it in detail, unpack its meaning, mentally investigate it, and fully examine the way it is with exceeding wisdom. Or if not all that, then at the very least, this noble son or daughter would make the transcendent state of wisdom into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that these genuine teachings would last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“Also, he or she would pay tribute to the transcendent state of wisdom, revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it, and offer it flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it. Then, even by just doing this, this noble son or daughter would earn much more merit than those sentient beings.”This paragraph follows the Sanskrit and Tibetan description of the offerings, which differs slightly from the above, even though neither commentator mentions it. The Sanskrit edition is lacking this final sentence, which appears in the Tibetan and has been included here, since it matches previous passages above.

Then the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “So it is, Kauśika. So it is. Based on this, that noble son or daughter would earn more merit. Based on this, that noble son or daughter would earn immeasurable merit. Based on this, that noble son or daughter would earn incalculable merit. Based on this, that noble son or daughter would earn inconceivable merit. Based on this, that noble son or daughter would earn unequaled merit. Based on this, that noble son or daughter would earn unlimited merit. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, the state of complete knowing of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas is born from the transcendent state of wisdom, whereas the offering that venerates the tathāgatas’ physical relics is something born from the state of complete knowing.Neither Hari nor Ratnākara explains this particular sentence. But since they make comments in other contexts about the relationship between the transcendent state of wisdom and the knowing of someone who has complete knowing (as mentioned in n.112), we might refer to those here. According to Ratnākara’s general viewpoint, the transcendent state of wisdom and the knowing of someone who has complete knowing are not mainly synonyms but are related as a cause and effect. So, for Ratnākara, this passage would suggest, more straightforwardly, that one should venerate the cause (the transcendent state of wisdom) of the state of complete knowing (which as a tathāgata is already appreciated), more than its results (the physical relics left by the tathāgata). According to Hari’s comments on another passage (2011–12), Dignāga’s Pīṇḍārtha 1.1 says, “The transcendent state of wisdom is the Tathāgata, the nondual knowing.” According to this principle, the transcendent state of wisdom is mainly just the Tathāgata, the body of truth (dharmakāya). For this reason, the text says “more merit would be earned” because one would be venerating the body of truth (dharmakāya), which is the knowing of someone who has complete knowing, through venerating her (figurative quality) as the mother (of the buddhas), which sheds light on that (body of truth itself). But, in regard to venerating the Tathāgata through his reflection‍—i.e., his body with physical form (rūpakāya)‍—the text suggests that there is not much merit because of not grasping the body of truth as superior.
