Hari: prajñāpāramitā jñānam advayaṃ sā tathāgata ity nyāyān mukhyato dharmakāyas tathāgata eva prajñāpāramitā. atas tad dyotikāyā mātuḥ pūjayā sarvajñajñānasya dharmakāyasya pūjanād bahutaraṃ puṇyaṃ prasavati. rūpa­kāya­prati­bimba­tathāgatapūjāyāṃ tu prati­viśiṣṭa­dharma­kāyāsaṃgrahān nādhikaṃ puṇyam iti.

“Therefore, Kauśika, a noble son or daughter should do the following. First, with intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she should develop the mindset of awakening. Then, based on this altruistic disposition, he or she should listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Next, for the sake of others, he or she should elucidate it in detail, unpack its meaning, mentally investigate it, and fully examine the way it is with exceeding wisdom. Or if not all that, then at the very least, this noble son or daughter should make the transcendent state of wisdom into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that these genuine teachings would last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“Also, he or she should pay tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it.

“Kauśika, that former accumulation of merit‍—derived from building stūpas out of the seven precious substances and enshrining the relics of the tathāgatas within them‍—does not come close in worth to even a fraction of this accumulation of merit. It does not come close in worth to one hundredth, one thousandth, one hundred thousandth, one ten millionth, one billionth, one ten billionth, one trillionth, or even one ten trillionth of it. Nor is any calculation, any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison adequate for comparing them.”This passage was translated following Ratnākara’s multiple word glosses (F71.a–b) such as tulanāya na kṣamate. Cf. BHSD kṣamati.

Then those forty thousand gods‍—who had alighted among that very assembly together with Śakra, powerful lord of the gods‍—said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Lord, please take in the transcendent state of wisdom. Lord, the transcendent state of wisdom should be taken in.According to Ratnākara, “ ‘Lord’ (marṣa) is a form of address whose meaning is ‘noble one.’ ” RK: mārṣe ti āmantraṇam| āry etyarthaḥ. Lord, the transcendent state of wisdom should be kept in mind. Lord, the transcendent state of wisdom should be chanted. Lord, the transcendent state of wisdom should be mastered. Lord, the transcendent state of wisdom should be passed on. Lord, the transcendent state of wisdom should be taught. Lord, the transcendent state of wisdom should be expounded. Lord, the transcendent state of wisdom should be explained. Lord, the transcendent state of wisdom should be recited.”

The Blessed One then said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, take in the transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, keep the transcendent state of wisdom in mind. Kauśika, chant the transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, master the transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, pass on the transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, teach the transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, expound the transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, explain the transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, recite the transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, the asuras might come to develop the following intentions: ‘Let us fight the gods in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three. Let us wage war against the gods in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three.’
“But, at that time, Kauśika, as a consequence of your remembering and reciting this transcendent state of wisdom, the intentions of the asuras will simply disappear.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, it is a great magical incantation, this transcendent state of wisdom. O Blessed One, it is an unlimited magical incantation, this transcendent state of wisdom. O Blessed One, it is a magical incantation without superior, this transcendent state of wisdom. O Blessed One, it is an unsurpassed magical incantation, this transcendent state of wisdom. O Blessed One, it is a magical incantation without equal, this transcendent state of wisdom. O Blessed One, it is a magical incantation equal to the unequaled, this transcendent state of wisdom.”

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “So it is, Kauśika, so it is. Kauśika, it is a great magical incantation, this transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, it is an unlimited magical incantation, this transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, it is a magical incantation without superior, this transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, it is an unsurpassed magical incantation, this transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, it is a magical incantation without equal, this transcendent state of wisdom. Kauśika, it is a magical incantation equal to the unequaled, this transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, by virtue of precisely this magical incantation‍—namely, the transcendent state of wisdom‍—the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas of the past have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Kauśika, also by virtue of precisely this magical incantation‍—namely, the transcendent state of wisdom‍—those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who come to be worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas at a future time will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Kauśika, also by virtue of precisely this magical incantation‍—namely, the transcendent state of wisdom‍—that those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who are at present worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas in the immeasurable and innumerable billionfold, great order world systems have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Also by virtue of precisely this magical incantation‍—namely, the transcendent state of wisdom‍—I myself have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

This point in the chapter corresponds (more or less) to the beginning of Chapter 19 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 28 in the Eighteen Thousand.“By virtue of precisely this magical incantation‍—namely, the transcendent state of wisdom‍—the ten virtuous ways of acting appear in the world, the four meditative states conducive to awakening appear in the world, the four immeasurable states conducive to awakening appear in the world, the four formless attainments governed by the factors in awakening appear in the world, the six types of extrasensory knowledge conducive to awakening appear in the world, and the thirty-seven factors in awakening appear in the world. In brief, by virtue of this magical incantation alone‍—namely, the transcendent state of wisdom‍—the eighty-four thousand categories of Dharma teachings develop in the world, and the knowing of a buddha, the knowing of a self-existent one, and the knowing of an inconceivable one appear in the world.

“Also, Kauśika, when there are no worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas appearing in the world, those bodhisattva great beings‍—who are compassionate and skillful in means as a natural outcome of their having heard the transcendent state of wisdom previously‍—come to this world out of their compassion for sentient beings. They cause the ten virtuous ways of acting to appear in the world. They cause the four meditative states conducive to awakeningIn contrast to the Sanskrit text as translated here, following Vaidya’s edition and some others, the Tibetan has negatives (byang chub kyi yan lag dang mi ldan pa’i, “not conducive to awakening” or “not governed by the factors of awakening”) for this and the sets of factors that follow, and conforms to the Sanskrit of Mitra’s and Wogihara’s root texts which read bodhyaṅga-viprayuktāni here in place of bodhyaṅga-samprayuktāni as in the preceding passage (n.302). to appear in the world. They cause the four immeasurable states conducive to awakening to appear in the world. They cause the four formless attainments governed by the factors of awakening to appear in the world. They cause the five types of extrasensory knowledge conducive to awakening to appear in the world. As an analogy, it is just like [the disappearance of] the moon’s halo, which causes all the medicinal herbs, stars, and lunar mansions to shine forth in an intense and potent manner.The interpretation of this analogy (also to be found in the long sūtras, e.g. the Twenty-Five Thousand at 19.14) is somewhat difficult. Hari suggests that oṣadhītārāḥ can be understood as a dvandvā (like the Tibetan sman dang skar ma) or a karmadhāraya compound. We have followed the Tibetan but it is worth noting that Hari (75) takes oṣadhi to refer to annual plants (not necessarily medicinal) and tārā to refer to the two auspicious planets (not necessarily stars). Hari: oṣadhītārā iti oṣadhyaś ca vrīhyādayaḥ, tārāś ca śukrabṛhaspatiprabhṛtaya iti tathoktāḥ. osadhya eva vā kāścin niśi tārā ivāvabhāsamānās tathocyante. tāsāṃ candraraśmibhir āpyāyanāt kāyaparipuṣṭiḥ prabhāvātiśayaś cotpadyata iti yathākramam āha : yathābalaṃ yathāsthāmam iti. akārānto ’pi auṇādikaḥ sthāmaśabdo grāhyaḥ. avabhāsayantīti svabhāvaṃ daṛśayanti. nakṣatrāṇi ceti puṣyādayaḥ. See MW oṣadhī; PTSD osadhi, osadhitārakā. BHSD oṣadhi. Wogihara’s root text reads oṣadhītārā, whereas Vaidya reads oṣadhīḥ tārā. According to Bill Mak (email communication June 9, 2020), ancient Indians saw the brilliance of the sun and the moon as literally taking away the brilliance of the stars and lunar mansions. Also, in popular Indian literature, medicinal plants are said to be nourished by the light of the moon (a.k.a. oṣadhipati), which they absorb. These plants are then thought to shimmer in a star-like way that can be seen only when the moon disappears. Kauśika, in this very same way, when the genuine Dharma disappears through a worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata passing away, then no worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas appear. Hence, any dharmic ways of life, any impartial ways of life, any peaceful ways of life, and any virtuous ways of life that are wisely discerned and manifested in the world are born from the bodhisattvas, who cause them to manifest and set them in motion through their skill in means. And that skill in means of the bodhisattvas should be known as being born from the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.72.a), the (genuine) Dharma here means nirvāṇa, since it is the most sublime Dharma. That very Dharma is impartiality because the three vehicles maintain it. That very Dharma is peace because it pacifies the emotional defilements. The “ways of life” are aimed at each of those qualities. “Virtuous ways of life” means behaving in wholesome ways. RK: dharmo nirvāṇaṃ sarva­dharmotkṛṣṭatvāt| sa eva samaḥ yānatrayasya dhāraṇatvāt| sa eva sa (śa)maḥ kleśaduḥkhopasa(śa)matvāt| tasmai caryā| kuśalaṃ sucaritaṃ tasya caryā.

“Moreover, Kauśika, it is because of this fact that those noble sons or daughters‍—who have taken in this transcendent state of wisdom, kept it in mind, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, recited it, written it out, paid attention to it, and recalled it‍—can expect, in connection with that, many worldly benefits.”
At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the Blessed One, “What worldly benefits do the noble sons or daughters come to possess?”

The Blessed One said, “No noble son or daughter will die through unforeseen adversities. None will die by poison. None will die by a sword. None will die by fire. None will die by water. None will die by punishment.According to Ratnākara (F.72.a), “punishment” includes penitent fasting and so on. RK: daṇḍa upavāsanādiḥ. None will die from an attack by someone else. Also, the national crises‍—caused by kings, princes, royal ministers, or high-ranking government officials, over and over‍—will simply disappear when any noble son or daughter remembers the transcendent state of wisdom and recites it. Also, no kings, princes, royal ministers, or high-ranking government officials‍—who prey on weakness and seek ways to attack them‍—will be able to find any vulnerability in any noble son or daughter who repeatedly remembers the transcendent state of wisdom and recites it again and again, because he or she would be supported by the transcendent state of wisdom.The Sanskrit term parigṛhīta has been translated consistently as “supported by” so that readers will be able to track the same term as it used in slightly different ways throughout the scripture. One should understand “supported by” here to imply that a bodhisattva is “shielded” from harm by the transcendent state of wisdom. Any kings, princes, royal ministers, or high-ranking government officials who do come near the noble sons and daughters will wish instead to speak and converse with them, and will consider them to be befriended. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, this transcendent state of wisdom serves all beings by offering benevolence in the form of a benevolent mindset, and offering compassion in the form of a compassionate mindset.According to Ratnākara (F.72.a), its quality of supporting “attends” to those beings, because being that way is its inherent nature. RK: parigṛhītatvaṃ pratyupasthite ti| tatsvabhāvena tathābhāvāt. According to Hari (239), the term “transcendent state of wisdom” refers here to a bodhisattva cultivating it, because the context here is an explanation of the benefits that belong only to bodhisattvas. The word “benevolence” means something to be shown by a friend. “Offering” that means exhibiting that. Since that offering would be illusory and so on, he says “in the form of a benevolent mindset,” which basically means in the form of a mindset aimed at benefiting them. The “compassion” toward someone merely deserving to be shown compassion is based on a figurative usage of that term. “Offering” it means being moved in that direction. Since that also could involve a different motive, he says “in the form of a compassionate mindset” means in the form of a mindset aimed at removing suffering. So therefore, even in the middle of a wilderness full of wild animals and crawling creatures, Kauśika, no humans or nonhumans‍—who prey on weakness and seek ways to attack‍—will ever find a vulnerability in any noble son or daughter, except as the maturation of a previous action.”

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 20 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 29 in the Eighteen Thousand.Just then, one hundred non-Buddhist wandering ascetics were coming toward the Blessed One, intending to criticize him.

But, from far away, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, saw the non-Buddhist wandering ascetics coming. Upon seeing them, he thought to himself, “These non-Buddhist wandering ascetics are definitely coming toward the Blessed One, intending to criticize him. Let me recall, recite by memory, and pass on just the part of this transcendent state of wisdom that I have taken in from the Blessed One thus far, since it will make the non-Buddhist wandering ascetics unable to attack the Blessed One or to disrupt his discourse on the transcendent state of wisdom.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, recalled by memory, recited, and passed on just the part of this transcendent state of wisdom that he had taken in from the Blessed One. At once those non-Buddhist wandering ascetics circumambulated the Blessed One from extremely far away and then left again the very same way they came, by the very same path.

Then the venerable Śāriputra had this thought: “Why did those non-Buddhist wandering ascetics circumambulate the Blessed One from extremely far away and then leave again the very same way they came, by the very same path?”

The Blessed One knew in his mind what kind of mental rumination the venerable Śāriputra was having, and said to the venerable Śāriputra, “O Śāriputra, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, noticed the mindsets of those non-Buddhist wandering ascetics intent upon criticizing something. Śakra then recalled this transcendent state of wisdom by memory, recited it, and passed it on to those non-Buddhist wandering ascetics so that those non-Buddhist wandering ascetics‍—whose inclination was to attack, deny, and dispute‍—would turn back. He told them, ‘Do not approach to attack the teaching on this transcendent state of wisdom. Do not interrupt the teaching on the transcendent state of wisdom.’ And Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, had my permission to deter them. Why is that? I did not see any one of those non-Buddhist wandering ascetics having a pure motive. They all had the intention to challenge, a hostile mindset, and an inclination to attack.”

Just then, the pernicious Māra thought, “These four assemblies and these gods from the realm of desire and the realm of form have approached and are seated in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata. It is certain that the bodhisattva great beings here will be foreordained [to reach] unsurpassable, perfect awakening. Let me attack them to stir up their aversion.”
Then the pernicious Māra magically conjured up a powerful army with four divisions that descended upon the Blessed One to attack.

Just then, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, had this thought: “Oh no! The pernicious Māra has magically conjured up an army with four divisions to attack the Blessed One.Ratnākara (F.72.a) specifies that (as usual in sūtra narrative) their military arrays had four divisions: elephant corps, cavalry, infantry, and chariot corps. RK: balakāyo balasamūhaḥ| tasya catvāry aṅgāni gajavājipattirathāḥ. However, this military array of an army with four divisions is neither the military array of King Bimbisāra’s army with its four divisions, nor the military array of King Prasenajit’s army with its four divisions, nor the military array of the Śākyas’ army with its four divisions, nor the military array of the Licchavis’ army with its four divisions.These names reference four kingdoms during the time of the Buddha. As Ratnākara (F.72.a) states, Bimbisāra was ruler of Magadha. Prasenajit was sovereign of Śrāvastī. “Śākyas” refers to the ruling warriors born from the Śākya clan (into which Siddhārtha Gautama was born). The Licchavis governed Vaiśālī. RK: bimbisāro magadhādhipaḥ| prasenajit srāvastīpatiḥ| śākyāḥ śākyakulajāḥ kṣatriyāḥ| licchavayo vaiśālakāḥ. It is one conjured up by the pernicious Māra. The pernicious Māra‍—who preys on weakness and seeks ways to attack‍—has been in pursuit of the Blessed One for a long time and is intent upon harming sentient beings. Let me recall this transcendent state of wisdom once again by memory, recite it, and pass it on.”

Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, then recalled this very transcendent state of wisdom by memory, recited it, and passed it on. And just as Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, recalled this very transcendent state of wisdom by memory, recited it, and passed it on, so too, as a result, the pernicious Māra and his army turned and left again by the very same path.

The gods from the Heaven of the Thirty-Three magically created heavenly māndārava flowers, flew through the sky to where the Blessed One was, and scattered those heavenly māndārava flowers, raining them down over the place where the Blessed One was.The māndārava flower (which can also be spelled other ways, e.g. mandārava, etc.) is a type of heavenly flower rained down on the Buddha as tribute. Then, in praise, they exclaimed, “Hurray! This transcendent state of wisdom has returned again at last!”

Then, once again, the gods took the heavenly māndārava flowers, scattered them, and rained them down on the place where the Blessed One was, saying this: “O Blessed One, neither Māra, nor Māra’s army, nor any deity will find any vulnerability in those sentient beings who speak of the transcendent state of wisdom, cultivate it, and go about their lives in it. O Blessed One, those sentient beings who listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, and, after listening to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it, will come to possess no small root of virtue. Since those sentient beings who come even within earshot of this transcendent state of wisdom will have performed some service to the former conquerors, what more needs to be said about those who will be taking it in, keeping it in mind, chanting it, mastering it, passing it on, teaching it, expounding it, explaining it, reciting it, training to attain the actual nature, progressing to attain the actual nature, and engaging in meditation to attain the actual nature?There may be a slight play here on the Sanskrit word tathatvam, a synonym of tathatā. In Sanskrit, the syntax can be taken to mean either that they train and so on “to be in accord with” the transcendent state of wisdom, or that they train and so on “to attain the actual nature” (tathatvāya; de bzhin nyid du), i.e., the true nature (tathatā) (of things). According to Ratnākara (F.72.b), the “actual nature” is the nature of the way that the transcendent state of wisdom is. RK: yathā prajñāpāramitā tathābhāvaṃ tathātvam. Note: tathatvam and tathātvam are variant spellings in Buddhist Sanskrit. O Blessed One, they will be sentient beings who have closely attended the tathāgatas.According Hari (245), “closely attended” means by donating to the tathāgatas’ retinues, by listening to what they taught, and through offerings to venerate them. (Hari also comments on why the Sanskrit compound word should be understood the way it is translated above). Hari: tathāgataparyupāsitā iti tāthāgatāḥ parivārapradānena taduktaśravaṇena ca pūjādibhiś ca paryupāsitā yais te tathoktāḥ. niṣṭhāntaṃ pūrvaṃ nipatatīti vyabhicāralakṣaṇatvāt paryupāsita śabdasya na pūrva nipātaḥ. Why is that? It is because the complete knowing should be sought from there, namely, from the transcendent state of wisdom. Just as all those extremely precious jewels that come out of the ocean should be sought from the ocean, just so, Blessed One, the extremely precious complete knowing of worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas should be sought from within the ocean of the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.72.b), in this analogy here the transcendent state of wisdom itself is the “ocean.” RK: prajñāpāramita iva mahāsamudraḥ.

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “True, Kauśika, true. The great jewel of complete knowing is, in particular, born from this ocean of the transcendent state of wisdom.”

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 21 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 30 in the Eighteen Thousand.Then the venerable Ānanda said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, the Blessed One is not praising any positive qualities of the transcendent state of generosity, nor is he mentioning its name.According to Ratnākara (F.72), one can understand the Sanskrit here to mean he speaks its “praises” or speaks of its “positive qualities.” RK: varṇaḥ stotraṃ guṇā vā. O Blessed One, the Blessed One is also not praising any positive qualities of the transcendent state of ethical discipline, the transcendent state of tolerant acceptance, the transcendent state of heroic perseverance, or the transcendent state of meditation, nor is he mentioning their names. Rather, the Blessed One is praising the positive qualities and mentioning the name of one thing alone, the transcendent state of wisdom.”

“So it is, Ānanda, so it is,” replied the Blessed One. “I am praising the positive qualities and mentioning the name of the transcendent state of wisdom alone, and not those of the other transcendent states. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, the transcendent state of wisdom takes the fore over the five other transcendent states.According to Hari (247), the Blessed One praises “the positive qualities of the transcendent state of wisdom and mentions its name,” because it “takes the fore over the other transcendent states” such as generosity, insofar as the dedication of these five transcendent states to the state of complete knowing happens at the beginning (of practicing these five) through the purity of the three applications (i.e., through the transcendent state of wisdom seeing the emptiness of any giver, gift, or recipient, etc.). Hari: prajñāpāramitā hī tyādi. yasmāt trimaṇḍala­viśuddhyā dānādīnāṃ sarvajñatāyāṃ pariṇāmenādau pravṛttatvāt pūrvaṃgamā tasmāt tadvarṇaṃ bhāṣe nāmadheyaṃ ca parikīrtayāmī ti pūrveṇa sambandhaḥ. O Ānanda, what do you think? Would generosity get the name transcendent state of generosity if it were not dedicated to the state of complete knowing?”According to Hari (247), to the phrase “if generosity were not dedicated to the state of complete knowing” one should add the phrase “by the transcendent state of wisdom” and understand it to mean “if generosity were not given, in a manner free of mental perception (of giver, gift or recipient), for the purpose of buddhahood for the sake of all sentient beings.” Hari: apariṇāmitaṃ dānaṃ sarvajñatāyām iti anupalambhayogena sarvasattvārthaṃ buddhatvāyāniryātitaṃ, prajñāpāramitayeti śeṣaḥ.
“Certainly not, Blessed One,” replied Ānanda.

The Blessed One asked, “O Ānanda, what do you think? What if ethical discipline were not dedicated to the state of complete knowing, if tolerant acceptance were not dedicated to it, if heroic perseverance were not dedicated to it, and if meditation were not dedicated to it? O Ānanda, what do you think? Would wisdom get the name transcendent state of wisdom if it were not dedicated to the state of complete knowing?”
“Certainly not, Blessed One.” replied Ānanda.
The Blessed One asked, “O Ānanda, what do you think? Is the wisdom that transforms the roots of virtue through dedicating them to the state of complete knowing inconceivable?”
“Yes, Blessed One. Yes,” replied Ānanda. “The wisdom that transforms the roots of virtue through dedicating them to the state of complete knowing is inconceivable.”

The Blessed One said, “Ānanda, for this reason, that wisdom gets the name transcendent state by virtue of its transcendent superiority, whereas any roots of virtue get the name transcendent state insofar as they are dedicated to the state of complete knowing by that wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.72.b), the transcendent state of wisdom is a perfection because it alone is transcendently perfect. The others are transcendent states because they are dedicated by that very wisdom to the state of complete knowing. RK: prajñāpāramitā hi svayem eva paramatvāt pāramitā, itarās tayaiva sarvajñatāyāṃ pariṇāmitatvena. O Ānanda, for this reason, the transcendent state of wisdom takes the fore as the leader and guide of the others.According to Hari (249), “leader” here is due to leading generosity and so on to buddhahood, and “guide” because it drives away all sudden attacks or occurrences. Hari: dānādīnāṃ buddhatve nayanān nāyikā. sarvopadravanirākaraṇāt pariṇāyikā. According to Ratnākara (F.72.b), a “leader” here is someone who draws others in his or her direction; a “guide” is someone who fosters growth in others (like a mother). RK: nāyike ti saṃgrāhikā| pariṇāyike ti saṃvardhikā. In this manner, the five transcendent states are within the transcendent state of wisdom alone, and, Ānanda, the term transcendent state of wisdom completely covers all six transcendent states. So therefore, Ānanda, when the transcendent state of wisdom is praised, the six transcendent states are being praised. O Ānanda, as an analogy, it is just like seeds scattered across the vast earth that grow when all the requisite conditions come together. That is to say, the earth is the ground for those seeds. And those seeds grow only when fixed in that earth.We have followed the Tibetan, which has taken some license to add reasons to the Sanskrit here, but shifted them slightly to capture the analogy and the Sanskrit. wording. The Sanskrit (Vaidya 40–41) reads tadyathāpi nāma ānanda mahāpṛthivyāṃ bījāni prakīrṇāni sāmagrīṃ labhamānāni virohanti| mahāpṛthivī ca teṣāṃ bījānāṃ pratiṣṭhā| mahāpṛthivī­pratiṣṭhitāni ca tāni bījāni virohantihere. The Tibetan reads kun dga’ bo ’di lta ste dper na sa chen po la sa bon btab pa tshogs pa dang phrad na skye bar ’gyur te | sa chen po ni sa bon de dag gi gzhi yin pas sa chen po la brten nas sa bon de dag skye bar ’gyur ro. In the very same way, Ānanda, when contained within the transcendent state of wisdom, the five transcendent states are supported by the complete knowing.Here, we should understand the analogy as follows: (1) The five seeds are dāna, etc.; (2) the transcendent state of wisdom governs those seeds being able to grow, i.e., governs their being transcendent states. (3) The six transcendent states, even when full-grown, are founded upon the earth. (4) The transcendent state of wisdom is the name of all conditions being fulfilled. Likewise, the five transcendent states grow when fixed within the transcendent state of wisdom, and they receive the name transcendent state because they are utterly supported by that transcendent state of wisdom. It is in this way, therefore, Ānanda, that the transcendent state of wisdom takes the fore as the leader or guide of the others.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “All these benefits of the transcendent state of wisdom‍—which the noble sons or daughters receive after taking it in, keeping it in mind, mastering it, chanting it, passing it on, teaching it, expounding it, explaining it, and reciting it‍—cannot be the only ones praised by the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata.According to Hari (250), the idea here is that these are not all the qualities of the whole mother transcendent state of wisdom, because of the absolute abundance of qualities that she possesses. RK: na tāvad ime bhagavann ity ādi. prajñāpāramitāyāḥ sarve guṇā iti samastāyā mātur amī sarve guṇā na bhavanti tasyā guṇātyantaprācuryād iti matiḥ. Otherwise, how else would the limited amount of this transcendent state of wisdom that I have taken in thus far from the Blessed One have already been passed on?”According to Hari (250), he explains here what other qualities the transcendent state of wisdom has. The “limited amount” of her that has been “taken in” by me here directly from the Blessed One has already been “passed on” to those non-Buddhist wandering ascetics who were caused by it to turn back just a moment ago‍—meaning that she has these qualities (in addition to those already mentioned by the Blessed One). Hari: kasyās tarhy amī guṇā ity āha: atha hi maye tyādi. bhagavato ’ntikād asyā yaḥ pradeśo mayodgṛhītaḥ sa cānantaram evānyatīrthyādi­nirākṛtau pravartitas, tasyā amī guṇā ity arthaḥ.

“Excellent, excellent, Kauśika,” said the Blessed One. “Furthermore, Kauśika, not only will one who takes in the transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it come to have those very benefits, but so too, Kauśika, will any noble son or daughter if he or she writes this transcendent state of wisdom down, makes it into a book, maintains it, and preserves it so that the genuine teachings last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“And if that noble son or daughter pays tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—reveres it, respects it, offers to it, honors it, and tends to it, then he or she will also have these worldly benefits that I am describing.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, I too will safeguard, defend, and protect any noble son or daughter who writes this transcendent state of wisdom down, makes it into a book, maintains it, and preserves it so that the genuine teachings last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“I too will safeguard, defend, and protect this noble son or daughter who pays tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—reveres it, respects it, offers to it, honors it, and tends to it. But I cannot even express how much more I will do for anyone who also writes down this transcendent state of wisdom, takes it in, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it, in addition to paying tribute to it‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revering it, respecting it, offering to it, honoring it, and tending to it.”

“Excellent, Kauśika, excellent,” said the Blessed One. “Beyond that, indeed, many hundreds of gods will come to be near that son or daughter who recites this transcendent state of wisdom. Many thousands of gods, many hundreds of thousands of gods, will come to hear the teachings. And when they hear the teachings, those gods will wish to arouse that Dharma teacher’s bold illumination.Accoding to Hari (252), “to arouse bold illumination” means to cause free and coherent expression. Hari: pratibhānam upasaṃhartavyam iti yukta­muktābhidhānam utpādayitavyaṃ. chanda iti vaktukāmatā. See also n.84. Even when that Dharma teacher does not wish to recite anything, the gods, out of sheer reverence for those teachings, will seek to arouse his or her bold illumination so that the noble son or daughter will just feel the urge to speak. This too is a worldly benefit that will be received by the noble son or daughter who takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it.

“Moreover, when speaking of this transcendent state of wisdom before the four assemblies, any noble son or daughter’s mindset will not be in a state of cringing, while praying that no one challengingly questions him or her, or is intent upon criticizing him or her. Why is that? It is because the transcendent state of wisdom safeguards, defends, and protects him or her. Abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she does not even identify any criticism. Abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she does not even identify a criticizer, nor does he or she even identify anyone who could be criticized, nor does he or she even identify that transcendent state of wisdom. Thus, the noble son or daughter supported by the transcendent state of wisdom is not interrogated by anyone in this way. Kauśika, this too is a worldly benefit that will be received by the noble son or daughter who takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it.

“Moreover, Kauśika, that noble son or daughter will become lovingly appreciated by mothers, fathers, friends, companions, relations, kin, mendicants, brahmins, and allies. Also, he or she will become both competent and capable of defeating, through the teachings, the challenging assertions that others repeatedly come up with. Also, when challenged through questioning by others, he or she will be fully capable of answering those challenging questions. Kauśika, this too is a worldly benefit that will be received by that noble son or daughter who takes in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it.

“Also, Kauśika, all those gods among the retinues of the Four Great Kings who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening will wish to come to wherever a noble son or daughter writes this transcendent state of wisdom down, makes it into a book, maintains it, preserves it, and venerates it. Having come there, they will gaze upon the transcendent state of wisdom in the book, pay obeisance to it, pay homage to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Only once they have gazed upon it, paid obeisance to it, paid homage to it, taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, and recited it will they wish to leave again.

“Also, Kauśika, among the gods in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, all those gods who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening will wish to come there. Having come there, they will gaze upon the transcendent state of wisdom in the book, pay obeisance to it, pay homage to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Only once they have gazed upon it, paid obeisance to it, paid homage to it, taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, and recited it will they wish to leave again.

“Also, Kauśika, among the Yāma gods, all those gods who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening will wish to come there. Having come there, they will gaze upon the transcendent state of wisdom in the book, pay obeisance to it, pay homage to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Only once they have gazed upon it, paid obeisance to it, paid homage to it, taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, and recited it will they wish to leave again.

“Also, Kauśika, among the Tuṣita gods, all those gods who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening will wish to come there. Having come there, they will gaze upon the transcendent state of wisdom in the book, pay obeisance to it, pay homage to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Only once they have gazed upon it, paid obeisance to it, paid homage to it, taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, and recited it will they wish to leave again.

“Also, Kauśika, among the Nirmāṇarati gods, all those gods who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening will wish to come there. Having come there, they will gaze upon the transcendent state of wisdom in the book, pay obeisance to it, pay homage to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Only once they have gazed upon it, paid obeisance to it, paid homage to it, taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, and recited it will they wish to leave again.

“Also, Kauśika, among the Paranirmitavaśavartin gods, all those gods who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening will wish to come there. Having come there, they will gaze upon the transcendent state of wisdom in the book, pay obeisance to it, pay homage to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Only once they have gazed upon it, paid obeisance to it, paid homage to it, taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, and recited it will they wish to leave again.

“Also, Kauśika, among the gods of the form realms up to the Brahmākāyika heaven, all those gods who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening will wish to come there. Having come there, they will gaze upon the transcendent state of wisdom in the book, pay obeisance to it, pay homage to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Only once they have gazed upon it, paid obeisance to it, paid homage to it, taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, and recited it will they wish to leave again.

“Also, Kauśika, do not think this is so only among the gods of the Brahmakāyika heaven. Kauśika, the way that it is among the gods of the Brahmakāyika heaven is exactly the same among the gods of the Brahmapurohita heaven…repeat the above refrain for this and the followingThe italicized comment “repeat the above refrain for this and the following” (peyālam) is a translation of what appears in the Sanskrit and Tibetan texts themselves. According to BHSD peyāla, the scribes used this abbreviation to avoid rewriting the refrain, but this comment often implies that the full text is to be recited.…and exactly the same among the gods in the heavens of Mahābrahmā, Parīttābha, Apramāṇābha, Ābhāsvara, Parīttaśubha, Apramāṇaśubha, Śubhakṛtsna, Anabhraka, Puṇyaprasava, Bṛhatphala, Asaṃjñisattva, Avṛha, Atapa, Sudṛśa, and Sudarśana.

“Finally, even among the gods of Akaniṣṭha, all those gods who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening will wish to come there. Having come there, they will gaze upon the transcendent state of wisdom in the book, pay obeisance to it, pay homage to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Only once they have gazed upon it, paid obeisance to it, paid homage to it, taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, and recited it will they wish to leave again.

“Thus, Kauśika, a noble son or daughter should develop the proper mindset as follows: ‘May those gods, nāgas, benevolent spirits, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and other nonhumans among the immeasurable and innumerable world realms come to see the transcendent state of wisdom in this book of mine, pay obeisance to it, pay homage to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Having seen it, paid obeisance to it, paid homage to it, taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, and recited it, may they return once again to their own homes. May this very gift of the teachings be given to them.’

“Kauśika, do not assume here that only those gods of the desire and form realms of this world of four continents who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening will wish to come there. Kauśika, this is not the way to understand it. Rather, Kauśika, you should know that even those gods in all the desire and form realms of the entire billionfold, great order world system who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening will wish to come there. Having come there, they will gaze upon the transcendent state of wisdom in the book, pay obeisance to it, pay homage to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Only once they have gazed upon it, paid obeisance to it, paid homage to it, taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, and recited it will they wish to leave again.

“Furthermore, Kauśika, any house, monastic residence, or palace where that noble son or daughter stays will be well protected and no harm will come to him or her there, except through the maturation of previous actions. Also, the noble son or daughter will receive this worldly benefit, since powerful gods, nāgas, benevolent spirits, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and other nonhumans will wish to come there.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “But, O Blessed One, how could a noble son or daughter know that gods, nāgas, benevolent spirits, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and other nonhumans are coming to hear the transcendent state of wisdom, and to gaze upon it, pay obeisance to it, pay homage to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it?”

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, whenever a noble son or daughter notices a sublime radiance in that location, he or she can know for certain: ‘A god, nāga, yakṣa, gandharva, asura, garuḍa, kinnara, mahoraga, human, or other nonhuman has come here or has approached.’According to the Hari (260), “that location” refers to the house, monastic residence, or palace (mentioned just above). Hari: tatra gṛhādāv. Also, Kauśika, whenever a noble son or daughter smells there an otherworldly scent not smelled before, he or she can know for certain: ‘A god, nāga, yakṣa, gandharva, asura, garuḍa, kinnara, mahoraga, human, or other nonhuman has come here or has approached.’According to the Hari (260), “smells an otherworldly scent” means he senses through his nasal consciousness a particular scent, which surpasses any in the human world. Hari: amānuṣaṃ gandhaṃ ghrāsyatī ti manuṣyalokāti­krāntaṃ viśiṣṭaṃ gandhaṃ ghrāṇa­vijñānenānu­bhaviṣyati.

“Moreover, Kauśika, whenever a noble son or daughter has a pure and clean way of behaving,According to Hari (260), “pure” in terms of internal purity due to not doing anything evil; “clean” in terms of external purity due to wearing clean clothes and so on. Hari: sarva­pāpākaraṇād adhyātmaśuddhyā caukṣasamudācāraḥ. vivikta­vastrādyupabhogena bāhyapariśuddhyā śucisamudācāra ḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.72.a), here “pure” is due to lacking impurity; “clean” is due to its association with flower offerings and so on. RK: aśucyabhāvāt caukṣaḥ| puṣpādiyogāt śuciḥ. then those gods, nāgas, benevolent spirits, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and other nonhumans will wish to come there because of his or her pure and clean way of behaving. Also, those gods, nāgas, benevolent spirits, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and other nonhumans‍—who have come there because of his or her pure and clean way of behaving‍—will be glad, delighted, and filled with joy and satisfaction. At the same time, the lackluster gods who previously occupied that place will want to slink away from it. Why is that? Those lackluster gods will want to slink away from there because they will simply be unable to endure the splendor, energy, and dignity of the extremely lustrous gods, nāgas, benevolent spirits, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and other nonhumans. Also, the more repeatedly those gods, nāgas, benevolent spirits, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and other nonhumans wish to come, the more the noble son or daughter will become increasingly trusting.According to Hari (261), “increasingly trusting” means increasingly faithful due to seeing it with complete deep belief. Hari: prasādabahula ity abhisam­pratyaya­darśanād abhivardhamāna­śraddhaḥ. That noble son or daughter will receive this worldly benefit too.

“Furthermore, a noble son or daughter will never come to have an impure and unclean way of behaving in the vicinity of this Dharma guide, since it is something to be revered, respected, and accomplished.Here, the phrase “this Dharma guide” refers to the transcendent state of wisdom in written form, which has been passed on to beings so that they can be brought to the state of complete knowing. Since neither Hari nor Ratnākara comments on the second clause of this sentence, the English translation has followed the Tibetan interpretation of the ambiguous Sanskrit compound gurugauravatāparipūri. But it may be worth pointing out that this phrase could also be translated “because of being filled with respect for its importance” or perhaps, in a different context, “out of fulfilling one’s devotion to it as the guru.” Also, Kauśika, that noble son or daughter will never develop any physical or mental exhaustion.According to Hari (262), no “physical exhaustion” means no fatigue due to travel and so on; no “mental exhaustion” means no fatigue due to a lack of alms-offerings and so on. Hari: kāyaklamatha iti deśāntaragamanādinā na kāyakhedaḥ. na cittaklamatha iti piṇḍapātādivaikalyān na cittakhedaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.72.a), “exhaustion” here means affliction. RK: klamathaḥ pīḍā. He or she will rest utterly at ease and move about at ease. When asleep, he or she will not have bad dreams, but rather will have dreams only of worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, stūpas, bodhisattvas, and the tathāgatas’ disciples. When hearing sounds in dreams, he or she will hear only the words of the transcendent state, only things conducive to awakening. He or she will see only Bodhi trees, and only worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas awakening beneath them, and, accordingly, setting in motion the wheel of the teachings for perfect awakening. He or she will see many bodhisattva great beings‍—devoted to reciting and chanting this very transcendent state of wisdom‍—and they will expound the skill in means: ‘The state of complete knowing should be apprehended in this or that way and the buddafield should be purified in this or that way.’ He or she will hear the sublime words of the blessed buddhas’ perfect awakening: ‘In this direction, in this area, in this world realm, someone called so-and-so will teach the Dharma attended and flanked by many hundreds of bodhisattvas and disciples, by many thousands of bodhisattvas and disciples, by many hundreds of thousands of bodhisattvas and disciples, by many tens of millions of bodhisattvas and disciples, by many billions of bodhisattvas and disciples, and by many trillions of bodhisattvas and disciples.’ Kauśika, a noble son or daughter who has dreams of this sort will sleep at ease and wake up at ease. He or she will experience the body being infused with energy, at ease, and very light.The Tibetan has not been precisely followed here, since it seems slightly corrupt and does not record the Sanskrit’s second ca. Tib. lus kyi nang du mdangs rab tu bcug zas shin tu bde ba myong bar ’gyur. Cf. MW prakṣepa, “adding to,” “increasing”; laghu, “in good health.” Conze’s interpretation of ojaḥprakṣiptaṃ ca kayaṃ sukham ca pratisaṃvedayati, laghu laghu ca pratisaṃvedayati is “when food is thrown into it, the body will still feel at ease and exceedingly light.” According to Schopen 1977 on Conze (pp. 149–50), there is a note on ojas (p. 29, n.2) in Jean Filliozat, Etude de démonologie indienne. Le Kumâratantra de Râvana et les textes parallèles indiens, tibétains, chinois, cambodgiens et arabes. Paris: Cahiers de la Société Asiatique IV, 1937. He or she will not develop a mindstream with excessive desire for food but will think of food in moderation.According to Ratnākara (F.72.a), “thinking of food” means bringing it to mind. RK: saṃjñā smṛti. Kauśika, as an analogy, it is just like monks while practicing meditation. Once they emerge from concentration, they do not have a strong desire for food, since, Kauśika, their mindset is overflowing with attentiveness and their thought of food is moderate. In the very same way, Kauśika, a noble son or daughter will not have a strong desire for food. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, the more a son or daughter has devoted him- or herself to the meditative state for cultivating the transcendent state of wisdom, the more spirits will wish to supply him or her with physical energy.Schopen, in his article on Conze (Schopen 1977, pp. 148–49), argues, in essence, that upasaṃhartavyaṃ is a corruption of an idea from the earlier tradition, in which the job of nonhumans (amanuṣyāḥ) is to “steal energy” from people, as opposed to devas, whose job it is to give it back to them. For that reason, Schopen suggests that we should read here apahartavyam instead of upasaṃhartavyaṃ. Given the elliptical nature of his comments, though, it is not clear whether Schopen would also insert a negation, i.e., no energy would be stolen by them, and hence a bodhisattva would still have more energy. In any event, Schopen’s point here does not seem to have bothered either Indian commentator, perhaps because Hari and Ratnākara had a more flexible understanding of nonhumans, i.e., gods. A noble son or daughter will receive this worldly benefit too.

“Moreover, Kauśika, suppose that a noble son or daughter were to write down the transcendent state of wisdom, make it into a book, preserve it in an elevated position, make devotional offerings to venerate it‍—but were not to take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, or recite it‍—then, based on that, that very noble son or daughter would earn a great amount of merit. But suppose that another noble son or daughter were to have faith, believe, and have conviction in the transcendent state of wisdom, and, with that trusting mindset, were to develop the proper mindset for awakening; were, based on an altruistic disposition, to listen to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it; were, for the sake of others, to elucidate it in detail, reveal its meaning, consider it mentally, and apply him- or herself in thoroughly examining it with a great deal of wisdom; or even just were to make the transcendent state of wisdom into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that the genuine teachings lasted for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“And suppose that this noble son or daughter also were to pay tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it, then, based on that, this noble son or daughter would earn an even greater amount of merit in comparison to the other.

“So therefore, Kauśika, that noble son or daughter who wishes to receive these distinguished worldly benefits should have faith, believe, and have conviction in the transcendent state of wisdom, and, with a trusting mindset, develop the proper mindset for awakening; based on an altruistic disposition, listen to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it; for the sake of others, elucidate it in detail, reveal its meaning, consider it mentally, and apply themselves in thoroughly examining it with a great deal of wisdom; or at least make it into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that the genuine teachings last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’

“He or she should pay tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Paying Tribute to the Stūpas and the Transcendent States That Contain Immeasurable Benefits,” the third chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 4: Extolling Positive QualitiesAs mentioned in the note to the title of chapter 3 (n.257), the multivalent term guṇa can be translated either as “positive qualities” or “benefits” depending on context. Although the previous chapter title was more about the “benefits,” this chapter emphasizes a bit more the “positive qualities” that lead to those benefits. Hence, even though there is some blurring of the lines between these two meanings, it is helpful, once again, to keep in mind that these different translations derive from the same word in Sanskrit.The content of this chapter corresponds to the content of Chapter 22 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 31 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: The Blessed One then addressed Śakra, powerful lord of the gods: “Kauśika, suppose that there were two lots placed before you. Suppose that in the first lot, one were to put the Jambu continent completely filled to the brim with the physical relics of the tathāgatas. And suppose that in the second lot one were to put this transcendent state of wisdom, written down. Kauśika, if you had to choose one of these two lots, which would you take?”

Śakra said, “If, Blessed One, I had to choose one of these two lots‍—namely, the first lot with the Jambu continent completely filled to the brim with the physical relics of the tathāgatas, or the second lot with this transcendent state of wisdom written down‍—then, Blessed One, of these two lots placed before me, I would take this very transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? I would do so, naturally, out of reverence for the guide of the tathāgatas, since it is the actual physical relic of the tathāgatas. Why is that? Because, as the Blessed One has said,In the following famous and important quote, there is a play on the multivalent words dharma (“teachings,” “truth,” “qualities”) and kāya (“body,” “collection,” “embodiment”) that cannot be translated into English in a way that captures the meaning, nor can the complex interpretive issues surrounding this passage be explained in these notes. Nonetheless, the English translation of dharmakāya in this case as the “body of truth” aims to approximate a parallel pun in English by playing on similar ambiguities that, in their own right, could be interpreted to fit the various doctrinal and interpretive differences by Hari and Ratnākara. For a historical and philological discussion of the complex Sanskrit linguistic, grammatical, and terminological issues at play in this and other similar passages, see Harrison 1992. ‘The blessed buddhas are the bodies of the truth.Ratnākara makes no direct comment on this sentence, but Hari explains it‍—in a passage too long and complicated to summarize here‍—as the thesis statement of a logical syllogism that proves his explanation of the transcendent state of wisdom as a synonym for a buddha’s knowing. Thus, according to Hari (268), the first statement of the quote here should be understood, according to what the Buddha taught elsewhere, as suggesting that the blessed buddhas have the nature of the transcendent state of wisdom, which is to say, have the inherent nature of illusion-like nondual knowing. Hari: yasmād uktaṃ pradeśāntare bhagavatā: dharmakāyā māyopamādvaya­jñāna­svabhāvāḥ prajñāpāramitātmakā buddhā bhagavanta iti. But, monks, do not see this real body as that body.Sanskrit: mā khalu punarimaṃ bhikṣavaḥ satkāyaṃ kāyaṃ manyadhvam; Tibetan: dge long dag ’di la ni sku dam par ma ’dzin par. According to Ratnākara (F.73.b), satkāya refers here to the Buddha’s body with physical form (rūpakāya). RK: sīdatīti sat| anityaḥ| sac cāsau kāyaś ceti satkāyo rūpakāyaḥ. Indeed, taking our cue from the Tibetan rendering sku dam pa, we have translated satkāya here as “this real body” and not as “the transitory collection,” which elsewhere translates satkāya in the most common context of its being the focus of erroneous views, the meaning rendered as ’jig tshogs in Tibetan,. While for the context of the present passage BHSD suggests that sad comes from √ās in the sense of a “real” physical body, see n.1343 for Ratnākara’s comments using another etymology for the “transitory collection” sense, i.e. when not referring to the Buddha’s body. According to Hari (269), this sentence expresses the quality to be logically proven about the “satkāya”‍—referring here to the bright (body of the Buddha) shining forth with the major and minor marks‍—namely that monks should consider it to have the nature of a conglomeration of atoms. Hari: mā khalu punar imaṃ lakṣaṇa­vyañjanojjvalaṃ śobhanaṃ satkāyaṃ he bhikṣavaḥ para­māṇusaṃcaya­svabhāvaṃ manyadhvam iti sādhyadharmaḥ kathitaḥ. Note that in similar passages from Pali the term “putrid body” or “corrupt body” (pūtikāya) is used in place of satkāya, e.g. Saṃyuttanikāya iii, 120. Instead, monks, see me through actualizing the body of truth.’Here, the words “body of truth” refer to the “collective representation of the teachings” on the transcendent state of wisdom or “essence of the teachings,” i.e., the actual transcendent state of wisdom that sees the truth. Based on this punchline, a reader is intended to re-evaluate that former understanding of “body of truth” (above in the first sentence). It can now be reinterpreted to mean that the Buddha’s true body is the teachings (or the qualities they point to), which must be actualized. Although Ratnākara and Hari disagree about how to understand this quote in relation to the transcendent state of wisdom (mentioned in the next sentence), they generally agree that the dharmakāya must be understood as eternal and can only be “seen” through realizing it oneself, not through considering the truth to be represented by the “real” or existent body that constitutes the buddha’s body with physical form (rūpakāya). According to Ratnākara (F.73.b), in the phrase “through actualizing the body of truth,” the word “truth” (dharma) refers to the noble path. That preeminent path‍—which is nothing but the transcendent state of wisdom‍—is not impermanent, because it is a constant stream. RK: dharma­kāya­pariniṣpattita iti| atrāryamārgo dharmaḥ| sa ca prakarṣagataḥ prajñāpāramitaiva| nānityaḥ pravāhanityatvāt. According to Hari (269), the phrase “through actualizing the body of truth” expresses the cause, suggesting that the monks should see him as the (fruit) actualized through actualizing‍—i.e., directly witnessing‍—the illusion-like nondual knowing. Hari: dharma­kāya­pariniṣpattito māyopamādvaya­jñāna­sākṣātkriyāniṣpattyā niṣpannaṃ māṃ drakṣyathe ty anena hetur uktaḥ. And hence the body of the tathāgatas should be seen as revealed at the pinnacle of reality, that is to say, as the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.73.b–74.a), the word “pinnacle” refers to the highpoint of “reality”‍—i.e., of those real things‍—because there is no reality beyond that, and because what is “revealed” (prabhāvita<vyāhṛta) at that very highpoint is identical to it (i.e., subject and object are not separate at the level of nondual reality). Śakra next explains the word “body” (as intended in the last line of the above quote) with the following phrase‍—“that is to say, the transcendent state of wisdom”‍—referring to the ultimate truth as nothing but the actual nature. The basic point here is that “the transcendent state of wisdom” that sees that truth is not separate from it. RK: bhūtakoṭiprabhāvita iti| bhūtā ni tattvāni teṣāṃ koṭir agram| tato ’dhikasya tattvasyābhāvāt| tasyām eva prabhāvito vyāhṛtas tādātmyāt. katamaḥ kāya ity āha| yadute tyādi| tathatāmātra­paramārthaṃ, taddarśanī prajñāpāramitā na tato bhidyata iti bhāvaḥ. According to Hari (269), in the phrase “what is revealed at the pinnacle of reality,” the word “reality” means the real body, i.e., the Tathāgata’s body. This is “revealed,” i.e., accomplished, “through actualizing the body of truth.” The following phrase‍—“that is to say, the transcendent state of wisdom”‍—means the Tathāgata’s body has the transcendent state of wisdom as its nature. Hari: eṣa can tathāgatakāyo bhūta kāyo bhūta­koṭiprabhāvito dharma­kāya­pariniṣpattyā niṣpanno, yad uta prajñāpāramitā tatsvabhāva iti.

“However, it is certainly not the case, Blessed One, that I lack reverence for the physical relics of the tathāgatas. I have nothing but reverence for those physical relics of the tathāgatas. Nevertheless, Blessed One, the physical relics of the tathāgatas receive devotional offerings to venerate them insofar as they have been born from the transcendent state of wisdom. Therefore, even the devotional offerings to venerate the physical relics of the tathāgatas can be accomplished through venerating precisely this transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? It is because those physical relics of the tathāgatas are born from the transcendent state of wisdom. Blessed One, it is just like, for instance, during the times when I am seated in my own divine seat in the Sudharma assembly hall of gods, and the prince gods come to attend to me.According to Ratnākara (F.74.a), the assembly hall Sudharma is located in the southwestern section of the heavenly city of Sudarśana. RK: ata eva sā sudharme tyucyate| asya ca sudarśanasya devanagarasya dakṣiṇapaścimadigbhāga iti. But also, out of reverence for me during those times when I am not seated there, the prince gods pay homage to that seat, circumambulate it, and then move repectfully away. Why is that? They do so only because they think, ‘Śakra, powerful lord of the Heaven of the Thirty-Three gods, sat in that seat and taught the teachings.’ In this very way, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom‍—which is, in effect, the cause and condition for the Blessed One’s exaltedness‍—is what brings forth the state of complete knowing of a worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata, whereas a tathāgata’s physical relics are, in effect, a repository for the state of complete knowing, not the causes or conditions that give rise to that knowing.According to Ratnākara (F.74.a), “lord” here means a great person. The word ākhyā should be understood here in the sense of “appearance” or “splendor” (pratibhāsa, i.e., ruci). He has “exaltedness” means he has the mind of a great one‍—in this case, the perfect Buddha whose nature is knowing. The phrase “in effect, the cause and condition” of that means that it has the nature of a cause and the nature of a condition. RK: īśo mahāpuruṣaḥ| tasyākhyā pratibhāso rūciḥ| mahatīśākhā (em. mahatīśākhyā?) asyeti maheśākhyaḥ| mahānmanā ity arthaḥ| sa ceha jñānātmā samyaksaṃbuddhaḥ| tasya hetupratyayabhūto hetusvabhāvo pratyayasvabhāvo ca. In this very way, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom should be venerated as the source of the knowing of someone who has the complete knowing that is venerated in the physical relics of the tathāgatas.According to Ratnākara (F.74.a), in the analogy of his own person that Śakra uses, Śakra himself corresponds to “the knowing of someone who has complete knowing,” while Śakra’s seat (venerated by those gods) corresponds to the “tathāgatas’ physical relics” that are “not the causes or conditions” for that knowing. Previously, Śakra had explained what is, in effect, a repository for the body as correlating merely to the body with physical form (rūpakāya). But notice here that the seat is standing in for the splendor of the great lord, i.e., the body of knowing. RK: tathāgatasye tyādi| tatra sarvajñajñānaṃ śakravat| tathāgata­dhātavo nāsya hetupratyayabhūtāḥ śakrasya tadāsanavat| pūrvatra kāyasyādhārabhūto rūpakāya evoktaḥ| iha tu maheśākhyasya jñāna­kāya­syāsanasthānīyaḥ. Note: The phrase “the transcendent state of wisdom should be venerated” was implied in the Sanskrit but has been added to the main text, following the recommendation of Hari (272): sarvajñajñānahetuke ti tathāyata­jñānahetukā, prajñāpāramitā pūjyeti śeṣaḥ. The Tibetan phrasing differs here, but can be understood as roughly equivalent: “What is venerated (pūjaṃ) in the physical relics of the tathāgatas is born from the knowing of someone who has complete knowing (sarvajñajñāna).” Tib. (F.54.a) bcom ldan ’das de ltar de bzhin gshegs pa’i sku la mchod pa ni thams cad mkhyen pa’i ye shes kyi rgyu las byung ba lags so. Therefore, Blessed One, of these two lots placed before me, I would take this very transcendent state of wisdom. However, it is certainly not, Blessed One, that I lack reverence for the physical relics of the tathāgatas‍—I have truly nothing but reverence for those physical relics of the tathāgatas. Nevertheless, Blessed One, the physical relics of the tathāgatas receive devotional offerings to venerate them because they are pervaded by the transcendent state of wisdom.Both Hari and Ratnākara explain this phrase only when it is repeated, with slight variation, after the example below. See the next note. Note: Schopen (p. 150; C.249.6 & C.250.14) suggests the Sanskrit word “pervaded” (paribhāvita; yongs su bsgos pa) may be a scribal error or corruption of “caused to thrive” (prabhāvita; yongs su sgrub pa). In producing this translation we considered the question Schopen raised about whether it should somehow be emended to prabhāvita, but we have not done so because both Ratnākara and Hari use the term paribhāvita and explain that. But it should still be noted that the Tib. reads yongs su bsgos pa (suggesting paribhāvita or *parivāsita) throughout, but refers back to this section with rab tu phye ba (*prabhāvita), suggesting that possible corruption. Incidentally, we have also not followed Schopen’s suggestion that the double negatives be rendered as an emphatic affirmative here. Although Schopen’s suggestion would, of course, be normal in Sanskrit usage, the second half of the sentence is emphatic already and it might seem more awkward and repetitive to have two strong affirmations.

“Never mind, Blessed One, whether in the first lot there were placed this Jambu continent completely filled to the brim with physical relics of the tathāgatas. Never mind, Blessed One, whether a single world system of four great continents were completely filled to the brim with physical relics of the tathāgatas. Never mind, Blessed One, whether a thousandfold, lower order world system were completely filled to the brim with physical relics of the tathāgatas. Never mind, Blessed One, whether a millionfold, medium order world system were completely filled to the brim with physical relics of the tathāgatas. Never mind, Blessed One, whether a billionfold, great order world system were completely filled to the brim with physical relics of the tathāgatas. If in the second lot one were to place this transcendent state of wisdom written down, if the Blessed One were to tell me to take whichever one of these two lots I prefer, and if I had to choose one of these two lots placed before me, then in that case I would take this very transcendent state of wisdom. However, it is certainly not the case, Blessed One, that I lack reverence for the physical relics of the tathāgatas. I have nothing but reverence for those physical relics of the tathāgatas. Nevertheless, Blessed One, the physical relics of the tathāgatas‍—which are pervaded by the transcendent state of wisdom‍—receive devotional offerings to venerate them because the physical relics of the tathāgatas are, in effect, a repository for the state of complete knowing and that state of complete knowing is born from the transcendent state of wisdom. Therefore, Blessed One, of these two lots placed before me, I would take this very transcendent state of wisdom. However, it is certainly not the case, Blessed One, that I lack reverence for the physical relics of the tathāgatas. I have nothing but reverence for those physical relics of the tathāgatas. Nevertheless, Blessed One, the physical relics of the tathāgatas, born from this transcendent state of wisdom, receive devotional offerings to venerate them specifically because they are pervaded by the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.74.b), the Tathāgata is extremely worthy of veneration because he physically manifests in all the worlds, but he is the most worthy of veneration because he possesses the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. Thus, the physical relics are worthy of veneration because they are a repository for that knowing. But if that is so, then for the very same reason the transcendent state of wisdom, which is the cause of that knowing, should be the most worthy of veneration among all the physical relics. RK: tathāgato hi sarva­lokābhyudgatatvāt pūjyataraḥ, pūjyatamaḥ sa ca sarvajñajñānam| tato yadi dhātavas tadādhāratvāt pūjyās tadā tajjananī tato pi pūjyatamā kā kathā dhātūnām iti. According to Hari (274), take the phrase “specifically because they are pervaded” to imply that a fruit is only absolutely pure if it has arisen from an absolutely pure cause. Insofar as the tathāgatas are “pervaded” by the arising of the great power of the transcendent state of wisdom, they are made to dwell everywhere. Hence, they “receive veneration” by virtue of their arising from that. Hari: yad uta prajñā­pāramitāparibhāvitatvād iti. suviśuddhāt kāraṇāt samutpannaṃ phalaṃ suviśuddham evopajāyata iti kṛtvā yasmāt prajñāpāramitāyā mahānu­bhāvatvotpādanena paribhāvitās tathāgatāḥ sarvathā vāsitās tasmāt tadutpattyā pūjāṃ labhanta.
“O Blessed One, as an analogy, it is just like the invaluably precious jewel that has the following positive qualities.

“When that invaluable jewel is put somewhere, then neither humans nor malevolent spirits are able to find any vulnerability there. Or, when this jewel is merely brought into a place where a malevolent spirit had taken possession of some man or woman, then that malevolent spirit leaves that person.
“Also, when that jewel is placed on the bloated body of someone afflicted by a wind disorder, then that wind disorder is suppressed; it decreases and subsides.
“Also, when that jewel is placed on the inflamed body of someone afflicted by a bile disorder, then that bile disorder too is suppressed; it decreases and subsides.
“Also, when that jewel is placed on the aching body of someone swollen with a phlegm disorder, then that phlegm disorder too is suppressed; it decreases and subsides.
“Also, when that jewel is placed on the body of someone suffering with a complex disorder of wind, bile, and phlegm, then that complex disorder is suppressed; it decreases and subsides.

“Also, that jewel illuminates the night’s darkest darkness. And, when it is placed in any hot location, then that location becomes cool. Likewise, when it is placed in any cold location, then that location becomes warm. Also, when the jewel is carried or placed in an area where poisonous snakes or other little creatures are rampant, then those poisonous snakes or other little creatures leave that place. Blessed One, when a man or woman bitten by a poisonous snake is shown the jewel, the poison is counteracted and vanishes along with the bite itself. O Blessed One, that jewel has positive qualities like these and also others of the following sort.

“When the jewel is placed on the eyes of someone with a tumor, a cataract, an eye ailment, or cloudiness in the eyes, then their eye disease will be eliminated and cured. O Blessed One, such a jewel might be endowed with positive qualities like these and others of this sort.

“When the jewel is placed in water, it makes the color of the water the same as its own color. When it is wrapped in a white cloth and tossed into the water, it makes that water white. Likewise, when it is wrapped or bound in a bright blue, yellow, red, or any other color cloth and tossed into the water, then it makes the water the same color. It also purifies the dirt in that water. That jewel is endowed with other similar positive qualities.”
Then Ānanda asked Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “But, Kauśika, are such jewels found only in the god realm or are they found also among the humans of the Jambu continent?”

Śakra said, “Ānanda, these jewels exist among the gods, but they are also found among the humans of the Jambu continent. However, the jewels on the Jambu continent are crude, few in number, limited in power, and lacking in these sorts of positive qualities. So, compared to the heavenly jewels, they do not come close to even a fraction of their worth. They do not come close to one hundredth, one thousandth, one hundred thousandth, one ten millionth, one billionth, one ten billionth, one trillionth, or even one ten trillionth of their worth. Nor is any calculation, any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison adequate for comparing them. Indeed, the jewels that exist among the gods are so refined and brimming with all sorts of positive qualities that any container into which a single jewel is tossed or thrown becomes desirable itself, even when that jewel is removed from the container. That intense desire for the container is because it is pervaded by the positive qualities of the jewel.The phrase “due to its being pervaded” has been added for clarity based on Hari (277) suggesting that it was left out or implied. Hari: tair maṇiratnaguṇair iti atra paribhāvitatvād iti śeṣaḥ.

“Similarly, Blessed One, the positive qualities of the transcendent state of wisdom and the knowing of someone who has complete knowing are the reason why the physical bodies of tathāgatas‍—including even the physical body of a worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata who has already passed into nirvāṇa‍—receive devotional offerings in veneration from people who consider the physical relics of the tathāgatas to have been, in effect, the vessel for the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. Furthermore, Blessed One, just as the teachings of the blessed buddhas should be venerated in all world systems by virtue of their being born from the transcendent state of wisdom, just so the Dharma teachings of the Dharma teacher should be venerated by virtue of their being born from the transcendent state of wisdom. That is to say, Blessed One, just as, as an analogy, a king’s attendant should be venerated without hesitation by the people, because of the king’s authority, just so, by virtue of the authority of the body of truth, the Dharma teacher’s Dharma teaching should be venerated without hesitation by the people.Here, the term “body of truth” (dharmakāya) seems to refer, for Hari, to the actual transcendent state of wisdom, but for Ratnākara only to the transcendent state of wisdom in a figurative sense. Either way, the word dharma means both the truth and the teachings. According to Ratnākara (F.74.b–75.a), here the king’s attendant should be venerated without hesitation everywhere. Likewise, the physical relics should be venerated because they are born from the transcendent state of wisdom and due to its authority.
