RK: rājapuruṣaś ca rājānubhāvād akutobhayaḥ sarvatra pūjyaḥ| evaṃ prajñā­pāramitānirjātatvāt tadanubhāvāc ca dhātavaḥ pūjyā. In the same way that the Dharma teachers and the Dharma teachings come to receive veneration, so too the physical relics of the tathāgatas come to receive veneration. Therefore, Blessed One, never mind whether in the first lot there was a billionfold, great order world system completely filled to the brim with physical relics of the tathāgatas‍—or even all the billionfold, great order world systems, as many as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, completely filled to the brim. If in the second lot one were to place this transcendent state of wisdom written down, and if I had to choose one of these two lots placed before me, because the Blessed One had told me to take whichever one of these two lots I preferred, then in that case, I would take this very transcendent state of wisdom. However, it is certainly not the case, Blessed One, that I lack reverence for the physical relics of the tathāgatas. I have nothing but reverence for those physical relics of the tathāgatas. Nevertheless, Blessed One, the state of complete knowing is pervaded by the transcendent state of wisdom and the veneration of the bodies of the tathāgatas is born from the state of complete knowing.This translation “pervaded by” (paribhāvita) follows the Sanskrit edition and manuscripts (e.g. the Cambridge Add.866 MS) and the commentators (Ratnākara and Hari), rather than Tibetan “revealed” (rab tu phye ba; *prabhāvita). Therefore, Blessed One, when the transcendent state of wisdom is venerated, the past, present, and future buddhas are venerated.

“Furthermore, Blessed One, a noble son or daughter who wishes to see, in accordance with the true nature of things, those blessed buddhas who are presently residing, staying, and spending time in immeasurable and innumerable world systems should go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, engage in meditation in the transcendent state of wisdom, and cultivate the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.75.a), the idea here is that the “true nature of things” is the body of truth, and that is just the transcendent state of wisdom (figuratively speaking). RK: dharmatā dharmakāyaḥ sa ca prajñā­pāramitaiveti bhāvaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.128.a) in a later section, “reside” means be there; “stay” means remain there; and “live their lives” means remain as long as they live. RK: tiṣṭhantī ti santi| dhriyanta iti avatiṣṭhante| yāpayantī ti yāvadāyur avatiṣṭhante.
Regarding what was said, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “That is true, Kauśika, that is true.

“Kauśika, those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening in the past have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening thanks to the transcendent state of wisdom.
“Kauśika, also those who will become worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas in the future will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening thanks to the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Kauśika, also those blessed buddhas who are presently residing, staying, and spending time in immeasurable and innumerable world systems realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening thanks to this very transcendent state of wisdom.

“Kauśika, also I myself‍—as the present worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata‍—have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening thanks to this very transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.75.a), up to this point the transcendent state of wisdom is described as causing perfect awakening, not as the body of truth (i.e., its result). To hint at that, Śakra speaks the next line. RK: iyatāpi bhagavatyāḥ samyak­saṃbodhi­kāraṇatvam uktaṃ na tu dharmakāyatvam| tadupasūcanāya punaḥ śakra āha| mahāpāramite tyādi.

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, it is a great transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom, because in it, Blessed One, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas accurately distinguish and properly see the deeds and mindsets of all sentient beings.”

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “So it is, Kauśika, so it is. To explain, Kauśika, it is for a long time that a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Because of this, he or she accurately and wisely discerns and beholds the mental activities of all sentient beings.”According to Ratnākara (F.75.a), “wisely discerns” means he knows these with nonconceptual knowing; “beholds” means he sees these in all aspects. RK: samyakprajānātī ti niṣprapañcena jñānena vettīty arthaḥ| saṃpaśyatī ti sarvākāraṃ paśyatīty arthaḥ.

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, does a bodhisattva great being go about life only in the transcendent state of wisdom and not in the other transcendent states?”

The Blessed One said, “Kauśika, a bodhisattva great being goes about life in all six transcendent states. Nevertheless, among those six, Kauśika, the transcendent state of wisdom alone takes the fore.According to Ratnākara (F.75.b), the entire Mahāyāna is condensed within the six transcendent states. The transcendent state of wisdom is chief among them, due to taking the fore and due to condensing all the others. RK: sarvamahāyānaṃ pāramitābhiḥ saṃgṛhītam| tāsu bhagavatī pradhānaṃ pūrvaṅgamatvāt saṃgrāhakatvāc ca. Whenever a bodhisattva great being is practicing generosity, preserving ethical discipline, engaging in tolerant acceptance, applying him- or herself with heroic perseverance, attaining the meditative states, or looking insightfully into things, Kauśika, the transcendent state of wisdom alone takes the fore for that bodhisattva great being. But, there is no difference among the six transcendent states, nor is any distinction mentally perceived, since they all are supported by skill in means, dedicated by the transcendent state of wisdom, and dedicated to the state of complete knowing.According to Ratnākara (F.75.b), the first phrase, “supported by skill in means,” expresses the overview, whereas “dedicated by the transcendent state of wisdom and dedicated to the state of complete knowing” express the explanation of that. RK: upāyakauśaly ena parigṛhītānām ity uddeśaḥ| prajñāpāramitayā sarvākārajñatāyāṃ pariṇāmitānām iti nirdeśaḥ. Kauśika, as an analogy, it is just like on the Jambu continent, where various trees of various heights and widths appear with various colors, various shapes, various leaves, various flowers, and various fruits. But there is no difference, nor is any distinction made, in the shadows of these trees. That is to say, one simply takes a shadow to be a shadow.According to Ratnākara (F.75.b), the “trees” with various flowers and so on is an analogy for the six transcendent states that are of (different) natures. The homogenous “shadows” are an analogy for these six being “supported by skill in means.” RK: upāyakauśaly ena parigṛhītānām ity uddeśaḥ| prajñāpāramitayā sarvākārajñatāyāṃ pariṇāmitānām iti nirdeśaḥ| tatra prakṛti­pāramitānāṃ dṛṣṭāntā nānāpuṣpādikā vṛkṣāḥ| ekarasā tu chāyā teṣām upāya­kauśalya­parigṛhītānāṃ dṛṣṭāntaḥ. In the same way, Kauśika, there is no difference among the six transcendent states, which are supported by skill in means, dedicated by the transcendent state of wisdom, and dedicated to the state of complete knowing, and no distinction is mentally perceived in them.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said this to the Blessed One: “Blessed One, this, the transcendent state of wisdom, is endowed with great positive qualities. Blessed One, this, the transcendent state of wisdom, is endowed with immeasurable positive qualities. Blessed One, this, the transcendent state of wisdom, is endowed with innumerable positive qualities.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Extolling Positive Qualities,” the fourth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 5: The Kinds of MeritAccording to Ratnākara (F.84.b), it is the chapter shedding light on the different kinds of merit. RK: puṇya sya paryāyā bhedās taddyotakaḥ parivartas tatparivartaḥ.The beginning of this chapter corresponds (more or less) to the beginning of Chapter 23 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 32 in the Eighteen Thousand, and its content mostly matches those chapters in the longer sūtras.
div.p: Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “Suppose, Blessed One, that a certain noble son or daughter were to do the following. First, with intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening. Then, based on this altruistic disposition, he or she would listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it.The following comments by Ratnākara have already been included in an earlier note (n.292) when these terms first occurred, but they are repeated here because Ratnākara actually made those comments on this specific passage. According to Ratnākara (F.76.a–b), one should “listen to it” based on the text and the meaning; “take it in” means repeat it; “keep it in mind” means make it stay in one’s heart; “chant” means read aloud; and “master” means completely understand. Up to here is taught for one’s own benefit. The words “pass it on” mean transmit it to others‍—this is the overview. The next three verbs are the detailed explanation of this: One should “teach it” based on speaking about the text, and “expound it” based on the meaning, and one should “explain it,” i.e., instruct others in it. Up to here is taught for the benefit of others. Finally, “recite it” means repeat it over and over because it brings benefit to oneself again and again. RK: tena śṛṇuyād granthato ’rthataś ca| udgṛṇhīyād āvartayet| dhārayed iti hṛdaye sthāpayet| vācayet pāṭhayet| paryavāpnuyād iti sarvathā avagaccheta| iyatā svārtho uktaḥ| pravartayed iti pareṣu sañcārayet| ayam uddeśaḥ| paro nirdeśaḥ| deśayed granthato bhāṣaṇāt| upadiśed arthataḥ| uddiśed ity avavadet| iyato parārthaḥ| svādhyāyed iti sutarām abhyaset| punaḥ punaḥ svārthakaraṇāt. Next, for the sake of others, he or she would elucidate it in detail, unpack its meaning, mentally investigate it, and fully examine the way it is with exceeding wisdom.According to Ratnākara’s creative etymologies here (F.76.b, already included earlier in n.293), “elucidate” (samprakāśayed) means consistently (sam-/santata) make clear (-prakāśayed ) the transcendent state of wisdom based on the words in the text. The phrase “in detail” means doing so from multiple angles. The word “unpack” means expanding upon those words. The phrase “mentally investigate it” means one should learn it by heart, connect with it, and observe it, because these steps produce tranquility (śamatha). Next, one should “fully examine it,” because thinking analytically about it produces insight (vipāśyanā). Up to here, he explains what should be done. But if that is not possible, he explains another possibility, with the words “or…at the very least,” and so on. RK: parebhyaś ca samprakāśayed iti san tataṃ prakāśayed granthataḥ| vistareṇe ti bahubhyaḥ prakāśanāt| vivṛṇuyād iti vi stareṇeti vartate| manasā ’nvavekṣete ti cittenānubudhya saṃyojya paśyet| sa(śa)mathotpādanāt| parimīmāṃsām iti vicārāṇāṃ vipaśyanotpādanāt| iyatā caryoktā| asyā abhāve pakṣāntaram āha| pustake ty ādinā| ata eva antaśaḥ śabdaḥ. Or if not all that, then at the very least, this noble son or daughter would make the transcendent state of wisdom into a book, maintain it, and preserve it so that these genuine teachings would last for a long time, while praying, ‘May the buddhas’ guidance here not be cut off.The following comments by Ratnākara were included in an earlier note (note 291) when these terms first occurred, but they are repeated here because Ratnākara actually made those comments on the present passage. According to Ratnākara (F.76.b), one should “maintain” precisely this “book,” meaning that one should keep it from perishing (which in India would have been done with string and two slabs of wood to protect it). One should “preserve” this in one’s home and so on. RK: pustakagatām iti| imām eva dhārayed iti| avinaśvarī kuryāt| sthāpayed iti svagṛhādau. May these genuine teachings not disappear. May the continued existence of this guidance provide assistance to bodhisattva great beings.’The following comments by Ratnākara were included in an earlier note (note 292) when these terms first occurred, but they are repeated here because Ratnākara actually made those comments on the present passage. According to Ratnākara (F.76.b–77.a), the “buddhas” are the gurus of the world; we should show gratitude toward them. This “guidance” of theirs is the wisdom that brings one to awakening. After wandering for three countless kalpas, they first developed it and then established it perfectly within their own tradition for the benefit of future beings. Since it has now reached us, we should not let “the buddhas’ guidance here be cut off”‍—meaning we should make sure it remains. To “provide assistance (anugrahopasaṃhāraḥ kṛta; phan gdags pa) to bodhisattva great beings” refers to what should be accomplished. And, it will be accomplished by “this guidance’s continued existence,” i.e., by its remaining in the world. RK: buddhāś ca lokaguravas teṣāṃ ca kṛtajñair asmābhir bhavitavyam| teṣām iyaṃ netrī bodheḥ prāpikā prajñā| tais trikalpāsaṃkhyeyā pariśrameṇotpādya samyag anāgatajanatārthāya svavaṃśe ’vasthāpitā| yāvad asmān upagatā| tato mā buddhanetrī samucchedo bhūd ity apīmāṃ sthāpayet|… bodhisattvānāṃ cānugrahopasaṃhāraḥ karaṇīyaḥ| sa ca kṛto bhaviṣyati| anayā sthāyitayā netryavaikalyena. Note: The Sanskrit word netrī means “guide rope” and can be translated more abstractly as “way” or even “guideline,” but it refers here more to the material teachings that help guide beings to awakening. Cf. DOP, Part II, pp. 639–640 s.v. netti.

“Now, suppose that after hearing the explication, the bodhisattva great being were to think,According to Ratnākara (F.77.a), “the explication” refers to the instructions to be presented to him or her. RK: imaṃ nirdeśam iti kartavyopadeśam. ‘Oh yes, this transcendent state of wisdom is extremely effective. Yes, this transcendent state of wisdom is extremely beneficial, extremely fruitful, and extremely rewarding.According to Ratnākara (F.77.a), “extremely effective” in the sense that the effects of the transcendent state of wisdom are great. This first adjective is the overview; the next three adjectives are the explication of this. “Extremely beneficial” refers to the previously mentioned benefits. “Extremely fruitful” is in the sense of the fruit of stream enterer, once-returner, non-returner, worthy one, solitary realizer, and buddha. “Extremely rewarding” is in the sense of all the blessings pervading activities. Up to here, he has just explained its qualities. RK: evaṃ mahārthike tyādi| mahān artha kāryam asyā iti mahārthikā| ayam uddeśaḥ| paro nirdeśaḥ| mahānuśaṃsā yathoktānuśaṃsaḥ| mahāphalā srotaāpannatvena, sakṛdāgāmitvena, anāgāmitvena, arhattvena, pratyekabodhyā, samyaksambodhyā ca| mahāvipākā sarvābhiś caryāvibhūtibhiḥ| bahu­mahā­guṇa­samanvāgate ty upasaṃhāraḥ| iyatā bhagavatīguṇa uktaḥ. Oh yes, this transcendent state of wisdom has so many positive qualities.’

“Also, suppose the bodhisattva great being were to have this firm conviction: ‘I must never abandon this transcendent state of wisdom. I must always safeguard this transcendent state of wisdom. I must always protect this transcendent state of wisdom since this transcendent state of wisdom is something supremely difficult to obtain.’

“And suppose that he or she were to pay tribute to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—with heavenly flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—respect it, venerate it, honor it, tend to it, and revere it.The word translated as “revere” (gurukuryād) idiosyncratically appears third in this list of activities, instead of last where it has been left for consistency with other lists.

“On the other hand, suppose that a second noble son or daughter were to make offerings in veneration to the transcendent state of wisdom. And suppose that in addition, after at the very least making it into a book, he or she were to give it, to present it, to offer it, and to let go of it for the sake of other noble sons or daughters seeking it, longing for it, and praying for it.According to Ratnākara (F.77.b), since the word artha here essentially means “wanting”‍—in the sense of wishing to venerate‍—we should understand them to be “seeking it” in connection with that wish; “longing” due to wishing intensely; and “praying” to the transcendent state of wisdom (herself) in order to venerate it. Here, “give it” means grant (access) to it; “present it” means help them approach it; “offer it” means let them have it; and “let go of it” due to lacking any regret. “At the very least” means even so much as. Here, “after a book being made” means either by oneself writing it down, the other person writing it down, oneself sponsoring it to be written down, or the other person sponsoring it to be written down. RK: arthanam arthaḥ | pūjāyām icchā tadyogād arthikāya| atiśayena tadyogāc chandikāya| yācamānāye ti pūjayituṃ bhaga­vatī­prārthamānāya| dadyād ity abhyupagacchet| upanāmayet sannipātayet| niryātayet grāhayet| parityajed avipratisārāt| antaśaḥ iti yāvād ity arthaḥ| pustakagatāṃ api kṛtve iti svayaṃ likhitvā tena vā likhitvā tanmūlyena vā svamūlyena vā lekhayitvā. According to Hari (286), “give” is due to presenting entirely the nature of the text and its meaning together with its fruit. Then, in order to demonstrate precisely this (giving), Śakra next says “present it,” “offer it,” and “let it go,” respectively, in accord with the divisions into the preliminary, main, and subsequent practices (of giving). These three phrases are taught based on a bodhisattva’s nonperception of any gift, giver, or recipient. Hari: dadyād iti granthārtha­svabhāvāyāḥ phalena saha sarvasvadānāt. etad eva prayogamaulapṛṣṭhāvasthābhedena yathākramaṃ darśayann āha: upanāmayen niryātayet parityajed iti. deyadāyaka­prati­grāhakānupalabdhibhedād vā padatrayam uktaṃ. Then, Blessed One, which of the two noble sons or daughters‍—the one with a mental attitude of letting go, or the one without a mental attitude of letting go‍—would earn more merit?”According to Ratnākara (F.77.b), his or her “mental attitude” aims at “giving away,” i.e., generosity. RK: parityāgo dānaṃ tasmai buddhir icchā asyeti tadbuddhiḥ.

In response, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “To explain, Kauśika, I will put the question about this back to you yourself. Kauśika, answer as you please. What do you think of the following?

“Suppose that a certain noble son or daughter, solely on his or her own, were to pay tribute to the physical relics of tathāgatas who passed into nirvāṇa, to circumambulate them, to maintain them, to revere them, to respect them, to venerate them, to honor them, and to tend to them. Now suppose that a second noble son or daughter, on his or her own, were to pay tribute to the physical relics of the tathāgatas, to revere them, to respect them, to venerate them, to honor them, and to tend to them. And suppose that, out of compassion for sentient beings, he or she were also to widely disclose them to others, give them to others, and share them so that such veneration would become widespread.According to Ratnākara (F.77.b), “disclose” in an assemblage and in its own inherent form. Here, he would “give” for the sake of veneration, and “share” their vibrations or anything secondary. “Widely” means extensively. “Widespread” means that one goes about life with them. RK: saṃprakāśayed iti gaṇataḥ svarūpataś ca| dadyāt pūjanāya| saṃvibhajed iti spharatām adhikānāṃ vā dānāt| vistāro vaipulyam| tena caratīti vaistārikī.

“Which of the two noble sons or daughters would earn more merit? Would it be the second one who venerated them on his or her own and also widely disclosed them to others, gave them to others, and shared them with others? Or would it be the first one who venerated them only on his or her own, by him- or herself?”

Śakra said, “Blessed One, of the two noble sons or daughters, the second noble son or daughter‍—who would venerate them on his or her own, and, out of compassion for sentient beings, widely disclose them to others, give them to others, and share them with others so that such veneration would become widespread‍—would earn more merit.”

“Exactly right, Kauśika, exactly right," said the Blessed One. “Likewise, Kauśika, since the second of the two sons or daughters would write this transcendent state of wisdom down, make it into a book, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, promote it, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and, in addition, after at the very least making it into a book, would give it, present it, offer it, and let it go for the sake of others seeking it, longing for it, and praying for it, he or she would earn more merit as a result of helping others with a mental attitude of letting go. Moreover, Kauśika, if that second son or daughter were to go wherever there are noble sons or daughters who might serve, in effect, as vessels of this transcendent state of wisdom, to give them this transcendent state of wisdom, and to share it with them, then, Kauśika, he or she would earn an even greater amount of merit than the son or daughter just mentioned.

“Now, Kauśika, suppose that a certain noble son or daughter were to inspire all sentient beings on the Jambu continent and establish them in the ten virtuous ways of acting.According to Hari (289), he or she would “inspire” through making efforts for them to grasp this teaching and “establish” them in it based on causing those who have grasped it to remain firm. Hari: grahaṇāya yatnakaraṇāt samādāpayet. gṛhītānāṃ sthirīkaraṇāt pratiṣṭhāpayet. According to Ratnākara (F.77.b), “inspire” means make them accurately grasp; “establish” means through their not lapsing. RK: samādāpayed iti samyak grāhayet| pratiṣṭhāyet anatikramāt. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit than that would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, out of intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she at the very least would make it into a book. Then, with this intense faith, deep belief, and firm conviction, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening, and with this altruistic disposition give the book to other bodhisattvas who with clear, trusting mindsets had developed the mindset of awakening with an altruistic disposition.The words and structure of the above three sentences have been significantly rearranged for the sake of clarity. The English translation also interprets awkward Sanskrit phrasing in the light of its Tibetan interpretation. Or, he or she would tirelessly help the bodhisattvas succeed, at least, in writing and chanting the transcendent state of wisdom. Being well versed in it, he or she would help them grasp it, help them see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it.According to Hari (289), “succeed” due to performing the practice themselves. “Well versed” in it due to having made efforts. The “bodhisattvas” refers to those who have developed the mindset of awakening. As a brief overview, it says “help them grasp the transcendent state of wisdom” due to training in the words and meaning. Then, he says (more specifically) he or she “would help them see it clearly” (saṃdarśayet) because he or she would accurately explain the meaning to those who are confused due to ignorance, doubt, and mistaken understandings; “take on the practice of it” because he or she would promote a virtuous objective to those who are careless due to idleness associated with addiction or a lack of desire; “be enthusiastic about it” because he or she would explain the advantage of superior heroic perseverance to those depressed through association with weak conviction, the idea that they are incapable, regrets, or cowardice; and “rejoice in it” because of delighting in the truly positive quality of having proceeded on the path correctly through being connected with unmistaken meditative concentration and insatiable heroic perseverance. Hari: svayaṃ pratipattyanuṣṭhānāt sampādayet. kṛtotsāhatvād udyuktaḥ. amum ity utpāditabodhicittaṃ. samāsato granthārthaśikṣaṇād grāhayed imāṃ prajñāpāramitām iti pūrveṇa sambandhaḥ. ajñāna­saṃśaya­mithyājñāna yogān mūḍhaṃ pratibsamyagartha­kathanāt saṃdarśayet. kausīdyācchandikatā vyāsaṅgayogāt pramattaṃ kuśalārthaṃ pravartanāt samādāpayet. hīnādhimuktyaśakyātma­sambhāvanākaukṛtya­bahu­kṛtyabhīrutāyogāt saṃlīnaṃ viśiṣṭavīryānu­śaṃsakathanāt samuttejayet. aviparīta­samādhya­saṃtuṣṭivīrya­prati­pattiyogāt samyak­prati­panna­bhūta­guṇābhinandanāt sampraharṣayet. According to Ratnākara (F.78.a), “succeed” in it refers to the deeds described. Being “well versed” in it, he or she would help the bodhisattvas grasp the transcendent state of wisdom. “With words” he or she would bring them along the path to accomplish awakening, and “see it clearly” (saṃdarśayet) exactly as it is through logical reasonings. Based on that, he or she would “take on the practice of it” through engaging in reciting it and honoring it and so on. Whatever is engaged in, he or she would be “enthusiastic about it,” i.e., be encouraged, based on the explanation of the positive quality of heroic perseverance and the fault of idleness. “Rejoice in it” means through praises of those who have striven to persevere. RK: tayā sampādayed yathoktaṃ kāryam| udyukto ’ bhiyuktaḥ sann amuṃ bodhisattvaṃ bhagavatīṃ grāhayet| bodhisādhanatvena pratipādayed vācā| tathaiva sandarśayed yuktibhiḥ| tataḥ samādāpayed vācanārcanā diṣu pravṛtteḥ| pravṛttaṃ samuttejayed utsāhayet| vīryakausīdyayor guṇadoṣākhyānāt| ārabdhavīrya­stutibhiḥ saṃpraharṣayet. Note: The English translation cleaves here to Sanskrit etymologies rather than the Tibetan terms that approximate them. With words, he or she would guide, educate, and mentor them in this transcendent state of wisdom, and would elucidate its meaning for them.According to Ratnākara (F.78.a), there are three types of people: the faithful, the averse, and the indifferent. Among these, he or she would “guide” the faithful with words alone, because the faithful progress by means of mere words about what is suitable; “educate” the averse with words alone, because wrong views are refuted through the logical reasonings; and “mentor” the indifferent, i.e., make them inspired by the transcendent state of wisdom, based on communicating its positive qualities. RK: vāce tyādi| trividho lokaḥ śrāddho vimukha udāsīnaś ca. tatra śrāddhaṃ vācaiva neṣyati, sa hi saṃbhāvitasya vācaiva gacchat. vimukhaṃ vineśyati, kudṛṣṭīnāṃ yuktibhir bādhanāt. udāsīnam anuneṣyaty anurañjayiṣyati bhagavatyā, tadguṇākhyānāt. According to Hari (289), here “words” means instructions, due to their stemming from what needs to be done. “Guide” means help them accomplish the transcendent state of wisdom. He or she would “educate” in accord with accurate observation with clear comprehension, and “mentor” through eliminating the faults of torpor or agitation. Hari: vidheyatāpādanād evaṃ vācā nuśāsanyā neṣyati prajñāpāramitāṃ prāpūrayiṣyati. tatra ca samprajanyena samyagupalakṣaṇatayā layauddhatyadoṣāpanayanād yathākramaṃ vineṣyaty anuneṣyati. In this way, he or she would help them purify their mindsets, help them become free of doubt,According to Hari (289), he or she would “purify their mindsets” due to eliminating their ignorance and mistaken understandings, and “make them free of doubt” due to removing their doubts. Hari: ajñānamithyājñānavigamāc cittaṃ viśodhayiṣyati. saṃśayajñānanirākaraṇān nirvicikitsaṃ kariṣyati. and would speak to them as follows: ‘Come, noble children, and train yourselves in this very path of the bodhisattvas. For, by training in it, going about your lives in it, and exerting yourselves in it, you will very quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Upon your awakening, the constituents underlying your clinging to existence will dissolve ineffably into the state revealing the pinnacle of reality. In that state, you will serve as a sublime guide to boundless masses of sentient beings.’According to Hari (289), “this very path of the bodhisattvas” means the transcendent state of wisdom. The three verbs “training,” “going about your lives,” and “exerting” are based, respectively, on the three different phases (of practice), namely, armor-like preliminary practice, not retreating, and heroically persevering. The “constituents underlying the clinging to existence dissolve” (upadhisaṃkṣaya) means the impure constituents are absent, i.e., in the body of truth (dharmakāya). Hari: bodhisattvamārga iti prajñāpāramitāyām. saṃnāhaprayogāni­vartanavīryabhedād yathākramaṃ śikṣamāṇaś caran vyāyacchamāna iti padatrayam uktaṃ. upadhisaṃkṣaya iti samalaskandhābhāve dharmakāya ity arthaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.78.a–b), “training” means through contemplation; “going about” means with tranquility (śamatha), and “exerting” means by way of clearly seeing (vipaśyamāna). The word “supreme” derives from it being at the absolute level. The “constituents underlying the clinging to existence” are either with worldly influences (sāsravāḥ) or with both worldly influences and no worldly influences (sāsravānāsravāḥ). The “dissolution” of them means their complete destruction. With that destruction as a precondition, he or she will “teach” the “masses of beings” (sattvadhātuḥ sattvarāśiḥ) based on the destruction of all obstructions. The “state revealing the pinnacle of reality” is the remaining state that is merely what it is (after that destruction of all obstructions). RK: śikṣamāṇaḥ cintayā| carana śamathena| vyāyacchamāno vipaśyamānayā| sattvadhātuḥ sattvarāśiḥ| ātyantikatvād anuttare| upadhayaḥ skandhāḥ sāsravāḥ sāsravānāsravā vā| teṣāṃ saṃkṣayaḥ samastakṣayaḥ| tannimittam| abhivineśyasi sarvāvaraṇakṣayāt| bhūtakoṭiprabhāvanatā tanmātraśeṣatā. Note: The single Sanskrit word upadhi is interpreted by Ratnākara as plural and is translated here as the phrase “the constituents underlying the clinging to existence.”

“O Kauśika, compared to any noble son or daughter just mentioned, this noble son or daughter‍—because of his or her mental attitude of letting go‍—would earn an even greater amount of merit.According to Ratnākara (F.78.b), it is referring here to the mental attitude of letting go in every way. RK: parityāgabuddhye ti sarvākārayā tyāgabuddhayā.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind all the sentient beings on the Jambu continent. Suppose that in this same manner, a noble son or daughter were to inspire and establish all the sentient beings within this world system of four great continents in the ten virtuous ways of acting.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind all the sentient beings within this world system of four great continents. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to inspire and establish all the sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system in the ten virtuous ways of acting.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind all the sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to inspire and establish all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system in the ten virtuous ways of acting.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to inspire and establish all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system in the ten virtuous ways of acting.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to inspire and establish all the sentient beings that there are within as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in the ten virtuous ways of acting. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit than that would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, out of intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would at the very least make it into a book. Then, with this intense faith, deep belief, and firm conviction, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening, and with this altruistic disposition give the book to other bodhisattvas who with clear, trusting mindsets had developed the mindset of awakening with the altruistic disposition. Or, he or she would tirelessly help the bodhisattvas succeed, at least, in writing and chanting the transcendent state of wisdom. Being well versed in it, he or she would help them grasp it, help them see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it. With words, he or she would guide, educate, and mentor them in this transcendent state of wisdom, and would elucidate its meaning for them. In this way, he or she would help them purify their mindsets, help them become free of doubt, and would speak to them as follows: ‘Come, noble children, and train yourselves in this very path of the bodhisattvas. For, by training in it, going about your lives in it, and exerting yourselves in it, you will very quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Upon awakening, the constituents underlying the clinging to existence will dissolve ineffably into the state revealing the pinnacle of reality. In that state, you will serve as a sublime guide to boundless masses of sentient beings.’
“O Kauśika, compared to any noble son or daughter just mentioned, this noble son or daughter‍—because of his or her mental attitude of letting go‍—would earn an even greater amount of merit.

“Furthermore, suppose that noble son or daughter were to establish and to place all the sentient beings that there are on the Jambu continent in the four meditative states.According to Ratnākara (F.79.a–b), regarding the four meditative states, here the sūtra (Twenty-Five Thousand, 8.37, and the Ten Bhūmis, 1.249–252), explains: “(1) Devoid of desires and devoid of nonvirtuous qualities (related to mental or verbal objects of desire) and harmful deeds (caused by desire and affliction), one abides well, after accomplishing the first meditative state, which involves thinking and analysis, as well as joy and happiness born from voidness. (2) Due to thinking and analysis subsiding and due to the mindset becoming one-pointed with internally clear serenity, one abides well, after accomplishing the second meditative state, which involves no thinking and no analysis, and yet has joy and happiness born from concentration. (3) Since a yogi abides well with (greater) equanimity due to disinterest in joy, he or she has mindfulness, clear comprehension, and experiences physical pleasure. The yogi abides well, after accomplishing the third meditative state free of joy, since the yogi has (greater) equanimity, mindfulness, and abides in the (physical) pleasure to which a noble one bears witness. (4) Due to relinquishing pleasure, due to relinquishing pain previously already (in the first meditative state), due to the end of mental dis-ease and ease (in the second and third meditative states respectively), the yogi abides well, after accomplishing the fourth meditative state, which is the utter purity of mindfulness and equanimity free of pleasure or pain.” RK: caturṣu dhyāneṣv iti| atra sūtram– viviktaṃ kāmair viviktaṃ pāpakarair akuśalair dharmaiḥ savitarkaṃ savicāraṃ vivekajaṃ prītisukhaṃ prathamaṃ dhyānam upasampadya viharati| sa vitarkavicāraṇāṃ vyupaśamād adhyātmaṃ saṃprasādāc cetasa ekotībhāvād avitarkam avicāraṃ samādhijaṃ prītisukhaṃ dvitīyaṃ dhyānam upasampadya viharati| sa prīter virāgād upekṣako viharati smṛtimān saṃprajānan sukhaṃ ca kāyena pratisaṃvedayate| yat tad āryā ācakṣate upekṣakaḥ smṛtimān sukhavihārīti niṣprītikaṃ tṛtīyaṃ dhyānam upasampadya viharati| sa sukhasya ca prahāṇāt duḥkhasya ca pūrvam eva prahāṇāt saumanasyadaurmanasyayor astaṅgamād aduḥkhāsukham upekṣāsmṛtipariśuddhaṃ caturthaṃ dhyānam upasampadya viharati” iti. Aside from these comments, Ratnākara lists and explains the different aspects of each state, and makes other comments, the gist of which are inserted in some of the parentheses above, even though they cannot be fully included here. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit than that would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, out of intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would at the very least make it into a book. Then, with this intense faith, deep belief, and firm conviction, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening, and with this altruistic disposition give the book to other bodhisattvas who with clear, trusting mindsets had developed the mindset of awakening with the altruistic disposition. Or, he or she would tirelessly help the bodhisattvas succeed, at least, in writing and chanting the transcendent state of wisdom. Being well versed in it, he or she would help them grasp it, help them see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it. With words, he or she would guide, educate, and mentor them in this transcendent state of wisdom, and would elucidate its meaning for them. In this way, he or she would help them purify their mindsets, help them become free of doubt, and would speak to them as follows: ‘Come, noble children, and train yourselves in this very path of the bodhisattvas. For, by training in it, going about your lives in it, and exerting yourselves in it, you will very quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Upon awakening, the constituents underlying the clinging to existence will dissolve ineffably into the state revealing the pinnacle of reality. In that state, you will serve as a sublime guide to boundless masses of sentient beings.’Both the Sanskrit and Tibetan, from this point onward, abbreviate the above refrain by leaving off the concluding sentence, namely, “Kauśika, compared to (sakāśād) any noble son or daughter just mentioned (paurvakāt), this noble son or daughter‍—due to his or her mental attitude of letting go‍—would earn an even greater amount of merit (puṇyam).”

“Now, Kauśika, never mind all the sentient beings on the Jambu continent. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to inspire and establish all sentient beings within a world system with four great continents in the four meditative states.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind all the sentient beings within a world system with four great continents. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to inspire and establish all sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system in the four meditative states.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind all the sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to inspire and establish all sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system in the four meditative states.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to inspire and establish all sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system in the four meditative states.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to inspire and establish all the sentient beings that there are within as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in the four meditative states. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit than that would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, out of intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would at the very least make it into a book. Then, with this intense faith, deep belief, and firm conviction, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening, and with this altruistic disposition give the book to other bodhisattvas who with clear, trusting mindsets had developed the mindset of awakening with the altruistic disposition. Or, he or she would tirelessly help the bodhisattvas succeed, at least, in writing and chanting the transcendent state of wisdom. Being well versed in it, he or she would help them grasp it, help them see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it. With words, he or she would guide, educate, and mentor them in this transcendent state of wisdom, and would elucidate its meaning for them. In this way, he or she would help them purify their mindsets, help them become free of doubt, and would speak to them as follows: ‘Come, noble children, and train yourselves in this very path of the bodhisattvas. For, by training in it, going about your lives in it, and exerting yourselves in it, you will very quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Upon awakening, the constituents underlying the clinging to existence will dissolve ineffably into the state revealing the pinnacle of reality. In that state, you will serve as a sublime guide to boundless masses of sentient beings.’

“Furthermore, Kauśika, suppose that in this same manner a certain noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings on the Jambu continent in the four immeasurable states…According to Ratnākara (F.80.a), the “four sublime states,” namely benevolence and so on‍—belong to the desire realm and have sentient beings as their meditative object. Since those meditative objects‍—sentient beings‍—are beyond measure, the states are (also called) the four immeasurable states, namely benevolence, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity. These four states correspond to the wish that sentient beings meet with happiness, that they are separated from suffering, that they not be separated from happiness, and that they be free from emotional defilements, in the following prayer: “Alas, may all sentient beings be met with happiness, may they be separated from suffering, may they never be separated from happiness, and may they be freed from the emotional defilements.” For someone on the level (of the path of preliminary practice) involving practice based on firm conviction, the meditative object of all four is sentient beings. For someone on the (first seven bodhisattva) levels of joy and so on, the meditative object is “the actual nature (tathatā).” For someone on the (eighth through tenth bodhisattva) levels of unmoving and so on, the practice is free of any meditative object. RK: maitryādayaḥ kāmāvacarāḥ sattvālambanāś catvāro brahmavihārāḥ| apramāṇasattvālambanāś catvāryapramāṇāni| maitrī karūṇā muditā upekṣā ca| aho vata sarvasattvāḥ sukhena yujyeran duḥkhena viyujyeran sukhena mā viyujyeran saṃkleśān muñcerann ity ebhir ākāraiḥ sukhasaṃyogāśayo duḥkhaviyogāśayaḥ sukhāviyogāśayo ’saṃkleśāśayaś ca| adhimukti­caryābhūmau catasropi sattvālambanāḥ| pramuditādisu saptasu tathatālambanāḥ| acalādiṣv anālambanāḥ.

[Here, repeat the entire refrain four more times, each with regard to all sentient beings, starting from those on the Jambu continent all the way up to those in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges. But in the subsequent three sets of refrains, where for the first it is said to suppose that he or she were to establish them “in the four immeasurable states,”The instruction text actually says the “four sublime states” (brahmavihārāḥ), but for ease of understanding we have used the synonym “immeasurable states” (see preceding note) here, as in the preceding paragraph. successively insert instead “in the four formless attainments,”According to Ratnākara (F.81.a), the “four formless attainments” are the four formless realms. Regarding these, the sūtra (Twenty-Five Thousand, 8.37 and The Ten Bhūmis, 1.253) says, “(1) Due to having surpassed the conception of physical form, having ended the conception of resistance, having paid no attention to conceptions of various other things, a yogi abides at ease, after accomplishing the sense field of the infinity of space, with the conception of infinite space everywhere. (2) Due to having surpassed the sense field of the infinity of space, the yogi abides well after accomplishing the sense field of the infinity of consciousness, with the conception of infinite consciousness everywhere. (3) Due to having surpassed the sense field of the infinity of consciousness, the yogi abides well after accomplishing sense field of nothing, with the conception of there being nothing anywhere. (4) Due to having surpassed the sense field of no conception, the yogi abides well after accomplishing sense field of there being neither conception nor nonconception anywhere. RK: ārūpyasamāpattayaś catvāra ārūpyāḥ| teṣu sūtram [F.81r] “sarvaśo rūpasaṃjñānāṃ samatikramāt prati­ghasaṃjñānām astaṅgamāt nānātvasaṃjñānām amanasikārāt anantam ākāśam iti ākāśānantyāyatanaṃ upasampadya viharati| sa sarvaśa ākāśānantyāyatana­saṃjñāsamati­kramād anantaṃ vijñānam iti vijñānānantyāyatanam upasaṃpadya viharati| sa sarvaśa vijñānānantyāyatana­saṃjñā­samtikramāt nāsti kiñcid ity ākiñcanyāyatanam upasaṃpadya viharati| sa sarvaśa ākiñcanyāyatana­saṃjñāsamati­kramāt naiva­saṃjñānā­saṃjñāyatanam upasampadya viharati” iti. As well as these comments, Ratnākara makes others that cannot be included here. “in the five types of extrasensory knowledge,”According to Ratnākara (F.81.b), the “five types of extrasensory knowledge” refers to (1) the knowledge of various types of magical powers, meaning that one enjoys many types of magical powers, such as magically causing the earth to shake magically causing one to become many and vice versa, magically causing oneself to appear or disappear, magically passing through walls, fences, and mountains without getting stuck, moving through the air, submerging oneself in the earth or water and emerging again, magically creating smoke or fire, and so on; (2) the divine ear faculty, meaning that one hears sounds‍—whether human or divine‍—with a divine ear faculty that transcends any human capability; (3) the knowledge of others’ mindsets, meaning that one psychically discerns, just as they are, the mindsets of other persons or beings, i.e., one discerns just as they are how their mindsets are attached or unattached, hateful or not hateful, confused or unconfused, up to the point of discerning the unsurpassed mindset; (4) the knowledge that remembers the occurrences in previous lives, i.e., one remembers anywhere from one former life up to one billion lives, and one remembers anywhere from one day up to one billion eons, for which one can explain the month of occurrence, name, birth, livelihood, period of remaining, and longevity, And one remembers the occurrences in previous lives‍—including the different types of aspects, areas, circumstances‍—saying, “I died in that place due to such and such, and was reborn in that other place” from one lifetime to another all the way up to the present lifetime; and (5) the divine eye faculty, meaning that one sees beings with the purified divine eye faculty that transcends human capability. Also, one discerns the karmic situation in which beings will find themselves when they are dying or being reborn, whether they have positive or negative qualities, are inferior or superior, or are born in a positive or negative state of existence. One discerns that those beings who are associated with physically, verbally, and mentally positive deeds have at that time the accurate views not criticized by the noble ones. As a result of their physically, verbally, and mentally positive deeds, they are reborn after their physical demise in positive states of existence in a heavenly world. One also discerns that those beings who are associated with physically, verbally, and mentally negative deeds will exist again with false views criticized by the noble ones. As a result of performing actions based on their false views, they will be reborn after their physical demise and death and will fall into negative states of existence in a hell realm. Thus, with the divine eye that transcends any human capability, one discerns, precisely as it is, the death and rebirth of transmigrating (saṃgatika) sentient beings throughout all world systems in the ten directions in the most distant phenomenal dimension and to the farthest reaches of space. RK: pañcābhijñāḥ| ṛddhividhijñānam| divyaśrotram| paracittajñānam| pūrvenivāsānu­smṛtijñānam| divyacakṣuś ceti| atra sūtram– “so ’nekavidham ṛddhi vidhaṃ pratyanubhavati| pṛthivīm api kampayati| eko bhūtvā bahudhā bhavati| bahudhāpi bhūtvā eko bhavati| āvirbhā[v]aṃ tirobhāvam api pratyanubhavati| tiraḥ kuḍyaṃ tiraḥ prākāraṃ tiraḥparvatam apy asakto gacchati| ākāśam api krāmati| pṛthivyām api unmajjananimajjanaṃ karoti| udakepi pṛthivyām iva gacchati| dhūmāyaty api prajvalaty api” iti vistāraḥ|| “sa divyena śrotra dhātunā atikrāntamānuṣyakena śabdān śṛṇoti divyān mānuṣakāṃś ca| sa para sattvānām api parapudgalānāṃ cetasaiva cittaṃ yathābhūtaṃ prajānāti| evaṃ [sarāgaṃ] vigatarāgaṃ sadoṣaṃ vītadoṣaṃ samohaṃ vītamohaṃ cittam iti yathābhūtaṃ prajānāti yāvad anuttaraṃ cittam iti yathābhūtaṃ prajānāti| sa ekāṃ jātim anusmarati| dve tiśro (sro) yāvajjātikoṭīniyuta­śata­sahasrāṇy apy anusmarati| ekaṃ divasaṃ dve trīṇi yāvat kalpakoṭīniyutaśatasahasrāṇy apy anusmarati| amutrāha māsam evaṃ nāmā evaṃ gotra evaṃ jātir evam āhāra evaṃ cirasthitika evam āyuṣyaparyantaḥ| so ’haṃ tataś cyutaḥ [F.82r]sann amutropapanno yāvat tataś cyuta ihopapanna iti yāvat sākāraṃ soddeśaṃ sanimittam anekavidhaṃ pūrvenivāsam anusmarati|| sa divyena cakṣuṣā viśuddhenāti­krāntamānuṣyakena sattvān paśyati| cyavamānānupapannān api suvarṇān durvarṇān hīnān praṇītān sugatau durgatau yathākarmopagān sattvān prajānāti| amī bhavantaḥ sattvāḥ kāyasucaritena vāksucaritena manaḥsucaritena samanvāgatā āryāṇām anapavādakāḥ samyagdṛṣṭayaḥ| tena kāyavāṅmanaḥsu­carita­hetunā kāyasya bhedāt sugatau svargaloke deveṣūpapadyante| ime punarbhavantaḥ sattvāḥ kāyaduścaritena vāgduścaritena manoduścaritena samanvāgatā āryāṇām apavādakā mithyādṛṣṭayaś ca mithyādṛṣṭi­karma­samādānahetoḥ kāyasya bhedāt paraṃmaraṇād apāya­durgati­vinipātaṃ narakeṣūpapadyante| iti hi divyena cakṣuṣā atikrāntamānuṣyakena daśadiśi loke sarvalokadhātuṣu dharmadhātuparame ākāśadhātuparyavasāne ṣaṅgatikānāṃ satvānāṃ cyutopapādaṃ yathābhūtaṃ prajānāti” iti. and “in all the meditative states, the immeasurable states, the formless attainments, and the types of extrasensory knowledge.” Finally, end with the following:]These bracketed instructions to repeat the recitation (peyālam) are in both the Sanskrit and Tibetan texts, and hence have been translated and left in the main text above.
“Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit than that would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, out of intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would at the very least make it into a book. Then, with this intense faith, deep belief, and firm conviction, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening, and with this altruistic disposition give the book to other bodhisattvas who with clear, trusting mindsets had developed the mindset of awakening with the altruistic disposition. Or, he or she would tirelessly help the bodhisattvas succeed, at least, in writing and chanting the transcendent state of wisdom. Being well versed in it, he or she would help them grasp it, help them see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it. With words, he or she would guide, educate, and mentor them in this transcendent state of wisdom, and would elucidate its meaning for them. In this way, he or she would help them purify their mindsets, help them become free of doubt, and would speak to them as follows: ‘Come, noble children, and train yourselves in this very path of the bodhisattvas. For, by training in it, going about your lives in it, and exerting yourselves in it, you will very quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Upon awakening, the constituents underlying the clinging to existence will dissolve ineffably into the state revealing the pinnacle of reality. In that state, you will serve as a sublime guide to boundless masses of sentient beings.’

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings on the Jambu continent in all the meditative states, the immeasurable states, the formless attainments, and the types of extrasensory knowledge. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within the world system with four great continents in all the meditative states, the immeasurable states, the formless attainments, and the types of extrasensory knowledge.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within the world system with four great continents in all the meditative states, the immeasurable states, the formless attainments, and the types of extrasensory knowledge. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system in all the meditative states, the immeasurable states, the formless attainments, and the types of extrasensory knowledge.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system in all the meditative states, the immeasurable states, the formless attainments, and the types of extrasensory knowledge. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system in all the meditative states, the immeasurable states, the formless attainments, and the types of extrasensory knowledge.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system in all the meditative states, the immeasurable states, the formless attainments, and the types of extrasensory knowledge. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system in all the meditative states, the immeasurable states, the formless attainments, and the types of extrasensory knowledge.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system in all the meditative states, the immeasurable states, the formless attainments, and the types of extrasensory knowledge. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings that there are within as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in all the meditative states, the immeasurable states, the formless attainments, and the types of extrasensory knowledge. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit than this would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, out of intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would at the very least make it into a book. Then, with this intense faith, deep belief, and firm conviction, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening, and with this altruistic disposition give the book to other bodhisattvas who with clear, trusting mindsets had developed the mindset of awakening with the altruistic disposition. Or, he or she would tirelessly help the bodhisattvas succeed, at least, in writing and chanting the transcendent state of wisdom. Being well versed in it, he or she would help them grasp it, help them see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it. With words, he or she would guide, educate, and mentor them in this transcendent state of wisdom, and would elucidate its meaning for them. In this way, he or she would help them purify their mindsets, help them become free of doubt, and would speak to them as follows: ‘Come, noble children, and train yourselves in this very path of the bodhisattvas. For, by training in it, going about your lives in it, and exerting yourselves in it, you will very quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Upon awakening, the constituents underlying the clinging to existence will dissolve ineffably into the state revealing the pinnacle of reality. In that state, you will serve as a sublime guide to boundless masses of sentient beings.’

“Furthermore, Kauśika, suppose that noble son or daughter were to write out this transcendent state of wisdom and chant it him- or herself. Suppose that, having written it out for others, he or she were to give it to others, as described before. Then, compared to the noble son or daughter just mentioned, Kauśika, without any effort, this noble son or daughter would earn an even greater amount of merit.

“Moreover, Kauśika, suppose that noble son or daughter, conversant with the meaning, were to chant this transcendent state of wisdom. Also, suppose, for the benefit of others, he or she were to write it out and give it to others, as described before. Finally, suppose he or she were to teach it and elucidate it based on both its words and meaning. Kauśika, without any effort, this noble son or daughter would earn an even greater amount of merit.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, can this transcendent state of wisdom even be taught?”According to Ratnākara (F.82.b), the basic idea of this question (which is implied through tone or indicated physically) is that there is nothing to be taught, because the transcendent state of wisdom is completely clear. RK: iyam apy upadeṣṭavye ti kākvā śiraḥkāyena vā praśnaḥ| naivopadeṣṭavyā, parisphuṭa[tv]ād iti bhāvaḥ. According to Hari (297–98), Śakra’s motivation is to ask a question regarding the precise referent of the words being understood‍—“What causes the transcendent state of wisdom to be taught?”‍—given the following words (cited from Mūlamadhyamakakārikā, verse 24.18ab, and the Mahā­yāna­sūtrālaṃkāra, verses 13.11–12ab, respectively): “precisely that which is a dependent arising is considered emptiness”…“since no thing exists free from the dimension of things” (which imply that the transcendent state of wisdom cannot be taught as something separate from dependent arising). RK: yaḥ pratītyasamutpādaḥ śūnyatā saiva te matā| dharmadhātuvinirmukto yasmād dharmo na vidyate || iti vacanāt pratīyamānapadārtha eva hi prajñāpāramitā kasmād upadiśyata ity abhiprāyavān āha: iyam api bhagavan prajñā­pāramitopadeṣtavye ti.

In response, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, this transcendent state of wisdom can even be taught to undiscerning noble sons or daughters. Why to them? Because, O Kauśika, in the future, when a counterfeit transcendent state of wisdom will come to exist,According to Ratnākara (F.82.b–83.a), “undiscerning” means having a dull intelligence, because those who have not perfectly perceived (the meaning), based on scripture and reason, are shaken by the words of others. So, the question “Why to them?” here means “What causes them to be shaken?” The answer is that (they are shaken by) the “counterfeit” transcendent state of wisdom that describes a meaning opposite to this very teaching, since these dull-minded, gullible people are fooled by it. RK: abudhyamānasye ti jaḍadhiyaḥ| tasya yuktyāgamābhyāṃ samyagagrāhitasya paravācā calanāt| kaḥ punar enaṃ cālayiṣyatīti| etad āha tatkasya hetor iti| atrottaram| utpatsyate h ityādi| bhagavatīdeśakenaiva yā tasyā viparītārthavarṇanā sā tasyāḥ prativarṇikā | tayaiva jaḍasya śraddhālor vañcanāt. undiscerning noble sons or daughters who wish to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening will hear that counterfeit transcendent state of wisdom and should not be left to go astray because of it.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, in the future, how will one be able to know, ‘This that is being taught is the counterfeit transcendent state of wisdom’?”

In response, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, in the future, there will appear some monks with unpurified physical bodies‍—with unpracticed ethical discipline, unconcentrated mindsets, and uncultivated wisdom‍—who are sheep-like and lacking in wisdom.This sentence‍—especially the multivalent Sanskrit word bhāvita, which has been translated differently in each of its four occurrences‍—has been interpreted based on the comments by Ratnākara (F.83.b) and Hari (299). Both commentators agree that these monks have failed to properly cultivate the three trainings of the path, namely, “ethical discipline,” “mindset” (citta)‍—here a synonym for concentration (samādhi)‍—and “wisdom.” Beyond that, Hari gives an extended explanation of why these “monks” refer to the Yogācāra proponents of consciousness only, whereas Ratnākara explains that these “monks” refer to the Sautrāntika scholars (who put forth interpretations of the transcendent state of wisdom). They will say, ‘We teach the transcendent state of wisdom,’ but they will teach its counterfeit. How, Kauśika, will they teach its counterfeit? They will claim, ‘The impermanence of physical forms means the utter annihilation of physical forms.’ Likewise, they will claim, ‘The impermanence of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness means the utter annihilation of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.’ And in this way, they will claim, ‘One who strives for utter annihilation like this is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.’ O Kauśika, statements such as these should be recognized as the counterfeit transcendent state of wisdom. One should never regard the impermanence of physical forms as the utter annihilation of physical forms. Likewise, one should not see the impermanence of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness as the utter annihilation of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. If one were to see this way, one would be going about life in a counterfeit transcendent state of wisdom. Therefore, Kauśika, the meaning of the transcendent state of wisdom must be taught by a noble son or daughter, and any noble son or daughter who actually teaches the meaning of the transcendent state of wisdom would earn more merit.

“Now suppose that a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings on the Jambu continent in the fruit of a stream enterer. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra.

Then, with this intense faith, deep belief, and firm conviction, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening, and with this altruistic disposition give the book to other bodhisattvas who with clear, trusting mindsets had developed the mindset of awakening with the altruistic disposition. Or, he or she would tirelessly help the bodhisattvas succeed, at least, in writing and chanting the transcendent state of wisdom. Being well versed in it, he or she would help them grasp it, help them see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it. With words, he or she would guide, educate, and mentor them in this transcendent state of wisdom, and would elucidate its meaning for them. In this way, he or she would help them purify their mindsets, help them become free of doubt, and would speak to them as follows: ‘Come, noble children, and train yourselves in this very path of the bodhisattvas. For, by training in it, going about your lives in it, and exerting yourselves in it, you will very quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Upon awakening, the constituents underlying the clinging to existence will dissolve ineffably into the state revealing the pinnacle of reality. In that state, you will serve as a sublime guide to boundless masses of sentient beings.’

“In addition, suppose he or she also said, ‘You will become someone who attains such qualities, namely, those associated with the transcendent state of wisdom.’ Kauśika, they would earn an even greater amount of merit than the noble son or daughter just mentioned. How so? From this merit, the fruit of a non-returner will come forth.

“Moreover, suppose that noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings on the Jambu continent in the state of a worthy one. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit than that would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, out of intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would at the very least make it into a book. Then, with this intense faith, deep belief, and firm conviction, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening, and with this altruistic disposition, give the book to other bodhisattvas who with clear, trusting mindsets had developed the mindset of awakening with the altruistic disposition. Or, he or she would tirelessly help the bodhisattvas succeed, at least, in writing and chanting the transcendent state of wisdom. Being well versed in it, he or she would help them grasp it, help them see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it. With words, he or she would guide, educate, and mentor them in this transcendent state of wisdom, and would elucidate its meaning for them. In this way, he or she would help them purify their mindsets, help them become free of doubt, and would speak to them as follows: ‘Come, noble children, and train yourselves in this very path of the bodhisattvas. For, by training in it, going about your lives in it, and exerting yourselves in it, you will very quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Upon awakening, the constituents underlying the clinging to existence will dissolve ineffably into the state revealing the pinnacle of reality. In that state, you will serve as a sublime guide to boundless masses of sentient beings.’

“In addition, suppose he or she also said, ‘You will become someone who attains such qualities, namely, those associated with the transcendent state of wisdom.’ Kauśika, he or she would earn an even greater amount of merit than the noble son or daughter just mentioned. How so? From this merit, the state of a worthy one will come forth. Furthermore, he or she would encourage them in this way: ‘Noble children, as you train in the transcendent state of wisdom, you will gradually come to attain the qualities of the buddhas and approach unsurpassed, perfect awakening. For, by training, going about life, and exerting yourselves in this, you will master the fruit of a stream enterer, you will master the fruit of a once-returner, you will master the fruit of a non-returner, you will master the state of a worthy one, you will master the state of a solitary realizer, and you will master the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of a buddha.’

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings on the Jambu continent in the state of a worthy one. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within the world system with four great continents in the state of a worthy one.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within the world system with four great continents in the state of a worthy one. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system in the state of a worthy one.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system in the state of a worthy one. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system in the state of a worthy one.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system in the state of a worthy one. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system in the state of a worthy one.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system in the state of a worthy one. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings that there are within as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in the state of a worthy one. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”

“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra. “Blessed One, it would not be easy to come up with any calculation for such masses of merit. It would not be easy to come up with any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison for such masses of merit.”

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, more merit than that would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, out of intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would at the very least make it into a book. Then, with this intense faith, deep belief, and firm conviction, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening, and with this altruistic disposition give the book to other bodhisattvas who with clear, trusting mindsets had developed the mindset of awakening with the altruistic disposition. Or, he or she would tirelessly help the bodhisattvas succeed, at least, in writing and chanting the transcendent state of wisdom. Being well versed in it, he or she would help them grasp it, help them see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it. With words, he or she would guide, educate, and mentor them in this transcendent state of wisdom, and would elucidate its meaning for them. In this way, he or she would help them purify their mindsets, help them become free of doubt, and would speak to them as follows: ‘Come, noble children, and train yourselves in this very path of the bodhisattvas. For, by training in it, going about your lives in it, and exerting yourselves in it, you will very quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Upon awakening, the constituents underlying the clinging to existence will dissolve ineffably into the state revealing the pinnacle of reality. In that state, you will serve as a sublime guide to boundless masses of sentient beings.’

“In addition, suppose he or she also said, ‘You will become someone who attains such qualities, namely, those associated with the transcendent state of wisdom.’ Kauśika, he or she would earn an even greater amount of merit than the noble son or daughter just mentioned. How so? From this merit, the state of a worthy one will come forth. Furthermore, he or she would encourage them in this way: ‘Noble children, as you train in the transcendent state of wisdom, you will gradually come to attain the qualities of the buddhas and will approach unsurpassed, perfect awakening. For, by training, going about life, and exerting yourselves in this, you will master the fruit of a stream enterer, you will master the fruit of a once-returner, you will master the fruit of a non-returner, you will master the state of a worthy one, you will master the state of a solitary realizer, and you will master the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of a buddha.’

“Now, Kauśika, suppose that a certain noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings on the Jambu continent in the fruit of a solitary realizer. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit than that would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, out of intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would at the very least make it into a book. Then, with this intense faith, deep belief, and firm conviction, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening, and with this altruistic disposition give the book to other bodhisattvas who with clear, trusting mindsets had developed the mindset of awakening with the altruistic disposition. Or, he or she would tirelessly help the bodhisattvas succeed, at least, in writing and chanting the transcendent state of wisdom. Being well versed in it, he or she would help them grasp it, help them see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it. With words, he or she would guide, educate, and mentor them in this transcendent state of wisdom, and would elucidate its meaning for them. In this way, he or she would help them purify their mindsets, help them become free of doubt, and would speak to them as follows: ‘Come, noble children, and train yourselves in this very path of the bodhisattvas. For, by training in it, going about your lives in it, and exerting yourselves in it, you will very quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Upon awakening, the constituents underlying the clinging to existence will dissolve ineffably into the state revealing the pinnacle of reality. In that state, you will serve as a sublime guide to boundless masses of sentient beings.’

“In addition, suppose he or she also said, ‘You will become someone who attains such qualities, namely, those associated with the transcendent state of wisdom.’ Kauśika, he or she would earn an even greater amount of merit than the noble son or daughter just mentioned. How so? From this merit, the fruit of a solitary realizer will come forth. Furthermore, he or she would encourage them in this way: ‘Noble children, as you train in the transcendent state of wisdom, you will gradually come to attain the qualities of the buddhas and will approach unsurpassed, perfect awakening. For, by training, going about life, and exerting yourselves in this, you will master the fruit of a stream enterer, you will master the fruit of a once-returner, you will master the fruit of a non-returner, you will master the state of a worthy one, you will master the state of a solitary realizer, and you will master the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of a buddha.’

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings on the Jambu continent in the fruit of a solitary realizer. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within the world system with four great continents in the fruit of a solitary realizer.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within the world system with four great continents in the fruit of a solitary realizer. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system in the fruit of a solitary realizer.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system in the fruit of a solitary realizer. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system in the fruit of a solitary realizer.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system in the fruit of a solitary realizer. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system in the fruit of a solitary realizer.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having established all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system in the fruit of a solitary realizer. Suppose that in this same manner a noble son or daughter were to establish all the sentient beings that there are within as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in the fruit of a solitary realizer. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”
