“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit than that would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, out of intense faith, deep belief, firm conviction, and a clear mindset that trusts in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would at the very least make it into a book. Then, with this intense faith, deep belief, and firm conviction, he or she would develop the mindset of awakening, and with this altruistic disposition, give the book to other bodhisattvas who with clear, trusting mindsets had developed the mindset of awakening with the altruistic disposition. Or, he or she would tirelessly help the bodhisattvas succeed, at least, in writing and chanting the transcendent state of wisdom. Being well versed in it, he or she would help them grasp it, help them see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it. With words, he or she would guide, educate, and mentor them in this transcendent state of wisdom, and would elucidate its meaning for them. In this way, he or she would help them purify their mindsets, help them become free of doubt, and would speak to them as follows: ‘Come, noble children, and train yourselves in this very path of the bodhisattvas. For, by training in it, going about your lives in it, and exerting yourselves in it, you will very quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Upon awakening, the constituents underlying the clinging to existence will dissolve ineffably into the state revealing the pinnacle of reality. In that state, you will serve as a sublime guide to boundless masses of sentient beings.’

“In addition, suppose he or she also said, ‘You will become someone who attains such qualities, namely, those associated with the transcendent state of wisdom.’ Kauśika, he or she would earn an even greater amount of merit than the noble son or daughter just mentioned. How so? From this merit, the fruit of a solitary realizer will come forth. Furthermore, he or she would encourage them in this way: ‘Noble children, as you train in the transcendent state of wisdom, you will gradually come to attain the qualities of the buddhas and will approach unsurpassed, perfect awakening. For, by training, going about life, and exerting yourselves in this, you will master the fruit of a stream enterer, you will master the fruit of a once-returner, you will master the fruit of a non-returner, you will master the state of a worthy one, you will master the state of a solitary realizer, and you will master the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of a buddha.’

“Now, Kauśika, suppose that a certain noble son or daughter were to develop the proper mindset aimed at the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of all sentient beings on the Jambu continent. On the other hand, Kauśika, suppose that a second noble son or daughter, who developed the proper mindset aimed at the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of all beings, were to give those beings this transcendent state of wisdom in written form. Or, Kauśika, suppose that the second noble son or daughter were to present this transcendent state of wisdom in written form to a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing, with the idea that the bodhisattva would train and engage in meditation in the transcendent state of wisdom, and, through this transcendent state of wisdom being cultivated, growing, expanding, and becoming extensive, the bodhisattva would perfect the qualities of a buddha.The translation here follows Tibetan because there seems to be a problem in the Sanskrit edition. Based on this, that second noble son or daughter would earn an even greater amount of merit than the noble son or daughter just mentioned. How so? It is absolutely certain he or she will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening and end the suffering of sentient beings.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit of having developed the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening for the benefit of all the sentient beings on the Jambu continent. Suppose that the first noble son or daughter were to develop the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening for the benefit of all the sentient beings within the world system with four great continents.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit of having developed the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening for the benefit of all the sentient beings within the world system with four great continents. Suppose that the first noble son or daughter were to develop the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening for the benefit of all the sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit of having developed the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening for the benefit of all the sentient beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system. Suppose that the first noble son or daughter were to develop the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening for the benefit of all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit of having developed the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening for the benefit of all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system. Suppose that the first noble son or daughter were to develop the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening for the benefit of all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit of having developed the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening for the benefit of all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system. Suppose that the first noble son or daughter were to develop the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening for the benefit of all the sentient beings within as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges.

“Now, Kauśika, suppose that a second noble son or daughter, who developed the proper mindset aimed at the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of all beings, were to give those sentient beings this transcendent state of wisdom in written form. Or, Kauśika, suppose that the second noble son or daughter were to introduce this transcendent state of wisdom in written form to a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing, with the idea that the bodhisattva would train and engage in meditation in this transcendent state of wisdom, and that in this way, as this transcendent state of wisdom is cultivated more and more and grows, expands, and becomes extensive, the bodhisattva would perfect it. Kauśika, that second noble son or daughter would earn more merit compared to the first noble son or daughter just mentioned. How so? It is absolutely certain that he or she will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening and end the suffering of sentient beings.

“Now, Kauśika, suppose that all the beings on the Jambu continent were first to become bodhisattvas beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Then, suppose that a certain noble son or daughter were to make a written book of this transcendent state of wisdom, give it, and introduce it to all beings who had fully set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Next, suppose that after making a written book of this transcendent state of wisdom, giving it, and introducing it to them, he or she would expound its words and meaning to them. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing this?”

“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra. “Blessed One, it would not be easy to come up with any calculation for such masses of merit. It would not be easy to come up with any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison for such masses of merit.”

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit than that would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, the noble son or daughter would make a written book of this transcendent state of wisdom, give it, and introduce it to those bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing who have the wish to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening very quickly. Then, he or she would expound its words and meaning. Next, he or she would instruct and coach them in this transcendent state of wisdom.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having introduced this transcendent state of wisdom to all those beings beyond regressing on the Jambu continent. Suppose that all sentient beings within the world system with four great continents were also to become bodhisattvas beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Then, suppose that after making a written book of this transcendent state of wisdom, a certain noble son or daughter were to give it, introduce it, and expound its words and meaning to them all.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having introduced this transcendent state of wisdom to all sentient beings beyond regressing within the world system with four great continents. Suppose that all beings within a thousandfold, lesser order world system were also to become bodhisattvas beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Then, suppose that after making a written book of this transcendent state of wisdom, a certain noble son or daughter were to give it, introduce it, and expound its words and meaning to them all.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having introduced this transcendent state of wisdom for the benefit of all sentient beings beyond regressing within a thousandfold, lesser order world system. Suppose that all the sentient beings within a millionfold, medium order world system were also to become bodhisattvas beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Then, suppose that after making a written book of this transcendent state of wisdom, a certain noble son or daughter were to give it, introduce it, and expound its words and meaning to them all.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having introduced this transcendent state of wisdom for the benefit of all sentient beings beyond regressing within a millionfold, medium order world system. Suppose that all the sentient beings within a billionfold, great order world system were also to become bodhisattvas beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Then, suppose that after making a written book of this transcendent state of wisdom, a certain noble son or daughter were to give it, introduce it, and expound its words and meaning to them all.

“Now, Kauśika, never mind the accumulation of merit from having introduced this transcendent state of wisdom for the benefit of all sentient beings beyond regressing within a billionfold, great order world system. Suppose that all the sentient beings within as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges were to become bodhisattvas beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Then, suppose that after making a written book of this transcendent state of wisdom, a certain noble son or daughter were to give it, introduce it, and expound its words and meaning to them all. Kauśika, what do you think? Would that noble son or daughter earn a great amount of merit by doing so?”

“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Śakra. “Blessed One, it would not be easy to come up with any calculation for such masses of merit. It would not be easy to come up with any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison for such masses of merit.”

The Blessed One said, “O Kauśika, an even greater amount of merit than that would be earned by any noble son or daughter who did the following. First, after making a written book of this transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would give it and introduce it to those bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing who wish to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening even more quickly. Then, he or she would expound its words and meaning. Next, he or she would instruct and coach them in this transcendent state of wisdom. On the other hand, Kauśika, suppose some other bodhisattva great being appeared and said, ‘I will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening more quickly than they will.’ If, Kauśika, that noble son or daughter were to instruct and coach that bodhisattva great being in the transcendent state of wisdom, then he or she would earn an even greater amount of merit than that noble son or daughter just mentioned.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, it is the case that as a bodhisattva great being approaches unsurpassed, perfect awakening, he or she is to be instructed and guided in the transcendent state of wisdom, and, in being instructed and guided in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she becomes close to the actual nature of things. And since that bodhisattva great being is becoming close to the actual nature of things, he or she transforms any offerings enjoyed‍—be they robes, alms, seats, beds, preventive medicines, remedies, and other requisites‍—into something extremely fruitful and extremely beneficial for those who made those offerings. Hence, this noble son or daughter would earn an even greater amount of merit than the other noble sons or daughters just mentioned.According to Hari (321–22), in this sentence Śakra explains that the merit for instructing and guiding a bodhisattva who has superior faculties and will quickly realize awakening is greater than bringing benefit to the others (mentioned in the paragraph above). This principle applies not only after that bodhisattva has awakened, but also when he or she has still not awakened. Among the (standard equipment of a monk), “robes” (cīvara) means cloth; “alms” means rice gruel and so on; “seats” means a twig mat and so on; “beds” means a mattress consisting of grass or wool and so on; “preventive medicines” are what works against being stricken by sickness; “remedies” means something suitable (to the occasion), such as a myrobalan fruit laxative and so on; and “other requisites” means a water filter and so on. “Those who made those offerings” means those who gave them robes and so on. The bodhisattva makes them “extremely fruitful” through his or her ability to bestow fruits in this life, and “extremely beneficial” through bestowing fruits in the next life. This explanation should be connected with (all the deeds and their merit described) previously above. The overall idea is that there is no difference between tathāgatas and bodhisattvas when it comes to their utter worthiness of receiving offerings (in terms of merit-making). Hari: na kevalam abhisambudhyānyeṣām arthakaraṇāt kṣiprābhijñaṃ bodhisattvam avavadato ’nuśāsataś ca bahutaraṃ puṇyaṃ, kiṃ tarhy anabhisambudhyāpīti pratipādayann āha: yathā yathā bhagavann ityādi. tatra cīvaraṃ vastraṃ. piṇḍapāto maṇḍakādi. śayanaṃ tūlikādi. āsanaṃ daṇḍāsanādi. vyādhibhir upahataṃ glānaṃ praty eti gacchati yuktarūpaṃ bhaiṣajyam harītakyādi. pariṣkāraḥ khallakādi. tān kārān kṛtān iti cīvarādin dattān ity arthaḥ. saṃdṛṣṭaphaladānasāmarthyān mahāphalān karoti. janmāntare phaladānān mahānuśaṃsāṃ karotī ti pūrveṇa sambandhaḥ. tathāgatānāṃ bodhisattvānāṃ ca paramada­kṣiṇīyatvena nirviśiṣṭatvād iti bhāvaḥ. Why is that? O Blessed One, it is because, in this way, a bodhisattva great being is becoming close to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”

Then the venerable Subhūti said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Excellent, Kauśika, it is excellent that you encourage, assist, and support those persons belonging to the bodhisattva path. Kauśika, it is something that must be done. Any noble disciple who wishes to assist all sentient beings encourages, assists, and supports these bodhisattva great beings in unsurpassed, perfect awakening in this way. This is to be done. Why is that? It is because of this that the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of bodhisattva great beings bears fruit. And if the bodhisattva great beings were not to develop the proper mindset of awakening, or to train in unsurpassed, perfect awakening, or to train in the six transcendent states, then, without that training, they could never realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. But since the bodhisattva great beings do train in the six transcendent states that constitute a bodhisattva’s training, they do indeed develop the proper mindset of awakening, and they do indeed realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Kinds of Merit,” the fifth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 6: Rejoicing and DedicatingThe content of this chapter corresponds to the content of Chapter 24 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 33 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: Then the bodhisattva great being MaitreyaAccording to Hari (325), here the word “bodhisattva” derives from his or her being endowed with accurate wisdom, and the word “great being” (mahāsattva) derives from his or her being replete with all types of means‍—because, according to a passage in the Amoghapāśa Sūtra, the words “bodhisattva” and “great being” are respectively terms for wisdom and means. Hari: samyakprajñāyogād bodhisattvaḥ. sarvākāropāya­sambaddhān mahāsattvāḥ. bodhi­sattva­mahā­sattvaśabdayoḥ prajñopāyābhidhāyitayāmoghapāśa­sūtre pāṭhād iti. According to Ratnākara (F.85.a), here the word “bodhisattva” refers to someone who has give rise to the mindset of awakening. Based on a distinction of a particular quality of a bodhisattva, the word “great being” refers to someone whose resolve is to dedicate (all merit to the awakening of others). RK: bodhisattvasy eti| utpāditabodhicittasya| yathoktaviśeṣaviśiṣṭatvān mahāsattvaḥ, pariṇāmanā vyavasāyo ’syeti mahāsattvasya. said to the elder, the venerable Subhūti, “Noble Subhuti, if one compares, on the one hand, the thing that is producing the meritThis chapter revolves around a slight play on the multivalent word vastu (dngos po), which the translation “thing” aims to capture. In the notion of disciples, meritorious actions are actual things that ultimately cause awakening. In the Mahāyāna, things like meritorious actions appear relatively to produce awakening, but ultimately nothing (i.e., no “thing”) produces awakening. Until the punchline is revealed later in the chapter, “thing” is used in deliberately ambiguous, paradoxical, and tongue-in-cheek ways to refer to (a) a particular causal action or quality that produces merit, (b) an object that someone might take as a meditative focus from a particular meditative perspective, (c) a material substance imagined to be real, and (d) an entity or particular mindset. In the lists of disciples, they generally mention three types of actions that produce merit: (1) generosity (dāna), (2) ethical discipline (śīla), and (3) cultivation (bhāvanā). But expanded lists include seven more, namely, (4) honoring (apaciti), (5) doing service (veyyāvacca), (6) not making profits (patti-anuppadāna), (7) rejoicing (abbhanumodanā), (8) teaching (desanā), (9) hearing/studying (savana), and (10) acting straightforwardly with the proper view (diṭṭh’ ujjukakamma). Cf. PTSD puñña (-kiriya-vatthūni), “qualities contributing to merit.” BHSD puṇyakriyāvastu, “object or item of a meritorious action.” associated with a bodhisattva great being’s dedication of rejoicing,According to Hari (326), this phrase means “associated with” that “dedication” which is preceded by “rejoicing.” Since a bodhisattva dedicates that rejoicing toward the unsurpassed, complete, perfect awakening of the tathāgatas by means of a mental activity whose object is free of mental perception, his or her mental activity of dedication is superior. Hari: anumodanāpariṇāmanāsahagatam iti anumodanā pūrvikā pariṇāmanā tathāgatānuttara­samyak­sambodhyanupalambhālambanena manaskāreṇa pariṇāmanād viśeṣaḥ pariṇāmanā manaskāras, tayā ca sahagatam iti samāsaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.85.a), in the phrase “associated with the dedication of rejoicing,” the word “rejoicing” means the virtue connected with rejoicing. It is explained in detail at the beginning of the (passage on) dedication without mental perception. RK: anumodanāsahagataṃ kuśalaṃ anumodanā| sā cānupa­lambha­pariṇāmasyādau vistareṇa vakṣyate| tasyāḥ pariṇāmanā| tayā sahagatam. and, on the other hand, on the part of all sentient beings, the thing that is producing the merit derived from generosity, the thing that is producing the merit derived from ethical discipline, and the thing that is producing the merit derived from cultivation, then the actual thing that is producing the merit associated with a bodhisattva great being’s dedication of rejoicing is called the foremost, the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled.”According to Ratnākara (F.85.a), it is the “foremost” and so on due to causing the fruit that is foremost and so on. This is because the respective cause for accomplishing the benefit of oneself and others that is included in each of the eleven levels (bhūmi), delightful (pramuditā) and so on, is also the causal potential (expressed) in these eleven descriptions. RK: agram ākhyāyate yāvad asamasamam ākhyāyate| tasmād agrā di| agrādiphala­hetutvāt| tad api kutaḥ ? yathākramaṃ pramuditādyekā­daśa­bhūmi­saṃgṛhīta­svaparārtha-saṃpattihetutvāt| paryāyatve pi sarveṣām upādānasāmarthyāt. Hari’s different interpretations of these descriptions is not concise enough to include here. For more info, see Sparham (ii, p. 224) or Wogihara (p. 228).

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the elder and bodhisattva great being Maitreya, “O Maitreya, a bodhisattva great being rejoices in all the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas‍—immeasurable among the immeasurable, innumerable among the innumerable, unlimited among the unlimited, inconceivable among the inconceivable, infinite and limitless among the infinite and limitless‍—who previously passed into the nirvāṇa dimension without any constituents remaining and have pervaded everywhere throughout each of the world’s ten directions in each of the immeasurable among the immeasurable, innumerable among the innumerable, unlimited among the unlimited, inconceivable among the inconceivable, infinite and limitless among the infinite and limitless billionfold, great order world systems.According to Ratnākara (F.86.a), although such tathāgatas passed into nirvāṇa, they do not stay there. They are worthy ones, in the literal sense that they have destroyed (hat avan) the enemies (ari) that are the defilements. They are “perfect buddhas” because their knowing of all things is unmistaken. With these two names, the accomplishment of a buddha’s relinquishment and knowing are taught. RK: īdṛśānāṃ tathāgatānāṃ parinirvṛtānām iti na tu tiṣṭhatām| arayaḥ kleśās tān hatavanta iti arhatām| aviparīta­sarva­dharmajñānāt samyak­sambuddhānām| anena padadvayena prahāṇajñānasampadāv ukte. Those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas eradicated their worldly ruts. They cut off their circuitous pathways.According to Ratnākara (F.86.a), because it is a worldly path, a “rut” refers to the conceit of a self in the Dharma. Because it is a worldly track, a “circuitous pathway” is craving. As is said, “The father is said to be the conceit of a self and the mother is said to be the craving.” Due to uprooting these two, they eradicate the ruts and cut off the circuitous pathway. RK: bhavapathatvād dharme asmimāno [vartmā]| vṛter anipratyayaḥ | saraṇidharaṇivat| muḍāgamaś ca| vartmanis tṛṣṇā bhavapadavītvāt| yathoktaṃ-“asmimānaḥ pitā ukto mātā tṛṣṇeti cocyate” iti| tayor ūnmūlanāt chinnavartmanāṃ chinnavartmanīnāṃ ca. They cut the leashes of dualistic proliferations and cyclic existence.According to Ratnākara (F.86.a), “dualistic proliferations” and “cyclic existence” refer, respectively, to the wrong views about things and wrong views about persons. RK: prapañco dvayakalpanā, bhavaḥ saṃsāraḥ| tayor netry au nāyike yathākramaṃ dharmadṛṣṭiḥ pudgaladṛṣṭiś ca. DOP netrikā, “leading rope of craving.” They exhausted their tears. They crushed their thorns.According to Ratnākara (F.86.a), because they are similar to thorns, the “thorns” are wrong views. Through pulverizing them, they are “crushed.” The rest of the descriptions were explained already in the scripture’s opening section. RK: kaṇṭakasādharmyāt kaṇṭakāḥ kudṛṣṭayaḥ| teṣāṃ kṣodanāt marditakaṇṭakāḥ| śeṣaṃ nidāna eva vyākhyātam. They completely laid down their burdens. They reached their own goals. They cut their worldly ties. They liberated their mindsets by accurate understanding. They achieved complete control over all mindsets.

“The bodhisattva great being rejoices in everything from when those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas first gave rise to the proper mindset, up to the point where they realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening and then passed into the nirvāṇa dimension without any constituents remaining and without their genuine teachings disappearing, as follows.According to Ratnākara (F.86.a–b), “from when” means beginning with. “First gave rise to the proper mindset” refers to the earth-like stage (which is the first of the twenty-two stages in the development of the mindset of awakening). “As follows” means that the following paragraphs are (a more detailed account of the roots of virtue planted) during that time period (i.e., from first giving rise to the proper mindset up through the genuine teachings disappearing). RK: prathamacittotpādo yaḥ pṛthivīsamaḥ| tam upādāya tata ārabhya| etasminn antara ity etāv atyavakāśe.

“That bodhisattva great being rejoices in those blessed buddhas’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation.These five “masses” (skandha) are qualities that constitute bodhisattvas, rather than the constituents (skandha) to which sentient beings cling. According to Hari (329), “the masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, and masses of wisdom refer to ethical discipline, concentration, and wisdom free of worldly influences (āsrava).” The “masses of liberation” refers to the abandonment of all obstructions. The “masses of seeing the knowing of liberation” refers to directly witnessing this distinctive knowing. Hari: anāsravaṃ śīlaṃ samādhiḥ prajñā ca śīlaskandhaḥ samādhiskandhaḥ prajñāskandhaḥ. sarvāvaraṇaprahāṇaṃ vimuktiskandhaḥ. viśiṣṭajñāna­sākṣātkaraṇaṃ vimukti­jñāna­darśanaskandhaḥ.

“That bodhisattva great being rejoices in their roots of virtue associated with the six transcendent states; their roots of virtue associated with the excellent qualities of a buddha; their roots of virtue associated with the transcendent state of the powers, the grounds for self-confidence, and so on;Since these “strengths, the grounds for self-confidence, and so on” are better known as qualities of a buddha, Ratnākara (F.86.a) explains here that these qualities also belong to bodhisattvas, but they have not reached the other shore yet. Hence, in order to delineate this, the term “transcendent state” (i.e., the state of going to the other shore) is mentioned to signify the path without worldly influences. RK: bodhisattvānām api balā dayaḥ santi| na tu te pāraṅgatāḥ| tatas tadvyavacchedāya pāramitā grahaṇam| anāśravo mārgaḥ parijñaḥ. their roots of virtue associated with the transcendent state of extrasensory knowledge; their roots of virtue associated with the transcendent state of complete knowing; their roots of virtue associated with the transcendent state of aspiration; and their roots of virtue associated with the excellent knowing of someone who has complete knowing.

“That bodhisattva great being rejoices in their care for others’ welfare; their great benevolence; their great compassion; their immeasurable and innumerable buddha qualities; their unsurpassed, perfect awakening; their unsurpassed ease of perfect awakening; their transcendent state of sovereignty over all things; and their development of the supremely miraculous power that is illimitable, unsurpassed, and has surpassed everything.

“That bodhisattva great being rejoices in the tathāgatas’ power of knowing reality as-it-is, which is unobstructed, unattached, unimpeded, unequaled, equal to the unequaled, without analogy, and illimitable;According to Ratnākara (F.86.b), “knowing of reality as-it-is” is the accurate knowing that belongs to tathāgatas. That knowing itself is the “power,” in that it has overcome everything else. RK: tathāgatānāṃ yathābhūta jñāna[ṃ] samyagjñānam| tad eva balaṃ sarvābhibhūtatvāt. the power of the buddhas’ knowing;According to Ratnākara (F.86.b), “the power of the buddhas’ knowing” is its potential for causal efficacy everywhere in all forms infinitely. RK: yad buddhajñānabalam iti| buddhajñāna sya balaṃ śaktir niravadhi­sarvākāraviśvārtha­kriyāyai. the buddhas’ seeing with knowing of the powers, namely,According to Ratnākara (F.86.b), the “buddhas’ seeing with knowing of the powers” refers to the actualization of the attainment. RK: balānāṃ yad buddhaiḥ jñānadarśanaṃ prāptisākṣātkaraṇam. the transcendent state of the ten powers, the realization overflowing with supreme ease, the four grounds for self-confidence, and so on; and the realization of the truth through actualizing the ultimate nature of all things.This and the subsequent sentence together hint at the relationship between the different meanings of the multivalent Sanskrit word dharma (“truth,” “things,” “teachings,” “qualities”). According to Ratnākara (F.86.b), how the “truth” is realized is by actualizing “the ultimate nature,” i.e., the accurate knowing, “of all things.” RK: sa katham ? sarvadharmāṇāṃ paramārtha sya samyagjñānasyābhinirhāreṇa niṣpādanena. According to Hari (331), “by actualizing the ultimate nature of all things” means by coming face to face with their nature being like magical illusions. Hari: paramārthābhinirhāreṇe ti māyopamatābhi­mukhī­karaṇena. Note: Hari and Ratnākara are explaining the “ultimate nature” according to their respective Yogācāra and Mādhyamika perspectives.

“That bodhisattva great being rejoices in the tathāgatas’ acts of setting in motion the wheel of the Dharma,This paragraph presents several metaphors that shed light on the different natures of the Dharma, i.e., teachings, as they are revealed to others. According to Ratnākara (F.86.b), “the Dharma” here refers to spiritual knowing, with its three phases and twelve aspects of teaching (see n.493). That itself is the “wheel” due to moving about in the world of gods and humans. “Setting in motion” means in the ten directions. RK: triparivartadvādaśākāraṃ lokottaraṃ jñānaṃ dharmaḥ| sa eva cakraṃ devamanuṣyeṣu caṃkramaṇāt | tasya pravartanaṃ daśasu dikṣu. According to Hari (331), by illuminating the self-realized “Dharma”‍—i.e., by teaching the transcendent state of wisdom‍—in order to make it be realized again and again, like a “wheel,” they are “setting it in motion” i.e., circulating it in the mindstreams of those to be tamed. Hari: dharmacakrapravartanam iti dharma sya svādhigamasya dyotikayā prajñāpāramitādeśanayā cakra vat punaḥpunar āmukhīkaraṇārthena pravartanaṃ vineyasaṃtāne vistārīkaraṇaṃ. presenting the torch of the Dharma,According to Ratnākara (F.87.a), “torch of the Dharma” refers to the speech that illuminates that very Dharma. “Presenting” that means mentally setting it forth. RK: tasyaiva dharmasya dyotanī vāg dharmolka (sic?)| tasyāḥ pragrahaṇaṃ manasā vyavasthāpanam. beating the drum of the Dharma,According to Ratnākara (F.87.a), “drum of the Dharma” refers to that very same speech and “beating” it means making it heard in order to conquer those to be tamed. RK: saiva vāg dharmabherī| tasyāḥ saṃpratāḍanaṃ vineyajayaśrāvaṇam. blowing the conch of the Dharma, sounding the conch of the Dharma,According to Ratnākara (F.87.a), “blowing” the conch of the Dharma refers to those to be tamed becoming aware (at first), and “resounding” it refers to them actualizing the contemplation and cultivation. RK: saiva vā dharmaśaṅkhaḥ| tasya pūraṇaṃ vineyair eva jñānam| tasya dharmaśaṅkhasya pravyāharaṇam| tam evālambya tadarthākāreṇa manasi kurvatāṃ cintābhāvanayoḥ pariniṣpattiḥ. According to Hari (331), “blowing” means filling up the minds of those to be tamed and “resounding” is due to it filling them up. Hari: vineyamanaḥprapūraṇād dharmaśaṅkhaprapūraṇaṃ. paurītvena dharmaśaṅkhapravyāharaṇaṃ. striking with the sword of the Dharma,According to Ratnākara (F.87.a), “the sword” is precisely the spiritual knowing arisen by force of cultivation. “Striking with” it means destroying the defilements (khaḍgapraharaṇam<vidhvaṃsanam): RK: bhāvānābalād utpannaṃ tad eva lokottaraṃ jñānaṃ dharmakhaṅgaḥ | tena praharaṇaṃ kleśānāṃ vidhvaṃsanam. According to Hari (331), “striking” is due to combating all the tough obstructions. Hari: dṛḍha­sarvāvaraṇa­praharanād dharmakhaḍgapraharaṇaṃ. showering with the rain of the Dharma,According to Ratnākara (F.87.a), “showering with the rain of Dharma” means showering with the activity of that very Dharma for more beings. RK: tasyaiva bhūyasi jane pravṛttiḥ dharmavṛṣṭiḥ| tasyāḥ pravarṣaṇam. According to Hari (331), “showering with the rain of Dharma” means in terms of (beings’) satisfaction. Hari: tarpaṇatvena dharma­vṛṣṭi­pravarṣaṇaṃ. making a sacrificial offering of Dharma,According to Ratnākara (F.87.a), the Dharma itself is the “sacrificial offering,” because it involves unrestrained sacrifice. RK: sa eva yajño nirargaḍatvāt| tasya yajanam. According to Hari (331), “making a sacrificial offering of Dharma” is because (of teaching it) in plain view and discernible (by others). Hari: vispaṣṭatvena vijñeyatvena ca dharmayajñayajanaṃ. nourishing sentient beings with the gift of the Dharma, and fulfilling wishes with the gift of the Dharma.According to Ratnākara (F.87.a), “nourishing” and “fulfilling” sentient beings with the gift of that Dharma means providing them with (Dharma). RK: tasya dharma sya dān ena sarvasattvā nāṃ saṃtarpaṇaṃ saṃpravāraṇaṃ ca pariveṣaṇam. According to Hari (331), it is “nourishing” sentient beings because it is agreeable to the mind and “fulfilling” because being able to hear it brings delight. Hari: manojñatvena sarvasattvasaṃtarpaṇaṃ. śravaṇīyatvena yathā sukhīkaraṇāt sampravāraṇaṃ.

“That bodhisattva great being rejoices in the roots of virtue‍—within those Dharma teachings and among the qualities of a buddha, the qualities of a solitary realizer, or the qualities of a disciple‍—that belong to all those who have become tamed, trained, resolved, determined, and devoted to awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.87.a), “tamed” is because he or she is introduced to the four truths by the applications of mindfulness; “trained” is by the four “correct exertions” and the four supernormal supports; “resolved” is due to absolute certainty through the five faculties and five strengths; and “determined” is due to not retreating. RK: vinītāḥ smṛtyupasthānaiḥ satyeṣv avatāraṇāt| śikṣitāḥ samyak­prahāṇarddhipādaiḥ| adhimuktā indriyabalair ekāntaniścayāt| niyatā avinivṛtteḥ. According to Hari (331), we should read the first three verbs as a group: “tamed” due to the taming in the buddha qualities; “trained” due to training in the solitary realizer qualities; “resolved” due to firm conviction in the disciple qualities. They are “determined” to realize their own awakening based on their determined disposition (gotra), and based on that they are “devoted to” complete awakening. Hari: buddhadharmeṣu vinayanād vinītāḥ. pratyeka­buddha­dharmeṣu śikṣaṇāc chikṣitāḥ. śrāvakadharmeṣv adhimokṣād adhimuktā iti yojyaṃ. svabodhyadhigamaṃ prati­niyatagotra­tvalābhān niyatāḥ. tata eva sambodhiparāyaṇāḥ. Note: For Ratnākara, it refers to the stages of the thirty-seven factors in awakening, whereas for Hari, this sentence is referring to the four types of Buddhist dispositions or spiritual lineages (gotra) echoed in the following sentences. He or she rejoices in the roots of virtue of all those bodhisattva great beings who are foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the blessed buddhas, and in all their roots of virtue associated with the six transcendent states. He or she rejoices in the roots of virtue of all those persons who belong to the path of a solitary realizer and are foreordained to be solitary realizers. He or she rejoices in the thing that is producing the merit derived from generosity, the thing that is producing the merit derived from ethical discipline, and the thing that is producing the merit derived from cultivation for those persons on the disciple path. He or she rejoices in both the roots of virtue free of worldly influences on the path of training and the roots of virtue free of worldly influences on the path of no more training, and rejoices in the roots of virtue planted with respect to the Dharma by ordinary people. He or she rejoices in the thing that is producing the merit derived from generosity, the thing that is producing the merit derived from ethical discipline, and the thing that is producing the merit derived from cultivation by all those blessed buddhas’ four assemblies, namely, the monks, nuns, male lay devotees, and female lay devotees.

“That bodhisattva great being rejoices in the roots of virtue planted by all beings, including gods, nāgas, yakṣas, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and spirits‍—and even animals‍—when the blessed buddhas were teaching the Dharma. He or she rejoices also in all those roots of virtue that sentient beings have planted when the blessed buddhas were passing into nirvāṇa, all those roots of virtue planted when the blessed buddhas had passed into nirvāṇa, and all those roots of virtue depending on the blessed buddhas, depending on the teachings, depending on the saṅgha, and depending on any persons worthy of mental cultivation.According to Ratnākara (F.87.a), “depending on the Buddha and so on” means he or she takes hold of them. The term “mentally cultivating” (manaso bhāvanam) means delighting (rañjanam). “Worthy of mental cultivation” means to be suitable for that, i.e., causing delight (prasādakarāḥ). RK: buddhā dināgamye ti kṣetrīkṛtya| mānaso bhāvanaṃ rañjanam| tasmai hitā manobhāvanīyā ḥ prasādakarā ity arthaḥ. According to Hari (331), persons “worthy of mental cultivation” refers to one, two, or three persons who bring delight to the mind (prasādakāriṇaḥ). Hari: manobhāvanīyān iti ekadvitri pudgalān manaḥprasāda­kāriṇaḥ.

“Without leaving out any of these roots of virtue, that bodhisattva great being essentializes them, combines them into one, considers them,According to Ratnākara (F.87.a), “essentializes” means to makes clear. “Combines” (piṇḍayitvā) and “considers” mean to examine with the mind concentrated. RK: abhisaṃkṣipye ty asya vivaraṇaṃ| piṇḍayitve ti tulayitve ti samāhitena manasā nirūpya. and completely rejoices in them with the foremost joyousness.According to Ratnākara (F.87.a), that one should “rejoice” means one should take joy as one’s mental object, and “joyousness” means the arising of joy. RK: anumodete ti prītyālambanaṃ kuryāt| anumodanaye ti prītyotpādanayā| agraye ti prakṛṣṭayā. According to Hari (331), the bottom line here is that when one feels great joy and preeminent faith in the inclination of the doer (of those deeds described), one would oneself produce preeminence by doing all the same deeds. Hari: kartur āśayātiśaya­śraddhātiharṣaḥ svayaṃ tatsarvasampādanāśayātiśayaṃ kuryād iti yāvat. He or she rejoices in them with a joyousness that is the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled.According to Ratnākara (F.87.a–b), “foremost” means drawn forth. Now why are there the eleven words? Because that joyousness is drawn forth in the eleven types of meditative objects. Why is that? Because that joyousness has as its meditative object those roots of merit that belong to the tathāgatas with their retinues in the middle and the ten surrounding directions. RK: agraye ti prakṛṣṭayā| kathaṃ tarhi ekādaśapadāni ? ekādaśavidhe py ālambane tasyāḥ prakarṣāt| tat kutaḥ ? madhye daśasu ca dikṣu ye tathāgatāḥ teṣāṃ saparicchadena yāni kuśalamūlāni tadālambanatvāt tasyāḥ. After having rejoiced in that way, he or she then pronounces the words, ‘I dedicate to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing. Let it bring forth unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’According to Ratnākara (F.87.b), in the above Subhūti has explained, in detail, how a bodhisattva rejoices and dedicates. But with the words “in that dedication” and so on, he asks Maitreya to describe the way that a bodhisattva should practice without mental perceptions in that dedication. RK: tad evam ukta ityādinā vistareṇa sthavirasubhūtir yathānumodya yathā bodhisattvaḥ pariṇāmayati taduktavān| yathā tu tatrānupalambhayogaḥ kartavyas tadupa­darśanāyārya­maitreyaṃ tatre tyādinā pṛcchati.

“Now, in all this, when a person on the bodhisattva path gives rise to that proper mindset and dedicates it based on such things, meditative objects, and meditative perspectives, would he or she even mentally perceive those things, meditative objects, and meditative perspectives in the way that makes them into signs?”The English translation of this crucial passage follows the Tibetan translation, which in turn follows Hari’s philological explanation that the extant Sanskrit text was meant to read “dedicates” instead of the variant reading “rejoices,” which appears in many Sanskrit manuscripts. Although Ratnākara seems to agree with Hari’s correction without mentioning it, Ratnākara offers a slightly different, more straightforward interpretation of the passage (compared to Hari’s below). According to Ratnākara (F.87.b), “such things” refers here to the tathāgatas in the ten directions; “meditative objects” refers to their roots of virtue; and “meditative perspectives” refers to the masses of ethical discipline, concentration, and so on (all of which were mentioned in the passage above). “That proper mindset” refers to the proper mindset of joyousness (i.e., not the mindset of awakening). The word “even” means notwithstanding the foregoing (i.e., that they rejoice based on the things, etc.). “Would he or she mentally perceive” is the conditional mood (of the verb in the passive construction), and the question aims to ask, “Is it possible that these be perceived?” The phrase “makes them into a sign” means mentally constructs. (Overall, this question is asked because of the implication that) without doing so, rejoicing and dedicating would seem impossible. RK: yair vastubhir iti daśadik tathāgatādibhiḥ| yair ālambanair iti tadīyaiḥ kuśalamūlaiḥ| yair ākārair iti śīlasamādhi­skandhādibhiḥ| tac cittam ity anumodanācittam| api tv iti kin tu| upalabhyerann iti śaktau liṅga (sic!)| upalabdhuṃ śakyata iti praśnaḥ| nimittīkarotī ti vikalpayati| anyathā anumodanāpariṇāmanayor ayogāt. Note: Ratnākara glosses the term ālambaṇaṃ not ārambaṇaṃ but these are, in effect, synonyms. According to Hari (332), after describing the mental activity of dedicating without any mental perception, Subhūti now raises a question about that topic for the sake of removing a possible faulty objection (to be explained below). The phrase “dedicates it based on such things” means he or she offers that mindset with its cause, i.e., belonging to the lineage of, or having the natural disposition (gotra) toward, the state of a tathāgata, and so on. Although in some extant Sanskrit manuscripts the text reads “rejoices” (instead of “dedicates” as just explained), in those manuscripts the meaning “dedicates” is what is to be grasped from it mentioning the mental activity of dedicating‍—and Subhūti explains it this way immediately afterward, when he asks, “What things does he or she dedicate?” In the next phrase, the term “meditative objects” refers to everything included in the masses of ethical discipline and so on. The “meditative perspectives” are simply the ways of mentally grasping those meditative objects. “That proper mindset” refers to the mental construct of a grasper. The Sanskrit interjection api nu (an indeclinable adverb) is present in the sense of the word kim (an interrogative). The meaning of Subhūti’s question is as follows: Since it is impossible to dedicate those things after making them into a sign, he or she imagines those things‍—which simply do not exist because of being in the past‍—and makes them into a perceptual object the way they manifest at the conventional level. But, at the level of ultimate reality, are they being mentally perceived exactly the same way? What is considered in this question is whether something is mentally perceived and whether it exists in that same way, because a mental perception of a thing‍—insofar as it contradicts valid cognition‍—is thoroughly ascertained to be an erroneous perception, like the knowing of a mirage and so on. Hence, a dedication procedure considered to be performed on the strength of that (error) would be weak, just like applying oneself based on the erroneous belief in “me” or “mine.” Hari: anupalambha­pariṇāmanā­manaskāraṃ nirdiśyedānīṃ tatra kucodyaparihārārthaṃ praśnayann āha : tatra bodhisattvayānika ityādi. yair vastubhiḥ pariṇāmayatīti yais tathāgata­tvādigotrahetubhir niryātayatīty arthaḥ. kvacid anumodete ti pāṭhaḥ. tatrāpi pariṇāmanā­manaskārasya prakrāntatvāt pariṇāmayatīty arthe grāhyaḥ. tathā cānantaram eva vakṣyati : katamair vastubhiḥ pariṇāmayatīti. yair ālambanair yair ākārair iti ālambanāni śīlādiskandhāḥ sarvadharmāḥ. tadgrahaṇaprakārā evākārāḥ. taccittam iti grāhakavikalpaṃ. api nv iti apinuśabdo nipātaḥ kiṃ śabdārthe vartate. tathopalabhyeran yathā nimittīkarotī ti vastvādīny animittīkṛtya pariṇāmayitum aśakyatvāt tāny atītatayāvidyamānāny evādhyāropya yathodbhāvanāsaṃvṛtyā viṣayīkriyante. kiṃ tattvatas tathaiva samupalabhyanta ity arthaḥ. yady upalabhyanta iti mataṃ, tathā ca sati, pramāṇavyāhatatvena viparyāso hi vastunaḥ (sic!) upalambho marīcyādijñānavat pariniścitaḥ || tasmāt tadbalato vṛttaḥ pariṇāmavidhir mataḥ ātmātmīya­viparyāsa­pravṛtta iva durbalaḥ iti matiḥ.

The bodhisattva great being Maitreya replied to the elder, the venerable Subhūti, “Venerable Subhūti, he or she would not mentally perceive those things, meditative objects, and meditative perspectives in the way that makes them into signs.”The distinction here between “things” (vastu), “meditative objects” (ālambana), and “meditative perspectives” (ākāra) might be translated (according to some philosophical schools in other contexts) as “real thing” (vastu), “perceptual content” (ālambana), and “perceptual form” (ākāra).

In response, the venerable Subhūti said to the elder, the bodhisattva great being Maitreya, “If a bodhisattva great being were to turn a nonexistent thing into a meditative object and turn a nonexistent meditative object into a sign, then how could this not involve an error of conception, an error of mindset, or an error of view? As for how this would be so, it is just as, for example, desire mentally constructs and imagines a nonexistent thing and thereby gives rise to an error of conception, an error of mindset, and an error of view that sees permanence in the impermanent, happiness in the painful, self in what lacks a self, and purity in the impure. On the other hand, if the proper mindset and its awakening were just like such a thing, meditative object, and meditative perspective, then everything‍—all the elements‍—would be exactly the same.According to Ratnākara (F.87.b), the first sentence asserts that it would be an error, namely, an error of conception, mindset, and view, due, respectively, to grasping something that does not exist as a sign, grasping that (sign), and considering it (to be real). The “desire” is an illustrating example aimed at proving (the assertion) of those (errors). “On the other hand” refers to what other systems might object to‍—their basic objection (to the implications of emptiness) is that a sacrificial offering would be like the yakṣa (to whom it is offered). In this sentence, the “proper mindset” refers to a dedicating mindset. “Everything” is a summary; “all the elements” explains that more precisely. RK: evan tarhi viparyāsāḥ syur iti| asata eva nimittodgrahāt grahaṇāt santīraṇāc ca yathokramaṃ saṃjñāyāś cittasya dṛṣṭeś ca viparyāsaḥ| eṣāṃ sādhanārtha rāgo dṛṣṭāntaḥ athāpī ti paramatam āśaṅkate| yādṛśo yakṣas tādṛśo balir iti bhāvaḥ| cittam iti pariṇāmanācittam| sarvadharmā ity uddeśaḥ| sarvadhātava iti nirdeśaḥ. Furthermore, if the proper mindset and its awakening were all just like such a thing, meditative object, and meditative perspective, then what mindset would he or she dedicate to unsurpassed, perfect awakening based on what thing, what meditative object, and what meditative perspective? In addition, what thing that produces the merit associated with rejoicing would a bodhisattva great being dedicate to what unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”According to Hari (335), someone might respond to the previous sentence, “Assuming it is the same as you say, where is the fault in it?” To address that, Subhūti continues with this next sentence. Its thinking is as follows: Just as the things and so on are not existing, the mindset of awakening would also not be existing. In this case, what mindset is a bodhisattva dedicating to awakening by means what things, etc.? Subhūti’s rhetorical question implies that a bodhisattva is not dedicating anything to anything else by any means. Hence, the error of dedicating (in the manner of the previous sentence) lies in the impossibility of dedicating anything at all. Hari: bhavatv evaṃ ko doṣa iti cet. āha : yadi ce tyādi. evaṃ manyate: yadi yathā vastvādikam asaṃvidyamānaṃ tathā bodhicittādikaṃ tadā katamair vastvādibhiḥ katamac cittādikaṃ kva bodhau pariṇāmayati. yāvatā naiva kenacit kiṃcit kvacit pariṇāmayati. ataḥ sarvathā pariṇāmānupapattau pariṇāmaviparyāsa iti. According to Ratnākara (F.87.b–88.a), with this sentence Subhūti explains the fault. “What things” and so on should be connected with the mindset of rejoicing. “What proper mindset” refers to the mindset of dedicating, because that dedication also has no inherent nature. The basic idea is that no dedication at all would exist. RK: yadi ce tyādinā doṣam āha| katamair vastv ādibhir iti anumodanācitta­sambandhibhiḥ| cittam iti pariṇāmanācittam| tasyāpi niḥsvabhāvatvāt| kve ti kasyām| pariṇāmanaiva na syād iti bhāvaḥ.

The bodhisattva great being Maitreya then said to the elder, the venerable Subhūti, “Noble Subhūti, this point is not to be described or taught in front of a bodhisattva great being who has just begun to set out on the journey.According to Hari (335), even though such a dedication might be erroneous in the ultimate level of reality, the mental activity of dedicating things and so on that exist, at the conventional level, is certainly asserted to gradually produce genuine realization. Hence, Maitreya responds to him with two alternative subtexts in mind, namely, (a) “Nothing more is to be said by you,” or (b) “Given that one would not be fixating upon those things and so on as ultimately real, such a dedication would not be wrong, because it would involve only an illusory person dedicating at the conventional level.” Hari: tattvena viparyasto ’py anityādivikalpavat pāramparyeṇa bhūtārthaprāpakaḥ saṃvṛtyā vastvāder vidyamānatvena pariṇāmanāmanaskāra iṣyata eva. ato na kiṃcid adhikaṃ tvayoktam ity abhiprāyeṇāha : nedaṃ ārya subhūta ityādi atha vā tasmin vastvālambanādau pāramārthikābhi­niveśavi­gamena saṃvṛtyā māyāpuruṣasyeva pariṇāmanān na viparyastapariṇāma ity abhiprāyavān āha: nedam ārya subhūta ityādi. Note: The single word “this” (idam) has been translated “this point” for clarity. Why is that? Because any trace of faith, any trace of appreciation, any trace of trust, and any trace of devotion that he or she might have would entirely disappear.According to Ratnākara (F.88.a), here “faith” and so on is in rejoicing, dedicating, and so on. Using the word “trace” here implies an interruption to their performance of these practices (in the future). RK: yad api hi syād bhavet| kva? anumodanāpariṇāmanādau| mātra grahaṇam anuṣṭhānavyavacchedārtham. However, noble Subhūti, this point can be described or taught in front of a bodhisattva great being who is beyond regressing or a bodhisattva great being who is supported by a virtuous friend, since, regarding such a point, he or she would not become deflated, depressed, dejected, or sunk in despondency, and his or her mental attitude would not become discouraged, shattered, frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror.According to Ratnākara (F.88.a), “supported” here means strengthened. RK: upastabdhaḥ sthirīkṛtaḥ. It is in this way that a bodhisattva great being should dedicate to the state of complete knowing the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing.”

The elder, the venerable Subhūti, then said to the bodhisattva great being Maitreya, “Maitreya, after rejoicing with that mindset, the rejoicing mindset‍—which the bodhisattva great being is to dedicate‍—will have dissipated, ceased, dissolved, and become totally transformed.For readability, this translation veers slightly from the Sanskrit and Tibetan co-relative sentence structure and interpolates the word “rejoicing” based on Ratnākara’s gloss (F.88.a): yad ity anumodakaṃ cittam. Hari’s gloss (336) equates the rejoicing mindset with the root of virtue associated with rejoicing (i.e., that which is producing merit that is being dedicated by a bodhisattva). Hari: yad ity anumodanāsaha­gataṃ kuśalamūlaṃ. tac cittam ity anumodakaṃ. So what mindset is the bodhisattva great being dedicating to unsurpassed, perfect awakening by what means? That is to say, what mindset‍—which is the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing‍—is the bodhisattva great being dedicating to unsurpassed, perfect awakening? Given that the two mindsets are never concurrent and that the inherent nature of a mindset cannot be dedicated, how could one mindset even dedicate another mindset?”Ratnākara’s (F.88.a) explanation of the passage is too abbreviated and Hari’s explanation (336) too detailed and lengthy to warrant including either here. To explain the essence of the issue here, although disciple schools teach that all things including mindsets are momentary‍—meaning that they are real but arise, dwell, cease, and dissolve within a single instant‍—this scripture is understood to refute this notion of impermanence by demonstrating that the disciple notion of causality, despite its relative value, is untenable at the ultimate level, and hence must be qualified and contextualized. In this particular passage on the practice of dedicating the merit of rejoicing to complete awakening, the objection is implied and refuted in order to highlight the ultimate understanding that is meant to accompany the relative description of the practice as described by the scripture itself above. In particular, the refutation here and below draws into question how the first rejoicing mindset can be dedicated by the subsequent dedicating mindset, given that the former must dissolve before the latter can appear, and hence they cannot have any causal relationship. After the refutation at the ultimate level, the correct way to dedicate one’s rejoicing to awakening will be taught.

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the elder, the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, let those bodhisattva great beings who have just begun to set out on the path not become frightened, not be terrified, and not be stricken with terror after hearing this explication. Pray tell instead, Subhūti, how should a bodhisattva great being dedicate to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing? How should the mindset associated with that rejoicing be properly supported by supporting the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing, and then properly dedicated by dedicating the mindset associated with rejoicing?”

Then, out of respect for the bodhisattva great being Maitreya, the elder, the venerable Subhūti, deferred to his authority on this point and spoke instead about the bodhisattva great being Maitreya, saying, “The bodhisattva great being Maitreya rejoices in all those past blessed buddhas in the immeasurable and innumerable billionfold, great order world systems in the ten directions, and in all those immeasurable and innumerable blessed buddhas in each direction of each billionfold, great order world system, as blessed buddhas who passed into nirvāṇa. He rejoices that they eradicated their worldly ruts; they cut off their circuitous pathways; they cut the leashes of dualistic proliferations and cyclic existence; they exhausted their tears; they crushed their thorns; they completely laid down their burdens; they reached their own goals; they cut their worldly ties; they liberated their mindsets by accurate understanding; and they achieved complete control over all mindsets.

“The bodhisattva great being Maitreya rejoices in everything from when those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas first gave rise to the proper mindset, up to the point where they realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening and then passed into the nirvāṇa dimension without any constituents remaining, and without their genuine teachings disappearing, as follows.

“The bodhisattva great being Maitreya rejoices in the roots of virtue connected with the transcendent states of the blessed buddhas, the accumulation of merit, and the accumulation of the roots of virtue.

“The bodhisattva great being Maitreya rejoices in those blessed buddhas’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation. He rejoices in their care for others’ welfare, great benevolence, great compassion, immeasurable and innumerable buddha qualities, and the Dharma taught by the blessed buddhas.

“The bodhisattva great being Maitreya rejoices in the roots of virtue of all those who are trained in those teachings, who have become resolved, and who have set out. He rejoices in the roots of virtue of all those bodhisattva great beings who are foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the blessed buddhas, and in all their roots of virtue associated with the six transcendent states. He rejoices in the roots of virtue of all those persons who belong to the path of a solitary realizer and are foreordained to be solitary realizers.

“The bodhisattva great being Maitreya rejoices in the roots of virtue derived from generosity, the roots of virtue derived from ethical discipline, and the roots of virtue derived from cultivation that belong to those persons on the disciple path. He rejoices in both the roots of virtue free of worldly influences on the path of training and the roots of virtue free of worldly influences on the path of no more training, and he rejoices in the roots of virtue planted with respect to the Dharma by ordinary people.

“The bodhisattva great being Maitreya rejoices in the gods, nāgas, yakṣas, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and spirits who have heard the Dharma, and after hearing it, planted roots of virtue. He rejoices even in those beings born as animals who heard the Dharma, and after hearing it, planted roots of virtue. He rejoices also in all those roots of virtue that sentient beings planted when the blessed buddhas were passing into nirvāṇa and had passed into nirvāṇa.

“Without leaving out any of those roots of virtue, the bodhisattva great being Maitreya essentializes them, combines them into one, considers them, and completely rejoices in them. After having rejoiced, he dedicates to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing. Maitreya, how can a bodhisattva great being, who is dedicating, have no error of conception, no error of mindset, and no error of view?”

The bodhisattva great being Maitreya replied to the elder, the venerable Subhūti, “Noble Subhūti, if a bodhisattva great being has no conception of a ‘proper mindset’‍—concerning either the proper mindset that is being dedicated or the proper mindset with which he or she is dedicating it‍—then the thing that is producing merit associated with rejoicing is dedicated to unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the bodhisattva great being. Since a bodhisattva great being does not conceive of that proper mindset, thinking, ‘This is the proper mindset,’ he or she has no error of conception, no error of mindset, and no error of view. On the other hand, suppose that a bodhisattva great being is dedicating the one proper mindset with the other proper mindset and has the conception of a ‘proper mindset’‍—that is to say, he or she does conceive of that proper mindset, thinking, ‘This is the proper mindset.’ Then, in this way, a bodhisattva great being would have an error of conception, an error of mindset, and an error of view.

“Also, suppose that a bodhisattva great being dedicates that proper mindset that he or she conceives of or considers in the following way. That is, when the proper mindset is simply being considered, he or she conceives of it as ‘dissipated, dissipated’‍—as ‘ceased,’ ‘dissolved,’ and ‘totally transformed’‍—while being aware that anything dissipated cannot be dedicated.According to Ratnākara (F.88.a), that he or she “conceives of” communicates the basic idea. That he or she “considers” is the detailed explanation‍—the meaning is that he or she pays attention in a nonerroneous manner. RK: evaṃ sañjānīta ity uddeśaḥ| evaṃ samanvāharatī ti nirdeśaḥ| aviparyāsena manasikarotīty arthaḥ| tad ityādi| tat cittam evaṃ samanvāhriyamāṇam iti sambandhaḥ. According to Hari (342–43), the “proper mindset” here is the mindset being dedicated (e.g., the rejoicing mindset). Due to the words “dissipated” and so on, he or she “conceives of” refers to when the bodhisattva arrives at the end of the preliminary stage of practice. He or she “considers” refers to when the bodhisattva progresses to the main stage of practice. Hari: yac cittam iti pariṇāmyamānaṃ. evaṃ saṃjānīta iti vakṣyamāṇakṣīṇatvādinā prayogapṛṣṭhāvasthāyāṃ pratipadyate. evaṃ samanvāharatī ti maulāvasthāyāṃ. Note: Repeating words twice‍—e.g., “dissipated, dissipated”‍—is a traditional feature of many meditation instructions aimed at insight (vipaśyanā), during which one is actually meant to identify each experience with a label repeated mentally twice. The three alternative labels are different angles on the same dissolution. Likewise, the proper mindset that is dedicating it also has the very same true nature. Furthermore, those things with which it is dedicating also have the very same true nature. Furthermore, those qualities to which it is dedicating also have the very same true nature.According to Ratnākara (F.88.a), he pays attention by means of the virtual synonyms “dissipated” and so on. It “cannot be dedicated” because, like the horns of a hare, it does not exist. “The very same true nature” refers to its very lack of inherent nature. “Those qualities” refers to the qualities of a buddha. RK: kṣīṇam ityādibhiḥ paryāyair manasikaroti| na tac chakyam iti| asattvāc chaśaviṣāṇavat| saiva dharmate ti niḥsvabhāvataiva| dharmeṣv iti buddhadharmeṣu. According to Hari (342–43), “the very same true nature of that mindset” means it alone is the true nature of dissipating and so on because that dedicating mindset also has no inherent nature (i.e., not just the mindset being dedicated but also the mindset dedicating). The phrase “those things” refers to the things, meditative objects, and meditative perspectives (mentioned above). The phrase “those qualities” refers to the complete knowing and so on (i.e., the awakening toward which it is dedicated). Hari: tasyāpi cittasya saiva dharmate ti pariṇāmakacittasya svabhāvavirahāt saiva kṣīṇatvādidharmatā. yair api dharmair iti vastvādibhiḥ. yeṣv api dharmeṣv iti sarvajñatādiṣu. Supposing that a bodhisattva great being dedicates like this, then he or she is dedicating correctly, not incorrectly. Hence, this is the very way that a bodhisattva great being should dedicate.The word “supposing” marks the beginning of a brief recap. The words “like this” mean free of mental perception, which means by paying attention in all cases with the single flavor of emptiness. RK: saced ityādinopasaṃhāraḥ| evam ity anupalambhena| sarvatra śūnyataikarasena manasikāreṇety arthaḥ.

“Beyond that, suppose that a bodhisattva great being rejoices in the immeasurable and innumerable future blessed buddhas in the same way as in those past blessed buddhas, who eradicated their worldly ruts, cut off their circuitous pathways, and cut the leashes of dualistic proliferations and cyclic existence.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in everything from when those blessed buddhas first will give rise to the proper mindset up to the point where they will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening and then pass into the nirvāṇa dimension without any constituents remaining and without their genuine teachings disappearing, as follows.
“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in those blessed buddhas’ roots of virtue associated with the six transcendent states.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in those blessed buddhas’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in their care for others’ welfare, great benevolence, great compassion, immeasurable and innumerable buddha qualities, and the Dharma that will be taught by those blessed buddhas.
“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in the roots of virtue of all those who will be resolved and will set out.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in the roots of virtue associated with the six transcendent states of all those bodhisattva great beings who will be foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the blessed buddhas, and the roots of virtue of all those persons who belong to the path of a solitary realizer and will be foreordained to be solitary realizers. He or she rejoices in the roots of virtue to be derived from generosity, the roots of virtue to be derived from ethical discipline, and the roots of virtue to be derived from cultivation by those persons on the disciple path. He or she rejoices in both the roots of virtue free of worldly influences on the path of training and the roots of virtue free of worldly influences on the path of no more training. And he or she rejoices in the roots of virtue to be planted with respect to the Dharma by ordinary people.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in the gods, nāgas, yakṣas, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and spirits who will plant roots of virtue. He or she rejoices even in those beings born as animals who will hear the Dharma, and after hearing it, will plant roots of virtue. He or she also rejoices in all those roots of virtue that sentient beings will plant when the blessed buddhas are passing into nirvāṇa and have passed into nirvāṇa.

“Without leaving out any of these roots of virtue, suppose that he or she essentializes them, combines them into one, considers them, and completely rejoices in them. Suppose that after rejoicing, he or she dedicates to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing. So, how is it that a bodhisattva great being, while dedicating, does not have any error of conception, any error of mindset, or any error of view?

“If a bodhisattva great being has no conception of a ‘proper mindset’‍—with respect to either the proper mindset dedicated or the proper mindset with which he or she is dedicating it‍—then the thing that is producing merit associated with rejoicing is dedicated to unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the bodhisattva great being. Since a bodhisattva great being does not conceive of that proper mindset, thinking, ‘This is the proper mindset,’ he or she has no error of conception, no error of mindset, and no error of view. On the other hand, suppose that he or she dedicates the one proper mindset with the other proper mindset and has the conception of a ‘proper mindset’‍—that is to say, he or she conceives of that proper mindset, thinking, ‘This is the proper mindset.’ Then, in this way, a bodhisattva great being would have an error of conception, an error of mindset, and an error of view.

“That said, suppose that a bodhisattva great being dedicates that proper mindset that he or she conceives of or considers in the following way. That is, when the proper mindset is simply being considered, he or she conceives of it as ‘dissipated, dissipated’‍—as ‘ceased,’ ‘dissolved,’ and ‘totally transformed’‍—while being aware that anything dissipated cannot be dedicated. Likewise, that proper mindset that is dedicating also has the very same true nature. Also, those things with which it is dedicating also have the very same true nature. Furthermore, those qualities to which it is dedicating also have the very same true nature. Supposing that a bodhisattva great being dedicates like this, then he or she is dedicating correctly, not incorrectly. Hence, this is the very way that a bodhisattva great being should dedicate.

“Beyond that, suppose that a bodhisattva great being rejoices in the immeasurable and innumerable present blessed buddhas in the same way as the future blessed buddhas, who have eradicated their worldly ruts, cut off their circuitous pathways, cut the leashes of dualistic proliferations and cyclic existence, and are now residing, existing, and abiding in immeasurable and innumerable billionfold, great order world systems.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in everything from when those blessed buddhas first gave rise to the proper mindset up to the point where they realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening and then passed into the nirvāṇa dimension without any constituents remaining, and without their genuine teachings disappearing, as follows.
“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in those blessed buddhas’ roots of virtue associated with the six transcendent states.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in those blessed buddhas’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in their care for others’ welfare, great benevolence, great compassion, immeasurable and innumerable buddha qualities, and the Dharma taught by the blessed buddhas.
“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in the roots of virtue of all those who are resolved and are setting out.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in the roots of virtue associated with the six transcendent states of all those bodhisattva great beings who are being foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the blessed buddhas, and the roots of virtue of all those persons who belong to the path of a solitary realizer who are being foreordained to be solitary realizers. He or she rejoices in the roots of virtue derived from generosity, the roots of virtue derived from ethical discipline, and the roots of virtue derived from cultivation by those persons on the disciple path. He or she rejoices in both the roots of virtue free of worldly influences on the path of training and the roots of virtue free of worldly influences on the path of no more training. And he or she rejoices in the roots of virtue being planted with respect to the Dharma by ordinary people.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in the gods, nāgas, yakṣas, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and spirits who are hearing the Dharma and, after hearing it, are planting roots of virtue. He or she rejoices even in those beings born as animals who are hearing the Dharma, and after hearing it, are planting roots of virtue. He or she also rejoices in all those roots of virtue that sentient beings are planting when the blessed buddhas are passing into nirvāṇa and have passed into nirvāṇa.

“Without leaving out any of these roots of virtue, suppose that he or she essentializes them, combines them into one, considers them, and completely rejoices in them. Suppose that after rejoicing, he or she dedicates to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing. So, how is it that a bodhisattva great being, while dedicating, does not have any error of conception, any error of mindset, or any error of view?

“If a bodhisattva great being has no conception of a ‘proper mindset’ regarding the one proper mindset he or she is dedicating with the other proper mindset, then the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing is dedicated to unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the bodhisattva great being. Since a bodhisattva great being does not conceive of that proper mindset, thinking, ‘This is the proper mindset,’ he or she has no error of conception, no error of mindset, and no error of view. On the other hand, suppose that he or she dedicates the one with the other proper mindset with the conception of a ‘proper mindset’‍—that is to say, he or she were to conceive of that proper mindset, thinking, ‘This is the proper mindset.’ Then, in this way, a bodhisattva great being would have an error of conception, an error of mindset, and an error of view.

“That said, suppose that a bodhisattva great being dedicates that proper mindset that he or she conceives of or considers in the following way. That is, when the proper mindset is simply being considered, he or she conceives of it as ‘dissipated, dissipated’‍—as ‘ceased,’ ‘dissolved,’ and ‘totally transformed’‍—while being aware that anything dissipated cannot be dedicated. Likewise, that proper mindset that is dedicating also has the very same true nature. Also, those things with which it is dedicating also have the very same true nature. Furthermore, those qualities to which it is dedicating also have the very same true nature. Supposing that a bodhisattva great being dedicates like this, then he or she is dedicating correctly, not incorrectly. Hence, this is the very way that a bodhisattva great being should dedicate.

“Beyond that, suppose that a bodhisattva great being rejoices in the immeasurable and innumerable past, future, and present blessed buddhas, who have eradicated or will eradicate their worldly ruts, cut off their circuitous pathways, and cut the leashes of dualistic proliferations and cyclic existence.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in everything from when those blessed buddhas first gave rise to the proper mindset up to the point where they have realized, are realizing, or will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, up to the point where they have passed, are passing, or will pass into the nirvāṇa dimension without any constituents remaining, and without their genuine teachings having disappeared, disappearing, or coming to disappear as follows.
“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in those blessed buddhas’ roots of virtue associated with the six transcendent states.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in those blessed buddhas’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in their care for others’ welfare, great benevolence, great compassion, immeasurable and innumerable buddha qualities, and the Dharma that has been taught, is being taught, and will be taught by those blessed buddhas.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in the roots of virtue of all those who have become resolved, are becoming resolved, and will become resolved, and who have set out, are setting out, and will set out.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in the roots of virtue associated with the six transcendent states of those bodhisattva great beings who were foreordained, will be foreordained, and are being foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the blessed buddhas, and the roots of virtue of those persons on the path of a solitary realizer who were foreordained, will be foreordained, and are being foreordained to be solitary realizers. He or she rejoices in the roots of virtue derived from generosity, the roots of virtue derived from ethical discipline, and the roots of virtue derived from cultivation for those persons on the disciple path. He or she rejoices in both the roots of virtue free of worldly influences on the path of training and the roots of virtue free of worldly influences on the path of no more training. And he or she rejoices in the roots of virtue that were planted, are being planted, and will be planted with respect to the Dharma by ordinary people.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being rejoices in the gods, nāgas, yakṣas, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and spirits who have heard, are hearing, and will hear the Dharma, and after hearing it, have planted, are planting, and will plant those roots of virtue. He or she rejoices even in those beings born as animals who have heard, are hearing, and will hear the Dharma, and after hearing it, have planted, are planting, and will plant roots of virtue.

“Suppose that the bodhisattva great being also rejoices in all those roots of virtue that sentient beings have planted, are planting, and will plant when the blessed buddhas have passed into nirvāṇa, will pass into nirvāṇa, and are passing into nirvāṇa.

“Without leaving out any of these roots of virtue, suppose that the bodhisattva great being essentializes them, combines them into one, considers them, and completely rejoices in them with the foremost joyousness. He or she rejoices in them with a joyousness that is the best‍—the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled. After having rejoiced in this way, he or she then recites the words, ‘I dedicate to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing. Let it bring forth unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’ So, how is it that such a bodhisattva great being, while making this dedication, does not have any error of conception, any error of mindset, or any error of view?
