“If the bodhisattva who is making a dedication considers those things that have dissipated, ceased, dissolved, and are totally transformed to be dedicated to a thing that is undissipating, then they are properly dedicated to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.89.a), that “thing that is undissipating” is unsurpassed, perfect awakening, which is the completely pure true nature of all things. They “properly dedicated” due to lacking any error‍—this is the basic idea. RK: sa ca dharmo ’kṣaya iti anuttarā samyaksambodhiḥ suviśuddha­dharma­tālakṣaṇā| evaṃ pariṇāmitaṃ bhavatī ty aviparyāsād iti bhāvaḥ. Furthermore, if the bodhisattva discerns that there is not one thing dedicating another thing, then this too is properly dedicated to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. In this way, Venerable Subhūti, while dedicating, a bodhisattva great being has no error of conception, no error of mindset, and no error of view. Why not? Because he or she does not fixate upon that dedication. Furthermore, if he or she likewise conceives of no mindset to be perceiving a mindset, and no thing to be perceiving a thing, then this too is properly dedicated to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. This is a bodhisattva great being’s unsurpassed way of dedicating.

“That said, if a bodhisattva great being were to conceive of an accumulation of merit, then he or she would not be dedicating to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.89.a), “accumulation of merit” refers to a meritorious action. Not conceiving of it means not being fixated upon it. RK: puṇyābhisaṃskāraḥ puṇyakarma| saṃjñānam abhiniveśaḥ. Why not? Because he or she would be fixated on that dedication. On the other hand, if it occurs to him or her that both the accumulation of merit and the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing are null and void, then he or she is dedicating to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.89.a), “void” and “null” are because of being, respectively, empty of self and other and empty of the characteristics associated with self and other. RK: viviktaḥ śāntaś ca yathākramaṃ svaparaiḥ svapara­lakṣaṇaiś ca śūnyatvāt. Furthermore, if he or she does not conceive of any conditioning factors as null and void, then that bodhisattva great being possesses the transcendent state of wisdom.There is a slight play on the multivalent Sanskrit word saṃskārāḥ (“conditioned things,” “conditioning factors”), which is meant to play off and build upon the meaning of its etymologically related multivalent term abhisaṃskārāḥ (translated here as “accumulation” and elsewhere as “cumulative construction”), which is mentioned in the previous sentence.

“That said, suppose that a bodhisattva great being were to conceive of the root of virtue of the blessed buddhas who have passed into nirvāṇa as a root of virtue that is exactly the same as that dedication.According to Ratnākara (F.89.a), in this sentence one is recalling the “root of virtue” of the buddhas and seeing it as “exactly the same” (as the dedication mentioned just above). “That dedication”‍—which here refers to something whose nature is the source of all qualities (dharmadhātu)‍—is being (incorrectly) seen as “exactly the same as” unsurpassed, perfect awakening, whose nature is the source of all qualities (dharmadhātu) that is obtained, due (supposedly) to the former’s permanent connection with the latter. RK: yad apī tyādinā smaraṇam| yādṛśa eve tyādinā nirūpaṇam| yādṛśa eva sa pariṇāma ity anuttarā samyaksaṃbodhir dharmadhatusvabhāvā tādṛśam eva tad iti labhyate nityābhisambandhāt| dharmadhātusvabhāvam eva tad ity arthaḥ. Suppose also that he or she were to conceive of both the root of virtue and that by which it is dedicated as being of the same type, characteristic, class, and nature.According to Ratnākara (F.89.a), “both” refers to “the root of virtue,” i.e., that which is associated with rejoicing, and “that by which it is dedicated,” i.e., the dedicating mindset. RK: tādṛśam eva tat kuśalamūlam iti yad anumodanāsaha­gatam| yenāpī ti pariṇāmanācittena| tad apī ti tad apy ubhayam. Then, with such conceptions, he or she would not be dedicating any root of virtue to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.89.a), “with such conceptions” means since the bodhisattva is fixating on these conceptions as having a real sameness and so on, he or she is not dedicating correctly. RK: sa ced evaṃ saṃjānīta iti| yady anenāpi tādṛśatvādinā tattvenābhiniviśate tadā na samyakpariṇāmayati. Why not? Because the blessed buddhas do not recommend dedicating any root of virtue by way of signs.A “sign” (nimitta) generally refers to something conceptual that represents something else. Some disciple meditations involve developing a mental image that serves as a sign of something else. That is to say, the past has dissipated, ceased, dissolved, and totally transformed. The future is not yet obtained. Anything located in the present is not mentally perceived. Hence, these three, which are not perceived, can be neither sign nor perceptual object.According to Ratnākara (F.89.b), beginning with the words (translated here as) “that is to say,” he explains another conceptualization of reality. The words “not yet obtained” mean not arisen. “The present” does not exist, because it is transitory by its very nature. Therefore “these three are not perceived” means they do not exist. There is “neither any sign” due to the nonfunctioning of those, “nor a perceptual object” that is functioning (to produce a perception). RK: yaccātītam ityādinā aparaṃ tattvavikalpam āha| asaṃprāptam anutpannam| sthitir nāstī ti prakṛtyaiva naśvaratvāt| tasmāt trayam api nopalabhyate, tato ’sat| tato naiva nimittam akāraṇatvāt| nākāraṇaṃ viṣayaḥ. If a bodhisattva great being were to make a root of virtue into a sign in this way, he or she would neither be considering, nor dedicating it to, unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.89.b), “in this way” means as a reality that has the quality of being “dissipated” and so on. “Makes into a sign” means perceives (that way). When doing so, he or she is “neither considering,” i.e., not correctly paying attention to, “nor dedicating,” i.e., not correctly dedicating. RK: e vam iti kṣīṇatvādinā tattvena| nimittīkarotī ty upalabhate| tadā na samanvāharati na samyagmanasikaroti| tadā na samyak pariṇāmayati.

“That is not to say that someone lacking mindfulness‍—in other words, someone who, out of a lack of either mindfulness or recognition, simply fails to make a root of virtue into a sign, consider it, or pay attention to it‍—would somehow be dedicating it to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.89.b), Maitreya says this sentence in case someone were to (mistakenly) think that the correct dedication is when a bodhisattva does not make a root of virtue into a sign because of not recalling or being uninformed. Thus, the idea here is that not making it into a sign is also “not dedicating” (correctly), in essence, because even a mere dedication is lacking here. RK: yadā tarhy anavabodhād asmaraṇād vā na nimittīkaroti tadā samyakpariṇāmaḥ syād iti ced āha| athe tyādi| evam apī ti| evam animittīkaraṇe pi na pariṇāmayati| pariṇāmanāmātrasyāpy abhāvād iti bhāvaḥ. Rather, when a bodhisattva great being considers such a sign without making it into a sign, then in this way he or she dedicates that root of virtue to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.89.b), with this sentence he explains when it is correctly dedicated. The words “such a sign” refer to the content of accurate knowing whose absence of a sign and absence of perceptual object has the quality of exact sameness and so on. He or she “considers it”‍—i.e., perfectly keeps it in mind‍—“without making it into a sign,” i.e., without mentally conceiving of it and fixating on it. RK: kadā tarhi samyakpariṇāmitaṃ bhavaty ata āha| athe tyādi| atha śabdo yadyarthaḥ| tad iti tādṛśatvādikam animittaṃ saviṣayatvañ ca| nimittam iti samyagjñānālambanam| samanvāharatī ti samyagmanasi­karoti| na ca nimittīkarotī ti na sañjānāti nābhiniviśate. Thus, it is in this that a bodhisattva great being should train, and it is such training that should be recognized as his or her skill in means.According to Ratnākara (F.90.a), “this” refers both to paying attention to reality and to not making it into a sign. The word (translated here as) “something” is in the sense of what is known in the world. The bodhisattva’s “skill in means” is something known in the world. RK: idaṃ tad ityādi| idam iti yad etad ubhayaṃ tattvasya manasikāro na ca nimittīkaraṇam| tac chabdaḥ prasiddhau| upāyakauśalaṃ yat prasiddham idaṃ tat. That very skill in means through which he or she dedicates the root of virtue is close to the state of complete knowing. And any bodhisattva great being who wishes to train in this skill in means should repeatedly listen to this very transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and be questioned about it. Why is that? It is because, without attaining the transcendent state of wisdom, it is impossible for anyone who has not listened to this very teaching to become well steeped in the activity of dedicating the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.90.a), Maitreya now begins to explain what the “means” is within “this skill in means.” Here, “one who has not listened to,” or learned, this very “transcendent state of wisdom,” i.e., the scripture itself, cannot “become well steeped in it,” i.e., cannot be immersed in it. To make sense, this idea needs to be connected back to the transcendent state of wisdom’s dedication that is actually producing the merit. RK: athāsmin upāyakauśale ka upāya ity ata āha| atra ce tyādi| iyam eve ti| sūtrātmikaiva| aśrutavate ti imām asyāṃ veti śeṣaḥ| prajñāpāramitāyā yā pariṇāmanā saiva puṇyakriyā, seyaṃ praveṣṭum avagāhituṃ na hi śakye ti sambandhaḥ| tatre ti tathā sati. That said, someone might claim that it is somehow possible for a bodhisattva great being not to rely on the transcendent state of wisdom and yet to dedicate the thing that is producing the merit to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. But anyone who would say such a thing should be told not to speak that way.

“Why is that? Because, suppose that even though those physical bodies and those conditioning factors are ceased, null, void, and free of any mental perception, a person were nevertheless to make reality as-it-is into a sign, and conceptualize it. Next, suppose that, by conceiving of a reality as-it-is in something that is not the reality as-it-is, he or she were to dedicate a mental perception to the freedom from mental perceptions.According to Ratnākara (F.90.a), if those too are not mentally perceived, then “reality as-it-is” means the unmistaken quality that it has ceased and so on. “Make into a sign” means through intellectually grasping it and “conceptualize” means through habitual fixation. One’s own “mental perception” is conceiving of “reality as-it-is,” i.e., ceased and so on, “something not the reality as-it-is,” i.e., in an appearance of a conceptualization. He (mistakenly) dedicates this “to the freedom from mental perceptions,” i.e., complete awakening. RK: api tv iti| yady api nopalabhyante tathāpi yathābhūtam aviparītaṃ niruddhatvādikam| nimittīkṛtye ty udgrahataḥ| vikalpya ce ty abhiniveśataḥ| ayathābhūte vikalpapratibhāse yathābhūta niruddhādisaṃjñī upalambhaṃ ātmīyaṃ anupalambhe samyaksaṃbodhau pariṇāmayet. If such a person were to do this, then he or she would not be dedicating the root of virtue to unsurpassed, perfect awakening in the way recommended by the blessed buddhas. Why not? Because even the blessed buddhas’ nirvāṇa‍—which is made into a sign, conceptualized, and mentally perceived through his or her own mental representation of it as ‘nirvāṇa’‍—would just be his or her own gross mental perception of it. And the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas have never stated that such a dedication by someone conceiving of a mental perception would bring great benefit.The term “someone conceiving of a mental perception” (upalambhasaṃjñinaḥ; dmigs pa’i ’du shes can) is used throughout this literature pejoratively. Cf. BHSD under upalambha: “having a false notion of mental perception”, upalambhasaṃjñinaḥ. Why is that? Because such a dedication would contain poison or thorns. It is as if, as an analogy, there were some delicious food that was also poisonous. Even if it seemed desirable from the perspective of color, smell, taste, and texture, it would be poisonous, and hence the wise would avoid it as something unfit for consumption. Of course, foolish and unwise people might think that this very same food should be consumed. And its taste might even bring pleasure to whoever were to consume such food because of its color, smell, taste, and texture. But once that food was digested, it would result in suffering.Here the scripture contains a double entendre for the multivalent Sanskrit term pariṇāma (“dedication,” “transformation,” and “digestion”). To be precise, the food metaphor requires the translation “digestion” in this case, but “dedication” is implicit. According to Ratnākara (F.90.a), “when it is digested” means in the end; “results” refers to the fruit of the ripening process. RK: pariṇāme ce ti avasāne ca| vipāka phalam. Cf. MW pariṇāma, “digestion.” That is to say, some might die or at least suffer on the brink of death because of that poisonous food. Just as in that analogy, Subhūti, in this very area, there are some who have misheard, misconstrued, and incorrectly practiced. Because of this, they do not know the meaning of what has been well described. Without understanding the real meaning that accords with reality, they will instruct and guide others as follows.

“ ‘Come, noble son, contemplate the masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation of those past, present, and future blessed buddhas. Contemplate those roots of virtue also that disciples have planted, will plant, and are planting in relation to those blessed past, future, and present buddhas, up to point where the genuine Dharma has not disappeared, will not disappear, and does not disappear. That is to say, contemplate those roots of virtue that have been planted, will be planted, and are being planted, starting from when those blessed past, future, and present buddhas first gave rise to the proper mindset up to the point where they have realized, are realizing, and will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and have passed, are passing, and will pass into the nirvāṇa dimension without any constituents remaining. Contemplate the roots of virtue that have been planted, will be planted, and are being planted by those bodhisattva great beings who were foreordained, will be foreordained, and are being foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the blessed buddhas. Contemplate the roots of virtue of those persons on the path of a solitary realizer who were foreordained, will be foreordained, and are being foreordained to be solitary realizers. Contemplate the roots of virtue of those persons on the disciples’ path who were foreordained, will be foreordained, and are being foreordained for the awakening of a disciple. Contemplate the roots of virtue of ordinary sentient beings.

“ ‘Without leaving out any of these roots of virtue, you should essentialize them, combine them into one, consider them, and completely rejoice in them.In this sentence, the English translation has followed the Tibetan translation, rather than the Sanskrit texts that add the phrase “of all the blessed buddhas in the innumerable and immeasurable world systems” because this phrase was not included in the passages above, which were virtually identical to one another. aprameyāsaṃkhyeyeṣu lokadhātuṣu atītānāgata­pratyutpannānāṃ buddhānāṃ bhagavatāṃ sarvalokadhātuṣu. After rejoicing, you should dedicate to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing.’

“As explained, such a dedication would be made by means of a sign, so it would be poisoned, just like in the example of the food itself being poisoned.According to Ratnākara (F.90.b), “poisoned” here means lacking efficacious potency. RK: evaṃ sa iti yo ’yam uktaḥ| saviṣatvād iti anarthakaraṇaśaktir atra viṣam. For those with a conception based on a mental perception, there is no dedicating. Why not? Because a mental perception is poisoned. Therefore, any person on the bodhisattva path should not train in this way. One might ask, ‘How should a person train instead? How should the root of virtue of the past, future, and present blessed buddhas be taken up? How should what is taken up be properly taken up? How should it be dedicated? How should a person properly dedicate what is dedicated to unsurpassed, perfect awakening?’ The answer is that any noble son or daughter on the bodhisattva path who wishes to avoid denigration of the tathāgatas should rejoice in all the roots of virtue and dedicate them as follows.According to Ratnākara (F.90.b), “how should” and so on are the questions. “The answer” is the response. The term “denigration” refers to injury due to false assertions about him being made. In the phrase “should be taken up” and so on, rejoicing is characterized by embracing (i.e., taking hold of) it, because it cannot be separated (from that). RK: katham ityādinā praśnāḥ| ihe tyādikam uttaram| abhyākhyānaṃ nindā| abhūtavāditayā khyāpanāt| parigrahītavyā dipadeṣu parigraheṇānumodanā lakṣyate, nāntarīyakatvāt.

“ ‘Just as the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas know the root of virtue with their buddha knowing and see it with their buddha eye, just so I rejoice in that root of virtue that is of a similar type, characteristic, class, and nature, recognized to be the true nature of things. And, in the very same way that the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas recommend, I dedicate this root of virtue being dedicated to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’

“The bodhisattva great being who rejoices and dedicates in this way is faultless. He or she rejoices in that root of virtue of the blessed buddhas perfectly; dedicates it to unsurpassed, perfect awakening; and does not denigrate the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. Such a noble son’s or daughter’s dedication is an unpoisoned dedication‍—that is to say, a perfectly great dedication and an utterly perfect dedication of the ultimate realm of things‍—made with an altruistic disposition and a firm conviction.According to Hari (358), “faultless” means not having caused any harm due to one’s not having faults. “Unpoisoned dedication” is due to lacking a mental perception as one’s object (of dedication). It is a “great dedication” due to causing the attainment of a buddha’s form body. It is a “dedication of the ultimate realm of things” due to causing the attainment of a buddha’s body of truth (dharmakāya). Here, “perfectly” means due to proceeding to the level of preliminary and subsequent practice. “Utterly perfect” means due to correctly advancing to the level of the main practice RK: anaparāddha iti nirdoṣatvād akṛtāparādhaḥ. apagatopalambha­viṣayatvān nirviṣaḥ pariṇāmaḥ. rūpa­kāya­dharmakāya­prāpti­hetutvād yathākramaṃ mahāpariṇāmo dharmadhātupariṇāmaḥ. pra­yoga­pṛṣṭhāvasthā­praṣṭhatvāt paripūrṇaḥ. maulāvasthāyāṃ samyak­pravṛttatvāt suparipūrnaḥ.

“Moreover, a noble son or daughter on the bodhisattva path should thus dedicate the ethical discipline, concentration, wisdom, liberation, and seeing the knowing of liberation as unenmeshed in the desire realm, form realm, or formless realm, and as something that is not past, present, or future. Why is that? Because, insofar as he or she is not enmeshed in the three realms or the three times, his or her dedication of them‍—exactly as they are‍—is also not enmeshed.According to Hari (358), not being “enmeshed” (aparyāpannaḥ) means not being stuck, because the three realms of desire and so on are not mentally perceived. Hari: aparyapannam iti kāmādidhātu­traya­syānu­palambhād apratiṣṭhitaṃ. According to Ratnākara (F.90.b), “ethical discipline” and so on refers to the masses (skandha) of ethical discipline and so on that constitutes a tathāgata. “Enmeshed in” means existing within. “Not being enmeshed in the three realms or three times” means not existing in the three realms or existing in the three times. The idea here is that their masses of “ethical discipline” and so on refers simply to the ultimate realm of things (dharmadhātu). RK: śīlā dīti tathāgataḥ śīlādiskandhaḥ| aparyāpannam anantarbhūtaṃ| tryadhvatrai­dhātukāparyāpannatvād iti adhvatraye dhātutraye cānantargatatvād ity arthaḥ| dharmadhātumātratvāt teṣāṃ śīlā dīnām iti bhāvaḥ. Furthermore, that thing to which that dedication is dedicated is not enmeshed.According to Ratnākara (F.91.a), “that thing to which” refers to perfect, complete awakening. RK: yatrāpi dharma iti samyaksambodhau. Thus, when the bodhisattva great being has firm conviction while dedicating in this way, then his or her dedication is an unspoiled, unenmeshed, and unpoisoned dedication, a perfectly great dedication, and an utterly perfect dedication of the ultimate realm of things.The commentators effectively treat the phrase “dedication of the ultimate realm of things” (dharmadhātupariṇāma) as referring to both (a) the thing dedicated, i.e., a dedication of the true nature of things, which is inseparable from the ultimate realm of things, or (b) the thing to which it is dedicated, i.e., a dedication to awakening, which is inseparable from the ultimate realm of things. In other words, the phrase “dedication of the ultimate realm of things” means that one is dedicating the ultimate realm of things, which is the true nature of all things, toward the ultimate realm of things, which is awakening. On the contrary, if he or she were to dedicate that thing while being fixated upon it and making it into a sign, then he or she would be dedicating it incorrectly. That said, the correct dedication is the following bodhisattva great being’s dedication: ‘Just as the blessed buddhas know and recommend the root of virtue dedicated to be properly dedicated to unsurpassed, perfect awakening, just so I make this dedication.’ And in this way, the root of virtue dedicated is properly dedicated to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”According to Ratnākara (F.91.a), this sentence is spoken in order to show that the two dedications recommended by the Buddha are the same in terms of being dedications of the ultimate realm of things. RK: buddhānujñāta­pariṇāmayor dharma­dhātu­pariṇāmatvena samatāṃ darśayitum āha| tatre tyādi.

Then the Blessed One commended the venerable Subhūti: “Excellent, Subhūti, excellent. Subhūti, you are acting as a teacher should by teaching the Dharma to the bodhisattva great beings.According to Hari (360), the Blessed One congratulates noble Subhūti with “excellent, excellent” because he had taught perfectly by way of a question (asked of Maitreya). Given that Maitreya taught this passage, how could the Blessed One otherwise direct his congratulation, “excellent,” toward someone else? Hari: praśnamukhena samyag upadeśakatvād āryasubhūte sādhu sādhv iti sādhukāraḥ. anyathārya­maitreyeṇopadiṣṭatvāt katham anyaṃ prati sādhukāraḥ sādhuḥ syāt. Why is that so? Because, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being’s dedication is a dedication of the ultimate realm of things.

“ ‘Just as the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas know and see it as the true nature of things, just so I rejoice in that root of virtue that is of a similar type, characteristic, class, and nature, recognized to be the true nature of things. And, in the very same way that the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas recommend, I dedicate this root of virtue being dedicated to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’These two lines express the two dedications of the ultimate realm of things mentioned in the previous notes.

“Now suppose that the mass of merit in this bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things were compared to the accumulation of merit by any noble son or daughter who had inspired and established all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in the ten virtuous ways of acting.According to Ratnākara (F.91.a), in relation to these two “dedications of the ultimate realm of things,” the eleven descriptions beginning with “foremost” and so on are because they are causes that draw forth the eleven bodhisattva levels beginning with the joyful level and so on. atre ty anayor dharma­dhatu­pariṇāmanayor agrā ditvaṃ pramuditādyekādaśa­bhūmi­gata­prakarṣahetutvāt. In this case, the mass of merit born from that very bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things would be called the foremost, the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled.

“Now, Subhūti, never mind a noble son or daughter inspiring and establishing all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in the ten virtuous ways of acting. Subhūti, suppose that the bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things were compared to the accumulation of merit from inspiring and establishing all those sentient beings in the attainment of the four meditative states. In this case too, the very same mass of virtue associated with that bodhisattva great being’s dedication would be called the foremost, the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled.

“Now, never mind a noble son or daughter inspiring and establishing all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in the attainment of the four meditative states. Subhūti, suppose that the bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things were compared to the accumulation of merit from inspiring and establishing all those sentient beings‍—in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—in the attainment of the four immeasurable states.

“Now, never mind a noble son or daughter inspiring and establishing all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in the attainment of the four immeasurable states. Subhūti, suppose that the bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things were compared to the accumulation of merit from inspiring and establishing all those sentient beings‍—in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—in the attainment of the four formless attainments.

“Now, never mind a noble son or daughter inspiring and establishing all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in the attainment of the four formless attainments. Subhūti, suppose that the bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things were compared to the accumulation of merit from inspiring and establishing all those sentient beings‍—in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—in the five types of extrasensory knowledge.

“Now, never mind a noble son or daughter inspiring and establishing all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in the five types of extrasensory knowledge. Subhūti, suppose that the bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things were compared to the accumulation of merit from inspiring and establishing all those sentient beings‍—in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—in being stream enterers.

“Now, never mind a noble son or daughter inspiring and establishing all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in being stream enterers. Subhūti, suppose that the bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things were compared to the accumulation of merit from inspiring and establishing all those sentient beings‍—in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—in being once-returners.

“Now, never mind a noble son or daughter inspiring and establishing all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in being once-returners. Subhūti, suppose that the bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things were compared to the accumulation of merit from inspiring and establishing all those sentient beings‍—in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—in being non-returners.

“Now, never mind a noble son or daughter inspiring and establishing all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in being non-returners. Subhūti, suppose that the bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things were compared to the mass of merit from inspiring and establishing all those sentient beings‍—in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—in being worthy ones.

“Now, never mind a noble son or daughter inspiring and establishing all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in being worthy ones. Subhūti, suppose that the bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things were compared to the mass of merit from inspiring and establishing all those sentient beings‍—in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—in being solitary realizers.

“Now, never mind a noble son or daughter inspiring and establishing all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges in being solitary realizers. Subhūti, suppose that the bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things were compared to the accumulation of merit from inspiring and establishing all those sentient beings‍—in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—to become bodhisattvas who had set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Hari (363), “set out” means entered the stage of (having given rise to) the mindset of awakening in aspiration and in action. Hari: samprasthitā iti praṇidhiprasthāna­cittābhyām pravṛttābhyāṃ pravṛttā ity arthaḥ. And suppose that every single one of those bodhisattvas were to take care of all the other bodhisattvas in each of the other billionfold, great order world systems, as many as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—offering them robes, alms, seats, beds, preventive medicines, remedies, and other requisites‍—for a period of as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges.According to Ratnākara (F.91.b), “other requisites” means the monastic requisites, i.e., a water filter and so on. RK: pariṣkārāḥ pariśrāvaṇādayaḥ. And suppose that they also were to give them all gifts, including every basis for happiness and every condition pleasant to experience. And suppose that they did all this with a conception based on a mental perception.According to Ratnākara (F.91.b), the words “basis for happiness” mean something providing a source of happiness. The “condition pleasant to experience”‍—i.e., a temple terrace for walking and so on‍—is “for pleasure” i.e., for a pleasurable experience. RK: sukhahetavaḥ sukhāḥ | teṣāṃ upadhānaiḥ ḍhaukanaiḥ| sukhasparśaḥ sukhānubhavaḥ| tena ye vihārāḥ caṃkramādayaḥ.

“Now differentiate again all those other beings and all those bodhisattvas in that very same manner, and suppose that each single bodhisattva were to take care of all the other bodhisattvas for a period of as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges.According to Hari (364–65), “in the same manner” means, to be more precise, in the step-by-step, one-by-one way that all those beings who have set out for complete awakening might take care of all the beings in the other world systems as described, and hence “give gifts with a mental perception.” The basic idea of the sentence is that one would again “differentiate,” i.e., consider, “all those” other “sentient beings” located in the other world systems as described, and each of those bodhisattvas would take care of “all those other” very same “bodhisattvas” for that many eons. Hari: etena paryāyeṇe ti te sarve samyak­sam­bodhi­samprasthitāḥ sattvā yathoktāparāpara­lokadhātu­sthita­sarva­sattvānām etena paryāyeṇa ikaika­sattva­krameṇopasthānāt tac ca dānam upalambha­saṃjñino dadyur iti yāvat. tān sarvasattvān ityādi. tān anyān yathoktalokadhātustithān sarvasattvān ekaikaṃ parikalpya kṛtvā punas tān eva sarvabodhisattvān ekaiko bodhisattvas tāvataḥ kalpān upatiṣṭhed iti vākyārthaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.91.b), “differentiate those sentient beings”‍—i.e., arrange them separately as those who are to be taken care of‍—“and also as those bodhisattvas,” i.e., arrange them separately as the caretakers. “Each bodhisattva” would take care of all those very same sentient beings because they had just begun (the path). RK: tān sarvasattvān ekaikaṃ parikalpye ti| upastheyatvena pṛthagavasthāpya| tāṃś ca sarvabodhisattvān iti| ekaikaṃ parikalpyeti vartate| upasthāpakatvena pṛthagavasthāpya| ekaiko bodhisattva iti| upatiṣṭhed iti sambandhaḥ| kim upatiṣṭhet? prakṛtatvāt tān eva sarvasattvān. And suppose that each single one were also to pay tribute to them‍—with robes, alms, seats, beds, preventive medicines, remedies, other requisites, every basis for happiness, and every condition pleasant to experience‍—revere them, respect them, venerate them, honor them, and tend to them. And in the same manner, suppose that each single one were to give gifts to all the bodhisattvas like this one by one, and, likewise, they all were to give them gifts like this. Subhūti, what do you think? Based on that, would those bodhisattvas earn a great amount of merit?”

“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” replied Subhūti. “It is not easy to come up with even a number for that mass of merit. It is not easy to come up with any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison for that mass of merit. Blessed One, if that mass of merit were physical in form, it could not be contained even in billionfold, great order world systems equal to that number of grains of sand.”

In response, the Blessed One said to the elder, the venerable Subhūti, “Correct, Subhūti, correct. But, Subhūti, when a person on the bodhisattva path, who is supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means, dedicates the root of virtue to unsurpassed, perfect awakening through this dedication of the ultimate realm of things, he or she earns far more merit than that. That is to say, the former accumulation of merit, which those bodhisattvas conceiving of a mental perception would earn through such generosity, does not come close in worth to even a fraction of the mass of merit born from the dedication of the ultimate realm of things. It does not come close in worth to one hundredth, one thousandth, one hundred thousandth, one ten millionth, one billionth, one ten billionth, one trillionth, or even one ten trillionth of it. Nor is any calculation, any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison adequate for comparing them. Why not? Because those former bodhisattvas‍—who conceive of a mental perception‍—might have given extremely numerous gifts, but those gifts are still enumerated as ‘extremely numerous.’ ”According to Hari (365), the very large amount of their gifts is incalculable as a number like one, two, and so on, and it is immeasurable because such a number cannot be measured even as immeasurable and so on, (but) that is “enumerated” to be “extremely numerous” and so on (because they still adhering to a mental perception). Hence, those (former) bodhisattvas’ accumulation of merit has that as a factor, when dedicating the masses of ethical discipline (described previously) as incalculable, etc., and immeasurable, etc., up to there not being “any comparison adequate for comparing them.” Hari: etad uktaṃ: yasmāt teṣāṃ suprabhūtaṃ dānaṃ dattaṃ sad ekatvādi saṃkhyādirahitam aprameyādi saṃkhyāyogād aprameyaṃ subahvityādi parisaṃkhyātaṃ bhavati tasmāt taddhetuka­puṇyābhi­saṃskāraḥ pariṇāmanā puṇyaskandhasyāprameyādisaṃkhyādirahitasya yāvad upaniṣadam api na (c.103b) kṣamata iti.

Then twenty thousand of the gods among the retinue of the Four Great Kings paid homage to the Blessed One, joining their hands in respect, and said, “Blessed One, such a dedication of the root of virtue to the state of complete knowing by those bodhisattva great beings who, in particular, are supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means is a great dedication, inasmuch as it surpasses the accumulation of merit derived from the same amount of giving by those bodhisattvas adhering to a mental perception.”

Then one hundred thousand gods from the Heaven of the Thirty-Three scattered and rained down upon the Blessed One heavenly showers of flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, heavenly showers of jewels, and heavenly showers of fabrics. They paid tribute to him with heavenly garlands of lights all around, and through the many heavenly ways of venerating him. They revered him, respected him, venerated him, honored him, and cared for him. They played musical tributes to the Blessed One with heavenly parasols, banners, bells, and flags. Then they spoke the following words: “Ah! Blessed One, how wonderful this is! A bodhisattva great being’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things is a great dedication, because, insofar as this great dedication is supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means, it surpasses the amount of merit derived from giving that could be accumulated by those bodhisattvas adhering to a mental perception.”

Likewise, the gods from the other retinues among the gods also came and paid tribute to him with supreme tribute, revered him with supreme reverence, respected him with supreme respect, venerated him with supreme devotional offerings, honored him with supreme honor, and cared for him with supreme care. They all expressed words to the exact same effect and made their voices resound.

[Here, repeat the refrain for each of the following different groups of gods in the realms of Yāma, Tuṣita, Nirmāṇarati, Paranirmitavaśavartin, Brahmakāyika, Brahmapurohita, Brahmapārṣadya, Mahābrahma, Parīttābha, Apramāṇābha, Ābhāsvara, Parīttaśubha, Apramāṇaśubha, Śubhakṛtsna, Anabhraka, Puṇyaprasava, Bṛhatphala, Asaṃjñisattva, Avṛha, Atapa, Sudṛśa, Sudarśana, and Akaniṣṭha.]These bracketed instructions for the reciter to repeat the recitation (peyālam) are in both the Sanskrit and Tibetan texts, and hence have been translated and left in the main text above.

Those gods all joined their hands together in reverence and paid homage to the Blessed One as follows: “Blessed One, it is wonderful, this dedication of the root of virtue by those bodhisattva great beings who are supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means! It is wonderful how much it surpasses the amount of merit that those bodhisattvas adhering to a mental perception would accumulate over such a long time and collect over such a wide area!”

Then the Blessed One addressed all those gods, beginning with the assembly from the Śuddhāvāsa heaven, and so on: “O gods, never mind the accumulation of merit of all the sentient beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening and who have given gifts in order to obtain unsurpassed, perfect awakening in this manner. Gods, suppose that all those sentient beings in each of the different billionfold, great order world systems, as many as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, were to do the following. Suppose that they all first were to give rise to the proper mindset for awakening by making an aspiration to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Then, while remaining for a period of as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, suppose each single bodhisattva were to give gifts‍—including robes, alms, seats, beds, preventive medicines, remedies, other requisites, every basis for happiness, and every condition pleasant to experience‍—to all other beings in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges. And suppose that they all were to give these gifts while conceiving of a mental perception. Then, differentiate again all those sentient beings and all those bodhisattvas in the very same manner, and suppose that each and every single bodhisattva, for a period of as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, were to take care of all other beings until they all became bodhisattvas‍—offering them robes, alms, seats, beds, preventive medicines, remedies, other requisites, every basis for happiness, and every condition pleasant to experience. And suppose that they were to give these gifts while conceiving of a mental perception.According to Ratnākara (F.91.b), in the previous passage “all bodhisattvas,” whose collective strength within a (single) world system was likened to the grains of sand in the River Ganges, were the caretakers of the “sentient beings,” whose collective strength within the (other) world systems was likened to the grains of sand in the River Ganges. But here in this passage the difference is that the bodhisattva caretakers are equal in number to the sentient beings to be taken care of in that passage. And these bodhisattvas each take care of “sentient beings,” whose collective strength within world systems was likened to the grains of sand in the River Ganges. Those beings and those bodhisattvas are distinguished just like before. RK: pūrvatra gaṅgāna­dīvālukopamaloka­dhātuvīryāḥ sarvabodhisattvā upasthāpakāḥ pratyekaṃ gaṅgāna­dīvālukopamaloka­dhātuvīryānāṃ sattvānām| tatra yāvanta upastheyāḥ sattvāḥ, iha tāvanta upasthāpakā bodhisattvāḥ pratyekaṃ gaṅgāna­dīvālukopamā lokadhātuvīryānāṃ sattvānām iti viśeṣaḥ| tān sarvasattvān ekaikaṃ parikalpitāṃś ca sarvabodhisattvān iti pūrvavat.

“Now suppose that a different bodhisattva great being, supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means, were to contemplate the past, future, and present blessed buddhas’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation. Suppose that he or she also were to contemplate the roots of virtue that have been planted, will be planted, and are being planted by those persons on the bodhisattva path, the path of a solitary realizer, and the disciple path, and by others there too. And without leaving out any of these roots of virtue, suppose that he or she were to essentialize them, combine them all into one, consider them, and completely rejoice in them with the foremost joyousness. Suppose that he or she also were to rejoice in them with the best, the most distinguished joyousness‍—the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled, unmatched, and inconceivable. After having rejoiced in this way, suppose that he or she would then recite the words, ‘I dedicate to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing. Let it bring forth unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’

“Supposing that those former bodhisattvas were to conceive of a mental perception of the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing and dedicating, their accumulation of merit would not come close in worth to even a fraction of the latter bodhisattva’s accumulation of merit. It would not even come close in worth to one hundredth, one thousandth, one hundred thousandth, one ten millionth, one billionth, one ten billionth, one trillionth, or even one ten trillionth of that accumulation of merit. Nor would any calculation, any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison be adequate for comparing them. Why is that? Because all those bodhisattvas would be giving gifts with a conception of a mental perception.”

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “The Blessed One has said, ‘Without leaving out any roots of virtue possessed by the past, future, and present blessed buddhas, the community of bodhisattvas, solitary realizers, disciples, or all sentient beings, a bodhisattva great being essentializes these roots of virtue, combines them into one, considers them, and completely rejoices in them with the foremost joyousness. He or she rejoices in them with the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled, the unmatched, and the most inconceivable joyousness.’ In that statement, Blessed One, to what extent do you mean that joyousness is the foremost?”According to Ratnākara (F.92.a), the descriptions “foremost” and so on are taught to be fourteen, so the answer to the question “To what extent is that foremost?” is to this extent. What is the purpose of saying this here? The fourteen types of being “foremost” themselves are based on the virtuous meditative objects in the ten directions, in the center (of those ten), and in the three times. In this case, where there are (only) thirteen phrases (i.e., “foremost,” “best,” and so on), the thirteen types are based on a different division of the ten directions and three times. In that division, how could the center not be included? Because the point at the center is so subtle, it contains (all ten) directions (i.e., it need not be mentioned separately here). RK: agra tvādīni caturdaśoktāḥ| kiyatā agratā bhavatīti praśnaḥ| iyatā bhavatīty uttaram| ko ’trābhiprāyaḥ ? agratvam eva daśadigma­dhyatryadhva­kuśalālambanatvāc caturdaśavidhaṃ bhavatīti| yatra tarhi trayodaśapadāni tatrāpi daśadiktrya­dhvabhedāt trayodaśavidham| tatra kathaṃ madhyamalokadhātoḥ saṃgrahaḥ? tanmadhye sūkṣmam avadhiṃ kṛtvā diśāṃ grahaṇāt.

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, suppose that a person on the bodhisattva path does not grasp, does not develop conceits about, does not mentally perceive, does not imagine, does not conceptualize, does not see, and does not identify these things as past, future, or present.According to Ratnākara (F.92.a), that the person “does not grasp” these things is due to their not appearing as something to be grasped; that he “does not develop conceits” is due to their not appearing as things; that he “does not mentally perceive” is due to their not appearing as a self-nature; that he “does not imagine” means he does not imagine them as a universal characteristic; that he “does not conceptualize” means he does not conceptualize them as particular characteristics; that he “does not see” is because he does not fixate on beliefs; and that he “does not identify” is because he does not fixate continuously. RK: na gṛṇhīte grāhyatvenāprati­bhāsāt| na manyate vastutvenāpratibhāsāt| nopalabhate svarūpato ’pratibhāsāt| na kalpayati sāmānyalakṣaṇena| na vikalpayati viśeṣalakṣaṇaiḥ| na paśyati dṛṣṭyā anabhiniveśāt| na samanupaśyati santatam anabhiniveśāt. Suppose that he or she instead reflects on them in this way: ‘All things are manifested by the imagination. They have neither been born nor produced. They have neither come nor gone.According to Ratnākara (F.92.a), here “imagination” means the imaginer of the unreal; “manifested” means made to appear; “not been born” means due to having no seeds (of birth); “not produced” means due to not being actualized; “not come” means from a past time or from a former world; and “not gone” means to the future or to another world. RK: kalpanā abhūtaparikalpaḥ| tathā viṭhapitāḥ saṃdarśitāḥ| ajātā nirbījatvāt| anirjātā aniṣpatteḥ| anāgatikā anāgatād adhvanaḥ pūrvalokād vā| agatikā atītādhvani paraloke vā. In this, no thing has come to exist, no thing is coming to exist, and no thing will come to exist. Furthermore, no thing has ceased, no thing is ceasing, and no thing will cease.’According to Ratnākara (F.92.a–b), for the sentences beginning from “no thing has come to exist” up to “no thing will cease,” the reason (to be supplied) is either because they lack any inherent nature or because they are completely imagined. RK: evaṃ yāvat nāpi nirudhyata iti| niḥsvabhāvatvāt parikalpatatvād veti hetuḥ. After reflecting on those things in such a way, suppose he or she rejoices in the true nature of things that those things possess. After rejoicing, suppose he or she dedicates to unsurpassed, perfect awakening in that very same way.According to Ratnākara (F.92.b), “like this” means their being thus with a nature imagined. “These things” that are that way refers to the positive qualities (described) having a dependent nature. The “true nature of things” means their emptiness of that inherent nature, which is the perceptual object of a naive person. The Blessed One says “after rejoicing, he dedicates in that very same way” in this section on rejoicing because it is necessary that the rejoicing be dedicated. RK: evam iti parikalpitena svabhāvena itthaṃbhūtān, etān dharmān iti paratantrarūpān kuśalān| dharmate ti bāla­buddhi­gocaraiḥ svabhāvaiḥ śūnyatā| anumodan ādhikāre ’pi tathā anumodya tathaiva pariṇāmayatī ti vacanam avaśya­pariṇāmanīyatvāt. Subhūti, it is to this extent that a bodhisattva great being’s joyousness is the foremost. Subhūti, that thing that produces the merit‍—derived from generosity by the former of the two bodhisattvas who conceives of a mental perception and believes in a mental perception‍—does not come close in worth to even a fraction of this accumulation of merit. It does not come close in worth to one hundredth, one thousandth, one hundred thousandth, one ten millionth, one billionth, one ten billionth, one trillionth, or even one ten trillionth of it. Nor is any calculation, any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison adequate for comparing them.

“Moreover, Subhūti, any person on the bodhisattva path who wishes to rejoice in the generosity, who wishes to rejoice in the ethical discipline, who wishes to rejoice in the tolerant acceptance, who wishes to rejoice in the heroic perseverance, who wishes to rejoice in the meditative concentration, or who wishes to rejoice in the wisdom of all the past, future, and present blessed buddhas should rejoice with the following knowledge.

“ ‘Generosity is the same as liberation.According to Ratnākara (F.92.b), “liberation” means nirvāṇa, and nirvāṇa is the purity of the ultimate realm of things. The meaning is that all of these, i.e., “generosity” and so on, are just like that (i.e., the same) at the ultimate level. RK: vimuktir nirvāṇam| tac ca dharmadhātoḥ sarvāvaraṇa­viśuddhitā, paramārthatas tathaiva sarvaṃ dānādīty arthaḥ. Ethical discipline is the same as liberation. Tolerant acceptance is the same as liberation. Heroic perseverance is the same as liberation. Meditative concentration is the same as liberation. Wisdom is the same as liberation. The seeing of liberation and knowing is the same as liberation. Joyousness is the same as liberation. That which is producing the merit associated with joyousness is the same as liberation. Dedication is the same as liberation. The blessed buddhas and the solitary realizers are the same as liberation. Their disciples who have passed into nirvāṇa are the same as liberation. Those past things that have ceased are the same as liberation. Those future things that have not come to exist are the same as liberation. Those present things that are occurring right now are the same as liberation. The past blessed buddhas and their disciples are the same as liberation. The future blessed buddhas and their disciples are the same as liberation. The present blessed buddhas‍—who are residing, staying, and spending time in immeasurable and innumerable world systems‍—and their disciples are the same as liberation. The past, present, and future blessed buddhas are the same as liberation. Thus, I rejoice with unsurpassed joyousness in that true nature of unfettered, unliberated, and unentangled things. After rejoicing, I dedicate the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing to unsurpassed, perfect awakening by means of not dedicating it, because nothing is transferred and nothing is destroyed.’According to Ratnākara (F.92.b), it says “by means of not dedicating” because that (dedication) also is not mentally perceived. With the words “nothing is transferred and nothing is destroyed,” the Blessed One explains how that could be so. The point is that a “dedication” either indicates a transference (of something from one state of existence) to another state or it means the destruction of a former state of existence and the arising of a subsequent state. In either case, since the source of things/qualities has no state of existence, (as he mentions in the bodhisattva’s dedication of the ultimate realm of things) neither transference nor destruction occurs. RK: apariṇāmanāyogeneti tasyāpy anupalambhāt| sa katham ity āha| asaṃkrāntito avināśata iti| tathā hy avasthāntara­saṃkrāntiḥ pariṇāmaḥ pūrva­vināśāparot­pattī vā| na ca dharmadhātor avasthā| na ca saṃkramo nāpi vināśa iti. According to Hari (373), insofar as everything is like an illusion, “nothing is transferred” because there is nothing cycling in cyclic existence, and “nothing is destroyed” because there is no destruction‍—these two phrases should be connected. Hari: māyopamatayā saṃsaraṇābhāvād asaṃkrāntito, vināśābhāvād avināśata iti yojyaṃ. Note: The English translation of this sentence follows the extant Sanskrit text. The Tibetan text differs slightly and would be translated, “By means of not dedicating to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing, I dedicate it that way as the true nature of things since it is neither transferred nor destroyed (avināśataḥ).” / Our version of this: “Having rejoiced, I dedicate the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing to unsurpassed, perfect awakening in the manner of not rejoicing, in accordance with the true nature of things, since there is no transference or destruction.” In fact the difference seems to be in one phrase added in Tibetan. Of course dharma/chos and vastu/dngos po both being translated as “things” is a little confusing and the connection between dharmas and dharmatā gets lost. Tib.: rjes su yi rang nas rjes su yi rang ba dang ldan pa’i bsod nams bya ba’i dngos po bla na med pa yang dag par rdzogs pa’i byang chub tu yongs su mi bsngo ba’i tshul gyis chos de dag gi chos nyid ji lta ba de ltar mi ’pho ba dang mi ’jig pas yongs su bsngo’o zhes bya ba’o.

“So, Subhūti, never mind those sentient beings in each of the other billionfold, great order world systems, as many as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, setting out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Never mind their giving rise to the proper mindset for the awakening of all sentient beings and setting out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening in each of the other billionfold, great order world systems, as many as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges. Never mind each and every single bodhisattva, for a period of as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, taking care of all those beings until they all were bodhisattvas‍—offering them robes, alms, seats, beds, preventive medicines, remedies, other requisites, every basis for happiness, and every condition pleasant to experience‍—and giving them these gifts while conceiving of a mental perception. Never mind all those bodhisattvas also taking care of all others in the same manner and giving them such gifts while conceiving of a mental perception.

“Instead, Subhūti, suppose that in the very same manner, all these sentient beings‍—who had already set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening in each of the other billionfold, great order world systems, as many as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—were, in addition, to proceed for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges to practice the ethical discipline consisting in virtuous physical deeds, virtuous verbal deeds, and virtuous mental deeds for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges while conceiving of a mental perception.According to Ratnākara (F.92.b), “practicing” ethical discipline means accepting the circumscriptions from a proper guru. RK: samādāye ti samyaggurutaḥ sāvadhikam ādāya. In the same manner, suppose that each and every single bodhisattva, remaining for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, were to proceed in addition to practice the ethical discipline consisting in virtuous physical deeds, virtuous verbal deeds, and virtuous mental deeds for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges while conceiving of a mental perception. And suppose that, in this same manner, all bodhisattvas also were to proceed to practice ethical discipline while conceiving of a mental perception.

“In comparison, suppose a single bodhisattva great being, supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means, were to rejoice in the past, present, and future blessed buddhas’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; in the past, present, and future blessed solitary realizers’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; in the past, present, and future disciples’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; and in all past, present, and future sentient beings’ accumulation of the roots of virtue.

“Also, suppose that, without leaving out any of these roots of virtue, he or she were to essentialize them, combine them into one, consider them, and completely rejoice in them with the foremost joyousness. And suppose that he or she were to rejoice in them with the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled, the unmatched, and the most inconceivable joyousness. And after rejoicing in this way, suppose that he or she were to dedicate to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing.

“Subhūti, insofar as those former bodhisattvas adhere to a mental perception, their accumulation of merit derived from ethical discipline would not come close in worth to even a fraction of the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing possessed by this latter bodhisattva. It would not come close in worth to one hundredth, one thousandth, one hundred thousandth, one ten millionth, one billionth, one ten billionth, one trillionth, or even one ten trillionth of that accumulation of merit of that thing that produces the merit associated with rejoicing. Nor would any calculation, any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison even be adequate for comparing them. Why is that? Because those former bodhisattvas would proceed to practice ethical discipline while conceiving of a mental perception.

“Subhūti, now never mind those sentient beings setting out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening in each of the other billionfold, great order world systems, as many as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—and, after doing so, proceeding to practice ethical discipline consisting in virtuous physical deeds, virtuous verbal deeds, and virtuous mental deeds for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, while conceiving of a mental perception.

“Instead, Subhūti, suppose that all these sentient beings had already set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges. And, after doing so, suppose that each and every single bodhisattva‍—up to the point where they all did the same‍—were, in addition, to proceed to practice tolerant acceptance in the face of being insulted, beaten, and verbally abused by everyone, remaining for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, while conceiving of a mental perception. Also, in this same manner, suppose that all bodhisattvas‍—in each of the other billionfold, great order world systems, as many as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges‍—also were to proceed to practice tolerant acceptance in the face of being insulted, beaten, and verbally abused by everyone, while conceiving of a mental perception. Thus, suppose also that they all were to proceed to practice tolerant acceptance in the face of being insulted, beaten, and verbally abused by everyone, remaining for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, while conceiving of a mental perception.

“In comparison, suppose that a single bodhisattva great being, supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means, were to rejoice in the past, present, and future blessed buddhas’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; in the past, present, and future blessed solitary realizers’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; in the past, present, and future disciples’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; and in all past, present, and future sentient beings’ accumulation of the roots of virtue.

“Also, suppose he or she, without leaving out any of these roots of virtue, were to essentialize them, combine them into one, consider them, and completely rejoice in them with the foremost joyousness. And suppose he or she were to rejoice in them with the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled, the unmatched, and the most inconceivable joyousness. Also, after rejoicing in this way, suppose he or she were to dedicate to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing.

“Subhūti, insofar as those former bodhisattvas adhere to a mental perception, their accumulation of merit associated with tolerant acceptance would not come close in worth to even a fraction of the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing possessed by this latter bodhisattva. It would not come close in worth to one hundredth, one thousandth, one hundred thousandth, one ten millionth, one billionth, one ten billionth, one trillionth, or even one ten trillionth of that accumulation of merit of that thing that produces the merit associated with rejoicing. Nor would any calculation, any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison even be adequate for comparing them. Why is that? Because those former bodhisattvas would proceed to practice tolerant acceptance while conceiving of a mental perception.

“Subhūti, now never mind those sentient beings setting out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, and proceeding to practice tolerant acceptance in the face of being insulted, beaten, and verbally abused by everyone in each of the other billionfold, great order world systems, as many as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, while conceiving of a mental perception.,

“Instead, Subhūti, suppose that all these sentient beings had already set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges. And, after doing so, suppose that each and every single bodhisattva were to proceed in addition to practice heroic perseverance, moving forward and making progress without being either dejected or overcome by drowsiness or sleepiness for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, while conceiving of a mental perception.

“In comparison, suppose that a single bodhisattva great being, supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means, rejoices in the past, present, and future blessed buddhas’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; in the past, present, and future blessed solitary realizers’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; in the past, present, and future disciples’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; and in all past, present, and future sentient beings’ accumulation of the roots of virtue.

“Also, suppose he or she, without leaving out any of these roots of virtue, were to essentialize them, combine them into one, consider them, and completely rejoice in them with the foremost joyousness. And suppose he or she were to rejoice in them with the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled, the unmatched, and the most inconceivable joyousness. Also, after rejoicing in this way, suppose he or she were to dedicate to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing.

“Subhūti, insofar as those former bodhisattvas adhere to a mental perception, their accumulation of merit derived from heroic perseverance would not come close in worth to even a fraction of the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing possessed by this latter bodhisattva. It would not come close in worth to one hundredth, one thousandth, one hundred thousandth, one ten millionth, one billionth, one ten billionth, one trillionth, or even one ten trillionth of that accumulation of merit of that thing that produces the merit associated with rejoicing. Nor would any calculation, any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison even be adequate for comparing them. Why is that? Because those former bodhisattvas would proceed to practice heroic perseverance while conceiving of a mental perception.

“Subhūti, now never mind those sentient beings setting out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, and, after doing so, proceeding to practice heroic perseverance, moving forward and making progress without being either dejected or overcome by drowsiness or sleepiness in each of the other billionfold, great order world systems, as many as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, while conceiving of a mental perception.

“Instead, Subhūti, suppose that all these sentient beings had already set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening in as many billionfold, great order world systems as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges. And, after doing so, suppose that they all were to proceed to attain the four meditative states for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, while conceiving of a mental perception.

“In comparison, suppose that a single bodhisattva great being, supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means, were to rejoice in the past, present, and future blessed buddhas’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; in the past, present, and future blessed solitary realizers’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; in the past, present, and future disciples’ masses of ethical discipline, masses of meditative concentration, masses of wisdom, masses of liberation, and masses of seeing the knowing of liberation; and in all past, present, and future sentient beings’ accumulation of the roots of virtue.

“Also, suppose he or she, without leaving out any of these roots of virtue, were to essentialize them, combine them into one, consider them, and completely rejoice in them with the foremost joyousness. And suppose that he or she were to rejoice in them with the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled, the unmatched, and the most inconceivable joyousness. Also, after rejoicing in this way, suppose that he or she were to dedicate to unsurpassed, perfect awakening the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing.

“Subhūti, insofar as those former bodhisattvas adhere to a mental perception, their accumulation of merit derived from the four meditative states would not come close in worth to even a fraction of the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing possessed by this latter bodhisattva. It would not come close in worth to one hundredth, one thousandth, one hundred thousandth, one ten millionth, one billionth, one ten billionth, one trillionth, or even one ten trillionth of that accumulation of merit of that thing that produces the merit associated with rejoicing. Nor would any calculation, any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison even be adequate for comparing them. Why is that? Because those former bodhisattvas would be attaining the four meditative states while conceiving of a mental perception.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Rejoicing and Dedicating,” the sixth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 7: HellAccording to Ratnākara (F.100.b), this chapter is the “Chapter on Getting Sidetracked” because it explains the seven ways of getting sidetracked. Nevertheless, it is called the “Chapter on Hell” because the part about hell is prominent. RK: nirhāra parivarto ’yaṃ saptāṅgasya nirhārasya dyotanāt| tathāpi nirayagranthasya bahutvān niraya parivarta ity ucyate.The beginning of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 25 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 34 in the Eighteen Thousand. Its content also includes the first part of Chapter 26 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (down to and including 26.24) and Hundred Thousand (down to and including 26.28), and of Chapter 35 in the Eighteen Thousand (down to and including 35.22); for the equivalent point in this chapter to the beginning of those new chapters in the long sūtras, see n.505.
div.p: Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is the actualization of the knowing of someone who has complete knowing.According to Hari (379), there are two aspects of the path of cultivation, namely the path of cultivation with worldly influences (sāsrava), which was the concern of the immediately preceding chapter, and the path of cultivation without worldly influences (anāsrava), which is the concern of this chapter. To describe the first aspect that is characterized by accomplishment, Śāriputra expresses its nature with the words in the above sentence. He describes the “transcendent state of wisdom” as an actualization because it is a cause for actualizing the knowing of a tathāgata. “Knowing everything” refers to the nature of seeing physical form and so on in an accurate way. Hari: sāsravabhāva­nāmārgānantaram anāsravo bhāvanāmārgo vaktavyaḥ. sa ca dvividha iti prathamam abhinirhāra­lakṣaṇaṃ bhāvanāmārgaṃ vaktuṃ svabhāvābhidhānād āha: atha khalv āyuṣmān ityādi. tathāgata­jñānasya niṣpādanahetutvāt sarva­jñajñāna­pariniṣpattiḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.93.a), the term “transcendent state of wisdom” refers to the nature of this very path of cultivation. When Śāriputra describes what that nature is like, we should understand the knowing of someone who has complete knowing as being actualized due to that. RK: ata āha| atha khalv ityādi| asya hi bhāvanāmārgasya svabhāvaḥ prajñāpāramitā| kīdṛśīty āha| sarvajñajñāna sya pariniṣpattiḥ yataḥ sā tathoktā. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is the knowing of someone who knows everything.”According to Hari (379), “knows everything” refers to the nature of accurately seeing physical form and so on. Hari: sarvajñatvam iti rūpādyaviparīta­darśana­svabhāva ity arthaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.93.a), “knows everything” is the accomplishment of the knowing (of “everything”) together with its relinquishing (of “everything”). That is to say, “knowing” refers to a knowing that has relinquished the habitual tendencies (vāsana; bag chags) connected to all the obstructions (āvaraṇa). RK: sarvajñatvam iti sarvā varaṇavāsanānu­sandhi­prahīṇaṃ jñā nam| prahāṇa­sampatsahitā jñānasampad ity arthaḥ.
“Śāriputra, it is so. It is exactly as you say,” said the Blessed One.

The venerable Śāriputra said, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is enlightening.According to Ratnākara (F.93.a), here the word “enlightening” either means that the state lights up, i.e., its accomplishment of relinquishing the causes of the darkness of all obscurations to be relinquished, or it means that its accomplishment of knowing causes the ultimate reality of all phenomena to appear. RK: atropapattir avabhāsa karoti| sarvāvaraṇatamaḥ­prahāṇam avabhāsaḥ | tatkareti prahāṇasampat| sarva­dharma­paramārtha­pratibhāso vā avabhāsaḥ| tatkareti jñānasampat. Blessed One, I pay homage to the transcendent state of wisdom. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is worthy of homage.According to Ratnākara (F.93.b), he says “I pay homage” now and it “should be paid homage” every moment in the future. RK: ataś caināṃ namaskaromi sāṃpratam| namaskaraṇīye ti āyatyāṃ pratikṣaṇam.

“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is unstained; Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is free from all the stains in the world.According to Ratnākara (F.93.b), he expresses the “relinquishing” with the two words “unstained” due to not observing any obstructions and “free from all the stains” because of not observing that which belongs to the three worlds. RK: idānīṃ padadvayena prahāṇam āha| anupalipte ti sarvāvaraṇam alakṣayāt| sarva­lokanirupalepe ti triadhātukam alakṣayāt.

“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is the source of light. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom removes the mental darkness within all three realms. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom dispels all the emotional defilements and erroneous beliefs that cause blindness.According to Ratnākara (F.93.b), now he explains the knowing with these three descriptions. “Source of light” means it causes the ultimate reality of all things to be seen. “Removes the mental darkness” refers to the knowing that is the opposite of ignorant darkness. As the ultimate reality of the three worlds, it pervades the objects in all three worlds entirely. “Dispels” means destroys all counterproductive forces, i.e., blinding emotional defilements and beliefs. Up to here, he has spoken entirely of the benefits to oneself that are accomplished. RK: idānīṃ padatrayeṇa jñānam āha| sarvadharmaparamārthapratibhāsa ālokaḥ, tatkarā | atra dvau hetū padadvayenāha| timiram ajñānam, tadviparyayo vitimiraṃ jñānam ity arthaḥ| sarva samagraṃ traidhātuka traidhātuka­paramārthaḥ tasmin vitimiram| tatkar ā ity ālambana­vyāptiḥ| kleśāś ca dṛṣṭayaś ca tā evāndhakārā ḥ| sarv e ca te te ca teṣāṃ apanetrī ti sarvavipakṣakṣayaḥ| iyatā sarveṇa svārtha­sampattir uktā.

“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom can be relied upon. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom leads to the highest stages. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom keeps the factors in awakening safe.According to Ratnākara (F.93.b), next Śāriputra speaks of the accomplishment of the benefits to others. “Can be relied upon” by those wishing to reach all stages. It “leads to the highest stages” means to the stages of a stream enterer, once-returner, and so on, up to solitary realizer’s awakening. It “keeps” the thirty-seven “factors in awakening safe” because it removes hindrances to them. Up to here, it teaches the objective for those (on the path of cultivation) belonging to the disciples’ path. RK: parārtha­sampattim adhikṛtyāha| āśrayaṇīye tyādi āśrayaṇīyā sarva padaprepsubhiḥ, agrā ṇi padāni strotaāpattiḥ sakṛdāgāmitvaṃ yāvat pratyekabodhiḥ,tat kar ā| teṣām upāyaḥ saptatriṃśad bodhipakṣ yāḥ| teṣāṃ kṣemakarī vighnaharaṇāt| iyatā hīnayānikānām artha uktaḥ.
“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is a source of sight to sentient beings who are blind. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is a source of sight beyond fear or distress.

“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom, which is possessed of the five eyes, causes all sentient beings to see the path.According to Ratnākara (F.94.a), the “five eyes” are essential for benefiting others. As a means to accomplish that benefit, the “transcendent state of wisdom” itself is (said to be) flanked by the “five eyes” based on the difference in cognitive objects. It itself is the (single) “sight” due to seeing the ultimate reality of all things with any difference. RK: pañcacakṣur ityādi te hi parārthapradhānāḥ| tasya saiva sādhanam ālambanabhedena pañcacakṣuḥ­parivārā| abhedena saiva cakṣu sarvadharmaparamārthadarśanāt.
