See also note 375 on “five types of extrasensory knowledge.” To summarize the explanation of the “five eyes” of bodhisattvas in the corresponding passage of the Twenty-Five Thousand (2.216–233): (1) The pure physical eye of bodhisattvas is able to see everything from one hundred leagues away up to the limits of the billionfold world system. (2) The pure divine eye of bodhisattvas is able to see the lives of the multitude of divine beings from birth to death just as they are, all the way up to the form realm heaven of Akaniṣṭha. (3) The pure wisdom eye of bodhisattvas is able to discern the nondual nature of all things, i.e., they see neither conditioned nor unconditioned things, good or bad things, and so on. (4) The pure Dharma eye of bodhisattvas is able to discern the Dharma and the faculties of those practicing it, from beginners up to those who are awakened. (5) The buddha eye is the knowing of all aspects of everything. It is obtained in a single moment of wisdom, after having attained the vajra-like meditative concentration, immediately preceding the realization of the mindset of awakening. By contrast, to summarize the “five eyes” of buddhas listed in the Mahāvastu 1.158–160: (1) The physical eye of buddhas is able to see the appearance and location of all the subtle, substantial (tattva), and luminous (prabhā) forms of other beings without obstruction, out to the limits of space, due to a vast accumulation of virtuous deeds. (2) The divine eye of buddhas is able to see very precisely, deeply, and clearly the multitude of divine beings, and into the mentally created (manomaya) forms, from the ground of the yakṣas up to very subtle form realms. (3) The wisdom eye of buddhas is able to see the nature of things more clearly than can stream enterers up through worthy ones (arhats). (4) The Dharma eye is the all-pervasive mental knowing in someone with the ten powers. (5) The Buddha eye is the knowing in someone with the eighteen distinctive qualities of a buddha. Cf. Dharmasaṃgraha, chapter 66. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is the sight. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom eliminates confusion, mental darkness, and blindness.According to Ratnākara (F.94.a), these three hindrances obstruct seeing. Among them, “confusion” means ignorance; “mental darkness” means the cause of not seeing; and “blindness” is an eye disease. Here, the Sanskrit word “eliminate” (vikaraṇī) or destroy is a condensed way of expressing, according to common usage, that it causes the eye disease to heal (vi timirakaraṇī). RK: vyāpino darśanasya trayo vibandhāḥ| [mohatamastimira­vikaraṇī ] | tatra moho ajñānam| tamo ’ndhakāram| timiraṃ cakṣurogaḥ| teṣāṃ vikaraṇī vidhvaṃsanī| saṃkṣepato vi timirakaraṇī timirasāmānyāt. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom does not create any things.According to Ratnākara (F.94.a), “all things” here refers to the constituents (skandha), elements (dhātu), or bases (āyatana) that are the three natures, namely, the imagined, the dependent, and the real natures. Among those natures, it does not “create” (a) the imagined nature, because it does not exist in the manner of characteristics; (b) or the dependent nature imagining what is unreal, because that is the counterpart of the (nonexistent) imagined; or (c) the real nature that is the source of things (dharmadhātu) because it has no beginning or end. If you ask whether it creates accurate knowledge, no, when there is accurate knowledge it arises simply by removing the error from that which has no beginning or end. RK: kim utpādayatīty ata āha| sarvadharmāṇām akaraṇī ti sarvadharmāḥ skandhadhātvāyatanāni| teṣāṃ trayaḥ svabhāvāḥ| kalpitaḥ paratantraḥ pariniṣpannaś ca| tatra kalpitaṃ na karoti tasya yathālakṣaṇam asattvāt| paratantro abhūtaparikalpaḥ| <<tam api na>> karoti tatpratipakṣatvāt| pariniṣpanno dharmadhātuḥ| tam api na karoty anādinidhanatvāt| samyagjñānaṃ karotīti cet ? na| anādinidhana­citta­santānabhrāntinivṛtyaiva samyagjñānasiddhe. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom introduces the path to those sentient beings who have gotten sidetracked. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is the very state of complete knowing. In the light of having relinquished the habitual tendencies connected to all the emotionally defiling obstructions and the obstructions to the objects of knowledge,According to Ratnākara (F.94.a), it introduces them to the “path” of complete knowing. Up to here, it has explained the terms in the opening sentence that “the actualization of the knowing of someone with complete knowing” is “knowing” for the benefit of others. The idea is that when it has relinquished any connections to habitual tendencies, it is the complete knowing. RK: kasya mārge? sarvajñatāyāḥ| iyatā sarvajñajñānapariniṣpattir iti yad uktaṃ tat parārthe ’pi darśitam| yad uktaṃ sarvajñatvam iti tat parārthe ’py āha| sarvajñataive tyādinā| yadā hi vāsanānusandhi­prahīṇatā tasyās tadāsau sarvajñatety arthaḥ. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom does not make any things arise. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom does not make any things cease. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom has not arisen or ceased.According to Ratnākara (F.94.b), it neither makes any purifying “things arise” nor defiling “things cease.” The idea is that it “has not arisen or ceased” either ultimately or in the manner that they appear, because the ultimate (reality) of these things is the source of things (dharmadhātu). Hence, they do not arise or cease, because they have no beginning or end and because they do not exist the way they appear to foolish ordinary beings. RK: anutpādike tyādi anutpādikā sarvadharmāṇām iti vaiyavadānikānām| anirodhikā sarvadharmāṇām iti sāṃkleśikānām| anutpannāniruddh eti svayam, paramārthato yathāpratibhāsaṃ ceti bhāvaḥ| paramārtho hy eṣāṃ dharmadhātuḥ| tenaiṣām anādinidhana tvād bālapratibhāsinā rūpeṇātyantam asattvān na iṣām utpādanirodhau. In the light of it being empty of its own inherent characteristics, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is the mother of bodhisattva great beings. In the light of it being the bestower of the precious awakened qualities of a buddha, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is the source of the ten powers.According to Ratnākara (F.94.b), it is called the “mother” or source of the bodhisattvas “in the light of its emptiness of its own inherent characteristics,” meaning, again, due to the third nature (of their three natures) being dependently arisen. Why is that? It bestows on them all the precious qualities of a buddha, since those causal states are the bodhisattva’s body. Hence, since it provides those to them, it is their source. Since the term “buddha” is used here (to qualify the type of qualities bestowed), the transcendent state of wisdom is implicitly understood to be the source of a buddha but not superior to the buddhas on that account. For this reason, he explains this more through her special qualities in the sentence beginning with the words “ten powers” and so on, which refers to the ten powers of the tathāgatas. RK: svalakṣaṇaśūnyatām upādāye ti pratītyasamutpannaṃ punar eṣāṃ tṛtīyaṃ svabhāvam upādāyā ha māte tyādi mātā jananī| kutaḥ ? sarvabuddhadharmā eva ratnā ni teṣāṃ dātrītvāt| tāny eva hi samagrāṇi hetvavasthāni bodhisatvānāṃ śarīram tata iyaṃ taddānāt teṣāṃ jananī| buddha grahaṇād buddhajananītvam arthād gamyate| na punas tāvatā buddhānām atiśayo gamyate| ato viśiṣṭair guṇaiḥ punas tad āha daśabale tyādinā| daśan tathāgatabalā ni| tatkar ā. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is unconquerable. In the light of it being the source of the four grounds for self-confidence,According to Ratnākara (F.95.a–b), a “ground for self-confidence” (vaiśāradyam) refers to (a buddha’s) innate property (bhāva) of being self-confident (viśāradaḥ) in proclaiming the truth proved in his experience (yuktavādī), like a (roaring) lion who is absolutely free of fear. The four grounds are (1) the self-confidence of being perfectly awake with respect to all things, (2) the self-confidence that all worldly influences have been permanently destroyed, (3) the self-confidence of having accurately ascertained and explained the things impeding awakening, and (4) the self-confidence concerning the way that the path leads beyond cyclic existence to all the accomplishments. Ratnākara unpacks the key terms in these four as follows: (1) “Being perfectly awake” refers to the tathāgata’s accomplishment of knowing, and (2) “all worldly influences permanently destroyed” refers to the accomplishment of relinquishing (negativity). This first pair of grounds describes a buddha’s personal benefits. (3) “Impeding awakening” refers to things that block nirvāṇa, and (4) “leads beyond cyclic existence” refers to the things that cause the attainment of nirvāṇa. This second pair of grounds is for the benefit of others, because he explains these things to others. Thus, the “transcendent state of wisdom” is said in this sentence to be the “source” of a buddha’s “grounds for self-confidence” for making these (four) true proclamations. RK: caturvaiśāradya­karītvād iti| siṃhavad atyantaniḥśaṃko yuktavādī viśāradaḥ| tasya bhāvo vaiśāradyam| catvāri vaiśāradyāni| abhi­saṃbodhi­vaiśāradyam| āśravakṣayavaiśāradyam| āntarāyikadha­[rma]vaiśāradyam| nairyāṇika­dharma­vaiśāradyaṃ ca| abhisambodhis tathāgatasya jñānasampat| āśravakṣayaḥ prahāṇasampat| tad ubhayaṃ svārthaḥ| āntarāyikā dharmā nirvāṇasya vibandhakāḥ nairyāṇikā dharmā nirvāṇasya prāpakāḥ| etad ubhayaṃ parārthaḥ| parebhyas tadākhyānāt| teṣu vaiśāradyam| tatprati­jñāpratiṣṭhāpaneṣv ity arthaḥ| tatkar ā. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom serves as a protector to sentient beings who have no protector.According to Ratnākara (F.95.a–b), “sentient beings have no protector” because they been drift about in the ocean of suffering from time without beginning without anyone even to protect them. “Protector,” i.e., someone to protect them, refers to the Blessed Buddha. The “transcendent state of wisdom” is the source of that protector. Up to here in the chapter concerns the actualization of the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. With the following sentence, he begins to explain precisely (what it is like) to know everything. RK: anāthāḥ sattvā anādau duḥkhasāgare bhramatām eṣām adyāpi trātur abhāvāt| teṣāṃ nāthas trātā bhagavān buddhaḥ| tatkar ā| iyatā sarvajñajñānapariniṣpattir eṣeti samarthitaḥ| eṣaiva sarvajñatvam iti samarthayitum āha| saṃsāretyā di. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is the antidote to cyclic existence.According to Ratnākara (F.95.b), “cyclic existence” means the constituents with worldly influences. The “antidote” to that is what eliminates that. RK: saṃsāraḥ sāśravāḥ skandhāḥ| tasya pratipakṣā hantrī. In the light of it not being something permanent, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom makes apparent the true nature of all things.According to Ratnākara (F.95.b), someone might object that the transcendent state of wisdom is what eliminates that. To address this possible objection, he says “due to not being something permanent.” “Something permanent” here refers to the ultimate realm of things of all things (dharmadhātu) because it is a homogenous nature that has no beginning or end. If the ultimate realm of all things were the antidote, then there would be no cyclic existence from the beginning (because something permanent cannot produce different distinct effects). However, since the “transcendent state of wisdom” (is not something permanent like that), has a beginning, and leads one higher and higher, it alone is the antidote. This first half of the sentence expresses the accomplishment of relinquishing. The second half expresses the accomplishment of knowing. The transcendent state of wisdom “makes apparent” the ultimate “nature of all things” because it appears within those things. RK: dharmadhātur eva tasya hanteti ced āha| akūṭasthatām upādāye ti| dharmadhātur hi kūṭastho anādinidhanaikarūpatvāt| sacet pratipakṣaḥ syāt tadā ādita eva saṃsāro na syāt| prajñāpāramitā punar ādimatī, uttarottaragāminī ceti saiva tasya pratipakṣaḥ| anena prahāṇasampad uktā| jñānasampadam āha sarvetyādinā| sarvadharmā ṇāṃ svabhāvaḥ paramārthaḥ| tasya vidarśanī tatpratibhāsatvāt. Ratnākara’s idea of the transcendent state of wisdom (in his final comment just above) can be compared to the illumination of images on a movie screen. The illumination is what makes the movie images appear, and the illumination itself (which is the nature of all the movie images) is apparent within those images shining forth. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is the instigator of the blessed buddhas fully setting in motion the wheel of Dharma that has three phases and twelve aspects.According to Ratnākara (F.95.b–96.a), “fully” means due to not lacking even one single thing. The “Dharma that has three sections and twelve aspects” refers to the teachings. Those themselves are (likened to) a “wheel” because they go round and round in the mindstreams of disciples. The “transcendent state of wisdom” is the “instigator” for setting those in motion, because it is due to it that the buddhas do so. The Dharma wheel consists in the four noble truths (1–4), each of which has three phases (a–c, making a total of twelve aspects). The four truths present the teachings as follows: “This is suffering. This is the source of suffering. This is the cessation of suffering. This is the path leading to the cessation of suffering.” The three phases of each of these four are (a) the phase of determining the nature of each of the four truths; (b) the phase of determining, respectively (for each truth), what is to be fully acknowledged, fully abandoned, actualized, and cultivated; and (c) the phase of determining what has been fully acknowledged, fully abandoned, actualized, and cultivated. Thus, subdividing the four truths by the three phases, the twelve aspects are, (1a–c) regarding the truth of suffering, the phase of determining its nature, what is to be fully acknowledged, and what has been fully acknowledged; (2a–c) regarding the truth of the source, the phase of determining its nature, what is to be fully abandoned, and what has been fully abandoned; (3a–c) regarding the truth of the cessation, the phase of determining its nature, what is to be actualized, and what has been actualized; and (4a–c) regarding the truth of the path, the phase of determining its nature, what is to be actualized, and what has been actualized. (The three phases and twelve aspects are set out and mentioned as such in the several canonical accounts of the Buddha’s first teaching in Sarnath. See, for example, The Sūtra of the Wheel of Dharma, 1.13.) RK: paripūrṇe tyādi paripūrṇam ekenāpy anūnatvāt, triparivartadvādaśākāraṃ ca yad dharmacakram dharmaḥ śāsanam tad eva cakraṃ, vineyasantāneṣu caṃkramaṇāt| tasya pravartan o pravartayitrī| buddhānāṃ tathaiva (tayaiva?) tasya pravartanāt| tatra dharmacakraṃ satyacatuṣṭayam| tasya trayaḥ parivartāḥ| idaṃ duḥkham| ayaṃ duḥkhasamudayaḥ| ayaṃ duḥkhanirodhaḥ| iyaṃ duḥkhanirodhagāminī pratipad iti| satyacatuṣṭayasya svabhāvaparicchedaḥ prathamaḥ parivartaḥ| tasyaiva yathākramaṃ pari­jñeyapraheya­sākṣātkartavya­bhāvayitavyatva­paricchedo dvitīyaḥ parivartaḥ| tasyaivātmanā pari­jñāta­prahīṇa­sākṣātkṛta­bhāvitatva­paricchedas tṛtīyaḥ| tasmāt triparivartaṃ tat| duḥkhasatyasya duḥkhasvabhāvaḥ parijñeyatvaṃ parijñātatvaṃ ceti traya ākārāḥ| samudayasatyasya tatsvabhāvaḥ praheyatvaṃ prahīṇatvaṃ ceti traya ākārāḥ| nirodhasatyasya tat svabhāvaḥ sākṣātkartavyatvaṃ sākṣātkṛtatvaṃ ceti traya ākārāḥ| mārgasatyasya tat svabhāvo bhāvayitavyatvaṃ bhāvitatvaṃ ceti traya ākārāḥ| tasmāt dvādaśākāraṃ tat. How does one dwell in the transcendent state of wisdom? How should one keep in mind the transcendent state of wisdom? How does one pay homage to the transcendent state of wisdom?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Śāriputra, “Śāriputra, one should dwell in the transcendent state of wisdom in exactly the same way that one dwells in the presence of the Teacher. Śāriputra, one should keep in mind the transcendent state of wisdom in exactly the same way that one keeps in mind the Teacher. Śāriputra, one should pay homage to the transcendent state of wisdom in exactly the same way that one pays homage to the Teacher.”According to Ratnākara (F.96.b), “dwell” means practice; “keep in mind” means recollect the qualities through venerating with offerings; and “pay homage” means through body, speech, and mind. RK: sthātavyam iti vartitavyam| pūjābhiḥ manasikartavyā guṇānusmaraṇaiḥ| namaskartavyā kāyavākcittaiḥ.

At that time, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, wondered from where exactly and on what basis the noble Śāriputra’s question arose. So, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the venerable Śāriputra, “From where exactly has noble Śāriputra’s question arisen? On the basis of what exactly has noble Śāriputra’s question arisen?”

Then Śāriputra replied to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, my question arose in consideration of the following. When supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means, a bodhisattva great being who dedicates the thing that is producing the merit associated with rejoicing accumulates merit that far surpasses the merit accumulated through generosity, ethical discipline, tolerant acceptance, heroic perseverance, and meditation by those previously mentioned bodhisattvas who cling to a mental perception. Yet again, Kauśika, the transcendent state of wisdom takes the fore over the five transcendent states in leading someone to travel the path to the state of complete knowing. Kauśika, imagine, as an analogy, one hundred, one thousand, or one hundred thousand people who were blind from birth. Without a guide they would never be able to travel a path on their own to arrive at any village, city, or town. Kauśika, just like those who are blind from birth, the so-called ‘transcendent states’ of generosity, ethical discipline, tolerant acceptance, heroic perseverance, and meditation receive their name from the transcendent state of wisdom, because without the transcendent state of wisdom they would all lack a guide and be unable to travel the path to the state of complete knowing. But, how does each of these lead to attaining the state of complete knowing? Kauśika, it is only when generosity, ethical discipline, tolerant acceptance, heroic perseverance, and meditation are supported by the transcendent state of wisdom that they are designated or called ‘transcendent states.’ In other words, this is when the five blind transcendent states gain their eyesight, so that they lead to traveling the path to the state of complete knowing and to attaining the state of complete knowing.”
Then the venerable Śāriputra asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, how should the transcendent state of wisdom be accomplished?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Śāriputra, “Śāriputra, one should observe the nonaccomplishment of physical forms. Śāriputra, one should observe the nonaccomplishment of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. Śāriputra, the nonaccomplishment of the five constituents of experience is called the accomplishment of the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Hari (385), since the constituents are like an illusion, attaining them means accomplishing the transcendent state of wisdom. Hari: rūpasye tyādi. māyopamatayā rūpāder adhigamo mātur abhinirhāra ity arthaḥ. Thus, by not mentally constructing anything, the accomplishment of the five constituents is called the accomplishment of the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.96.b), here the “transcendent state of wisdom” refers to the path of cultivation. That which is not mentally constructing, i.e., not mentally perceiving all things, is the accomplishment, i.e., actualization, of the transcendent state of wisdom. RK: prajñāpāramite ti bhāvanāmārgaḥ| yaḥ pañcānāṃ skandhānāṃ yāvat sarvadharmāṇām anabhisaṃskāro ’nupalambhaḥ so ’syābhinirhāraḥ pariniṣpādanam. According to Hari (385), “not mentally constructing anything” means applying oneself toward attainment through the nonarising of any particular thing, insofar as its nature is the actual nature. Hari notes further that, although some Sanskrit manuscripts read here “by not accomplishing” (instead of “by not mentally constructing”), that phrase too should be understood as having the very same meaning he just explained. Hari: tathatārūpatvāt sarva­dharma­viśeṣānutpādanenādhigama­prayogo ’nabhisaṃskāras tena skandhānām adhigama iti yāvat. kvacid anabhinirhāreṇe ti pāṭhaḥ, tatrāpy ayam evārtho grāhyaḥ.

At this, the venerable Śāriputra asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, when accomplished by such an accomplishment, what thing does the transcendent state of wisdom confer?”According to Ratnākara (F.96.b), “what thing” should be understood as what thing that has no worldly influences (i.e., is beneficial). RK: katamaṃ dharmam ity anāśravaḥ| na kaścid dharmam iti dharmāṇām arpaṇāyāś cānupalambhād ity arpaṇā. According to Hari (385), Śāriputra asks this for the sake of the conferral of realization by not mentally perceiving any things. Hari: sarvadharmānupalambhenābhisamayārpaṇārtham āha: evam abhinirhṛte tyādi.

The Blessed One said, “When accomplished in this way, the transcendent state of wisdom confers nothing. And, Śāriputra, when it confers nothing, it is considered to be the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.96.b), he says “nothing” because there is no mental perception of it conferring things. RK: na kaścid dharmam iti dharmāṇām arpaṇāyāś cānupalambhād ity arpaṇā. According to Hari (385), it “confers nothing” means that it causes a certain thing to appear in a practitioner’s mindstream as an erroneous mental perception and so on. Then “it is considered the transcendent state of wisdom” because (by understanding the mental perception to be erroneous) the transcendent state of wisdom is not erroneous‍—this is the idea here. Hari: na kaṃcid dharmam arpayatī ti yathopalambhādiviparyāso bhavati tathā kaṃcid dharmaṃ yogisaṃtāne samutpādayati. tadā prajñāpāramiteti saṃkhyāṃ gacchaty, aviparyastatvād iti matiḥ.
Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, does the transcendent state of wisdom not confer even the state of complete knowing?”

The Blessed One said, “Kauśika, you ask, ‘Does the transcendent state of wisdom not confer even the state of complete knowing?’ It does not confer it in the manner of a mental perception. It does not confer it in the manner of a conception. And it does not confer it in the manner of a mental construction.”According to Ratnākara (F.97.a), in the manner of a “mental perception” means as a form grasped by the foolish. “Conception” means a label. “Mental construction” means a fabrication. RK: upalambho bālagrāhyeṇa rūpeṇa| nāma saṃjñā| [ abhi ] saṃskāraḥ kalpanā. According to Hari (386), “mental perception” means a mental perception of an external thing. “Name” refers to the four immaterial (i.e., mental) constituents. “Mental construction” means mental effort, thinking “Since a certain thing is like an illusion, it is real in that way,” and so on. Hari: tatropalambho bāhyārthopalambhaḥ. nāma catvāro ’rūpiṇaḥ skandhāḥ. abhisaṃskāro māyopamatayā kaścid evaṃtattvam ityādicittābhogaḥ. Note: The translations of abhisaṃskāra and nāma follow Ratnākara who disagrees slightly with Hari about which of the terms’ different meanings are most applicable here.
“Then, Blessed One, in what manner does it confer it?” asked Śakra.

“Kauśika,” said the Blessed One, “it confers it in the manner that it confers nothing.”According to Ratnākara (F.97.a), in this reply, “it confers nothing” due to a practitioner not conceptualizing the conferral of things. “The manner in which it confers it” is through a practitioner not conceptualizing anything. RK: uttaraṃ yathe ty ādinā yathā nārpayatī ti dharmāṇām arpaṇāyāś cāvikalpanāt| tathe ti avikalpanayā.

Śakra said, “It is extraordinary, Blessed One, the extent to which the transcendent state of wisdom does not cause anything to arise or cease,According to Ratnākara (F.97.a), it “does not cause anything to arise” because of not conceptualizing any things that arise and so on‍—this is the idea. RK: na kañcid ity ādi| dharmāṇām utpādādīnāṃ cāvikalpanād iti bhāvaḥ. and to which, regarding the nonarising and nonceasing of all things, the transcendent state of wisdom takes part yet does not take part.”sarvadharmāṇāmanutpādāya anirodhāya pratyupasthitā anupasthitā prajñāpāramitā. chos thams cad skye ba ma mchis pa dang/ ’gogs pa ma mchis pa’i slad du nye bar gnas la gnas pa’ang ma lags pa’i shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa. According to Ratnākara (F.97.a), it “does not take part” because it does not do anything. RK: ataś ca anupasthite ty akiñcitkaratvāt. Note that a possible alternative way of reading this passage might be to take pratyupasthitā to refer to anutpāda and anupasthitā to refer to anirodha: “takes part in the non-arising of all things, and does not take part in their nonceasing.” In context, however, such an interpretation would seem out of place. For comparison see the equivalent but rather different statements in the Twenty-Five Thousand (25.17–18) and Hundred Thousand (25.139–140), in which parts of Śakra’s statement appear instead in Subhūti’s following remark about such perspectives being misleading if taken as a way of notionally conceiving the transcendent state of wisdom.

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, if a bodhisattva great being were nevertheless to have conceptions of it being so, he or she would make the transcendent state of wisdom distant, make it useless, and make it futile. That bodhisattva great being would not give rise to the transcendent state of wisdom.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “That is true, Subhūti. That is true. That is the way that they would make this transcendent state of wisdom distant, that they would make it useless, and that they would make it futile. They would not give rise to this transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, when the transcendent state of wisdom is elucidated, it is not elucidated as physical forms. It is not elucidated as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. It is not elucidated as a stream enterer’s fruit, or as a once-returner’s fruit, or as a non-returner’s fruit, or as a worthy one’s fruit, or as a solitary realizer’s fruit, or as a buddha’s fruit.”

The elder Subhūti said, “Blessed One, specifically, it is a great transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom.”Neither Ratnākara nor Hari comment here on the expression “specifically” (yad uta; ’di lta ste), which is used repeatedly throughout this passage. But since Ratnākara elsewhere (F.226.a) suggests that the expression highlights the particular quality of something that has been pointed out only generally, we can understand it to distinguish the transcendent state of wisdom from all other transcendent states in terms of its various particular qualities mentioned one by one below. Cf. RK: yaduta śabdaḥ sāmānyenoddiṣṭasya viśeṣaṃ dyotayati.
“Subhuti, what do you think?” asked the Blessed One. “In what way specifically is it a great transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom?”

The elder Subhūti said, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom neither increases nor decreases physical forms. It neither brings together nor disperses them. Likewise, it neither increases nor decreases feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. It neither brings together nor disperses them. The transcendent state of wisdom neither strengthens nor weakens the tathāgata-powers that belong to a tathāgata. It neither brings together nor disperses them. Furthermore, it neither increases nor decreases the state of complete knowing. It neither brings together nor disperses it. Why is that? Because, Blessed One, the state of complete knowing is neither brought together nor dispersed. Imagine that a bodhisattva great being were even to conceive of it in such a way. Or, worse still, imagine that a bodhisattva great being were to conceive of it as follows: ‘Once endowed with the knowing of someone who has complete knowing, I will teach the Dharma for the benefit of sentient beings, and in this way, I will cause them to attain complete nirvāṇa.’ In this case, that bodhisattva great being would not be going about his or her life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Why not? Because that mental perception of a sentient being‍—namely, ‘I am someone who will cause these sentient beings to attain complete nirvāṇa’‍—is not a natural outcome of the transcendent state of wisdom. It itself is a gross mental perception of a sentient being. How so? Because it should be known that the transcendent state of wisdom is free of the inherent nature of a sentient being and free of giving rise to one.According to Ratnākara (F.97.a), “sentient being” means the substance of a person. In the phrases “free of the inherent nature” or “free of giving rise to one” like that (i.e., like the substance of a person), the term “inherent nature” refers to a defining characteristic, and “giving rise” means producing. The transcendent state of wisdom is free of both of these. RK: sattvaḥ pudgaladravyaṃ| tadvad asvabhāvajātikā| svabhāvaḥ lakṣaṇam| jātir utpattiḥ| tābhyāṃ rahitā. The transcendent state of wisdom’s lack of an inherent nature should be known as a sentient being’s lack of an inherent nature. The transcendent state of wisdom’s voidness should be known as a sentient being’s voidness. The transcendent state of wisdom’s inconceivability should be known as a sentient being’s inconceivability. The transcendent state of wisdom’s indestructible true nature should be known as a sentient being’s indestructible true nature. The transcendent state of wisdom’s nonrealization should be known as a sentient being’s nonrealization. The transcendent state of wisdom’s nonactualization of any real thing should be known as a sentient being’s nonactualization of any real thing. A tathāgata’s attainment of powers should be known as a sentient being’s attainment of powers. Thus it is, Blessed One, that specifically the transcendent state of wisdom should be known as the great transcendent state.”

Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being who develops firm conviction in this profound transcendent state of wisdom is never worried, doubtful, or confused. Blessed One, that bodhisattva great being understands this transcendent state of wisdom, learns it, and communicates it in terms of its meaning and its qualities.

“Can it be known where such a bodhisattva great being died before being reborn here? For how long a time has he or she been practicing?”It is this question of Śāriputra’s that opens Chapter 26 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and Chapter 35 in the Eighteen Thousand.

The Blessed One replied to the venerable Śāriputra, “Śāriputra, it should be known that only after serving and supplicating blessed buddhas in other world systems has any bodhisattva great being died there and been reborn here. Why is this? Śāriputra, it is only through serving and supplicating blessed buddhas in other world systems that a bodhisattva great being, who has died there and been reborn here, has come to be able to listen to this profound transcendent state of wisdom being discussed, taught, expounded, and demonstrated. After hearing this transcendent state of wisdom, a bodhisattva great being in the transcendent state of wisdom will give rise to a conception of the Teacher, thinking, ‘The Teacher is right in front of me. I see the Teacher,’ and thereby develop the proper mindset. And if that bodhisattva great being is immersed in listening, listens devotedly, and never interrupts the explanation as this transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, taught, expounded, and explained, then, Śāriputra, such a bodhisattva great being should be known as having been practicing for a long time. Śāriputra, such a bodhisattva great being should be known as having served many buddhas.”

Then the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “But, Blessed One, is it possible to listen to the transcendent state of wisdom, to consider it, to bear it in mind, to look into it, and to reflect on it?According to Ratnākara (F.98.b), “listen to it” means based on the words; “consider it” means based on the meaning; “bear it in mind” means for another time; “look into” it means when contemplating; and “reflect on” it means when cultivating it because of fixing it in the mind. RK: śakyā punar iti kāṅkṣā praśnaḥ| śrotuṃ śabdataḥ| upalakṣayitum arthataḥ| samanvāhartuṃ kālāntare smartum| upapādayituṃ cintākāle| upadhārayituṃ bhāvanākāle cetasi sthirīkaraṇāt. Is it possible to expound or learn it by means of this or that attribute, indication, or sign, thinking that the transcendent state of wisdom is this, that it is here, or that it is there?”According to Ratnākara (F.98.b), the pronoun “this” refers to what is appearing, “there” refers to the basis of what is appearing, and “by means of that” means by that which it is appearing. The “characteristic” is the defining characteristic, like, for instance, the dewlap of a cow and so on. The “indication” is an artificial mark like, for instance, the bell on a cow. The “sign” is an inherent quality like, for instance, the whiteness of a (white) cow. The word “expounded” means by others and “learned” means from someone else. RK: iyam iti pratibhāsamānā| ihāmutra ve ti pratibhāsamānādhārā| anene ti pratibhāsamānena| ākāreṇe ti lakṣaṇena| tadyathā gauḥ sāsnādimatvena| liṅgene ti kṛtrimeṇa cihnena| tadyathā gaurghaṇṭādinā| nimittene ti svābhāvikena cihnena| tadyathā gauḥśuklatvādinā| nirdeṣṭuṃ parasmaiḥ| śrotuṃ parasmāt.

“Absolutely not, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One. “It is impossible, Subhūti, to expound or learn the transcendent state of wisdom, or to consider it, to bear it in mind, to look into it, or to reflect upon it by means of any constituents underlying the clinging to existence, any elements, or any bases. Why is that so? Because it is devoid of all things. Subhūti, since it is absolutely devoid of all things, it is not possible to expound or learn the transcendent state of wisdom, to consider it, to pay attention to it, to look into it, or to reflect on it by means of the constituents underlying the clinging to existence, the elements, or the bases. Nor can the transcendent state of wisdom be comprehended anywhere other than the constituents underlying the clinging to existence, the elements, or the bases. Why is it not possible? Subhūti, that is because the constituents underlying the clinging to existence, the elements, and the bases are empty, void, and null. Hence, the transcendent state of wisdom and those very constituents underlying the clinging to existence, the elements, and the bases‍—insofar as they are empty and void‍—are something free of duality, and free from creating duality. Since they are null, nothing is mentally perceived. That freedom from mental perception of any things is called the transcendent state of wisdom. Since it is not a conception, a name, a term, or a conventional expression, it is called the transcendent state of wisdom.”
The elder Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, how long has such a bodhisattva great being, who has practiced for a long time, engaged in meditation in this profound transcendent state of wisdom?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhuti, it can be broken down and analyzed according to the differences in faculties among bodhisattva great beings. But, Subhuti, after having seen many hundreds, thousands, and hundreds of thousands of buddhas, and having engaged in spiritual practice in their presence, certain bodhisattvas might still be unable to have trust or to have firm conviction in this transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? It is because they previously had no devotion when this profound transcendent state of wisdom was being discussed, taught, expounded, and demonstrated. Since they lacked devotion, they were not eager to learn. Since they were not eager to learn, they did not serve. Since they did not serve, they did not ask about it. Since they did not ask about it, they did not have faith. Since they did not have faith, for that reason they drifted away from the assemblies.According to Ratnākara (F.99.b), “for that reason” means due to lacking faith. RK: tata iti śraddhāviyogāt. According to Hari (397), they “drifted away” because of their distracted mindset‍—this must be supplied to express the meaning. Hari: tataḥ parṣadbhyo ’pakrāntā iti pratikṣepa­citteneti śeṣaḥ. In connection with that, they performed the karmic deed of casting aside the Dharma, repeated it, concluded it, and reinforced it.According to Ratnākara (F.99.b), “the karmic deed of casting aside (vyasana) the Dharma” means dismissing the genuine Dharma. Each of them “performed it” because they did the karmic deed once; “repeated it” because they did it again and again; “concluded it” because they did it fully; and “reinforced it” because of gaining the potential to give fruit that will ripen. RK: dharma­vyasanasaṃvartanīyaṃ karma saddharmapratikṣepaḥ| kṛta ṃ sakṛtkaraṇāt| sañcitaṃ punaḥ punaḥ karaṇāt| ācitaṃ paripūrṇam| upacitaṃ vipākadānaśaktilābhāt. And, having done so, they are drifting away even right now as the profound transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, taught, expounded, and demonstrated. Since they lack reverence, faith, and firm conviction, they are not fully collected in body and mind.According to Ratnākara (F.99.b), “fully collected in body” means coming to listening (to the teachings), and “collected in mind” means undistracted. RK: kāyena sāmagrī śrotum āgamanam| cittena nirvikṣepatā. By not being fully collected, they neither perceive this transcendent state of wisdom, nor see it, nor understand it, nor make it an experience.According to Ratnākara (F.99.b), “neither perceive” means in terms of the text, “nor see” means in terms of its meaning, and “nor understand” means in terms of the distinctions between the three natures. Based on that, they do not “make it an experience” due to their misunderstanding. RK: na jānanti granthataḥ| na paśyanti arthataḥ| na budhyante svabhāvatrayabhedena| tato na vedayante ’viparītabodhāt. According to Hari (397), “neither perceive, nor see, nor understand” means, respectively, through the three ways of validly cognizing, i.e., direct perception, inference, and received tradition (āgama). For the phrase “make it an experience,” one should supply the phrase “for others.” Hari: pratyakṣānumānāgama­pramāṇair yathāsaṃkhyaṃ na jānanti na paśyanti na budhyante. ata eva na vedayante parān iti śeṣaḥ. Note: The translation leans toward Ratnākara who appears to interpret vedayante differently from Hari. Cf. MW vedayante, “to experience,” “to make known.” In this way, they do not have faith in the transcendent state of wisdom. Because they do not have faith, they do not listen. Since they do not listen, they do not perceive. Since they do not perceive, they do not see. Since they do not see, they do not understand. Since they do not understand, they perform the karmic deed of casting aside the Dharma, repeat it, perfect it, and increase it. By doing so, they become conditioned to do the karmic deed leading to inferior wisdom. After becoming conditioned to do karmic deeds leading to inferior wisdom, repeating, perfecting, and piling them up further as this transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, taught, expounded, and demonstrated, they will come to dismiss it, repudiate it, and criticize it. Then, after repudiating it, they will drift away from it. But, Subhūti, by dismissing, repudiating, and criticizing this transcendent state of wisdom, they come to dismiss, repudiate, and criticize the state of complete knowing of the blessed buddhas of the past, future, and present. Having corrupted and scorched their own mindstreams, they will discourage other persons, deter them, and turn away anyone who has little presence of mind, little wisdom, little virtue, or a small root of virtue; who, among those undeveloped beginners with little faith, are barely faithful; who, among those undeveloped beginners with little love, are barely loving; who, among those undeveloped beginners with little trust, are barely trusting; and who, among those undeveloped beginners with little eagerness, are barely eager.This translation follows the Sanskrit which differs slightly from the Tibetan (F.101.b). They will say, ‘You should not train in this.’ They will make such claims as, ‘This is not the speech of the Buddha.’ In this way, once they have corrupted their own proper mindstreams and deterred themselves, and once they have corrupted others’ mindstreams and deterred them too, they will come to dismiss the transcendent state of wisdom. When they dismiss the transcendent state of wisdom, they are dismissing the state of complete knowing. When they are dismissing the state of complete knowing, they are dismissing the blessed buddhas of the past, future, and present. They will become withdrawn from the presence of the blessed buddhas, completely removed from the Dharma, and completely excluded from the community. Thus, they will come to be completely outside the reach of the Three Jewels in absolutely every way by absolutely all means. Through the actions that disrupt the welfare and happiness of sentient beings, they will perform karmic deeds leading to the ripened result of the great hells. By performing and accomplishing such accumulations of karmic deeds‍—that is to say, the karmic deeds leading to casting aside the Dharma and the karmic deeds leading to inferior wisdom‍—they will be reborn in the great hells for many hundreds, many thousands, many hundreds of thousands, many billions, many tens of billions, many trillions, and many tens of trillions of years.According to Ratnākara (F.100.a), “accumulations of karmic deeds” refers to the activity of karmic deeds. “Performing” means doing and “accomplishing” means increasing. RK: karmābhisaṃskāraḥ karmakriyā| upasthāpitene ti kṛtena| samutthāpitene ty upacitena. They will wander from great hell to great hell. For those who have wandered from great hell to great hell for a very long time in that way, the dissolution of the universe by fire will come to manifest.According to Ratnākara (F.100.a), “dissolution of the universe” refers to the destruction of the universe (at the end of an eon). In it, everything from the level of the Uninterrupted Hell up to the Brahmā Heaven and so on is destroyed by fire. RK: saṃvartanī pralayaḥ, āvīcitalādā ca brahmalokādy asyām agninā kṣīyate sā tejaḥsaṃvartanī. When their world’s dissolution by fire has manifested, they will be ejected from that world system into the great hells in other world systems and they will be reborn in those great hells.According to Ratnākara (F.100.a), they are “ejected” with a body of the intermediate state and “are reborn” with a body for their state of rebirth. RK: kṣepsyante antarābhavaśarīreṇa| upapatsyante upapattibhavaśarīreṇa.

“Once ejected and reborn in the great hells, they will continue to wander even there from great hell to great hell. For those who have wandered from great hell to great hell even there, once again in just the same way, the dissolution of the universe by fire will manifest. When their world’s dissolution by fire has manifested, those who have died will be ejected from that world system into the great hells of other world systems and they will be reborn in those great hells.

“Even there, they will wander from great hell to great hell in just the same way. For those who have wandered even there from great hell to great hell for a very long time, the dissolution of the universe by fire will manifest in just the same way. When that world’s dissolution by fire has manifested in that way, those who have died once again will be ejected again from that into this very same world system because of the very same karmic deed, which will remain undestroyed. Being ejected, they will be reborn in the great hells. They will again and again experience those sufferings of the great hells. They will experience [this] again and again as long as the world’s dissolution by fire is manifesting. In this way, they will experience their karmic deed as causing pain multiple times. Why is that? Simply because of the harmful words they spoke.”

Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the five karmic deeds that ripen immediately are not even a semblance, a mere appearance, or a mere reflection of this type of mental and verbal harmful conduct.”According to Hari (397), “the five karmic deeds that ripen immediately” are (1) killing one’s mother, (2) killing one’s father, (3) killing a worthy one (arhat), (4) causing a split in the community, and (5) shedding the blood of a tathāgata with malicious mindset. These deeds are said to “ripen immediately” because they cause rebirth immediately (after death) in the Uninterrupted (avīci) Great Hell. Hari: pañce ti mātṛvadhaḥ pitṛvadho ’rhadvadhaḥ saṃghabhedas tathāgata­duṣṭa­citta­rudhirotpādaś cety etāni pañca karmāṇy anantaram avīcimahāniraye janmahetutvād ānantaryāṇi.

“It is true, Śāriputra. It is true,” said the Blessed One. “The five karmic deeds that ripen immediately are not even a semblance, a mere appearance, or a mere reflection of this type of mental and verbal harmful conduct‍—that is to say, the karmic deed performed, repeated, concluded, and reinforced. When this transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, taught, expounded, and demonstrated, certain listeners will consider it something to be rejected, dismissed, repudiated, and criticized. They will say, ‘You should not train in this.’ They will make the claim, ‘This is not the speech of a tathāgata.’ They will also deter other sentient beings from it. Having corrupted their own mindstreams, they will corrupt the mindstreams of others. Having poisoned their own mindstreams, they will poison the mindstreams of others. Having damaged themselves, they will also damage others. Not knowing and not understanding the profound transcendent state of wisdom themselves, they will seize upon others, saying the words, ‘You should not train in this.’ Śāriputra, I do not recommend even seeing such people. Why associate with them? Why honor them and provide them material support? Why stay with them? What reason is there? Śāriputra, such persons should be known as harmers of the Dharma. Śāriputra, such persons should be known as being like trees whose trunks are rotten.According to Ratnākara (F.100.a), these are types of persons who are “like” completely putrid “trees whose trunks are rotten” (kasambaka). When not eliminated, it also destroys leaves other than its own. RK: kasambakaṃ pūtipūrṇam| tadatyaktam anyāny api parṇāni nāśayati| jātir jātam | kasambaka vaj jātam eṣām iti kasambakajātāḥ. Cf. BHSD 174, kaśambakajātāḥ, “tree that is rotten inside.” Śāriputra, such persons should be known as poisonous black snakes emerging from virulent black weeds.According to Ratnākara (F.100.a), “black” indicates a special type of grass, called kaṇḍā (probably a type of bamboo) in Prākṛit language. If it is not removed from a field, it also destroys the field. “Emerging” means coming out of. These black snakes are poisonous black vipers that attack, bite, and poison (their victims). RK: kṛṣṇā tṛṇaviśeṣaḥ prākṛtabhāṣayā kaṇḍā| sāpi kṣetrād anapanītā kṣetraṃ nāśayati| nirjātir niṣpattiḥ| kṛṣṇāvan nirjātir eṣām iti kṛṣṇānirjātikāḥ| kṛṣṇāhi kṛṣṇasarpaḥ saviṣaḥ saṃsraṣṭā daṣṭvā saviṣ īkaroti. Śāriputra, any persons who consider listening to them will meet with misfortune through their misguidance.In this sentence, there is a slight play on different usages of the same Sanskrit word vyasana, implying that the karmic effect is similar to the cause, i.e., the karmic deed of “casting aside” (vyasana) leads to “misfortune” (vyasana). According to Ratnākara (F.100.a), “through their misguidance” means through their not following a path with faith, learning, and so on. “Misfortune” refers here to the suffering of the hells and so on. RK: anayene ti tebhyaḥ śrutaśraddhādinā ’mārgeṇa| vyasanaṃ nirayādiduḥkham. Śāriputra, those who harm the transcendent state of wisdom should be known as harmers of the Dharma.”
Śāriputra said, “The Blessed One has not explained how much such a person’s body suffers when reborn in the great hells.”

The Blessed One said, “Śāriputra, I will not mention how much such a person’s body suffers when reborn in the great hells. Why not? People should not hear how much such a person’s body suffers when reborn in the great hells, because just hearing it might cause hot blood to spurt out of their mouth.According to Hari (400), the Buddha does not explain it, because hearing the precise extent of suffering to a body in the great hell‍—i.e., having the greatness of suffering explained‍—might become a cause for “hot blood to spurt out” and so on, due to their extremely fearful and forceful reactions against violent behavior. This sentence is an overview of what follows. Hari: yasmān mahānārakakāyapramāṇaṃ śrutvā duḥkhamahattvaṃ pratipadya mahābhayena sahasā prati­pakṣa­samudācārādi­virodhenoṣṇa­rudhirāgamanādayaḥ syus tasmān nākhyāyata iti samudāyārthaḥ. Alternatively, they might die or be brought to the brink of death, afflicted with intense agony, burnt by fire, or pierced by glowing hot pokers. Or else, they might fall off of a great cliff, or just shrivel up and fade away. In order to prevent them suffering such great calamities, it is best not to hear how much such a person’s body suffers when encountering these negative consequences.”

The Blessed One did not give in to the venerable Śāriputra by telling him how much such a person’s body suffers. Śāriputra asked the Blessed One a second and even a third time: “Blessed One, please tell me how much such a person’s body suffers. It will light the lamp for future generations to know the way that such a person’s body gets ensnared in the great hells because mental and verbal karmic deeds have been performed, repeated, concluded, and reinforced.”According to Ratnākara (F.100.a), “the lamp” is knowing of the ripened results of the deeds discussed in the passage. RK: ālokaḥ prakṛtasya karmaṇo vipākajñānam.

The Blessed One said, “Śāriputra, the lamp has already been lit for future generations to know for how long a time such a person’s body suffers because of the accumulation of harmful karmic deeds through harmful mental and verbal conduct being performed, repeated, concluded, and reinforced. My explanation of the immeasurability of that extremely painful suffering should light up the lamp for any virtuous noble son or daughter, thinking, ‘I do not wish to meet any sufferings of this kind.’According to Hari (400), “the lamp for future generations” refers to the lamp of Dharma because of causing revulsion (saṃvega) toward doing negative deeds. Since it is the cause of lamp light, it is described (and translated here figuratively) as “lighting” the lamp. RK: paścimāyā janatāyā āloka iti aśuklakarmakāriṇaḥ saṃvegotpādād dharmālokaḥ| saṃvega iti taddhetutvāt tathoktaḥ. Based on that alone, he or she would refrain from any karmic deeds harming the Dharma, he or she would perform only accumulations of merit, and he or she would never go against the genuine Dharma even at the cost of his or her life.”

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, a noble son or daughter would show proper restraint in physical karmic deeds, verbal karmic deeds, and mental karmic deeds.According to Hari (400–1), this is said because they would perform positive physical, verbal, and mental deeds based on mindfulness and clear comprehension in sequential order. Hari: susaṃvṛta­kāya­karma­vākkarma­manaskarmaṇe ti yathākramaṃ smṛti­saṃprajanyatām upādāya kāyikavācika­mānasika­kuśalānuṣṭhānād uktam. Why so? Because, Blessed One, such a great mass of demerit is produced by speaking such harmful words.According to Hari (401), “such harmful words” here refers to saying things like “this is not the teaching of the Buddha.” Since it is merely implied here but not expressed, one should keep in mind that improper physical and mental deeds are also to be restrained. Hari: evaṃrūpeṇe ti naitad buddhavacanam ity evaṃsvabhāvena. asyopalakṣaṇamātratvāt viparītakāya­manas­karmaṇāpīti jñeyaṃ. Yet, Blessed One, which karmic deeds produce such a great mass of demerit?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, such a great mass of demerit is produced by speaking harmful words in the following way. Subhūti, certain confused persons right here‍—who are renunciants in the well-explained monastic discipline of the Dharma‍—will consider the profound transcendent state of wisdom something to be repudiated, to be denied, and to be rejected.According to Hari (402), “to be repudiated” means by announcing there to be contradictions between one passage and another; “to be denied” means as explained previously (by denying the truth of it); and “to be rejected” means due to refutations of the means of valid cognition such as direct perception and so on. Hari: dūṣayitavyām iti paraspara­virodhodbhāvanayā. pratikṣeptavyām iti pūrvavat. pratibādhitavyām iti pratyakṣādi­pramāṇa­bādhanāt. Note: “by denying the truth of it” comes from Hari (397) sāmarthyāpahnavāt pratikṣepsyanti. And, when the transcendent state of wisdom is rejected, the buddha’s awakening of the blessed buddhas is rejected. When the buddha’s awakening is rejected, the state of complete knowing of the blessed buddhas of the past, future, and present is rejected. When the state of complete knowing of the blessed buddhas of the past, future, and present is rejected, the genuine Dharma is rejected. When the genuine Dharma is rejected, the disciple community of the Tathāgata is rejected. When the disciple community of the Tathāgata also is rejected, those confused persons come to be completely outside the reach of the Three Jewels in absolutely every way by absolutely all means, and fully embrace the utterly immeasurable and innumerable accumulation of nonvirtuous karmic deeds.”
At this, the Venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “What will be the cause or condition for such a noble son or daughter to consider this transcendent state of wisdom something to be rejected?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, such a noble son or daughter will be controlled by Māra. Alternatively, he or she will lack faith and trust in profound truths because of his or her former karmic deeds leading to inferior wisdom. Then, Subhūti, endowed with these two negative qualities, such a noble son or daughter will reject this transcendent state of wisdom. Moreover, such a noble son or daughter will fall into the hands of unvirtuous friends, will not be committed to practice, will become fixated on the constituents, and will seek out different weaknesses to put others down while raising themselves up.According to Hari (403), “four characteristics” refers to the four causes of rejection: (1) being under the influence of Māra, (2) having no firm conviction in the profound truth, (3) being fixated upon the constituents and so on, and (4) embracing an unvirtuous spiritual friend. Hari: caturbhir ākārair iti mārādhiṣṭhāna-gambhīra­dharmānadhimokṣa-skandhādyabhiniveśa- pāpamitra­parigrahaiś caturbhiḥ pratikṣepahetubhir ity arthaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.100.b), when the Blessed One explains the four causes, the phrase “seek out different weaknesses to put others down while raising themselves up” refers to one of four. For this reason, he says the following sentence. The causes of casting aside (vyasana) the Dharma are taught to total six in number, but as abbreviated in the Ornament (AA 2.26–2.27), there are “four” mentioned here, because the other ones are included in the “and so on” of the phrase “being fixated upon the constituents and so on.” RK: punar aparam ityādinā caturo hetūnāha| ātmotkarṣī parapaṃsako doṣāntaraprekṣīty eko rāśiḥ| ata evāha| ebhir api caturbhir ityādi| tadatra dharmavyasanahetavaḥ ṣaḍ uktāḥ| śāstre tu saṃkṣepataś catvāraḥ| śeṣāṇāṃ skandhādyabhiniveśe ’ntarbhāvāt. Subhūti, any noble son or daughter with all four of these symptoms will consider this transcendent state of wisdom something to be rejected, when it is being discussed, taught, expounded, and demonstrated.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Hell,” the seventh chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 8: PurityThe beginning of this chapter corresponds to a point part of the way through Chapter 26 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (starting at 26.25) and Hundred Thousand (starting at 26.29), and of Chapter 35 in the Eighteen Thousand (starting at 35.23). This chapter also includes the entire content of Chapter 27 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand and Chapter 36 in the Eighteen Thousand‍—see n.533; and part of the content of Chapter 28 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (down to and including 28.41) and Hundred Thousand (down to and including 28.278), and of Chapter 37 in the Eighteen Thousand (down to and including 37.36)‍—see n.552.
div.p: The venerable Subhūti then said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, it is difficult to have firm conviction in the transcendent state of wisdom if someone is not committed to practice, is devoid of the roots of virtue, or has fallen into the hands of unvirtuous friends.”

“True, Subhūti. It is exactly as you say,” said the Blessed One. “It is difficult to have firm conviction in the transcendent state of wisdom if someone is not committed to practice, has inadequate roots of virtue, is dull-minded, is listless, has little learning, has inferior wisdom, is reliant upon unvirtuous friends, is uninterested in learning, is an uninquisitive type, and is not committed to developing virtuous qualities.”According to Ratnākara (F.100.b), “listless” means weak-willed; “reliant upon” means confused by. RK: anarthikene ti mandacchandena| upastabdho vismitaḥ.
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, how profound is the transcendent state of wisdom, since it is so difficult to have firm conviction in it?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, physical forms are neither fettered nor liberated. Why not? Subhūti, physical forms are neither fettered nor liberated because they do not have the inherent nature of physical forms. Likewise, Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness too are neither fettered nor liberated. Why not? Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are neither fettered nor liberated because they do not have the inherent nature of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.

“Subhūti, the past of physical forms is neither fettered nor liberated. Why not? Because, Subhūti, physical forms do not have the inherent nature of the past. Subhūti, the future of physical forms is neither fettered nor liberated. Why not? Because, Subhūti, physical forms do not have the inherent nature of the future. Subhūti, the present of physical forms is neither fettered nor liberated. Why not? Because, Subhūti, physical forms do not have the inherent nature of the present. Likewise, Subhūti, the past of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is neither fettered nor liberated. Why not? Because, Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness do not have the inherent nature of the past. Subhūti, the future of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is neither fettered nor liberated. Why not? Because, Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness do not have the inherent nature of the future. Subhūti, the present of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is neither fettered nor liberated. Why not? Because, Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness do not have the inherent nature of the present.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, it is difficult to have firm conviction in the transcendent state of wisdom. Blessed One, it is supremely difficult to have firm conviction in the transcendent state of wisdom if someone is not committed to practice, has not planted the roots of virtue, has fallen into the hands of unvirtuous friends, has come under the influence of Māra, is lazy, has lackluster perseverance, is robbed of mindfulness, and has inferior wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.101.a), the idea is that it is “supremely difficult to have conviction” in it because it is supremely profound. It is well known that a fetter that is previously fastened refers to a former impurity, whereas liberation, i.e., the subsequent removal of that fetter, refers to purity. But in this case purity means that both the fetter and the liberation are absolutely nonexistent. In this sense, the transcendent state of wisdom’s characteristic of purity is supremely profound. RK: paramaduradhimoc eti paramagambhīratvād iti bhāvaḥ| prākpravṛtto hi bandhaḥ prāgaśuddhiḥ paścāttannivṛttilakṣaṇo mokṣaḥ śuddhir iti prasiddham| iha tu bandhamokṣayor atyantābhāvaḥ śuddhir iti paramagambhīraṃ viśuddhilakṣaṇaṃ.

“True, Subhūti. It is exactly as you say,” said the Blessed One. “Subhūti, it is difficult to have firm conviction in the transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, it is supremely difficult to have firm conviction in the transcendent state of wisdom if someone is not committed to practice, has not planted the roots of virtue, has fallen into the hands of unvirtuous friends, has come under the influence of Māra, is lazy, has lackluster perseverance, is robbed of mindfulness, and has inferior wisdom. Why so? Subhūti, the purity of physical forms is the purity of the fruit, and the purity of the fruit is the purity of physical forms.The “the fruit” here refers to the fruitional factors or dharmas, i.e. the phenomena that belong to the sphere of awakening itself rather than that of saṃsāra or the path. That being said, Subhūti, the purity of physical forms and the purity of the fruit are free of duality, free from creating duality, not different, and not separate.According to Ratnākara (F.101.a), they are “free of duality” because of having the same nature, and “free from creating duality” because of not deviating (from that). The explanation of these first two is “not different, not separate.” RK: advayaṃ tādātmyāt| advaidhīkāram avyabhicārāt| anayor eva nirdeśo abhinnam acchinnam iti. That being said, Subhūti, the purity of physical forms is due to the purity of the fruit, and the purity of the fruit is due to the purity of physical forms. Likewise, Subhūti, the purity of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is the purity of the fruit, and the purity of the fruit is the purity of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. That being said, Subhūti, the purity of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness and the purity of the fruit are free of duality, free from creating duality, not different, and not separate. That being said, Subhūti, the purity of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is due to the purity of the fruit, and the purity of the fruit is due to the purity of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the purity of physical forms is the purity of the state of complete knowing, and the purity of the state of complete knowing is the purity of physical forms. That being said, Subhūti, the purity of physical forms and the purity of the state of complete knowing are free of duality, free from creating duality, not different, and not separate. That being said, Subhūti, the purity of physical forms is due to the purity of the state of complete knowing, and the purity of the state of complete knowing is due to the purity of physical forms.According to Ratnākara (F.101.b), here “the state of complete knowing” refers to the state of complete knowing of everything (i.e., the complete knowing of a buddha, not of a worthy one, arhat). That state of complete knowing of everything involves two kinds of purity. First, that which is the purity of the constituents one by one is the purity of the state of complete knowing of everything. Its purity is their purity. This is the first purity of the state of complete knowing of everything. Second, the purity of everything, one by one from the constituents up to the unmixed qualities of a buddha, which are the emptiness and so on, and the purity of the transcendent state of wisdom and of the state of complete knowing of everything‍—all this is “not different, not separate.” This is the second purity of the state of complete knowing of everything. This is not in the words of the sūtra, but through implication it is to be grasped. RK: sarvajñate ti sarvākārajñatā| tasyā viśuddhir dvividhā| yā skandhānām ekaśo viśuddhiḥ pāramitānāṃ ca sā sarvākāra­jñatāviśuddhiḥ| yā ca tasyāḥ sā teṣām ity ekā sarvākārajñatāviśuddhiḥ| adhyātmaśūnyatādīnāṃ skandhādīnāṃ ca yāvad āveṇika­buddha­dharmāṇam ekaśo yā viśuddhir yā ca prajñāpāramitāyā yā ca sarvākārajñatāyāḥ sarvam etad abhinnam acchinnam iti dvitīyā sarvākāra­jñatāviśuddhiḥ| tad asyāṃ na paṭhyate upalakṣaṇatvāt tad grāhyam. Likewise, Subhūti, the purity of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is the purity of the state of complete knowing, and the purity of the state of complete knowing is the purity of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. That being said, Subhūti, the purity of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness and the purity of the state of complete knowing are free of duality, free from creating duality, not different, and not separate. That being said, Subhūti, the purity of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is due to the purity of the state of complete knowing, and the purity of the state of complete knowing is due to the purity of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.”

Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound.”This statement of Śāriputra’s corresponds to the opening of Chapter 27 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand and Chapter 36 in the Eighteen Thousand.

“Because it is pure, Śāriputra,”According to Ratnākara (F.101.b–102.a), the “transcendent state of wisdom” means the purity of the transcendent state of wisdom. It is “profound,” i.e., difficult to fathom, “due to” everything “being pure,” i.e., absolutely empty. RK: prajñāpāramite ti prajñā­pāramitāviśuddhir ity arthaḥ| sā gambhīrā duravagāhā sarvadharmāṇāṃ viśuddhatvād atyantaśūnyatvād. Note: The word “profound” and so on in this sentence and in the eight adjectival phrases below (each of which is followed by the Blessed One’s refrain “because it is pure”) are linked by Hari and Ratnākara to nine stages on the path of cultivation, even though Hari and Ratnākara do not necessarily agree how the adjectives link to those stages. said the Blessed One.
“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is enlightening,” said Śāriputra.
“Because it is pure, Śāriputra,” said the Blessed One.
“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is the seeing,” said Śāriputra.
“Because it is pure, Śāriputra,” said the Blessed One.

“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is free of transmigrating,”According to Ratnākara (F.102.a), “free of transmigrating” means not being reborn in other forms of existence, “because it is pure.” RK: sā apratisandhir asaṃkrāntir bhavāntare viśuddhatvād. said Śāriputra.
“Because it is pure, Śāriputra,” said the Blessed One.

“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is free of emotional defilement,”According to Ratnākara (F.102.a), “free of emotional defilement because it is pure” is because everything is undefiled in its nature. RK: sā asaṃkleśo viśuddhitvāt prakṛtyasaṃkliṣṭatvāt sarvadharmāṇām. said Śāriputra.
“Because it is pure, Śāriputra.” said the Blessed One.
“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is free of attainment, free of realization,” said Śāriputra.
“Because it is pure, Śāriputra,” said the Blessed One.
“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is free of coming into being,” said Śāriputra.
“Because it is pure, Śāriputra,” said the Blessed One.
“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is free of rebirth in the desire realm, the form realm, or the formless realm,” said Śāriputra.
“Because it is pure, Śāriputra,” said the Blessed One.
“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom does not know or conceive,” said Śāriputra.

“Because it is pure, Śāriputra,”According to Hari (410), it “does not know” inherent natures, and it does not “conceive” distinctions, “because it is pure” because it is the antidote to the (cognitive and emotional obscurations of) three realms of existence. Hari: na jānātī tyādi. svarūpaṃ na jānāti. prabhedaṃ na saṃjānīte, viśuddhatvād iti traidhātuka­pratipakṣatvāt. According to Ratnākara (F.102.a), it “does not know” means it does not grasp, and it does not “conceive” means it does not conceptualize. RK: na jānatī ti na gṛṇhāti| na saṃjānīta iti na vikalpayati. said the Blessed One.
Śāriputra then asked, “Blessed One, what does the transcendent state of wisdom not know or conceive?”

The Blessed One said, “Śāriputra, the transcendent state of wisdom does not know or conceive physical forms. Why is that? Because it is pure, Śāriputra. Likewise, Śāriputra, the transcendent state of wisdom does not know or conceive feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Why is that? Because it is pure, Śāriputra.”According to Ratnākara (F.102.b), in the next eight exchanges Śāriputra raises the first two possible objections and Subhūti the subsequent six. Each statement (is followed by a question mark because it) implies a question and so on concerning the reasons for precisely that conclusion by asking what, in regard to what, or in what manner is this so? The Blessed One’s responses give the reasons for those conclusions. RK: atra ca siddhānta eva kā kvā kuta etad iti hetusāpekṣāś codyasūcanādyāni| siddhāntahetavaḥ parihārāḥ| tatra dve codye śāriputrasya ṣaṭ subhūteḥ.

Śāriputra said, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom performs no service or disservice in relation to the state of complete knowing.”According to Ratnākara (F.102.b), “performs no service or disservice” is the conclusion. The implied question or objection is: Why is this the case, because if it does nothing, then how could it be the path? The reply “because it is pure” means: since the state of complete knowing is the purity of the source of everything (dharmadhātu), and that is pure, i.e., pure by nature. For this reason, the transcendent state of wisdom does nothing for it, but it counteracts the adventitious obscurations. RK: nāpakāraṃ karoti na copakāram iti siddhāntaḥ| sa kutaḥ ? yadi hy akiṃcitkarī kathaṃ sā mārga iti codyam| viśuddhitvād iti parihāraḥ| sarvajñatā hi dharmadhātuviśuddhiḥ sa ca viśuddhaḥ prakṛtiśuddhaḥ| tataḥ sā asyākiñcitkarī| kevalam āgantukāvaraṇakṣayaṃ prativyāpriyate.
“Because it is pure, Śāriputra,” said the Blessed One.

Śāriputra said, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom neither apprehends nor disregards anything.”According to Ratnākara (F.102.b), “neither apprehends nor disregards” is the conclusion. The implied question or objection is: Why is this the case, because if it does not apprehend, then it must either be a conceptualization or (at least) not wisdom itself. The reply “because it is pure” means that in the same manner and for the very same reason that it does not see things as things, it sees them as the nature of things. And in this very sense, it is pure. RK: na gṛṇhāti na tyajatī ti siddhāntaḥ| sa kutaḥ ? yadi hy asau gṛṇhāti vikalpaḥ syāt| atha na gṛṇhāti prajñaiva na syād iti codyam| viśuddhatvā di parihāraḥ| yathā hi yata evāsau dharmān dharmalakṣaṇair na paśyaty, ata eva dharmatayā paśyati| ata eva viśuddhaḥ| viśuddhatvāt na gṛṇhāti na ca tyajatī ti.
“Because it is pure, Śāriputra,” said the Blessed One.

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the purity of physical forms is because of the purity of a self.”According to Ratnākara (F.102.b–103.a), the purity of the constituents is taught to be the purity of the fruit, i.e., the purity of complete knowing. And their purity is because of the purity of the self, because the purity, i.e., absolute nonexistence, of a self is the same as theirs is too. The implied question or objection is: Why is this the case, because if the constituents, fruits, and complete knowing are absolutely nonexistent, then there would be nothing. The reply “because it is absolutely pure” means that at the absolute level, those things simply have no existence as particulars. For this reason, those things do exist as the true nature of things, i.e., their emptiness. RK: skandhaviśuddhitaḥ phalaviśuddhiḥ sarvajñatāviśuddhiś coktā| sā ca teṣāṃ viśuddhir ātmaviśuddhyā| yathā hy ātmano viśuddhir atyantam asattā tathā teṣām apīti siddhāntaḥ| sa kutaḥ ? yadi hi skandhaphala­sarva­jñatānām atyantam asattā tadaitā(te)na kiñcid iti codyam| atyantaviśuddhitvād iti parihāraḥ| atyantam asanta eva dharmāḥ svalakṣaṇaiḥ| ata eva tacchūnyatālakṣaṇayā dharmatayā santīty arthaḥ.
“Because it is absolutely pure, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One.

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, the purity of feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors is because of the purity of a self. Blessed One, the purity of consciousness is because of the purity of a self.”
“Because it is absolutely pure, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti said, “Blessed One, the purity of the fruit is because of the purity of a self.”
“Because it is absolutely pure, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti said, “Blessed One, the purity of the state of complete knowing is because of the purity of a self.”
“Because it is absolutely pure, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One.

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, the freedom from attainment and freedom from nonrealization is because of the purity of a self.”Ratnākara’s explanation of the reasons for the conclusions here and below are only slightly different from his explanation above, so his comments will not be reproduced here or below.
“Because it is absolutely pure, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti said, “Blessed One, there is no limit to physical forms, because there is no limit to a self.”
“Because it is absolutely pure, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti said, “Blessed One, there is no limit to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness because there is no limit to a self.”
“Because it is absolutely pure, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti then said, “Blessed One, the recognition of this by a bodhisattva great being is the same as the transcendent state of wisdom that belongs to a bodhisattva great being.”
“Because it is absolutely pure, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One.

The venerable Subhūti said, “Certainly, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom dwells neither on this shore’s bank, nor the opposite shore’s bank, nor does it dwell split somewhere between the two shores’ banks.”According to Ratnākara (F.103.b), the “transcendent state of wisdom” here is referring to a (disciple’s) complete knowing (i.e., not a bodhisattva’s or a buddha’s). The phrase “this shore’s bank” refers to cyclic existence. “The opposite shore’s bank” refers to nirvāṇa. It also does not dwell between the two shores. The meaning here is that none of these three are perceived. RK: ’prajñāpāramitā’ iti sarvajñatā|’ nāpare tīre’ iti saṃsāre| ’na pare tīre’ iti nirvāṇe| nāpi tayor antarāle sthitā’| trayaṃ nopalabhyata ity arthaḥ. Note: By identifying the “transcendent state of wisdom” with a disciple’s complete knowing, Ratnākara is commenting on this sentence as the beginning of the third of eight topics in the Ornament. Just above in his comments, he explains that the shift of topics happens mid-chapter here at this point and this third topic has eight subtopics. Cf. RK (F.103.b): prathamaparivartena sarvākārajñatoktā| ito ’rdhasaptamaiḥ parivartair mārgajñatā| aṣṭam anavamayoḥ parivartayor ardhābhyāṃ sarvajñatā vaktavyā| tasya aṣṭau vastūni.
“Because it is absolutely pure, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One.

The venerable Subhūti then said, “Blessed One, any bodhisattva great being who has conceptions of it as being so leaves behind this transcendent state of wisdom and distances him- or herself from this transcendent state of wisdom.”

“Excellent, excellent, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One. “It is exactly so, Subhūti, it is exactly so. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, attachments arise both through names and through signs.”According to Ratnākara (F.104.a), “attachment” refers to preoccupation (sakti), i.e., the imagination of something unreal. RK: saṅgaḥ saktir abhūtaparikalpaḥ. According to Hari (414), “names” refers to the mere designations of transcendent state of wisdom and so on, and “signs” refers to the things that are imagined and so on. “Attachment” refers to the error of dwelling in preoccupation. This is taught to be like when things conjured up by an illusionist appear, but a person, out of habitual fixation on existence, has not recognized their nature to be the appearance of something without essence. Similarly, the disciples and so on who have not recognized the nature of existence are not fully aware of the fact that (cyclic existence and nirvāṇa) are the same, because, insofar as they lack the skill in means of a virtuous friend and so on, they can become attached to perceiving things as errors when understanding them by means of signs. Hence, they come to be distant from the mother of the conquerors (i.e., the transcendent state of wisdom). On the other hand, bodhisattvas, who have cast away the erroneous signs and habitual fixations on existence, are fully aware of all the qualities of physical form and so on and are fully aware of the fact that they are the same. Hence, they come to be close to the mother of the conquerors. Subhūti expresses this implicitly through saying the following line: “It is amazing” and so on. Hari: tatra prajñā­pāramitetyādivya­padeśamātraṃ nāma. kalpitādilakṣaṇaṃ vastu nimittaṃ. saktisthānaviparyāsaḥ saṅgaḥ. etad uktaṃ : māyākāranirmitavastunaḥ pratibhāse ’viditatatsvarūpasya bhāvābhiniveśitayā naiḥsvābhāvyāpratibhāsa iva kalyāṇa­mitrādyupāya­kauśalavaikalyān nimittayogena pratipattau viparyāsa­lakṣaṇopalambha­saṅgasambhavāt, tatsamatāparijñānam avijñātabhāvarūpāṇāṃ śrāvakādīnām nāstīty atas teṣāṃ dūrībhāvo jinajananyā iti. bodhisattvādīnāṃ tūtsāritabhāvābhiniveśabhrāntinimittānāṃ rūpādi­sarva­dharma­parijñānam eva tatsamatāparijñānam ity atas teṣāṃ samyag āsannībhāvo ’syā mātur iti sāmarthyāt kathitam ity āha: āścaryaṃ bhagavann ityādi.

In response, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, in describing even these attachments, it is amazing how well the Blessed One has described, explicated, and pinpointed this transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.103.b), “described” it in terms of the sixteen characteristics on the path of seeing, “explicated” it in terms of the twelve preliminary practices, and “pinpointed” it, because it is not fixed in either cyclic existence or nirvāṇa.
