RK: ākhyātā ṣoḍaśabhir dṛṣṭimārgalakṣaṇaiḥ| sunirdiṣṭā dvādaśabhiḥ prayogaiḥ| supariniṣṭhitā saṃsāranirvāṇayor apratiṣṭhānāt.
Then the venerable Śāriputra asked Subhūti, “Venerable Subhūti, what are the attachments?”

The venerable Subhūti said, “Śāriputra, conceiving of physical forms as empty is attachment. Likewise, Śāriputra, conceiving of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness as empty is attachment. Conceiving of past things as past things is attachment. Conceiving of future things as future things is attachment. Conceiving of present things as present things is attachment. Conceiving of a person on the bodhisattva path earning so much merit through first developing the proper mindset is attachment.”
Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, in what way is it attachment?”

The venerable Subhūti said, “Kauśika, a bodhisattva great being might conceive of that mindset of awakening, thinking, ‘This is the first mindset of awakening,’ and dedicate it, thinking, ‘I dedicate this to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’ But it is not actually possible for a noble son or daughter who has set out upon the great path to dedicate the essential nature of a mindset.According to Ratnākara (F.104.b), the first conceptualization‍—“first mindset of awakening”‍—is an attachment and the second conceptualization‍—“I dedicate it”‍—is dedicating it after having conceptualized it as such. These are attachments because that mindset does not exist as a past thing and awakening does not exist as a future thing. Since someone might ask whether that “essential nature of a mindset” permanently exists, the next sentence states that it is “not possible” because one cannot dedicate something permanent. Therefore, these conceptualizations are attachments, due to their construction of something unreal. RK: idaṃ tad prathamaṃ bodhicittam iti| eṣa vikalpaḥ prathamaḥ saṅgaḥ| bodhau pariṇāmayāmī ti| evaṃ vikalpya pariṇāmanā dvitīyaḥ| katham imau saṅgau ? tasya citta­syātītatvenāsattvāt| bodheś cānāgatatvena| cittaprakṛtiḥ sadāstīti ced āha| na ce tyādi| na śakyā iti nityasya pariṇāmāyogāt| tasmād ubhau vikalpau, abhūtakalpanāt. For this very reason, Kauśika, when a noble son or daughter helps others recognize it, helps them practice it, helps inspire them toward it, and helps them rejoice in it, then that noble son or daughter should do so in conformity with the truth for the sake of unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.105.a), “for this very reason” means due to the cause of relinquishing attachments. “Helps others recognize it” means by helping those who have not heard it previously to delight in it. “Helps them practice it” means by making them accurately understand how to practice. “Helps inspire them” means by urging along those who are slow. “Helps them rejoice” means by congratulating heroic efforts undertaken. It is “in conformity with the truth” due to the perfectly relinquishment of attachments. RK: tasmāt tarhī tyādinā| tasmād iti svayaṃ saṅgavivarjanāt kāraṇāt| tarhī ti pare ṣāṃ sandarśanā dikāle| sandarśayate ti pūrvamaśrutavataḥ rocayatā| samādāpayate ti anuṣṭhānāya samyag grāhayatā| samprahaṣayate ti mantharān protsāhayatā| sampraharṣayate ti ārabdhavīryān sādhukāraiḥ| bhutānugamaḥ saṅgatyāgāt. A noble son or daughter who helps others practice it as authorized by the Buddha in this way does not bring him- or herself down by doing so, but rather relinquishes all the points of his or her own attachment.”According to Ratnākara (F.105.a), “does not bring him- or herself down” means does not do anything offensive to the buddhas. The second half of the sentence explains the benefit to being in accordance with reality oneself. RK: na kṣiṇot īti buddheṣv aparāddhaṃ na karoti| svayam bhūtānugame ’nuśaṃsam āha| imāś ce tyādinā.

Then the Blessed One commended Subhūti: “Excellent, Subhūti, excellent. You help the bodhisattva great beings relinquish all the points of attachment. Therefore, Subhūti, I will also make known the other extremely subtle attachments. Listen and pay very good attention to them, as I describe them to you.”
Assenting with “Very well, Blessed One,” the venerable Subhūti listened to the Blessed One.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, a faithful noble son or daughter here might pay attention to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata through signs.According to Hari (418), he or she would “pay attention through signs” because they are face to face with the Tathāgata’s physical body (rūpakāya) and so on as a perceptual object. Hari: nimittato manasikarotī ti rūpa­kāyādyālambanenābhi­mukhīkaraṇāt. But as long as signs are present, attachment is present. Why is this so? Because, Subhūti, insofar as attachment arises through signs, he or she rejoices, thinking, ‘I rejoice in those qualities of the Blessed One and the past, present, and future buddhas,’ and then dedicates these, thinking, ‘I dedicate the source of virtue associated with rejoicing to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’ But, Subhūti, the true nature of things is not something past, future, or present. Something that is not past, future, or present is free from the three times. And something that is free from the three times cannot be dedicated, cannot be made a sign, cannot be made an object, and cannot be seen, heard, considered, or known.”According to Ratnākara (F.105.a–b), it “cannot be dedicated” because this would involve the three times. It “cannot be made a sign” and “cannot be made an object” because it does not create something previously inexistent and because it is difficult to recognize, since it “cannot be seen, heard, considered, or known,” i.e., insofar as it is not understood, respectively, through the eyes, ears, nose, tongue, body or mind. RK: na sā śakyā pariṇāmayitum iti tryadhvaprasaṅgāt| na nimittīkartuṃ nārambaṇīkartum iti apūrvatvenākāraṇatvāt durbodhatvāc ca| yato na sā dṛṣṭaśrutamatavijñātā cakṣuṣā śrotreṇa ghrāṇajivhākāyair manasā vā ’pratītatvāt yathākramam.
“Blessed One, the essential nature of things is profound,” said Subhūti.
“Because it is void, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One.
“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound in its essential nature,” said Subhūti.
“Because it is pure in its essential nature, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One. “Since the transcendent state of wisdom is void in its essential nature, it is profound in its essential nature.”
Subhūti said, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is void in its essential nature. I pay homage, Blessed One, to the transcendent state of wisdom.”

The Blessed One said, “All things too, Subhūti, are void in their essential nature, and that voidness of all things in their essential nature is the transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata has perfectly awakened to all things being uncreated.”
Subhūti then asked, “In that case, Blessed One, has the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata not perfectly awakened to anything?”

The Blessed One said, “To explain, Subhūti, in their essential nature, those things are nothing. Their essential nature is free of any essential nature. Yet, that freedom from essential nature is the true nature of all things, because they share a single defining characteristic, namely, their lack of any defining characteristic. Given this fact, Subhūti, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata does not perfectly realize any things. Why not? Because, Subhūti, there are no two true natures of things. Indeed, Subhūti, there is only one true nature of all things. Subhūti, that true nature of all things is free of essential nature, and that freedom from essential nature is the essential nature. In this way, Subhūti, all these points of attachment are void.”
“Blessed One, the true nature of things is profound,” said Subhūti.
“Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound, as profound as space,” said the Blessed One.
“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to comprehend,” said Subhūti.

“That is because, Subhūti, no one becomes perfectly awakened,”According to Hari (422), “no one becomes perfectly awakened” because one’s essential nature is the inherent nature of the actual nature. Hence, since one does not realize it through any aspects of the Buddha’s unmixed qualities of physical form and so on, it is held to transcend thought. For this reason, it is difficult to recognize‍—this is the point. Hari: na kaścid abhisambudhyata iti rūpādyāveṇika­buddha­dharmādyākāraiḥ prakṛtes tathatāsvābhāvyād anabhi(P.118b)sambodhena yasmāc cintātikrāntatvam iṣyate, ato ’syā durbodhateti yāvat. said the Blessed One.
“The transcendent state of wisdom, Blessed One, is inconceivable,” said Subhūti.
“That is because the transcendent state of wisdom is unknowable through a mindset and inaccessible to a mindset,” said the Blessed One.

“Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is uncreated,”This statement of Subhūti’s corresponds to the opening of of Chapter 28 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand and Chapter 37 in the Eighteen Thousand. said Subhūti.
“Indeed, Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is uncreated because a creator is not something mentally perceived,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti then asked, “In that case, Blessed One, how should a bodhisattva great being go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, then he or she does not go about life in physical forms. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.106.a–b), in response to the question of how the practice is to be done, here “physical forms” and so on refers to all things, but the constituents of experience are used as a way of implying this. In this passage, the idea that “he or she does not go about life in physical forms” up to “consciousness” means that he or she does not conceive of physical forms and so on. The practice with respect to physical forms and so on is expressed as “he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom” at that time. RK: kathaṃ prayogaḥ kartavyaḥ? atra rūpādayaḥ sarvadharmāḥ| skandhās tūpalakṣaṇam| rūpe yāvad vijñāne na caratī ti rūpādīn na sañjānīta ity arthaḥ| tadā carati prajñāpāramitāyām iti rūpādiprayogaḥ. Likewise, Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, then he or she does not go about life in feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, then he or she does not go about life conceiving of physical forms as impermanent. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, then he or she does not go about life conceiving of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness as impermanent. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, then he or she does not go about life conceiving of physical forms as empty. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. The same is true for feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors. Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, then he or she does not about life conceiving of consciousness as empty. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she does not go about life conceiving of physical forms as fulfilling or unfulfilling, but goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Also, the fulfillment or unfulfillment of physical forms is not physical forms. Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she does not go about life conceiving of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness as fulfilling or unfulfilling. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Also, the fulfillment or unfulfillment of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is not feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.
“If he or she does not go about life even in these ways, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, it is wonderful how much the Blessed One has explained to bodhisattva great beings about the state of having attachment and the state of not having attachment.”According to Hari (425), “wonderful” is because he expresses one other way (to practice) by explaining another topic. By explaining practice in this way, he explains the state of being “with attachment,” i.e., the constituents of experience as having the nature of a mental perception, and “without attachment,” i.e., the transcendent state of wisdom as having the nature of being free of any mental perception. Hari: anyārthakathanena prakārāntarābhidhānād āścaryaṃ, yasmād evaṃ prayogakathanena sasaṅgatā rūpādīnām upalambharūpatvam, asaṅgatā ca mātur anupalambha­svabhāvatā kathitā.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being does not go about life conceiving of physical forms as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Hari (426), this sentence explains the practice of not being attached to physical forms and so on. The term “with attachment” means with a mental perception, and “without attachment” means free of mental perception. Hari: rūpādiṣv asaṅgaprayogam āha rūpaṃ sasaṅgam asaṅgam ityādinā. sasaṅgam asaṅgam iti sopalambham anupalambham ity arthaḥ. Note: The second half of this chapter is framed by the central play on the Sanskrit words sasaṅga/asaṅga. The translation “with/without attachment” aims to capture the meaningful ambiguity in the Sanskrit that could mean either subjectively “attached/unattached” or objectively “impeding/not impeding.” This ambiguity allows for cyclic existence/nirvāṇa to be presented as objectively impure/pure according to disciples and as only subjectively impure/pure by bodhisattvas. The same is true for feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being does not go about life conceiving of consciousness as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being does not go about life conceiving of the eye as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Likewise, he or she does not go about life conceiving of anything‍—including all the way up to the feelings generated through mental contact‍—as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.

“A bodhisattva great being does not go about life conceiving of the earth element as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. He or she does not go about life conceiving of anything‍—including all the way up to the consciousness element‍—as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.

“A bodhisattva great being does not go about life conceiving of the transcendent state of generosity as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. The same is true for the transcendent state of ethical discipline, tolerant acceptance, heroic perseverance, and meditation. He or she also does not go about life conceiving of the transcendent state of wisdom as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Likewise, a bodhisattva great being does not go about life conceiving of the qualities‍—that is to say, the thirty-seven factors in awakening, the powers, the grounds for self-confidence, the types of experiential knowledge, and the eighteen distinctive qualities of a buddha‍—as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.The thirty-seven factors in awakening were listed previously (n.219).

“A bodhisattva great being does not go about life conceiving of the fruition of a stream enterer as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. He or she does not go about life conceiving of the fruition of a once-returner as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. He or she does not go about life conceiving of the fruition of a non-returner as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. He or she does not go about life conceiving of the state of a worthy one as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. He or she does not go about life conceiving of the state of a solitary realizer as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. He or she does not go about life conceiving of buddhahood as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, he or she does not go about life conceiving of even the state of complete knowing as with attachment or without attachment. Instead, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Subhūti, going about life in this way, the bodhisattva great being does not give rise to attachment to physical forms. Nor does the bodhisattva great being give rise to attachment to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness.

“Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being does not give rise to attachment to anything from the eye up to the feelings generated through mental contact. The bodhisattva great being does not give rise to attachment to anything from the earth element up to the consciousness element. The bodhisattva great being does not give rise to attachment to the transcendent state of generosity, the transcendent state of ethical discipline, the transcendent state of tolerant acceptance, the transcendent state of heroic perseverance, the transcendent state of meditation, or the transcendent state of wisdom. The bodhisattva great being does not give rise to attachment to the thirty-seven factors in awakening, the powers, the grounds for self-confidence, the types of experiential knowledge, or the eighteen distinctive qualities of a buddha.Most other terms in this sentence were explained in previous footnotes. According to Hari (455–56, regarding a later passage), the four types of “experiential knowledge” concern (1) the teachings (dharma), i.e., methods; (2) meanings (artha), i.e., the defining characteristic of things; (3) expressions (nirukti), i.e., the language of the people; and (4) illumination (pratibhāna), i.e., the distinctions of things. These (types of experiential knowledge) arise from complete knowing’s non-attachment and non-resistance, because the resistance of the cognitive obscuration disappears when they are attained. Hari: pratisaṃvitpāramiteyam iti paryāye dharmalakṣaṇe janapadabhāṣāyāṃ dharmaprabhede ca yathākramam adhigatāsvādana­jñeyāvaraṇa­pratighātābhāvena sarva­jñānā­saṅgāpratighātitām upādāya dharmārtha­nirukti­pratibhāna pratisaṃvid ākārāś catvāraḥ. The bodhisattva great being does not give rise to attachment to the fruition of a stream enterer, the fruition of a once-returner, the fruition of a non-returner, the state of a worthy one, the state of a solitary realizer, buddhahood, or even the state of complete knowing. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, the state of complete knowing is neither attached nor fettered, neither liberated nor transcended.According to Ratnākara (F.106.b), it is not “attached” due to lacking any attachment, and hence it is not “fettered” by defilements in the three realms. Therefore, it is not “liberated” from the defilements, nor has it “transcended” the three realms. RK: asakte tyādi| sarvasaṅgābhāvād asaktā| tato na baddhā kleśais traidhātuke| tato na muktā kleśaiḥ| na samatikrāntā traidhātukam. Due to having transcended all such attachments, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, it is wonderful how profound is this Dharma called the transcendent state of wisdom. Blessed One, it does not get deflated whether or not it is being taught. Moreover, it does not get inflated whether or not it is being taught.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Excellent, Subhūti, excellent. It is so, Subhūti, it is so. Subhūti, it is just like, for instance, if the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata were to spend his whole life praising the virtue of space, there would be no inflation of space. Likewise, if he were not to praise its virtue, there would be no deflation of space. Subhūti, it is just like, for instance, with illusory persons. If one were to praise their virtue, they would not be attached or afflicted. Also, if one were not to praise their virtue, they would not be resentful or afflicted.According to Ratnākara (F.106.b), the meaning of the word “afflicted” is “attached” (in the first sentence) and “resentful” in the second. RK: saṃkliśyate śabdasyārthaḥ pratihanyata iti dvitīyasyānunīyata iti. Cf. BHSD anunaya. Subhūti, the true nature of things would be exactly the same, whether or not it was praised, or for however long it was praised.”

The elder Subhūti said, “Blessed One, while performing difficult noble deeds, going about life in the profound transcendent state of wisdom, and cultivating that transcendent state of wisdom, a bodhisattva great being is neither disheartened nor overexcited, and, in this state, he or she engages in meditation and does not turn back. Blessed One, this particular cultivation of the transcendent state of wisdom is the cultivation of space.According to Ratnākara (F.107.a), it is a “cultivation of space” (viz. the cultivation of something like space) because there is no appearance of a single characteristic (in it). RK: ākāśabhāvanā ekalakṣaṇatāpratibhāsāt. Blessed One, I would pay homage to any bodhisattva great being who has donned such armor. Why is that? Blessed One, anyone who fastens this armor together with space is someone who wishes to don such armor for the sake of beings.According to Hari (429), it has been said, “After perceiving everything to be, in reality, like space, there can be nothing superior to the wisdom and so on. However, performing difficult noble deeds (with that in mind) is superior because one puts on the armor for the sake of the complete knowing of all aspects.” Hari: etad uktaṃ: ākāśopamān api sarvadharmān adhyālambya tattvataḥ sambhavat­prajñādiprakarṣābhāve ’pi sarvākārajñatāyāḥ kṛtaśaḥ saṃnāhakaraṇād atiśayo duṣkara iti. According to Ratnākara (F.107.a), he explains this bodhisattva to be someone who aims for perfect awakening for the sake of all beings. “With space” means with a method that is like space, because there is no giving up of such (a method). Hence, since sentient beings are not something perceived and are infinite, the performance of difficult noble deeds arises from an awakening (of a bodhisattva) motivated by the benefit of all. RK: ayaṃ sann āha iti yaḥ sarvasattvārthāya samyaksambodhau| ākāśene ti ākāśanibhena yogena tadatyāgāt| tato duṣkaratā sattvānām anupalambhād anantāc ca viśvārtham uddiśya bodheḥ. And, Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being is someone who has donned that great armor. Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being is a hero who wishes to don such armor for the sake of sentient beings, who are like space. That bodhisattva great being wishes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening for the sake of sentient beings, who are like the source of qualities.According to Hari (429), the two comparisons‍—“like space” and “like the source of qualities” (dharmadhātu)‍—are taught due to the difference between worldly and spiritual accomplishments. Hari: ākāśasamānāṃ dharmadhātusamānām iti laukikalokottara­prasiddhibhedena dvayam uktaṃ. Blessed One, he or she wishes to completely liberate space. Blessed One, he or she wishes to lift up space. That bodhisattva great being, who has attained the armor of the perfection of great heroic perseverance, has donned the armor for the sake of beings who are like space, who are like the source of qualities.”According to Hari (429), he or she has “attained the armor of the perfection of great heroic perseverance” means that in the activities connected with the armor-like practice, he or she has acomplished great efforts filled with emptiness and compassion. He or she has “donned the armor” means he or she endures the three types of difficult noble deeds (regarding noble motivation, practice, and conduct). Hari: mahā­vīrya­pāramitāsaṃnāhaprāpta iti saṃnāha­prayogakāritreṣu śūnyatā­karuṇā­garbhamahāvyavasāya­sampannaḥ. saṃnāhaṃ saṃnahyata iti trividhaduṣkaram utsahate.

Then one of the other monks, with his hands cupped together in reverence, prostrated to the Blessed One and said, “Blessed One, I pay homage to the transcendent state of wisdom. This is because the transcendent state of wisdom does not cause any thing to arise and does not cause any thing to cease.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the venerable Subhūti, “Noble Subhūti, where will a bodhisattva great being, who engages in such a meditation in this transcendent state of wisdom, become engaged in meditation?”According to Ratnākara (F.107.a), the question is: “where” in the resultant state “will a bodhisattva great being, who engages in such a meditation” in the causal state, “become engaged in meditation”? RK: ya evaṃyogam āpatsyata iti hetvavasthāyām, kva sa iti phalāvasthāyām.

Subhūti said, “Kauśika, a bodhisattva great being who engages in meditation in this transcendent state of wisdom will engage in meditation in space. Kauśika, such a bodhisattva great being‍—who thinks that he or she should train in the transcendent state of wisdom and engage in meditation‍—will engage in meditation in intermediate space.”According to Hari (430), “in space” means in the emptiness of the object to be known (jñeya); “in intermediate space” means in the lack of inherent nature of knowledge (jñāna). Hari: ākāśa iti jñeyaśūnyatve. abhyavakāśa iti jñānaniḥsvabhāvatve. According to Ratnākara (F.107.a), “space” means outer space, where stars travel. But here it refers to perfect awakening, because it has a similar quality. “Intermediate space” refers to a hidden, formless place above Mount Meru on the earth. But here it refers to an arhat’s awakening, because it has a similar quality. RK: ākāśa iti ākāśaṃ tārāpatham| iha tu tatsādharmyād anuttarā bodhiḥ| abhyavakāśaṃ bhūmerūpariṣṭhād acchannam arūpiṣṭhānam| iha tu tatsādharmyādd hīnabodhiḥ| tad ubhayaṃ phalam| tasya yathābhavyaṃ prāpter avandhyaprayogaḥ. Note: these two (ubhayam) are (1) bodhisattvas in “space” who attain bodhi and (2) worthy ones in the space of the formless realm.

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “Let the Blessed One give his order and I will provide the safeguard for, defend, and protect any noble son or daughter who keeps this transcendent state of wisdom.”Neither commentator explains the meaning of the word “keeps” in this particular case. Although Schopen 1977 (p. 142) argued convincingly that “keeps” here originally meant “carries around,” the translation is purposely ambiguous. On the one hand, previously when the scripture was speaking of relics, Ratnākara (F.69.a) explains the word “keeps” as signifying carrying them in a small casket and so on (dhārayed vā karaṇḍādivahanāt). But on the other, when the scripture was speaking of the transcendent state of wisdom in the context of protection, Ratnākara (76.a) explains the point to be that one “keep it in one’s heart” (dhārayed iti hṛdaye sthāpayet).
Then Subhūti asked Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, do you identify any thing that you will safeguard, defend, and protect?”
“Definitely not, noble Subhūti,” replied Śakra.

Subhūti said, “Thus, Kauśika, if a bodhisattva great being remains in the transcendent state of wisdom as explicated, then that transcendent state of wisdom itself will be his or her safeguarder, defender, and protector.According to Ratnākara (F.107.b), Śakra wants to safeguard, defend, and protect. But Subhūti explains to him that the transcendent state of wisdom is the so-called “protector” accompanied by the “safeguarder” and “defender.” RK: śakras tasya bodhisattvasya rakṣāvaraṇaguptiṃ kartukāmaḥ| rakṣāvaraṇā bhyāṃ sahitā guptis tathoktā| taṃ subhūtir āha. See also n.263. On the other hand, if he or she ever lacks the transcendent state of wisdom, then humans and nonhumans who prey on weakness and seek ways to attack will find any vulnerability in him or her. Moreover, Kauśika, one should think that safeguarding, defending, and protecting any bodhisattva great being going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom is the same as safeguarding, defending, and protecting space. So, what do you think, Kauśika? Do you have the power to safeguard, defend, and protect an echo?”
“Definitely not, noble Subhūti,” replied Śakra.

Subhūti said, “Kauśika, it is exactly the same when the bodhisattva great being, going about life and abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom, recognizes that all things are like an echo.According to Ratnākara (F.107.b), both the large scriptures (on the transcendent state of wisdom) set out seven types of appearances (instead of just the one here, an echo), saying, “All things are like magical illusions, mirages, dreams, echoes, false appearances, reflections, or cities of gandharva ghosts.” RK: mahatyor bhagavatyoḥ saptadhā khyātiḥ paṭhyate| “māyā-marīci-svapna-pratiśrutkā-pratibhāsa-pratibimba-gandharvanagaropamāḥ sarvadharmāḥ” iti. See, for example, the Twenty-Five Thousand, 61.2 (although it should be noted that the similes there are eight in number, do not correspond exactly, and come in a different context). Also, according to Ratnākara (F.107.b–8.a), there are two types of transcendent state of wisdom, namely, the spiritual and the pure worldly, as is taught in The Dhāraṇī “Entering Into Nonconceptuality” (Avikalpapraveśadhāraṇī, Toh 142, 1.13): “A bodhisattva who dwells in the source free of conceptualizations sees all things as homogenous like space. In post-meditation, he or she sees all things‍—with knowledge that is undistinguished from the object of knowledge‍—to be like magical illusions, mirages, dreams, false appearances, echoes, reflections, water-moons, and magical creations.” Thus, it concerns the spiritual when he mentions “space” in this passage, and it concerns the pure worldly when he says the bodhisattva “recognizes” (it as an echo). RK: dvividhā prajñāpāramitā lokottarā śuddhā laukikī ca| yathoktaṃ drumavikalpapraveśāyāṃ dhāraṇyām avikalpadhātupratiṣṭhito bodhisattvo jñeyanirviśiṣṭena jñānena ākāśasamatalān sarvadharmān paśyati| tatpṛṣṭhalabdhena māyā­marīci­svapna­pratibhāsa­pratiśrutkāprati­bimbodaka­candra­nirmitasamān sarvadharmān paśyati” iti| ato lokottaram adhikṛtyāha ākāśasye tyādi| śuddhalaukikīm adhikṛtyāha| tatkim ityādi| parijānātī ti. More generally, note that although an echo is the most common simile in this scripture, there are also passages with series of four or five similes at 9.17 and 30.5. The bodhisattva great being abides in such a way that he or she does not develop conceits about those things, identify them, perceive them, or conceive of them. Nor are they found, seen, observed, or mentally perceived. If the bodhisattva great being abides in this way, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.108.a–b), he or she does “not develop conceits about those things” by fixating on existence; does “not identify” through viewing (incorrectly); does “not perceive” through grasping; and does “not conceive of” by imagining. These are the four ways of fabricating a grasper. Then, to explain the four ways of fabricating something to be grasped, he says that they are “not found,” i.e., not considered existent; “not seen,” i.e., not viewed, “not encountered,” i.e., not grasped; and “not mentally perceived (upalabhyante), i.e., not conceptualized. Without these eight ways of fabricating a grasper and a grasped, that the bodhisattva great being “abides” means that he or she goes about life that way with respect to all things. RK: ataś ca tān iti sarvadharmān na manyate bhāvābhiniveśena| na samanupaśyati dṛṣṭyā| na jānāti grahaṇena| na sañjānīte kalpanayā| iti caturvidhaṃ grāhakakalpanam| tathaiva grāhyavikalpanam āha te ce tyādinā| na vidyanta iti sattvena na manyante| na saṃdṛśyanta iti na dṛśyante| na saṃvidyanta iti na gṛhyante| nopalabhyanta iti na vikalpyante| ity evam aṣṭadhā grāhyagrāhakayor akalpanena viharatī ti sarvadharmeṣu carati.

Then, through the Buddha’s miraculous power, the Four Great Kings in this billionfold, great order world system, all the Śakras who are powerful lords, all the Great Brahmā gods along with Great Brahmā, who is the earth protector, all approached the Blessed One. After approaching, they made obeisance to the Blessed One’s feet with their heads, then circumambulated the Blessed One three times and stood to one side. While standing to one side, they all recollected the one thousand buddhas through the Buddha’s miraculous power and the Buddha’s blessings.

The transcendent state of wisdom expounded by the monk named Subhūti, through these very terms, words, and syllables, is this chapter on the transcendent state of wisdom in which Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, alone asks the questions and causes the questions to be asked. In the same location, the very location where this transcendent state of wisdom was uttered, the bodhisattva great being Maitreya will utter this transcendent state of wisdom after he realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening.In the Tibetan translation, the passages that constitute this paragraph and the preceding one are linked without a break, and can be interpreted as implying that the thousand buddhas, as well as Maitreya, will all teach this chapter. Since Maitreya’s teaching of it is taken up as a topic at the start of the following chapter, however, that is where the emphasis must lie.
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Purity,” the eighth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 9: PraiseThe beginning of this chapter corresponds to passages in Chapter 28 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (28.42) and Hundred Thousand (28.279), and in Chapter 37 in the Eighteen Thousand (37.37). Note that the mention of names and sounds etc. here is reflected, in the long sūtras, in their mention in the closing passages of the preceding chapters that monks named Subhūti will continue, in the dispensations of future buddhas including Maitreya, to question their buddhas using these very same words. This chapter goes on to include (from the point marked by note 582) the content of the whole of Chapter 29 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 38 in the Eighteen Thousand.According to Ratnākara (F.117.a), this chapter is called “praise” because (its description of the many) aspects of the transcendent state of wisdom is a praise to it. RK: bcom ldan ’das ma la rnam pa ’di rnams kyis bstod de des na bstod pa’i don gyi le’u ni bstod pa’i le’u’o.
div.p: Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, this ‘transcendent state of wisdom’ is a mere name.According to Hari (435), it is to explain the path of insight that Subhūti says “transcendent state of wisdom.” This term now refers‍—within the topic of (a worthy one’s) complete knowing‍—to the path of seeing that consists in sixteen moments (derived from subdividing each of the four noble) truths into (four aspects), namely (1) the receptivity to the knowledge of the truth, (2) the knowledge of the truth, (3) the subsequent receptivity to the truth, and (4) the subsequent knowledge of the truth. Hari: adhunā darśanamārgārtham āha: prajñāpāramiteti pratisatyaṃ dharmajñānakṣāntir dharmajñānam anvayajñānakṣāntir anvayajñānam ity evaṃ ṣoḍaśakṣaṇātmakaḥ sarvajñatādhikāre darśanamārga ity arthaḥ. Note: Hari (435–41) and Ratnākara (F.109.b–112.b) demonstrate in the next ten or so back-and-forths between interlocutors precisely how the different statements correlate to these sixteen moments on the path of seeing. But these comments have not been included in the notes (except for glossing an unusual usage) because they are not easily extracted from others concerning the Ornament. Its name is not mentally perceived as such, insofar as what is called a ‘name’ simply consists in a sound;According to Ratnākara (F.108.b–109.a), “a mere name” means that the transcendent state of wisdom is a (mere) designation, but not the referent, because there is no relation between a name and a referent, and hence one cannot cognize the referent (transcendent state of wisdom) from the name (transcendent state of wisdom). Otherwise, if one were to say “I myself am a god,” then one would turn into that very god‍—such would be the absurd consequence. The next sentence comes, nevertheless, in case someone insists that it does exist in a mere designation: “Its name…” and so on. “As such” (idam) means with these very words. That name is “not mentally perceived” because it is impossible (for one to perceive a thing) in a sound that is empty of a referent. “Simply consists in a sound” means that it is both the expression and what is expressed. So, whether or not that very name appears together with a referent or without a referent, it is impossible (for one to perceive a thing in it) and it is established as not existing (as such). RK: |ming tsam mo zhes bya ba ni ming yin gyi don ma yin te| ming dang don ni ’brel ba med pa nyid| |kyis de dag las don rtogs pa med do| |gzhan du na bdag nyid ni lha yin no zhes smras na| lha nyid du ’gyur ba thal bar ’gyur ro| |de lta mod kyi ming tsam na yod do zhe na| de’i phyir ming de yang zhes bya ba la sogs pa gsungs so| |’di zhes bya ba ni ming tsam nyid kyis so| |mi dmigs pa zhes bya ba ni don gyis stong pa’i sgra la mi rigs pa’i [phyir] ro| |de ci’i phyir zhe na| ngag gis dngos po zhes bya ba ni brjod par byed pa brjod par bya ba’i don dang bcas zhes bya ba’i don to| |don dang bcas kyang rung don med kyang rung ste| ming de nyid mi rigs te med par grub bo. nor is the transcendent state of wisdom itself either found or mentally perceived.According to Ratnākara (F.109.a), if one were to ask how the transcendent state of wisdom is not mentally perceived, the transcendent state of wisdom is without either name or referent because they are contradictory to the transcendent state of wisdom. In that regard, it has no referent, and there is no cognition of a referent from a sound that has no relation with a referent. One might (object by) saying that the reason it has no referent is that it has no relation with a thing. That being so, through the sound the thing (i.e., transcendent state of wisdom) would not be affected, nor would there be any engagement with the thing that is (the sound’s) referent. (To respond to this objection), it is for this very reason that the transcendent state of wisdom (is said) not to exist as a name or a referent; that is to say, not mentally perceiving any referent means not mentally perceiving a transcendent state of wisdom. RK: ji ltar de mi dmigs she na| shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa la ming dang don ni med de shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa dang ’gal ba’i phyir ro| |de la de’i don kyang med de| don dang ’brel ba med pa’i sgra las ni don rtogs pa med de| gal te dngos po dang ’grel [sic ’brel?] ba med pa’i phyir don ni med pa yin no zhe na| de ltar na sgra las dngos po gos par mi ’gyur te| don gyi dngos po la yang ’jug par mi ’gyur ro| |de’i phyir shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa ni ming dang don med pa ste| don mi dmigs pa ni shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa mi dmigs pa’o. The transcendent state of wisdom is exactly the same as a name, and a name is exactly the same as the transcendent state of wisdom. Neither of the two things is found or mentally perceived. So, Blessed One, after realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening, why will the bodhisattva great being Maitreya discuss this transcendent state of wisdom using these very terms, words, and syllables in precisely this location?”

The Blessed One replied to the venerable Subhūti, “It is because, Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being Maitreya will awaken with the conviction, ‘Physical forms are not permanent, not impermanent. Physical forms are not fettered and not liberated. They are absolutely pure.’ Likewise, he will awaken with the conviction, ‘Feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are not permanent, not impermanent. Feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are not fettered and not liberated. They are absolutely pure.’ For this reason, Subhūti, after the bodhisattva great being Maitreya realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening, he will discuss this very transcendent state of wisdom using these very terms, words, and syllables in precisely this location.”
At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Ah! Blessed One, this transcendent state of wisdom is completely pure!”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is completely pure because physical forms are completely pure. Likwise, Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is completely pure because feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are completely pure.

“Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is completely pure because the nonarising, nonceasing, nondefilement, and nonpurification of physical forms are completely pure. The same is true for feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors. Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is completely pure because the nonarising, nonceasing, nondefilement, and nonpurification of consciousness are completely pure.

“Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is completely pure because space is completely pure. Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is completely pure because physical forms are free of stains and free of apprehending.According to Hari (437), it is free of “stains” because, insofar as it lacks either arising or nonarising, it is not stained by the root or gross defilements. It is free of “apprehending” because, insofar as it lacks either visibility or invisibility, it has no defilements. Hari: rūpanirupalepā­parigrahataye ti samudayā­samudayavisaṃyogāt sarva­kleśopakleśa­nirupaliptam| iti ṣaṣṭhaḥ. prabhavāprabhavābhāvenā­samkleśāt parigrahe ṇa nirmuktam| iti saptamaḥ. In the same way, Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is completely pure because feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are free of stains and free of apprehending.

“Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is completely pure because space and an echo are inexpressible, inexpressive, and free of mental perception.According to Ratnākara (F.110.b), just as an echo is like space in not expressing anything whatsoever about a mountain or a valley, just so all things are also “inexpressive” (and “inexpressible”) like space. In that regard, the “transcendent state of wisdom is completely pure.” RK: tshig tu med pa dang| brjod du med pa ni ji ltar brag ca ni ri dang| ri sul du nam mkha’ ltar cung zad kyang brjod pa med pa’o| |de bzhin du chos thams cad kyang nam mkha’ ltar brjod du med pa ste| de ni shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa yongs su dag pa’o. Note that the Tibetan translation of the Quintessence as cited here enshrines an earlier Tibetan rendering of apravyāhāra as tshig tu med pa instead of the one used in the Tibetan translation of the Eight Thousand, smrar med pa. Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is completely pure because it is not stained by anything that is a stain or a nonstain.”

In response, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Ah! Blessed One, any noble son or daughter who comes to be even within earshot of this transcendent state of wisdom will profit greatly. How much more so those who take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. They will have no eye disease, no ear disease, no nose disease, no tongue disease, and no body disease. They will not become confused and will not die through taking poison. They will have many thousands of gods at their back following them from behind. Wherever a Dharma teacher who is a noble son or daughter discusses the transcendent state of wisdom on the eighth, the fourteenth, or the fifteenth day, he or she will earn a tremendous amout of merit.”

In response, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “So it is, Subhūti, so it is. Subhūti, those noble sons or daughters will have many thousands of gods at their back following them from behind. Many thousands of gods will come to that place, all wishing to hear the Dharma. They will safeguard, defend, and protect the Dharma teacher who is discussing this transcendent state of wisdom. Why so? Because, Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is a jewel unsurpassed in the world of gods, humans, and asuras. Hence, Subhūti, a noble son or daughter will earn a tremendous amout of merit in connection with it. Nevertheless, Subhūti, while this profound transcendent state of wisdom is being written, taken in, kept in mind, chanted, mastered, passed on, taught, expounded, explained, and recited, there will be many interruptions. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, there are many opponents to such great jewels. The greater a jewel’s value, the more formidable its opponents. And, Subhūti, this great jewel‍—namely, the transcendent state of wisdom‍—is unsurpassed in the world and practiced for the welfare and happiness of the world.According to Hari (440), the “great jewel” is a state of nonconceptualization as a countermethod that causes the actualization of the fruit, because it involves no conceptualization of any things as one attends to it for the sake of the welfare and happiness of all beings, and because, for the sake of this jewel, which is nonconceptual and virtuous by nature, there is no mixing of measures with countermeasures (in order to realize it). Hari: sarvasattvahitasukhā dyarthaṃ pratyupasthita tvena sarva­dharma­vikalpābhāvād avikalpaka­kalyāṇa­prakṛti­ratnārthena nyāyānyāyāsaṃśleṣāt phalasākṣātkaraṇaṃ pratyupāyo ’vikalpakatvaṃ mahāratnam iti caturdaśaḥ. One attends to it in a manner free of dissolution, because everything is free of arising, free of ceasing, and free of emotional defilement.According to Ratnākara (F.111.b), “in a manner free from dissolution” means by way of lasting. RK: mi ’jigs pa’i tshul gyis zhes bya ba ni rtag pa’i tshul gyis so. That is to say, Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom does not cling to anything, does not defile anything, and does not apprehend anything.According to Hari (440), the transcendent state of wisdom “does not cling to” any imagined thing, “does not defile” any dependent thing, and “does not apprehend” any actualized thing‍—these are the three different meanings (of “thing” in this sentence). It is taught (in this section) that it does not conflate things with signs since it is free of any practice or lack of practice. Hari: kalpitabhāvaṃ nāvalīyate, paratantraṃ na saṃkliṣyate, pariniṣpannaṃ na parigṛhṇātī ty arthabhedaḥ. etad uktaṃ: pratipattyapratipatti-vinirmuktatvena dharmāṇāṃ nimittair asaṃsarga iti pañcadaśaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.111.b), this sentence responds to the question, “If one attends to it for the benefit and welfare of beings, does it then involve conceptualization?” He states that it “does not cling to,” i.e., it does not fixate; “does not defile,” i.e., does not dwell; and “does not apprehend,” i.e., does not conceive. Hence, nonconceptualization means that it is not conceptualizing in these three ways. RK: gal te phan pa dang bde ba’i phyir nye bar gnas na de lta na ni rnam par rtog par ’gyur ro zhe na| rab ’byor shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa zhes bya ba la sogs pa gsungs te| zhum par mi ’gyur zhes bya ba ni mngon par zhen pa med pa’o| |mi sbyor zhes bya ba ni mi gnas pa’o| |yongs su mi ’dzin zhes bya ba ni yang dag par mi shes pa ste| de’i phyir rnam par mi rtog pa ni rnam pa gsum du rnam par mi rtog pa’o. Why not? Because, Subhūti, all those things are neither experienced nor mentally perceived. That is to say, Subhūti, because it is free of mental perception, the transcendent state of wisdom is unstained.Here, instead of the Sanskrit edition’s “because it is free of mental perception” (anupalabdhitaḥ), the Tibetan translation (F.113.b) reads “because it is unarisen” (ma skyes pa’i phyir). Subhūti, what is unstained is just the transcendent state of wisdom. To explain, Subhūti, inasmuch as physical forms lack stains, the transcendent state of wisdom is unstained. The same is true for feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors. Subhūti, inasmuch as consciousness lacks stains, the transcendent state of wisdom is unstained. Subhūti, if a bodhisattva great being does not conceive of it even as such, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. However, Subhūti, this very transcendent state of wisdom is certainly not entering or settling down in anything, not revealing or demonstrating anything, and not leading toward or leading away from anything.”According to Hari (441–42), after having explicated the topic of complete knowing in detail, he summarizes it in the context of the three types of complete knowing by including the entire meaning within this sentence. In this sentence, “not entering anything” means not coming into being through the door of giving rise to the proper mindset; “not settling down on anything” means not establishing itself through the practice instructions and so on; “not revealing anything” means in the form of a universal through the armor-like practice; “not demonstrating anything” means in the form of a particular through the practice of setting out; “not leading toward” means not procuring anything through the practice of accumulation; and “not leading away from” means not causing anything to depart through the practice of going beyond cyclic existence. Hari: vistareṇa nirdiśyaivaṃ sakalārthasaṃgrāhakatvena trisarvajñatāmadhye sarvākārajñatām upasaṃharann āha: sā khalu punar ityādi. tatrāveśikā cittotpādadvāreṇotpādikā, niveśikā vavādādinā pratiṣṭhāpikā, saṃdarśikā saṃnāhapratipattyā sāmānyarūpeṇa, nidarśikā prasthānapratipattyā viśeṣarūpeṇa, āvāhikā sambhārapratipattyā prāpikā, nirvāhikā niryāṇapratipattyā niṣkarṣikā. sarvatra na kasyacid dharmasyety abhisambandhanīyaṃ. According to Ratnākara (F.112.a–b), “not…anything” means no mindset or any concurrent constituent of a mindset. “Not entering anything” means not closely placing anything on a meditative focus; “not settling down on anything” means not producing a tranquil mental state (śamatha) while placing anything on that meditative focus; “not revealing anything” means not generating insight (vipaśyanā); “not demonstrating anything” means because nothing is actualized; “not leading toward” means not attaining anything as a result; and “not leading away from” means that it itself does not lead to the end of anything. RK: chos gang yang zhes bya ba ni sems dang sems las byung ba mtshungs par ldan pa’o| |’jug par byed pa ni dmigs pa la nye bar ’jog par byed pa’o| |nges par ’jog par byed pa ni de nyid la ’jog cing zhi gnas skyed par byed pa’o| |yang dag par ston par byed pa ni lhag mthong skyed par byed pa’o| |nges par ston par byed pa na mngon sum du byed pas so| |yongs su thob par byed pa ni ’bras bur gyur pa’i chos thob par byed pa’o| |nges par thob par byed pa ni de nyid mthar phyin par ’dren pa ste| thams cad kyang med do zhes bya ba dang sbyar ro.

Then, in the sky, many thousands of gods roared with cheers of excitement while waving their garments in applause, and exclaimed,According to DOP p. 272, cela-ukkhepa (Skt. cailavikṣepa), the waving of garments is a sign of applause. “Ah! We are witnessing the wheel of Dharma being set in motion a second time on the Jambu continent!”

Then the Blessed One said to the elder Subhūti, “Subhūti, this is not the wheel of Dharma being set in motion a second time. Nor is there a setting in motion of any Dharma, or any causing of it to stop. It is in this way, Subhūti, that a bodhisattva great being possesses the transcendent state of wisdom.”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, it is a great transcendent state that belongs to that bodhisattva great being who has no attachment to any things. That bodhisattva great being wishes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening but does not awaken to any Dharma, and hence, he or she will neither set in motion a wheel of Dharma, nor reveal any Dharma. Why not? Because no Dharma is mentally perceived, nor is any Dharma indicated, nor is any Dharma set in motion.According to Ratnākara (F.112.b), it is teaching that all things (dharma) are not perceived, due to which there is no Dharma whatsoever that one can “awaken to.” For this reason, there is not any purifying Dharma that is “taught” to “anyone.” RK: chos thams cad ni dmigs pa med de| gang gi phyir byang chub par ’gyur ba’i chos gang yin pa de yang med do zhes ston to| |gang gi phyir rnam par byang ba’i chos gang zhig gang la ston par ’gyur ba ni med do. Why not? Because, Blessed One, absolutely no Dharma has come into being, nor will any Dharma be caused to stop. Why not? Because, Blessed One, from the beginning absolutely no things have come into being, insofar as all things are void by nature.”According to Ratnākara (F.112.b), the phrase “not come into being” means not arisen. “No things” refers to defilement here. RK: mngon par ma grub pa ni ma skyes pa’o| |chos gang yang zhes bya ba ni kun nas nyon mongs pa’o.

In response, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “You are right, Subhūti, you are right. The empty state neither starts nor stops. The state free of signs neither starts nor stops. The state free of inclinations neither starts nor stops.According to Ratnākara (F.112.b), the three states of meditative concentration‍—the “empty state,” the “signless state,” and the “uninclined state”‍—are described here as examples of things that purify. RK: stong pa nyid la sogs pa’i ting nge ’dzin gsum ni rnam par byang ba rnams kyi dper brjod pa’o. Subhūti, this sort of teaching‍—which is a teaching about all things‍—is not taught by anyone, or learned by anyone, or accepted by anyone.According to Ratnākara (F.112.b), the “teaching” refers here to the teaching on the absolute. RK: bstan pa ni don dam pa bstan pa’o. It also has not been actualized by anyone, nor is it actualized by anyone, nor will it be actualized by anyone. Also, through this Dharma teaching, no one has attained complete nirvāṇa, will attain complete nirvāṇa, or is attaining complete nirvāṇa. Furthermore, through this Dharma teaching, no one has been made a venerable person.”According to Ratnākara (F.112.b), the basic meaning of the latter part of the paragraph from “not actualized by anyone” up to “made venerable” is based on the nonexistence of precisely that thing that constitutes a person. RK: sus kyang bstan pa med ces bya ba la sogs pa nas| sbyin pa’i gnas su byas pa med do zhes bya ba ga las shes she na| gang zag gi dngos po ’di nyid med pa’i phyir ro zhes dgongs so.

From this point on until the end of the chapter, the content is equivalent to that of chapter 29 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of chapter 38 in the Eighteen Thousand. Note that the points presented in this section as simply being stated by Subhūti are presented in the long sūtras in the form of Subhūti and the Buddha in dialogue.At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, from the perspective of the nonexistence of space, it is a nonexistent transcendent state.Using the outline of the eight topics in the Ornament, Ratnākara (F.112.b-13.b) explains that this sentence begins the fourth of eight topics, namely the practice (prayoga) aimed at realizing all the aspects (sarvākārābhisambodhi) to be known by a buddha who achieves the “complete knowing of all aspects of everything” (sarvākārajñatā). According to Ratnākara, by definition these “aspects” are the perspectives by which one’s knowing of things is circumscribed when focused on meditative objects, such as the four noble truths and so on. The fourth topic speaks first of twenty-seven aspects (ākāra)‍—from the first aspect of things’ being “nonexistent” all the way up to their twenty-seventh aspect of being “unmoving”‍—that are realized by a buddha. Among the twenty-seven, the first twelve consist in four perspectives on the first three noble truths of suffering, source, and cessation. The latter fifteen concern the fourth noble truth, that of the path. In the first sentence here, the first, “nonexistent transcendent state” means the transcendent state of the aspect of nonexistence. All subsequent transcendent states should be understood this way too (i.e., the transcendent state of the corresponding aspect). The four aspects regarding the truth of suffering: “nonexistence,” “equality,” “voidness,” and “indestructibility” are explained in this paragraph. Here, when Subhūti says that the transcendent state of nonexistence is “like space,” it is because physical forms and so on are nonexistent. That is to say, in the same way that the nonexistence of any physical substance is the defining characteristic of space, so too the nonexistence of any inherent nature is the defining characteristic of physical forms and so on. RK: …asatpāramiteyam iti asadākārām ārabhya yāvad acalapāramiteyam ity acalākārāḥ| teṣu catvāraś catvāro duḥkhasamudayanirodhasatyeṣu pariviṣṭāḥ| pañcadaśa mārgasatye| asadākārapāramitā| asatpāramitā| evam uttarā api sarvāḥ| tatra duḥkhe catvāraḥ-asat-samatā-vivikta-anavamṛdya| ākāśasamatām iti| ākāśavad rūpāder asattām| yathā hy ākāśasya rūpidravyābhāvo lakṣaṇaṃ tathā rūpādīnāṃ svalakṣaṇābhāvaḥ. Blessed One, from the perspective of its freedom from the mental perception of any things, it is a transcendent state equal to the unequaled.According to Ratnākara (F.113.b), the “equality” of pleasure and pain is the freedom from mental perceptions of any things. RK: sarvadharmānupalabdhisamatām iti sarvadharmānupalabdhir eva sukhaduḥkhayoḥ samatā. Blessed One, from the perspective of its absolute emptiness, it is a void transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.113.b), “absolute emptiness” means the emptiness of both a person and an inherent nature. RK: atyantaśūnyatām iti pudgalena svabhāvena ca śūnyatām ity arthaḥ. Blessed One, from the perspective of its freedom from the mental perception of any things, it is a transcendent state that cannot be harmed.According to Ratnākara (F.113.b), the “transcendent state that cannot be harmed” means it has that as its aspect. “What cannot be harmed” means the lack of a self, because, while suffering is the cause of harm, a self is that which is to be harmed. Thus, we should understand “from the perspective of its freedom from the mental perception of any things” to refer to those things that harm or are harmed. RK: bādhakaṃ duḥkhaṃ bādhyatvād avamṛdya ātmā| anavamṛdyā ’nātmā| tadākāratvāt anavamṛdyapāramitā| sarvadharmāṇāṃ bādhyabādhakānām anupalabdhitāṃ nirūpalambhatāṃ upādāya.

“Blessed One, from the perspective of its not being mental or physical, it is a transcendent state free of any footing. Blessed One, from the perspective of its neither coming nor going, it is a transcendent state free of inherent nature. Blessed One, from the perspective of its freedom from conceptualizing any things, it is a transcendent state free of speaking. Blessed One, from the perspective of its freedom from the mental perception of any constituents underlying the clinging to existence, it is a transcendent state free of naming.According to Ratnākara (F.113.b), among the next four aspects, which concern the truth of the source, the “footing” is a cause, i.e., the previous constituents; the “inherent nature” is the thing that belongs to self, i.e., the activity of the body; “speaking” is the activity of the speech; and “naming” is the activity of the mind. The absence of these is, respectively, freedom from “footing”, “inherent nature”, “speaking”, and “naming.” It is called such and such transcendent state, because each transcendent state has one of these as its aspect. The phrase “not being mental or physical” means the absence of the “mental,” i.e., immaterial constituents, and “physical,” i.e., material constituents. Since “coming” and “going” are the activity of the body, “not coming or going” is referring to the absence of that. Since the cause of speech is conceptualization, “freedom from conceptualization” is referring to the absence of that. RK: samudaye catvāraḥ| tathā hi– padaṃ hetuḥ prāktanāḥ skandhāḥ. svabhāva ātmano bhāvaḥ kāyakarma| vacanaṃ vākkarma| nāma manaskarma kleśāś ca| eṣām abhāvo yathākramam| apadam, asvabhāvaḥ, avacanam, anāma| tadākāratvāt tatpāramitā| anāmāśarīratām iti| nāma arūpiṇaḥ skandhāḥ| śarīraṃ rūpaskandhaḥ| tayor abhāvo ’nāmaśarīratā| gatyāgatī kāyakarma| tadabhāvo ’nāgatir agatiś ca| vācāṃ hetur vikalpaḥ| tadabhāvo ’vikalpatā.

“Blessed One, from the perspective of the fact that no things are moving, it is an unmoving transcendent state. Blessed One, from the perspective of all things being ungraspable, it is an unseizable transcendent state. Blessed One, from the perspective of having the quality of not dissipating, it is a transcendent state that is nondissipating. Blessed One, from the perspective of things not coming into being, it is a transcendent state that does not arise.According to Ratnākara (F.113.b), the next group of four aspects concern the truth of cessation. To explain, “unmoving,” “unseizable,” “not dissipating,” and “nonarising” refer to the absence of, respectively, moving, being graspable, dissipating, and arising. It is called such-and-such transcendent state because each transcendent state has one of these as its aspect. The lack of moving is the fact that all things are unmoving. All things are ungraspable because one thing does not grasp another thing. The “association with a nondissipating thing” means it has no “association” with a “thing” that is “dissipating.” “Not coming into being” means the absence of arising. RK: nirodhe catvāraḥ| tathā hy asau gamanaṃ vā syāt grāhyaṃ vā kṣayo vā utpattir vā| eṣām abhāvo yathākramaṃ agamanaṃ, asaṃhāryaṃ, akṣayaḥ, anupapattiḥ| tadākāratvāt tatpāramitā| sarvadharmāṇām agamanatā gatyabhāvaḥ| sarvadharmāṇām agrāhyatā| na hi dharmo dharmaṃ gṛṇhātīti| kṣaya eva dharmaḥ| tena yogaḥ| tadabhāvād akṣayadharmayogatā| abhinirvṛttir utpattiḥ| tadabhāvād anabhinirvṛttitā.

“Blessed One, from the perspective of its freedom from the mental perception of any doer, it is a transcendent state free of doing.According to Ratnākara (F.113.b–14.a), the next fifteen aspects concern the truth of the path. Being “free of doing” is the path, because a doer is not mentally perceived by means of it. RK: akārako mārgas tena kārakānupalabdheḥ. Blessed One, from the perspective of the fact that no things have a self, it is a transcendent state free of a knower.According to Ratnākara (F.114.a), being “free of knowing,” i.e., not seeing, is the path, because a knower or seer is not mentally perceived by means of it. RK: ajānak āpaśyako mārgas tena jānakapaśyakānupalabdheḥ. Blessed One, from the perspective of its lack of death and rebirth, it is a transcendent state free of transmigration.According to Ratnākara (F.114.a), being “free of transmigration” is the path, because of seeing the impossibility of death and rebirth by means of it. RK: asaṃkrāntir mārgas tena cyutyupapattyor ayogadarśanāt. Blessed One, from the perspective of its freedom from the mental perception of past, present, or future objects, it is a transcendent state beyond taming.According to Ratnākara (F.114.a), being “beyond taming” is the path, because, by means of it, there is no conforming (vinaya) to the nature of things in the three times. RK: avinayo mārgas tena tryadhvasamāyā dharmaprakṛter avinayāt. Blessed One, from the perspective of its causing discernment of nonarising, it is a transcendent state like a dream, an echo, a reflected image, a mirage, and a magical illusion.According to Ratnākara (F.114.a), the path is “a dream,” because even without a seer, one sees the object. The path is “an echo,” because even though the object does not exist, one mentally perceives the object. The path is “a reflected image,” i.e., a reflection, because even though the object does not exist, one sees the object. The path is “a mirage,” because even though no constituent in its water exists, it appears to worldly people tormented by thirst for it. The path is “a magical illusion,” because it appears without any sign. The phrase “causing discernment of nonarising” means that these five metaphors illuminate the path as nonarising, because such and such appears even without a cause for appearing. RK: svapno mārgaḥ vinaiva draṣṭāram arthadarśanāt. pratiśrutko mārgaḥ, asati śabde śabdopalambhāt| pratibhāsaḥ pratibimbaṃ sa mārgaḥ, asaty arthe ’rthadarśanāt| marīcir mārgaḥ, asati tajjalaskandhe pipāsataptalokadhātoḥ pratibhāsāt| māyā mārgo ’nimittā pratibhāsāt| anutpādavijñāpanatām iti yataḥ pañcabhir apy ebhir anutpādo mārga ādīpyate| pratibhāsahetum antareṇa tathā tathā pratibhāsāt. Blessed One, from the perspective of its not having the nature of attachment, hatred, or confusion, it is an undefiled transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.114.a), the path is “undefiled” because, by means of it, “attachment” and so on are not mentally perceived. RK: asaṃkleśo mārgas tena rāgādyanupalabdheḥ. Blessed One, from the perspective of there being no mental perception of its basis, it is a nonpurifying transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.114.a), the path is “nonpurifying” because, by means of it, a defiled sentient being (i.e., the basis to be purified) is not mentally perceived. RK: avyavadānaṃ mārgas tena kliṣṭasattvānupalabdheḥ. Blessed One, from the perspective of space being free of stains, it is an unstained transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.114.a), the path is “unstained” because the ultimate realm of things, like space, appears free of stains by means of it. RK: anupalepo mārgaḥ, ākāśavan nirupalepasya dharmadhātos tena darśanāt. Blessed One, from the perspective of its going beyond thinking of any things, it is an unproliferating transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.114.a), the path is “unproliferating” because it is the antidote to proliferation. RK: aprapañco mārgaḥ prapañcapratipakṣatvāt. Blessed One, from the perspective of its unmoving quality, it is a transcendent state free of developing conceits.According to Ratnākara (F.114.a), the path is “free of developing conceits” because it is the remover of all developments of conceit. RK: amananā mārgaḥ, sarvamananāsamudghātitvāt. Blessed One, from the perspective of its remaining in the ultimate realm of things, it is an unshaken transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.114.a–b), the path is “unshaken,” because always remains as the nature of the ultimate realm of things. This ends the explanation of twenty-seven aspects in (a worthy one’s) complete knowing. RK: acalo mārgaḥ, dharmadhātuśarīratvena śāśvatatvāt| iti sarvajñatākārāḥ.

“Blessed One, from the perspective of its freedom from any false nature of all things, it is a transcendent state free of attachment.According to Ratnākara (F.114.b), next are the thirty-six aspects in (a bodhisattva’s) complete knowing of the paths. In the corresponding verse of the Ornament, the word “cause” refers to the source (of suffering). There, the (noble truths of) the source and path are explained first as a means of approaching the result. After that, the (noble truths of) suffering and cessation are explained, because they are the result. Among those, there are, respectively, eight aspects (source), seven aspects (path), five aspects (suffering), and sixteen aspects (cessation). In this scripture, the eight aspects (to be discerned) about the source of suffering are (the following six aspects): free of attachment, not an origination, null, free of faults, free of emotional defilements, and without existence. But these (six) represent eight aspects, because “free of faults” includes the three (poisons), namely being free of attachment, hatred, and confusion. The source (of suffering) is the (five) former constituents. In Sanskrit etymology, the word rāga (“attachment”) should be understood to mean that the constituents “are dyed” (rājyante) in it. The “things” (in this sentence) have a nature that is imagined. (One should discern the aspect of) the source as free of attachment. RK: atha mārgajñatākārāḥ ṣaṭtriṃśat| hetuḥ samudayaḥ| ataḥ samudayamārgau prathamata uktau phalopāyatvāt, duḥkhanirodhau paścād uktau phalatvāt, teṣv ākārā yathākramam| aṣṭau sapta pañca ṣoḍaśeti ca kīrtītāḥ sūtre tatra samudaye ’ṣṭau-virāga-asamutthāna-śānta-nirdoṣa-niṣkleśa-niḥsattva| kathaṃ tadaṣṭau ? yato nirdoṣe tritayaḥ-arāga-adveṣa-amoha| prāktanā hi pañcaskandhāḥ samudayaḥ| rajyante ’sminn iti rāgaḥ| parikalpitaḥ svabhāvo dharmāṇām| vi gato rāgo ’smād iti virāgaḥ samudayaḥ. Blessed One, from the perspective of its freedom from conceptualizing all things, it is a transcendent state that is free of origination.According to Ratnākara (F.114.b), “the freedom from any false nature of all things” refers to the emptiness of the false nature, i.e., imagined nature. RK: sarvadharmāṇām avitathatām iti vitathena kalpitena svabhāvena śūnyatām. Blessed One, from the perspective of its freedom from mentally perceiving signs of any things, it is a null transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.114.b), “origination” refers to the cause of defilement, i.e., conceptualization. (A bodhisattva discerns the aspect of) the source as “free of origination” because there is no conceptualization in it. It is “free of conceptualizing all things” because it is not a grasper. RK: samutthānaṃ kleśahetur vikalpaḥ| tasyāsminn abhāvād asamutthānaḥ samudayaḥ| sarvadharmāṇāṃ nirvikalpatām iti agrāhakatvāt. Blessed One, from the perspective of the transcendent state of positive qualities, it is a transcendent state free of faults.According to Ratnākara (F.114.b), (a bodhisattva discerns the aspect of) source as “null,” i.e., as cessation, because when the ultimate truth appears, there is no mental perception of all the signs of things as appearances of things. For this very reason, it is the transcendent state free of (the “faults”) of attachment, aversion, and confusion. RK: śāntaḥ samudayaḥ, nirodha ity arthaḥ| tatkutaḥ? sarveṣāṃ dharmanimittānāṃ dharmapratibhāsānāṃ paramārthapratibhāse ’nupalabdheḥ| ata eva arāgapāramitā adoṣapāramitā amohapāramitā ca. Blessed One, from the perspective of its absence of imagination, it is a transcendent state free of emotional defilements.According to Ratnākara (F.114.b–15.a), “transcendent state of positive qualities” means the transcendent state in which attachment, aversion, and confusion are destroyed. (A bodhisattva discerns the aspect of) the source as free of emotional defilements, because all defilements are absent. The “absence of imagination” means the absence of a grasper. RK: guṇapāramitām iti rāgadoṣamohakṣayapāramitām| sarvakleśābhāvān niṣkleśaḥ samudayaḥ| parikalpāsattām iti grāhakāsattām. Blessed One, from the perspective of the pinnacle of reality, it is a transcendent state free of a sentient being.According to Ratnākara (F.114.b–15.a), also, a sentient being is someone afflicted and suffering. (A bodhisattva discerns the aspect of) the source as free of a sentient being. The “pinnacle of reality” refers to the emptiness of persons and things. RK: api ca sattvo vikliṣṭaḥ syād duḥkhī ca | tadabhāvān niḥsattvaḥ samudayaḥ| bhūtakoṭitām iti dharmapudgalaśūnyatām.

“Blessed One, from the perspective of there being no cause for the cause of all things, it is a transcendent state beyond measure.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), now regarding the path, the seven aspects (discerned by bodhisattvas) are: beyond measure, not conforming to the two extremes, undivided, unaffected, nonconceptual, immeasurable, and unattached. Among those, the path is “beyond measure” from the perspective of its false nature, i.e., “the lack of a cause for the cause of all things.” RK: mārge sapteti-apramāṇa-antadvayānanugama-asambhinna-aparamṛṣṭa-avikalpa-aprameya-asaṅga| apramāṇo mārgaḥ| sarvadharmāṇāṃ samutthānasyāsamutthānatām alīkatvam upādāya. Blessed One, from the perspective of its not fixating on all things, it is a transcendent state not conforming to the two extremes.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), the path is “not conforming to the two extremes.” It is “not fixating on all things” because of its emptiness of existent things as they appear and its nonconformance with nonexistent things. RK: antadvayānanugamo mārgaḥ| sarvadharmeṣv anabhiniveśatām iti| yathāpratibhāsaṃ bhāvasya tacchūnyatayā cābhāvasyānanugamaḥ (sic!). Blessed One, from the perspective of its not differentiating any things, it is an undivided transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), the path is “undivided.” Being “not differentiating” means not mentally perceiving distinction. RK: asambhinno mārgaḥ| asambhedanatām iti bhedānupalabdhim. Blessed One, from the perspective of its not wishing for any levels of the disciples or solitary realizers, it is an unaffected transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), the path is “unaffected” because through it, there is no longing for the disciples’ path. RK: aparāmṛṣṭo mārgas tena hīnayānāspṛhaṇāt. Blessed One, from the perspective of the equality of conceptualizations, it is a nonconceptual transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), the path is “nonconceptual.” The “equality of conceptualizations” means not mentally perceiving conceptualizations. RK: avikalpo mārgaḥ| vikalpasamatām iti vikalpānām anupalabdhim. Blessed One, from the perspective of the true nature of things beyond measure, it is an immeasurable transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), the path is “immeasurable.” From the perspective of “the true nature of things beyond measure” means because the meditative object is the true nature of things beyond measure. RK: aprameyo mārgaḥ| apramāṇadharmatām upādāye ti| apramāṇadharmatālambanatvāt. Blessed One, from the perspective of its freedom from attachment to any things, it is an unattached transcendent state.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), the path is “unattached.” The “nonattachment to all things” means the freedom from mental perceptions. RK: asaṅgo mārgaḥ| sarvadharmeṣv asaṅgatām iti nirupalambhatām.

“Blessed One, from the perspective of all things being unconditioned, it is a transcendent state of the impermanent.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), the five aspects concerning (the truth of) suffering are impermanent, suffering, empty, no-self, and lacking any characteristic. “The impermanent” are simply not permanent. Hence, “all things being unconditioned” means they do not arise in the manner they appear. RK: duḥkhe pañca-anitya-duḥkha-śūnya-anātma-lakṣaṇa| sāsravāḥ pañcaskandhā duḥkham| tad anityaṃ nityaṃ na bhavaty eveti | sarvadharmāṇām asaṃskṛtatām iti yathāpratibhāsam anutpannatām. Blessed One, from the perspective of the true nature of things being like space, it is a transcendent state of suffering.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), “suffering” is (discerned in the noble truth of) suffering because, like space, it lacks the characteristic of happiness. RK: duḥkhaṃ duḥkhaṃ sukhavilakṣaṇatvād ākāśavat. Blessed One, from the perspective of all things not being mentally perceived, it is a transcendent state of the empty.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), the “empty” is (discerned in the noble truth of) suffering because nothing is mentally perceived. RK: śūnyaṃ duḥkhaṃ sarvadharmānupalabdheḥ. Blessed One, from the perspective of its not fixating upon any things, it is a transcendent state of no-self.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), “no-self” is (discerned in the noble truth of) suffering because of the impossibility of fixating on anything. RK: anātmā duḥkhaṃ yathāpratibhāsaṃ sarvadharmeṣv abhiniveśāyogāt. Blessed One, from the perspective of no things coming into being, it is a transcendent state without any characteristic.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a), “lacking any characteristic” is (discerned in the noble truth of) suffering because of the absence of the three characteristics of conditioned things, i.e., birth, aging, and impermanence. Things “not coming into being” means their being unconditioned in accordance with both their appearance and the ultimate reality. RK: trīṇi saṃskṛtalakṣaṇāni| jātir jarā ’nityatā ca| tadabhāvād alakṣaṇaṃ duḥkham| anabhinivṛttitām iti yathāpratibhāsaṃ yathāparamārthaṃ cāsaṃskṛtatām.

“Blessed One, from the perspective of its being infinite and limitless, it is a transcendent state of all the emptinesses.According to Ratnākara (F.115.a–b), there are sixteen aspects concerning (the truth of) cessation. To explain, cessation refers to mere “emptiness” that is pure. And emptiness is undivided, because there is no mental perception of any dividers. Thus, like space, (the truth of) cessation is “infinite and limitless.” The sixteen emptinesses, which are homogenous, are (listed) in order because they are delimited by stage and amplitude. For this reason, he says this sentence refers to the (sixteen emptinesses), from the transcendent state of internal emptiness, the transcendent state of external emptiness, and so on, up to transcendent state of the emptiness of the nature of nonexistence. For this reason, there are sixteen aspects in the truth of cessation. This ends the section on the aspects of (a bodhisattva’s) complete knowing of the path. RK: nirodhe ṣoḍaśa| tathā hi nirodhaḥ śūnyata iva viśuddhā| na ca śūnyatā bhidyate bhedakānām anupalabdheḥ| tasmād ākāśavad anantāparyanto nirodhaḥ| anukrameṇa vistāreṇa vā paricchedād yathākramaṃ tataḥ ṣoḍaśa śūnyatāḥ santy ekarasāḥ| ata āha| sarva­śūnyatā­pāramiteyam anantāparyantatām upādāye ti| adhyātmaśūnyatā­pāramiteyaṃ bahirdhāśūnyatā­pāramiteyaṃ yāvad abhāvasvabhāva­śūnyatā­pāramitetyādi| ataś ca nirodhasatye ṣoḍaśākārāḥ| iti mārgajñatākārāḥ.
