Blessed One, from the perspective of their not being mentally perceived, it is a transcendent state of the factors in awakening, beginning with the applications of mindfulness and so on.According to Ratnākara (F.115.b), from this sentence mentioning the “applications of mindfulness” up to the end of the chapter, Subhūti speaks of the 110 aspects of (a buddha’s) complete knowing of all aspects (distinctly from those just mentioned). The total of 110 aspects known by (a buddha’s) knowing of all aspects (includes thirty-seven aspects of a disciple, thirty-four aspects of a bodhisattva, and thirty-nine aspects of a buddha, i.e., 37 + 34 + 39 = 110). Among these, the first are the thirty-seven “factors in awakening” (bodhipakṣadharma), which are subdivided into seven subgroups “beginning with the applications of mindfulness (smṛtyupasthānam) and so on.” Since these individually are not mentally perceived, they are called “transcendent states of” such and such (within the thirty-seven factors) because each of the transcendent states has that as its aspect. For details of the thirty-seven, see n.219. RK: sarvākārajñatākārāḥ daśottaraṃ śatam…tān adhikṛtya smṛtyupasthānā d ītyādikaṃ āparivartasamāpteḥ| smṛtyupasthānādibodhi­pakṣya­dharma-pāramiteyam iti smṛtyupasthānādayaḥ saptavargā anekaśo vibhaktāḥ saptatriṃśadbodhipakṣyā dharmā bhavanti| pratyekaṃ teṣām anupalambhāt tadākārāḥ pāramitāḥ.

“Blessed One, from the perspective of the three gateways to complete liberation not being mentally perceived, it is an empty, signless, and uninclined transcendent state. Blessed One, from the perspective of its not being mentally perceived, it is a transcendent state of complete liberation.According to Ratnākara (F.115.b–16.a), it is the three “gateways to complete liberation”‍—namely, (1) the transcendent state of emptiness, (2) the transcendent state of the freedom from signs, and (3) the transcendent state of freedom from inclination‍—because these three are not mentally perceived. It is the “eight liberations”‍—namely, (1) the transcendent state of the first (subtle form) liberation, (2) the transcendent state of the second (formless) liberation, (3) the transcendent state of the bright liberation, (4) the transcendent state of liberation of the stage of limitless space, (5) the transcendent state of liberation of the stage of limitless consciousness, (6) the transcendent state of liberation of the stage of nothing whatsoever, (7) the transcendent state of liberation of the stage of neither conception nor no conception, and (8) the transcendent state of liberation of the stage of the cessation of feeling and conception‍—because these eight are not mentally perceived. RK: |de nas rnam par thar pa’i sgo gsum ni stong pa nyid kyi pha rol tu phyin pa dang| mtshan ma med pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| smon pa med pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa yin te| rnam par thar pa’i sgo mi dmigs pa’i phyir ro| rnam par thar pa brgyad ni dang po’i rnam par thar pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| gnyis pa’i rnam par thar pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| sdug pa’i rnam par thar pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| nam mkha’ mtha’ yas skye mched kyi rnam par thar pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| rnam shes mtha’ yas skye mched kyi rnam par thar pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| cung zad med pa’i skye mched kyi rnam par thar pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| ’du shes med ’du shes med min skye mched kyi rnam par thar pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| ’du shes dang tshor ba ’gog pa’i rnam par thar pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa yin te| rnam par thar pa brgyad mi dmigs pa’i phyir ro. Blessed One, from the perspective of the first meditative state and so on not being mentally perceived, it is a transcendent state of the nine incremental states of abiding.According to Ratnākara (F.116.a), it is the transcendent state of “the nine incremental states of abiding”‍— including (1–4) the transcendent states of abiding in the first through the fourth meditative states (dhyāna) in the subtle form realm (see n.381), and (5–9) the transcendent states of abiding in the stages of limitless space, limitless consciousness, nothing whatsoever, neither conception nor no conception, and the cessation of feeling and conception (see n.381)‍—because they too are not mentally perceived. RK: mthar gyis gnas pa’i snyoms par ’jug pa dgu ni de’i phyir bsam gtan dang po la gnas pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa nas bsam gtan bzhi pa la gnas pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa zhes bya ba’i bar dang| nam mkha’ mtha’ yas skye mched la gnas pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa nas ’du shes dang tshor ba ’gog pa la gnas pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa zhes bya ba yin te| bsam gtan dang po la sogs pa mi dmigs pa’i phyir ro. Blessed One, from the perspective of suffering and so on not being mentally perceived, it is a transcendent state of the four truths.According to Ratnākara (F.116.a), it is the transcendent state of the four noble truths‍—suffering, source, cessation, and path‍—because they are also not mentally perceived. RK: sdug bsngal gyi pha rol tu phyin pa dang| kun ’byung gi pha rol tu phyin pa dang| ’gog pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| lam gyi pha rol tu phyin pa dang| bden pa bzhi mi dmigs pa’i phyir ro. Blessed One, from the perspective of the transcendent state of generosity and so on not being mentally perceived, it is the transcendent state of the ten.According to Ratnākara (F.116.a), this sentence refers to the transcendent states (associated with) the ten (levels of a bodhisattva)‍—namely, generosity, ethical discipline, tolerant acceptance, heroic perseverance, meditation, wisdom, skill in means, aspiration, strength, and knowledge‍—because nothing that runs contrary to them is mentally perceived. The last thirty-four aspects mentioned up to here concern those on the bodhisattva path. RK: pha rol tu phyin pa bcu ste| de’i phyir sbyin pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa nas thabs dang smon lam dang| stobs dang ye shes kyi pha rol tu phyin pa yin te| de dag gi mi mthun pa’i phyogs de mi dmigs pa’i phyir ro| |de ni byang chub sems dpa’i lam la gnas pa’i rnam pa sum cu rtsa bzhi’o.

“Blessed One, from the perspective of its inability to be harmed, it is a transcendent state of power.According to Ratnākara (F.116.a), the “transcendent state of power” refers to the transcendent state of the ten powers, namely, from the transcendent state of the power of knowing what occurs and what cannot occur, up to the transcendent state of the power of knowing the extinction of worldly influences. The phrase “from the perspective of its inability to be harmed” (anavamṛdyatā) also signifies (a buddha’s) power. For the ten powers, see n.124. RK: |stobs kyi pha rol tu phyin pa ni stobs bcu ste de’i phyir gnas dang gnas ma yin pa mkhyen pa’i stobs kyi pha rol tu phyin pa nas zag pa zad pa mkhyen pa’i stobs kyi pha rol tu phyin pa’i bar du’o| |mi brdzi ba’i slad du’o zhes bya ba de ni yang stobs kyi don to. Note: The thirty-nine aspects of a buddha begins here, but Ratnākara only mentions this at the end of the chapter. Blessed One, from the perspective of its absolutely not being deflated, it is a transcendent state of self-confidence.According to Ratnākara (F.116.a–b), the “transcendent state of self-confidence” has four aspects: (1) the transcendent state of self-confidence of being perfectly awake, (2) the transcendent state of self-confidence that all worldly influences are extinguished, (3) the transcendent state of self-confidence about things leading beyond cyclic existence, and (4) the transcendent state of self-confidence about the things impeding awakening. “Absolutely not being deflated” means self-confident. (Note: Elsewhere the third and fourth are in reverse order.) RK: |mi ’jigs pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa ni rnam pa bzhi ste| mngon par rdzogs par byang chub pa la mi ’jigs pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| zag pa zad pa la mi ’jigs pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| nges par ’byung ba’i chos rnams la mi ’jigs pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| bar du gcod pa’i chos rnams la mi ’jigs pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa’i shin tu ma zhum pa zhes bya ba ni mi ’jigs pa’i don to. Blessed One, from the perspective of its lack of attachment or enmity toward the state of complete knowing, it is a transcendent state of experiential knowledge.According to Ratnākara (F.116.b), the “transcendent state of the types of experiential knowledge” are (1) the transcendent state of the experiential knowledge of things (dharma), (2) the transcendent state of the experiential knowledge of meanings (artha), (3) the transcendent state of the experiential knowledge of terminological interpretation (nirukti), and (4) the transcendent state of the experiential knowledge of illuminating (pratibhāna). The things, meanings, terms, and illumination are both understood and known experientially. “The lack of attachments or grudges” (pratighātitā) means having the power to not be attached or angry, which is the meaning of the “experiential knowledge.” RK: |so so yang dag par rig pa ni chos so so yang dag par rig pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| don so so yang dag par rig pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| nges pa’i tshig so so yang dag par rig pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa dang| spobs pa so so yang dag par rig pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa’o| |chos thams cad dang| don dang| nges pa’i tshig dang| spobs pa rnams shes shing yang dag par rig pa’o| |de la chags pa med pa dang| thogs pa med pa zhes bya ba ni de la chags pa med pa dang| thogs pa med par nus pa’o| |de ni so so yang dag par rig pa’i don to. Blessed One, from the perspective of its having transcended quantification, it is a transcendent state of all the distinctive qualities of a buddha.According to Ratnākara (F.116.b), this refers to the transcendent state of the eighteen distinctive qualities of a buddha. They are “distinctive” because these qualities are not shared by others. They are “transcendent states” because they have these qualities as their aspects. The transcendent states of a buddha’s eighteen distinctive qualities are (1–3) the three transcendent states of freedom from error, from useless speech, and from memory failures; (4–6) the three transcendent states of freedom from an unconcentrated mindset, from conceiving differences, and from unconsidered indifference; (7–12) the six transcendent states of freedom from waning interest, heroic deeds, mindfulness, concentration, wisdom, and liberation; (13–15) the three transcendent states in which all his physical, verbal, and mental activities are accompanied and preceded by awareness; and (16–18) the three transcendent states in which his seeing and being aware of the past, present, and future are unattached and unhindered. In the phrase “having transcended quantification” the word “quantification” means thinking about what it is like. That he has transcended such thinking indicates something unshared with others (i.e., something distinctive to a buddha). Note: see also note 120. RK: sangs rgyas kyi chos ma ’dres pa thams cad kyi pha rol tu phyin pa zhes bya ba la| sangs rgyas kyi chos kyang yin la ma ’dres pa yang yin pas na| sangs rgyas kyi chos ma ’dres pa’ o| |gzhan dag dang thun mong ma yin pa’i phyir de ni sangs rgyas kyi chos ma ’dres pa’o| |thams cad kyang yin la sangs rgyas kyi chos ma ’dres pa thams cad do| sangs rgyas kyi chos ma ’dres pa bco brgyad ces bya ba’i don to| |de dag gi rnam pa nyid kyi phyir pha rol tu phyin pa’o| |’di lta ste| ’khrul pa med pa dang| ca co med pa dang| dran pa nyams pa med pa zhes bya ba pha rol tu phyin pa gsum dang| thugs mnyam par ma bzhag pa mi mnga’ ba dang| tha dad pa’i ’du shes mi mnga’ ba dang| so sor ma brtags pa’i btang snyoms mi mnga’ ba’i pha rol tu phyin pa gsum dang| ’dun pa dang| brtson ’grus dang| dran pa dang| ting nge ’dzin dang| shes rab dang| rnam par grol ba nyams pa mi mnga’ ba’i pha rol tu phyin pa drug dang| lus dang ngag dang| yid kyi las thams cad ye shes sngon du ’gro zhing ye shes kyi rjes su ’brang ba gsum dang| dus gsum thams cad la ma chags ma thogs pa’i ye shes ’jug pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa gsum ste de ni sangs rgyas kyi chos ma ’dres pa bco brgyad kyi pha rol tu phyin pa’o| |bgrang ba’i tshul las shin tu ’das pa zhes bya ba la ’dra bar sems pa ni bgrang ba’o| |de’i tshul ni ldan pa ste ’dres pa’o| |de las shin tu ’das pa ni ’das pa ste| de ni ma ’dres pa’i don to. Blessed One, from the perspective of all the teachings not being false, it is a transcendent state of the actual nature of the Tathāgata.The point in this sentence revolves around the similar Sanskrit etymology of the words “as they are” (tathā), “actual nature” (tathatā), and “actualized one” (tathāgata), and the relationship between dharma as “teaching” and “things” as well as dharmatā as “true nature”‍—all of which are difficult to explain in translation. Briefly, according to Ratnākara (F.116.b–17.a), since the Tathāgata taught the way things are in reality, he is the Tathāgata (viz. he who realized the way things are). His actual nature (viz. being the way things are) is the nature of the Tathāgata (viz. the nature of realizing the way things are), because his nature is that transcendent state. “All the teachings” (sarvadharma) here means the Buddha’s teachings. Their nature is “nonfalse” because they are only true. RK: ji lta ba’i chos de kho na bzhin du gsungs pas na de bzhin gshegs pa’o| |de’i de bzhin nyid ni de bzhin gshegs pa nyid de de’i ngo bo nyid de ni pha rol tu phyin pa’o| |chos thams cad ni sangs rgyas thams cad kyi bka’o| |de dag gis ma log pa de bzhin nyid ni bden pa nyid kyi phyir ro. According to Hari (456), regarding the phrase “the actual nature of the Tathagāta,” it is “from the perspective of their not being false”‍—i.e., because no characteristic or meaning was lacking‍—in all the teachings taught by the Tathāgata that there is a single aspect of the actual nature taught by all the buddhas. Hari: tathāgatatathate ti sarvasya dharmasya buddhair bhagavadbhir bhāṣitasya lakṣaṇārthāvaikṛtyād avitathatām upādāya sarva­buddha­bhāṣitatathatākāra ekaḥ. Blessed One, from the perspective of all things lacking inherent nature, it is a transcendent state of self-existence.According to Hari (456), “from the perspective of all things lacking inherent nature,” i.e., because the realization of that is not dependent on anything else, the single aspect of “self-existence” belongs to those who are not subject to (i.e., independent of) all things. Hari: svayambhūpāramiteyam iti sarva­dharmāsvabhāvatām upādāya tadadhigamasyāpara­pratyayatvāt sarva­dharmavaśavartina svayambhvākāra ekaḥ. Note: Ratnākara’s (F.117.a) comments here are not translated because they interweave Sanskrit grammatical principles and etymological glosses with hermeneutics aimed at justifying his own philosophical viewpoint against Haribhadra’s interpretation, and too much background would be necessary to explain them. Blessed One, from the perspective of its complete knowing of all aspects of all things, it is a transcendent state of the knowing of someone who has complete knowing, namely, the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.117.a), “knowing of someone who has complete knowing” is a synonym of buddhahood. For this reason, (the corresponding passages of) the large (25,000- and 100,000-line) Transcendent State of Wisdom scriptures conclude instead by referring to buddhahood rather than the words “knowing of someone who has complete knowing” (see the Twenty-Five Thousand, 29.91, and Hundred Thousand, <ref [end ch. 29] nya, F.348.a>). Regarding the phrase “the complete knowing of all aspects of all things,” we should understand it to mean “the complete knowing of” the nature of all things and “all the aspects of” the nature of “all things.” “From the perspective of its complete knowing” means from the perspective of its very nature. These thirty-nine aspects of someone abiding on the path of a buddha are the aspects known by the complete knowing. If one combines these (thirty-nine) aspects (with the thirty-seven aspects of the fruition of a disciple and the thirty-four aspects of the fruition of a bodhisattva), then it makes these 110 aspects known by (a buddha’s) knowing of all aspects. However, in the three types of complete knowing (i.e., that of buddha with 110 aspects, of a bodhisattva with 36 aspects, and a worthy one (arhat) with 27 aspects), there are a total of 173 (for details see Brunnhölzl 2011, vol. 2, pp. 296–7). RK: thams cad mkhyen pa’i ye shes ni sangs rgyas nyid ces bya ste don gcig go| |de’i phyir yum chen mo dag las sangs rgyas nyid kyi rnam pa zhes tha mar gsungs so| |’dir ni rnam pa thams cad mkhyen pa’i pha rol tu phyin pa’o zhes bstan te| |chos thams cad kyi ngo bo nyid dang| rnam pa thams cad ni chos thams cad kyi ngo bo nyid kyi rnam pa thams cad do| |de dag mkhyen pa ni de’i ngo bo ste| de nyid de de’i slad du’o| |sangs rgyas kyi lam la brten pa sum cu rtsa dgu ni rnam pa thams cad mkhyen pa’i rnam pa’o| |rnam kun mkhyen nyid rnam pa la| |gcig tu bsdus na brgya dang bcu|| thams cad mkhyen nyid rnam gsum la| |brgya dang bdun cu rtsa gsum mo.
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Praise,” the ninth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 10: Extolling the Positive Qualities of RecollectionThe beginning of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of chapter 30 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of chapter 39 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: Then it occurred to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Blessed One, those noble sons or daughters who come even within earshot of this transcendent state of wisdom will have served the former conquerors, planted the roots of virtue under numerous buddhas, and have been accepted by virtuous friends. So, Blessed One, what more needs to be said about those noble sons or daughters who take in the transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. By taking it in, keeping it in mind, chanting it, mastering it, passing it on, teaching it, expounding it, explaining it, and reciting it, they will be training to attain the actual nature, progressing to attain the actual nature, and engaging in meditation to attain the actual nature.According to Hari (245), the “actual nature” (tathatvam) means the nature (bhāva) as it is (tathā), in the sense of not being otherwise. (This interpretation holds true even though the Sanskrit reads as adjectival tatha instead of adverbial tathā). The three verbs “training,” “progressing,” and “engaging in meditation” are all to be described at the level of preliminary practice with that (actual nature) as the motivating factor. Hari: tathatvāya śikṣiṣyanta ityādi. tathābhāvas tathatvam ananyathārthena. ata eva nirdeśādd hrasvatvaṃ, tannimittaṃ śikṣiśyanta ityādipadatrayaṃ prayogādyavasthāsu vācyaṃ. According to Ratnākara (F.117.b), “training” means through tranquility (shamatha); “progressing” means through extraordinary insight (vipaśyanā; lhag mthong); and “engaging in meditation” means through tranquility and extraordinary insight together. RK: de bzhin nyid du slob pa dang zhes bya ba ni zhi gnas kyis so| |bsgrub pa dang zhes bya ba ni lhag mthong gis so| |rnal ’byor du byed pa zhes bya ba ni zhi gnas dang lhag mthong gis so. Note: The translation “to attain the actual nature” (instead of the more fluid “to actualize it”) has been used so that, in accordance with the commentators, readers can connect this discourse about tathatvam and tathatā, both of which are translated “actual nature.” Cf. Macdonell, p. 189, where the dative case “for” can be interpreted to mean “to attain” and hence the translation “train to attain the actual nature” or “train to actualize it.” Cf. also PTSD sikkhati + dat., “train oneself for the purpose of…”. BHSD vinayāya sikkhati, “train oneself to give up.” Such noble sons and daughters wsill have not merely a small root of virtue. They will come to closely attend numerous buddhas.According Hari (245), “closely attend” means by donating to the tathāgatas’ retinues, by listening to what they taught, and through offerings to venerate them. (Hari also comments on why the Sanskrit compound word should be understood the way it is translated above). Hari: tathāgataparyupāsitā iti tāthāgatāḥ parivārapradānena taduktaśravaṇena ca pūjādibhiś ca paryupāsitā yais te tathoktāḥ. niṣṭhāntaṃ pūrvaṃ nipatatīti vyabhicāralakṣaṇatvāt paryupāsita śabdasya na pūrva nipātaḥ. They will ask the blessed buddhas about this very transcendent state of wisdom and fully pursue their questions with them about it.Regarding the immediately subsequent usage of these terms, according to Ratnākara (F.118.a) “ask” means questioning all points about which they are uncertain; “fully pursue their questions” means doing the same with regard to all answers (to those previous questions) about which they are uncertain. RK: |yongs su dris pa dang| yongs su dri bar bgyis pa zhes bya ba ni the tshom gyi gnas thams cad yongs su dris pa dang| gang gis the tshom gyi lan de byas pas ni yongs su dris par byas pa’o. Also, having already listened to this transcendent state of wisdom in the presence of the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, they will listen to it again at that time. And after doing so, they will take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. They will also train to attain the actual nature, progress to attain the actual nature, and engage in meditation to attain the actual nature. In this respect, these noble sons or daughters should be understood as having planted the roots of virtue under numerous buddhas. When this very profound transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, taught, expounded, explained, and recited, they will not become deflated, depressed, dejected, or sunk in despondency, and their mental attitudes will not become discouraged, shattered, frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror.”

Then the venerable Śāriputra knew in his mind the mental ruminations of Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, and addressed the Blessed One: “O Blessed One, when this very profound transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, taught, and expounded, a noble son or daughter has intense faith in it, deep belief in it, firm conviction in it, a clear mindset that trusts in it, and develops the proper mindset for awakening. If so, he or she will take in the transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, train to attain the actual nature, progress to attain the actual nature, and engage in meditation to attain the actual nature. Anyone who does so in this way is to be considered a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing. Why is that? Because, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound, and, Blessed One, to have such firm conviction in this very transcendent state of wisdom would be impossible if someone had inadequate roots of virtue, had not asked about it, had not previously heard it, and had not previously gone about life in the presence of the blessed buddhas. However, if a noble son or daughter were to have no conviction in it, were to have no understanding of it, and were to consider it something to be rejected, then they would also have to have previously rejected this profound transcendent state of wisdom while it was being discussed. Why is that? It is simply because, Blessed One, to have firm conviction in this profound transcendent state of wisdom would be impossible for anyone who did not previously go about life in it, because their roots of virtue would be limited. Furthermore, those who are rejecting this profound transcendent state of wisdom now as it is being discussed in this way should be understood to have rejected this profound transcendent state of wisdom in the past. This is because when this profound transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, they have no faith in it, no acceptance of it, no predilection for it, no appetite for it, no verve in it, no care for it, and no firm conviction in it,Ratnākara suggests here that the problem is not just that these people lack faith, etc., but actually that they have faith and so on in the opposite of what the transcendent state of wisdom is teaching. That is to say, according to Ratnākara (F.118.a), their “faith” is believing in a false nature (vitathatā); their “acceptance” involves a wish to grasp that; their “predilection” is a wish to not let go; their “appetite” is a desire to listen to words and meanings; their “verve” is enthusiasm (for that); their “care” is for merit to serve as an antidote for attachment, aggression, confusion, and laziness; their “firm conviction” is in an objective reality (dngos po) established by direct perception, inference, and scriptural authority that they can grasp, saying, “It is this way and cannot be any other way.” RK: |de kho na nyid ma yin par yang dag par yid ches pa ni dad pa’o| ’dzin par ’dod pa ni bzod pa’o| spong bar mi ’dod pa ni sred pa| tshig dang don nyan par ’dod pa ni ’dun pa’o| brtson ’grus ni spro ba’o| |dge ba’i rtsa ba’i bar du gcod pa chags pa dang| zhe sdang dang| gti mug dang| le lo’i gnyen po ni bag yod pa’o| |mngon sum dang| rjes su dpag pa dang| yid ches pa’i lung gis nges pa’i dngos po la ’di nyid yin gyi gzhan du ni ma yin no zhes nges par ’dzin pa ni mos pa’o| |mi ’phrogs pa’i rten byed pa’i las can te| de gtso bor byed pa ni pha rol rgol ba la sogs pas mi ’phrogs pa’i phyir ro. as a result of their previously not having asked any blessed buddhas or their disciples about it, or having pursued with them their questions until they were fully addressed.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the venerable Śāriputra, “Noble Śāriputra, this transcendent state of wisdom is profound. It is not at all surprising that any bodhisattva great being who has not previously gone about life in this profound transcendent state of wisdom would not have conviction in it, when it is being taught.”

Next, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, I pay homage to the transcendent state of wisdom. Blessed One, anyone paying homage to the transcendent state of wisdom is paying homage to the knowing of someone who has complete knowing.”

“That is true, Kauśika, that is true,” said the Blessed One. “Kauśika, anyone paying homage to the transcendent state of wisdom is paying homage to the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. Why is this true? Because, Kauśika, the state of complete knowing belonging to the blessed buddhas is born from the transcendent state of wisdom, and the transcendent state of wisdom is manifested again as something born from the knowing of someone who has complete knowing.According to Ratnākara (F.118.a), the point of the homage is to demonstrate the mutual causality of the transcendent state of wisdom and the knowing of someone who has complete knowing, because that transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to obtain. RK: shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa dang thams cad mkhyen pa phan tshun rgyu nyid du nges par bstan pa la phyag bya ba’i don te de ni rnyed par dka’ ba nyid do. According to Hari (463), since buddhahood is attained by repeatedly practicing the transcendent state of wisdom at the level of a bodhisattva, the “state of complete knowing” is (said to be) “born from” it. Since “the transcendent state of wisdom” shines forth at the level of buddhahood in order to tame beings, the transcendent state of wisdom is (said to be) manifested by “the knowing of someone who has complete knowing.” Given that they are related as cause and effect in this way, when one pays homage to the transcendent state of wisdom, one pays homage to the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. Hari: bodhisattvāvasthāyāṃ mātur abhyāsena buddhatvaprāptes tataḥ sarvajñatā nirjātā, buddhatvāvasthāyāṃ vineyebhyaḥ prajñāpāramitāprakāśanāt sarvajñajñāna prabhāvitā ca prajñāpāramit ety evaṃ hetuphalasambandhāt prajñāpāramitānamaskāre sarvajñajñānasya namaskāraḥ. Thus, a bodhisattva great being should go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. As such, a bodhisattva great being should dwell in the transcendent state of wisdom, progress in the transcendent state of wisdom, and engage in meditation in the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Hari (463), “one should go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom” by means of one’s knowing consisting in its study, contemplation, and cultivation. These steps should be, respectively, connected with the three subsequent verbs (i.e., “dwell” in it, “progress” in it, and “engage” in meditation in it). Hari: śrutacintābhāvanāmayajñānair yathākramaṃ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ caritavyam ityādipadatrayaṃ yojyaṃ.

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the Blessed One, “When going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, how does a bodhisattva great being dwell in the transcendent state of wisdom? When going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, how does a bodhisattva great being engage in meditation in the transcendent state of wisdom?”

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Good, Kauśika, good. It is indeed good that you think to ask the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata this and to fully pursue such questions regarding the meaning here. The miraculous power of the Buddha has given rise even to this illumination of yours.

“Here, Kauśika, when going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, a bodhisattva great being neither dwells on physical forms nor the notion of physical forms. Kauśika, since a bodhisattva great being neither dwells on physical forms nor the notion of physical forms, he or she does not engage in meditation on physical forms.According to Ratnākara (F.118.a), when it says he or she “does not dwell on physical forms” and so on, it means he or she does not fixate on them. Since a bodhisattva “engages in meditation” aimed at not dwelling on those physical forms and so on, he or she arrives at the engagement in meditation, that is to say, he or she engages in (the true) meditation on physical forms and so on. RK: |gzugs mi gnas so zhes bya ba la sogs pa ni bshad pa ste mngon par zhen pa zhes bya ba’i don to| |gang gi phyir mi gnas pa de’i phyir rnal ’byor du byed pa ni sbyor ba thob pa’o| |de ni gzugs la sogs pa la mi gnas pa la sbyor ba’o. The same is true for feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors. Kauśika, since a bodhisattva great being neither dwells on consciousness nor the notion of consciousness, he or she does not engage in meditation on consciousness.

“Here, Kauśika, a bodhisattva great being does not meditate upon the notion of physical forms. Since a bodhisattva great being does not meditate upon the notion of physical forms, he or she does not dwell on physical forms. The same is true of feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors. A bodhisattva great being does not meditate upon the notion of consciousness. Since a bodhisattva great being does not meditate upon the notion of consciousness, he or she does not dwell on the notion of consciousness. In this way, a bodhisattva great being dwells and engages in meditation in the transcendent state of wisdom.”

Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, this transcendent state of wisdom is profound. Blessed One, this transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to fathom. Blessed One, this transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to grasp intellectually. Blessed One, this transcendent state of wisdom is beyond measure.”

“It is true, Śāriputra, it is true,” said the Blessed One. “Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being does not dwell on the notion that physical forms are profound. Śāriputra, since a bodhisattva great being does not dwell on the notion that physical forms are profound, he or she engages in meditation on physical forms. The same is true of notions that feelings, conceptions, or conditioning factors are profound. Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being does not dwell on the notion that consciousness is profound. Śāriputra, since a bodhisattva great being does not dwell on the notion that consciousness is profound, he or she engages in meditation on consciousness.

“Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being does not engage in meditation on the notion that physical forms are profound. Śāriputra, since a bodhisattva great being does not engage in meditation on the notion that physical forms are profound, he or she does not dwell on the notion that physical forms are profound. The same is true of notions that feelings, conceptions, or conditioning factors are profound. Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being does not engage in meditation on the notion that consciousness is profound. Śāriputra, since a bodhisattva great being does not engage in meditation on the notion that consciousness is profound, he or she does not dwell on the notion that consciousness is profound.”

At this, the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the profound transcendent state of wisdom should be discussed before a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing who is foreordained for awakening. Why so? Because, Blessed One, he or she will not become worried, doubtful, or confused about it, and contest it.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, asked the venerable Śāriputra, “Noble Śāriputra, if this profound transcendent state of wisdom were discussed instead before a bodhisattva great being not yet foreordained for awakening, what fault might there be?”According to Hari (467), “What fault might there be?” asks the question “Which of the many faults, such as worry, doubt, and so on, could there be?” Hari: ko doṣa iti katamaḥ kāṅkṣādidoṣaḥ syāt.

At this, the venerable Śāriputra said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, just for any unforeordained bodhisattva great being who receives this transcendent state of wisdom to be in its presence, to pay it obeisance, to attend to it respectfully, and to listen to it, he or she can be understood, Kauśika, to have already come a long way, to have traveled the path a long time, and to have completely matured his or her roots of virtue. So, once that bodhisattva great being hears it, he or she will not be frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror.According to Hari (468), since someone not frightened and so on (by hearing the transcendent state of wisdom) must possess the gotra (innate disposition, potential, or nature) of a bodhisattva, the Buddha’s reply suggests that “there will be no fault.” Hari: gotrasāmarthyād anutrāsādir ato na kaścid ity āha: dūrata ityādi. Furthermore, he or she will receive the foreordination for unsurpassed, perfect awakening not long afterward, so his or her foreordination can be regarded as close at hand. That is to say, he or she will be foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening, at the very least, during the time of the next one, two, or three worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. Moreover, that bodhisattva great being will please those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, and not displease them. So, his or her being in the presence of the tathāgatas will bear fruit in terms of foreordination; he or she will receive the foreordination for unsurpassed, perfect awakening through his or her being in the presence of the tathāgatas. Also, to be in the presence of the tathāgatas, to pay them obeisance, to attend them respectfully, and to serve them until becoming foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening will also continue to bear fruit until he or she has realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”

At this, the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, any bodhisattva great being who receives this transcendent state of wisdom to be in its presence, to pay it obeisance, to attend to it respectfully, and to listen to it can be understood, Blessed One, to have already come a long way, to have traveled the path a long time, and to have completely matured his or her roots of virtue. But how much more could be said, Blessed One, about a bodhisattva great being who not only listens to it, but also takes it in, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it.”

Then the Blessed One said to Śāriputra, “That is true, Śāriptura. That is true. Śāriputra, any bodhisattva great being who receives this transcendent state of wisdom to be in its presence, to pay it obeisance, to attend to it respectfully, and to listen to it can be understood, Śāriptura, to have already come a long way, to have traveled the path a long time, and to have completely matured his or her roots of virtue. But how much more could be said, Śāriptura, about a bodhisattva great being who, after listening to it, takes it in, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it.”
Then Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, I find myself illuminated by this point. Sugata, I find myself illuminated to illustrate this point with the following examples or analogies.

“To illustrate the point, Blessed One, if, for example, it should be known that any noble son or daughter on the bodhisattva path who sits down at the epicenter of awakening‍—even in a dream‍—is close to realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening, then the very same should be known, Blessed One, about a noble son or daughter who obtains this profound transcendent state of wisdom to be in its presence, to pay obeisance to it, to attend to it respectfully, and to listen to it. How much more could be said about a bodhisattva great being who, after listening to it, takes it in, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it. Blessed One, such a person on the bodhisattva path should be understood to have come a long way and to have traveled the path a long time. Such a person on the bodhisattva path is close to foreordination. The blessed buddhas will, in the future, foreordain him or her as a bodhisattva great being who will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. This being so, any bodhisattva great being who takes rebirth, at the very least, to be in the presence of this profound transcendent state of wisdom should be understood to have traveled the path a long time and to have completely matured roots of virtue. How much more could be said, Blessed One, about a bodhisattva great being who, after listening to it, takes it in, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it. Why is this so? Blessed One, those other beings are disposed by their accumulation of negative karmic deeds conducive, for the most part, toward the destruction of the Dharma. As such, they will, for the most part, have mindsets that are agitated and resistant toward this profound transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.120.b), the word “resistant” means averse toward it, and “agitated” means recoiling from it. RK: |mi mthun pa zhes bya ba ni rgyab kyis phyogs pa’o| |g.yos par ’gyur ro zhes bya ba ni ldog par ’gyur ba’o. Not having accumulated the roots of virtue for the most part, they neither approach nor trust in the pinnacle of reality. However, Blessed One, if their mindsets, for the most part, are approaching and trusting in the pinnacle of reality, they should be recognized as noble sons or daughters who have accumulated the roots of virtue and accumulated them well.According to Ratnākara (F.120.b), “approach” means apply themselves in a way that leads to it; “trust in” means without concepts. In this way, (this passage explains that) the practice of an irreversible (bodhisattva) involves the mindset approaching and trusting in the pinnacle of reality. RK: |rab tu ’jug pa’i zhes y aba ni ’jug pa’i tshul gyis sbyor bar byed pa’o| |rtog pa med pa ni rab y aba pa’o| |de ltar sems rab tu ’jug pa dang| rab y aba pa ste ’di ni phyir mi ldog pa’i sbyor ba’o.

“To illustrate the point, Blessed One, suppose, as an analogy, a man were to emerge from within a forest one, two, three, four, five, or ten thousand miles wide, and upon doing so, he were to see cowherds, shepherds, property lines, bunches of gardens, groves, or other telltale signs indicative of a village, a town, or a city. After seeing these telltale signs, he might take them to suggest that a village, a town, or a city is close at hand. And he might gain some assurance of his safety and feel he no longer needs to watch out for thieves. In the same way, Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being approaches this profound transcendent state of wisdom. Blessed One, he or she would know, ‘I am getting very close to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. It will not be long before I receive the foreordination for unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’ He or she also would never be frightened, terrified, or afraid of falling to the level of a disciple or solitary realizer. Why not? Because he or she would see the telltale signs, namely, that he or she is receiving this profound transcendent state of wisdom to be in its presence, to pay obeisance to it, to attend to it respectfully, and to listen to it.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Śāriputra, “It is so, Śāriputra. It is as you say. Śāriputra, please venture to illuminate the point even further, in the manner in which you are presently speaking and will continue to speak, due to the miraculous power of the Buddha.”

At this, the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “To illustrate the point, Blessed One, suppose, as an analogy, that a certain man wants to see the ocean, and travels to see it. Wherever he is on the way to the ocean, when he sees either a tree or a mountain‍—or any sign of a tree or a mountain‍—he should know that the ocean must still be far away. But when he sees neither a tree nor a mountain‍—nor any sign of a tree or a mountain‍—he should know that the ocean is very close at hand. Why? The ocean lies down a gradual downhill slope, within which there are neither trees nor mountains, nor any sign of trees or mountains. So, even without seeing the ocean directly with his own eyes, he can still conclude that he is close to the ocean, or that it is not much farther from there. Likewise, Blessed One, when listening to this profound transcendent state of wisdom, a bodhisattva great being would sense that he or she is still close to unsurpassed, perfect awakening even without having been foreordained by the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas face to face. Why would he or she sense it? Because he or she is receiving the profound transcendent state of wisdom to be in its presence, to pay obeisance to it, to attend to it respectfully, and to listen to it.

“To illustrate the point, Blessed One, it is certainly the case that when spring arrives, various buds appear on the different trees whose leaves and flower blossoms have withered. When the new buds are appearing, the people on the Jambu continent see the telltale signs in the forests and become glad that it will not be long before forest flowers and fruits appear. Why are they glad? Because these telltale signs are appearing in the trees. In the same way, Blessed One, when any bodhisattva great being is receiving this profound transcendent state of wisdom to be in its presence, to pay obeisance to it, to attend to it respectfully, and to listen to it, he or she is approaching the profound transcendent state of wisdom. Since a bodhisattva great being is introduced to this profound transcendent state of wisdom only because of his or her previous roots of virtue, he or she should be understood to have roots of virtue that have completely matured. As witnesses to the previous buddha, the gods would become delighted, and, full of joy and satisfaction, would think, ‘These very same signs also led previous bodhisattva great beings to become foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. So it will definitely not be long before this particular bodhisattva great being receives foreordination for unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’

“To illustrate the point, Blessed One, suppose a certain pregnant woman feels a heaviness in her womb, her body is agitated, and she feels excessively fatigued.According to Ratnākara (F.120.b), “agitated” means anxious. RK: bskyod na zhes y aba ni skyo ba’o… Suppose she does not feel like moving around very much. She barely eats. She gets very little rest or sleep. She speaks few words and is low in energy. She regularly feels pain and often finds herself crying aloud. She also does not feel like being physically intimate, as she also considers herself to be experiencing such physical discomforts on account of not previously paying proper heed when she indulged sexually, had intercourse, became engrossed in it, and did it repeatedly.According to Hari (475), she feels a “heaviness” in the womb because of the time to give birth being close. Her body “is agitated” because of its distention; “on account of not previously paying proper heed” means on account of engaging in sexual intercourse; the three descriptions “indulged sexually,” “had intercourse,” and “became engrossed” respectively refer to the preliminary, main, and subsequent stages (of her sexual activity); “did it repeatedly” means because of revisiting it constantly again and again. She is experiencing “such” unbearable “physical discomfort.” The word “considers” is supplied here (as it is implied by the Sanskrit grammar). Hari: āsannaprasavakālatvād gurugarbhā. aṅgavijṛmbhaṇād veṣṭate. paurvakeṇāyoniśomanasikāreṇa grāmyadharmapratisaṃyuktena, prayoga­maulapṛṣṭhāvasthāsu yathākramam āsevitena niṣevitena bhāvitena, sarvatra punaḥpunar āmukhīkaraṇād bahulīkṛtene māṃ vartamānām evaṃrūpām asahyāṃ kāyena vedanāṃ duḥkham anubhavāmī ti cintayatīti śeṣaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.120.b), why she does not feel like being intimate is explained in the next sentence. The phrase “on account of not previously paying proper heed” means due to her causal thought (which led to her sexual activity) being tinged by desire. She “indulged sexually” because she nurtured that desire. She “had intercourse” means she acted upon her desire. She “became engrossed” because she consummated it. She “did it repeatedly” because she did all that the same way over and over. RK: |ji ltar ’grogs pa la dad che bar mi ’gyur zhe na| sngon gyi tshul bzhin zhes bya ba la sogs pa gsungs so| |tshul bzhin ma yin pa’i yid la byed pa zhes bya ba ni ’dod chags dang bcas pa’i rgyu’i rnam par rtog pa’o| kun du bstan pa zhes bya ba ni de bskyed pa’i phyir ro| |nges par bstan pa zhes bya ba ni ’dod chags la spyod pa’o| |bsgoms pa zhes bya ba ni yang dag par phrad pa’i phyir ro| |mang du byas pas zhes bya ba ni yang dang yang du de ltar byed pa’i phyir ro. When such telltale signs occur, then just as one notices them, Blessed One, one would know for sure that it will not be long before she gives birth. In the same way, Blessed One, when a bodhisattva great being with completely matured roots of virtue approaches this profound transcendent state of wisdom to be in its presence, to pay obeisance to it, to attend to it respectfully, and to listen to it, and, after doing so, his or her mindset delights in it and develops the aspiration to attain the transcendent state of wisdom, then it should known for sure, Blessed One, that it will not be long before this bodhisattva great being receives the foreordination.”The phrase translated “with completely matured roots of virtue” (dge ba’i rtsa ba yongs su smin pa) appears in the Tibetan (F.121.b) but is lacking in the Sanskrit text.
At this, the Blessed One said to Śāriputra, “Excellent, Śāriputra, excellent. Śāriputra, this illumination of yours is due to the miraculous power of the Buddha.”

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, it is wonderful how well the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata supports these bodhisattva great beings, how well he protects them, and how well he provides for them.”According to Hari (475), after the words “how well…” (Subhūti describes) the support of a master/protector’s assistance and so on. It is “wonderful” because the Blessed One “supports the bodhisattvas” well and “protects them” well through foreordination and so on after they enter the stages and perfection practices, and he “provides for them” well by assigning them virtuous friends. Also, the wonder is that, out of compassion toward all, he assists beings differently in each case by (providing) positive conditions appropriate for those with distinct dispositions. Hari: yāvad vacanena sanāthā ityādiparigrahaḥ. yasmād vyākaraṇādibhir bhūmiṣu pāramitāsu ca niveśanāt suparigṛhītāḥ suparīttāś ca, kalyāṇamitra­pratyarpaṇāc ca suparīnditā bhagavatā bodhisattvās tasmād āścaryaṃ. sarvaviṣayatve ’pi kṛpāyā viśiṣṭāśaya­sampatti­bhavyatābalena pratiniyatajanānugraho vismayaḥ.

The Blessed One said, “It is because, Subhūti, the bodhisattva great beings have practiced being sympathetic to the world, to bring many beings welfare and happiness.According to Hari (477), in this sentence he explains the positive conditions for those with distinct dispositions. It says they have practiced (a) for beings’ “welfare” (hita) because of bringing benefit in the future, (b) for their “happiness” (sukha) because of bringing benefit in the present, and (c) “being sympathetic to the world” because of making efforts to increase virtuous tendencies and decrease nonvirtuous tendencies created in the past. This all is connected to Subhūti’s previous statement. Hari: viśiṣṭāśayasampattim evāha tathā hī tyādinā. anāgata­sambandhenārtha­kāritvād bahujanahitāya pratipannāḥ. vartamāna­kālatayedānīm arthakaraṇād bahujanasukhāya, atītakālasambandhena kṛtānāṃ kuśalākuśala­vāsanānāṃ yathākramaṃ vivṛddhaye parihāṇaye vā yatnakaraṇāl lokānukampāyai pratipannā iti pūrveṇa sambandhaḥ. As a result of their sympathy for the welfare and happiness of both gods and humans, they have practiced sympathetically with a wish to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening in order to benefit the great multitude of beings, and after doing so, to teach them the unsurpassed Dharma.”
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, when a bodhisattva great being goes about life in this transcendent state of wisdom, how does he or she accomplish the cultivation of the transcendent state of wisdom?”

The Blessed One said, “If, Subhūti, while going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, a bodhisattva great being does not identify any increase in physical forms, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. The same is true of feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors. And, if he or she does not identify any increase of consciousness, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.

“If a bodhisattva great being does not identify any decrease in physical forms, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. The same is true of feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors. And, if he or she does not identify any decrease of consciousness, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.

“If a bodhisattva great being does not identify any thing, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. If he or she also does not identify even any nonthing, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.121.a), here “any thing” or “any nonthing” refers to any things that are virtuous, nonvirtuous, indeterminate, and so on. “He or she does not identify” even them. RK: chos dang chos ma yin pa ni mtshon pa ste| dge ba dang mi dge ba dang| lung du ma bstan pa la sogs pa’i chos rnams kyang yang dag par rjes su mi mthong. In this way, he or she accomplishes the cultivation of the transcendent state of wisdom.”
Subhūti said, “Blessed One, what you are teaching is inconceivable.”

The Blessed One said, “This is because, Subhūti, physical forms are inconceivable. The same is true of feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors. Consciousness is inconceivable. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being does not even conceive of physical forms as inconceivable; he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. He or she does the same with respect to feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors. He or she does not even conceive of consciousness as inconceivable; he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.”
At this, the venerable Śāriputra asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, who will have firm conviction in such a profound transcendent state of wisdom?”
The Blessed One said, “Śāriputra, as someone who has gone about life in this transcendent state of wisdom, a bodhisattva great being will have firm conviction in this transcendent state of wisdom.”

The venerable Śāriputra asked, “Blessed One, how does a bodhisattva great being become ‘someone who has gone about that life’? How does he or she earn the designation ‘someone who has gone about that life’?”According to Hari (478), the first question is about gaining the nature of “someone who has gone about that life.” The second question is about gaining the designation of “someone who has gone about that life.” The Blessed One replies in the following sentence. Hari: kathaṃ bhagavaṃś caritāvī bodhisattvo mahāsattvo bhavatīti svarūpalābhapraśnaṃ kathaṃ caritāvītināmadheyaṃ labhata ity anvartha­saṃjñālābha­praśnaṃ ca pariharann āha: iha śāriputre tyādi. Note the “that” in the translation “someone who has gone about that life” (caritāvī) has been added, because the connotation of the Sanskrit term is akin to saying “someone who has lived the life” almost in the sense of “someone who has lived a holy life.”

The Blessed One said, “Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being at this level does not conceptualize the strengths. He or she does not conceptualize even the grounds for self-confidence.According to Ratnākara (F.121.a), the word “even” is meant to imply (if this thing is not even imagined, then) “how much more could be said about other things.” RK: kyang gi sgra ni chos gzhan rnams lta ci smos zhes pa’o. He or she does not conceptualize even the qualities of a buddha. He or she does not conceptualize even the state of complete knowing. Why not? Because, Śāriputra, the strengths are inconceivable. Even the grounds for self-confidence are also inconceivable. Even the qualities of a buddha are also inconceivable. Even the state of complete knowing is inconceivable. Thus, Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being who has gone about that life does not go about life in any things; he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. In this way, he or she is called ‘someone who has gone about that life.’ He or she earns the designation ‘someone who has gone about that life.’ ”

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is a treasure trove.According to Ratnākara (F.121.a), the “treasure” refers to the fruition, because the transcendent state of wisdom is the cause of everything from attaining perfect awakening up to setting in motion the wheel of Dharma teachings. Hence, this sentence refers to the practice that bestows the treasure of such fruits. RK: |bcom ldan ’das shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa ni rin po che’i phung po’o zhes bya ba ni ’bras bu rin po che ni yang dag par rdzogs pa’i byang chub nas| chos kyi ’khor lo bskor ba’i bar gyi rgyu yin pa’i phyir| ’di ni ’bras bu rin po che sbyin par byed pa’i sbyor ba’o. According to Hari (479), the transcendent state of wisdom is “profound” because it is an antidote in which there is no identity of things or persons. This being so, the transcendent state of wisdom is a “treasure trove” because it bestows the treasure of the fruitions‍—beginning with entering the stream up to unsurpassed, perfect awakening‍—upon those who do the preliminary practices that bestow the treasure of such fruitions by proceeding to the first fruit through the path of seeing and so on. Hari: dharma­pudgala­nairātmya­pratipakṣatvena gambhīrā satī prathama­phala­darśanādi­pratipattyā phala­ratna­dānaprayogavatāṃ srotāapattyādyanuttara­samyak­sambodhi­phala­ratnasya dātrītvād ratnarāśiḥ. Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is a pure trove because of its space-like purity.According to Ratnākara (F.121.a), this sentence refers to the practice that is pure, because of seeing the space-like purity of those very fruits (i.e., the “treasure” in the previous sentence). RK: teṣām eva phalānām ākāśa vadviśuddhidarśanād iti śuddhakaḥ prayogaḥ|| Hari: ākāśa syeva śuddhatām upādāya. According to Hari (479), the transcendent state of wisdom is a “pure trove, because of its space-like purity,” insofar as it causes those who do the preliminary practices to eliminate the cognitive and emotionally defiling obscurations, by cultivating the purity of physical form and so on. Hari: rūpādi­viśuddhi­bhāvanayā tatprayogavatāṃ kleśa­jñeyāvaraṇa­prahāṇa­hetutvena śuddharāśiḥ. Yet, Blessed One, surprisingly, numerous obstacles could arise for a noble son or daughter when taking in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeping it in mind, chanting it, mastering it, passing it on, teaching it, expounding it, explaining it, reciting it, and writing it out.”According to Hari (479), it is “surprising” that obstacles can arise even though it is extremely powerful. Hari: mahānubhāvatve ’py antarāyasambhava ity āścaryaṃ.

At this, the Blessed One said, “It is true, Subhūti. It is true that many obstacles could arise for a noble son or daughter when taking in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeping it in mind, chanting it, mastering it, passing it on, teaching it, expounding it, explaining it, reciting it, and writing it out. Why? Because, Subhūti, the pernicious Māra will put his energy into creating obstacles for that noble son or daughter when he or she is taking in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeping it in mind, chanting it, mastering it, passing it on, teaching it, expounding it, explaining it, reciting it, and writing it out. In response to that, if a noble son or daughter can write out this transcendent state of wisdom quickly, in, say, one, two, or three months, then he or she should do nothing but write it out. If a noble son or daughter must write it out over the course of a year or longer than that, then, even in that case, Subhūti, he or she should still do nothing but write it out. Why so? Because, Subhūti, it is the case that such truly precious things invite numerous obstacles.”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, when this transcendent state of wisdom is being taken in, kept in mind, chanted, mastered, passed on, taught, expounded, explained, recited, and written out, the pernicious Māra will put his energy and effort into creating obstacles in many ways.”

“Nevertheless, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One, “the pernicious Māra will make efforts, but he has no power to create obstacles for any bodhisattva great being who commits him- or herself at once to the task without breaks from beginning to end.”The next sentence begins to describe the positive qualities gained through practice, i.e., the third subtopic within the Ornament’s fourth topic.

Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, when the pernicious Māra exerts himself in creating obstacles for the noble son or daughter taking in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeping it in mind, chanting it, mastering it, passing it on, teaching it, expounding it, explaining it, reciting it, and writing it out, then how can these noble sons and daughters, at this very moment, take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out? Also, whose miraculous power will allow these noble sons and daughters to take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out?”

At this, the Blessed One said to Śāriputra, “Śāriputra, it is due to the miraculous power of the blessed ones, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, that these noble sons and daughters are able to take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, as they train to attain the actual nature, progress to attain the actual nature, and engage in meditation to attain the actual nature. How so? Because, Śāriputra, this is the true nature of things. Those blessed buddhas‍—who are residing, continuing to abide, and thriving in immeasurable and innumerable world systems‍—will bear in mind and support anyone taking in this transcendent state of wisdom, keeping it in mind, chanting it, mastering it, passing it on, teaching it, expounding it, explaining it, reciting it, and writing it out.The phrase “speaking about it” (bhāṣyamāṇām) appears first in this line of verbs in the Sanskrit edition. But since it seems to come out of nowhere and since there is no equivalent in Tibetan, the phrase is not included in the main text and only noted here.

“As such, those blessed buddhas will bear in mind and support these noble sons or daughters here who take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, as they train to attain the actual nature, progress to attain the actual nature, and engage in meditation to attain the actual nature. Indeed, Śāriputra, those noble sons or daughters borne in mind and supported by the buddhas will encounter no obstacles.”

At this, Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, all these bodhisattva great beings who, in the future, listen to this transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, as they train to attain the actual nature, progress to attain the actual nature, and engage in meditation to attain the actual nature, will listen to this transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, as they train to attain the actual nature, progress to attain the actual nature, engage in meditation to attain the actual nature, and actually accomplish it in this way by virtue of a buddha’s miraculous power, blessings, and support.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Śāriputra, “That is true, Śāriputra, that is true. By virtue of a buddha’s miraculous power, blessings, and support, all those bodhisattva great beings will listen to this transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, as they train to attain the actual nature, progress to attain the actual nature, and engage in meditation to attain the actual nature. Śāriputra, they will be perceived by the Tathāgata. Śāriputra, they will be influenced miraculously by the Tathāgata. Śāriputra, they will be seen by the Tathāgata. Śāriputra, they will be watched over by the Tathāgata with the eye of a buddha.

“After those bodhisattva great beings have listened to this transcendent state of wisdom, taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, recited it, and written it out, as they trained to attain the actual nature, progressed to attain the actual nature, and engaged in meditation to attain the actual nature, they will approach unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and abide by the actual nature for the sake of unsurpassed, perfect awakening.In this paragraph, the English translation has omitted a refrain describing the activities by those bodhisattvas in the future, because all these activities are implied in the past tense description and, if repeated twice, the comprehensibility of the passage would be undermined. Note: Although “taken in” (udgrahya) is missing in the Skt. and Tib., it is included it here with the assumption that it was probably omitted due to a simple scribal eye skip.

“Śāriputra, even those bodhisattva great beings who take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, but who, after writing it out, do not train to attain the actual nature, do not train progress to attain the actual nature, do not engage in meditation to attain the actual nature, and do not abide by the actual nature for the sake of unsurpassed, perfect awakening, will be perceived by the Tathāgata. Śāriputra, they will be influenced miraculously by the Tathāgata. Śāriputra, they will be seen by the Tathāgata. Śāriputra, they will be watched over by the Tathāgata with the eye of a buddha. Also, Śāriputra, their toil and mental exertion will be extremely effective, extremely beneficial, extremely fruitful, and extremely rewarding.According to Hari (486), the “toil” is writing out the transcendent state of wisdom. The “mental exertion” is chanting and remembering it. Hari: sa pariśrama iti tallikhanaṃ. parispanda iti dhāraṇavācane. According to Ratnākara (F.122.a), it will be “extremely effective” in terms of the stages of a bodhisattva in the future; “extremely beneficial” in terms of the positive conditions for a bodhisattva; “extremely fruitful” after unsurpassed awakening; and “extremely rewarding” in terms of the positive conditions in their last rebirth according to family, genetic dispositions, and so on. Their “toil” means their exhaustion. Their “mental exertion” means applying themselves. RK: mahārthika āyatyāṃ bodhisattvabhūmibhiḥ| mahānuśaṃso bodhi­sattva­sampattibhiḥ| mahāphalaḥ paścād anuttarāyā bodhyāḥ| mahāvipākaḥ caramabhavabhāvinībhiḥ kula­gotra­lakṣaṇādi­sampattibhiḥ| pariśramaḥ klamathaḥ| parispando vyāpāraḥ| iti mahārthatādiguṇaḥ. How could this be so? It is because the transcendent state of wisdom is intimately connected with the ultimate reality of everything, so sentient beings who attend to it gain insight into reality as-it-is.According to Hari (486), the meaning here is that the transcendent state of wisdom is primarily beneficial because sentient beings attend to it‍—through listening to it and so on‍—in order to gain insight into the true nature of everything through the gateway of everything lacking inherent nature. Hari: yasmāt sarva­dharma­naiḥsvābhāvya­mukhena dharmāṇāṃ tattvaprativedhāya śravaṇādikrameṇa pratyupasthitā tasmāt tasyā evānuśāṃso mukhyata ity arthaḥ.

“Furthermore, Śāriputra, after the Tathāgata passes into nirvāṇa‍—when the fresh essence of his Dharma and Vinaya has been obtained in the period before the time when the genuine Dharma disappears‍—these scriptures associated with the six transcendent states will spread in the south, then spread from the south farther to the east, and then from the east farther to the north.According to Ratnākara (122.a–b), the next passage is taught because‍—insofar as the Blessed One taught the Dharma in the sixteen major cities of the central region‍—someone might wonder how it came to “spread.” Understand here that “fresh essence (navamaṇḍa < abhinavasāra) has been obtained” by those disciples to be tamed. The phrase “fresh essence has been obtained” is an adjectival compound that modifies “Dharma and Vinaya.” This scripture can be described as “Dharma and Vinaya” because it is both “Dharma”‍—insofar as it is the Dharma qua teaching (rather than Dharma qua realization)‍—and “Vinaya” (lit. “guiding” or “taming”), insofar as it tames the emotional defilements. Once the Blessed One has passed into nirvāṇa, the genuine Dharma is to remain in the world for five thousand years. “The time when the genuine Dharma disappears” is after that. The phrase “in the period before” means as that time is drawing nearer. RK: nanu madhyadeśe ṣoḍaśeṣu mahānagareṣu bhagavatā dharmo deśitaḥ| tasya kathaṃ tato ’nyatra pracāra ityata āha| navamaṇḍa ityādi| navamaṇḍo ’bhinavaḥ sāraḥ| sa prāpto vineyair asyeti tathokte dharmavinaye | dharmaś cāsau deśanādharmatvād vinayaś ca kleśavinayād iti dharmavinayaḥ | bhagavati parinirvṛte saddharmasya loke ’vasthānaṃ pañcavarṣasahasrāṇi tato ’ntardhānakālaḥ| tatsamaye sannidhāne. According to Hari (487), the meaning to be expressed is “like fresh essence” (navamaṇḍa iva < abhinavasāra iva). This is “obtained” by these and those Dharma teachers. Although this phrase comes after the main verb, it should be connected to the main clause, “the scriptures will spread” (i.e., Hari is explaining how to make sense of the awkward Sanskrit syntax). Hari: navamaṇḍaprāpta iti navamaṇḍa ivābhinavasāra ivābhidheye ’rthe taistair dharmabhāṇakaiḥ prāpte sati pracariṣyanti sūtrāntā iti pūrveṇa sambandhaḥ. Note: This translation follows Ratnākara and the Tib. shA ri’i bu yang pha rol tu phyin pa drug dang ldan pa’i mdo sde ’di dag ni de bzhin gshegs pa ’das nas kyang dam pa’i chos ’jig pa’i dus kyi tshe chos ’dul ba snying po sar pa thob pa na lho phyogs su ’byung bar ’gyur ro | | lho phyogs nas kyang bar ta nir ’byung bar ’gyur ro | | bar ta ni nas yang byang phyogs su ’byung ste. Śāriputra, the Tathāgata will bear in mind those noble sons and daughters. At that time, they will take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, or at least write it out and make it into a book. Śāriputra, they will be perceived by the Tathāgata. Śāriputra, they are influenced miraculously by the Tathāgata. Śāriputra, they are seen by the Tathāgata. Śāriputra, they are watched over by the Tathāgata with the eye of a buddha.
Śāriputra asked, “At that time, will this transcendent state of wisdom also become widespread in the northern part of the northern region?”

The Blessed One said, “Śāriputra, those bodhisattva great beings in the northern part of the northern region who hear this profound transcendent state of wisdom and engage in meditation in it will make it widespread. Śāriputra, if they listen to this transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, as they train to attain the actual nature, progress to attain the actual nature, and engage in meditation to attain the actual nature, then they should be understood to have traveled the path a long time.”

Śāriputra asked, “Blessed One, how many bodhisattva great beings will there be in the northern part of the northern region? Will there be many or few bodhisattva great beings who listen to this transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, as they train to attain the actual nature, progress to attain the actual nature, and engage in meditation to attain the actual nature?”

The Blessed One said, “Śāriputra, there will be many, a great many bodhisattva great beings in the north, that is, in the northern part of the northern region.According to Ratnākara (122.b), the precise location of its spread is delimited from other regions by the phrase “in the northern region” and from other parts of that by the phrase “in the northern part.” “Widespread” means it spread widely. It says there will be (first) “many,” referring to a small faction of followers, (and second) “a great many,” meaning (that the small faction) is to increase to a majority. “Nevertheless” implies even though. (Ratnākara interjects here that the other following words are easy to understand so he will not further comment upon them, but the following sentence connects the dots in the entire passage to this point.) Up to here, the decline in the teaching is explained in terms of four factors: (1) the passing away of the Blessed One, (2) the extraction of the essence, (3) descending toward the time of the Dharma’s disappearance, and (4) the timeliness for passing on the Dharma. Nevertheless, contingent upon the above four factors, the teachings will be passed on. Hence, these qualities are contingencies for the teachings (to spread). RK: uttarasyāṃ diśī ti digantarāṇāṃ vyavacchedaḥ| uttare digbhāga iti| tasyā api bhāgāntarāṇāṃ, vistāreṇa caratīti vaistārikī| uttarāpatha iti vacanaṃ dakṣiṇāpathā der vyavacchedāya| bahava ity alpakapakṣyasya subahava iti bahutvaprakarṣāya| kiñcāpī ti yady api| śeṣaṃ subodham| atra caturbhiḥ kāraṇaiḥ śāsanāpacaya uktaḥ| bhagavato ’tyayena| navamaṇḍaprāptatvena antardhānakālasannipātena pravartakālyatvena ca| tathāpi śāsanaṃ tān avalaṃbya pravartiṣyata iti śāsanāvalambatvaguṇaḥ. Nevertheless, Śāriputra, few among the many bodhisattva great beings will also listen to this transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, as they train to attain the actual nature, progress to attain the actual nature, and engage in meditation to attain the actual nature. And, there will be few who, as this transcendent state of wisdom is being spoken of, will not become deflated, depressed, dejected, or sunk in despondency, and whose mental attitude will not become discouraged, shattered, frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror. These few bodhisattva great beings will be known as having traveled the path for a long time. They will have followed the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, asked them questions, made further inquiries of them, and fully pursued their questions with them. Also, those noble sons and daughters on the bodhisattva path will have venerated the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. They will become perfect in the ethical disciplines, and will bring many people benefit originating specifically from my very own unsurpassed awakening.According to Ratnākara (122.b), “the ethical disciplines” is a plural word meant to imply “and so on.” Thus, the meaning here is “the ethical discipline and so on” (i.e., meditation and wisdom are also implied). RK: śīleṣv iti bahuvacanam ādyarthaṃ śīlādiṣv ity arthaḥ. How will this be so?According to Hari (490), he asks rhetorically “How will this be so?” because these bodhisattvas will have forgotten hearing this teaching as they are attaining the stage of a buddha. Then, he addresses this qualm in the following sentence. Hari: vismṛta­buddha­bhūmi­prāpakadharma­śravaṇāḥ katham anuttarāṃ bodhim ārabhyārthakāriṇa iti tat kasya hetor ity āśaṅkyāha: tathā hi teṣām ityādi. I myself have given these noble sons and daughters this very teaching on the state of complete knowing, so that even in future lives they will do the practices connected with the state of complete knowing‍—that is to say, connected with the transcendent state of wisdom‍—and they themselves will present and be zealous about this very teaching, which specifically concerns unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.122.b), they will do “the practices,” i.e., preliminary practices, “even in future lives,” i.e., even as they go beyond this life and the next. Being “zealous” about that teaching means being passionate about it. RK: jātivyativṛttānām apī ti tāṃ taduttarāṃ ca jātim atikrāntānām api samudācārāḥ prayogāḥ| abhinandanam abhilāṣaḥ. Also, insofar as they will be perfectly resolved and focused on those practices in this transcendent state of wisdom, they will not be able to be broken even by Māra.According to Hari (491), the phrase “resolved about those practices” means well established in them. They will “not be able to be broken,” i.e., they will not be able to be prevented from awakening, because they will have developed the quality of unbreakabilty due to attaining the causes such as the Tathāgata’s protection (as explained earlier by Hari). Hari: samudācāreṣu teṣu vyavasthitāḥ, na śakyā bhedayitum iti tathāgataānāthya­karaṇa­lābhenābhedyatāguṇotpatter bhedayituṃ bodher nivartayitum āśakyāḥ. So, how could they be liable to be broken by any other beings, especially, because of their aspirations or mantras?According to Ratnākara (F.123.a), “due to their aspirations or mantras” means due to (evil) prayers or magic spells. RK: chandato mantrato ve ti| abhiprāyeṇābhicārena vā. Hari explains that the phrase “due to their aspirations or mantras” (as translated from Sanskrit here) can be interpreted in two opposite ways, depending, it seems, on whether the “aspirations or mantras” belong to the bodhisattvas or to their enemies. According to Hari (491), the phrase “due to aspirations” means due to wishes held for or against the Mahāyāna scriptures. Regarding the phrase “due to mantras,” some scholars claim that it means that those bodhisattvas are not able to be broken because of their own repeated practice of reciting mantras and its many benefits, namely, “It is due to mantras that gods protect, that the mind turns toward virtue, and that illnesses, untimely deaths, and misfortunes are destroyed.” Other scholars claim that the phrase “due to mantras” means that the pernicious Māra cannot turn bodhisattvas back from the transcendent state of wisdom by any means, i.e., by force of magical potions and mantras (viz. evil incantations). Hari: chandata iti sūtrānta­mahā­yānābhilāṣataḥ. mantrata iti rakṣanti devatā mantraiḥ kuśale vartate manaḥ vyādhayo ’kālamṛtyuś ca daurbhāgyaṃ ca praṇaśyatīti bahvanuśaṃsān mantracaryābhyāsāc ca bhedayituṃ na śakyanta ity eke. māreṇa pāpīyasā sarva­prakāreṇauṣadhīsāmarthyān mantrasāmarthyāc ca prajñāpāramitāto nivartayitum aśakyā ity apare. Note: in Ratnākara above, the Sanskrit MS reading (N92-59b, line 2) has been emended from vyavacanena to abhicārena, but P. Jaini emends it to vyavacāraneṇa. Why are they not liable? Because of their strong and steady drive toward unsurpassed, perfect awakening, those noble sons and daughters will hear this transcendent state of wisdom and gain extraordinary enthusiasm, trust, and delight in it.According to Hari (491), “enthusiasm” that seeks something beyond this world; “trust” that is founded in the Buddha, Dharma, and Saṅgha; “delight” that, according to sacred texts, indicates the purity of one’s mindset. Hari: tatra prītir yā lokottaradharmaparyeṣṭiḥ. prasādo buddha­dharma­saṃghāśrayaḥ. prāmodyaṃ yā śuddhatā cittasyety ārṣam. Also, for many people, they will plant the roots of virtue, specifically those of unsurpassed, perfect awakening. How so? Because, face to face with me in my presence, those sons and daughters previously uttered the following vow: ‘We will practice the bodhisattva’s way of going about life to help many beings‍—many hundreds, many thousands, many hundreds of thousands, many millions, many billions, many trillions, and many quadrillions of beings‍—set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening, practice toward it, see it accurately, be inspired by it, rejoice in it, prevail in it, and become established in it.According to Ratnākara (F.123.a), we will help them “set out” for it by just going forth (on to the path) and “prevail in it” due achieving a superior state through cultivating it. We will help them “become established in it” means will make them become steadfast in it. RK: prasthāpayiṣyāma iti prasthānamātreṇa| samprabhāvayiṣyāma iti bhāvanāprakarṣasaṃpādanāt| pratiṣṭhāpayiṣyāma iti sthirīkariṣyāmaḥ. We will also help them not to regress from it.’ How so?
