Śāriputra, in my mind, I gazed into the hearts of those noble sons and daughters on the bodhisattva path and rejoiced in the vow that they uttered, namely: ‘We will practice the bodhisattva’s way of going about life to help many beings‍—many hundreds, many thousands, many hundreds of thousands, many millions, many billions, many trillions, and many quadrillions of beings‍—set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening, practice toward it, see it accurately, be inspired by it, rejoice in it, prevail in it, and become established in it. We will also help them not to regress from it.’ In this way, those noble sons and daughters will come to have extraordinarily firm conviction,According to Hari (493), they will have “extraordinarily firm conviction” because, through their belief in the extraordinary, profound meaning, they will take hold of the extraordinary fruits. Hari: udārādhimuktikā iti gambhīrodārārthādhimokṣeṇodāra­phala­parigrahād udārādhimuktikāḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.123.a), regarding them having “extraordinarily firm conviction,” we can abbreviate and state what is said in the two large scriptures on the transcendent state of wisdom this way: “They come to have extraordinarily firm conviction. After they bestow extraordinary gifts in (the form of) sights, sounds, scents, flavors, textures, and mental qualities, they accumulate extraordinary roots of virtue. Then, after they reap the extraordinary fruits, they come to reap fruit after fruit for the sake of sentient beings” (see the Twenty-Five Thousand, 30.74). Thus, this sentence ends the short section on the quality of reaping extraordinary fruit. RK: udārādhimuktikā bhaviṣyantī ti| yathoktaṃ mahatyos tad eva saṃkṣipya brūmaḥ| “te khalu udārādhimuktikā bhaviṣyanti| rūpa­śabda­gandha­rasa­sparśa­dharmeṣu ta udārāṇi dānāni datvā udārāṇi kuśalamūlāny abhisaṃskṛtya udāraṃ vipākaṃ parigṛhya sattvānām arthāya vipākād vipākaṃ parigṛhīṣyanti” iti udāraphalaparigrahaguṇaḥ. because of which they will also set their minds on transferring themselves to other buddhafields. In those buddhafields, wherever the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas teach the Dharma, they will listen extensively to this profound transcendent state of wisdom again in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, face to face with them. Those noble sons and daughters will help many beings‍—many hundreds, many thousands, many hundreds of thousands, many millions, many billions, many trillions, and many quadrillions of beings‍—set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening, practice in it, see it accurately, be inspired by it, rejoice in it, prevail in it, and become established in it.”

At this, the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “It is wonderful, Blessed One, the extent to which there is nothing in the past, present, or future that the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata has not seen, not heard, not sensed, and not discerned. There is not any thing that he has not perceived, or any being’s activity that he has not discerned. For example, he has even perceived the activity of future bodhisattva great beings, who will wish for awakening, perfect the noble disposition, and apply themselves with heroic perseverance. He has perceived those who, at that time, will take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out. He has perceived those who, at that time, will make efforts in these six transcendent states to benefit beings. But he also has perceived that, among these noble sons and daughters seeking, searching, and striving for this profound transcendent state of wisdom, some striving bodhisattvas will attain this profound transcendent state of wisdom, some will not attain this profound transcendent state of wisdom, and some, even without striving, will attain this profound transcendent state of wisdom. What is the reason for this?”

When Śāriputra had spoken, the Blessed One said to him, “It is true, Śāriputra, it is true. There is nothing in the past, present, or future that the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata has not seen, not heard, not sensed, or not discerned. Also, it is true, Śāriputra, that in that time period some bodhisattvas, who are seeking this profound transcendent state of wisdom, searching for it, and striving for it, will attain this profound transcendent state of wisdom. It is true, Śāriputra, that even without seeking this profound transcendent state of wisdom, searching for it, and striving for it, some bodhisattvas will attain this profound transcendent state of wisdom. How could this be the case? It is because any bodhisattva great being who has sought and searched for this transcendent state of wisdom without forsaking the task in the past will come to attain this profound transcendent state of wisdom without seeking, searching, and striving for it, solely based on their former aspiration for the roots of virtue. Afterward, these scriptures and others that properly express this very transcendent state of wisdom will spontaneously come to those bodhisattvas, appear to them, and present themselves to them.According to Ratnākara (F.123.b), “properly express” means accurately express. They will “come” because they are close at hand, “appear” because they are obtained when effort is involved, and “present themselves” when no effort is involved. RK: abhivadantī ti samyagvadanti| upagamiṣyanti sannidhānataḥ| upapatsyante prayatne sati lābhāt| upanaṃsyante ayatnenaiva lābhāt. Why is that? Śāriputra, it is simply the case that any bodhisattva great being who seeks and searches for this transcendent state of wisdom without forsaking the task will attain this transcendent state of wisdom, even after passing on to the next birth or passing on to yet another birth. And other scriptures connected to the transcendent state of wisdom will spontaneously come to him or her, appear to him or her, and present themselves to him or her.”

At this, the venerable Śāriputra asked the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, will these very same collections of scriptures connected with the six transcendent states spontaneously appear to those noble sons and daughters, and present themselves to them, but not to others?”

The Blessed One said, “Śāriputra, those and others too‍—which will be profound collections of scriptures‍—will spontaneously appear to those noble sons and daughters, and will spontaneously present themselves to them. Why is that? Śāriputra, it is simply the case that if those bodhisattva great beings help others set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening, practice toward it, see it accurately, be inspired by it, rejoice in it, prevail in it, become established in it, become beyond regressing, and train in that themselves, then, Śāriputra, when they pass on to the next birth, those very profound collections of scriptures‍—connected with freedom from mental perception, with emptiness, and with the six transcendent states‍—will spontaneously come to them, appear to them, and present themselves to them.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Extolling the Positive Qualities of Recollection,” the tenth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 11: Māra’s ActivityThe beginning of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 31 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 40 in the Eighteen Thousand. From the point marked by note 707, the chapter goes on to include the first parts of Chapter 32 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 41 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: Then the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “O Blessed One, now that the Blessed One has extolled the positive qualities of the noble sons and daughters, please tell us, Blessed One, will they encounter any obstacles?”According to Hari (499), right after the (chapter on the bodhisattva’s) positive qualities, one might still wonder what are the practices that need to be cultivated through eliminating those faults that cause obstacles to practice. So, he describes everything in the following chapter in order to explain the faults that create obstacles. The question “will they encounter any” means how many will they encounter? Hari: guṇānantaraṃ ke punas te prayogāntarāyakarā doṣā yeṣāṃ parivarjanena prayogā bhāvayitavyā ity antarāyakarān doṣān vaktum āha: guṇā ime bhagavann ityādi. kecit punar iti kiyantaḥ punar ity arthaḥ.
At this, the Blessed One said, “Subhūti, they will encounter the many activities of Māra as obstacles.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, what are the many types of Māra’s activities that they will encounter as obstacles?”According to Ratnākara (F.123.b), the question “what are the many” arises due to Subhūti wanting to know the details concerning the “many (activities of Māra)” mentioned generally by Blessed One. His reply basically points to the forty-six (activities of Māra) being explained. RK: bahūnī ti sāmānyenokte viśeṣajijñāsayā praśnaḥ, kiyadrūpāṇī ti| teṣām ityādy uttaram| vakṣyamāṇāni ṣaṭcatvāriṃśad iti bhāvaḥ.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, one should understand the first of Māra’s activities to involve what a long time it will take for bodhisattva great beings, discussing the transcendent state of wisdom, to become illuminated by it.According to Ratnākara (F.123.b-24.a), the words “discussing” are a synecdoche for all the bodhisattva practices. “What a long time it will take … to become illuminated” refers to the long time it will take to consummate the transcendent states (pāramitā), and to the much longer time that awakening will take. This is the fault involving illumination taking a long time. RK: bhāṣamāṇā dipadāni sarvacaryāṇām upalakṣaṇāni| cireṇa pratibhānam utpatsyata iti cireṇa pāramitānāṃ pūraṇam| tataś cireṇaiva bodhiḥ syāt| iti cirapratibhānadoṣaḥ.

“Next, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra that those bodhisattva great beings will become distracted just as they are being illuminated by it.According to Ratnākara (F.124.a), “become distracted” means become shaken up, because, when it is happening, someone who lacks the skill in means will immediately develop pride. And awakening will take longer to develop for those who look down upon other bodhisattvas in lower families. The fault here is that illumination will be fleeting. RK: vikṣepsyata iti uddhataṃ bhaviṣyati| anupāyakuśalasya kṣiprataraṃ tadutpattau mānotpatteḥ| tato ’nyān bodhisattvān avamanyamānasya nīcakuleṣūtpattyā cireṇaiva bodhiḥ syād ity āśupratibhānadoṣaḥ. Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when those bodhisattva great beings who write out the transcendent state of wisdom are gaping at it, laughing at it, and ridiculing it.According to Ratnākara (F.124.a), “gaping at it” means physically opening the mouth with pride; “laughing at it” means laughing aloud at it; and “ridiculing it” means putting it down out of pride. This is the defect referring to physical behavior. RK: jṛmbhamāṇā iti sadarpakāyaprasārāḥ| hasanta iti aṭṭahāsān kurvantaḥ| uccavagghayanta iti darpād uttiṣṭhantaḥ| iti kāyakarmavaiguṇyam.

“Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when their mindsets get distracted as they study it. Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when their minds get caught up in something else as they write out the transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when they fail to be mindful.According to Ratnākara (F.124.a), their “mindsets get distracted” means they are not focused. They “get caught up in something else” means they have something in mind due to other causes, but they “fail to be mindful” by themselves, because, even in their different spiritual activities, they forget the teachings. This is the fault involving mental defects. RK: vikṣiptacittā ity asamāhitā| anyonyavijñānasamaṅgina iti paraiḥ smarayitavyāḥ| svayaṃ tu smṛtiṃ na pratilapsyante| bhinneṣv apīryāpatheṣūddeśa­vismaraṇād veti cittavaiguṇyadoṣaḥ.

“Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when those bodhisattva great beings laugh at one another as they write it out.According to Hari (501), in this sentence he begins to explain the study and so on that is disposed toward inappropriateness. When he says “write it out,” it is meant to include study and so on by implication. Hari: ayogavihitasvādhyāyāditāṃ nirdiśann āha: parasparam upahasanta ity ādi. likhiṣyantī ti upalakṣaṇāt svādhyāyādikaṃ grāhyaṃ. Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when they ridicule one another as they write it out. Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when their eyes look wildly about as they write it out. Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when their writings are incongruous.According to Ratnākara (F.124.a), “look about wildly” means this way and that way. “Incongruous” refers to the incomplete degree to which their book is confirmed in the teachings. This is the fault that their study and so on is disposed toward inappropriateness. RK: vikṣiptacakṣuṣa itas tataḥ| visāmagrī ti yāvatām āgamane pustakam ākṛṣyate tāvatām asamagratety ayogavihitasvādhyāyāditā doṣaḥ.

“Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when they get up from their seats and walk away, complaining that they are gaining no firm grounding in it, or enjoyment from it. Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when they get up from their seats and walk away with a mindset that lacks trust, complaining that they are not foreordained for this transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when they wish not to study the transcendent state of wisdom and to back away from it, complaining that it mentions nowhere in it the name of the city, town, or village in which they were born; it mentions nowhere in it the name or lineage of their father and mother; and it mentions nowhere in it the family in which they were born. Also, each time they back away from it, they will again and again take rebirths for the same number of eons in cyclic existence as the number of times they developed such improper mindsets. There, all over again, they will need to engage in meditation. Why? Because, Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when bodhisattva great beings who do not study this transcendent state of wisdom do not develop themselvesAccording to Ratnākara (F.124.a), they “do not develop themselves” means they do not ripen. RK: na nirjāyante na niṣpadyante. to have the worldly and spiritual qualities.

“Beyond that, Subhūti, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when, Subhūti, those persons on the bodhisattva path dismiss and abandon this transcendent state of wisdom, which brings forth the knowing of someone who has complete knowing, and hence they do not wish to seek this collection of scriptures, which bring forth the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. Subhūti, since they do not wish to train themselves or to go beyond cyclic existence to the worldly and spiritual qualities, they do not train in this transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.124.a), “dismiss” means mentally; “abandon” means verbally and physically; “going beyond cyclic existence” means reaching the end of the trainings. RK: vivarjya cetasā| utsṛjya kāyavāgbhyām| niryāṇ aṃ śikṣāparyantagamanam. By not training in it, they do not go beyond cyclic existence to the worldly and spiritual qualities. In this way, these narrow-minded persons dismiss and abandon the transcendent state of wisdom‍—which is the root of fully comprehending the worldly and spiritual qualities as they are‍—and instead wish to cling to the branches.According to Hari (502), “brings forth” means gives rise to; “dismiss” means dismiss the practice and “abandon” the intention; and “narrow-minded” means those with little intelligence. The “root of fully comprehending it as it is” means the primary cause of full knowledge of the perfect Dharma. “Branches” refers to the Disciple Vehicle and so on. Hari: āhārikām iti utpādikām. vivarjyotsṛjye ti prayogāśayābhyām ity vācyaṃ. parīttabuddhaya iti svalpabuddhayaḥ. yathābhūtaparijñāyā mūlam iti samyagdharmāvabodhasya pradhānaṃ kāraṇaṃ. praśākhām iti śrāvakayānādikaṃ. This would be just like, for instance, a dog thinking it should give up a bunch of food from a master and seek a mouthful of water from the servant. Thus, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when, Subhūti, in the future some persons on the bodhisattva path think that they should give up the transcendent state of wisdom‍—the root of the knowing of someone who has complete knowing‍—and seek buddhahood, the sap, in the paths of disciples and solitary realizers, its branches and foliage.Note here that the English translation follows two Sanskrit readings found in Cambridge Add.866 MS (slide 00185) rather than in the modern Sanskrit editions (such as by Mitra, Wogihara, and Vaidya): (a) it follows the Add.866 reading buddhatvam (“buddhahood”), which is confirmed by Tib. sangs rgyas nyid, buddhatvam, where the modern Sanskrit editions read vṛddhatvam (“growth”), probably because the syllable bu looks similar to the syllable vṛ and they were confused either by the original scribes in their copying of the manuscripts or in the readers of those manuscripts. Why is this so? It is because those small-minded people will not know that the transcendent state of wisdom brings forth the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. So, they will think they should dismiss the transcendent state of wisdom, abandon it, and give it up. In addition, they will think they should study the other collections of scriptures that recommend the stages of disciples and solitary realizers. As such, these bodhisattvas will be engaged in something like the branches and foliage.According to Ratnākara (F.124.a–b), “foliage” means a collection of leaves. They are “engaged in something like foliage” means they have applied themselves to the collections of scriptures that are like foliage. RK: patrapalālam patrasamūhaḥ | palālopamāpratipannā iti palāśopameṣu sūtrānteṣv abhiyuktāḥ. Note here that the English translation “engaged in foliage” follows the Cambridge Add.866 MS reading śākhāpatra­palālopamā­pratipannā (as opposed to the modern editions, which read śākhāpatra­palālopamāḥ pratipannāḥ) and Ratnākara, which reads palālopamāpratipannā. Why is that? Subhūti, that is because a bodhisattva great being does not train in the manner that persons on the disciple path or the path of a solitary realizer do. So, Subhūti, how do persons on the disciples’ and solitary realizers’ path train? Subhūti, they have the notion, ‘We will tame only ourselves, pacify only ourselves, and completely liberate only ourselves.’ Then, in order to tame, pacify, and completely liberate themselves, they undertake preliminary practices to accumulate all the roots of virtue.According to Ratnākara (F.124.b), they “tame” by taming the five sense faculties; “pacify” by taming the mind; and “completely liberate” by ending rebirth. They undertake “preliminary practices,” i.e., ways to apply themselves, in order “to accumulate all the roots of virtue.” RK: damayiṣyāmaḥ pañcendriyadamanāt| śamayiṣyāmaḥ cetaso damanāt| parinirvāpayiṣyāmaḥ punarbhavakṣayāt| kuśalamūl ānām abhisaṃskāro niṣpādanāni| tadarthāḥ prayoga vyāpārāḥ. By contrast, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being does not train like that. Rather, a bodhisattva great being trains with the following notion: ‘I will establish myself in the actual nature and in order to benefit the whole world, I will also establish all sentient beings in that actual nature. I will completely liberate the immeasurable masses of sentient beings.’ It is in this way that a bodhisattva great being would undertake preliminary practices to accumulate all the roots of virtue, but he or she would not develop conceits about them.According to Ratnākara (F.124.b), they “would not develop conceits about them” because they do not mentally perceive those beings or themselves. RK: na ca tair mantavyam iti teṣām ātmanaś cānupalambhād. It would be, for instance, just like a man, who had never seen an elephant, looking to determine its color and shape. If he were to arrive at the elephant in the dark, he might look around wherever there was some light. Seeing the footprint of an elephant, the man might think that he should investigate the elephant’s footprint and determine the elephant’s color and shape from that. So, what do you think, Subhūti? Would this man seem to be of a learned nature?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “In the very same way, Subhūti, those persons on the bodhisattva path should be understood to have a similar nature. So, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when, Subhūti, they wish to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening‍—without knowing or inquiring about this transcendent state of wisdom‍—and give up on it, thinking that they should instead seek the collections of scriptures that recommend the stages of the disciples and solitary realizers. It would be, for instance, Subhūti, just like a man seeking jewels. Suppose that after seeing the ocean, he were not to dive in, look for jewels there, and acquire them, but instead‍—because he imagined the water in a cow’s hoofprint to be the same as the ocean‍—he were to investigate a cow’s hoofprint as a source of jewels. What do you think, Subhūti? Would this man seem to be of a learned nature?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “In the very same way, Subhūti, those persons on the bodhisattva path should be understood to have a similar nature. Even after obtaining this profound transcendent state of wisdom, they reject it without immersing themselves in it and without considering it. They think that they should seek those collections of scriptures that recommend the stages of the disciples and solitary realizers through living without any concern for others. Those scriptures do not describe the bodhisattva path. Instead, they describe retreating from the world with the idea of merely taming oneself, pacifying oneself, and completely liberating oneself.According to Hari (505), “retreating from the world” refers to isolating oneself physically and so on. Hari: pratisaṃlayanam iti kāyādivivekaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.124.b), “retreating from the world” refers to meditative concentration. RK: pratisaṃlayanaṃ samādhiḥ| They also describe the fruitions of a stream enterer, a once-returner, a non-returner, a worthy one, and a solitary realizer that are to be attained. All this is taught in connection with the stages of disciples and solitary realizers. They think, ‘I will become completely liberated when my mindset is freed from worldly influences by not clinging to this present life.’ The bodhisattva great beings should not develop such a mindset toward this life. Why not? Because, Subhūti, those bodhisattva great beings who have set out on the great journey have donned the great armor. They should never develop an improper mindset that lacks concern for others. Why not? Since those guides of the world are genuine persons who bring benefit to the world, they should train in the six transcendent states continuously and constantly forever.According to Ratnākara (F.124.b), “forever” means until awakening; “constantly” means this rebirth; and “continuously” means every day. RK: nityakālam ity ā bodheḥ| satataṃ caitat pratijanma bhāvāt samitaṃ ca pratyahaṃ bhāvād iti satatasamitam. So, one should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when, Subhūti, those kinds of persons on the bodhisattva path who have immature roots of virtue, faulty and narrow minds, and weak dispositions have given up on this transcendent state of wisdom. Without knowing or understanding the collection of scriptures associated with the six transcendent states, they think that they should seek the collections of scriptures that recommend the stages of the disciples and solitary realizers. Those kinds of persons on the bodhisattva path would be, for instance, just like a builder or his apprentice wanting to build or construct a palace the same size as the Vaijayanta Palace.According to Hari (505), “builder” means carpenter and “apprentice” means his student. “The Vaijayanta Palace” is in the middle of the plains (of the Heaven of the Thirty-Three Gods) on the top of Mount Meru, in a golden city called Sudarśana (“Beautiful”), which is (roughly translated) five million miles around and twenty miles high. The palace is known as the Vaijayanta Palace. Each of the Vaijayanta Palace’s four sides is twenty miles in length, while its upward rise has been constructed with a splendid appearance. Understand the pair words “build or construct” to mean in a slow or fast type of way. Note: The translation of distance into miles in this section is very rough. These approximate estimates are based on the ancient Indian distance measure called yojanas, which are reported by different sources to be anywhere between 2.5 and 9 miles. Hari: palagaṇḍa iti takṣakaḥ. palagaṇḍāntevāsī ti tacchiṣyaḥ. vaijayantasya prāsādasye ti sumerutalamadhye sudarśanaṃ nāma nagaraṃ sauvarṇaṃ. dairghyeṇārdhatṛtīya yojana­sahasrapramāṇam ekaikaṃ pārśvaṃ. ucchrayeṇa dvyardhayojanaṃ. tatra nagarasya madhye śakrasya vaijayanto nāma prāsādo, dairghyeṇārdha­tṛtīya­yojana­śata­pramāṇam ekaikaṃ pārśvaṃ. ucchrayeṇa yathāśobhaṃ kṛto vaijayantaprāsādaḥ. cirakṣipraprabhedena kartukāmo nirmātukāma iti padadvayaṃ. Suppose that the builder or his apprentice were to search for a building site the size of the palace of the sun and moon.In ancient Indian cosmography, the sun and the moon were considered heavenly bodies with their own “palace.” Cf. PTSD vimāna, entry 7. Also, suppose that, while searching, he were to see a building site the size of the palace of the sun and moon and to think that its size would measure up to his purpose. But what do you think, Subhūti? If he wanted to build or construct a palace the size of the Vaijayanta Palace, would the size of the sun and moon measure up to that palace?”

“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.According to Hari (505), “definitely not” means absolutely not. He is implying that the builder’s notion of the cause does not match his notion of the effect. That is to say, the size of the Vaijayanta Palace is much larger than the palaces of the sun and moon, which are, respectively, (roughly) four to five hundred miles across. Hari: no hīdam iti sūryācandramasor vimānād yathāyogaṃ pañcāśatsaikayojanād vaijayantasyādhika­pramāṇatvena hetu­phalānurūpa­viparyayasūcanān naivety arthaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.124.b), the size (of an orb) would be much too small compared to the Vaijayanta Palace. RK: tac ca vaijayantād atīvālpapramāṇam.

The Blessed One said, “In the very same way, Subhūti, in the future, such persons on the bodhisattva path will first hear and receive the transcendent state of wisdom, then leave it behind, abandon it, and think to seek the state of complete knowing through those collections of scriptures associated with the stages of the disciples and solitary realizers, which recommend thinking, ‘We should tame ourselves, pacify ourselves, and completely liberate ourselves.’ But such scriptures lead them only to tame themselves, pacify themselves, and completely liberate themselves.The implication here is that the disciple path leads only to “complete liberation,” whereas the bodhisattva path leads to the “state of complete knowing”‍—i.e., a state of awakening beyond the personal liberation described in those scriptures. So what do you think, Subhūti? Would this man seem to be of a learned nature?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, one should understand those kinds of persons on the bodhisattva path also to encounter an activity of Māra. Subhūti, it would be, for instance, like a certain man wanting to see a universal emperor. The man might see a universal emperor and, upon seeing him, grasp his superficial features, thinking that this ruler has this or that sort of outward appearance, form, majesty, and prosperity.According to Ratnākara (F.125.a), a “universal emperor” (cakravartin‍—lit. “wheel turner”) is so called because his wheel (cakra) turns (pravart ana) in the world. (This can be interpreted to mean either that his chariot wheels can go anywhere he wishes in the universe or that he makes things happen in the world). The “wheel” is a supernatural, atmospheric, thousand-spoked wheel with a central nave and an outer rim. It can be made of either iron, copper, silver, or gold, according to the requirements (of the different ranks of universal emperors). His “outward appearance” is like gold from the Jambu River. His “form” is replete with the thirty-two marks of a great person. His “majesty” means his power, influence, and aura of light. His “prosperity” means his good fortune. RK: loke cakrasya pravartanāt cakravartī| tat punaś cakram apauruṣam ākāśagāmi sahasrāraṃ sanābhikaṃ sanemikam, āyasaṃ tāmraṃ raupyaṃ sauvarṇa vā yathāyogam| varṇo jāmbunadanibhaḥ| saṃsthānaṃ dvātriṃśanmahāpuruṣalakṣaṇopetam| tejaḥ sarvam abhibhūḥ prabhāvaḥ prabhāmaṇḍalaś ca| ṛddhiḥ sampattiḥ. The man might also see a regional king and, upon seeing him, might also grasp his superficial features. That said, since it is beyond that man’s ken to grasp a ruler’s distinctive indications, the man might say that, compared to the universal emperor, the regional king has the very same sort of outward appearance, form, majesty and prosperity.Here, the point is that outwardly the universal emperor and regional ruler may appear the same, but their less obvious qualities are different. According to Ratnākara (F.125.a), as for what is distinctive (about a universal emperor), there are seven precious things: the precious wheel, the precious elephant, the precious horse, the precious minister, the precious bursar, and the wish-fulfilling jewel. RK: viśeṣa taḥ saptaratnāni‍—cakraratnaṃ hastiratnaṃ aśvaratnaṃ pariṇāyakaratnaṃ gṛhapatiratnaṃ maṇiratnaṃ ceti. He only mentions six here, but most lists include the precious queen (strīratna) and/or the precious general (senapatiratna). But what do you think? Would this man seem to be of a learned nature?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “In the very same way, Subhūti, one should understand persons on the bodhisattva path also to encounter an activity of Māra when, in the future, they first hear and receive the transcendent state of wisdom, then leave it behind, abandon it, and think to seek the state of complete knowing through those collections of scriptures associated with the stages of the disciples and solitary realizers. Again, Subhūti, I do not recommend any bodhisattva great being to seek the state of complete knowing through these kinds of collections of scriptures associated with the stages of the disciples and solitary realizers. On the contrary, the Tathāgata has made it known that without having trained in the skill in means of bodhisattva great beings in the transcendent state of wisdom, a bodhisattva great being will never go beyond cyclic existence to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Why is that? It is because the attainment of a bodhisattva is not made clear in the other collections of scriptures.According to Ratnākara (F.125.a), “not made clear” means confusing or perplexing because (in those scriptures) the topic is undeveloped and trivial. The “attainment of a bodhisattva” is so called because he or she attains (lit. is elevated) due to it. RK: dhandhayati mohayaty aparisphuṭatvād alpatvāc ceti dhandhakaḥ| yasmād dhetor bodhisattvaḥ samudeti sa bodhisattvasamudāgamaḥ. For this reason, Subhūti, the Tathāgata saw the benefit in the transcendent state of wisdom and, through various means, helped bodhisattva great beings see the transcendent state of wisdom clearly, be enthusiastic about it, rejoice in it, settle down in it, and become established in it with the wish that those bodhisattva great beings would not regress from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. So, Subhūti, what do you think? Suppose those bodhisattva great beings were to reach the great path and enter into the path beyond regressing, only then to dismiss it, turn back from it, and think they should instead seek the lower path.According to Ratnākara (F.125.a), they “reach it” due to merely obtaining it. They “enter into it” through listening to it and so on. “Dismiss it” means abandon it. “Turn back from it” means turn themselves away from it. RK: avāpya lābhamātreṇa| samāsādya śravaṇādibhiḥ| vivarjya tyaktvā| vivartya parāṅmukhībhūya. Would they strike you as being of a learned nature?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, it would be, for instance, just as if a hungry man obtained food that was extremely flavorful, had beneficial effects, had pleasurable effects, had a life-lengthening quality, and sated his hunger and thirst. But suppose that he thought instead to seek cheap rice porridge. Then, after obtaining it, he thought to dismiss the extremely flavorful food, throw it out, and consume the cheap rice porridge. What would you think, Subhūti? Would that person seem to be of a learned nature?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “In the very same way, Subhūti, some bodhisattvas in the future, who hear and receive the transcendent state of wisdom, will leave it behind, abandon it, give it up, and distance themselves from it. After leaving it behind, abandoning it, giving it up, and distancing themselves from it, they will think to seek the state of complete knowing through those collections of scriptures associated with the paths of the disciples and solitary realizers, which recommend the stages of the disciples and solitary realizers. So, what would you think, Subhūti? Should those bodhisattvas be understood to be of a learned nature?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “In the very same way, Subhūti, one should understand this too to be an activity of Māra arising for them. Subhūti, it would be, for instance, just like a man who receives a precious jewel, but considers it to be the same as a gem with half its value and quality. What do you think, Subhūti? Should such a man be understood to be of a learned nature?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “In the very same way, Subhūti, some persons on the bodhisattva path in the future will hear and receive this profound and brilliantAccording to Ratnākara (F.125.b), it is “profound” because it is a diadem on the ultimate truth and “brilliant” because it is an illumination utterly free of stains. RK: gambhīraṃ paramārthaśekharatvāt| prabhāsvaraṃ paramanirmalālokatvāt. transcendent state of wisdom, but consider it to be the same as the paths of the disciples and solitary realizers. They will think to seek the state of complete knowing and skill in means on the stages of the disciples and solitary realizers. What do you think, Subhūti? Should such persons be understood to be of a learned nature?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, one should understand this too to be an activity of Māra arising for these bodhisattva great beings. Furthermore, when this profound and brilliant transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, taught, expounded, explained, taken in, chanted, recited, and even just written out, there could arise numerous illuminating experiences that cause mental distraction. Subhūti, one should understand these too to be an activity of Māra arising for these bodhisattva great beings.”
At this, the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “But, Blessed One, is it possible to write out the transcendent state of wisdom?”

“Definitely not, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One. “One should also understand it to be an activity of Māra when, Subhūti, some bodhisattva great beings might write out the transcendent state of wisdom in letters, then think that the transcendent state of wisdom either is or is not the written transcendent state of wisdom. Alternatively, they might fixate on the transcendent state of wisdom as something either in the letters or not in the letters.According to Ratnākara (F.125.b), the “activity of Māra” is considering the transcendent state of wisdom as any of (four possibilities, namely,) (1) “is” or (2) “is not the written transcendent state of wisdom”; or is (3) “in the letters,” or (4) “not in the letters.” To explain (the refutation of these four possibilities here), the transcendent state of wisdom cannot be (1) the written transcendent state of wisdom (i.e., the first possibility), because the transcendent state of wisdom lacks any visible form, smell, and so on; (2) not the written transcendent state of wisdom (i.e., the second possibility), because it is its ultimate reality; (3) the letters of the written transcendent state of wisdom (i.e., the third possibility), because all things are beyond letters; and (4) not the letters of the written transcendent state of wisdom (i.e., the fourth possibility), because those letters are its natural outcome, because those letters themselves do point toward the transcendent state of wisdom, and because one realizes the transcendent state of wisdom by focusing on those letters themselves as (containing) the meaning. Thus, the four possible faults here are fixating on (1) the writing, (2) nonexistence, (3) letters, and (4) nonletters. RK: ye kecit prajñāpāramitā likhite ti, asatī ti, akṣarāṇī ti, anakṣare ti vā manyante sarvaṃ tan mārakarma| tathā hi na sā likhitā rūpagandhādivirahāt| nāsatī paramārthasattvāt| nākṣarāṇi sarvadharmāṇām anakṣaratvāt| n ānakṣarā tanniṣyandatvād eṣām akṣarāṇām| ebhir eva ca tasyāḥ sūcanāt| etāny eva ca tenārthenālambya tasyā adhigamāt| tad evaṃ likhanābhiniveśo ’sattvābhiniveśo ’kṣarābhiniveśo ’nakṣarābhiniveśaś ceti catvāro doṣāḥ.

“Furthermore, one should understand the following to be activities of Māra that arise for some bodhisattva great beings.This sentence‍—“One should understand the following to be activities of Māra that arise for some bodhisattva great beings”‍—has been added to clarify ahead of something that the Blessed One himself clarifies only at the end of the following, very long relative clause. Subhūti, when some bodhisattva great beings are writing out the transcendent state of wisdom, their attention might be focused on their countries, or their villages, towns, market towns, communities, kingdoms, principalities, or parks.According to Ratnākara (F.125.b–26.a), “countries” refers to (countries) such as Mālava and so on; “villages” (grāma) refers to nonaffluent dwelling areas with one hundred or one thousand homes; “towns” (nagara) refers to affluent dwelling areas with one thousand to one hundred thousand homes; “market towns” (nigama) are just towns, but are even more affluent; “provinces” (janapada) refers to areas like Kāśi (the area around modern-day Vārāṇasī) and so on; “kingdoms” (rāṣṭra) are collections of those provinces; and “principalities” (rājadhānī) are cities inhabited by rulers. RK: deśo mālavādiḥ| śatena sahasreṇa vā saṃkhyātagṛho ’nāḍhyānāṃ nivāso grāmaḥ| sahasreṇa lakṣeṇa vā saṃkhyātagṛha āḍhyānāṃ nivāso nagaram| nagaram eva ca vastubhūyiṣṭhaṃ nigamaḥ| maṇḍalaṃ kāśyādi janapadaḥ| tatsamūho rāṣṭram| rājādhyuṣitaṃ nagaraṃ rājadhānī. Their attention might be focused on teachers, literature, or thieves. Their attention might be focused on waterfront areas, streets, or vehicles.According to Ratnākara (F.126.a), “literature” means prose or poetry. “Waterfront areas” refers to the riverside steps (where townspeople bathe). “Streets” (viśikhā) means roads for carriages. “Vehicles” (śibikā) means palanquins (or similar vehicles). RK: ākhyānaṃ gadyakāvyam| gulmo ghaṭṭaḥ| viśikhā rathyā| śivikā yāpyayānam. Their attention might be focused on comfort, discomfort, or dangers. Their attention might be focused on women, men, or those in between. Their attention might be focused on separating from pleasant or unpleasant things.According to Hari (513), “separating from pleasant or unpleasant things” means (the concerns of) not being connected with pleasant or unpleasant things. Hari: priyāpriyavyatyastaḥ priyāpriyaviyogaḥ.

“Their attention might be focused on something connected with their mothers, fathers, brothers, sisters, friends, relatives, kin, or companions. Their attention might be focused on something connected with their wives, sons, or daughters. Their attention might be focused on something connected with their houses, food, or drinks. Their attention might be focused on something connected with their clothes, furniture, livelihoods, or tasks to be done.According to Ratnākara (F.126.a), “livelihoods” means the resources for living, the means of livelihood. The “tasks needing to be done” refers to past events. RK: jīvitā rthāḥ pariṣkārāḥ jīvitapariṣkārāḥ| According to Hari (513), “tasks needing to be done” means the things that constantly need to be done. Hari: iti kartavyatā satatakaraṇīyatā. Their attention might be focused on desires, aversions, or confusions. Their attention might be focused on the seasons, auspicious times, or inauspicious times. Their attention might be focused on songs, music, dances, poetry, plays, or stories.According to Ratnākara (F.126.a), “stories” means accounts of past events. RK: iti hāsaḥ purāvṛttam. Their attention might be focused on scholarship, business dealings, levity, or dancing. Their attention might be focused on sorrows, endeavors, or their self.

“Subhūti, when this transcendent state of wisdom is being spoken, taught, expounded, explained, taken in, chanted, recited, or even just written out, the pernicious Māra will draw bodhisattva great beings to these and other focuses of attention, create obstacles for them, and make them mentally distracted. So, the bodhisattva great beings should recognize them to be the activities of Māra and, having recognized them, should let them go.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, their attention might be focused on kings, princes, elephants, horses, vehicles, or spectating at the docks.According to Hari (513), “spectating at the docks” (darśanaṃ < akṣadarśanaṃ) means watching dice games and so on by the waterfront. Hari: gulmā kṣa darśanaṃ ghaṭṭa sthāne pāśakādidarśanaṃ. Note: The Tibetan translation la gcan gyi cho lo lta ba incorporates Hari’s gloss. Subhūti, one should understand this to be the activity of Māra for those bodhisattva great beings.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, their attention might be focused on their fires, their fantasies, or growing their wealth and resources. Subhūti, one should understand this to be the activity of Māra for those bodhisattva great beings.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, when this transcendent state of wisdom is spoken, taught, expounded, explained, taken in, chanted, recited, or even just written out, bodhisattva great beings might encounter obstacles to donations, tributes, robes, alms, seats, beds, preventive medicines, and other requisites. That is to say, they might indulge in donations, tributes, or celebrity, or be mentally distressed about those things.According to Ratnākara (F.126.a), “obstacles” means impediments; “donations” means getting robes and so on; “tributes” means (signs of) respect; “celebrity” means renown; “indulging” means delighting in those things; “mentally distressed” is by impediments to getting robes and so on. RK: antarāyā vighātāḥ| lābhaś cīvarādiprāptiḥ| satkāra ādaraḥ| śloko yaśaḥ| tair āsvāda uddharṣaḥ| cittotpīḍā cīvarādivighātaiḥ. According to Hari (513), “donations” means getting precious things and so on; “tributes” means splendid diadems and so on; “celebrity” means renown; “indulging” means experiencing those things. Hari: lābhasatkāraślokāsvāda iti tatra lābho ratnādiprāptiḥ, satkāraḥ śrīpaṭṭabandhanādiḥ, śloko yaśaḥ. teśām anubhavanam āsvādaḥ. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should also understand this to be the activity of Māra for those bodhisattva great beings. One should recognize all these as activities of Māra and, having recognized them, one should let them go.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, when this transcendent state of wisdom is spoken, taught, expounded, explained, taken in, chanted, recited, or even just written out, the pernicious Māra might attack, disguised as a monk, and might draw them to the very profound collections of scriptures associated with the stages of the disciples and solitary realizers, saying, ‘Train in this. Write this out. Explain this. Recite this. Through doing so, the state of complete knowing will be accomplished.’ But, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being with skill in means would not feel any desire for these.According to Ratnākara (F.126.a), “skill in means” since he or she does not fall to an inferior level of awakening. The fault of someone without skill in means is seeking the opposite of that in the profound collection of scriptures. RK: kiñcāpī ti yadyapi| upāyakauśalyam iti yena hīnabodhau na pated iti| upāyakauśalya­rahitasya gambhīrasūtrānte atanmārgaṇadoṣaḥ. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, these collections of scriptures discuss emptiness, freedom from signs, and freedom from inclinations, but they do not explain the skill in means of bodhisattva great beings. Not recognizing that those scriptures contain a difference in knowledge and skill in means, those bodhisattva great beings might think to leave behind this profound transcendent state of wisdom. After eschewing this profound transcendent state of wisdom, they might think to seek the skill in means in those collections of scriptures associated with the stages of the disciples and solitary realizers. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should also understand this to be the activity of Māra.

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 32 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and to the beginning of Chapter 41 in the Eighteen Thousand. The remainder of this chapter corresponds to part of Chapter 32 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (down to 32.62) and Hundred Thousand, and to the entirety of Chapter 41 in the Eighteen Thousand.“Furthermore, Subhūti, an enthusiastic Dharma student might wish to take in the transcendent state of wisdom, but the Dharma teacher might be lazy and unwilling to teach the Dharma.According to Ratnākara (F.126.a), a “Dharma teacher” (bhāṇako < guruḥ) preaches (the Dharma), and a Dharma student (dhārmaśravaṇikaḥ śiṣyaḥ) has the goal of listening to the Dharma. RK: dharma sya bhāṇako vaktā guruḥ| dharmaśravaṇaṃ prayojanam eṣām iti dhārmaśravaṇikaḥ śiṣyaḥ. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.According to Ratnākara (F.126.a), “the necessary conditions” refers to a complete state, while “the lack of necessary conditions” is due to a deficiency (in that regard). RK: samagrabhāvaḥ sāmagrī| viparyāsāt visāmagrī.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the Dharma teacher might not be lazy and might be willing to bestow the transcendent state of wisdom, but the Dharma student might be lazy and busy.According to Ratnākara (F.126.a), being “lazy” refers to indolence. Being “busy” also refers to indolence, i.e., that of householders. Both descriptions are a deficiency of being lazy with respect to interest (in the Dharma). RK: kilāsaṃ kausīdyam| bahukṛtyatā gṛhiṇām sapi kausīdyam iti cchanda­kilāsavaidhuryam. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the enthusiastic Dharma student might wish to take in the transcendent state of wisdom, to keep it in mind, to chant it, to master it, to pass it on, or even just to write it out. He or she might also be educated, intelligent, and mindful. But the Dharma teacher might have wandered off somewhere else.According to Ratnākara (F.126.a), “might have wandered off” means might have gone (somewhere else). RK: kṣepsyata iti karme karttariprayogaḥ| gamiṣyatītyarthaḥ. Note: Ratnākara’s comment also explains why, in terms of Sanskrit grammar, the active sentence construction applies here. Alternatively, the Dharma teacher might not understand it through its brief explanation, not understand it through its detailed explanation, or not understand anything beyond its words.According to Ratnākara (F.126.a), someone could “understand it through its brief explanation” through special knowledge when something general is explained, or “understand it through its detailed explanation” through special knowledge when anything is explained. A person who does not “know anything beyond the words” is a literalist, because they know nothing more than exactly what is said. RK: udaghaṭitajñaḥ sāmānyokte viśeṣajñānāt| vipañcitajñaḥ kiñcid apy uktau viśeṣaparijñānāt| anabhijñaḥ padaparamaḥ| sa hi yad ucyate tad eva jānāti nādhikam. Whichever the case, Subhūti, this too is a lack of the necessary conditions for this transcendent state of wisdom to be spoken, taught, expounded, explained, recited, trained in, or even just written out. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should also understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the Dharma teacher might not be lazy, might know much more beyond the words, and might be willing to bestow this transcendent state of wisdom. But the Dharma student might have set out for somewhere else. Alternatively, he or she might not understand through a brief explanation, not understand through a detailed explanation, or not understand anything beyond the words. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should also understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the Dharma teacher, desirous of worldly things, might desire donations, tributes, and robes. But the Dharma student might be content with few desires, be reclusive, or not wish to offer his or her wealth. Subhūti, this is a lack of the necessary conditions for this transcendent state of wisdom to be trained in and written out. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, a Dharma student might be faithful, wishing to listen to this transcendent state of wisdom, to understand its meaning, to make offerings for it, and to give everything up. But the Dharma teacher might have no faith, have few desires, and have no wish to teach. Hence, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should also understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, a Dharma student might be faithful, wishing to listen to this transcendent state of wisdom and to understand its meaning. But a Dharma teacher might not possess the scriptures or have them accessible for some reason, because of some obstacle to the Dharma. Hence, Subhūti, that Dharma teacher who has not obtained [the teachings] might rebuff that Dharma student. In that case, Subhūti, this is a lack of the necessary conditions. A bodhisattva great being should also understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra against those taking in the transcendent state of wisdom, chanting it, mastering it, or even just passing it on and writing it out.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, a Dharma teacher might wish to teach, but the Dharma student might have no longing to listen. In that case Subhūti, this is a lack of the necessary conditions for taking up the transcendent state of wisdom, keeping it in mind, chanting it, mastering it, or even just passing it on and writing it out. A bodhisattva great being should also understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the Dharma student might be weighed down by sleepiness and weighed down physically, and, because of being weighed down by sleepiness and physically fatigued, might not wish to listen. But the Dharma teacher might wish to teach. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should also understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the Dharma teacher might be weighed down by sleepiness and weighed down physically, and, because of being weighed down by sleepiness and physically fatigued, might not wish to teach. But the Dharma student might wish to listen. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should also understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, when this transcendent state of wisdom is being written out, spoken, and trained in, a man might come there and describe the unpleasantness of the hells, the unpleasantness of the animal births, the unpleasantness of the hungry ghost realms, and the unpleasantness of the asura bodies. The man might say, ‘The hells involve such and such sufferings, the animal births involve such and such sufferings, the hungry ghost realms involve such and such sufferings, the asura bodies involve such and such sufferings, and conditioned things involve such and such sufferings, so you must end your suffering right here and now.’Ending one’s own suffering “right here and now” (ihaiva), i.e., starting now in this very birth, is an activity of Māra, because it entails a breach of the bodhisattva vow to remain in painful cyclic existence until all beings are freed. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should also understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, when this transcendent state of wisdom is being written out, spoken, and trained in, a man might come to that place. He might describe the pleasantness of the gods, saying, ‘The gods enjoy such and such happiness. The heavens have such and such pleasure. In such and such desire realms, the sense pleasures can be enjoyed. In such and such form realms, the meditative states can be attained. In such and such formless realms, the attainments can be attained. Nevertheless, when all these are thoroughly examined with wisdom, they all are merely painful rebirths. As the Blessed One said, “Monks, I do not speak highly of anything resulting in cyclic existence as it only lasts for the snap of a finger. Everything conditioned is impermanent. Everything leading to cyclic existence is suffering. Everything within the three realms is empty. All things lack a self.” Thus, understanding that all things are perishable, impermanent, suffering, and bound to deteriorate, the wise should attain the fruition of a stream enterer, the fruition of a once-returner, the fruition of a non-returner, and the state of a worthy one right here and now.According to Hari (519), “perishable” in the sense that the continuity of their existence is interrupted; “impermanent” in the sense that impermanent things are momentary; “suffering” in the sense that they are connected with existential suffering (samskāraduḥkhatā); and “bound to deteriorate” in the sense that the suffering of change (vipariṇāmaduḥkha) is certain to arise. It should be understood here that the words “perishable” and so on are explaining the previous sentences, namely “conditioned things are impermanent” and so on. Hari: tatra aśāśvataṃ prabandhocchedāt, anityaṃ kṣaṇikānityatayā, duḥkhaṃ samskāraduḥkhatāyogāt, vipariṇāmadharmakaṃ vipariṇāmaduḥkha­sadbhāvād iti tad evaṃ sarvam aśāśvatam ityādi sarvaṃ hi samskṛtam anityam ityāder vyākhyānam ity avasātavyaṃ. Let us never again meet with the many sufferings of success and failure!’ In regard to these statements, some bodhisattvas might become disenchanted.According to Hari (519), they “become disenchanted” means they will turn away from practicing for the sake of awakening due to a desire for positive states of being, such as the first fruit of a stream enterer and so on. Hari: saṃvegam āpatsyanta iti prathama­phalādisugatyabhilāṣeṇa bodhicārikāvimukhatāṃ kariṣyanti. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should also understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, some monastic Dharma teachers might be fond of solitude, but the Dharma students might adore community. So, a Dharma teacher might say he will bestow this transcendent state of wisdom on those who follow him, and not upon those who do not follow him. Yet, even though noble sons and daughters might follow that Dharma teacher with desire, longing, and devotion for the teachings, they will never give themselves the opportunity to receive them. Moreover, when that Dharma teacher has any need for goods, they will not wish to offer him those. So, wherever the teacher might go, he will encounter a shortage of food, a lack of provisions and security, and impediments to survival.According to Hari (521), “a shortage of food” means nourishment will be difficult to obtain because food will be lacking. A “lack of provisions and security” (ayogakṣemaś) is due to lacking “provisions,” i.e., getting whatever is desired, or “security,” i.e., being free from attacks. They are called “impediments to survival” because they end one’s life. Hari: durbhikṣa iti bhaktarahitatvena duḥprāpyabhikṣaḥ. ayogakṣema iti iṣṭāvāptir yogaḥ, nirupadravatvaṃ kṣemas, tadubhayābhāvād ayogakṣemaḥ. jīvitendriyanirodhāj jīvitāntarāyaḥ. For their part, those Dharma students might hear from others, ‘That place has a shortage of food and a lack of provisions and security, and impediments to survival in it.’According to Hari (521), after the phrase “impediments to survival in it,” one must supply the phrase “those who reach that place without obstacles.” Hari: tasmiṃś ca pradeśe jīvitāntarāyo ’pi bhaved iti, anantarāyāṃ diśaṃ gaccheyur bhavanta iti śeṣaḥ. Even to those noble sons or daughters who reach that place without obstacles, the Dharma teacher might say, ‘Noble sons and daughters, there is the danger of a food shortage in this place. You may join me, but please do not regret it later when you are faced with the danger of a food shortage.’ With subtle hints like this, the Dharma teacher will repel them. Being overcome with fear, the Dharma students will interpret such words to be signs that he is rejecting them and being ungenerous. Finding him ungenerous, they will cease to pursue him. Subhūti, this too is a lack of the necessary conditions for the bodhisattva great being to write out this transcendent state of wisdom, speak about it, train in it, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. Subhūti, he or she should understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the Dharma teacher might set out for a place where there are dangerous little creatures, dangerous carnivorous animals, and dangerous nonhumans.According to Hari (522), “little creatures” such as scorpions and so on. “Carnivorous animals” are predators. The “nonhumans” are hungry ghosts and so on. Hari: tatra jantur vṛścikādiḥ, duṣṭagraho vyāḍaḥ, pretādir amanuṣyaḥ. Going about life and dwelling there, he might proceed farther into a wilderness area infested with snakes and thieves that lacks drinkable water and has scarcely any food.According to Hari (522), a “wilderness area” means a dangerous place. “Snake” means a serpent. Hari: kāntāraṃ bhayasthānaṃ, sarīsṛpaḥ sarpaḥ. Note: the translation has followed Ratnākara’s explanation of the Sanskrit compounds (from ch. 19): vyāḍa iḥ kā ntāraṃ vyāḍakāntāram| pānīya varjitakāntāraṃ pānīyakāntāram. He might tell his Dharma students the following: ‘Noble sons and daughters, you should know that we are setting out for a place where there are dangerous little creatures, dangerous carnivorous animals, and dangerous nonhuman flesh eaters, in a wilderness area infested with snakes and thieves that lacks drinkable water and has scarcely any food.According to Hari (522), a “nonhuman flesh-eaters” means yakṣas and so on who feed on flesh. Hari: mānsāśī yākṣādiḥ kravyādaḥ. Noble sons and daughters, you should know that you may experience such sufferings.’ With these subtle hints, he will repulse them. They will be overcome with fear, and in being so, they will cease to follow him and turn back the other way. Subhūti, this obstacle to the transcendent state of wisdom will also arise when it is being taught, expounded, explained, recited, and so on, to the point of it even just being written out. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, a monastic Dharma teacher might be inclined toward families that are friendly and give him alms, and because of that, he might constantly consider it necessary to look in on and visit families that are friendly and give him alms. Because he is greatly occupied with constantly having to look in on them, he might repulse the Dharma students by saying that there is someone he just needs to look in on or just needs to visit. Subhūti, this obstacle to the transcendent state of wisdom will also arise when it is being written out and mastered. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should understand this lack of the necessary conditions to be the activity of Māra.

“Thus, Subhūti, the pernicious Māra strives in these various ways to ensure that no one will take in the transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind it, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, or write it out or have it written out. Therefore, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should recognize how all these obstacles of lacking the necessary conditions are, in fact, the activities of Māra. And after recognizing them as such, the bodhisattva great being should abandon them.”

At this, the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, what motivates the pernicious Māra here to engage in such great efforts and to strive through his tricks to ensure that no one will take in the transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, write it out, or have it written out?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, he is motivated by the fact that the transcendent state of wisdom has given birth to the blessed buddhas’ state of complete knowing, and the Tathāgata’s teachings have given birth to the abandonment of the defilements by immeasurable, innumerable beings. Since the pernicious Māra has not found any vulnerability in those who have abandoned the defilements, he has become afflicted with suffering, depressed, and shot through with pangs of grief. Motivated by this, he agitatedly engages in great efforts whenever the transcendent state of wisdom is being written out and mastered. With great effort, he strives through his tricks to ensure that no one can write out or master this transcendent state of wisdom.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the pernicious Māra might come in the guise of an ascetic and sow discord. In this way, he might deter the noble sons or daughters who have just begun to set out on the path. Subhūti, the pernicious Māra will sow doubt by saying, ‘This transcendent state of wisdom is not something to which venerable ones should listen. Rather, the transcendent state of wisdom is just like what is transmitted in my scriptures and what is contained in my scriptures.’According to Hari (524), “transmitted in my scriptures” because it is in accord with the other scriptures; “contained in my scriptures” because it is shown in their own Arthaviniścaya sūtra (Distinctly Ascertaining the Meanings, Toh 317, a Lesser Vehicle work) and so on. He will “sow doubt” because the collections of scriptures similar to the Mother (Transcendent State of Wisdom) will cause doubt to arise. Hari: aparasūtrānulomanāt sūtrāgataṃ. svasminn arthaviniścayādisūtre dṛśyamānatvāt sūtraparyāpannaṃ. jananīsadṛśasūtropa­saṃhāreṇa saṃśayotpādanāt saṃśayaṃ prakṣepsyati. In this way, too, going further than that, Subhūti, after coming in the guise of an ascetic and sowing discord, he will cause bodhisattvas who have just begun to set out on the path to fall into doubt, if they are small-minded, weak-minded, narrow-minded, blind, and not yet foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Hari (524), they are “small-minded, weak-minded, narrow-minded”‍—expressed in that order‍—because they do not comprehend the three natures, i.e., the imagined, dependent, and real nature. Hari: kalpitādi­svabhāva­trayāparijñānād alpabuddhikān mandabuddhikān parīttabuddhikān iti yathākramaṃ vācyaṃ. Note: The Tibetan translation puts this sentence before the previous sentences to link it more directly with what Māra says. Those who come to doubt will neither take in the transcendent state of wisdom, nor keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, write it out, or have it written out. Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being should understand this too to be the activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, after conjuring up a monastic retinue, the pernicious Māra might come in the guise of the Buddha and carry out the activity of a māra, saying, ‘Any bodhisattva who goes about life in the profound dharmas realizes the pinnacle of reality. That is to say, he becomes a disciple, not a bodhisattva like this bodhisattva.’According to Hari (525), he “realizes the pinnacle of reality” means he attains the nirvāṇa of the disciples‍—the desire for that (state) is, in principle, produced by this statement. Hari: bhūtakoṭiṃ sākṣātkarotī ti śrāvakanirvāṇam adhigacchati. niyamād anena tatrābhilāṣo janyate. Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being should understand this too to be the activity of Māra.

“In this way, Subhūti, when the transcendent state of wisdom is being written out and mastered, the pernicious Māra causes a great many other activities of a māra to arise. A bodhisattva great being should recognize these. After doing so, he or she should abandon them and cease to be attached to them. Instead, a mindful and wisely discerning bodhisattva great being should apply heroic perseverance.”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “So it is, Blessed One. So it is, Sugata. Blessed One, those things that are extremely precious have many plotters against them. Why is that? Because they are difficult to obtain and extremely valuable, Blessed One, since they are the foremost. Therefore, they have many plotters against them. Just so, Blessed One, for the most part this transcendent state of wisdom has an abundance of obstacles that arise. Blessed One, those who are rendered inactive, due to the power of such obstacles, should be understood to be bodhisattvas magically influenced by Māra. Blessed One, they have just begun to set out on the path. Blessed One, they are small-minded. Blessed One, they are weak-minded. Blessed One, they are narrow-minded. Blessed One, they are wrong-minded. Also, by thinking that they should not take in the transcendent state of wisdom, should not keep it in mind, should not chant it, should not master it, should not pass it on, should not teach it, should not expound it, should not explain it, should not recite it, should not have it written out, and should not even so much as write it out, their mindsets do not progress toward the noblest among noble things.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “So it is, Subhūti. So it is. Subhūti, those bodhisattvas should be understood to be magically influenced by Māra if they think they should not take in the transcendent state of wisdom, should not keep it in mind, and should not chant it, and who think they should not master it, should not pass it on, should not teach it, should not expound it, should not explain it, should not recite it, should not have it written out, and should not even so much as write it out.Note: The sentence order in the Tibetan differs from the Sanskrit here. Subhūti, they have just begun to set out on the path. Subhūti, they are small-minded. Subhūti, they are weak-minded. Subhūti, they are narrow-minded. Subhūti, they are wrong-minded. Also, they have mindsets that do not progress toward the noblest among noble things if they do not wish to take in the transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, have it written out, or even so much as write it out.

“Nevertheless, Subhūti, these activities of Māra will arise and also a great number of Māra’s faults‍—which create obstacles to this transcendent state of wisdom‍—will arise.Note: Conze has interpreted this very differently: “And while it is true that these deeds of Mara are bound to arise, a great many agencies will arise in their turn that oppose the faults of Mara.” But if, Subhūti, those bodhisattvas wish to take in the transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, have it written out, and at the very least write it out, it should be understood that it is due to the Buddha’s miraculous power and the Buddha’s blessings that they so wish. Through the Buddha’s support, they will take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, have it written out, and at the very least write it out. Why is that? It is because, even though the pernicious Māra makes great efforts to create obstacles, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata makes great efforts too in order to provide support.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Māra’s Activity,” the eleventh chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 12: Showing the WorldThe Sanskrit word loka refers to both the world and the sentient beings inhabiting it. The chapter first focuses on loka as the world, but then as the sentient beings in it. So the title of the chapter could also be understood as “The Chapter on Showing Sentient Beings.”The beginning of this chapter corresponds to points in Chapter 32 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (32.63) and Hundred Thousand, and to the beginning of Chapter 42 in the Eighteen Thousand, covering the remainder of those chapters; and at the point marked by note 781 the chapter continues with content that corresponds to Chapter 33 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and to Chapter 43 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: Then the Blessed One once again addressed the venerable Subhūti: “Subhūti, as an analogy, it is just like a woman who has many sons‍—whether five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, one hundred, or one thousand. They would all make great efforts if their mother were sick. They would think, ‘How can the threats to our mother’s life be removed? How can our mother be kept alive a long time? How can our mother’s body be kept from deteriorating? How can our mother survive a long time? How indeed can our mother be kept free of deterioration? How can our mother not encounter painful feelings, not dwell in a numb state, and not experience unpleasantness with regard to her body?According to Hari (529), “not dwell in a numb state” means dwell without happiness; “unpleasantness” means something mentally disagreeable. Hari: asparśavihāra iti asukhavihāraḥ. amanāapa iti cittenānabhipretaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.128.a), to “dwell in” a numb state means to roam about in it; “unpleasantness” means mental displeasure, due to there being a cause of pain. RK: sparśavihāra iti sparśapracāraḥ| amanaāpa iti amanojñaḥ duḥkhahetutvāt. Why ask this? Because it is she who gave birth to us. She is the one who undertook difficult noble deeds for us, gave us life, and showed us the world.’ Hence, those sons would keep their mother well tended to with all the sources of happiness, keep her well protected, and keep her well cared for.According to Hari (530), they “keep” her, i.e., another (beside themselves), “well tended” with sustenance, like food and so on. They “keep her well protected” in the sense of providing protection from thieves and so on. They “keep her well cared for” in the sense of removing physical dirt and so on. Hari: kenacid āhārādyupastambhena sudhāritām anye ’pi dhārayeyuḥ. caurādibhyo vihitarakṣatvena sugopāyitāṃ gopāyeyuḥ. kāyikamalādyapanayanena sukelāyitāṃ kelāyeyuḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.128.a), “keep well tended” means keep her well nourished. They would nourish her so that she is well nourished. “Protect” means preserve. “Take care” means keep free of impure things. The word “care” is based on her (possibly) catching a disease. RK: sudhṛtāṃ dhārayeyur iti supuṣṭāṃ puṣṭīyuḥ| tathā puṣṭīyuḥ yathā supuṣṭā syāt| gopāyanaṃ rakṣaṇam| kelāyanaṃ śodhanaṃ vyādhiharaṇāt kelāśabdaḥ. They would think, ‘May she come across no painful feelings or painful encounters.According to Ratnākara (F.128.a), “painful encounters” such as an itch and so on means the mental factor of contact born from the coming together of object, organ, and consciousness. It is “painful” in the sense of causing pain. RK: kaṇḍvādiduḥkho vā sparśa iti| viṣayendriya­vijñāna­sannipātajaś caitasikaḥ sparśaḥ| sa ca duḥkhahetur duḥkha. May she physically come into contact with nothing undesirable, whether it be through the eyes, ears, nose, tongue, body, or mind; because of the bodily wind, bile, phlegm, or a mixture of these; because of snake bites, mosquitos, crawling creatures, humans, or nonhumans; or because of calamities or misfortunes.’According to Hari (530), “due to calamities” (āpāta) means due to lightning strikes and so on. “Due to misfortunes” (utpāta) means due to pestilent illness and so on. “Nothing undesirable” means the occurrence of an undesired thing. Hari: āpātato vidyudādipātāt. utpātataḥ sarvopasargikarogataḥ. aniṣṭanipāto ’niṣṭavastusambhavaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.128.a), “calamities” means on the part of nonhuman actors. “Misfortunes” refers to lightning and so on. RK: āpāto ’manuṣyāṇām| utpāto vidyudādiḥ. In this way, those sons would attend to their mother with all the sources of happiness, take care of her, keep her in mind, and protect her.According to Ratnākara (F.128.a), “keep her in mind” in the sense of being interested in her well-being. RK: mamakāreṇa mamāyeyuḥ. They would think, ‘This is the mother who gave birth to us. She is the one who undertook difficult noble deeds for us, gave us life, and showed us the world.’

“In just the same way, Subhūti, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas closely attend to this transcendent state of wisdom. Also, it is through the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas’ miraculous power, blessings, and close attentiveness that everyone else writes out this transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, and recites it.According to Ratnākara (F.128.a), the “tathāgata” here refers to the Buddha Śākyamuni. “Furthermore, all those” (in the next sentence) refers to not only Śākyamuni (but to others as well). RK: tathāgatasye ti śākyamuneḥ| yepī ti na kevalaṃ śākyamuniḥ. Furthermore, all those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas—who presently reside, stay, and spend time in other world systems, out of their compassion for those worlds, in order to bring about the benefit, welfare, and happiness of a great multitude of people‍—feel compassion toward gods, humans, and all sentient beings.According to Ratnākara (F.128.a), “reside” means be there; “stay” means remain there; and “live their lives” means remain as long as they live. RK: tiṣṭhantī ti santi| dhriyanta iti avatiṣṭhante| yāpayantī ti yāvadāyur avatiṣṭhante. And as a result of their compassion, they closely attend to this transcendent state of wisdom and are ardently concerned about it, thinking, ‘What can keep the transcendent state of wisdom surviving a long time? What can keep the transcendent state of wisdom entirely free of deterioration? What can prevent the pernicious Māra or his demon cohort from presenting obstacles to those discussing this transcendent state of wisdom, writing it out, and training in it?’ In this way, Subhūti, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas take care of this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, and protect it.

“Why do they do that? It is because this transcendent state of wisdom is the mother who gives birth to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. Having given birth to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, she is the one who made them see the state of complete knowing and showed them the world.According to Ratnākara (F.128.a), she is “the one who gives birth to” the state of complete knowing and “who makes them see it,” i.e., leads them to it. RK: janayitrī ti| asyāḥ sarvajñatāyāḥ| darśayitrī ti prāpikā. Subhūti, she is the one who gives birth to the knowing of someone who has complete knowing and who makes the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas see that world. Thus, she shows them the world, in the sense that their state of complete knowing is born from her.According to Ratnākara (F.128.a), “thus” means just as she gives birth to them. RK: evam iti janakatvenaiva.

“All the past worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, realize unsurpassed awakening as a result of this very transcendent state of wisdom. All the future worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, will realize unsurpassed awakening as a result of this very transcendent state of wisdom too. All those present worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas‍—who presently reside, stay, and spend time in the immeasurable and innumerable world systems of the ten directions, out of their compassion for those worlds, in order to bring about the benefit, welfare, and happiness of a great multitude of people‍—feel compassion toward gods, humans, and all sentient beings. And it is from the perspective of their compassion that they have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening; they too have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening as a result of this very transcendent state of wisdom. And I, the present worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata, have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening as a result of this very transcendent state of wisdom too.
“Subhūti, in this way, she is the one who gives birth to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas and shows them the world.”

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “The Blessed One has said that she is the one who shows the world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. Blessed One, how is she the one who shows the world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas? And, Blessed One, what do the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas call this world?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, the Tathāgata has called the world the five constituents underlying the clinging to existence. What are the five? Subhūti, the Tathāgata has called the world these five constituents underlying the clinging to existence, namely, physical forms, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.”
