Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, how could the transcendent state of wisdom show the five constituents to the tathāgatas? Blessed One, what does the transcendent state of wisdom show?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom shows the tathāgatas the world as the five constituents, since they neither perish nor are destroyed.This passage involves a play on the Sanskrit word loka (which can refer to either the world or the people in it) and its etymological meaning as that which perishes (lujyate). When the Tathāgata says here that the five constituents (which constitute the world and people) “neither perish nor are destroyed,” he is suggesting that they are not merely momentary and impermanent continua as the disciple teachings suggest, but rather are empty of inherent nature as the Mahāyāna explains. According to Hari (538), the meaning of this phrase “neither perish nor are destroyed” is that the five constituents, respectively, are not breakable because they are momentary and do not disappear due to the impermanence of their continua (of causes and effects). Hari: na lujyante na pralujyanta iti kṣaṇika­prabandhānityatābhyāṃ yathākramaṃ na naśyanti na pranaśyantīty arthaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.128.b), the word loka is explained as deriving from the verbal root √luj, in the sense of perishing. (Generally speaking) the “worlds” (or people) “perish,” i.e., due to momentariness, or are “destroyed,” i.e., due to their continuum ceasing. In the use of the word loka (in the plural here), what is etymologized (as perishing) goes against the Sanskrit agentive yuc affix rule. RK: loka ity ākhyātā iti lujo vināśe| lujyante pralujyante kṣaṇikatvāt santānanirodhāc ceti lokāḥ| karttur yuc| nairukto varṇavyatyayaḥ. How could she show that they neither perish nor are destroyed?According to Ratnākara (F.128.b), he asks how the worlds/people are shown by her. The basic idea is that since the “world” (loka) literally means perishing, what is the superiority of the transcendent state of wisdom? The implication here is that the transcendent state of wisdom must be superior to it, since, other than its perishable quality, the world is not something shown. Someone might nevertheless object that it is a contradiction to say that the world (i.e., that which perishes) does not perish or get destroyed. But there is no contradiction. In this context, the logical connection (of that phrase) is to that transcendent state of wisdom (i.e., the grammatical subject) that shows the world, i.e., the five constituents that do not perish or get destroyed. (Note: unlike Haribhadra, Ratnākara suggests that the sentence here is a reference to the truly established (pariniṣpanna) nature of the five constituents from the Yogācāra standpoint.) RK: kathaṃ loka iti darśitā iti| yadi luñjanta iti lokāḥ | tadā ’syāḥ ko ’tiśaya iti bhāvaḥ| viśvādarśitam iti lujyatvād anyat| yato ’tiśayaḥ syād ity arthaḥ| na lujyanta ityādi| nanu tathāpi vyāhatam etat na lujyante na pralujyante tasmāl loka iti? nāsti vyāghātaḥ| yasyāyam atra sambandhaḥ| loka iti pañcaskandhāḥ| te bhagavatyā prajñāpāra[mi]tayā na lujyante na pralujyanta iti darśitāḥ. Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is the one who shows the world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas in this way, due to the fact, Subhūti, that the five constituents have the nature of emptiness‍—inasmuch as they lack an inherent nature‍—and that emptiness neither perishes nor is destroyed.According to Haribhadra (538), in ultimate reality, due to their lack of an inherent nature, the two natures of being momentary and having impermanent continua‍—i.e., the two absences of the natures of (conditioned) things and dharmas (according to disciples)‍—do not exist as the emptiness and so on of the constituents. But in conventional reality, they do exist. This is the idea here. Hari: tattvato ’svabhāvatvāt skandhānāṃ śūnyatāditvena vastudharmasvabhāvābhāve kṣaṇikaprabandhānityate na bhavataḥ. saṃvṛtyā tu sta iti bhāvaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.128.b), he answers the rhetorical question by speaking of emptiness and so on. In the next sentence, freedom from signs and so on is the same as emptiness. RK: śūnyate tyādinottaram| yathā śūnyatā evam animittādaya ity āha na cetyādinā. Also, Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is the one who shows the world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas in this way, inasmuch as the freedom from signs, the freedom from inclinations, the freedom from conditionings, the freedom from arisings, the freedom from things, and the ultimate realm of things do not perish and are not destroyed.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that sentient beings are immeasurable and sentient beings are innumerable.According to Haribhadra (538), to explain the knowing of all beings and their mental ruminations (in the next two paragraphs), he says “furthermore” and so on. Hari: sarva­sattva­citta­caritajñānaṃ nirdiśann āha : punar aparam ityādi. And how does the Tathāgata discern accurately that sentient beings are immeasurable and that sentient beings are innumerable? Subhūti, since sentient beings have no inherent nature, he discerns accurately that sentient beings are immeasurable and that sentient beings are innumerable. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of just such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that sentient beings are immeasurable and that sentient beings are innumerable.

“Beyond that, Subhūti, the Tathāgata discerns the mental ruminations of the immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings to be nothing but the unreal nature of sentient beings.According to Ratnākara (F.128.b–29.a), the (Blessed One’s) knowledge of the mental ruminations of innumerable sentient beings is explained, and it is not explained as his knowing their quality of innumerability or destructibility. Rather, he says “furthermore,” meaning after having previously taught the knowledge of sentient beings’ immeasurability, then he explains the knowledge of their mental ruminations. In that explanation, he asks how could there be knowledge of the immeasurability of sentient beings? (The answer is) because, insofar as they lack an inherent nature, there is no measure and no number to them. Then he asks how could there be knowledge of their mental ruminations? (The answer is) because, insofar as beings are nonexistent, their mental ruminations also do not exist. RK: sattvānāṃ cittacaryāsv ity anenāprameyāṇāṃ sattvānāṃ cittacaritajñānaṃ nirdiṣṭam| tac ca na vināśatvāprameyatājñānena| tataḥ punaraparam ityādinā sattvāprameyatājñānam uktvā yo pī tyādinā taccittacaritajñānam āha| tatra kathaṃ sattvānām aprameyatājñānam. yatas teṣāṃ niḥsvabhāvatayā na pramāṇaṃ na saṃkhyā| kathaṃ tac cittacaritānāṃ jñānam ? sattvāsattayā teṣām apy asattvāt. According to Hari (539), the phrase “nothing but (eva) the unreal nature of sentient beings” and so on refers to his full comprehension of sentient beings as having an innumerable and so on nature because their inherent nature is like an illusion. Hari: sattvāsvabhāvataye tyādi. sattvānāṃ māyopamasvabhāvatvād aprameyādirūpeṇa parijñānaṃ. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately the immeasurable and innumerable mental ruminations of the immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings. Subhūti, in this way, the transcendent state of wisdom is the one who shows the world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas.According to Ratnākara (F.129.a), she is the “one who shows the world” because beings are also the world (i.e., the Sanskrit word loka refers to both the world and beings). RK: lokasya sandarśayitrī ti sattvānām api lokatvāt.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings’ mindsets that are undistracted are undistracted mindsets.The word saṃkṣipta is translated “undistracted” here to help contrast it with the opposite term vikṣipta (“distracted”) in the following paragraph. According to Ratnākara (F.129.a), distraction refers to (the mindset) wandering outward. RK: bahirgatir vikṣepaḥ. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings’ undistracted mindsets are undistracted mindsets? Subhūti, he discerns accurately their undistractedness from dissipation, and dissipation being due to nondissipation.According to Ratnākara (F.129.a), “undistractedness” means resting in the mindset’s original nature, and that itself is nothing but the “dissipation” of that mindset. Also, that is a “nondissipation” because the essential nature of a mindset is inexhaustible. Therefore, he “discerns accurately their undistractedness from dissipation, and dissipation being due to nondissipation.” RK: saṃkṣepo hi cittasya prakṛtau sthānam| tac cāsya kṣaya eva| sa cākṣayaś cittaprakṛter akṣayatvāt| tasmāt sa tathāgataḥ saṃkṣepaṃ kṣayataḥ kṣayaṃ cākṣayato yathābhūtam prajānāti. According to Hari (540), he discerns the mindset engaged in the true nature through having that as a meditative focus, at the conventional level, to be an “undistractedness based in dissipation,” i.e., based in destruction. He also “discerns the dissipation,” i.e., destruction, at the ultimate level, “in accordance with reality” as “nondissipation,” i.e., a nondestruction because the (sentient being) possessing the (quality of) dissipation does not exist. Hari: sa saṃkṣepaṃ kṣayataḥ kṣayaṃ cākṣayata iti tadālambanena dharmatāyāṃ praviṣṭaṃ cittaṃ sa saṃkṣepaṃ kṣayato vināśataḥ saṃvṛtyā jānāti. kṣayam api vināśaṃ kṣayiṇo ’sattvāt paramārthato ’kṣayam avināśaṃ yathābhūtam prajānāti. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings’ mindsets that are undistracted are undistracted mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings’ mindsets that are distracted are distracted mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings’ distracted mindsets are distracted mindsets? Subhūti, he discerns accurately that those mindsets are distracted from the true nature of things but they are free of characteristics, unwaning, utterly unwaning, and undistracted.According to Hari (541), “the true nature of things” here means their not arising from the ultimate realm of things. At the conventional level, they are “distracted mindsets,” i.e., outwardly engaged due to inattentiveness toward that nature. But at the ultimate level, he “discerns” them, “in accordance with reality” to be “free of characteristics,” i.e., lacking any inherent nature; “unwaning” due to the impermanence of the moments; “utterly unwaning” due to the cessation of the continuum; and “undistracted” due to the impossibility of wandering outwardly. Hari: dharmatāta iti dharmadhātor anutpādataḥ. tadamanasikāreṇa bahiḥ pravṛttāni cittāni vikṣiptāni saṃvṛtyā. paramārthataḥ punar alakṣaṇāni svabhāvavirahitāni. kṣaṇānityatvenākṣīṇāni. prabandhoparamād avikṣīṇāni, bahirgamanāsambhavād avikṣiptānī ti yathābhūtam prajānāti. According to Ratnākara (F.129.a), “free of characteristics” means empty of their own inherent characteristics. RK: alakṣaṇāni hī ti| yasmāt svalakṣaṇaiḥ śūnyānītyarthaḥ. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings’ mindsets that are distracted are distracted mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings’ mindsets that are immeasurable and unwaning are mindsets that are immeasurable and unwaning. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings’ mindsets that are immeasurable and unwaning are immeasurable and unwaning mindsets? Subhūti, the mindset inhabited by the Tathāgata is free of ceasing, free of arising, free of dwelling, independent, unequaled, immeasurable, and incalculable.According to Hari (541), “inhabited” means the mindset inhabited until the end of cyclic existence out of compassion. It is “free of ceasing, free of arising, and free of dwelling” because these three characteristics of conditioned things are not logically possible. Hence, since it cannot have the nature of possessing a property, it is “independent.” Since it cannot be measured, it is “immeasurable.” It is indestructible, since, like the ultimate realm of things, it cannot be destroyed. Hari: adhiṣṭhitam iti mahākaruṇayāsaṃsāram adhiṣṭhitaṃ taccittaṃ. yathāyogaṃ tri­vidha­saṃskṛta­lakṣaṇāsambhavād anirodham anutpādam asthitam. ato dharmirūpatvāyogād anāśrayaṃ. pramātum aśakyatvenāprameyaṃ. dharmadhātuvad vināśānupapatter akṣayaṃ bhavati tathāgatasya. Hence, he discerns accurately that such a mindset is unwaning and immeasurable, in the same way that space is unwaning and immeasurable.According to Hari (541), mentally he “discerns, in accordance with reality,” the immeasurable and indestructible nature of all sentient beings’ mindsets to be like “space,” because they appear in his meditative concentration (as if) on the surface of the mirror. Hari: yenaiva cittenākāśam ivāprameyākṣayatayā sarvasattvānāṃ cittāprameyākṣayatāṃ svasamādhidarpaṇatale pratibhāsanād yathābhūtam prajānāti. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings’ mindsets that are immeasurable and unwaning are immeasurable and unwaning mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets that are emotionally defiled are emotionally defiled mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ emotionally defiled mindsets are emotionally defiled mindsets? Subhūti, those mindsets unfixed by convention are defiled by a lack of emotional defilement.According to Hari (541), being merely due to a cognitive error, the emotional defilements are characterized as emotional defilements, actions, and births. (Those mindsets) are “defiled,” i.e., infected by force of their opposite, “by a lack of emotional defilement” at the ultimate level. “Not fixed by convention” means their activities are unrestricted. Hari: asaṃkleśasaṃkliṣṭānī ti bhrāntimātrāstitvāt. kleśa­karma­janmalakṣaṇaiḥ saṃkleśais tattvato ’saṃkleśair viparyāsabalāt saṃkliṣṭāny upahatāni. asaṃketānīti apratiniyatavṛttīni. According to Ratnākara (F.129.b), “defilements,” i.e., afflictions, means polluted by the root and subsidiary emotional defilements. They are “defiled by a lack of emotional defilement,” meaning emotional defilement is absent. The mindsets are “not fixed by convention”‍—i.e., the verbal root √kit here is in the sense of dwelling‍—meaning that the defilements do not dwell in the mindsets’ true nature at all, because they are adventitious. RK: asaṃkleśasaṃkliṣṭānī ti saṃkliṣṭiḥ saṃkleśaḥ kleśopakleśair malinīkaraṇam| na saṃkleśo ’saṃkleśaḥ | tena saṃkliṣṭāni| yato ’saṃketāni| kita nivāse| cittadharmatayā na samyak sthānaṃ teṣām āgantukatvāt. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets that are emotionally defiled are emotionally defiled mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets that are emotionally undefiled are emotionally undefiled mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ emotionally undefiled mindsets are emotionally undefiled mindsets? Subhūti, those mindsets are luminously clear by nature.According to Ratnākara (F.129.b), the true nature of the mindsets that are “luminously clear” purely “by nature” are “undefiled,” i.e., permanently free of stains. RK: prakṛtī tyādi| cittadharmatā hi prakṛtya iva prabhāsvarā nirmalā nityam asaṃkliṣṭe ti. According to Hari (542), their mindsets are “luminously clear by nature”‍—meaning their innate, inherent nature in itself is completely pure‍—(a) because even though the mindsets as they are at the stage of an ordinary person have arisen from cognitive error and are thus impure, they lack any inherent nature, and (b) because one can encounter the antidote by wholeheartedly understanding, through valid cognition, the nature of forms endowed with the qualities of nonarising and so on, and hence, the attachment (rāga) and so on clouding those mindsets can be removed irreversibly. Hari: prakṛtiprabhāsvarāṇī ti pṛthagjanāvasthāyām aviśuddha­bhrānti­kāraṇa­nirjātatvena tathābhūtāny api cittāni naiḥsvābhāvyāt, pramāṇopapannānutpādādi­rūpātmāvabodha­parāyaṇatvena pratipakṣodayād apy anivartyānīti śakyāpaneyarāgādisahāyatvāt prabhāsvarāṇi pariśuddha­nijasvabhāva­mātrāṇi. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets that are emotionally undefiled are emotionally undefiled mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that are stuck are stuck mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that are stuck are stuck mindsets? Subhūti, those mindsets are stuck by not sticking.According to Hari (543), “stuck”‍—i.e., fixated due to attachment and so on‍—“in the unstuck,” i.e., in a place impossible to remain, such as in enjoying attainments and so on. Hari: anālayalīnānī ty asthānārhe ’nālaye samāpattyāsvādanādau rāgādihetutvena līnāny abhiniviṣṭāni. According to Ratnākara (F.129.b), “the unstuck” means not clinging. It means that they are “stuck by not sticking,” i.e., by not clinging, because a mindset is stuck, but its essential nature is not, insofar as a mindset’s essential nature is neither “me” nor “mine.” RK: anālayalīnānī ti| anālayo ’layanam| alayanenaiva līnāni| cittaṃ hi līnaṃ na cittaprakṛtiḥ| na hi cittaprakṛtir na mahyaṃ na meti vā. Note: The translation of the Sanskrit compound follows the Tibetan and Hari’s analysis of a locative relation, rather than Ratnākara’s suggestion of an instrumental relation. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that are stuck are stuck mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets that are uplifted are uplifted mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that the uplifted mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings are uplifted mindsets? Subhūti, those ungraspable mindsets cannot be uplifted.According to Hari (543), in this context it is said that excited or agitatedly doubtful mindsets are handled by paying attention to emotionally moving things (like impermanence and death) that cause them to become tranquil (śamatha). Stuck or sluggishly doubtful mindsets are handled by paying attention to delightful things that cause them to become uplifted. Mindsets captured equally (by both excitement and sluggishness) are handled by paying attention to the effortlessness that causes equanimity. Thus, the “uplifted mindsets” are caused by freedom from attachment (rāga) and so on. But the “ungraspable”‍—i.e., something impossible to grasp (or be handled)‍—“cannot be uplifted” further. Hari: agrāhyāṇi subhūte tāni cittāni na pragrahītavyānī ti tatra: uddhatam auddhatyābhiśaṅki vā cittaṃ saṃvejanīya­vastu­manaskāreṇa śamathanimittena, līnaṃ layābhiśaṅki vā cittam abhipramodanīya­vastu­manaskāreṇa pragrahanimittena, samaprāptaṃ cittam anābhoga­manaskāreṇopekṣā­nimittena ca gṛhītam. ity evaṃ virāgādihetutvena pragṛhītāni cittāni. punar agrahaṇārhatvenāgrāhyāṇi bhūyo na pragṛhītavyāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that are uplifted are uplifted mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings with worldly influences are mindsets with worldly influences. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings with worldly influences are mindsets with worldly influences? Subhūti, he discerns those mindsets lacking inherent nature to be fabrications of something unreal.According to Hari (544), mindsets with worldly influences have innate natures that do not exist, because they are reversible due to encountering the antidote, and hence they are the same as something unreal. Hari: sāsravāṇi cittāni pratipakṣodayān nivartyatvenāvidya­mānanija­svabhāvāni, tataś cāsattātulyāni. According to Ratnākara (F.130.a), “inherent nature” (svabhāva) means the thing existing (bhāva) only in itself (sva, ātma). Since that is lacking, they are “lacking inherent nature.” The fabricators of the unreal are worldly influences, in the sense that a mindset is proliferating due to them. Why do mindsets have worldly influences on account of their connection with those? Because they are “fabrications of something unreal,” in that they create the realms‍—i.e., desire, subtle form, and formless realms‍—that are not real. RK: ātmany eva bhāvaḥ svabhāvaḥ| tadabhāvād asvabhāvāni| visaraty ebhiś cittam ity āsravā asatsaṃkalpāḥ| tadyogāt sāsravāṇi| kutaḥ? yato ’satsaṃkalpāni| asatāṃ kāma­rūpārūpyabhavānāṃ kalpanāt. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that have worldly influences are mindsets with worldly influences.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that do not have worldly influences are mindsets free of worldly influences. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that do not have worldly influences are mindsets free of worldly influences? Subhūti, he discerns those mindsets in the state of absence to be naturally effortless.According to Hari (544), the mindsets free of worldly influences meet their end in the absence of the emotional defilements to be abandoned through seeing and cultivation (on the bodhisattva path), and hence they are “effortless,” in that they lead the mindstream to become completely purified automatically. Hari: abhāvagatikāni subhūte tāni cittāny anābhogānī ti anāsravāṇi cittāni darśana­bhāvanāheya­kleśānām abhāvaparya­vasānāni, tataś ca svarasena pariśuddha­saṃtāna­pravartanād anābhogāni. According to Ratnākara (F.130.a), “in the state of absence” (is an adjectival compound meaning that these mindsets) have “absence,” i.e., emptiness, as their “state.” Thus, “effortless” means that the mindsets are free of worldly influences due to proliferating in the desire, subtle form, and formless realms. RK: abhāvagatikānī tyādi| abhāvaḥ śūnyatā| sā gatir eṣām iti tathoktāni| tasmād anābhogāni kāmarūpārūpyabhaveṣu visaraṇāt, tato ’nāsravāṇīty arthaḥ. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings free of worldly influences are mindsets free of worldly influences.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that have attachment are mindsets with attachment.The next six paragraphs, including this one, concern the mindsets with and without the three poisons, namely, attachment, aversion, and confusion. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that have attachment are mindsets with attachment? Subhūti, having attachment is not the accurate state of a mindset, and a mindset’s accurate state is not having attachment. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets with attachment are mindsets with attachment.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that are free of attachment are mindsets free of attachment. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that are free of attachment are mindsets free of attachment? Subhūti, the voidness of a mindset is not a mindset’s state of attachment, and the accurate state of a mindset free of attachment is not a mindset that has attachment.According to Ratnākara (F.130.a), a mindset has attachment; the essential nature of a mindset does not. This means that the mindset having attachment is absolutely free of attachment. The “voidness” means its emptiness. That it does “not have attachment” means it is free of attachment. RK: cittasya sarāgatā na cittaprakṛteḥ| tasmād vi naiva rāgaṃ sarāgāṇīty arthaḥ| yaś cittasya vigama ityādi| vigamaḥ śūnyatā| na sā sarāgate ti saiva vītarāgatety arthaḥ. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings that are free of attachment are mindsets free of attachment.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the aggressive mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings are aggressive mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that the aggressive mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings are aggressive mindsets? Subhūti, the aggressiveness of a mindset is not the accurate state of a mindset, and the accurate state of a mindset is not the aggressiveness of a mindset. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that the aggressive mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings are aggressive mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets free of aggression are mindsets free of aggression. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets free of aggression are mindsets free of aggression? Subhūti, a mindset’s voidness is not a mindset’s freedom from aggressiveness, and the accurate state of a mindset free of aggression is not the aggressiveness of a mindset. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets free of aggression are mindsets free of aggression.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets with confusion are mindsets with confusion. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets with confusion are mindsets with confusion? Subhūti, a mindset with confusion is not the accurate state, and the accurate state of a mindset is not a mindset with confusion. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets with confusion are mindsets with confusion.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets free of confusion are mindsets free of confusion. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets free of confusion are mindsets free of confusion? Subhūti, the voidness of a mindset is not a mindset with confusion, and the accurate state of a mindset free of confusion is not a mindset with confusion. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets free of confusion are mindsets free of confusion.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ subtle mindsets are subtle mindsets.The next five paragraphs, including this one, concern mindsets connected with the three realms‍—desire, subtle form, and formless‍—i.e., mindsets that are subtle or gross, expansive or unexpansive, or beyond measure. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ subtle mindsets are subtle mindsets? Subhūti, mindsets connected with not increasing belong to not increasing.According to Hari (547), since, in reality, their connection with a cause is empty, they do not arise. Hence, those mindsets are “connected with not increasing” due to the absence of manifestation. The mindsets “belong to not increasing” because they are not bound for the worlds of desire and so on. Because they are not mentally perceived, “that is how” he discerns them as “subtle.” Hari: asamutthānayogāni subhūte tāni cittāny asamutthāna paryāpannānī ti tattvato ’nutpatteḥ kāraṇa­sambandhaśūnyatvenāsamutthānayogāni. prādurbhāvavirahāt. kāmādidhātāv apratibaddhatvāt tāni cittāny asamutthānaparyāpannāni. evam anupalambhād avipulāni. According to Ratnākara (F.130.b), because their connection with “increasing”‍—i.e., becoming gross‍—is refuted, the mindsets are connected with not increasing. Because their overextension is refuted, they “belong to not increasing” based on the true nature of things. Therefore, they are subtle. RK: asamutthāne tyādi samutthānaṃ vistāraḥ| tad yoga pratiṣedhād asamutthānayogāni| atiprasārapratiṣedhād asamutthāna­paryāpannāni dharmatayā| tasmād avipulāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ subtle mindsets are subtle mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ gross mindsets are gross mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ gross mindsets are gross mindsets? Subhūti, those mindsets do not shrink, do not grow, and do not disappear, because those mindsets absolutely are not the voidness.According to Hari (547), since they do not cease, they “do not shrink.” Since they do not arise, they “do not grow.” Hence, since they do not go anywhere and are “not void,” they “do not disappear.” Since their inherent nature is the ultimate realm of things, “that is how” he discerns “gross mindsets.” Hari: avināśābhāvān na hīyante, utpādābhāvān na vivardhante, ata eva kvacidgamanābhāvenā­vigamatvān na vigacchanty, evaṃ dharmadhātusvābhāvyād vipulāni cittānī ti. Note: The words translated “voidness” (vigama) and “disappear” (vigacchanti) are both derivations of the Sanskrit verb vi-√gam, and hence a slight play on words here. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ gross mindsets are gross mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets that are unexpansive mindsets are unexpansive mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that the unexpansive mindsets of immeasurable sentient beings are unexpansive mindsets? Subhūti, those mindsets do not come from anywhere, do not go anywhere, and are not based anywhere.According to Hari (548), they “do not come from anywhere” because there is no coming to them from a past time due to the refutation of a real effect inherent in the cause (satkārya). They “do not go anywhere” because it is impossible to go to a future time due to disappearing entirely. They are not based anywhere because they lack a singular or plural nature even in the present. Hence, they are “unexpansive.” Hari: satkārya­pratiṣedhenātītād adhvanas tadāgamanavaikalyād anāgatikāni cittāni. sarvātmanā vināśād anāgate ’pi kāle gamanānupapatter agatikāni. pratyutpanne ’py ekānekasvabhāvavaidhuryād aparyāpannāni. evam amahadgatāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ unexpansive mindsets are unexpansive mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ expansive mindsets are expansive mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ expansive mindsets are expansive mindsets? Subhūti, those mindsets are the same in terms of their sameness and the same in terms of their inherent nature.According to Hari (548), in reality, they are “the same in terms of their sameness” because, in having the same lack of nature of a self and so on, they do not arise. But at the conventional level, they are “the same in terms of” their illusory “inherent nature” because they appear with a nature that bears all the qualities. Thus, they are “expansive.” Hari: tattvato ’nutpādarūpatvād ātmādiniḥsvabhāvatulyatvena samatāsamāni cittāni. tathyasaṃvṛtyā tu sarvaguṇāvāhakarūpeṇa pratibhāsanān māyāsvabhāvasamāni. evaṃ mahadgatāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ expansive mindsets are expansive mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets beyond measure are mindsets beyond measure. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets beyond measure are mindsets beyond measure? Subhūti, those mindsets are beyond measure since they have no basis.According to Hari (549), since they “have no basis”‍—i.e., their determinate basis is not found‍—they are “beyond measure.” Hari: aniśrayatvād iti na hi pratiniyatas teṣām āśrayo vidyata ity apramāṇāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets beyond measure are mindsets beyond measure.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets with manifestations are mindsets with manifestations.The next four paragraphs, including this one, concern mindsets connected with the defining characteristics of material and mental substances. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets with manifestations are mindsets with manifestations? Subhūti, the inherent nature of mindsets is that they have similar perceptions.According to Hari (549), they have “similar perceptions” because of being the illusion-like self-knowing. Absolutely all knowings are forms of similar perceptions. “The inherent nature of mindsets,” i.e., their nature of accumulating latent tendencies, is due to their having accumulated virtuous and unvirtous latent tendencies. This is “how” he discerns “mindsets with manifestations.” (Note: Hari is using here the well-known creative etymology that momentary mindsets (citta) are something accumulated (cita) through virtue and nonvirtue). Hari: samadarśanāni subhūte tāni cittāni cittasvabhāvānī ti māyopamātmasaṃvedanatayā samadarśanāni. tulyopalambharūpāṇi sarvāṇy eva jñānāni kuśalākuśalavāsanābhiś citatvāc cittasvabhāvāni saṃcitavāsanārūpāṇi. evaṃ sanidarśanāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets with manifestations are mindsets with manifestations.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets without manifestations are mindsets without manifestations. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets without manifestations are mindsets without manifestations? Subhūti, since a mindset has no characteristics and is devoid of any object, it is imperceptible and does not appear either to the first three types of eyes or to all types of eyes.According to Hari (549–50), “has no characteristics” means that, at the ultimate level, it lacks the nature of existing. “Devoid of any object” means that it is impossible for there to be a connection between (a perceptual) cause and effect with their natures, respectively, being the past and the future. For these reasons, a “mindset is imperceptible and does not appear to the three types of eyes or to all” of the five types of “eyes,” namely the flesh, (divine, wisdom, dharma, and buddha eyes). Hari: tattvato vartamāna­svarūpa­viraheṇālakṣaṇatvād atītānāgata­rūpābhyāṃ saha yathākramaṃ kāryakāraṇa­sambandhānupapattyā ’rthaviviktatvāt trayāṇāṃ māṃsādi cakṣuṣāṃ sarveṣāṃ vā pāñcānām aviṣayatvenānavabhāsagatam adṛśyaṃ cittaṃ. evam anidarśanāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets without manifestations are mindsets without manifestations.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets with resistance are mindsets with resistance. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets with resistance are mindsets with resistance? Subhūti, having fabricated the unreal, those mindsets are empty and contingent upon their object.According to Hari (552), “having fabricated” means having superimposed as having the nature of an arising and so on. “The unreal” means not existing. The “mindsets” are “empty”‍—i.e., lacking an inherent nature‍—and, at the conventional level, “contingent upon,” i.e., dependent upon, their “object.” Hari: asatā vidyamānenotpādādirūpeṇa saṃkalpitāny adhyāropitāni cittāni śūnyāny asvabhāvāny ālambanavaśikāni saṃvṛtyālambana­paratantrāṇi. evaṃ pramāṇabādhitatvāt sapratighāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets with resistance are mindsets with resistance.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets without resistance are mindsets without resistance. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets without resistance are mindsets without resistance? Subhūti, those mindsets free of duality are arisen in the unarisen.According to Hari (552), the mindsets are “free of duality” since they lack arising or nonarising. They are “arisen”‍—i.e., existing at the conventional level‍—“in the unarisen,” i.e., absolutely not existing at the ultimate level. Due to the impossibility of any means of valid cognition, “that is how” he discerns the mindsets “without resistance.” Hari: utpādānutpāda­rahitatvenādvayabhūtāni. tattvato ’bhavanam eva saṃvṛtyā bhavanam ity abhūtasambhūtāni. evaṃ pramāṇopapannatvād apratighāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ mindsets without resistance are mindsets without resistance.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ inferior mindsets are inferior mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ inferior mindsets are inferior mindsets? Subhūti, in that mindset that is in accordance with reality, there is no developing the conceit of an inferior mindset.According to Hari (552–53), due to habitual fixation on a self and so on, the mindset that is inferior, i.e., has reached a lesser state. “In accordance with reality” means being aware of the lack of identity. “Not developing conceit” means the mental perception with beliefs about the perishable collection and so on. Therefore, at the conventional level, “that is how” he discerns it as “inferior.” Hari: ātmādyabhiniveśena sottarasya nyūnāvasthāṃ prāptasya cittasya yā yathābhūtatā nairātmyasaṃvedanatā na tatrāsti manyamānatā satkāyādi­dṛṣṭyupalambhatā. tasmād evaṃ saṃvṛtyā sottarāṇi. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ inferior mindsets are inferior mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ superior mindsets are superior mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ superior mindsets are superior mindsets? Because, Subhūti, a mindset is not mentally perceived even as an atom, and, as a consequence, those mindsets are free from dualistic proliferations.According to Hari (553), at the ultimate level a mindset (cittam) is not “even” a mere “atom.” Hence, its substantial nature is not “mentally perceived.” Therefore, those mindsets are “free from proliferations” because they are lacking in any cause for material forms and so on due to their naturally pure nature at the level of an ordinary person, just like at the level of buddhahood. “This is how” he discerns them at the ultimate level as “superior.” Hari: yasmāt paramārthato ’ṇumātram api cittaṃ vastusvarūpaṃ nopalabdhaṃ tasmād buddhatvāvasthāyām iva pṛthagjanāvasthāyāṃ prakṛti­vaiyavadānika­svabhāvena sarva­rūpādi­nimittāpagamān niḥprapañcāni. evaṃ tattvato niruttarāṇi cittāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ superior mindsets are superior mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ unconcentrated mindsets are unconcentrated mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ unconcentrated mindsets are unconcentrated mindsets? Subhūti, similar to the dissimilar, those mindsets get carried away with the dissimilar, and hence are unconcentrated.According to Hari (553), since the mindsets are different from their faults of arising and so on, they are “similar” to their “dissimilar” object to be grasped. Insofar as the active mindsets are the subjects grasping that (dissimilar object), they are “unconcentrated,” because they have not accomplished the meditative concentration out of their distraction. “This is how” he discerns them as “unconcentrated.” Hari: utpādādidoṣavaiṣamyād asamena grāhyeṇa samāni. tadgrāhakatvena pravṛttāni cittāni vikṣepād akṛtasamādhānatvenāsamavahitāni. evaṃ asamāhitāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ unconcentrated mindsets are unconcentrated mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ concentrated mindsets are concentrated mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ concentrated mindsets are concentrated mindsets? Subhūti, similar to the similar, those mindsets get carried toward the similar, and hence those concentrated mindsets are similar to space.According to Hari (553), since no faults can be (ultimately) different, the mindsets are “similar”‍—in the sense of being equal to the arising and so on‍—“to the similar,” i.e., the ultimate realm of things. They are “carried toward the similar” because they have counteracted distraction. At the ultimate level, they are “similar to space,” in that their inherent nature cannot be found. “This is how” he discerns them as “concentrated.” Hari: sarvadoṣavaiṣamyānupapatteḥ samo dharmadhātus, tena sahānutpādādinā tulyatvāt samāni. vikṣepāt kṛtapratīkāratvena samavahitāni. tattvato ’vidyamāna­svabhāvatvenākāśasamāni. evaṃ samāhitāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ concentrated mindsets are concentrated mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ unliberated mindsets are unliberated mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ unliberated mindsets are unliberated mindsets? Subhūti, in being naturally liberated, those mindsets have the inherent nature of not existing.According to Hari (553), “mindsets are naturally liberated” because, according to logic, there is no birth. They have the “inherent nature of not existing” because an existent nature cannot be found. Hence, they are “unliberated mindsets” because there is no liberation caused by removing bonds. Hari: nyāyānuyāyijanma­rahitatvāt svabhāvavimuktāni cittāni avidya­mānasattārūpatvād abhāvasvabhāvāni. tataś ca bandhāpanayana­pūrvakamokṣābhāvād avimuktāni cittāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ unliberated mindsets are unliberated mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ liberated mindsets are liberated mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ liberated mindsets are liberated mindsets? Subhūti, the Tathāgata simply does not mentally perceive a past, present, or future mindset, because a mindset does not exist.According to Hari (553), a mindset “does not exist” insofar as it lacks a singular or plural inherent nature. Hence, “the Tathāgata does not mentally perceive” any mindset in the three times. Therefore, those mindsets are “liberated” because the two types of obscurations are naturally absent. Hari: yasmād ekānekasvabhāvavaidhuryeṇāsattvāc cittaṃ traikālikaṃ tathāgaten ānupalabdhaṃ. tasmāt prakṛtyā dvividhāvaraṇavigamād vimuktāni. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ liberated mindsets are liberated mindsets.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ imperceptible mindsets are imperceptible mindsets. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ imperceptible mindsets are imperceptible mindsets? Subhūti, since a mindset does not exist, it is imperceptible. Subhūti, since a mindset is not real, it cannot be an object of consciousness. Since a mindset is not something actualized, it cannot be grasped by a divine eye, a wisdom eye, or‍—needless to say‍—a flesh eye, insofar as it does not appear.According to Hari (554), since it “does not exist”‍—i.e., it lacks characteristics‍—the “mindset is imperceptible,” i.e., the imagined mindset (parikalpita; kun btags). “Since it is not real” ‍—i.e., it lacks a cause‍—it is not “an object of consciousness”, i.e., the dependent mindset (paratantra; gzhan dbang). It “cannot be grasped” because, as its own nature is not found, it is not actualized, i.e., the actualized (pariniṣpanna; yongs grub) mindset. One should understand the qualities “imperceptible” and so on each to be connected with the three eyes, namely the divine, wisdom, and fleshly eyes, or, “insofar as they do appear,” to “any” of the five eyes‍—including the buddha eye and dharma eye with the other (three mentioned by name in the sentence). Hari: asattvāt subhūte ’dṛśyam iti lakṣaṇaśūnyatvenāsattvād adṛśyaṃ kalpitaṃ cittaṃ. hetvabhāvenābhūtatvād avijñeyaṃ paratantraṃ. svarūpāvidyamānatvena pariniṣpannatvād agrāhyaṃ pariniṣpannaṃ cittaṃ. pratyekaṃ prajñācakṣur ādibhis tribhiḥ sambandhanīyam. pañcānāṃ vā buddha­dharma­cakṣurādīnām anavabhāsagatatvād adṛśyā dikam avagantavyaṃ. Subhūti, that is how, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that immeasurable sentient beings’ imperceptible mindsets are imperceptible mindsets. For, Subhūti, in this way, the transcendent state of wisdom shows the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas this world.According to Hari (554), since these words are a concluding summary of the previous series of subtopics, this sentence is something to (logically) connect with each (of those subtopics) beginning with the Tathāgata knowing the “distracted mindsets” and so on up to this point. Hari: evaṃ hi subhūte prajñāpāramite tyādyupasaṃhārapadaṃ saṃkṣiptacittajñānādihārakānte ’pi pratyekaṃ sambandhanīyaṃ. According to Ratnākara (F.130.b), in this summarizing statement the word “world” (loka) refers to the sentient beings that constitute a world (i.e., not the outer world). RK: |’jig rten ni sems can gyi ’jig rten no.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately what is accepted and denied by immeasurable and innumerable other sentient beings and individuals.According to Hari (556), in terms of a logical affirmation, an “acceptance” refers to any mindset’s movement toward its own premise (viṣaya). In terms of a logical negation, a “denial” refers to any mindset’s movement away from another’s premise. “What is accepted and denied” is based on what has arisen from these two. He uses the two distinct terms “sentient beings” and “individuals” to differentiate those beings known to be in the world and beyond the world. Hari: vidhimukhena yaḥ svaviṣaye cittaprasaraḥ so ’yam unmiñjaḥ. pratiṣedhamukhena viṣayāntarāc cittasyāpasarpaṇaṃ nimiñjaḥ. tatsaṃjātatvād unmiñjitanimiñjitāni. laukika­lokottara­prasiddhibhedāt parasattvānāṃ parapudgalānām iti dvayam uktaṃ. Note: Both Ratnākara and Hari go on to explain “what is accepted and denied” (unmiñjitanimiñjitāni‍—lit. “what beings are open and closed to”) as referring to particular philosophical positions that those beings accept or deny. And how, Subhūti, as a result of such a transcendent state of wisdom, does the Tathāgata discern accurately what is accepted and denied by immeasurable and innumerable other sentient beings and individuals? Subhūti, the Tathāgata discerns accurately that all these mindsets that arise based upon physical forms are arising. The Tathāgata discerns accurately that all these mindsets that arise based upon feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are arising.

“Subhūti, how does the Tathāgata know what is accepted and denied in those mindsets to be based upon physical forms? Likewise, how does the Tathāgata know what is accepted and denied in those mindsets to be based upon feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness? Subhūti, when those other sentient beings and individuals hold that the Tathāgata exists after dying, they are referring to his physical form.According to Hari (556), absolutely “all mindsets” means the mindsets that arise based upon physical forms and so on in terms of what they affirm and deny. Hari: rūpaniśritānī tyādi. sarvāṇy eva vidhipratiṣedhamukhena rūpā dipañcaskandhāśritāny utpadyante cittānīty arthaḥ. When they hold that the Tathāgata does not exist after dying, they are referring to his physical form. When they hold that the Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after dying, they are referring to his physical form. When they hold that the Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after dying, they are referring to his physical form.

“Likewise, when they hold that the Tathāgata exists after dying, they are referring to his feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. When they hold that the Tathāgata does not exist after dying, they are referring to his feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. When they hold that the Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after dying, they are referring to his feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. When they hold that the Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after dying, they are referring to his feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.

“When they hold that the self and the world are eternal‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to physical forms. When they hold that the self and the world are not eternal‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to physical forms. When they hold that the self and the world are both eternal and not eternal‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to physical forms. When they hold that the self and the world are neither eternal nor not eternal‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to physical forms.

“Likewise, when they hold that the self and the world are eternal‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. When they hold that the self and the world are not eternal‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. When they hold that the self and the world are both eternal and not eternal‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. When they hold that the self and the world are neither eternal nor not eternal‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.

“When they hold that the self and the world are finite‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to physical forms. When they hold that the self and the world are infinite‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to physical forms. When they hold that the self and the world are both finite and infinite‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to physical forms. When they hold that the self and the world are neither finite nor infinite‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to physical forms. Likewise, when they hold that the self and the world are finite‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. When they hold that the self and the world are infinite‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. When they hold that the self and the world are both finite and infinite‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. When they hold that the self and the world are neither finite nor infinite‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.

“When they hold that the soul is the body‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to physical forms. When they hold that the soul is something other than the body‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to physical forms. Likewise, when they hold that the soul is the feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. When they hold that the soul is something other than feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness‍—and that this alone is the truth while everything else is a delusion‍—they are referring to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.

“That is how, Subhūti, as a result of this transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata knows that what is accepted and denied refers to physical forms. That is how, Subhūti, the Tathāgata knows that what is accepted and denied refers to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.

“But beyond that, Subhūti, as a result of this transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately what is accepted and denied by immeasurable and innumerable other sentient beings and individuals in the following way. That is to say, in this state, Subhūti, the Tathāgata knows physical forms. But how, Subhūti, does the Tathāgata know physical forms? Subhūti, the Tathāgata knows physical forms as the actual nature.According to Hari (558), this sentence means (as Nāgārjuna taught in the Root Verses on the Middle Way, RKIV, v.18): “You have asserted that which is dependent arising to be emptiness itself.” In essence, this section describes his full knowing of what is accepted and denied as the actual nature, because he fully knows the constituents from the perspective of the actual nature. Hari: tathā subhūte tathāgato rūpaṃ jānāti yathā tathate ti yaḥ pratītyasamutpādaḥ śūnyatā saiva te matā | iti tathatākāreṇa rūpādi­skandha­parijñānād unmiñjitādīnām api tathatāparijñānam iti yāvat. In this very way, the Tathāgata knows physical forms. That is also to say, in this state, Subhūti, the Tathāgata knows feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. But how, Subhūti, does the Tathāgata know feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness? Subhūti, the Tathāgata knows feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness as the actual nature. In this very way, the Tathāgata knows feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. Hence, Subhūti, as a result of this transcendent state of wisdom, the Tathāgata discerns accurately what is accepted and denied by immeasurable and innumerable other sentient beings and individuals. Since, Subhūti, he has shown this actual nature to be the actual nature of the Tathāgata, the actual nature of the constituents, and the actual nature of what is accepted and denied,According to Hari (558), he “shows the actual nature” means he establishes the actual nature in accord with all phenomena as having a single nature. With these words, he describes the knowledge of showing the actual nature. tathatāṃ prajñapayatī ti sarva­dharmānuyāyinīṃ tathatām ekarūpeṇa vyavasthāpayati. anena tathatā­prajña­panajñānam uktaṃ. Subhūti, he also has shown that the actual nature of the world is exactly the same as the actual nature of the constituents. How so? Subhūti, the Tathāgata has taught that the five constituents are what is termed ‘the world.’ Therefore, Subhūti, the actual nature of the constituents is the actual nature of the world. The actual nature of the world is the actual nature of all things.

“The actual nature of all things is the actual nature of the fruit of a stream enterer. The actual nature of the fruit of a stream enterer is the actual nature of the fruit of a once-returner. The actual nature of the fruit of a once-returner is the actual nature of the fruit of a non-returner. The actual nature of the fruit of a non-returner is the actual nature of the fruitional state of a worthy one. The actual nature of the fruitional state of a worthy one is the actual nature of a solitary realizer. The actual nature of a solitary realizer is the actual nature of a tathāgata. Thus, the actual nature of a tathāgata, the actual nature of the constituents, the actual nature of all things, and the actual nature of the noble disciples and solitary realizers is just one single actual nature. It is free from the multitude of things and nonthings because it is free of singularity, free of plurality, free of perishing, free of changing, free of duality, and free from creating duality.

“Subhūti, in this way, the Tathāgata has realized the actual nature, as a result of the transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, that is how the transcendent state of wisdom shows the world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. Subhūti, that is how the Tathāgata shows this world to the world and, in this way, is the sight of this world. The transcendent state of wisdom is the mother of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. She engenders them and gives them birth. In this way, Subhūti, the tathāgata awakens to the actual nature. He knows the actual nature. He knows the unfalsifiable actual nature. And he knows the undifferentiable actual nature. Thus, Subhūti, the Tathāgata is called the Tathāgata insofar as he has realized the actual nature.”The implication here is that the Tathāgata is one who has arrived at or realized (āgata) the actual nature (tathatā).

The elder Subhūti said, “Blessed One, the actual nature is profound. Blessed One, through this actual nature, the awakening of the blessed buddhas is revealed and elucidated. Blessed One, who else has firm conviction in it? Does a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing have it? Does a worthy one who has fulfilled his aspirations have it? Or does a person with the correct viewpoint have it? For surely, Blessed One, the Tathāgata has already realized and explained these extremely profound topics.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “That is true, Subhūti. That is true. The Tathāgata has already realized and explained these extremely profound topics. Subhūti, the Tathāgata has already realized and explained that actual nature as unperishing. He has already realized and explained it as the imperishability of the imperishable.”According to Hari (558), he taught the “imperishability” of the actual nature as “imperishable,” by saying (in the Twenty-Five Thousand, 28.73), “It is the true nature of things that is already established, whether or not the tathāgatas arise.” Hari: akṣayākṣayaivākhyāte ti utpādād vā tathāgatānām anutpādād vā tathāgatānāṃ sthitaivaiṣā dharmāṇāṃ dharmateti vacanād akṣayā tathatākṣayatvena nirdiṣṭā.

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 33 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 43 in the Eighteen Thousand. The content of the rest of this chapter corresponds to the content of the entirety of these chapters in the long sūtras.Then the gods of the desire and form realms, with Śakra, the powerful god, as their chief, together with the twenty thousand gods belonging to the Brahmakāyika heaven, approached the Blessed One, paid obeisance to the Blessed One’s feet with their heads, and stood to one side. Standing to the side, those gods of the desire and form realms said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, profound things are elucidated.According to Ratnākara (F.133.a), “things” here refers to the wisdoms. RK: chos zhes bya ba ni ye shes rnams so. In them, Blessed One, how are characteristics established?”

The Blessed One said, “In them, gods, characteristics are established to be empty. In them, gods, characteristics are established to be free of signs and free of inclinations. In them, gods, characteristics are established to be free of conditioning, free of arising, free of ceasing, free of emotional defilement, free of purification, and free of any state of being, nirvāṇa, the ultimate realm of things, and the actual nature. How so? Because, gods, these characteristics are not dependent upon anything. Gods, since these characteristics are just like space, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata has not established them to be characteristics. These characteristics are not considered physical forms. Likewise, these characteristics are not considered feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. These characteristics are not dependent upon physical forms. Likewise, these characteristics are not dependent upon feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. These characteristics are not established by gods, nāgas, humans, or spirits. These characteristics cannot be shaken off by the world with its gods, humans, and asuras. Why not? Because even the world with its gods, humans, and asuras possesses the very same characteristics. Furthermore, these characteristics cannot be established by hand. Gods, someone could ask, ‘Can space be established by hand?’ Nevertheless, gods, would that person be speaking correctly to say such a thing?”

At this, the gods of the desire and form realms said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, space is not established by anyone. Why is that? Blessed One, since space is unconditioned, space is not established by anyone.”

Then the Blessed One addressed the gods of the desire and form realms: “Gods, so it is. Whether or not the tathāgatas arise, these characteristics are established in the way they are. Why is that? Because the Tathāgata already realized and explained these to be the way that they are established. Therefore, gods, the Tathāgata is called ‘Tathāgata.’ ”The implication here is that the Tathāgata is one who has arrived at or realized (āgata) (the characteristics) as they are (tathā).

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the Tathāgata has realized these profound characteristics and the knowing belonging to the tathāgatas that is free of attachment to those, namely, the transcendent state of wisdom. The transcendent state of wisdom is the scope of the tathāgatas leading to knowing free of attachment.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “That is true, Subhūti. That is true. Indeed, Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom shows the world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas in this way. Subhūti, insofar as the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas depend upon and abide in this transcendent state of wisdom as the Dharma, these qualities always are established. But the tathāgatas realize them through nondwelling, and hence they depend upon and abide in nothing but the Dharma. They pay tribute to the Dharma, revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it. This transcendent state of wisdom alone is the true nature of things, Subhūti, so they pay tribute to the Dharma, revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, through this transcendent state of wisdom the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas bring about the state of complete knowing, and hence the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas are appreciative and grateful. Subhūti, when people describe the Tathāgata as ‘appreciative and grateful,’ they are speaking accurately to say this. Subhūti, out of appreciation and gratefulness, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata promotes and protects any vehicles in which he has traveled and any paths through which he has realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Subhūti, one should see this as the Tathāgata’s appreciation and gratefulness.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the Tathāgata has realized all things to be uncreated, unchanged, and unconditioned. Subhūti, one should see this too as the Tathāgata’s appreciation and gratefulness, because, Subhūti, it is as a result of this very transcendent state of wisdom that the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata’s knowing has proceeded in this same way with regard to all things. Also, by this very means, the transcendent state of wisdom is the one who shows this world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas.”

At this, the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “If, Blessed One, all things are not to be known and not to be seen, then how, Blessed One, can the transcendent state of wisdom be the one who shows this world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Excellent, Subhūti. It is excellent that you think the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata should be fully questioned about this point. Subhūti, you said, ‘All things are not to be known and not to be seen.’ It is true, Subhūti, it is true. All things are not to be known and not to be seen. Subhūti, how could all things be not to be known and not to be seen? It is because all things are empty. It is because all things are not dependent. It is in this way, Subhūti, that all things are not to be known and not to be seen. In this way, Subhūti, as a result of the transcendent state of wisdom, the tathāgatas realize all these things. In this way too, Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is the one who shows this world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, insofar as physical forms are not seen, and likewise feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are not seen. Thus, indeed, Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is the one who shows this world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas.”
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, how could physical forms not be seen? How could feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness not be seen?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, if no consciousness with the object ‘physical forms’ arises, then in this way physical forms are not being seen. Likewise, Subhūti, if no consciousness with the object ‘feelings,’ ‘conceptions,’ ‘conditioning factors,’ or ‘consciousness’ arises, then in this way feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are not being seen. And, Subhūti, when physical forms, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are not being seen, it is precisely the world being seen, since, Subhūti, the Tathāgata has seen the world in this way. Thus, indeed, Subhūti, is the transcendent state of wisdom showing this world to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. But, how could the transcendent state of wisdom show the world? It indicates the world to be an ‘empty world.’ It causes the world to be discerned in this way and shows it in this way. Through the purity of the world, it indicates the world to be an ‘inconceivable world,’ a ‘null world,’ a ‘void world.’ It causes the world to be discerned in this way and shows it in this way.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Showing the World,” the twelfth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 13: The InconceivableThe beginning of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 34 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 44 in the Eighteen Thousand. Its content corresponds to the first part of those chapters, down to 34.16 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and the similar passage in the Hundred Thousand, and down to 44.13 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Ah! Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound, in that it provides assistance with a great task.According to Ratnākara (F.136.b), it is “profound” because it provides assistance with a great task. “Oh!” is an exclamation of joy. RK: bya ba chen pos nye bar gnas so zhes bya ba la sogs pa gsungs te| gang gi phyir bya ba chen pos nye bar gnas pa de’i phyir zab pa’o| |kye ma ’i sgra ni dga’ pa’o. Note: This chapter plays upon slightly different usages of the Sanskrit word kṛtya. One should understand the translation of the singular “task” to mean a “service” (kṛtya) being performed for others and the translation of the plural “tasks” to mean the “duties” (kṛtya) of others being performed. Cf. BHSD pratyupasthita + krtyena, in the sense of “waits upon” others “with service.” Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom alone provides assistance with an inconceivable task, an incomparable task, an immeasurable task, an incalculable task, and a task equal to the unequaled.”According to Ratnākara (F.136.a), in the last chapter the Blessed One explained the defining characteristics that consist in (the three types of complete) knowings. So now he will explain the distinctive characteristics. RK: shes pa’i bdag nyid kyi mtshan nyid bshad nas khyad par gyi mtshan nyid brjod par bya. According to Hari (569), this sentence describes distinctive characteristics through the first modifiers “inconceivable,” “incomparable,” “immeasurable,” and “incalculable,” which, respectively, summarize the four instants within (the realization of) the truth of suffering. Hari: acintyakṛtyene tyādi. acintyātulyāprameyāsaṃkhyeyatābhis tāvadviśeṣair yathākramaṃ duhkha­satya­catuḥkṣaṇasaṃgṛhītair viśeṣalakṣaṇam uktaṃ. Note: The other twelve (of the sixteen) instants in the realization of the three other noble truths are connected to other passages elsewhere.

The Blessed One said, “That is true. Subhūti, that is true. The transcendent state of wisdom provides assistance with a great task. The transcendent state of wisdom provides assistance with an inconceivable task, an incomparable task, an immeasurable task, an incalculable task, and a task equal to the unequaled.

“And how, Subhūti, could this transcendent state of wisdom provide assistance with an inconceivable task? Subhūti, it is because the state of a tathāgata, the state of a buddha, the state of a self-existent being, and the state of someone who has complete knowing is inconceivable.According to Hari (570), previous masters say that, in this context, the four states, i.e., “the state of a tathāgata” and so on, should be connected with four types of wisdom, namely, mirror-like wisdom and so on. The “task” is “inconceivable” because it actualizes the state of a tathāgata and so on. The “task” is “incomparable” and so on should also be construed in just the same way in subsequent passages. Hari: tatra tathāgata tvam ityādipadacatuṣṭayam ādarśādijñāna­catuṣṭayabhedena yojyam iti pūrvācāryāḥ. tathāgata­tvādiniṣpādanād acintya­kṛtyatvam. evam eva uttaratrāpy atulyakṛtyatvādikaṃ yojyaṃ. According to Ratnākara (F.136.b), “the state of a tathāgata” means one can speak to the way things actually are; “the state of a buddha” means one is oneself perfectly awakened; “the state of a self-existent being” means one has achieved mastery over all things; and “the state of knowing of someone who has complete knowing” means the pure, unobstructed knowing of all aspects of everything. RK: de la de bzhin gshegs pa nyid ni chos ji lta na bzhin du gsungs pa’o| sangs rgyas nyid ni rang nyid mngon par rdzogs par byang chub pa’o| |rang byung nyid ni chos thams cad la dbang bsgyur ba nyid do| |thams cad mkhyen pa nyid ni chos thams cad kyi rnam pa thams cad la dag pa dang thogs pa med pa’i ye shes so. In this way, indeed, this transcendent state of wisdom provides assistance with an inconceivable task, since it cannot be conceived by a mindset. Why not? Because no mindset, no mental activity, and no mental factor leads to it.According to Hari (571), in this sentence the word “mindset” refers to a mental consciousness; “mental activity” refers to a mental action (karma) and the verbal and physical actions born from it; and “mental factor” refers to understanding (prajñā) and so on (i.e., the other mental factors conjoined with consciousness). The idea is that none of these “lead to it”‍—i.e., to buddhahood and so on‍—in the manner of a real thing that supports it, because buddhahood is free of all error (whereas they are not free of error). Hari: cittaṃ manovijñānaṃ. cetanā mānasaṃ karma tajje vākkāyakarmaṇī. caitasiko vā prajñādiḥ. ālambakabhāvenātra buddhatvādau na pravartate, sarva­viparyāsāpagamād iti bhāvaḥ.

“Also, Subhūti, how could this transcendent state of wisdom provide assistance with an incomparable task? Subhūti, it is because the state of a tathāgata, the state of a buddha, the state of a self-existent being, and the state of someone who has complete knowing can neither be conceived nor compared.According to Hari (571), “cannot be compared” means its own nature cannot be determined by the intellect. Hari: tulayituṃ ve ti buddhyā svarūpam avadhārayituṃ. In this way, indeed, this transcendent state of wisdom provides assistance with an incomparable task.

“Also, Subhūti, how could this transcendent state of wisdom provide assistance with an immeasurable task?
