Subhūti, it is because the state of a tathāgata, the state of a buddha, the state of a self-existent being, and the state of someone who has complete knowing is immeasurable.According to Hari (571), it is “immeasurable” since it cannot be measured by anything. Hari: aprameyaṃ hī ti yasmāt pramātum aśakyaṃ. In this way, indeed, this transcendent state of wisdom provides assistance with an immeasurable task.

“Also, Subhūti, how could this transcendent state of wisdom provide assistance with an incalculable task? Subhūti, it is because the state of a tathāgata, the state of a buddha, the state of a self-existent being, and the state of someone who has complete knowing is incalculable.According to Hari (571), it is “incalculable” since it cannot enumerated as one and so on. Hari: asaṃkheyaṃ hī ti yasmād ekatvādinā gaṇayituṃ na pāryate. In this way, indeed, this transcendent state of wisdom provides assistance with an incalculable task.

“Also, Subhūti, how could this transcendent state of wisdom provide assistance with a task equal to the unequaled? Subhūti, it is because a tathāgata, a buddha, a self-existent being, someone who has complete knowing does not have an equal, so how could there be anything superior to him?According to Hari (571), “how could there be anything superior to him?” asks how, since there is nothing equal to him, there could be something more distinguished than something unequaled. For this reason, he says “equal to,” i.e., the same as, that which has an “unequaled” nature. Hence, “equal to the unequaled” refers to the Blessed One who has complete knowing. Hari: kutaḥ punar uttara iti samābhāvād asamasya kasmāt punar uttaraḥ prativiśiṣṭo bhaviṣyati. ato ’samenātmanā samas tulya ity asamasamo bhagavān sarvajñaḥ. In this way, indeed, this transcendent state of wisdom provides assistance with a task equal to the unequaled.”

The elder Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, is it only the state of a tathāgata that is inconceivable, incomparable, immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled? Likewise, is it only the states of a buddha, of a self-existent being, and of someone who has complete knowing that are inconceivable, incomparable, immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled? In other words, are physical forms also inconceivable, incomparable, immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled? Likewise, are feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness also inconceivable, incomparable, immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled? In other words, are all things also inconceivable, incomparable, immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “You are right. Subhūti, you are right. Physical forms also are inconceivable, incomparable, immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness also are inconceivable, incomparable, immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled.

“Likewise, all things also are inconceivable, incomparable, immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, in the true nature of physical forms there is no mindset, no mental activity, no mental factor, and no comparing. Likewise, Subhūti, in the true nature of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness there is no mindset, no mental activity, no mental factor, and no comparing. Likewise, Subhūti, in the true nature of all things there is no mindset, no mental activity, no mental factor, and no comparing.

“Likewise, Subhūti, it is because physical forms are also inconceivable and incomparable. Likewise, Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are also inconceivable and incomparable. Moreover, Subhūti, all things are also inconceivable and incomparable.

“Subhūti, physical forms are also immeasurable. Likewise, Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are also immeasurable. How so? Because no measurement of physical forms is discerned. Likewise, Subhūti, no measurement of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is discerned. Moverover, Subhūti, no measurement of all things is discerned. Subhūti, why is no measurement of physical forms discerned? Likewise, Subhūti, why is no measurement of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness discerned? Moreover, Subhūti, why is no measurement of all things discerned? Because, Subhūti, no measurement of physical forms is found. Likewise, Subhūti, it is because no measurement of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is found. Moreover, it is because no measurement of all things is found.

“Subhūti, why is no measurement of physical forms found? Likewise, Subhūti, why is no measurement of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness found? Moreover, why is no measurement of all things found? Because, Subhūti, all things are beyond measure.

“Also, Subhūti, physical forms are incalculable. Likewise, Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are incalculable. Moreover, Subhūti, all things are incalculable, because they go beyond quantification.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, physical forms are equal to the unequaled. Likewise, Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are equal to the unequaled. Moreover, Subhūti, all things are equal to the unequaled, because, Subhūti, all things are equal to space. So then, what do you think, Subhūti? With respect to space, is there anything equal to it, any quantification of it, any measurement of it, a comparison to it, a mindset about it, or any mental factors regarding it?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “In this very way, Subhūti, in this manner, all things also are inconceivable, incomparable, immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled. And, Subhūti, these qualities of a tathāgata are inconceivable, because they stop all mindsets. They are incomparable, because they surpass all comparisons. Subhūti, the terms inconceivable and incomparable refer to a quality within consciousness. Likewise, Subhūti, the terms immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled refer to qualities of a tathāgata that are immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled, because they end all comparisons, numbers, and measurements. Just as space is immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled, these qualities are immeasurable, incalculable, and equal to the unequaled. By comparing them to space, they are incomparable like space. Ah! Not being comparable with anything comparable to space, these qualities bear no similarity to it, and hence, Subhūti, these things are called incomparable.

“Just as space is inconceivable, these qualities are inconceivable. Just as space is incomparable, these qualities are incomparable. Just as space is immeasurable, these qualities are immeasurable. Just as space is incalculable, these qualities are incalculable. Just as space is equal to the unequaled, these qualities are equal to the unequaled.”

There and then, as the Blessed One was speaking about this inconceivability, incomparability, innumerability, and equality with the unequaled, five hundred monks became free of clinging and their mindsets were liberated from worldly influences. Also, two thousand nuns became free of clinging and their mindsets were liberated from worldly influences.The Sanskrit omits this sentence entirely, but here we are following the Tibetan which reads the same sentence twice, once for monks and once for nuns, because it makes sense given the distinction between lay practitioners in the next line. Since Skt. reads bhikṣuśatānām only, one could argue that this compound could have been (with some creativity) construed as bhikṣu­bhikṣuṇiśatānām, although the Tibetan comes up with a different number of nuns. Also, six thousand male lay devotees had their Dharma eye concerning things purified and they became free from passion and free from impurities. Also, three thousand female lay devotees had their Dharma eye concerning things purified and they became free from passion and free from impurities. Also, twenty thousand bodhisattvas attained the state of acceptance that all things are free of arising, and they were all foreordained by the Blessed One for unsurpassed, perfect awakening in this present Good Eon. Also, those male and female lay devotees whose Dharma eye concerning things had been purified became free from passion and free from impurities, and were foreordained. Also, their mindsets became free of clinging and liberated from worldly influences.

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound. Ah! Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom provides assistance with a great task.”

The Blessed One said, “You are right, Subhūti. You are right. The transcendent state of wisdom provides assistance with a great task. How so? Because, Subhūti, the state of complete knowing is entrusted to it, the stage of a solitary realizer is entrusted to it, and the stage of all disciples is entrusted to it.According to Hari (576), the “state of complete knowing is entrusted to it” means the state of complete knowing is invariably connected to or dependent upon the transcendent state of wisdom. Hari: sarvajñatā samāyukte ti sarvākārajñatā prajñā­pāramitāyāṃ pratibaddhā. Subhūti, as an analogy, it is just like for an annointed, kṣatriya king who has obtained status and power over his subjects. All his royal tasks, his municipal tasks, and his national tasks are entrusted to his ministers. Because of this, the king comes to have few worries and lays down his burdens. In the very same way, Subhūti, all the qualities of a buddha, the qualities of a solitary realizer, and the qualities of a disciple are entrusted to the transcendent state of wisdom. In that regard, the transcendent state of wisdom performs its task and, Subhūti, by this means, it provides assistance with a great task to ensure that one does not apprehend physical forms and one does not fixate upon them. Likewise, this transcendent state of wisdom provides assistance with a great task to ensure that one does not apprehend feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness, and one does not fixate upon them. Moreover, the transcendent state of wisdom provides assistance so that one does not apprehend the fruition of a stream enterer, the fruition of a once-returner, the fruition of a non-returner, or the fruition of a worthy one, and one does not fixate upon them. It also provides assistance to ensure that one does not apprehend the fruition of a solitary realizer’s awakening, and one does not fixate upon it. It also provides assistance to ensure that one does not apprehend the state of complete knowing, and one does not fixate upon it.”
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, how does the transcendent state of wisdom provide assistance to ensure that one does not apprehend the state of complete knowing and one does not fixate upon it?”
The Blessed One asked in return, “Subhūti, what do you think? Can you identify a state of a worthy one that one could either apprehend or fixate upon?”
“No, definitely not,” said Subhūti. “I do not see a state of a worthy one that can either be apprehended or fixated upon.”

“Exactly so, Subhūti. Exactly so,” said the Blessed One. “Subhūti, I also do not see a state of a tathāgata. Subhūti, as I myself do not see a tathāgata, I do not apprehend it and I do not fixate upon it. Therefore, Subhūti, the state of complete knowing also has no apprehending and no habitual fixation.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, since the state of complete knowing also has no apprehending and no habitual fixation, Blessed One, those bodhisattva great beings with limited roots of virtue who have just begun to set out on the path may hear this explication and be frightened, terrified, and stricken with terror. Nevertheless, Blessed One, those bodhisattva great beings who possess the causes, who have performed services for former conquerors, and who have planted the roots of virtue over a long period will hear this profound transcendent state of wisdom and have firm conviction in it.”According to Ratnākara (138.a), “nevertheless” and so on teaches about fortunate disciples. Those “who possess the causes” are from the noble family. Those “who have performed services for former conquerors” means they have earned the merit that is beneficial. “Over a long period” means over a long lime. “Planted” means accumulated. “Roots of virtue” means they have received (the teachings of) the Blessed Mother and so on. RK: ’on kyang zhes bya ba la sogs pas ni skal ba dang ldan par bstan to| |rgyu phun sum tshogs pa zhes bya ba ni rigs dang ldan pa’o| |sngon gyi rgyal ba la bgyi ba bgyis zhes bya ba ni phan pa’i bsod nams byas pa’o| |yun ring po ni dus yun ring po’o; | bskrun pa ni bsags pa’o| |dge ba’i rtsa ba ni bcom ldan ’das yum thob pa la sogs pa’o.
“That is true, Subhūti. That is true,” said the Blessed One.

Then the gods of the desire realm and the form realm said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound, difficult to see, and difficult to comprehend. Blessed One, those sentient beings who have firm conviction in this transcendent state of wisdom have performed services for former conquerors and have planted the roots of virtue over a long period. Suppose that, for an eon or more than an eon, all beings in a billionfold, great order world system were to go about life at the stage of proceeding based on faith. Now suppose that, even for one day, a single person were to take pleasure in the state of accepting this profound transcendent state of wisdom, to seek it, to contemplate it, to consider it, to reflect upon it, and to meditate upon it. O Blessed One, this single person alone would be better than the others.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the gods of the desire realm and the form realm, “Gods, if a noble son or daughter were to listen to this profound transcendent state of wisdom, one would expect nirvāṇa to occur more quickly for him or her than for those going about life at the stage of proceeding based on faith for an eon or more than an eon.”
Then the gods of the desire realm and the form realm said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, it is a great transcendent state, this transcendent state of wisdom.”

After saying this, they paid obeisance at the Blessed One’s feet with their heads, and circumambulated the Blessed One three times. “We are departing from the presence of the Blessed One,” they said pleasantly, and they set out. After going a short way, they disappeared. The gods of the desire realm left for the desire realm and the gods of the form realm left for the Brahmā world system.
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Inconceivable,” the thirteenth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 14: The ComparisonsThe beginning of this chapter corresponds to points within Chapter 34 of the Twenty-Five Thousand (34.17) and Hundred Thousand, and within Chapter 44 of the Eighteen Thousand (44.14). From the point marked by note 801 until the end of the chapter, the content corresponds to the entirety of Chapter 35 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 45 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, when just by hearing this profound transcendent state of wisdom, a bodhisattva great being has firm conviction in it, he or she is not deflated, not depressed, and does not turn back from it. He or she is not confused, doubtful, or worried about the transcendent state of wisdom, but rather delights in it. Blessed One, in what realm will such a bodhisattva great being die and where will he or she be reborn?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, when just by hearing this profound transcendent state of wisdom, a bodhisattva great being has firm conviction in it, he or she is not deflated, not depressed, and does not turn back from it. That bodhisattva great being is not confused about the transcendent state of wisdom, not doubtful about it, and not worried about it, but rather delights in seeing and hearing it, keeps it in mind, and cultivates it.According to Ratnākara (F.139.a), in this context, “confused” means lacking in certainty; “doubtful” means having second thoughts; “worried” means turned around about it. RK: rmongs pa ni nges pa med pa’o| the tshom ni yid gnyis so| |som nyi ni phyin ci log go. That bodhisattva great being does not depart from the attentiveness associated with the transcendent state of wisdom and does not become mentally discouraged about it, but rather develops the longing to take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, and pass it on. And that bodhisattva great being forms a connection with a Dharma teacher, follows them, and does not abandon them. As an analogy, in the very same way that a cow with a newborn calf does not abandon it, Subhūti, that bodhisattva great being who has listened to the transcendent state of wisdom does not abandon the Dharma teacher, while keeping the transcendent state of wisdom on him or her physically or in the form of a book.According to Ratnākara (F.139.a), “keeping it on him or her physically” means keeping (a physical representation of the Transcendent State of Wisdom) in the area of his heart. RK: |lus la thogs pa ni snying ga’i phyogs su thogs pa’o. Such a bodhisattva great being will die exclusively among humans and then be reborn exclusively among humans.”
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, could any bodhisattva great being who is endowed with these same positive qualities have been reborn here after having died in other buddhafields?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, one should understand that any bodhisattva great being endowed with these same positive qualities could have been reborn here after having died in other buddhafields, where he or she served other blessed buddhas, made inquiries, and fully pursued his or her questions.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, one should understand that any bodhisattva great being endowed with these same positive qualities also could have been reborn here after having died in the Tuṣita god realm, where he or she served the bodhisattva great being Maitreya, asked him about the transcendent state of wisdom, inquired about it, and fully pursued his or her questions about it. On the other hand, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being could be reborn among humans after having previously listened to this profound transcendent state of wisdom, but neither asked about it, nor inquired about it, nor fully pursued his or her questions about it. But if, when this profound transcendent state of wisdom is being taught, he or she is confused, worried, or has a deflated mindset, then one should understand such a bodhisattva great being not to have been an inquiring type in the past either. How so? Because when this profound transcendent state of wisdom is being taught, that bodhisattva great being becomes confused, worried, and has a deflated mindset.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, that bodhisattva great being may previously have heard this profound transcendent state of wisdom, asked about it, inquired about it, and fully pursued his or her questions about it for one, two, three, four, or five days, but now may have only a passing faith that repeatedly gets sidetracked, and hence he or she loses interest in inquiring about it. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, he or she previously did not thoroughly ask about it, thoroughly inquire about it, and fully pursue his or her questions about it. Such a bodhisattva great being sometimes follows this transcendent state of wisdom in order to listen to it, but sometimes has no desire to do so. He or she repeatedly tosses it aside, becomes disheartened, has an unstable mind, and is like cotton wool.According to Hari (584), he or she is “like cotton wool”‍—i.e., similar to a cotton cloth constructed by creating unwoven ends‍—due to having a similarly flimsy character. Hari: tūlapicūpamaś ce ti tūlavarti­karaṇāya saṃskṛtaṃ tūlakaṃ tūlapicuḥ. tenānava­sthitasādharmyāt sadṛśas tadupamaḥ. One should know such a bodhisattva great being to have traveled the path only for a brief time. Having only recently arrived at the path, such a bodhisattva abandons any faith, trust, or longing. He or she does not uphold this profound transcendent state of wisdom, follow it, or embrace it.According to Ratnākara (F.139.a), “uphold” means know and “follow” means act in conformity. Instead, he or she might desire either one of the two other levels, namely, the disciple level or the solitary realizer level.

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 35 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 45 in the Eighteen Thousand. The content of the remainder of the chapter corresponds to the entirety of these chapters.“As an analogy, Subhūti, it is like being in the middle of the ocean after a shipwreck. One can be sure that until people have reached the other shore, they will drown if they do not grab hold of a piece of wood or a plank, or find support on a dead body. But, Subhūti, during a shipwreck in the middle of that ocean, one can be sure that those people will not drown if they do grab hold of a piece of wood or a plank, or find support on a dead body. They will successfully cross to the other shore without hindrance and come to abide on dry ground, unharmed and uninjured. Likewise, Subhūti, any bodhisattva who only has a small amount of faith, trust, appreciation, and longing does not depend for support on the transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, one can be sure that such a bodhisattva will meet with a downfall midway along the path. Without ever reaching the state of complete knowing, he or she will come to abide in the states of the disciples or solitary realizers. But there are bodhisattva great beings who have faith, acceptance, predilection, longing, heroic perseverance, interest, firm conviction, altruistic disposition, renunciation, devotion, joy, delight, trust, appreciation, and unflinching endurance to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and they depend for support on the transcendent state of wisdom. And those bodhisattva great beings who do have such faith, acceptance, predilection, longing, heroic perseverance, interest, firm conviction, altruistic disposition, renunciation, devotion, joy, delight, trust, appreciation, and unflinching endurance to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening will attain the transcendent state of wisdom and come to abide in the state of complete knowing.

“Subhūti, as an analogy, it is like a woman or man carrying around water with an unfired clay pot. Subhūti, one can be sure that the pot will not last long and will quickly break apart and disintegrate. Why is that? Since the pot has not been fired, it will come to be nothing but earth in the end. Likewise, Subhūti, and even more so, a bodhisattva might have the faith, the acceptance, the predilection, the longing, the heroic perseverance, the interest, the firm conviction, the altruistic disposition, the renunciation, the devotion, the joy, the delight, the trust, the appreciation, and the unflinching endurance to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, but if such a bodhisattva great being is not completely supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means, then, Subhūti, one can be sure that he or she will meet with a downfall midway along the path. Subhūti, what is the downfall midway along the path? It is the level of a disciple or the level of a solitary realizer.

“Subhūti, as an analogy, it is like a woman or man carrying around water from a river, a lake, a well, or another water source with a well-fired clay pot. Subhūti, one can be sure that, when carrying that water, the pot will reach the house successfully without hindrance. Why is that? Because the pot is well fired. Likewise, Subhūti, and even more so, a bodhisattva might have the faith, the acceptance, the predilection, the longing, the heroic perseverance, the interest, the firm conviction, the altruistic disposition, the renunciation, the devotion, the joy, the delight, the trust, the appreciation, and the unflinching endurance to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and if they are completely supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means, then, Subhūti, one can be sure that they will not meet with a downfall midway along the path. Unharmed and uninjured, they will come to abide in the state of complete knowing.

“Subhūti, as an analogy, it is like an unwise man who launches into the water a seafaring boat that has been hitched to its moorings for a long time. Without caulking the holes and without doing any repairs, he might board the ship, which is buckling beneath its load with its goods piled up and overflowing.According to Hari (588–89), “without caulking the holes” through plugging any leaks with bark caulk and “without doing any repairs” through replacing any rotten wood. “He might board the ship buckling beneath its load” means he sets out carrying a heavy load. Hari: anākoṭitām iti udakapraveśasthāne valkalādānāt. aparikarmakṛtām iti pūtikāṣṭhānapanayanāt. bhārārtām abhirūḍha iti gurubhārabharitām utkalitaḥ. But, Subhūti, one can be sure that the boat, as it is, will sink without his cargo making it across the water. Since his goods will be falling to one side while his boat falls apart on the other, that unwise merchant lacking in skill in means will suffer a great loss of wealth and will have lost a great source of riches. Likewise, and even more so, Subhūti, a bodhisattva might have the faith, the acceptance, the predilection, the longing, the heroic perseverance, the interest, the firm conviction, the altruistic disposition, the renunciation, the devotion, the joy, the delight, the trust, the appreciation, and the unflinching endurance to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, but if such a bodhisattva lacks the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means, then, Subhūti, one can be sure that he or she will sink midway without ever reaching the ocean-like state of complete knowing. When that bodhisattva meets with a downfall, he or she will have lost masses of his own and other’s wealth and riches, specifically because he or she has been deprived of the state of complete knowing, the source of great wealth. But, Subhūti, what is the downfall of a bodhisattva midway along the path? It is the level of a disciple or the level of a solitary realizer.

“On the other hand, Subhūti, as an analogy, it is like an intelligent merchant whose seafaring boat is well hitched with its moorings, the boat’s holes well caulked, and the boat’s repairs well done. After all this has been done, he might launch the boat in the water, load his goods, and fill it up with everything evenly secured. Then, when the wind is suitable, he might sail gradually in the direction intended for his voyage. Subhūti, one can be sure that this boat will not sink in the water. It will go to any place to which it needs to go. Also, this merchant will come to have great profits, namely, worldly riches. Likewise, Subhūti, bodhisattvas might have the faith, the acceptance, the predilection, the longing, the heroic perseverance, the interest, the firm conviction, the altruistic disposition, the renunciation, the devotion, the joy, the delight, the trust, the appreciation, and the unflinching endurance to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. If such a bodhisattva great being is supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and not lacking in the skill in means, then, Subhūti, one can be sure that this bodhisattva great being will not sink midway and meet with a downfall. Instead, he or she will come to abide in unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Why is that? Subhūti, it is this way because such a bodhisattva great being has the faith, the acceptance, the predilection, the longing, the heroic perseverance, the interest, the firm conviction, the altruistic disposition, the renunciation, the devotion, the joy, the delight, the trust, the appreciation, and the unflinching endurance to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. That bodhisattva great being also has the qualities supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and does not lack the skill in means. So, he or she will not proceed to the level of the disciple or the level of the solitary realizer. Rather, he or she will be oriented toward the qualities that become the state of complete knowing, progress toward them, and lead to realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Subhūti, as an analogy, it is like a very aged, decrepit old man, one hundred twenty years old. Suppose he were to develop a physical illness because of a wind disorder, a bile disorder, a phlegm disorder, or a mixture of all these. Subhūti, what do you think? Would this man be able get up out of bed without being supported?”
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One continued, “But, Subhūti, if that man were able to get up out of bed, he would definitely not have the strength to proceed a half mile. Even if he could somehow get up out of bed, that man, who had degenerated because of old age and sickness, would lack the strength to proceed farther. Likewise, and even more so, Subhūti, a bodhisattva might have the faith, the acceptance, the predilection, the longing, the heroic perseverance, the interest, the firm conviction, the altruistic disposition, the renunciation, the devotion, the joy, the delight, the trust, the appreciation, and the unflinching endurance to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. But if, without being supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and without skill in means, such a bodhisattva were nevertheless to set out to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, then, Subhūti, one can be sure that he or she would sink down and meet with a downfall midway along the path. That is to say, he or she would come to abide on the level of a disciple or on the level of a solitary realizer. Why is that? Because he or she is not supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and lacks the skill in means.

“Subhūti, as an analogy, it is like a very aged, decrepit old man, one hundred twenty years old. Suppose he were to develop a physical illness because of a wind disorder, a bile disorder, a phlegm disorder, or a mixture of all these. But suppose two strong men supported him well from beneath the left and right armpits and held him up well, saying, ‘Sir, just go wherever you desire, and as far as you desire to go, we will hold you up. You should have no fear of falling on the path to where you need to go until you have reached a resting place.’ Likewise, Subhūti, a bodhisattva might have the faith, the acceptance, the predilection, the longing, the heroic perseverance, the interest, the firm conviction, the altruistic disposition, the renunciation, the devotion, the joy, the delight, the trust, the appreciation, and the unflinching endurance to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. But if such a bodhisattva is helped up by the transcendent state of wisdom and endowed with the skill in means, then, Subhūti, one can be sure that this bodhisattva great being will not sink down or meet with a downfall midway along the path. Rather, this bodhisattva great being has the strength to reach his or her destination, namely, that of unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Comparisons,” the fourteenth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 15: The GodsThe beginning of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 36 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 46 in the Eighteen Thousand. The chapter goes on to entirely include (where marked by note 826) the corresponding content of Chapters 37 of the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand and Chapter 47 of the Eighteen Thousand; and also (where marked by note 835) the beginning of Chapter 38 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand and of Chapter 48 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: Then the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, how should a beginning bodhisattva great being dwell in the transcendent state of wisdom and train in it?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, a beginning bodhisattva great being who wishes to train in this transcendent state of wisdom should attend virtuous friends, be devoted to them, and serve them. Subhūti, one should understand a bodhisattva great being’s virtuous friends to be those who instruct and guide him or her in this transcendent state of wisdom, and expound its meaning. They expound the meaning of the transcendent state of wisdom for him or her, saying, ‘Noble child, come and engage in meditation in the transcendent state of generosity.According to Ratnākara (F.140.a), “meditation” (yoga) refers to either a preliminary practice (prayoga) or an applied practice (abhiyoga). RK: yogam iti prayogam abhiyogaṃ vā. According to Hari (594), “engage in meditation” means be engrossed in practice through not apprehending a recipient, giver, or gift and so on. Hari: yogam āpadyasve ti deya­dāyaka­prati­grāhakādyanupalambha­yogena pratipattiparo bhava. Likewise, engage in meditation in the transcendent state of ethical discipline, tolerant acceptance, heroic perseverance, and meditation.

“ ‘Noble child, whatever gifts you give, dedicate them all to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Noble child, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as physical forms. Likewise, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness.According to Hari (594), “do not hold fast” means to not cling to it through fixating on physical form and so on. Hari: mā rūpataḥ parāmṛkṣa iti mā rūpādyabhiniveśayogena grahīr ity arthaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.140.a), “do not hold fast” means do not cling to it as having the nature of those things (i.e., physical forms and so on). RK: parāmṛkṣa iti tadbhāvena mā grahīḥ. Why not? Because, noble child, the state of complete knowing is not held fast.According to Hari (594), “not held fast” means at the ultimate level, there is no way to contemplate it because its nature is nonarising. Hari: aparāmṛṣṭā hītyādi. tattvato ’nutpādarūpatvāt sarvākārajñatā na kenacit prakāreṇa pratyavamarśaṇīyā. According to Ratnākara (F.140.a), “not held fast” means it is not conceptualized through any qualities. RK: aparāmṛṣṭe ti sarvadharmair avikalpitā.

“ ‘Noble child, whatever ethical discipline you uphold, dedicate it all to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.The text here contains a meta-textual instruction for reciters to “repeat” (peyālam) the above, while substituting certain lines for the five other transcendent states one by one. In this translation, rather than simply write “repeat,” the refrain has simply been translated and inserted, because it would take a reader more time to figure out a note about what to repeat, than to read through the extra half page of spelling it out. According to Hari (594), one should repeat the four-line refrain that extends from the previous “…dedicate them all to unsurpassed, perfect awakening” to “state of complete knowing is not held fast” for each of the transcendent states, while inserting the phrases about ethical discipline and so on. Hari: peyālam iti tat sarvam anuttarāyām ityādi yāvat kulaputra sarvajñat ety ayaṃ granthaḥ śīlādipāramitācatuṣṭaye ’tideśanīyaḥ. Noble child, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as physical forms. Likewise, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Why is that? Because, noble child, the state of complete knowing is not held fast.

“ ‘Noble child, whatever tolerant acceptance you practice, dedicate it all to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Noble child, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as physical forms. Likewise, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Why is that? Because, noble child, the state of complete knowing is not held fast.

“ ‘Noble child, whatever heroic perseverance you apply yourself with, dedicate it all to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Noble child, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as physical forms. Likewise, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Why is that? Because, noble child, the state of complete knowing is not held fast.

“ ‘Noble child, whatever meditation you engage in, dedicate it all to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Noble child, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as physical forms. Likewise, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Why is that? Because, noble child, the state of complete knowing is not held fast.

“ ‘Noble child, whatever wisdom you are getting familiar with, dedicate it all to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.140.a), “getting familiar with” means practicing repeatedly. RK: parijayo ’bhyāsaḥ. Noble child, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as physical forms. Likewise, do not hold fast to unsurpassed, perfect awakening as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Why is that? Because, noble child, the state of complete knowing is not held fast.

“ ‘In addition, noble child, do not covet the level of a disciple or the level of a solitary realizer.’ In this way, virtuous friends should gradually introduce a beginning bodhisattva great being to the transcendent state of wisdom.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, having set out to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, those bodhisattva great beings perform these difficult noble deeds. Since those bodhisattva great beings approach generosity, ethical discipline, tolerant acceptance, heroic perseverance, meditation, and wisdom in the way just mentioned, they do not long even to attain their own independent nirvāṇa.According to Hari (595), “in the way just mentioned” refers here to their doing (those difficult noble deeds) regularly to actualize the state of a buddha. Hari: evaṃrūpam iti buddha­tvaniṣpādakam avikalaṃ kāraṇam ity arthaḥ. Instead, they wish to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and while seeing the masses of beings utterly afflicted, they themselves are not frightened by cyclic existence.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Yes, Subhūti. Yes. The bodhisattva great beings who perform difficult noble deeds have set out for the world’s benefit and happiness, out of sympathy for the world. They each think, ‘I will become a shelter for the world. I will become a refuge for the world. I will become a sanctuary for the world. I will become an ultimate resort for the world. I will become an island for the world. I will become a light for the world. I will become a guide for the world. And after realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening, I will become a resting place for the world.’ And with such thoughts, they engage in heroic perseverance toward their goal of unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“How do those bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a shelter for the world? Subhūti, they shelter this world from those sufferings in the realms of cyclic existence. They provide protection in order to eliminate their sufferings. And they engage in heroic perseverance. Subhūti, that is how the bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a shelter for the world.

“How do those bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a refuge for the world? Subhūti, they liberate those beings who are subject to birth, aging, sickness, and death from being subject to the mental disturbances of grief, lamentation, suffering, and despair.As negative mental states, duḥkha and daurmanasya can range in degree and quality. They have been translated “suffering” and “despair” here, since duḥkha and daurmanasya together seem to characterize cyclic existence and since these are meant as umbrella terms for the negative tinge of all experience in cyclic existence. Cf. BHSD, daurmanasya, “dejected”; Apte, daurmanasya, “mental pain,” “despair.” Subhūti, that is how the bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a refuge for the world.

“How do those bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a sanctuary for the world? Subhūti, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas teach to sentient beings that truth that leads to freedom from clinging.”According to Hari (597), “freedom from clinging” means freedom from the arising of physical forms and so on. A “sanctuary” is in the sense of removing the causes of suffering. Hari: aśleṣāye ti rūpādyanutpādāya. layanam iti duḥkha­hetu­nivartanārthena.
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, how could there be freedom from clinging?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, that which is free from the bonds of physical forms is free from clinging to physical forms. That which is free from clinging to physical forms is free from the bonds of physical forms. That which is free from the bonds of physical forms is free from the arising and ceasing of physical forms. That which is free from the arising and ceasing of physical forms is free from clinging to physical forms. To be free from clinging to physical forms is to be free from the bonds of physical forms. To be free from the bonds of physical forms is to be free from clinging to physical forms.

“Likewise, that which is free from the bonds of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is free from clinging to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. That which is free from clinging to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is free from the bonds of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. That which is free from the bonds of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is free from the arising and ceasing of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. That which is free from the arising and ceasing of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is free from clinging to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. To be free from clinging to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is to be free from the bonds of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. To be free from the bonds of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is to be free from clinging to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. Subhūti, that is how there is freedom from clinging, due to seeing and knowing that all things are free of being clung to and free of being bound. Subhūti, that is how the bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a sanctuary for the world.

“How do those bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become an ultimate resort for the world? Subhūti, that which is the ultimate destination of physical forms is not physical forms.According to Hari (598), in the sense of reaching the highest point, “the ultimate destination” means the nonarising of physical forms, i.e., their lack of inherent nature, is “not the physical forms” at the conventional level. Hari: yat subhūte rūpasya pāraṃ na tad rūpaṃ iti prakarṣagamanārthena, rūpasya yat pāram anutpādo niḥsvabhāvatā na tad rūpaṃ bhavatī ti saṃvṛtyā. And yet, Subhūti, physical forms are just the way that the ultimate destination is. Likewise, Subhūti, that which is the ultimate destination of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is not feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. And yet, Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are just the way that the ultimate destination is. That is to say, all things are the ultimate destination, just as physical forms, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, if all things are the ultimate destination, just as physical forms, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are, then surely, Blessed One, the bodhisattva great beings would already be fully awakened to all things.According to Hari (598), “already awakened” means that due to (realizing) the nature of emptiness, “the bodhisattvas” would have understood “all things,” even now, just as someone on the stage of a buddha (would understand them). The idea (behind Subhūti’s question) is that, based on this fact, it would serve no purpose to apply oneself toward (accomplishing what is taught in) this scripture. Hari: abhisambuddhā eve ti idānīm api śūnyatārūpatvād buddhatvāvasthāyām ivādhigatāḥ sarvadharmās tasmād etatsūtrābhiyogo vyartha iti bhāvaḥ. How so? Blessed One, because, in that state, there are no conceptualizations.”According to Hari (599), he asks “how so” meaning how could they be awakened, because conceptualizations, which are by nature erroneous, still exist (for those bodhisattvas). To address this doubt, he explains the next sentence. Its meaning is that there is absolutely no conceptualization in the ultimate destination of physical form and so on, because the ultimate destination is the nature of the nonarising of conceptualization. Hari: bhrāntyātmaka­vikalpa­sadbhāvāt katham abhisambuddhā iti tat kasya hetor ity āśaṅkyāha : na hy atre tyādi. vikalpa­syānutpādarūpatvād rūpādipāre vikalpo naivāstīty arthaḥ.

“That is true, Subhūti. That is true,” said the Blessed One. “In that ultimate destination, there is no conceptualization. Hence, Subhūti, the bodhisattva great beings are already awakened to all things, because they have no conceptualizations.According to Hari (599), after the phrase “already awakened to all things” one should supply the phrase “only at the level of ultimate reality.” That said, the overall idea here is that, at the level of conventional reality, applying oneself to the (teachings in this) scripture is effective for removing the superimpositions to which one is attached since the beginning of time. Hari: abhisambuddhā eva bhavanti sarvadharmā iti tattvata eveti śeṣaḥ. saṃvṛtyā punar anādikālīna­samāropāpanayanāya sūtrābhiyogaḥ saphalo bhaved iti bhāvaḥ. Subhūti, the bodhisattva great beings still encounter that extreme difficulty, and hence they reflect deeply upon all things. But they neither actualize those things nor are deflated by them. They reflect as follows: ‘This is how we should awaken to all these things. After we realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, that is how we will teach and elucidate these things.’ Subhūti, that is how the bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become an ultimate resort for the world.

“How do those bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become an island for the world? Subhūti, those places delimited all around by water, either in the rivers or in the great oceans, are called islands. Subhūti, in this very way, physical forms are delimited all around by the past and the future. Likewise, Subhūti, in this very way, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are delimited all around by the past and the future. Through this delimitation, Subhūti, all things are delimited all around by the past and the future. Such a delimitation of all things, Subhūti, is peaceful, excellent, completely liberated, harmonious, and free of error. Subhūti, that is how the bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become an island for the world.

“How do those bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a light for the world? Subhūti, those bodhisattva great beings in this state, who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, radiate wisdom toward those sentient beings who have been blinded by mental darkness and ensnared within the eggshell-like veil of unawareness for a long time. In this way, the bodhisattva great beings eliminate what is obscuring the knowing of everything. Subhūti, that is how bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a light for the world.

“How do those bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a guide for the world? Subhūti, those bodhisattva great beings in that state, who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, teach the truth leading to what is naturally free from the arising and ceasing of physical forms.RK (F.141.b): anutpādaś cānirodhaś ceti samāhāradvandvaḥ| prakṛtiḥ sarvadharmāṇāṃ dharmatā| tayā teṣām anutpādānirodhāya dharmadeśanāt pariṇāyakāḥ pari to netāraḥ paramārthabodhakā iti pariṇāyakakāritram. Likewise, they teach the truth leading to what is naturally free from the arising and ceasing of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. Also, they teach the truth leading to what is naturally free from the arising and ceasing of the qualities of an ordinary person. They also teach the truth leading to what is naturally free from the arising and ceasing of the qualities of a disciple. They also teach the truth leading to what is naturally free from the arising and ceasing of the qualities of a solitary realizer. They also teach the truth leading to what is naturally free from the arising and ceasing of the qualities of a bodhisattva. They also teach the truth leading to what is naturally free from the arising and ceasing of the qualities of a buddha. They also teach the truth leading to what is naturally free from the arising and ceasing of all qualities of all things. Subhūti, that is how the bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a guide for the world.

“And how do those bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a resting place for the world?According to Hari (602), the “resting place of the world” means the basis (of everything), because it is a summary of three types of operative functions. Among those, he will explain below how (they serve as) the basis, due to effortlessness, activity, and benefiting beings in terms of teaching of the qualities within the rubric of the complete knowing of everything. First, he teaches the functioning of their effortlessness in the following sentences. Hari: lokasya gatir bhavantī ti tri­vidha­kāritra­vyāpāropasaṃhārād āśrayā bhavanti. tatra sarva­jñatādhikāra­dharma­daiśika­tvenānā­bhoga­pravṛtta­sattvārthopasamhārād āśrayā bhavantīty anābhogakāritraṃ tāvat kathayann āha: iha subhūta ityādi. Subhūti, those bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening teach people in the world the truth that physical forms rest upon space.It is difficult for an English translation to capture this passage’s play on two Sanskrit words, gati and gatikā, both of which can refer either to going to a destination or to the destination itself (in the sense of either a state of existence or a refuge). In line with the commentators’ explanations, the word gati is translated as “resting place” and the word gatikā as “rest upon” because the primary usages here are connected with the idea of a resting place in the sense of refuge. According to Hari (602), they “rest upon space” means they are dependent upon space. Hari: ākāśagatikam ity ākāśaniṣṭhaṃ. According to Ratnākara (F.141.b), since (going to) the “resting place” of a village just means arriving there, the resting place of the fruit of the three vehicles by going effortlessly also just means arriving there. But that resting place does not require effort, because a person does not go there physically or mentally, but arrives there, nevertheless, absolutely without effort. Thus, all things “rest upon space” because (coming or going to) the resting place of space does not involve coming or going to space, i.e., it is just (all things’) inherent nature. Therefore, they rest upon space means they are like space. RK: grāmasya hi gatiḥ prāptir eva| evam anābhogena gamanena yānatraya­phalasyāpi gatiḥ prāptir eva| sā tu nirābhogā| na hi pumān kāyena cetasā vā gantvā tat prāpnoti kintv ābhogata eva| tathā hy ākāśagatikāḥ sarvadharmāḥ| na hy ākāśasya gati r āgati r vā| svabhāva evāsya gatiḥ| tasmād ākāśagatikā ity ākāśasamāḥ. Likewise, those bodhisattva great beings teach people the truth that feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness rest upon space. Subhūti, in the very same way, all things rest upon space, in that they neither come to rest nor go to rest, just like space.According to Hari (602), “space” refers to the mere absence of a substance. It does “not come to rest” because there is no coming from a past time. It does “not go to rest” because there is no going to a future time. Hari: tatra: dravyābhāvamātram ākāśaṃ. atītāt kālād āgamanābhāvād anāgataṃ. anāgate ca kāle gativaikalyād agataṃ. Space is not something that has come or gone. It is not created, not transformed, not conditioned, not stationed, not fixed, and not established.According to Ratnākara (F.141.b–142.a), “not created” because it is not arisen. It is “not transformed” because it does not arise in another state. It is “not conditioned” because it is not created through causes and conditions being combined. It is the opposite of being “stationed” due to abiding after arising; of being “fixed” due to abiding everywhere; and of being “established” due to dwelling in anything at all. RK: akṛtam anutpāditatvāt| avikṛtam avasthāntarānutpādanāt| anabhisaṃskṛtaṃ hetupratyayaiḥ sambhūya tasyākaraṇāt| sthitam utpadya vṛtteḥ| saṃsthitaṃ sam antataḥ sthiteḥ| vyavasthitaṃ kvacid eva sthiteḥ| eṣāṃ viparyāsād āsthitam asaṃsthitam avyavasthitam | According to Hari (602), “not created” because an agentive cause does not exist. It is “not transformed” because there is no cause of its destruction. It is “not conditioned” because conditioning factors cannot be fixed. Due to lacking inherent nature in the three times, it is, respectively, “not stationed,” “not fixed,” and “not established.” Hence, it has “neither arisen nor ceased.” Hari: kārakahetor sattvād akṛtaṃ. vināśahetuvirahād avikṛtaṃ. sthāpakasaṃskārānupapatter anabhisaṃskṛtam. kālatraye svabhāvaviyogād yathākramam asthitaṃ, asaṃsthitaṃ avyavasthitaṃ. ataś cānutpannam aniruddham. It has neither arisen nor ceased. Likewise, all things have neither come nor gone. They are not created, not transformed, not conditioned, not stationed, not fixed, and not established. They have neither arisen nor ceased. And, in being like space, they are free of conceptualization.According to Hari (602), “in being like space, they are free of conceptualization” means because they are homogenous (like space), they lack conceptualization. Hari: ākāśakalpatvād avikalpā iti svatulyatvān nirvikalpāḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.142.a), “free of conceptualization” means free of effort, because things consist in only their true nature‍—this is the idea. RK: ākāśakalpatvād avikalpā nirābhogāḥ| dharmatāmātratvād dharmāṇām iti bhāvaḥ. Why is that? Subhūti, that which is the emptiness of physical forms neither comes nor goes. Likewise, that which is the emptiness of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness neither comes nor goes. Subhūti, in this very way, that which is the emptiness of all things neither comes nor goes. Why is that? Since all things rest upon emptiness, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon the freedom from signs, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon the freedom from inclinations, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon the freedom from conditioning, they do not depart from that resting place.According to Hari (603), all things have the nature of “emptiness” and so on (below), because their nature is not to arise. Their nature is “emptiness” because of being like an illusion; “free from signs” because all signs are lacking; “free of inclinations” because of going beyond the scope of desire; and “free of conditioning” because of being luminously clear by nature like the sky. “They do not depart from that resting place” means they do not go beyond that inherent nature. Hari: anutpādasvabhāvatvāt sarvadharmāḥ śūnyatādisvabhāvāḥ. tatra: māyopamatvāc chūnyatā. sarvanimittavigamād ānimittaṃ. prārthanā­viṣayātikrāntatvād apranihitaṃ. gagaṇavat prakṛti­prabhāsvaratvād anabhisaṃskāraḥ. tāṃ gatāṃ na vyativartanta iti taṃ svabhāvaṃ nātikrāmanti. Subhūti, since all things rest upon the freedom from arising, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon the freedom from birth, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon the freedom from any state of being, they do not depart from that resting place.According to Hari (604), all things rest upon “freedom from arising” because they are separated from any imagined suffering; “freedom from birth” due to lacking dependently constructed (vikalpita) suffering; and “freedom from any state of being” due to detachment from suffering in its true nature. Hari: kalpitaduḥkhavivekād anutpādagatikāḥ. vikalpitaduḥkhavirahād ajātāgatikāḥ. dharmatāduḥkhaviyogād abhāvagatikāḥ. Subhūti, since all things rest upon the dream, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon the self, they do not depart from that resting place.According to Hari (604), all things rest upon “the dream” because, insofar as the dream-like mere appearance is existing, what is imagined does not (actually) arise. They rest upon “the self” because, in the same way that a self is not found to exist because it is refuted by valid cognition, the arising of anything dependently constructed (vikalpita) does not exist. Hari: kalpitasamudayābhāvāt svapnopamapratibhāsamātrasya vidyamānatvena svapnagatikāḥ. yathātmā pramāṇabādhitatvān na vidyate tadvad vikalpitasamudayāsattvād ātmagatikāḥ. Subhūti, since all things rest upon the lack of limit, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon the null, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon nirvāṇa, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon nonremoval, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon not coming, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon not going, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon not moving, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon physical forms, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness, they do not depart from that resting place. Subhūti, since all things rest upon the state of a worthy one, the state of a solitary realizer, and the state of unsurpassed, perfect awakening, they do not depart from that resting place. For this reason, the bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening teach the truth that all things rest upon space. Subhūti, that is how the bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening become a resting place for the world.”
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, who knows this profound transcendent state of wisdom?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, one can be sure that those bodhisattva great beings who have maintained the practice and have fully cultivated the roots of virtue in the presence of previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas will come to know this profound transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.142.a), “maintained the practice” means they observe the practices, i.e., the six transcendent states. RK: caritāḥ ṣaṭpāramitā rakṣantīti caritāvinaḥ. Cf. BHSD caritāvin, “those who have pursued the religious course.”

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 37 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and the Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 47 in the Eighteen Thousand.Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, when those bodhisattva great beings come to know this profound transcendent state of wisdom, what will be their inherent nature?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, when those bodhisattva great beings come to know this profound transcendent state of wisdom, they will possess the inherent nature devoid of needing to withdraw.”According to Ratnākara (F.142.b), the Buddha is speaking here of those whose “inherent nature” is “devoid of needing” i.e., wishing, “to withdraw,” i.e., to heal. This describes their four “natures,” namely, (1) being devoid of emotional defilements, (2) being devoid of the marks of emotional defilements, (3) being devoid of the signs of emotional defilements, and (4) being devoid of counteracting antidotes. Among those, (1) “emotional defilements” refers to attachment and so on; (b) “marks of emotional defilements” refers to the negativities in the body and so on that have created the emotional defilements; (3) “signs of emotional defilements” refers to improper attentiveness and so on; and (4) “counteracting antidotes” refers to nonattachment for attachment, nonaggression for aggression, and nondelusion for delusion. RK: vinayo ’panayaḥ| tam arhantīti vainayikāḥ | tair viviktaḥ svabhāva eṣām iti tathoktāḥ| iyatā catvāraḥ svabhāvā uktāḥ| kleśavivekaḥ| kleśaliṅgavivekaḥ| kleśa­nimittavivekaḥ| vipakṣa­pratipakṣavivekaś ca| tatra kleśo rāgādiḥ| kleśaliṅgaṃ kleśakṛtaṃ kāyādidauṣṭhulyam| kleśanimittam ayoniśomanasikārādi| vivakṣa­pratipakṣau rāgārāgau dveṣādveṣau mohāmohau ca.

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, will the bodhisattva great beings alone be rested upon in this way? That is, after realizing just such a resting place, will they teach this very same resting place to sentient beings? In this way, will they become the resting place of sentient beings?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “So it is, Subhūti, so it is. The bodhisattva great beings will come to have this resting place alone. After having realized just this resting place, they will teach this very resting place to sentient beings. In this way, they will become the resting place for sentient beings. Subhūti, that is how the bodhisattva great beings who have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening will become the resting place for immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, such a bodhisattva great being performs difficult noble deeds, having donned the armor, thinking, ‘I will help immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings attain complete nirvāṇa.’ ”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “True, Subhūti, true. Such a bodhisattva great being performs difficult noble deeds, having donned the armor, thinking, ‘I will help immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings attain complete nirvāṇa.’In order to capture the play on words in the passage that follows, the Sanskrit term saṃnaddha is rendered in English here as both “donned” and “fastened”‍—as in the Tibetan, which uses the two translations bgos pa and ’brel pa. But, Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being who has donned the great armor does not fasten the armor to physical forms and does not fasten it for the sake of physical forms. Likewise, he or she does not fasten their armor to feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness, and does not fasten it for the sake of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. He or she does not fasten their armor to the level of a disciple, and they do not fasten it for the sake of the level of a disciple. He or she does not fasten their armor to the level of a solitary realizer, or for the sake of the level of a solitary realizer. He or she also does not fasten their armor to the level of a buddha, or for the sake of the level of a buddha. Why not? Ah! Subhūti, such a bodhisattva great being, who has donned the great armor, does not fasten his or her armor to anything.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, such a bodhisattva great being, who has donned the great armor and goes about life in the profound transcendent state of wisdom in this way, would not hope for the three possible states.According to Ratnākara (F.142.b), “would not hope for” means would not see it as something to be gained. Here, a bodhisattva should not see there to be “three” possible states. The idea is that a bodhisattva should see there to be only one (possibility, i.e., buddhahood). RK: na pratikāṃkṣitavyānī ti na labhyatvena draṣṭavyāni| atra trīṇi na draṣṭavyānīty ekam eva sthānaṃ draṣṭavyam iti bhāvaḥ. What are these three possible states? The level of a disciple, the level of a solitary realizer, and the level of a buddha.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, what is the purpose you see in saying as you did, ‘Such a bodhisattva great being, who has donned the great armor and goes about life in the profound transcendent state of wisdom in this way, would not hope for the three possible states. What are these three possible states? The level of a disciple, the level of a solitary realizer, and the level of a buddha’? Subhūti, the levels of the disciples and solitary realizers, indeed, are neither possible states nor appropriate places for such a bodhisattva great being who has donned the great armor and goes about life in profound transcendent state of wisdom in this way.According to Hari (609), not “possible states” means it is not a possibility, due to their lacking the causes (for such lesser attainments). Not “appropriate places” means they would have no occasion to do so, since such a fruition is not possible. Hari: hetuvaikalyād asthānam asambhavaḥ. phalāsambhavād anavakāśo ’navasaraḥ. On the other hand, since the bodhisattva great being has donned this armor for the sake of sentient beings, he or she would hope exclusively for the level of a buddha.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound. It cannot be cultivated by any means. No one has cultivated it. No one cultivates it. No one will cultivate it. There is nothing that one can cultivate and nowhere to cultivate it. Why not? Because, Blessed One, there is nothing actualized in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.143.a), “actualized” means like a real thing. RK: pariniṣpanno vastubhūtaḥ. According to Hari (610), “nothing is actualized” refers to the characteristics of an agent, an object, and an action and so on. Hari: na hi kaścid dharmaḥ pariniṣpanna iti kartṛkarmakriyādilakṣaṇaḥ. Blessed One, this cultivation of the transcendent state of wisdom, to be specific, is a cultivation of space. This cultivation of the transcendent state of wisdom, to be specific, is a cultivation of all things. This cultivation of the transcendent state of wisdom, to be specific, is a cultivation of freedom from attachment. This cultivation of the transcendent state of wisdom, to be specific, is a cultivation of the infinite. This cultivation of the transcendent state of wisdom, to be specific, is a cultivation of the nonexistent. This cultivation of the transcendent state of wisdom, to be specific, is a cultivation free of apprehending.”According to Ratnākara (F.143.a), it is a “cultivation of space” and “a cultivation of all things” because, insofar as the true nature alone is seen, there is no perception of all things. It is “a cultivation of freedom from attachment” and “a cultivation of the infinite” because it includes all things through the state of complete knowing. It is “a cultivation of the infinite” because nonperception is not obstructed toward all things and all things are infinite. It is “a cultivation of the nonexistent” because the grasped object is not perceived. It is “a cultivation free of apprehending” because the grasping subject is not perceived. RK: ataś cākāśabhāvanaiṣā sarvadharmabhāvanā ca dharmatāmātradarśanād dharmāṇāṃ cānupalabdheḥ| ata ev āsaṅga bhāvanaiṣā ’nantabhāvanā ca sarvadharmeṣv anupalabdher avyāghātād dharmāṇāṃ cānantyāt| asato bhāvanā ’sadbhāvanā grāhyānupalambhāt| aparigrahabhāvanā grāhakānupalambhāt. According to Hari (610), a “cultivation of space” means a cultivation like an illusion at the ultimate level. In the following sentences, he explains that very cultivation in terms of the divisions of the state of complete knowing of all things and so on. Among those, it is “a cultivation of all things” because the state of complete knowing of all things includes the nonarising of any realization. It is “a cultivation of freedom from attachment” because it trains in all paths without habitual fixation through the knowing of the path. It is “a cultivation of the nonexistent” because it has the nature of the special path through the comprehension of all aspects. It is “a cultivation free of apprehending” because it has the characteristic of the path to the conclusion through the realization of the culmination. Hari: tatra sarvākārajñatayā sarvābhisamayānutpāda­saṃgrahāt sarvadharmabhāvanā. mārgajñatayānabhiniveśena sarvamārgaśikṣaṇād asaṅgabhāvanā. sarva­jñatayāśeṣavastu­saṃgrahād anantabhāvanā. sarvākārābhi­sambodhena viśeṣamārgarūpatvād asadbhāvanā. mūrdhābhisamayena niṣṭhāmārgalakṣaṇatvād aparigrahabhāvanā.

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “That is true. Subhūti, that is true. Indeed, one should understand any bodhisattva great being who is abiding in the profound transcendent state of wisdom to be beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Subhūti, that bodhisattva great being does not give rise to habitual fixation upon this profound transcendent state of wisdom. He or she does not fixate upon anything preached by others or taught by others. He or she does not develop faith in others. Subhūti, when this profound transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, such a bodhisattva great being does not become deflated, depressed, discouraged, frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror. He or she does not become worried, doubtful, or confused. He or she fathoms the transcendent state of wisdom, has firm conviction in it, and rejoices in seeing and hearing it. Subhūti, one can be sure that such a bodhisattva great being also asked about the transcendent state of wisdom in the past. How so? Because, Subhūti, he or she is not frightened, not terrified, and not stricken with terror.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, when such a bodhisattva great being is not frightened, not terrified, and not stricken with terror as the profound transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, what is the perspective with which that bodhisattva great being reflects upon the transcendent state of wisdom?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, that bodhisattva great being reflects upon the transcendent state of wisdom with his or her mindstream inclined toward the state of complete knowing.”According to Ratnākara (F.143.a), “deeply reflected upon” means it is taken as meditative object. Each Sanskrit adjectival compound with the words “inclined,” “bent,” and “predisposed” implies a substratum (ādhāraḥ) that is the “mindstream,” i.e., the causal continuum of a mindset. RK: vyavacārite ty ālambitā| nimnaprāgbhārapravaṇa śabdā ādhārārthāḥ| taiḥ saha bahuvrīhiḥ| santatiś cittasantānaḥ.
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, how is the mindstream inclined toward the state of complete knowing deeply reflected upon?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, a mindstream inclined toward the state of complete knowing is deeply reflected upon by a mindstream inclined toward space, bent toward space, predisposed toward space. But, Subhūti, such a deep reflection with a mindstream inclined toward the state of complete knowing is the deep reflection of the transcendent state of wisdom.The words “transcendent state of wisdom” have been added here for clarity, following Ratnākara (F.143.a–b) who states, “After the phrase ‘such a deep reflection,’ we should add the words ‘of the transcendent state of wisdom.’ ” RK: iyaṃ sā vyavacāraṇe ti prajñāpāramitāyā iti śeṣaḥ. How so? Because, Subhūti, the state of complete knowing is immeasurable; the state of complete knowing is beyond measure. Subhūti, that which is immeasurable and beyond measure is not physical forms, not feelings, not conceptions, not conditioning factors, and not consciousness. It is not attainment, not realization, not comprehension, not the path, not the fruition of the path, not the knowing, and not the consciousness. It not a product, not a destruction, not an arising, not a dissolution, not a cessation, not a becoming, and not a disappearing. Also, it is not created by anything. It has neither come from anywhere nor gone to anywhere, nor does it remain in any place or in any location. Instead, it is considered immeasurable and beyond measure. The state of complete knowing is immeasaurable just as space is immeasurable. And, that immeasurability cannot be awakened by anything. It cannot be awakened by physical forms, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. It cannot be awakened by the transcendent state of generosity, the transcendent state of ethical discipline, the transcendent state of tolerant acceptance, the transcendent state of heroic perseverance, the transcendent state of meditation, or the transcendent state of wisdom. Why not? Because, Subhūti, the state of complete knowing is nothing but physical forms. Likewise, Subhūti, the state of complete knowing is nothing but feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. Indeed, Subhūti, the state of complete knowing is nothing but the transcendent state of generosity, the transcendent state of ethical discipline, the transcendent state of tolerant acceptance, the transcendent state of heroic perseverance, and the transcendent state of meditation, and nothing but the transcendent state of wisdom.”

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 38 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and the Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 48 in the Eighteen Thousand. In the long sūtras, the gods’ questions and the Buddha’s reply are considerably longer, but culminate in the mention of the Buddha’s initial reluctance to teach the profound Dharma he had discovered on awakening, as related in many of the accounts of his life.Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, together with the gods of the desire realm, and also Brahmā, the earth protector, with the gods of the form realm, approached the Blessed One. After approaching, they paid obeisance to the Blessed One’s feet with their heads. Then they circumambulated the Blessed One three times and stood to one side. Standing to one side, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, together with the gods of the desire realm, and also Brahmā, the earth protector, with the gods of the form realm, said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound. Blessed One, this transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to fathom. Blessed One, this transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to see. Blessed One, this transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to comprehend. After having just realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening through seeing its meaning while seated at the epicenter of awakening, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata’s mindset was inclined toward being free of concerns, not toward teaching the Dharma.”

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, together with the gods of the desire realm, and also Brahmā, the earth protector, with the gods of the form realm, “It is true. Gods, it is true. Ah! Gods, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound. Gods, this transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to fathom. Gods, this transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to see. Gods, this transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to comprehend. After having just realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening through seeing its meaning while seated at the epicenter of awakening, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata’s mindset was inclined toward being free of concerns, not toward teaching the Dharma. He thought, ‘I have indeed realized the profound truth. In it, no one has been awakened, no one is awakened, and no one will be awakened, and that is the profundity of this truth. This truth is profound just as space is profound. This truth is profound just as self is profound. This truth is profound just as all things not coming is profound. This truth is profound just as all things not going is profound. I have realized this truth.’ ”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, together with the gods of the desire realm, and also Brahmā, the earth protector, with the gods of the form realm, said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, it is wonderful. Sugata, it is amazing. The truth that you have taught is disbelieved by the whole world. And yet, Blessed One, this truth is taught so that people do not hold on to things, but rather go about life taking them up.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Gods,” the fifteenth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 16: The Actual NatureThe content of this chapter corresponds to all the remaining content of Chapter 38 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (beginning at 38.15) and the equivalent in the Hundred Thousand, and to all the remaining content of Chapter 48 in the Eighteen Thousand (beginning at 48.9).
div.p: Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Ah! Blessed One, this truth is taught to be free from the mental perception of any things.This chapter plays on different usages of the Sanskrit word dharma, meaning “truth,” “things,” “teachings,” “qualities,” and “virtues.” It also plays on several other words, as will be seen below. Ah! Blessed One, this truth is not obstructed in any direction. Ah! Blessed One, this truth has an unobstructed nature, because, insofar as it is like space, it is free from the mental perception of any places.The translation follows the glosses by RK: ataś ca apratihatalakṣaṇo ’pratihatasvabhāvaḥ| sarvapadā nāṃ sarvavastūnāṃ anupalabdhitaḥ. Ah! Blessed One, this truth is beyond any simile, because it has no counterpart.According to Hari (617), “beyond any simile” means beyond comparison. It has “no counterpart” because there is nothing like it. RK: apratimo nirupamaḥ advitīyatvād iti sadṛśābhāvāt. Ah! Blessed One, this truth has an unopposed nature because it lacks any opposition.According to Hari (617), “because it lacks any opposition” means because it has fully transcended all counteractions (needed to remove ignorance). The “unopposed nature” means the nature that is not counteracted. Hari: niṣpratyarthikatvād iti sarva­pratipakṣa­samatikrāntatvāt, apratilakṣaṇo ’pratipakṣalakṣaṇaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.144.a), it has an “unopposed nature” means it lacks the nature of a perception, because it “lacks any opposition” to it, i.e., it has no opposing thing (to perceive).
