RK: prati bodhakaṃ lakṣaṇam asye ti pratilakṣaṇaḥ| tadabhāvād apratilakṣaṇaḥ| niṣpratyarthikatvād iti virodharahitatvāt. Ah! Blessed One, this truth is not a locatable thing, because it has not truly come into being.According to Hari (617), “not truly come into being” means unborn. Hari: anabhinirvṛttatvād iti ajātatvāt. According to Ratnākara (F.144.a), “because it has not truly come into being,” i.e., there is absolutely no support anywhere for something that has not truly come into being. RK: anabhinirvṛttatvād iti| na khalv anabhinirvṛttasya kvacit pratiṣṭhā. Ah! Blessed One, this truth is not an arising, because all births are free of birth.According to Hari (617), it is “not arising because all births are free of birth,” i.e., because its nature is not found in any state of existence, such as the god realms and so on. Hari: sarvopapattyanupapattitvād iti devādisarvagatiṣv avidyamānarūpatvād anutpādaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.144.a), “not an arising” means it is free of arising. The reason is “because all births are free of birth.” To explain, there is no arising of something existent, because it cannot have the property of arising. There is also no arising of something nonexistent, because it is absolutely nonexistent, like the horns of a donkey. RK: yato ’nutpāda utpādarahitaḥ| kuto ’nutpādaḥ ? sarvotpattīnām anutpattitvāt| na hi satām utpattir utpattilakṣaṇāyogāt| nāpy asatām| asattvād eva kharaviṣāṇavat. Ah! Blessed One, this truth is not a pathway, because it is free from the mental perception of all paths.”According to Ratnākara (F.144.a), it is “not a pathway” because it is not a pathway, i.e., a road, to the lower births, higher births, or nirvāṇa. For that reason, it says “it is free from the mental perception of all paths.” RK: durgatipathaḥ sugatipatho nirvāṇapathaś ceti panthānaḥ| tadabhāvād apathaḥ | ata evāha| sarvapathānupalabdhitvāt.

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, and Brahmā, the earth protector, and those gods of the desire and form realms said to the Blessed One, “Ah! Blessed One, the elder disciple noble Subhūti is born after the Blessed One.According to Hari (618), using the phrase “born after” means to suggest that the “disciple” is a child of “the Blessed One” who is born after him, i.e., just like the Tathāgata. Hari: bhagavataḥ sambandhī śrāvako ’yam anu paścāj jātas tathāgata ivety anujātaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.144.a), in the phrase “born after,” the word “after” (anu) is in the sense of a similarity (i.e., he takes after the Blessed One). RK: anu ḥ sādṛśye. Note: To capture the implications within the play on the Sanskrit word anujātaḥ the Tibetan translates it here as “born after in a similar manner” (rjes su mthun par skyes), but later as “born after” (rjes su skyes pa). The English translation leaves “born after” throughout the chapter, since this phrase in English can have a similar range of implications. How so? Because, Blessed One, whenever the noble elder Subhūti teaches a truth, he teaches that truth from the starting point of emptiness.”According to Ratnākara (F.144.b), “how so” implies the question how does he have the quality of being “born after”? In the following response, the idea is that the son (i.e., Subhūti) is like the Blessed One (i.e., the father), because they both explain emptiness. RK: tat kasye tyādi tat kasyānujātatvaṃ? uttaraṃ tathā hī tyādinā| bhagavata eṣa putraḥ sadṛśaś ca śūnyatāvāditvād iti bhāvaḥ.

Then the venerable Subhūti addressed Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, Brahmā, the earth protector, and the gods of the desire and form realms as follows: “Gods, you are saying that this elder Subhūti is born after the Tathāgata.Subhūti’s quotation of Śakra’s statement ends here. But Subhūti continues to refer to himself in the third person throughout the rest of his explanation of their statement below. In being unborn, the elder Subhūti is born after the Tathāgata.According to Hari (618), “being unborn” means, ultimately, not having arisen. Hari: ajātatvād iti tattvenānutpannatvād. According to Ratnākara (F.144.b), with the words “in being unborn” he explains another implication of being “born after” him. The idea here is that the father and son are similar in terms of “being unborn.” You might ask, “If unborn, then how can they be father and son?” The idea is that they are father and son at the conventional level. RK: ajātatvād ity anujātatve hetum āha| ajātatvena pitāputrayoḥ sādṛśyād iti bhāvaḥ| yady ajātaḥ tau kathaṃ pitāputrau? saṃvṛtyeti bhāvaḥ. Note: Some readers may wonder whether a line of text was accidentally omitted here, because Subhūti’s mention of “being unborn” is not a direct reference to anything said above (as some might expect it to be). Although a scribal error could certainly have dropped a sentence about being “unborn” from the root texts at some point, one can easily justify the idea of “being unborn” here, as it is implied by the above words “not an arising” and “free of birth.” That is to say, the elder Subhūti has followed after the Tathāgata’s actual nature.According to Hari (618), Subhūti is the disciple “born after” and has “followed after”‍—i.e., attained or subsequently reached sameness with‍—the “actual nature” of that very Tathāgata. Hari: anujātas tathāgatasya śrāvakaḥ subhūtis tasyaiva tathatām anuyātah prāptas tādātmyenānugataḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.144.b), with the words “followed after,” he explains another implication (of being called “born after”). He has “followed after,” i.e., gone after “the Tathāgata’s actual nature.” Thus, he is born after the Tathāgata. The idea is that after following the Tathāgata, he is “born after,” i.e., after following him. RK: anuyāta ity ādinā hetvantaram āha| anuyāto ’ nugatas tathāgatasya tathatām| tato ’py anujātas tathāgatasya| anugamya jāto ’nujāta iti bhāvaḥ. Just as the Tathāgata’s actual nature has neither come nor gone, just so, Subhūti’s actual nature has neither come nor gone. In this very way, Subhūti has followed after the Tathāgata’s actual nature. From the very beginning, the elder Subhūti has followed after the Tathāgata’s actual nature.According to Ratnākara (F.144.b), one might ask whether he is still someone who has “followed after” once he has attained the state of a worthy one (arhat) or just before then? Subhūti addresses this by explaining it with the following sentence‍—“From the very beginning…”‍—and so on. RK: kiṃ punaḥ prāpte ’rhattve ’nuyātaḥ kiṃ vā pūrvam evety ata āha| ādita ityādi. Note: The Sanskrit editions and manuscripts have many typos confusing the two words anuyāta and anujāta, probably due to the close similarity of the syllables jā and yā in Middle Indic. In any event, here (and elsewhere), the English translation follows the evidence of the Tibetan Degé edition wherever it is clear. How so? The Tathāgata’s actual nature is the same as all things’ actual nature. All things’ actual nature is the same as the Tathāgata’s actual nature. Also, both the actual nature belonging to the Tathāgata and the actual nature belonging to all things is the very same actual nature that belongs to the elder Subhūti. That is the actual nature that the elder Subhūti has followed after and because of which he is born after the Tathāgata.According to Hari (619), “due to which he is born after the Tathāgata” means as consequence of attaining the actual nature, he is “born after” (the Tathāgata). Hari: ato ’nujāta iti tathatāprāptiyogād anujātaḥ. That said, while the Tathāgata’s actual nature is not the actual nature that is followed after, the elder Subhūti is indeed born after the Tathāgata,According to Ratnākara (F.144.b), it is “not the actual nature” because the term “actual nature” has no referent to be expressed. RK: atathate ti| tathatāśabdānabhidheyatvāt. According to Hari (617), to be precise, the phrase “not an actual nature” means that the “actual nature” too is an illusion-like convention, since a refutation without an object (to refute) is not possible. Hari: atathate ti nirviṣayapratiṣedhānupapattes tathatāpi sāṃvṛtī māyopameti yāvat. From here forward, the Tibetan begins to translate anujātaḥ simply as rjes su skyes pa (“born after”), with the subsequent implications of the term in mind. insofar as the continuum of the Tathāgata is the same as the continuum of the actual nature.According to Hari (620), one might ask how Subhūti could be “born after” since his nature, in reality, is the actual nature of all things. So, Subhūti begins to explain with this sentence. The “continuum” refers to the actual nature of a causal stream at the conventional level. Hari: tattvato dharmatārūpatve katham anujāta ity āha : tathāgatasye tyādi. sthitite ti sāṃvṛtyā prabandhadharmatā. According to Ratnākara (F.144.b), with these words he explains another implication of being “born after” him. The continuum of things (including consciousness) is the actual nature. Since this cannot be superseded, it is established as that (actuality). RK: anujātatve hetvantaram āha tathāgatasye tyādinā| tathatā hi dharmāṇāṃ sthitiḥ| anatikramaṇīyatvāt tathāvasthitā. The elder Subhūti is born after the Tathāgata with that continuum. Just as the Tathāgata’s actual nature is unchanging, free of change, nonconceptual, and free of conceptualization, so too Subhūti’s actual nature is unchanging, free of change, nonconceptual, and free of conceptualization.According to Ratnākara (F.144.b–145.a), it is “unchanging” because it continues in its former inherent nature; “free of change” because it does not arise in another inherent nature; “nonconceptual” because it does not have the inherent nature of conceptualization; and “free of conceptualization” because it does not have the objective scope of conceptualization. RK: yathe tyādinā| pūrvasvabhāvānuvṛtteḥ avikārā | svabhāvāntarānutpatteḥ nirvikārā | vikalpasvabhāvābhāvād avikalpā | vikalpāviṣayatvāt nirvikalpā. According to Hari (620), it is “unchanging” and “free of change” because, in it, there is, respectively, neither an increase nor a decrease, and it is “nonconceptual” and “free of conceptualization” because it is devoid of any conceptualization of self or other. Hari: tatrādhikanyūna­vikārābhāvād yathākramam avikārā nirvikārā, svaparavikalpaviyogād avikalpā nirvikalpā. Note: The Tibetan differs slightly from the Sanskrit reading seen by the commentators. Hence, insofar as he is unchanging, free of change, nonconceptual, and free of conceptualization, the elder Subhūti is born after the Tathāgata with that actual nature.

“Furthermore, just as the Tathāgata’s actual nature‍—in being unchanging, free of change, nonconceptual, and free of conceptualization‍—is not obstructed anywhere, so too the actual nature of all things is not obstructed anywhere.According to Ratnākara (F.145.a), it “not obstructed anywhere” because it is all pervasive. RK: na kvacit pratihanyata iti sarvagatatvāt. How so? There is only one single actual nature belonging to the Tathāgata and belonging to all things, that is to say, the nondual actual nature that is free of duality and free from creating duality.According to Ratnākara (F.145.a), it is “only one single” is the assertion. Someone might ask whether it is “single” due to belonging to a same category? He says no, it is “free of duality.” To explain, duality is a division. Due to lacking division, it is “free of duality”‍—meaning it just “one.” To this, someone might object that even “one single” (actual nature) must be divided by virtue of its relationships, in the same way that spaces to the east and west can be divided. Similarly, it must be a division to speak of the actual nature of physical forms, the actual nature of feelings, and the actual nature of conceptions. In response to this possible objection, Subhūti says it is “free from creating duality.” Also, for these two reasons (i.e., being “free of duality” and “free from creating duality”), it is called the “actual nature free of duality” (advayatathatā). Generally, it is called the “actual nature” because of being always exactly like that (actuality) (tathā). In the phrase “actual nature free of duality,” the description “free of duality” is used because that (actuality) is indivisible on its own, and the description “actual nature” is used because it is indivisible due to (its relationships with) others. RK: ekaivaiṣe ti pratijñā| tat kim ekajātīyatvād ekā ? nety āha| advaye ti| dvayaṃ bhedas, tadabhāvād advaye ty eko śabdasyārthaḥ| ekam api sambandhibhedād bhidyate| tadyathā pūrvākāśam aparākāśam iti| tadvad rūpatathatā vedanātathatā saṃjñātathatety eṣa bhedo bhavatīti ced āha| advaidhīkāre ti| ataś ca advayatathatā| nityaṃ tathaiveti tathatā| advayā ca svayam abhedāt tathatā ca parato ’py abhedād ity advayatathatā. Note: Ratnākara’s comments also clarify that the Sanskrit advayatathatā (i.e., “nondual actual nature”) is technically a descriptive determinative compound (karmadhāraya). The translation here follows the Sanskrit commentaries and takes it to be the predicate of the previous sentence, whereas the Tibetan translation treats it as the mere subject of the following sentence. That actual nature is not in anything. That actual nature is not the result of anything. Also, that actual nature is not a property of anything.According to Ratnākara (F.145.a), the actual nature is “not in anything” and “not the result of anything,” i.e., it is not something based in anything or caused by anything, like, for instance, water in a well or like a shoot of barley. Therefore, since it is not connected to anything, the actual nature is “not a property of anything.” Since it is not a property of anything, it is “a nondual actual nature free of duality and free of creating duality.” For this reason, it is (described above as) not obstructed anywhere. RK: na kvacid ityādinā| tat khalu tasya bhavati yad yadādhāraṃ yaddhetukaṃ vā| tadyathā kūpodakaṃ yavāṅkura iti| tathatā tu na kvacit nā pi kutaścit| tasmāt kvacid apratibaddhatvān na sā kasyacit| yataḥ sā na kasyacit tataḥ sā ’dvayā ’dvaidhīkārā ’dvayatathatā| tato na kvacit pratihanyate. Since that actual nature is not a property of anything, it is a nondual actual nature free of duality and free from creating duality. Thus, it is because of an uncreated actual nature that the elder Subhūti is born after the Tathāgata. Such an uncreated actual nature never becomes anything that is not the actual nature. Since that actual nature never becomes anything that is not the actual nature, it is a nondual actual nature free of duality and free from creating duality.According to Ratnākara (F.145.b), the “actual nature” is “uncreated” because it is permanent. It “never becomes anything that is not the actual nature” because it is always exactly like that (actuality) (tathā). Therefore, it is “nondual” (also in this sense) because it is indivisible by time divisions. RK: akṛtatathataye ti| akṛtā cāsau nityatvāt tathatā ca| na sā kadācin na tathate ti nityaṃ tathaiva bhāvāt| tataḥ sā ’dvaye ti kālabhedenāsyābhedāt. In this way, too, the elder Subhūti is born after the Tathāgata.

“Just as the Tathāgata’s actual nature is nonconceptual and free of conceptualization about anything anywhere, so too Subhūti’s actual nature is nonconceptual and free of conceptualization about anything anywhere.According to Ratnākara (F.145.b), “anywhere” means in all the world systems, and “anything” means in all things. It is “nonconceptual” because it does not conceptualize and “free of conceptualization” because it has eliminated all conceptualizations. RK: sarvatre ti sarvalokadhātuṣu| sarvadharmaṣv iti sarvavastuṣu| avikalpā teṣām avikalpanāt| nirvikalpā tathaiva sarvavikalpānāṃ prahāṇām. Note: Ratnākara’s comments also clarify that the Sanskrit akṛtatathatā is technically a descriptive determinative compound (karmadhāraya). The translation already takes this into account. It is true that Subhūti has been miraculously created out of the Tathāgata’s actual nature, but the actual nature of Subhūti and the Tathāgata is neither split up nor divided, because no divider is mentally perceived. In this way, too, the elder Subhūti is born after the Tathāgata.

“Just as the Tathāgata’s actual nature is not anywhere different from the actual nature of everything, so too Subhūti’s actual nature is not anywhere different from the actual nature of everything.According to Ratnākara (F.145.b), he explains another implication (of being called “born after”). It is not “anywhere different” means it is not something different from his not mentally perceiving a divider. RK: hetvantaram āha yathe tyādinā nānyatre ti nānyā bhedaka­tvānupalabdheḥ. Something that is not anywhere different from the actual nature of everything is not an actual nature that belongs to anything. It is merely the actual nature, that is to say, the actual nature of everything. The elder Subhūti has arrived at such an actual nature by becoming similar to that actual nature that is not anywhere different.According to Ratnākara (F.145.b), in this final phrase, “that actual nature” is “not anywhere different” because there is no second (reality). RK: dvitīyābhāvād ananyā cāsau tathatā ceti ananyatathatā. But, in doing so, no one has arrived at any similarity to anything.According to Ratnākara (F.145.b), “in doing so” means in such an encounter. “No one”‍—i.e., neither Subhūti nor anyone else‍—“has become similar to anything,” i.e., to the Tathāgata or anything else. The idea here derives from his seeing all things and persons as lacking identity. RK: atre ty asminn upagame| na kaścit subhūtir anyo vā| na kvacid iti tathāgate ’nyasmin vā ’nugatim upagataḥ| dharma­pudgala­nairātmya­darśanād iti bhāvaḥ. Cf. Hari: anugatim sadrṣatā. In this way, too, the elder Subhūti is born after the Tathāgata.

“Just as the Tathāgata’s actual nature is neither past, nor present, nor future, so too all things’ actual nature is neither past, nor present, nor future. Likewise, the elder Subhūti is said to be born after that actual nature and born after the Tathāgata, because it is due to the Tathāgata’s actual nature that he has assimilated to the actual nature. That is to say, it is due to the Tathāgata’s actual nature that he has assimilated to the past actual nature, and it is due to the past actual nature that he has assimilated to the Tathāgata’s actual nature. It is due to the Tathāgata’s actual nature that he has assimilated to the future actual nature, and it is due to the future actual nature that he has assimilated to the Tathāgata’s actual nature. It is due to the Tathāgata’s actual nature that he has assimilated to the present actual nature, and it is due to the present actual nature that he has assimilated to the Tathāgata’s actual nature. It is due to the Tathāgata’s actual nature that he has assimilated to the past, present, and future actual nature, and it is due to the past, present, and future actual nature that he has assimilated to the Tathāgata’s actual nature. Thus, indeed, Subhūti’s actual nature‍—which is the past, present, and future actual nature‍—and the Tathāgata’s actual nature are free of duality and free from creating duality. Likewise, Subhūti’s actual nature and all things’ actual nature is free of duality and free from creating duality. The actual nature that belonged to the Blessed One as a bodhisattva is the very same actual nature that belonged to the Blessed One when he realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Since it is due to that very same actual nature that this bodhisattva great being completely realized complete, perfect awakening, he comes to be called by the name ‘Tathāgata.’ ”According to Ratnākara (F.146.a), the implication here is that the word tathāgata (“arrived at the actuality”) refers to someone who “arrives at” (āgata) “the actual nature” (tathatā) or someone who knows actual nature, because any (Sanskrit verb) meaning “to go to” or “arrive at” means “to know.” RK: gamir jñānārthaḥ tathatāgatā ’nena tasmāt tathāgata iti bhāvaḥ.

Just at the very moment when the Tathāgata’s actual nature was being explicated, the earth underwent six changes, displaying the eighteen great signsIn this description of an auspicious earthquake, a modular element of many Buddhist texts, the Sanskrit uses six different verb roots each repeated with adverbial prefixes that establish three degrees of intensity for each. A literal translation would be: “The earth shook (akampat), shook excessively (prākampat), and shook completely excessively (saṃprākampat). Then, the earth trembled (acalat), trembled excessively (prācalat), and trembled completely excessively (saṃprācalat). Then, the earth cracked open (avedhat), cracked open excessively (prāvedhat), and cracked open completely excessively (saṃprāvedhat). Then, the earth rattled (araṇat), rattled excessively (prāraṇat), and rattled completely excessively (saṃprāraṇat). Then, the earth lurched (akṣubhyat), lurched excessively (prākṣubhyat), and lurched completely excessively (saṃprākṣubhyat). Finally, the earth thundered (agarjat), thundered excessively (prāgarjat), and thundered completely excessively (saṃprāgarjat).” Ratnākara (F.146.a) explains the “six changes (ṣaḍvikāram) displaying the eighteen great signs” when a tathāgata’s actual nature is being explicated, saying that among the six changes, three verbs refer to movements, and three verbs refer to sounds. By dividing these six into mild, medium, and strong, there arise “eighteen signs.” Among these, the three movements are (1) trembling in its parts, (2) shaking entirely, and (3) lurching, i.e., trembling absolutely profusely. The sounds are (4) cracking open, (5) rattling like a cup hit with a stick, and (6) thundering like fresh rain clouds. Six changes are also described (as an additional passage in many texts, but not here) as follows: “The earth rode up in the east and plunged down in the west. It rode up in the west and plunged down in the east. It rode up in the south and plunged down in the north. It rode up in the north and plunged down in the south. It rode up in the center and plunged down at the perimeters. It rode up at the perimeters and plunged down in the center.” RK: asyāṃ deśyamānāyāṃ yad adbhutam abhūt tad āha| asyām ityādinā| ṣaḍavikāram aṣṭādaśamahānimittaṃ yathā bhavati tathā akampata yāvat saṃprāgarjat| atra trayo dhātavaś calanārthāḥ| trayaḥ śabdārthāḥ| mṛdumadhyādhimātra­kriyābhedād aṣṭādaśamahānimittāni bhavanti| tatra cala nam aṅgataḥ| kampa naṃ sākalyena| sākalyenātyantaṃ kampanaṃ kṣobha ḥ| vedha ḥ śabdaḥ| raṇ itaṃ daṇḍāhatakāṃsīvat| garja naṃ navamahāmeghavat| ṣaḍvikārās tadyathā-“pṛthivyāḥ pūrvā digunnamati, paścimā ’vanamati| paścimā digunnamati, pūrvā ’vanamati| [dakṣiṇā digunnamati], uttarā ’vanamati| uttarā digunnamati, dakṣiṇā ’vanamati| madhye unnamati|ante ’vanamati| ante unnamati madhye ’vanamati” iti. of a tathāgata realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening.The genitive word “of” should mean “like” here. Cf. RK: kiṃvad ityāha| tathāgatasya ce tyādi. That is to say, the earth shook, shuddered, and juddered. Then, the earth rocked, reeled, and tottered. Then, the earth creaked, crackled, and cracked. Then, the earth rattled, clattered, and crashed. Then, the earth lurched, careened, and convulsed. Finally, the earth rumbled, roared, and thundered.

Again, the elder Subhūti addressed the gods: “Gods, Subhūti is born after the Tathāgata in this very way. Nevertheless, the elder Subhūti is not born after physical forms. He is not born after feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. He is not born after the fruition of a stream enterer, the fruition of a once-returner, the fruition of a non-returner, the fruitional state of a worthy one, the fruitional state of a solitary realizer, or the fruitional state of a buddha. Why not? Because no things are observed or mentally perceived that could be born after a tathāgata, or that could cause him to be born after themselves.According to Ratnākara (F.146.a), “no things are observed” is the major premise and no things “are mentally perceived” is the inferential reason (that proves the premise). The things “that could be born after” the Tathāgata are his own qualities. The things “that could cause him to be born after themselves” are actions. RK: na saṃvidyanta iti pratijñā| nopalabhyanta iti hetuḥ| yair iti svadharmaiḥ| anujāyete ti kartari liṅ| ye ce ti tāthāgatā dharmāḥ| anujāyerann iti karmaṇi liṅ. Note: Ratnākara’s comments also explain the optative verbs here as being, respectively, agentive and objective. In this way, too, the elder Subhūti is born after the Tathāgata.”

Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, it is a profound way of going about life, this actual nature.”According to Ratnākara (F.146.a), one might ask how is the “actual nature” a “practice”? It becomes such, through remaining in nothing but that. RK: tathatā kathaṃ caryā? tanmātre ’vasthānāt.
At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Śāriputra, “True. Śāriputra, it is true. It is a profound way of going about life, this actual nature.”

Right when this explication of the actual nature was being given, the mindsets of three hundred monks became free of clinging and liberated from worldly influences. The Dharma eye concerning things of five hundred nuns became purified, free from passion, and free from impurities. Also, five thousand gods, who had previously prepared themselves for it, attained the state of acceptance that all things are free of arising. Also the mindsets of the bodhisattvas became free of clinging and liberated from worldly influences.

Śāriputra then knew that the mindsets of the bodhisattvas had become free of clinging and liberated from worldly influences. So he asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, what is the cause or condition for the mindsets of these bodhisattvas to become free of clinging and liberated from worldly influences?”

The Blessed One said, “Śāriputra, it is that these bodhisattvas previously served five hundred buddhas, and they practice generosity, preserve ethical discipline, engage in tolerant acceptance, undertake heroic perseverance, and attain the meditative states. But, they were not supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and lacked the skill in means. Śāriputra, even though these bodhisattvas had a path that was emptiness, went about life in the freedom from signs, and paid attention in the freedom from inclinations, they came to actualize the pinnacle of reality and went beyond cyclic existence at the level of a disciple, not at the level of a buddha, because they lacked the skill in means.

“Śāriputra, suppose, as an analogy, that a massive bird‍—with a physical body two hundred miles, four hundred miles, six hundred miles, eight hundred miles, or one thousand miles long‍—was living among the gods in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, but wished to go to the Jambu continent, even though its wings were unformed, atrophied, or broken. With this intention to set out for the Jambu continent, suppose that the bird were to depart from the assembly of the gods in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three. But after initially descending, suppose that it were to get stuck midway in the intermediate space between heaven and earth and then think, ‘Oh no! I should return to the Heaven of the Thirty-Three.’ What do you think, Śāriputra? Would that massive bird have the strength to return to the Heaven of the Thirty-Three?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” replied the venerable Śāriputra.

The Blessed One said, “Suppose instead it were to think, ‘Alas! I can still continue uninjured and unharmed to the Jambu continent.’ What do you think, Śāriputra? Should that massive bird set out for the Jambu continent?”

“Definitely not, Blessed One,” replied Śāriputra. “He would become injured and harmed. He would fall to the Jambu continent either to meet his death or to suffer on the brink of death. Why is that? Blessed One, the fact is that his physical form is massive, his wings are inadequate, and he is falling from a high place.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Śāriputra, “You are right, Śāriputra. You are right. Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being could develop the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening and then remain for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges, practicing generosity, preserving ethical discipline, engaging in tolerant acceptance, undertaking heroic perseverance, and attaining the meditative states. With the development of the proper mindset, he or she could even set out on the great journey for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Nevertheless, if such a bodhisattva great being is not supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and lacks the skill in means, then he or she will fall to the level of a disciple or the level of a solitary realizer.

“Furthermore, such a bodhisattva great being pays attention to the ethical discipline, concentration, wisdom, liberation, and seeing the knowing of liberation of the past, future, and present blessed buddhas. But such a bodhisattva great being maintains these by means of signs, and hence does not understand or see the ethical discipline of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. He or she also does not understand or see the concentration, the wisdom, the liberation, or the seeing the knowing of liberation of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. Because he or she does not understand or see them, such a bodhisattva great being does not hear the word emptiness. Or else, he or she mentally turns the word into a conventional sign and, after doing so, wishes to dedicate everything to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Because of this, one can be sure that he or she will become stuck on the level of a disciple or the level of a solitary realizer. Why is that? Because, Śāriputra, such a bodhisattva great being is not supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and lacks the skill in means.”

Śāriputra said, “As I understand the implication of what the Blessed One is saying, it is doubtful, Blessed One, that any bodhisattva great being who is not supported by the transcendent state of wisdom, lacks the skill in means, and lacks a virtuous friend will attain unsurpassed, perfect awakening, even if he or she possesses the great requisite of merit.According to Ratnākara (F.146.b), here ends the defining characteristics of the knowing aspects, distinctive qualities, functions, and natures (i.e., the fifth subtopic of the Ornament’s fourth topic, realizing all the aspects). Now the factors conducive to liberation will be taught. In order to make clear what is implied by the explanation of the things some bodhisattvas lack, he begins, “As I understand,” and so on. RK: iha jñāna­viśeṣakāritra­svabhāva­lakṣaṇe ukte| bodhisattvānāṃ mokṣabhāgīyaṃ vaktavyam| tac ca tadvirahiṇāṃ bodhisattvānām arhattva­prāptikāraṇa­nirdeśena sūcitaṃ vyaktikartuṃ| śāriputra āha| yathāhaṃ bhagavann ityādi. Therefore, Blessed One, any bodhisattva great being wishing to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening should cultivate the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means.”According to Ratnākara (F.146.b), he or she should “cultivate the transcendent state of wisdom” in order to know the ultimate reality of all things and in order not to create conventional signs while going about life in the transcendent states of generosity and so on. Regarding the phrase “skill in means,” the means is never abandoning the mindset of knowing of all aspects and perfectly dedicating all merit to awakening. RK: prajñāpāramitā bhāvayitavye ti sarva­dharma­paramārtha­jñānāya| caryamāṇasya ca dānapāramitāder animittīkaraṇārtham| upāyakuśalene ty upāye kuśalena| tatropāyaḥ sarvākāra­jñatā­cittasya nityam atyāgaḥ| sarvapuṇyānāṃ ca bodhau samyakpariṇāmanā.

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Śāriputra, “Yes, Śāriputra. Yes. It is doubtful, Śāriputra, that any bodhisattva great being who is not supported by the transcendent state of wisdom, lacks the skill in means, and lacks a virtuous friend will attain unsurpassed, perfect awakening, even if he or she possesses the great requisite of merit. Therefore, Śāriputra, any bodhisattva great being wishing to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening should cultivate the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, together with those gods of the desire realm and the earth protector Brahmā, and together with those in the form realm, said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound. Blessed One, unsurpassed, perfect awakening is difficult to attain. Blessed One, realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening is something extremely difficult to attain.”

At this, the Blessed One said to those gods of the desire realm headed by Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, and to those gods of the form realms headed by the earth protector, great Brahmā, “Yes, gods. Yes. The transcendent state of wisdom is profound. Gods, unsurpassed, perfect awakening is difficult to attain. Gods, realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening is something extremely difficult to attain for those who have inferior wisdom, lackluster perseverance, insufficient conviction, no skill in means, and unvirtuous friends that they closely attend to.”

Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “As the Blessed One has said, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound. Unsurpassed, perfect awakening is difficult to attain. Realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening is something extremely difficult to attain. But, Blessed One, how could perfect awakening be difficult to attain? How could realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening be something extremely difficult to attain, given that no one is awakened in it? Why not? Since, Blessed One, all things are empty, nothing is observed or able to become awakened. That is to say, Blessed One, all things are empty. This Dharma was taught in order to relinquish a thing that is not observed. Likewise, anyone who could know or realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and anything that could be known or realized‍—all these things are empty. Therefore, Blessed One, from this perspective, it seems to me that unsurpassed, perfect awakening is easy to attain, not difficult to attain.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, since unsurpassed, perfect awakening is a nonoccurrence, it is difficult to attain. Subhūti, since unsurpassed, perfect awakening is not really existent, it is difficult to attain. Subhūti, since unsurpassed, perfect awakening is nonconceptual, it is difficult to attain. Subhūti, since unsurpassed, perfect awakening is unfabricated, it is difficult to attain. Realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening is something extremely difficult to attain.”

Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the venerable Subhūti, “Venerable Subhūti, from this perspective, unsurpassed, perfect awakening is difficult to attain. Realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening is extremely difficult to attain. Why is that? Venerable Subhūti, space does not think, ‘I will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’ Yet, in this way, such things are to be realized. How is this so? Because, Venerable Subhūti, all things are like space. Also, if, Venerable Subhūti, unsurpassed, perfect awakening were easy to attain, then as many bodhisattvas as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges would not be turning away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. But since as many bodhisattvas as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges are turning away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening, Subhūti, it is understood that unsurpassed, perfect awakening is difficult to attain. Realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening is extremely difficult to attain.”
At this, the venerable Subhūti asked Śāriputra, “Śāriputra, do physical forms turn away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, is the thing that turns away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening somewhere other than physical forms?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, do feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness turn away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, is the thing that turns away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening somewhere other than feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, is it the actual nature of physical forms that turns away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.

Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, is it the actual nature of feelings, the actual nature of conceptions, the actual nature of conditioning factors, or the actual nature of consciousness that turns away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, is the thing that turns away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening somewhere other than the actual nature of physical forms?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.

Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, is the thing that turns away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening somewhere other than the actual nature of feelings, the actual nature of conceptions, the actual nature of conditioning factors, or the actual nature of consciousness?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, do physical forms realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, do feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, does any thing other than physical forms realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, does any thing other than feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, does the actual nature of physical forms realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.

Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, does the actual nature of feelings, the actual nature of conceptions, the actual nature of conditioning factors, or the actual nature of consciousness realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, does a thing somewhere other than the actual nature of physical forms realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.

Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, does a thing somewhere other than the actual nature of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, should physical forms be realized in unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, should feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness be realized in unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, should anything anywhere other than physical forms be realized in unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, should anything anywhere other than feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness be realized in unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, should the actual nature of physical forms be realized in unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.

Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, should the actual nature of feelings, the actual nature of conceptions, the actual nature of conditioning factors, or the actual nature of consciousness be realized in unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, should a thing somewhere other than the actual nature of physical forms be realized in unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.

Subhūti asked, “Then, Venerable Śāriputra, should anything anywhere other than the actual nature of feelings, the actual nature of conceptions, the actual nature of conditioning factors, or the actual nature of consciousness be realized in unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, what do you think, Venerable Śāriputra? Does the actual nature turn away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Then, what do you think, Venerable Śāriputra? Is there anything in the actual nature that turns away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.

“Then what, Venerable Śāriputra, is the thing that turns away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening?” asked Subhūti. “What is it that dwells in that true nature of things alone by the method of being free from dwelling on anything?According to Ratnākara (F.147.b), he dwells in the true nature of things “by the method of being free from dwelling on anything,” i.e., by means of not dwelling on all things. The “true nature of things alone,” i.e., when pure, means awakening, and hence the basic reason (for dwelling in that alone) is that it is pure by nature. RK: sarva­dharmāsthānayogene ti| sarvadharmaṣv asthānam eva yogo nyāyaḥ| tena dharmatāyāṃ sthitaḥ| dharmataiva śuddhā bodhiḥ| sā ca prakṛtyaiva śuddheti bhāvaḥ. Furthermore, Śāriputra, what is the thing that is the actual nature? Or, Venerable Śāriputra, might it be the actual nature that is turned away?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.

Subhūti then asked, “Venerable Śāriputra, among all the things that are not being mentally perceived as a reality or as a continuum, what is the thing that is turned away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”

At this, the venerable Śāriputra said to the venerable Subhūti, “From the perspective of the reality of things, through which the elder, the venerable Subhūti, explicates the topic, nothing turns away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.147.b), “the perspective of the reality of things” means the mode of examining the ontological nature of things. RK: dharmanayajātiḥ dharmāṇāṃ tattvaprakāraḥ. Nonetheless, Venerable Subhūti, the Tathāgata described three persons belonging to the bodhisattva path. According to the venerable Subhūti’s explication, these three persons are not separately established, because only one single path, namely, the path of the buddha, is the path of the bodhisattva.”

Then Pūrṇa, son of Maitrāyaṇī, asked the venerable Śāriputra, “Venerable Śāriputra, does the elder Subhūti not accept even a single bodhisattva as belonging to the disciple path, the path of a solitary realizer, or the great path? The elder Venerable Śāriputra should be asked this first.”

Then the venerable Śāriputra asked the venerable Subhūti, “Venerable Subhūti, do you not accept even a single bodhisattva as belonging to the disciple path, the path of a solitary realizer, or the great path?”

Subhūti asked in return, “Venerable Śāriputra, in the actual nature of the actual nature, do you identify even a single bodhisattva who belongs to a disciple path, a path of a solitary realizer, or a great path?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra. “The actual nature is not even mentally perceived as having three aspects‍—how much less as a bodhisattva.”
Subhūti asked, “Venerable Śāriputra, is the actual nature mentally perceived as even having a single aspect?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.
Subhūti asked, “Venerable Śāriputra, do you perhaps identify in the actual nature even a single quality of a bodhisattva?”
“Definitely not, Venerable Subhūti,” said Śāriputra.

Subhūti asked, “Venerable Śāriputra, although a quality of a bodhisattva is not being mentally perceived as a reality or as a continuum, why do you have the idea that this person belongs to the disciple path, that person to the path of a solitary realizer, and another person to the great path? Venerable Śāriputra, if a bodhisattva great being hears that these three persons who appear distinct are not different, are free of distinctions, and are undifferentiable in terms of their actual nature, but his or her mindset does not become deflated, depressed, or discouraged, then one can be sure that he or she will go beyond cyclic existence by means of awakening.”

Then the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Excellent, Venerable Subhūti, excellent. It is exactly so, Subhūti, exactly so. Subhūti, just as it is illuminating to you through the Tathāgata’s miraculous power and you are eloquently explaining it through the Tathāgata’s blessings, just so, if a bodhisattva great being hears that these three persons who appear distinct are not different, are free of distinctions, and are undifferentiable in terms of their actual nature, but his or her mindset does not become deflated, depressed, or discouraged, then one can be sure that he or she will go beyond cyclic existence by means of awakening.”
Then the venerable Śāriputra asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, by means of what awakening will such a bodhisattva great being go beyond cyclic existence?”
The Blessed One said, “Śāriputra, such a bodhisattva great being will go beyond cyclic existence by means of unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”

Then the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, if any bodhisattva great being wishes to go beyond cyclic existence to unsurpassed, perfect awakening, then how should that bodhisattva great being dwell? How should that bodhisattva great being train?”According to Ratnākara (F.148.a), the question is how a bodhisattva should “dwell” in terms of being disposed toward them and “should train” in terms of repeated practice. RK: sthātavyam iti spṛhaṇataḥ| śikṣitavyam abhyāsataḥ.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, if any bodhisattva great being wishes to go beyond cyclic existence to unsurpassed, perfect awakening, then that bodhisattva great being should dwell in impartiality toward all beings. He or she should develop an impartial mindset toward all beings. He or she should not regard others with an unimpartial mindset. He or she should regard others with a benevolent mindset. He or she should regard others with the mindset of benefiting.

“That bodhisattva great being should regard others with a positive mindset. He or she should regard others with a humble mindset. He or she should regard others without a hateful mindset. He or she should regard others without a harmful mindset. He or she should regard others without a hurtful mindset.

“That bodhisattva great being should regard others by affectionately conceiving of them as a mother, father, son, or daughter. In this very way, the bodhisattva great being who wishes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening should dwell in such regard for all beings. He or she should train with the thought, ‘I am the protector of all beings.’ Also, he or she should dwell in ending wrongdoing by him- or herself. He or she should practice generosity, preserve ethical discipline, engage in tolerant acceptance, undertake heroic perseverance, attain the meditative states, get familiar with wisdom, and examine dependent arising, in order and in reverse. He or she should also incite others to take on these practices, speak highly of them, and show them approval for doing so. In this way, he or she should dwell in the attainment of a bodhisattva’s irreversible fault-free state and in the maturing of beings up to the point of realizing the noble truths.According to Ratnākara (F.148.b), the “noble truths” means the (four) noble truths of suffering and so on as the objects that are, respectively, fully acknowledged, eliminated, actualized, and cultivated when realizing the five resultant wisdoms as a stream enterer and so on, up to the point of “the attainment of a bodhisattva’s irreversible fault-free state.” RK: satyeṣv iti duḥkhādisatyaviṣayeṣu yathākramaṃ pari­jñāna­prahāṇasākṣātkriyābhāvanāsu strotaāpattyādipañca­phala­jñānasākṣātkriyāyāṃ yāvad bodhisattvanyāmāvakrāntau. Note: Ratnākara’s commentary on the above paragraph explains the previous steps on the path leading up to “attainment of a bodhisattva’s irreversible fault-free state” (bodhi­sattva­nyāmāvakrānti)‍—i.e., the path of insight (darśanamārga)‍—which is effectively the bodhisattva’s irreversible state of perfection (samyaktva­niyāmāvakranti). But these comments have not been included here due to requiring additional contextualization beyond the scope of these notes. The translation “irreversible fault-free state” (nyāma) makes an attempt to account for the well-known play on the Sanskrit word nyāma, meaning irreversible (ni+yāma) and fault-free (ni+āma). Cf. BHSD niyāma, nyāma. Then, he or she should also incite others to take on these practices, speak highly of them, and show them approval for doing so. In this way, a bodhisattva who yearns for this, and trains in it, will encounter no obstructions in anything from physical forms up to the continuum of Dharma.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Actual Nature,” the sixteenth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 17: The Attributes, Indications, and Signs of Those Beyond RegressingThe beginning of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 39 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 49 in the Eighteen Thousand. From the point marked by n.909, the chapter goes on to include content corresponding to the whole of Chapter 40 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 50 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: Then the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, what are the attributes, indications, and signs of a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing? Or, Blessed One, how else can we know that such a bodhisattva great being is beyond regressing?”According to Ratnākara (F.149.a), here “attributes” refers to that with which things appear or manifest; “indications” refers to that through which things are approached or gone toward; “signs” refers to that through which things are examined or evaluated. Even though the three (terms) have a single referent, the underlying basis is piled up with synonyms (paryāya) in order to individually remove the doubts of different beings. Alternatively, it is (just) another repetition (paryāya) (i.e., distinctions without a difference). For this very reason, in the question “how else” and so on, the phrase “or else” (vā) should be connected with “how,” meaning by what means (other than the characteristics, etc.) can we recognize them. RK: ākriyante vyajyanta ebhir iti ākārāḥ| liṅgyante gamyanta ebhir iti liṅgāni| nimīyante nirūpyanta ebhir iti nimittāni| ekārthatve pi trayāṇām upādānanaṃ sattvāntaraśaṅkāyā vyavacchedārtham paryāyaskandham| athavā aparaḥ paryāyaḥ| katham ityādi| ata eva vā śabdaḥ| kathaṃ ve ti kena prakāreṇa jānīyāma iti sambandhaḥ. Note: Earlier Ratnākara distinguished these same Sanskrit terms with a slightly different meaning. An “attribute” (ākāraḥ) is the defining characteristic, like, for instance, the dewlap of a cow and so on. An “indication” (liṅgam) is an artificial mark, like, for instance, the bell on a cow. A “sign” (nimittam) is an inherent quality, like, for instance, the whiteness of a (white) cow.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, the ground of an ordinary person, the ground of a disciple, the ground of a solitary realizer, and the ground of a buddha are all called the ground of the actual nature, because, in their actual nature, they all are free of duality, free from creating duality, nonconceptual, and free of conceptualization.According to Ratnākara (F.149.a), the Sanskrit word ca here is used in the sense of a reason (and translated as “because”) meaning since, “in their actual nature, they all are free from duality.” “They all” are just the actual nature, because all things, such as physical forms and so on, do not exist in the manner of observable characteristics, and hence the actual nature is neither a single thing, nor a “duality,” nor a plurality. For this reason, “free from duality” means not separate from the actual nature; “free from creating duality” means not divided from it; “nonconceptual” is in the sense of lacking the inherent nature of conceptualization; and “free of conceptualization” is because it is beyond the scope of conceptualization. “That actual nature” that has such characteristics is “arrived at” as “the true nature of things.” RK: sarvāś ca ityādi| ca śabdo hetau| yasmāt sarvā etās tathatayā ’dvayāḥ| rūpādīnāṃ sarvadharmāṇāṃ yathālakṣaṇam asattvāt tathataivaitāḥ| tathatā caikaiva na dve na bahavyaḥ| tasmāt tathatayā ’dvayā abhinnāḥ| advaidhīkārā acchinnāḥ| vikalpasvabhāvavirahāt avikalpāḥ | vikalpāgocaratvāt nirvikalpāḥ itīti| evaṃlakṣaṇā yā tathatā tāṃ tathatāṃ dharmatām avatarati. Hence each of those beings arrives at the actual nature as the true nature of things.According to Ratnākara (F.149.b), this sentence explains how they arrive. “When they dwell in” this actual nature‍—meaning because they see nothing but that‍—they “do not conceptualize it” accurately or “imagine it” in a false way. They arrive at it “in this way” i.e., this being so. RK: katham avataratīty āha tathatāyām ityādi| sthitas tanmātradarśanāt| na kalpayati samyak na vikalpayati vitatham| evam avataratī ty evaṃsaty avatarati. When such individuals dwell in this actual nature, they do not conceptualize it or imagine it, and in this way they arrive at it. But even when they just hear how to arrive at that actual nature and subsequently drift away from it, they know what it is not like, and hence they never become worried, perplexed, doubtful, or confused.According to Ratnākara (F.149.b), “to arrive at that actual nature” refers to what is arrived at through the steps explained. “Even when they just hear (śrutvāpi)” means right after they hear. “Subsequently” means also at a later time. They do not become “worried” means they do not object to it, in connection with their knowing “what it is not like.” “Perplexed” (vimati) should be construed heuristically as not conjuring up “different (vi) thoughts (mati)” about it, i.e., either what it is like or not like. For this reason, he says such individuals are not “doubtful or confused,” i.e., as a refutation of his misunderstanding. RK: evam avatīrṇa ityādi| uktena krameṇāvatīrṇaḥ san| yathe ti yayā mātrayā śrutvāpī ti śravaṇānantaram api| tato pi vātikramye ti tata uttarakālam api| na kāṅkṣatī ti na bādhate| naivam itī ti sambandhaḥ| vi vidhā matiḥ vimatiḥ| tan na karoti| evaṃ vā na veti| ata evāha na vicikitsatī ti| na dhandhāyatī ty apratipatteḥ pratiṣedhaḥ. Instead, they have firm conviction and immerse themselves in the fact that the actual nature is exactly the way it is.According to Ratnākara (F.149.b–50.a), “in the fact that” everything is just the actual nature and there are no physical forms and so on, they have “firm conviction” based on their faith and “immerse themselves” based on their understanding. RK: api tv ityādi| tathataiva sarvaṃ na santi rūpādaya ity adhimuñcati śraddhayā| avagāhate prajñayā. For this reason, they never ramble on about anything whatsoever, but rather speak words that are beneficial‍—as opposed to unbeneficial‍—and do not look down upon anything that others have done or not done. Subhūti, one can be certain that bodhisattva great beings possessing these attributes, indications, and signs are beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings do not look up to other renunciants or brahmins with the thought, ‘These blessed renunciants or brahmins know what is to be known and see what is to be seen.’According to Ratnākara (F.150.a), “they do not look up to,” i.e., do not view with reverence, the “other renunciants,” i.e., those ascetics who draw one away from these teachings. He explains what that means with the next phrase: “thinking” that these renunciants “know” through worldly knowing or “see” through spiritual knowing. RK: anyeṣām iti| ito dharmād vāhyānām śramaṇānām iti pravrajitānām| mukham ullokayati praṇataḥ paśyati| kim itīty āha| ime ityādi| jānanti laukikena jñānena| paśyanti lokottareṇa. Such bodhisattva great beings neither pay homage to different gods, nor think to give the different gods any flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, or lamps, nor do they depend on different gods.According to Ratnākara (F.150.a), they do “not give” any donations out of devotion. They do “not depend” in the sense that they do not serve them or seek refuge in them (instead of in the Three Jewels). RK: dātavyam iti bhaktito deyam| vyapāśrayata iti sevate śaraṇaṃ vā gacchati. Subhūti, one can be certain that bodhisattva great beings possessing these attributes, indications, and signs are also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Also, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing are neither reborn in the lower realms nor take the form of a woman.According to Hari (667), explicitly the Buddha is saying that such bodhisattvas would not be “reborn in the lower realms,” i.e., have a rebirth in a hell, hungry ghost, or animal realm, but implicitly this includes not being born with false views, in a place where the Buddha’s teachings cannot be heard, or in barbarian cultural settings. The phrase “nor take the form of a woman” precludes their becoming a woman, and one can surmise that these bodhisattvas also do not come to lack the intellectual or sensory faculties (necessary to have access to the teachings), and based on the Sanskrit word ca that implies “nor” elsewhere, i.e. the bodhisattvas also do not take birth among the long-lived gods. Hari: nāpāyeṣūpapadyata iti naraka­preta­tiryagupapattyabhāvaḥ sākṣāt kathitaḥ. upalakṣaṇatvād asya mithyā­dṛṣṭi­buddha­vacanā­śravaṇa­pratyanta­janapadotpādābhavo grāhyaḥ. na ca strībhāvaṃ parigṛhnātī ti strībhāvapratiṣedhavacanād indriyajaḍamūkabhāvaṃ na gṛhṇātīti labhyate. ca śabdān na dīrgḥāyuṣkadevopapattiṃ gṛhṇātity arthaḥ. Note: In ancient India, a woman was considered the property of a man without the ability to do as she pleased, meaning that women were not freely able to listen to or practice the Buddhist teachings. Hence, this was considered an undesirable birth.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing proceed to practice the ten virtuous ways of acting. They themselves desist from killing and also help others practice desisting from killing. They themselves desist from taking what is not given and also help others practice desisting from taking what is not given. They themselves desist from sexual misconduct and also help others practice desisting from sexual misconduct. They themselves desist from beer, wine, liquor, and other intoxicants, and also help others practice desisting from beer, wine, liquor, and other intoxicants. They themselves desist from false words and also help others practice desisting from false words. They themselves desist from slanderous words and also help others practice desisting from slanderous words. They themselves desist from harsh words and also help others practice desisting from harsh words. They themselves desist from idle chatter and also help others practice desisting from idle chatter. They themselves desist from covetousness and also help others practice desisting from covetousness. They themselves desist from malevolence and also help others practice desisting from malevolence. They themselves desist from false views and also help others practice desisting from false views. In this way, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing themselves proceed to practice the ten virtuous ways of acting and also help others recognize the ten virtuous ways of acting, help others practice them, help others be inspired by them, help others rejoice in them, help others become established in them, and help others become unwavering in them. Even in the midst of dreams, they absolutely do not transgress in any way or manner the ten virtuous ways of acting, either when alone or in a group. They do not behave this way even mentally. That is to say, even in the midst of dreams, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing are predisposed toward the ten virtuous ways of acting. Subhūti, one can be certain that bodhisattva great beings possessing these attributes, indications, and signs are also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, whenever bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing master a teaching and give a teaching, they master it and give it with the following mindset: ‘I am mastering this teaching for the benefit of all sentient beings and I am giving it to them for the sake of their happiness and welfare.’ They think, ‘May this teaching fulfill the virtuous intentions of all sentient beings.’According to Ratnākara (F.150.b), any “teaching” refers to (the twelve categories of teachings), such as scriptures, songs, and so on. They give the teaching for the benefit and welfare of others, while thinking, “May it be for their benefit and welfare alone.” RK: yaṃ yaṃ dharmam iti sūtrageyādikam| dadāti ca parasmai hitāya sukhāya caiṣa bhavatv iti teṣām eva hitasukhāya. Also, they give the gift of the teachings to all sentient beings universally. Subhūti, one can be certain that bodhisattva great beings possessing these attributes, indications, and signs are also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, when the profound teachings are being taught, such bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing do not become worried, perplexed, doubtful, or confused. Instead, they speak words that are beneficial, well measured, and affectionate. They rarely feel drowsy or sleepy.According to Ratnākara (F.150.b), the five hindrances (to spiritual practice) are longing for sensory pleasure, malevolence, sluggishness and tiredness, excited agitation, and doubtfulness. The Blessed One says (only) “they rarely feel sleepy or lazy,” but by implication they are free of (all the five) tendencies, i.e., not endowed with them. RK: pañca nivaraṇāni| kāmacchando vyāpādastyānamiddham auddhatyakaukṛtyaṃ vicikitsā ce ti| ebhir asaṃvāso ’samanvāgamaḥ| tam āha| alpastyānamiddhaś ca bhavatī ty upalakṣaṇatvāt. They are also free of negative propensities.According to Ratnākara (F.150.b), the Blessed One says “free of negative propensities.” (In the Ornament, this is described as) “having eliminated all,” i.e., whatever there are, of the “negative propensities,” e.g., the bonds of hatred. RK: sarva yathā bhavati tathā ’nuśayasya dveṣānubandhasya hānaṃ sarvānuśayahānam| tad āha niranuśayaś ca bhavatī ti.

“Whenever coming or going, they do not come or go with a wandering mindset.According to Ratnākara (F.150.b), “wandering” means distracted. RK: bhrāntaṃ vikṣiptam. That is to say, while applying mindfulness, they come and go. Without sluggishness, they lift their feet up from the ground and set them down upon the ground. With nothing but ease, they lift them up and set them down. Without hastiness, they lift them up and set them down.According to Ratnākara (F.150.b), “hastiness” means without looking carefully at the ground. The implication is that (if hasty) they would be distracted in moving about, standing, sitting, or lying down. So, mindfulness and monitoring toward these things is the tenth indication possessed by that bodhisattva. RK: sahase ti asamīkṣya bhūmim| upalakṣaṇaṃ caitac caṃkramasthānaniṣadyāśayaneṣu vikṣepasyeti| smṛtisaṃprajanyaṃ daśamaṃ teṣu talliṅgam. Vigilant, they never trample over any patch of ground.

“Moreover, such bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing have robes and physical belongings that are not infested with lice.According to Ratnākara (F.150.b), “not infested with lice” means not having lice, because the word yūkila follows the principle of the suffix meaning “possessing” (ilac), as in having a tail (picchila) (Cf. Kāś. om V.2.100). RK: yūkāyogād yūkilaḥ| picchāditvād ilac. Note: BHSD, yūkila, cites this very line and incorrectly supposes yūkila to be a scribal corruption. Their conduct is pure. They encounter few afflictions and few mishaps. Also, absolutely none of the eighty thousand species of parasites found in the bodies of other sentient beings ever appear in their bodies in any way or manner.According to Ratnākara (F.151.a), they do not “appear” means they are not born in or on their bodies in order to feed on them. RK: sambhavanti jāyante kāyasya bhakṣaṇāya. Why is that? It is because those bodhisattva great beings’ roots of virtue are the noblest in the whole world.According to Ratnākara (F.151.a), “noblest” means most distinguished. RK: abhyudgatānī ti prativiśiṣṭāni. Cf. Tib. ’jig rten thams cad las mngon par ’phags pa yin no. The more that such bodhisattva great beings’ roots of virtue mature, the more they embrace physical purity, embrace verbal purity, and embrace mental purity.”
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, what is the mental purity to be recognized in such bodhisattva great beings?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, the more that such bodhisattva great beings’ roots of virtue mature, the more they embrace a mindset with few concerns, a mindset free of blameshifting, a mindset free of falsity, a mindset free of crookedness, and a mindset free of deceit.According to Ratnākara (F.151.a), they have “a mindset with few concerns” because their concerns are few and their mindset is undistracted. They are free of “a mindset of crookedness,” i.e., dishonesty. “Blameshifting” is something that serves as a means of hiding one’s own faults and “deceit” is something that aims to deceive others. “Falsity” is feigning to possess qualities that one does not have in order to deceive others. RK: cittālpakṛtyatā alpatvād avikṣepāc ca| citta sya kauṭilyaṃ kuṭilatā| yat tu svadoṣapracchādanopāyas tac chāṭhyam, yat paravañcanāya sa vaṅkaḥ| paravañcanārtham abhūtasvaguṇasaṃdarśanaṃ māyā. With such mental purity, Subhūti, they have surpassed the level of the disciple and solitary realizer. Subhūti, this is the mental purity to be recognized in such bodhisattva great beings. Subhūti, one can be certain that bodhisattva great beings possessing these attributes, indications, and signs are also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing do not give weight to donations, tributes, or celebrity. They do not give weight to robes, alms, accommodation, preventive medicines, or the other requisites. They are not at all envious or jealous. When the profound teachings are being taught, they are not disheartened but with unwavering, deep discernment respectfully listen to the teachings from others. Also, all the teachings to which such bodhisattva great beings respectfully listen they integrate into the transcendent state of wisdom. Then, in the light of that transcendent state of wisdom, they integrate all worldly skills and daily activities with the true nature of things. So, they view nothing as disconnected from the ultimate realm of things, and they view absolutely everything as connected with it. Subhūti, one can be certain that bodhisattva great beings possessing these attributes, indications, and signs are also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, suppose that the pernicious Māra were to magically conjure up the eight intense hells, each containing many hundreds, thousands, or hundreds of thousands of bodhisattvas. Then, suppose he were to say to a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing, ‘All these bodhisattva great beings, whom tathāgatas foreordained as beyond regressing, were reborn in these intense hells. Since you are foreordained as beyond regressing, you too will fall into these intense hells just like they did. Renounce your mindset of awakening and abandon it once and for all!According to Ratnākara (F.151.a), “renounce” means to confess this as a sin. “Abandon it” means absolutely give it up as a principle. RK: pratideśayet ye ayam atyayato deśaya| pratiniḥsaje ti pratiniyamenātyantikatvena parityaja. What is the use of your buddhahood? By acting as I suggest, you will not be reborn in the hells, but rather go to heaven.’According to Ratnākara (F.151.a), “as I suggest” refers to abandoning the mindset of awakening. RK: evam iti bodhicittatyāge sati. If that bodhisattva great being’s mindset does not waver or get deterred even when Māra speaks this way, then he or she knows that a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing cannot possibly be involuntarily reborn in the lower realms.According to Ratnākara (F.151.a), “when Māra speaks this way” means even when something similar is said by Māra. RK: evam apī ti| īdṛśe ’pi māreṇoktau. Subhūti, one can be certain that a bodhisattva great being possessing these attributes, indications, and signs is also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the pernicious Māra may come in the guise of an ascetic and say to the bodhisattva great being, ‘Renounce what you have previously learned and abandon what you have previously embraced. If you renounce it and give it up as I suggest, then I will come to you in this way again and again. What you have just now learned is not the words of the Buddha, but rather the poetry composed by a poet. What I am telling you, however, is what the Buddha taught. These are the words of the Buddha.’ If such a bodhisattva wavers and gets deterred after hearing this, then one can know that such a bodhisattva is not foreordained by a tathāgata, not assured unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and not dwelling in the dimension of someone beyond regressing.According to Ratnākara (F.151.b), “the dimension of someone beyond regressing” refers to the original nature. RK: avinivartanīyasya dhātuḥ prakṛtiḥ. However, Subhūti, if that bodhisattva great being does not waver or get deterred even after having heard these words of Māra, then he or she relies upon nothing but the true nature of things, free of arising, ceasing, and conditioning. So, he or she does not come to develop faith in others.Cf. BHSD pratisaraṇa. This is analogous to a monastic worthy one, whose worldly influences have been eliminated, not coming to develop faith in others; by achieving direct realization in the true nature of things, he cannot be led astray by the pernicous Māra. Likewise, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing cannot be browbeaten by persons belonging to the paths of disciples or solitary realizers, nor can their qualities revert to the levels of the disciples or solitary realizers.According to Ratnākara (F.151.b), his or her qualities “cannot revert” means he or she is not negatively affected by others. RK: apratyudāvartanīyadharme ti pareṇa sambadhyate. Since their final goal is perfect awakening, they are assured the state of complete knowing. When bodhisattva great beings dwell in the dimension of those beyond regressing, they cannot be led astray by any others. Subhūti, one can be certain that a bodhisattva great being possessing these attributes, indications, and signs is also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, someone else may try to persuade the bodhisattva great being beyond regressing as follows: ‘This is going about life in cyclic existence, not going about life as a bodhisattva. End your suffering in this very life and you will no longer experience sufferings and despair in the realms of cyclic existence.According to Hari (676), “this” refers to going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. “Here in this very life” means in this very birth. After the words “end your suffering,” we should add the words “by cultivating the four noble truths.” Hari: eṣe ti prajñāpāramitācaryā. ihaivety asminn eva janmani. duḥkhasyāntaṃ kuru caturāryasatyabhāvanayeti śeṣaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.151.b), the “someone else” is criticizing the bodhisattva path with the words “going about life in cyclic existence” and so on. He is trying to make the bodhisattva take on the śrāvaka path with the words “in this very life” and so on. RK: saṃsāre cārike ty ādinā bodhisattvamārgaṃ dūṣayati | ihaiva tvam ityādinā śrāvakādimārgaṃ grāhayati. Oh, alas! In this life, first, you will fail to escape this physical body of yours, so why do you think you need to take on another physical body again?’According to Hari (676), “Oh, alas!” here is an exclamation of sorrow. Since “in this life, first,”‍—i.e., at a later time in it‍—“you will will fail to escape this physical body”‍—i.e., not become actualized without realizing the true nature of things‍—“why do you think you need to take on another physical body again,” i.e., by force of your aspirations and so on (to be reborn again and again in the future) in order to benefit sentient beings. Hari: aho vate tyādi aho kaṣṭam ihaiva tāvat tavāyam ātmabhāvo dharmatādhigama­vaikalyena yadottarakālam apariniṣpanno ’ nabhinirvṛtto bhaviṣyati tadā kasmāt punas tvam anyam ātmabhāvaṃ praṇidhānādibalena sattvārthaṃ pratigrahītavyaṃ manyasa ity arthaḥ. māro ’yam ity anyamārgopadeśino ’vabodhena nivartanāsambhavād avinivartanīyaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.151.b), “to escape” means that one’s life cycles come to an end. The (untranslated) Sanskrit word vā indicates two possibilities. He implies (you should aim to escape) either “in this very life” or “alas” (it is sad). Ratnākara’s gloss: abhinirvṛtta iti kṣīṇāyuḥ| vā śabdo vikalpe| ihaive tyādikaṃ vā vakṣyati| aho vate tyādikaṃ vā. Subhūti, if that bodhisattva great being does not waver or get deterred even with such talk,According to Ratnākara (F.151.b), “does not get deterred” means the mindset is not agitated, because he or she knows the inferior path described by Māra to be Māra’s talk. RK: cittaṃ na kupyati na calatī ti mārabhāṣitasya hīnamārgasya Mārabhāṣitatvena jñānāt. then the pernicious Māra himself will come to persuade him as follows.

“ ‘Do you want to see something? There are bodhisattva great beings who have served the blessed buddhas with robes, alms, accommodation, preventive medicines, and other requisites for as many eons as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges. They have engaged in spiritual practice in the presence of as many blessed buddhas as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges. They have attended to as many blessed buddhas exactly as there are grains of sand in the River Ganges. They have asked questions and fully pursued their questions about the way that bodhisattva great beings should dwell in this very bodhisattva path. Also, the tathāgatas themselves have explained the way that bodhisattva great beings should dwell and those bodhisattva great beings have already dwelled in that way. They have already gone about life in that way. And they have already engaged in meditation in that exact way. Nevertheless, still to this day, even they have not realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Although they have dwelled in the instruction and guidance and have trained accordingly, they have not attained the state of complete knowing. So, how could you possibly attain unsurpassed, perfect awakening?’

“If, even upon hearing this, that bodhisattva great being also does not waver or get deterred, then the pernicious Māra will magically conjure up some monks in that very area and tell the bodhisattva, ‘These monks have already become worthy ones with their worldly influences eliminated. All those who set out for awakening have, by now, already attained the state of a worthy one and are dwelling in the state of a worthy one. So, how could you still be attaining unsurpassed, perfect awakening?’ Even when such things are being said and explicated, if the bodhisattva great being’s mindset does not waver or get deterred, then one can be certain that such a bodhisattva great being is also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Suppose that upon hearing such divisive words from someone else, the mindset of such bodhisattva great beings does not completely abandon the true nature of things, turn away from it, or deviate from it, because they are aware that all those divisive words are the activities of Māra.According to Ratnākara (F.151.b–52.a), “divisive words” refers to words aimed at their giving up the Mahāyāna. The logical connection is that “upon hearing” those “from someone else”‍—i.e., from Māra‍—their mindset “does not completely abandon” it, i.e., they are not deterred. From what? From “the true nature of things,” which in this context refers to things themselves while going about life in the Mahāyāna. From that, their mindset “does not turn away,” i.e., turn their back on it. “Does not deviate” means not contrary to it, i.e., not extremely proud about the disciples’ path. RK: viveko mahāyānatyāgaḥ| tadarthāni vacanāni vivekapadāni| tāni parato mārāditaḥ śrutvā cittaṃ na parihīyate iti sambandhaḥ| na calatīty arthaḥ| kuta ityāha| dharmatāyā iti dharma eva dharmatā mahāyānacaryā, tataḥ na pratyudāvartata iti na vimukhībhavati| nānyathābhāva iti na viparyayaḥ| na hīnayāne bahumāna ity arthaḥ. Note: The translation already interprets the sentence according to Ratnākara’s other comments here, namely: tānī ti vivekapadāni| ca śabdo hetau| yasmān mārakarmāṇi jānāti. Then, Subhūti, it would be impossible and out of the question for such bodhisattva great beings, going about life this way in the transcendent states, not to attain the state of complete knowing. It is impossible and out of the question that the pernicious Māra could find any vulnerability in such bodhisattva great beings. They go about life training in this way. They never fail to go about life as explained by the Tathāgata. They also abide with the attentiveness associated with these transcendent states. Suppose that upon hearing such divisive words from someone else, bodhisattva great beings recognize them as the activities of Māra, and their mindset does not completely abandon the true nature of things, turn away from it, or deviate from it, because they are aware that all those divisive words are the activities of Māra. Then, Subhūti, one can be certain that any such bodhisattva great being possessing these attributes, indications, and signs is also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing does not cumulatively construct a conception of physical forms and does not give rise to a conception of physical forms. The same is true for the conception of feelings, conceptions, and conditioning factors. He or she does not cumulatively construct a conception of consciousness and does not give rise to a conception of consciousness. Why not?
