Because a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing has attained the bodhisattva’s irreversible fault-free state, because things are empty of inherent characteristics. He or she does not mentally perceive such things, cumulatively construct them, or give rise to them. For this reason, this bodhisattva great being, who has acceptance and knowing of nonarising, is called beyond regressing.According to Hari (679), “due to things being empty of inherent characteristics” means because the bodhisattva (who is about to realize emptiness) has a mental perception of a judgment about things such as physical form and so on as having a true nature empty of inherent nature. He or she has “attained,” i.e., achieved, “the bodhisattva’s irreversible fault-free state,” i.e., the acceptance of the knowing of things in relation to suffering. He or she “does not mentally perceive such things” because he or she does not “cumulatively construct them” or “give rise to them.” He or she “has acceptance and knowing of nonarising” means has arrived at acceptance as described. Hari: tathā h ītyādi. svalakṣaṇaśūnyair iti svabhāvaśūnyadharmatayā rūpādidharmāvabodhānupalambhād bodhisattvanyāmaṃ duḥkhe dharmajñānakṣāntim adhigato ’vakrāntaḥ saṃs tam api dharmaṃ nopalambhate yatas taṃ nābhisaṃskaroti notpadayatī ty arthaḥ. anutpādajñānakṣāntika iti yathānirdiṣṭakṣānti lābhī. According to Ratnākara (F.152.a), one can be sure that a bodhisattva dwelling in the path of seeing has the indication of being beyond regression. What is that? It consists in acceptance and knowing, i.e., a moment of acceptance and a moment of knowing. RK: darśanamārge sthitasya bodhisattvasyāvaivartikaṃ liṅgaṃ veditavyam| kiṃ tat ? kṣāntijñānalakṣaṇāḥ kṣāntikṣaṇāḥ jñānakṣaṇāś ca. According to Ratnākara (F.152.a), “cumulatively construct it” means “give rise” to it. In the sentence, he or she does not perceive “such things,” i.e., physical form and so on. A mental perception is a cumulative construction, which is to give rise to things. RK: abhisaṃskarotī ti utpādayatītyarthaḥ| tam api dharmam iti rūpā dikaṃ nopalabhata ityādi| atra upalambha evābhisaṃskāraḥ| sa evotpādanam| anutpādajñāne kṣāntir asyeti bahuvrīhiḥ. Note: Ratnākara’s latter comment also suggests that the Sanskrit compound here should be read as the acceptance of knowing of things, rather than “has acceptance and knowing of nonarising.” Subhūti, one can be certain that a bodhisattva great being possessing these attributes, indications, and signs is also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 40 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and the Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 50 in the Eighteen Thousand.“Furthermore, Subhūti, the pernicious Māra will approach in the guise of a monk and dissuade the bodhisattva great being, saying, ‘It is like space, this complete knowing.According to Ratnākara (F.152.a), “dissuade” means to get rid of a desire. RK: vicchandayisyatī ti vigatacchandaṃ kariṣyati. Such a thing as complete knowing does not exist. Such a thing as complete knowing cannot be found. Who is supposed to perfectly understand it?According to Ratnākara (F.152.a), “perfectly understand” means with spiritual knowing. RK: ājñāsyati lokottareṇa jñānena. Who is supposed to realize it? No one will go beyond cyclic existence through this. There is no one who could awaken and nothing to be awakened. There is no one who could perfectly understand and nothing to be perfectly understood. In every way such things are like space. You are exerting yourself in vain.According to Ratnākara (F.152.a), “exerting yourself” means being afflicted. RK: vihanyase kliṣyase. Explaining that one should realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening was merely an activity of Māra. The Buddha never taught it.’ That noble son or daughter should recognize, bear in mind, and understand that such an attempt to keep him or her back is merely the activity of Māra.According to Ratnākara (F.152.a), “to keep him or her back” means to cause the abandonment of the mindset of awakening. RK: vivecanatā bodhicittatyājanam. After thinking of it in this way, he or she should cultivate a mindset fixed upon complete, perfect awakening. He or she should cultivate a mindset that is unshakeable and cannot be led astray. Subhūti, one can be certain that a bodhisattva great being possessing these attributes, indications, and signs is also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing turns away from the levels of the disciples and solitary realizers and turns toward the state of complete knowing. Such a bodhisattva great being attains the first meditative state at will, and then the second, third, and fourth in a similar manner. He or she abides in these four meditative states, gets familiar with them, and attains them. Then, he or she avoids involuntary rebirth by force of these meditative states, and once again takes on the qualities of someone in the desire realm.According to Hari (681), the bodhisattva “abides in the meditative states” by first encountering the meditative states in order to abide at ease in this present world. He or she “gets familiar with them” by not actualizing the fruits of those states (i.e., being reborn in pure realms). He or she “attains the meditative states” by focusing on the subsequent knowing realized through eliminating the offshoots of the different states in order to counteract (rebirth in) the subtle form realm and the formless realm. He or she “avoids involuntary rebirth” (in the subtle form or formless realms) “by force of the meditative states” because he or she takes hold of a favorable physical body and “once again takes on the qualities of someone in the desire realm” because, at the level of subsequent knowing with regard to suffering, a bodhisattva cannot be reborn through achieving a total lack of desire. These (last two) sentences should be connected (as two parts of the same action). Hari: atra dhyānāni dṛṣṭa­dharma­sukhavihārārtham abhimukhīkaraṇād dhyānair viharati. tatphalāsākṣātkaraṇād dhyānaparajayaṃ ca karoti. tattadaṅga­prahāṇenādhigatānvaya­jñānasya rūpārūpya­dhātu­pratipakṣatva­jñāpanārtham ālambanād dhyānāni ca samāpadyate. anukūlātmabhāvasya parigrahān na ca dhyānavaśenopapadyate. duḥkhe ’nvayajñānāvasthāyāṃ bodhisattvasya vairāgyalābhānupapattyā sa punar eva kāmāvacarān dharmān adhyālambata iti yojyaṃ. Subhūti, one should know this characteristic, too, of the nonregression of bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing are not individuals who give weight to words. They are not individuals who gives weight to praise, renown, or celebrity. They are not attached to titles. They have an unagitated mindset and a mindset of benefiting all sentient beings. Whether coming or going, they do not come or go with a wandering mindset. Whether coming or going, they come and go with nothing but mindfulness. If they live as householders, they are not excessively attached to sense pleasures or inclined toward them.According to Hari (682), they are neither “attached”‍—i.e., fixated upon‍—the sense pleasures obtained, nor “inclined”‍—i.e., wishing for‍—any that are not yet obtained. Hari: prāpteṣu kāmeṣu kāmābhiṣvaṅgo ’bhiniveśaḥ. aprāpteṣv abhiprāyaḥ prārthanā. They partake of sense pleasures only with a sense of aversion and fear. Subhūti, it is just as if, for instance, a man needed to eat food amid a den of thieves. He would eat his food only with a sense of fear. He would eat his food only with a sense of departing, thinking that he should get out of there as soon as possible. As such, he would eat his food with reservations. Likewise, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing who live as householders partake of all sense pleasures entirely without seeking them, without any greed for them, and without being addicted to them. They are absolutely free from seeking out satisfying or pleasurable objects for their five senses.According to Ratnākara (F.153.a), “satisfying” due to producing very much mental satisfaction, or “pleasurable” due to producing very much pleasure. “Absolutely free of seeking” means always free of seeking. RK: atyarthaṃ saumanasyajananāt priyarūpaiḥ| atyarthaṃ sukhajananāt sātarūpaiḥ| praśaṃsāyāṃ rūpapa iti| anarthikā eve ti nityam anarthikāḥ. Note: The footnote translation here does not include Ratnākara’s citation of the principle regarding the Sanskrit words priyarūpa and sātarūpa as pleonastic (svarthika) derivative nouns. Cf. Balamanoramāyāṃ taddhitaprakriyā 5|4|40. Those who live as householders do not earn their living in unsuitable or suitable ways, but earn their living only with virtue. They also do not approach death with a sense of guilt and do not cause harm to others. How so? Because, in directing all sentient beings toward the state of supreme bliss, they are genuine persons, great persons, excellent persons, eminent persons, virtuous persons, worthy persons, noble persons, heroic persons, bull-like persons, workhorse-like persons, lotus-like persons, water lily-like persons, thoroughbred-like persons, elephant-like persons, lion-like persons, and persons who are a leaders of disciples.According to Ratnākara (F.153.a), the sixteen epithets here correlate to the five aspects conducive to liberation, the four aspects conducive to penetrative insight, and the (first) seven moments on the path of seeing. RK: pañcabhir mokṣabhāgīyaiś caturbhir nirvedhabhāgīyaiḥ saptabhiś ca darśanakṣaṇair yogāt satpuruṣair ity ādini ṣoḍaśapadāni. In this very way, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings live as householders because they have come to attain such ability by means of the transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, one can be certain that a bodhisattva great being possessing these attributes, indications, and signs is also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, such bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing always have the great yakṣa Vajrapāṇi standing behind them. They are difficult to attack, unconquerable by humans or nonhumans, and unparalleled among sentient beings.According to Hari (684), the expressions “difficult to attack,” “unconquerable,” and “unparalleled” are, respectively, because they are physically, verbally, and mentally unsurpassable. Hari: anabhibhavanīya­kāya­vāk­cittatvād yathāsaṃkhyaṃ durdharṣānatikramaṇīya­durāsada­padāni vacyāni. Their mindset is undistracted. Their sense faculties are unimpaired. Their mental faculties are complete rather than incomplete. They are endowed with the vital power of a bull among humans but are not unchaste persons.According to Hari (684), someone is “endowed with vital power” due to having sexual organs concealed within their sheaths. Hari: vastigatakośaguhyatvāt puruṣavṛṣabhendrayasamanvāgataḥ. They absolutely never employ any special mantras, herbs, magic spells, medicinal potions, and so on to magically overpower women.According to Ratnākara (F.153.a), “special mantras” means a particular kind of mantra that belongs to kidnapping deities. “Herbs” are those of annual plants. “Magic spells” are mantras of feminine deities. “Medicinal potions” are herbal remedies. The words “and so on” refer to curses such as magical diagrams, mantras, and so on. RK: mantrajātir mantraviśeṣaḥ grahadevatāyāḥ| auṣadhiḥ phalapākāntā| strīdevatāyā mantro vidyā| bhaiṣajyam auṣadham| ādi śabdena yantra­mantrādi­parigrahaḥ. According to Hari (682), some scholars say that “special mantras” refers to the kind of mantras belonging to Noble Tāra and others. “Herbs” refers to the false daisy plant and others produced by the (light of the) moon, sun, and celestial bodies. The “magic spells” and “medicinal potions” are nothing but those special type of mantras and the herbs. The words “and so on” refer to curses such as magical diagrams. Other scholars say that “special mantras” refers to the spiritual. “Herbs” are the combination of many substances. The “magic spells” are worldly. The “medicinal potions” are made from single ingredients. Still other scholars say that “special mantras” derives from the close bond between a man and his deity. “Herbs” are for counteracting diseases that have arisen. The “magic spells” derive from the close bond between a woman and her deity. The “magical potions” are for preventing future misfortunes. Hari: āryatārādimantraprakāro mantrajātiḥ. candra­sūrya­grahotpādita­bhṛṅgarājādir oṣadhiḥ. mantrajātyoṣadhy eva vidyābheṣajādi. ādi śabdād yantrādiparigraha ity eke. mantrajātir lokottarā. bahudravyasamāhāralakṣanauṣadhiḥ. vidyā laukikī bheṣajam ekāṅgikam ity apare. puruṣadevatāsambandhān mantrajātiḥ. utpannavyādhipratikārārtham oṣadhiḥ. strīdevatāsambandhād vidyā. bheṣajam anāgatotpātapratiṣedhārtham ity anye. Note: Ratnākara seems to take the “women” to be subjugated, but in Hari’s comments, some seem to suggest that women are the subjugators. Their livelihood is pure, not sinful. Their disposition is neither belligerent nor combative.According to Ratnākara (F.153.a), “belligerent” means quarreling physically and “combative” means verbally. RK: vigrahaḥ kāyena kalaho vivādas tu vācā. Their viewpoints are straightforward. They neither raise themselves up nor put others down. They are endowed with these positive qualities and others. They do not make predictions to men or women that they will have a son or daughter. They do not have faults like this, indicating things and so on.This translation follows the Tibetan “indicating” (kun ston pa’i nyes pa; *ādarśana), because the Sanskrit edition’s ādeyatādoṣa makes less sense and could easily be an error for ādarśanadośa in most scripts. Cf. Negi vol. 1, ka, for kun ston pa, ādarśana. Subhūti, one can be certain that a bodhisattva great being possessing these attributes, indications, and signs is also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, there are more attributes, indications, and signs of a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing that I will point out. One should recognize any bodhisattva great being possessing these to be beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Now, what are those? Specifically, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with the dependent arising of the constituents, elements, and bases.According to Hari (684), Ārya Vimuktisena (seventh century) said the use of the words “preoccupation” and “occupation” informs us that, respectively, the previous preoccupation concerns the truths of suffering and its origin and the subsequent occupation concerns the truths of cessation and the path. Others have said that the “preoccupation” is the first habitual fixation on the constituents, whereas “occupation” is the subsequent habitual fixation on the absolute. Hari: yogānuyoga grahaṇena duḥkhasamudayasatyayor nirodhamārgasatyayoś ca yathākramaṃ yogānuyoga­paurvāparyaṃ jñāpitam ity āryavimuktisenaḥ. skandhādiṣu prathamo ’bhiniveśo yogaḥ. paścād atyantābhiniveśo ’nuyoga ity apare. According to Ratnākara (F.153.a–b), being “preoccupied” is a mental function involving habitual fixation, whereas being “occupied” is a mental function free of habitual fixation. To “stay” in those refers to one’s concentration (samādhi) being possessed with such things. RK: yogaḥ sābhiniveśā vṛttiḥ| anuyogo nirabhiniveśā vṛttiḥ| tābhyāṃ vihāras tayor vihāras tadvān samādhiḥ. Note: This translation concerns Ratnākara’s comments on the Ornament verse that correlates here to the Aṣṭa. His other technical comments are already reflected in the translation and not translated here, i.e., RK: yogāś cānuyogaś ca yogānuyogaṃ, tadanuyuktās tena saha yuktāḥ| sahārthe ’nu śabdaḥ| na viharantī ti sambandhaḥ. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with the kind of talk of those who delight in socializing. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of kings. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of thieves. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of armies. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of battles. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of villages, cities, towns, communities, kingdoms, or principalities. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of themselves. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of ministers or important officials. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of women, men, or those in between. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of travels, parks, monasteries, palaces, pools, lakes, ponds, lotus ponds, forests, gardens, or mountains. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of yakṣas, rākṣasas, pretas, piśācas, kaṭapūtanas, or kumbhāṇḍas.These are different types of otherworldly beings. To use English equivalents may confuse more than help. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of food. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of food, drink, clothing, jewelry, perfumes, wreaths, or fragrant oils. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of streets, squares, intersections, highways, marketplaces, vehicles, or estates. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of songs, dances, storytelling, actors, dancers, or buskers. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of oceans, rivers, or islands. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of anything that contradicts the truth. They do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of pleasures for ordinary people.

“Instead, they stay bound to the preoccupation and occupation with talk of the transcendent state of wisdom and never lack the attentiveness associated with the state of complete knowing. Meanwhile, they do not stay bound to any preoccupation or occupation with talk of strife, wars, arguments, or disputes.

“They wish only for virtue and do not wish for nonvirtue. They speak in praise of harmony and do not speak in praise of disharmony.According to Ratnākara (F.154.b), “harmony” means the knowing of everything’s sameness and “disharmony” means the mental construct of everything’s difference. RK: abhedaḥ samatājñānam| bhedo nānātvavikalpaḥ. They wish for friendship and do not wish for enmity. They speak the truth and do not speak the opposite of the truth. Wanting nothing more than to see the tathāgatas, they develop the proper mindset to be reborn in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who reside, stay, and spend time in other world systems. Hence, they never fail to see the tathāgatas, to serve them, or to attend to them.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the bodhisattva great beings who pass away from the desire realms, form realms, or formless realms take rebirth right here in the central area of the Jambu continent, where sentient beings are skilled in the arts, in poetry, in mantras, in magic spells, in treatises, in reading signs, and in the meaning of the teachings. A few take rebirth in the bordering countries. But most take rebirth in the central area. At the very least, those who are reborn in the bordering countries take rebirth in the big cities, and they too are found to have these positive qualities. Subhūti, one can be certain that bodhisattva great beings possessing these attributes, indications, and signs are also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhuti, it never occurs to bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing to think, ‘I am beyond regressing’ or ‘I am not beyond regressing.’ In this way, bodhisattvas beyond regressing do not become doubtful, uncertain, or disheartened about their own level. Subhūti, this is analogous to stream enterers not being worried, doubtful, or uncertain about what frutional level of a stream enterer they belong to. In the very same way, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing are not worried, doubtful, or uncertain about what bodhisattva level they belong to. They do not become disheartened about their own level.According to Ratnākara (F.154.b), “doubt” means uncertainty and “disheartened” means low in energy. RK: vicikitsā saṃśayaḥ| saṃsīdanaṃ mandotsāhatā. They very quickly recognize when the activities of Māra have arisen again and again, and do not fall under their influence again and again. Subhūti, this is analogous to how a man committing a harmful act that brings immediate results never fails to possess the mindset that results immediately afterward.According to Ratnākara (F.154.b), “the mindset that results immediately afterward” means the torment that follows immediately afterward. RK: ānantaryacittene ti| anantaryapaścāt tāpena. Right up until the point of dying, that man cannot get rid of that mindset or escape it. The mindset just accompanies him right up until the time of his death. In the very same way, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing have a mindset beyond regressing that remains firmly on the level that they belong to and cannot be shaken from it. Even the whole world with its gods, humans, and asuras cannot move or shake them from it. They very quickly recognize when Māra’s activities have arisen again and again, and do not fall under their influence again and again. Those bodhisattva great beings who have passed on to a future birth without worry or doubt about their level do not develop either the mindset of a disciple or a solitary realizer. Those who have passed on to a future birth also think, ‘It is not the case that I will not awaken. I am abiding at a level that is my own and will absolutely realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. At my level, I cannot be misguided by others and I cannot be browbeaten.’ How so? They are abiding there because they possess a mindset that cannot be led astray and a knowing that cannot be led astray.

“If the pernicious Māra were to come along disguised as a buddha, he might approach such bodhisattva great beings and say, ‘Actualize the state of a worthy one in this very life! You are not foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Bodhisattva great beings who realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening are endowed with attributes, indications, and signs that you do not have. So, why are you going about life in this transcendent state of wisdom?’ If the mindset of those bodhisattva great beings changes, then, Subhūti, one can be sure that such bodhisattva great beings have not been foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by any previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas.

“On the other hand, suppose that they keep this in mind and think, ‘Aha! The pernicious Māra is magically disguised as a buddha and has come along. This is a form magically empowered by Māra or magically created by Māra, but not the Tathāgata! Everything in reality is exactly the way the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata has already taught and not otherwise!’According to Ratnākara (F.155.a), when it says “exactly that way… and not otherwise” it is implicitly understood to mean “complete, perfect awakening is sure to be mine.” A “magically empowered form of a buddha” means the magical creation of a buddha. “In reality” means in essence. RK: tathā tan nānyathe ty ukte ’rthād gamyate bhaviṣyaty eva me ’nuttarā samyaksambodhir iti| buddhādhiṣṭhānaṃ buddhanirmāṇam| addhe ti tattvata ity arthaḥ. If they carefully consider things and keep this in mind, they will think, ‘Aha! The pernicious Māra has created a magically empowered form of a buddha and wishes to hold me back from unsurpassed, perfect awakening!’ If in response to such a thought Māra retreats, then, Subhūti, one can be sure that these bodhisattva great beings were foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, and that such bodhisattva great beings are abiding at the level of bodhisattvas beyond regressing. Subhūti, if bodhisattva great beings are found to have these attributes, indications, and signs as their qualities, then one would understand, ‘Aha! These bodhisattva great beings have, in reality, been foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, and these bodhisattva great beings are abiding at the level of bodhisattvas beyond regressing. How so? It is because these attributes, indications, and signs are found possessed by all bodhisattva great beings who are beyond regressing.’ Subhūti, one can be certain that bodhisattva great beings possessing these attributes, indications, and signs are also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing even give themselves away and give up their lives for the sake of apprehending the genuine truth. Therefore, out of their appreciation and devotion for the blessed buddhas of the past, present, and future, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing make the greatest of efforts for the sake of apprehending the genuine truth. They apprehend the genuine truth as a consequence of their appreciation and devotion to the truth, thinking, ‘The blessed buddhas are the bodies of the truth.’According to Ratnākara (F.155.a), “give themselves away” means “selling themselves in the market” (vikraya) and so on, whereas “giving up their lives” means dying. The truth taught by the blessed buddhas is that everything is empty. Confused persons contest that very truth. For themselves or others to apprehend that, they give up even their lives. This is because for them to do that for the truth is to have “appreciation and devotion” to the buddhas, because they know the Tathāgata to be “the body of truth” and because they see their own future buddhahood. RK: ātma naḥ parityāgo vikrayādi| jīvita sya parityāgo maraṇam| buddhair bhagavadbhir deśito dharmaḥ sarvadharmāḥ śūnyā iti| tam eva mohapuruṣāḥ pratikṣipanti| tasya svayaṃ paraiś ca parigrahāya jīvitam api tyajati| sa hi buddheṣu yat premagauravaṃ tad dharme karoti| dharmakāyās tathāgatā iti jñānāt| ātmanaś ca bhāvibuddhatva­darśanāt. Note that earlier in this sūtra (4.2), Śakra says, “As the Blessed One has said, ‘The blessed buddhas are the bodies of the truth (dharmakāya). But, monks, do not see (manyadhvam) this real body (satkāyam) as that body. Instead, monks, see me through actualizing (niṣpatti) the body of truth (dharmakāya).’ And hence the body of the tathāgatas should be seen as revealed (prabhāvita) at the pinnacle of reality (bhūtakoṭi), that is to say, as the transcendent state of wisdom.” They are not only apprehending the genuine truth of the blessed buddhas of the past, but are also precisely apprehending the genuine truth of the blessed buddhas of the present and future. Regarding this, they think, ‘I too have entered the ranks of those blessed buddhas of the future. I too am foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. I too have apprehended the genuine truth.’ Seeing this also as their purpose, they give themselves over to it and even give up their lives for the sake of apprehending the genuine truth. They are never disheartened or become lazy about it. Subhūti, one can be certain that bodhisattva great beings possessing these attributes, indications, and signs are also beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.
“Furthermore, Subhūti, when the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata is teaching the truth, bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing are neither worried nor doubtful.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, when the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata is teaching the truth, such bodhisattva great beings are neither worried nor doubtful. But is that not the case when a disciple is teaching the truth?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, such bodhisattva great beings are also neither worried nor doubtful when a disciple is teaching the truth. Why? Because they have attained the state of acceptance that all things are free of arising. Hence, they listen to the true nature of things that is not contradicted by any things, and while listening, they are neither worried nor doubtful.Once again, the above passage involves a play on the word dharma, which means “truth,” “things,” and “teachings.”

“Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being endowed with such positive qualities is beyond regressing. Subhūti, one can be certain that these attributes, indications, and signs are also possessed by a bodhisattva great being who is beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Characteristics, Indications, and Signs of Those Beyond Regressing,” the seventeenth chapter in “The Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 18: EmptinessAccording to Ratnākara (F.159.b), “Chapter on Emptiness” means the chapter primarily concerned with emptiness. RK: śūnyatā pradhānaḥ parivartaḥ śūnyatāparivartaḥ. He says that while the previous chapter took the path of seeing as the context in which to explain the signs of being beyond regressing, in terms of their numbers and distinctive qualities in the four stages of insight, this chapter explains the profound path of cultivation. RK: nirvedhāṅgadṛṅmārgayor avaivartikaliṅgāni saṃkhyayā viśeṣataś coktani| bhāvanāmārgas tu gambhīraḥ.The content of this chapter corresponds to the first part of Chapter 41 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (down to 41.46) and the equivalent in the Hundred Thousand, and to the first part of Chapter 51 in the Eighteen Thousand (down to 51.52).
div.p: Then the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, it is wonderful that a bodhisattva great being is endowed with great positive qualities. O Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being is endowed with boundless positive qualities. Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being is endowed with measureless positive qualities.”According to Ratnākara (F.155.b), “endowed with great positive qualities” means that their accumulation of positive qualities is great, because it is extremely profound and because it has been amassed through uninterrupted effort over a period of two countless eons. For this reason, it is “boundless” because it cannot be evaluated qualitatively and “ measureless” because it cannot be quantified. RK: mahān guṇasambhāraḥ paramagambhīratayā kalpā­saṃkhyeyadvaya­nirantara­prayatnalabhyatayā ca| ata eva viśeṣato jñātum aśakyatvād apramāṇāḥ saṃkhyātum aśakyatvād aparimitāḥ.

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “That is true, Subhūti. That is true. Why is that? It is because a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing has attained the infinite, limitless knowing that cannot be led astray by the disciples or solitary realizers.”According to Ratnākara (F.155.b), this “bodhisattva great being” is on the path of cultivation beyond turning back and is “beyond regressing” from the path of cultivation. The knowing is “infinite” because it surpasses calculation; “limitless” because it is extremely vast; and “cannot be led astray” means that (the bodhisattva’s knowing of) the perfect pinnacle and so on cannot be perfectly understood even “by the disciples or solitary realizers”‍—such is the profundity of the path of cultivation. RK: bodhisattvo mahāsattva iti bhāvanāmārgastho ’vaivartikaḥ| avinivartanīyene ti bhāvanāmārgasthena| saṃkhyātikramād anantam| paramodāratvād aparyantam| asaṃhāryaṃ samyagagrādyaṃ samyag jñātum aśakyaṃ| śrāvaka­pratyekabuddhair apīti bhāvanāmārgasya gāmbhīryam. According to Hari (695), it “cannot be led astray” since it cannot be shaken by the whole world. Hari: sarvalokākampyatvenāsaṃhāryaṃ.

Subhūti said, “The Blessed One could certainly explain for eons equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges the attributes, indications, and signs belonging to a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing.According to Ratnākara (F.155.b), “could certainly explain” means it is possible, but the implication is that doing so would not be a benefit to beings. For that reason, he says what he does next. RK: pratibalaḥ śakto nirdeṣṭum | kin tu sattvārtho na syād iti bhāvaḥ| tataḥ kim ityāha| ata eve tyādi| eva kāro bhinnakramaḥ| sthānānī ty ataḥ pareṇa draṣṭavyaḥ| sarvāṇi gambhīrāṇī ti vīpsārthaḥ. Note: Ratnākara also explains here that the word “just” (eva) is out of order in the Sanskrit text and rightfully qualifies the “points,” rather than being after the word “that being so.” Also, Ratnākara and Hari both suggest that the Sanskrit repeats the word “profound” twice (translated “extremely profound) to indicate that it is something on the path to be practiced again and again. Cf. Hari: gambhīrāṇi gambhīrāṇī ti vīpsābhidhānaṃ paunaḥpuṇyābhyāsamārgajñāpanārthaṃ. That being so, Blessed One, could you please outline just the extremely profound points connected with the transcendent state of wisdom for such a bodhisattva great being?”According to Ratnākara (F.155.b), the “points” refers to the meditative objects on the path of cultivation. “Connected with the transcendent state of wisdom” means based on that. The Sanskrit optative here should be understood (as translated) to mean “could you please outline.” This is a request for instruction by Subhūti. RK: sthānānī ti bhāvanāmārgālambanāni| prajñāpāramitāyāṃ pratisaṃyuktāni tadādhāraṇīty arthaḥ| sūcayitavyānī ti sūcyantām ityarthaḥ| iti subhūter adhyeṣaṇā. Note: Both commentaries explain the entire chapter below as concerning the different aspects of the path of cultivation in conjunction with the outline of topics in the Ornament.

“Excellent, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One. “It is excellent that you want to learn about the points that are extremely profound.According to Hari (696), “want to learn about” these means he wants to be instructed in them. Hari: nigamayitukāma iti pratipādayitukāmaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.155.b), “want to learn about” means he wants to practice these on the path of cultivation. RK: nigamayitukāma iti bhāvanāmārge yojayitukāmaḥ. Subhūti, the term profound here refers to emptiness. That is to say, Subhūti, the term profound here refers to the freedom from signs, freedom from inclinations, freedom from conditioning, freedom from arising, freedom from birth, freedom from any state of being, and freedom from passion. It also refers to cessation, to nirvāṇa, and to dissolution.”According to Hari (696–97), (the point on the path of cultivation) is “emptiness” because it is devoid of a grasper; “freedom from signs” because it lacks anything grasped; “freedom from inclinations” because there is not even a substantially existent nondual knowing to be attained separate from the grasper or grasped; “freedom from conditioning” because it is not associated with arising and so on or because it continues effortlessly; “freedom from arising” because it is the nonexistence of a cause; “freedom from birth” because it is the end of rebirth; “freedom from any state of being” because of lacking any counterproductive forces on the path of seeing; and “freedom from passion” because it is the dissolution of counterproductive forces on the path of cultivation. It is “cessation” because it is the quality of the nonarising of suffering in the future; “nirvāṇa” because it is the station of abiding delightfully in peace without signs; and “dissolution” because it is the fruit of not wandering in suffering in this life. Some say that the different meanings of emptiness and so on should be expressed in this way. One should also understand all these words to be pointing toward the ultimate realm of things through different types of exclusions. Hari: grāhakavivekena śūnyatā. grāhyābhāvād ānimittaṃ. grāhyagrāhakaviviktādvayajñānasyāpi vastusato ’dhigantavyasyāsattvād apraṇihitam. jātyādilakṣaṇa­trayavisaṃyogād anābhoga­pravṛttatvād vānabhisaṃskāraḥ. hetor asattvād anutpādaḥ. pratisaṇdhivigamād ajātiḥ. darśana­mārga­vipakṣavirahād abhāvaḥ. bhāvanā­mārga­vipakṣaviśleṣād virāgaḥ. āyatyāṃ duḥkhānutpattidharmatvān nirodhaḥ. animitta­śānta­sukha­vihāra­padasthānatvān nirvāṇaṃ. dṛṣṭe dharme duḥkhāpracāraphalatvād vigama ity evam esāṃ śūnyatādīnām arthabhedo vācya ity eke. sarvair apy etaiḥ padair vyāvṛttibhedād dharmadhātur eva nirdiśyata iti grāhyaṃ. According to Ratnākara (F.156.a), the nature (bhāva) of all things as they appear and the nature of them as they truly are is “emptiness.” It itself is the “freedom from signs” because signs cease in the experience of it. It itself is “freedom from inclinations” because those who experience it have no aspiration for the three realms. It itself is “freedom from conditioning” because those who experience it do not perform karmic deeds that lead to rebirth. It itself is “freedom from arising” because those who experience it do not have arisings due to any karmic deeds. It itself is “freedom from birth” because those who experience it know (that things are) unborn by nature. It itself is “freedom from any state of being” because it is insubstantial. It itself is the purity of “freedom from passion” because those who experience it uproot the emotional defilements and negative propensities. It itself is “cessation” because those who experience it are blocked from future rebirth. It itself is “nirvāṇa” because those who experience it overcome the latent impressions. It itself is “dissolution” because those who experience it put an end to the constituents with worldly influences. RK: gambhīram itītyādi| sarvadharmāṇāṃ yathāpratibhāsam bhāvo yathātattvaṃ ca bhāvaḥ śūnyatā | saivānimittaṃ tadvedane nimittanirodhāt| saivāpraṇihitaṃ tadvidāṃ traidhātuke ’praṇidhānāt| saivānabhisaṃskāras teṣāṃ punarbhavāya karmākaraṇāt| saivānutpādas teṣāṃ tena karmaṇā’nutpādāt| saivājātis teṣāṃ prakṛtyajātatājñānāt| saivābhāvo ’dravyatvāt| saiva virāga śuddhiḥ kleśānuśayasamudghātāt| saiva nirodhas teṣāṃ punarbhavavirodhāt| saiva nirvāṇaṃ teṣāṃ vāsanāsamudghātāt| saiva vigamas teṣāṃ sāśravaskandhoparamāt.

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, is this term referring exclusively to these things alone, but not to everything?”According to Ratnākara, the question is whether the term “profound” refers to these “things”‍—i.e., to emptiness and so on‍—but not to “everything,” i.e., the constituents, elements, and so on. Cf. RK: dharmāṇām iti śūnyatādinām| sarvadharmāṇām iti skandhadhātvādīnām.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, the term profound does also refer to everything. How so? Because, Subhūti, physical forms are profound. Likewise, Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are profound. Subhūti, how are physical forms profound? How are feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness profound? Subhūti, physical forms are as profound as the actual nature. Likewise, Subhūti, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are as profound as the actual nature.

“Subhūti, since the actual nature of physical forms is profound, physical forms are profound. Likewise, Subhūti, since the actual nature of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is profound, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are profound. Subhūti, the profundity of physical forms is where there are no physical forms. Likewise, Subhūti, the profundity of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness is where there are no feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness.”According to Ratnākara (F.156.b), the profundity of physical forms is “where”‍—i.e., in the actual nature of form and so on‍—the imagined forms and so on that we experience do not appear, since they appear as ultimately existent nature. RK: yatre ti yasyāṃ rūpāditathatāyāṃ vedyamānāyāṃ kalpitaṃ rūpādi na prakhyāti iyaṃ rūpā dīnāṃ gambhīratā | paramārthasatā rūpeṇa teṣāṃ prakhyānāt.

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, it is amazing how well your subtle skill in means points us toward nirvāṇa and keeps us away from physical forms.According to Ratnākara (F.156.b), “subtle” refers to his refined objects of knowledge. The Blessed One “points us toward nirvāṇa” because nothing imaginary appears to a bodhisattva beyond regressing, who is on the path of cultivation. The Blessed One “keeps us away from physical forms” and so on, because those appear as the ultimate reality. So, a bodhisattva (develops) the longing to end all the mental projections of form and so on by means of nirvāṇa. RK: sūkṣmeṇe ti nipuṇavedyena| nirvāṇaṃ ca sūcitam| bhāvanā­mārga­sthasyāvaivartikasya sarveṣāṃ parikalpitānām aprakhyānāt| rūpā dibhyaś ca nivāritaḥ| teṣāṃ paramārthasya prakhyānāt| tatra nirvāṇena rūpādisamāropāntam utkatā. Likewise, Blessed One, it is amazing how well your subtle skill in means points us toward nirvāṇa and keeps us away from feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, suppose that a bodhisattva great being contemplates, considers, and meditates upon these extremely profound points connected with the transcendent state of wisdom, thinking,According to Ratnākara (F.157.a), “these” points‍—i.e., the emptiness and so on mentioned above‍—are “connected with the transcendent state of wisdom,” meaning the wisdom that has transcended conceptualization, i.e., the transcendent state of wisdom qua the path of seeing and the path of cultivation. But the transcendent state of wisdom that is the stages of insight (on the path of preliminary practice) is implicit within these two. So here, “connected,” i.e., associated with, means reliant on these three types (of transcendent state of wisdom). The connection is that one “contemplates, considers, and meditates upon” those. RK: imānī ti yathoktāni śūnyatādīni| prajñā­pāramitā­prati­saṃyuktānī ti vikalpa pāramitai va prajñā prajñāpāramitā darśanamārgo bhāvanāmārgaś ca| nirvedhabhāgīyāni tu tādarthyāt prajñāpāramitā| tasyāṃ trividhāyāṃ pratisaṃyuktāni sambaddhāni tadādhārāṇīty arthaḥ| ya imāni cintayiṣyati tulayiṣyati upanidhāsyatī ti sambandhaḥ. ‘I should abide in the transcendent state of wisdom in the way that it is prescribed. I should train in the transcendent state of wisdom in the way that it is explained. I should go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom in the way that it is expounded.’According to Ratnākara (F.157.a–.b), “should abide” through conclusive certainty based on contemplation; “should train” by developing tranquility through considering; “should go about life” by developing extraordinary insight by means of meditating; “prescribed” by listening to the words in the text; “informed” by making the meaning known; “expounded” by making the esoteric meaning known. There are four types of reasoning: (1) the “reasoning of dependence” concerns the arising of an effect in dependence upon all its causes; (2) the “reasoning of cause and effect” concerns the efficacy by which particular effects arise from particular causes; (3) the “reasoning of proof” is the valid investigation by which some point is proved, and (4) the “reasoning of the nature of things” (establishes) the rule for the inherent nature of things, e.g. fire burns and water causes moisture. Through these four reasonings, one “contemplates,” i.e., investigates, the object of meditative concentration devotedly and consistently by sitting cross-legged in a solitary place. “Considering” means making the mind calm through the nine stages of meditation aimed at tranquility (śamatha). “Meditating upon” these things means the four stages of meditation aimed at extraordinary insight (vipaśyanā), i.e., one pulls things apart, analyzes them, investigates them, and engages in examining them. Here, (a) pulling them apart is being aware of them phenomenologically; (b) analyzing them means being aware of them ontologically, as the text mentions (below) the “five constituents” and so on; (c) investigating them means transforming them into a (single) sign (nimittīkaraṇaṃ) by way of the nonconceptual mind in connection with wisdom, as the text mentions (below) “everything is empty” and so on, and (d) examining them means accurately evaluating them. This describes the nature of the path of cultivation. RK: sthātavyam iti cintayaikāntaniścayāt| śikṣitavyam iti tulanena śamathotpādanāt| pratipattavyam ity upanidhyānena vipaśyanotpādanāt| ājñaptaṃ granthaśravaṇāt| ākhyātam arthajñāpanāt| upadiṣṭaṃ rahasyakīrtanāt| catasro yuktayaḥ| apekṣāyuktiḥ sarvahetūn apekṣya kāryotpatteḥ| kāryakāraṇayuktiḥ tebhyaḥ pratyekaṃ kāryaviśeṣotpatteḥ| upapattiḥ sādhanayuktiḥ| yena pramāṇena yo ’rthaḥ sidhyati| dharmatāyuktiḥ svabhāvaniyamo dharmāṇām| agni eva dahaty āpa eva kledayantītyādi| ābhiḥ samādhyālambanasya rahasi paryaṅkam āruhya satkṛtya sātatyena nirūpaṇaṃ cintā| tatraiva navākāreṇa śamathena cetasaḥ samīkaraṇaṃ tulanam| dharmān vicinoti pravicinoti parivitarkayati parimīmāṃsām āpadyata iti caturākārā vipaśyanā upanidhyānam|| tatra yāvadbhāvikatājñānaṃ vicayaḥ| pañcaiva skandhā ityādi yathāvadbhāvikatājñānaṃ pravicayaḥ| sarva ete śūnyā ityādi nirvikalpena manasā prajñāsahagatena nimittīkaraṇaṃ parivitarkaḥ| santīraṇaṃ parimīmāṃsā| iti bhāvanāmārgasya svabhāvaḥ. Likewise, suppose that a bodhisattva great being engages in meditation even just for a single day, while applying him- or herself, meditating, reflecting, practicing, striving, and exerting him- or herself.According to Ratnākara (F.157.b), “likewise” means in the stages taught. “Pondering” is by means of meditation aimed at tranquility (śamatha). “Meditating” is by means of the meditation aimed at extraordinary insight (vipaśyanā). “Reflecting” is by means of both (meditations aimed at tranquility and extraordinary insight). “Practicing” is by means of persevering in meditation aimed at tranquility. “Striving” is by means of persevering in the meditation aimed at extraordinary insight. “Exerting” is by means of persevering in both. RK: tathe tyādi| tathe ti yathoktena krameṇa sampādayamānaḥ śamathena| upanidhyāyana vipaśyanayā| upaparīkṣamāṇa ubhābhyām| prayujyamānaḥ śamathavīryeṇa| ghaṭamāno vipaśyanāvīryeṇa| vyāyacchamāna ubhayavīryeṇa. How great is the karmic deed that such a bodhisattva great being performs on that single day?According to Ratnākara (F.157.b), “How great is the karmic deed…?” is a rhetorical question that he answers himself with the following example. RK: svayam eva bhagavān pṛcchati kiyatakarma karotī ti| uttaraṃ svayam evāha tadyathāpī tyādinā.

“It is just as if, for instance, a man motivated by his lust and his fanciful thoughts were to arrange a meeting with a beautiful, charming, and attractive woman. But suppose that the woman were held back by someone else and could not get herself out of the house.According to Ratnākara (F.157.b–58.a), “held back by someone else” means she is not permitted or allowed by someone else. RK: paraparigṛhīte ti parakīyā na vaśayen na śaknuyāt. In that case, what do you think, Subhūti? What would that man’s fanciful thoughts be continually concerned with?”

Subhūti said, “That man’s fanciful thoughts would be continually concerned with nothing but the woman. He might think about her coming and what he will do with her once she has come. He might think how he will flirt with her, amuse himself with her, and have intercourse with her.”
The Blessed One asked, “So, Subhūti, what do you think? How many fanciful thoughts would that man generate in the course of a day?”
“Blessed One, that man would generate many fanciful thoughts in the course of a day,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, that man would generate in the course of a day the same number of fanciful thoughts as the number of eons that a bodhisattva great being leaves cyclic existence behind, turns his or her back on it, and puts an end to it.According to Ratnākara (F.158.a), “leaves cyclic existence behind” means abandons it, which arises from a series of births by someone returning to it; “turns his or her back on it” means due to the returner not returning; and “puts an end to it” means by not moving from a past to a future life. RK: saṃsārād ity āgāmino janmaprabandhāt| chorayatī ti tyajati| vipṛṣṭhīkaroti āgāmino ’nāgamanāt| vyantīkarotī ty aparāntataḥ pūrvāntānayanāt. During that time, such a bodhisattva great being abides in this transcendent state of wisdom, trains in it, progresses in it, meditates on it, and engages in meditation on it as prescribed, informed, expounded, explained, and explicated.According to Ratnākara (F.158.a), “abides” due to having eliminated doubts through contemplation; “trains” through tranquility; “progresses” through extraordinary insight; “meditates” through both of those; “engages in meditation,” i.e., the cultivation of it. With just these few words, a single karmic deed is explained. RK: tiṣṭhati cintayā saṃśayacchedāt| śikṣate śamathena| pratipadyate vipaśyanayā| upanidhyāyatī tyubhābhyām| evaṃ yogaṃ bhāvanām āpadyate| iyatā ekaṃ karmoktam. Such a bodhisattva great being also abandons any faults that make him or her regress from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Given this fact, Subhūti, suppose that, on the one hand, a bodhisattva great being were occupied with the meditative state of the transcendent state of wisdom, were abiding in the state of attentiveness associated with the transcendent state of wisdom, and were to do just that much activity for a single day. Now, on the other hand, suppose that another bodhisattva, who lacks the transcendent state of wisdom, were to remain for eons equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges, and throughout that time were to give others gifts. That former bodhisattva‍—who engaged in meditation in the transcendent state of wisdom even just for a single day‍—would then be superior to the latter one.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, suppose that a bodhisattva great being were to remain for eons equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges, and throughout that time were to give gifts to stream enterers and to provide them ongoing support.According to Hari (702), to “give” means to offer, and to “provide ongoing support” means (to do so) over a long period. Hari: dadyāt pratiṣṭhāpayed iti niryātayec cirasthitikaṃ ca kuryād ity arthaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.158.a), “give” by bestowing things, and “provide ongoing support” without holding back. RK: dadyād arpaṇataḥ| pratiṣṭhāpayed avipratisārataḥ. Also, suppose that, in a similar manner, he or she were to give gifts to once-returners, non-returners, and worthy ones, and to provide all of them ongoing support. Also, suppose that, in a similar manner, he or she were to give gifts to solitary realizers and to provide all of them ongoing support. Also, suppose that, in a similar manner, he or she were to give gifts to worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas and provide all of them ongoing support. But also, suppose that he or she lacked the transcendent state of wisdom. Now suppose that, on the other hand, another bodhisattva great being were, for even just a single day, to engage in meditation in this transcendent state of wisdom, as expounded, explained, and explicated. Subhūti, this latter bodhisattva great being would then earn much more merit than the former one.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, suppose that a bodhisattva great being were to remain for eons equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges, and throughout that time were to give gifts to stream enterers and so on, up to perfect buddhas, and to provide them ongoing support, and also to fully perfect ethical discipline. But also, suppose that he or she lacked the transcendent state of wisdom. Now suppose that, on the other hand, another bodhisattva great being‍—who abides in the transcendent state of wisdom‍—were to arise from his or her attentivenessAccording to Hari (703), “from his or her attentiveness” means from cultivating the transcendent state of wisdom. Hari: tato manasikārād iti prajñāpāramitābhāvanātaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.158.a), “attentiveness” refers to abiding delightfully (in concentration). RK: manasikāro vihāraḥ. and teach the Dharma. Subhūti, this latter bodhisattva great being would then earn much more merit than the former one.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, suppose that a bodhisattva great being were to remain for eons equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges, and throughout that time were to give gifts to stream enterers and so on, up to perfect buddhas, and to provide them ongoing support, and also to perfect his or her ethical discipline and to possess tolerant acceptance. But also, suppose that he or she lacked the transcendent state of wisdom. Now suppose that, on the other hand, a bodhisattva great being‍—who abides in the transcendent state of wisdom‍—were to arise from his or her attentiveness and teach the Dharma. Subhūti, this latter bodhisattva great being would then earn much more merit than the former one.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, suppose that a bodhisattva great being were to remain for eons equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges, and throughout that time were to give gifts to stream enterers and so on, up to perfect buddhas, and to provide them ongoing support, and also to perfect his or her ethical discipline, to possess tolerant acceptance, to undertake heroic perseverance, and to engage in meditation on the meditative states and qualities of the factors in awakening. But also, suppose that he or she lacked the transcendent state of wisdom. Now suppose that, on the other hand, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being were to give the gift of the Dharma in a similar manner, and dedicate it to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Subhūti, this latter bodhisattva great being would then earn much more merit than the former one. Beyond that, Subhūti, suppose that this very same bodhisattva great being were not only to give others the gift of the Dharma in a similar manner, and dedicate it to unsurpassed, perfect awakening, but also were to do so using the particular dedication taught in the transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, this bodhisattva great being would then earn an even greater amount of merit.

“Beyond that, Subhūti, suppose that this very same bodhisattva great being were not only to give others the gift of the Dharma in a similar manner, and dedicate it to unsurpassed, perfect awakening using the particular dedication taught in the transcendent state of wisdom, but also, after dedicating it, were to fail to engage in meditation in retreat ever again. Then, Subhūti, this bodhisattva great being‍—who gave the gift of the Dharma in a similar manner while failing to engage in meditation in retreat‍—would not earn as much merit as another bodhisattva great being who not only gave the gift of the Dharma in a similar manner but also actually engaged in meditation in retreat again and again, while supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and while making sure the retreat never lacked the transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, such a bodhisattva great being would earn an even greater amount of merit.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, the Blessed One previously taught that a cumulative construction is a conceptualization. So, why is the Blessed One now saying that a bodhisattva great being would ‘earn more merit’?”There is some wordplay here. Subhūti’s question is making reference to the fact that the Blessed One uses the Sanskrit word abhisaṃskāra in both a negative and a positive sense in different places in this scripture. In the present positive context here, it is translated as “accumulation” (abhisaṃskāra, in relation to merit). But in an earlier negative context, it was translated as “cumulative construction” (abhisaṃskāra, in relation to physical forms and so on). For instance, in chapter 1, the Blessed One explains that bodhisattvas should not engage in the “cumulative construction” (abhisaṃskāra) of physical forms and so on because a “cumulative construction” is a conceptualization, i.e., a purely mental construct, rather than the transcendent state of wisdom (1.20, see also n.111). Thus, according to Ratnākara (F.158.b), since the Blessed One has repeatedly taught in this section here that a bodhisattva will “earn more merit,” Subhūti raises the following rhetorical objection: Since the “cumulative construction/accumulation” (i.e., of physical forms and so on) is a conceptualization and since the “cumulative construction/accumulation” of merit must also be like that, the words “much more merit” should amount to much more conceptualization. RK: iha bahutaraṃ puṇyaṃ prasavatī ti bāhulyenoktam| ataḥ subhūteś codyam abhisaṃskāro vikalpaḥ| tadvat puṇyābhisaṃskāro ’ pi| tasmād bahutaraṃ puṇyam iti bahutaro vikalpa uktaḥ syād iti. According to Hari (706), a cumulative construction, i.e., a mental effort, is a “conceptualization,” i.e., an error. So the question is why‍—given that any such act would be accompanied by karmic seeds of conceptualization‍—are you saying here that “he or she would earn more merit” at the time of dedication? Hari: abhisaṃskāraś cittābhogo vikalpo viparyāso yadā kathaṃ vikalpabījānugamāt tadā bahutaraṃ puṇyaṃ pariṇāmanākāle prasavatīty ucyate.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being who is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom at this point knows the accumulation of merit simply to be empty.According to Ratnākara (F.158.b), “at this point” refers to the time when a bodhisattva is on the path of cultivation. RK: idānīm iti bhāvanāmārgakāle. According to Hari (706), “at this point” refers (in particular) to the stage of eighth division of the path of cultivation. Hari: idānīm iti aṣṭamaprakārabhāvanāmārgāvasthāyāṃ. He or she knows it simply to be futile. He or she knows it simply to be useless. He or she knows it simply to be essenceless.According to Ratnākara (F.158.b), “empty” because it does not exist in accord with any characteristics. The Sanskrit suffix -ka (on the word śūnyaka, etc., in the text) is meant here in the sense of something unknown, i.e., the foolish do not know it simply to be “empty.” RK: yathālakṣaṇam asattvāc chūnyaḥ| ajñātārthe ka pratyayaḥ| bālair ajñātaḥ śūnyaka ity eva. And yet, Subhūti, the more that a bodhisattva great being considers everything to be this way, the more that he or she becomes someone who does not lack the transcendent state of wisdom. Also, Subhūti, the more that a bodhisattva great being becomes someone who does not lack the transcendent state of wisdom, the more that he or she earns merit that is immeasurable and incalculable.”
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, what is the difference or distinction between immeasurable and incalculable?”
The Blessed One said, “Subbūti, it is immeasurable because any measurements of it are rendered naught. Subhūti, it is incalculable because it is impossible to exhaust one’s calculations about it.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, could this be the manner in which physical forms are immeasurable? Likewise, could this be the manner in which feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are immeasurable?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, you asked, ‘Could this be the manner in which physical forms are immeasurable? Likewise, could this be the manner in which feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are immeasurable?’ Yes, Subbūti, this is the manner in which physical forms themselves are immeasurable. Likewise, this would be the manner in which feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness themselves are immeasurable.”
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, what does the term immeasurable refer to?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, the term immeasurable refers simply to emptiness. Subhūti, the term immeasurable refers simply to freedom from signs. Subhūti, the term immeasurable refers simply to freedom from inclinations.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, does the term immeasurable refer exclusively to emptiness? Blessed One, does the term immeasurable refer exclusively to freedom from signs? Blessed One, does the term immeasurable refer exclusively to freedom from inclinations? Does it not refer to other things?”
The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, what do you think? Have I not made it known that all things are empty?”
Subhūti said, “Blessed One, the Tathāgata has made it known that all things are simply empty.”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, anything that is empty is also inexhaustible, and its emptiness is also its immeasurability.According to Ratnākara (F.159.a), it is “inexhaustible” (akṣaya) like space. Like space, it is immeasurable. RK: akṣayā apī ti ākāśavat| aprameyātāpī ty ākāśasyeva. Therefore, Subhūti, no difference or distinction between such things is actually mentally perceived. Subhūti, the Tathāgata has made this known using the following expressions: immeasurable, incalculable, inexhaustible, emptiness, freedom from signs, freedom from inclinations, freedom from conditioning, freedom from arising, freedom from birth, freedom from any state of being, freedom from passion, cessation, and nirvāṇa.Note: The Sanskrit text here omits “discontinuation” (vigama), which was mentioned earlier in the chapter. It also includes “inexhaustible” (akṣayam), which was not in the original list, but was just mentioned previously. Subhūti, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata has proclaimed this explication as a result of his teaching.”According to Hari (710), this phrase means due to his explication that flows naturally from the Tathāgata’s compassion. Hari: tathāgatasya karuṇāniṣyanda­nirdeśatvād. According to Ratnākara (F.159.a), to break down the Sanskrit compound precisely, “his teaching” refers to his compassion, based on which he teaches. “As a result of his teaching” means as the natural outcome of his compassion. The result is itself the explication‍—i.e., the two words in the Sanskrit compound have a single locus. RK: deśanābhinirhāranirdeśa eṣa iti| deśyate ’nayeti deśanā karuṇā, tasyā ’bhinirhāro niṣyandaḥ, sa cāsau nirdeśaś ca. Cf. abhinirhāra (in dharmadeśanābhinirhāra), “skillful devices.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, it is amazing how well the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata teaches the true nature of all things, and yet the true nature of all things is inexpressible!According to Ratnākara (F.159.a), it is “inexpressible” because it is not an object of expression. RK: avāggocaratvād anabhilāpyāḥ. And, Blessed One, as I understand that which the Blessed One is explaining, it is that all things also are inexpressible.”
“You are right, Subhūti. You are right,” said the Blessed One. “Subhūti, all things are inexpressible. Why? Because, Subhūti, the emptiness of all things is something that cannot be expressed.”
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, is there an increase or a decrease in that inexpressible thing?”
“Definitely not, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One.

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, if such an inexpressible thing were neither to increase nor decrease, then the transcendent state of generosity also would neither increase nor decrease. It would be the same for the transcendent state of ethical discipline, the transcendent state of tolerant acceptance, the transcendent state of heroic perseverance, the transcendent state of meditation, and the transcendent state of wisdom‍—they too would neither increase nor decrease. And, Blessed One, if these six transcendent states were neither to increase nor decrease, then, Blessed One, how could any bodhisattva great beings ever realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening by way of the six transcendent states increasing? And how could they even really approach unsurpassed, perfect awakening? That is to say, Blessed One, bodhisattva great beings who do not perfect each transcendent state cannot even really approach unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”

“That is true, Subhūti. That is true,” said the Blessed One. “Subhūti, there is certainly no thing at the core of a transcendent state that increases or decreases.According to Ratnākara (F.159.a), “the thing at the core of a perfection” refers to the characteristic of emptiness. RK: pāramitārthasye ti śūnyatālakṣaṇasya. But also, Subhūti, while going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivating it, bodhisattva great beings with skill in means would never think that their transcendent state of generosity is either increasing or decreasing. Instead, they would know such a transcendent state of generosity to be nothing but a designation. Also, when giving others gifts, such bodhisattva great beings would dedicate their attentive mental states, developments of the proper mindset, and roots of virtue to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.159.a), they give others gifts with these “attentive mental states” and “developments of the proper mindset.” (They dedicate) the “roots of virtue” connected with those “developments of the proper mindset.” RK: sa dānaṃ dadat tān manasikārāṃs tāṃś cittotpādān iti yair dānaṃ dadāti| tāni ca kuśalamūlānī ti yāni tais cittotpādaiḥ saṃprayuktāni. And they would dedicate them in accord with that unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Hari (713), he is explaining that on the conventional level, the path of cultivation performs a function in accord with the way that complete, perfect awakening is. Hari: saṃvṛtyā tu yathānuttarā samyaksambodhis tathā bhāvanāmārgo ’rthakriyākārīti kathayann āha: api tu khalu punar ityādi. According to Ratnākara (F.159.a–.b), “they would dedicate them in accord with that unsurpassed, perfect awakening” means that the dedication to awakening cannot be in accord with something else. RK: yathā bodhis tathā pariṇāmayatī ti| anyathā na sā pariṇāmanā bodhaye syāt.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, while going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivating it, bodhisattva great beings with skill in means would never think that their transcendent state of ethical discipline is increasing or decreasing. Instead, they would know such a transcendent state of ethical discipline to be nothing but a designation. Also, when proceeding to uphold ethical discipline, such bodhisattva great beings would dedicate their attentive mental states, developments of the proper mindset, and roots of virtue to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. And they would dedicate them in accord with that unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, while going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivating it, bodhisattva great beings with skill in means would never think that their transcendent state of tolerant acceptance is increasing or decreasing. Instead, they would know such a transcendent state of tolerant acceptance to be nothing but a designation. Also, when practicing tolerant acceptance, such bodhisattva great beings would dedicate their attentive mental states, developments of the proper mindset, and roots of virtue to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. And they would dedicate them in accord with that unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, while going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivating it, bodhisattva great beings with skill in means would never think that their transcendent state of heroic perseverance is increasing or decreasing. Instead, they would know such a transcendent state of heroic perseverance to be nothing but a designation. Also, when undertaking heroic perseverance, such bodhisattva great beings would dedicate their attentive mental states, developments of the proper mindset, and roots of virtue to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. And they would dedicate them in accord with that unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, while going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivating it, bodhisattva great beings with skill in means would never think that their transcendent state of meditation is increasing or decreasing. Instead, they would know such a transcendent state of meditation to be nothing but a designation. Also, when attaining the meditative states, such bodhisattva great beings would dedicate their attentive mental states, developments of the proper mindset, and roots of virtue to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. And they would dedicate them in accord with that unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, while going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivating it, bodhisattva great beings with skill in means would never think that their transcendent state of wisdom is increasing or decreasing. Instead, they would know such a transcendent state of wisdom to be nothing but a designation. Also, when going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivating it, such bodhisattva great beings would dedicate their attentive mental states, developments of the proper mindset, and roots of virtue to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. And they would dedicate them in accord with that unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”
Then the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, what is unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, unsurpassed, perfect awakening is the actual nature. And, Subhūti, that actual nature neither increases nor decreases. If bodhisattva great beings abide continuously and repeatedly in attentive mental states connected to the actual nature, then they get close to unsurpassed, perfect awakening and their attentive mental states never decrease.

“Thus, Subhūti, there is neither increase nor decrease in that inexpressible thing. Subhūti, there is neither increase nor decrease in the transcendent states. Subhūti, there is neither increase nor decrease in all things. Therefore, Subhūti, bodhisattva great beings who abide in these kinds of attentive mental states are approaching unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Emptiness,” the eighteenth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 19: The Noble Lady GaṅgadevīAccording to Ratnākara (F.159.b), the chapter “The Noble Lady Gaṅgadevī” means the chapter characterized (lakṣita) by the noble lady Gaṅgadevī (whose role in it becomes clear only at the end). RK: gaṅgadevābhaginyā lakṣitaḥ parivartas tatparivartaḥ.The beginning of this chapter corresponds to the remainder of Chapter 41 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (starting at 41.47) and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 51 in the Eighteen Thousand (51.53). At the point marked by note 979, the chapter goes on to include content corresponding to the whole of Chapter 42 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 52 in the Eighteen Thousand; and at the point marked by note 1010, the chapter goes on to include content corresponding to the whole of Chapter 43 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 53 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: Then the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, does a bodhisattva great being realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening because of the first mindset of awakening to arise?The Sanskrit term cittotpāda means “arising of the mindset,” where the term “mindset” generally refers to any kind of mindset, i.e., proper or improper. In this part of the chapter it specifically refers to the mindset of awakening. To make this clearer in the main text, the initial occurrences of the word “mindset” have been translated as “mindset of awakening.” Or does a bodhisattva great being realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening because of the last mindset of awakening to arise? That is to say, Blessed One, the first mindset that arises does not coexist with the last mindset that arises, so, Blessed One, how could there be any accumulation of the roots of virtue by a bodhisattva great being?”According to Ratnākara (F.159.b), the idea of the objection here is that awakening (which arises from the accumulation of merit and wisdom) cannot be the result of a former and a subsequent mindset, because they do not coexist with each another. RK: na pūrveṇa cittena bodhir na ca paścimena tayor asamavadhānād iti codyam. According to Hari (717), “does not coexist with” means the former does not have an immediate connection with the latter, because, insofar as the first and last moments arise and dissolve without the presence of the other, they have no direct relation to one another. Given their lack of any direct relation, he asks, “So how could there be any…?” in order to imply that there actually could never be an “accumulation of the roots of virtue,” because there is no arising of a (single) mindset appearing with the (two) different objects (i.e., causes that must be accumulated). Hence, he is implying that there could be no perfect awakening. Hari: asamavahita iti paścimaprathamayor yathākramaṃ niranvayodayavināśena parasparam asambandhād asaṃśliṣṭaḥ. katham iti sambandhābhāvād viśiṣṭārtha­pratibhāsi­cittānutpādān naiva kuśalamūlānām upacayo bhavati. tato nānuttarā samyaksambodhir iti bhāvaḥ.

At this, the Blessed One asked the venerable Subhūti in return, “Subhūti, what do you think? In the burning out of an oil lamp, does the first flame to strike the wick burn it up or does the last flame to strike the wick burn it up?”According to Hari (718), “the first flame to strike” refers to the flame encountered in the first moment. Hari: prathamābhinipātene ti prathamakṣaṇamilitena. According to Ratnākara (F.159.b), this section is the rebuttal (of the above objection) by way of the example of a lamp. The Ornament states this section’s objection as follows: “Awakening is not connected to the former or the subsequent mindset. (Awakening is known) through the example of a lamp.” The Ornament then states the rebuttal: “The nature of things (which is awakening) is profound in eight ways.” The actual nature of pure awakening is the characteristic taught in the eighteen (types). But the characteristic of awakening with its retinue is taught as the eight types of profundity. RK: evamukta ityādi prāg evamuktā t| iyatā dīpadṛṣṭāntena parihāraḥ| atra śāstram‍— pūrveṇa bodhir no yuktā manasā paścimena vā||4-58ab|| iti codyam| dīpadṛṣṭāntayogena ||4-58c|| bodhir iti vartate| anena parihāraḥ||atha śāstram‍—gambhīrā dharmatāṣṭadhā…||4-58d|| yā bodhir iti vartate| kevalāyā bodhes tathataiva lakṣaṇam uktam aṣṭādaśe| saparicchadāyās tu bodher lakṣaṇam aṣṭavidhaṃ gāmbhīryam.

“Blessed One, certainly neither,” replied Subhūti. “Blessed One, the wick is not burned up because of the first flame striking it, and yet the wick cannot be burned up without the cause of that first flame striking it. Blessed One, the wick is not burned up because of the last flame striking it, and yet the wick cannot be burned up without the cause of that last flame striking it.”
The Blessed One asked, “But, Subhūti, what do you think? Does the wick get burned up?”
“Blessed One, it does get burned up,” said Subhūti. “Sugata, it does get burned up.”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, in the same way, the first mindset to arise does not cause the bodhisattva great being to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and yet he or she does not realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening without the cause of the first mindset having arisen. The last mindset to arise does not cause the bodhisattva great being to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and yet he or she does not realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening without the cause of the last mindset having arisen. He or she awakens neither because of those mindsets arising nor because of anything other than those mindsets arising‍—and yet the bodhisattva great being does realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”The translation follows the gloss of RK: anyatra tebhya iti vinā tebhyaḥ.

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, such a dependent arising is profound.According to Ratnākara (F.160.a), the following passage up to where it says “he or she intensively cultivates” is teaching the eight profundities (of the true nature of things). RK: evam ukte bhagavān ityādinā parijayaṃ karotī ty etad antenāṣṭavidhaṃ gāmbhīryam. The eight profundities belonging to the nature of things are listed in the Ornament as “the profundity in arising, ceasing, actual nature, knowledge objects, knowing, practice, nonduality, and skill in means.” Cf. RK: utpāde ca nirodhe ca tathatāyāṃ gabhīratā| jñeye jñāne ca caryāyām advayopāyakauśale|| AA 4.59. According to Hari (721), after specifying the resultant contingencies (above), in order to describe a bodhisattva’s characteristics that are beyond regressing (bhāvanāmārga), he now explains the scope of a bodhisattva on the path of cultivation in which there are eight types of profundities. Beginning with this sentence, he first describes the profundity of arising. Hari: prasaṅgāgataṃ nirdiśyedānīṃ bhāvanā­mārgastha­bodhi­sattvānām avaivartika­lakṣaṇakathanāya yasmin viṣaye ’ṣṭavidhaṃ gāmbhīryaṃ tad vaktavyam ity utpādagāmbhīryaṃ tāvat kathayann āha: gambhīro ’yaṃ bhagavann ityādi. Blessed One, the bodhisattva great being certainly does not realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening because of the first proper mindset arising, and yet he or she certainly does not realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening without the cause of the first proper mindset having arisen. He or she also certainly does not realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening because of the last proper mindset arising, and yet he or she certainly does not realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening without the cause of the last proper mindset having arisen. He or she certainly awakens neither because of those mindsets arising nor without those mindsets arising‍—and yet the bodhisattva great being does realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”
At this, the Blessed One asked the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, what do you think? Will his or her mindset that has ceased come to arise again?”

“Blessed One, definitely not,”According to Ratnākara (F.160.a), the “mindset”‍—i.e., the erroneous one that consists in the imagination of the unreal‍—“that has ceased” refers to a mindset exhausted due to the exhausting of (the ordinary sentient being’s) mental continuum (upon awakening at the level of a bodhisattva beyond regressing on the path of cultivation). He says “definitely not” (no hi<naiva) because the habitual fixation on subject and object that is the cause of error is exhausted‍—this is the idea. This ends the (first) profound aspect of arising. RK: yac cittam iti bhrāntam abhūta­parikalpātmakam| niruddham iti santānakṣayāt kṣīṇam| no hī ti naivety arthaḥ| bhrāntikāraṇasya dvayābhiniveśasya kṣayād iti bhāvaḥ| ity utpādagāmbhīryam. said Subhūti.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Does his or her mindset that has arisen have the quality of cessation?”
“Yes, Blessed One, it does have the quality of cessation,” said Subhūti.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Will his or her mindset that has the quality of cessation come to cease?”

“Blessed One, definitely not,”According to Ratnākara (F.160.a), the “mindset that has arisen” refers to the mindset free of error, because, insofar as its error has been exhausted but it depends upon a mindset, it is (talking about) the arising of the mindset (of an awakened being)‍—this is the basic idea. It has the “quality of cessation” moment by moment. “Will it cease?” asks the question whether that continuum will cease. He says “definitely not” because automatically its arising and its aspirations and so on are made exceedingly inexhaustible.
