RK: yac cittam utpannam iti abhrāntam| bhrāntikṣayāc cittādhīnatvāc ca cittotpatter iti bhāvaḥ| nirodhadharmi bhagavann iti pratikṣaṇam iti bhāvaḥ| nirotsyata iti santānanirodheneti bhāvaḥ| no hīdam iti svarasata utpatteḥ praṇidhānādibhiś ca saviśeṣam akṣayīkṛtatvād iti bhāvaḥ. According to Hari (721), in this passage “arisen” refers to a past thing in the process of perishing. Since, in reality, it has the nature of cessation, he says “the quality of cessation.” Regarding this term “quality of cessation,” the observed “quality” is “cessation,” i.e., emptiness. Hari: tatrotpannam atītaṃ vinaṣṭasattākam iti. tattvena nirodharūpatvād āha: nirodhadharmī ti nirodhaḥ śūnyatā sa eva dharmo ’sya vidyata iti nirodhadharmi.yāvat. Note: The Sanskrit editions have “not arisen” (anutpanna) in place of “arisen” (utpanna), but the translation here follows the Tibetan, which is confirmed by both Ratnākara’s and Hari’s explanations. said Subhūti.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Does that mindset that has not arisen have the quality of cessation?”According to Ratnākara (F.160.a), “that mindset that has not arisen” refers to what was taught in the profundity of arising immediately before as “not having the quality of cessation.” The repetition of those words‍—i.e., “not having the quality of cessation”‍—is now in relation to this (mindset). RK: yac cittam anutpannam ity utpādagāmbhīrye yad uktam yan na nirodhadharmī ty anantaram eva yad uktaṃ| yan nirodhadharmī ty asmiṃs teṣāṃ punaruktatā syāt.
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Does the mindset that has the quality of cessation cease?”
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Will the mindset that has the qualities of neither arising nor ceasing cease?”According to Ratnākara (F.160.a), “that mindset” refers to the mental continuum. Its having “the qualities of neither arising nor ceasing” means that it has neither beginning nor end. RK: yac cittam iti yac cittasantānaḥ| anutpādānirodhadharmī ty anādinidhanam.
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Will such a quality‍—which naturally has nothing but cessation as its essential nature‍—cease?”According to Ratnākara (F.160.a), “the quality” called nirvāṇa. The phrase “cessation as its essential nature” means it has the essential nature of having ceased, i.e., a cessation (nirodha<niruddham). RK: yo dharma iti nirvāṇākhyaḥ| nirodho niruddham| tatsvabhāvo ’syeti svabhāvaniruddhaḥ.
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Will this true nature of things cease?”According to Ratnākara (F.160.a), here “the true nature of things” means emptiness. This ends the section on the profundity of cessation. RK: dharmate ti śūnyatā| iti nirodhagāmbhīryam.
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Will the actual nature remain as it is?”
“Blessed One, the actual nature will remain as it is,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? If the actual nature will remain as it is, then would it not remain unchanging?”According to Ratnākara (F.160.a–b), “remain as it is” means to the limits of space; “would it not remain unchanging” means like an actual nature. He replies “definitely not” because the idea is that it lasts in the form of a stream. RK: tathaiva sthāsyatī ti yāvad ākāśam| mā kuṭasthā bhūd iti tathatāvat| no hīdam iti pravāhanityatvād iti bhāvaḥ.
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Is the actual nature profound?”

“Blessed One, it is profound,”According to Ratnākara (F.160.b), he says “the actual nature is profound” due to its subtlety, which he too is accurately understanding. This ends the section on the profundity of the actual nature. RK: tathaiva sthāsyatī ti yāvad ākāśam| mā kuṭasthā bhūd iti tathatāvat| no hīdam iti pravāhanityatvād iti bhāvaḥ| gambhīrā tathate ti samyagjñānād api sūkṣmatvād ity tathatāgāmbhīryam. said Subhūti.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Is there a mindset in the actual nature?”According to Ratnākara (F.160.b), a “mindset in the actual nature” means it is contained in that nature because of being aware of it. He says “definitely not” because that knowing is not connected to an object of knowing in the sense of something different (from it). RK: tathatāyāṃ cittam iti tadveditvāt tasyā ādheyam| no hīdam iti jñānād bhede vedyatvāyogāt.
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Is the mindset the actual nature?”According to Ratnākara (F.160.b), “is the mindset the actual nature” because of their single same characteristic (of emptiness)‍—this is the idea. RK: cittaṃ tathate ty ekalakṣaṇād iti bhāvaḥ.
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Is the mindset something other than the actual nature?”According to Ratnākara (F.160.b), “is the mindset other than the actual nature” due to a mere difference in characteristics. He says “definitely not” because it has the same nature despite its difference in characteristics. Thus, even though it is ungraspable, the actual nature is something to be aware of as such. This ends the section on the profundity of the object of knowledge. RK: anyat tathatāyā lakṣaṇabhedād eva| no hīdam iti lakṣaṇabhedepi tādātmyāt| tad evam agrāhyatve pi vedyā tathateti jñeyagāmbhīryam.
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, do you see the actual nature?”According to Ratnākara (F.160.b), the Blessed One asks “do you see” the nature of a mindset? He says “definitely not” because it is ungraspable. Yet, he knows the mind also to be precisely that which is grasped. This ends the section on the profundity of knowing. RK: samanupaśyasi tvam iti cittarūpam| no hī ty agrāhyatvāt| tad eva grāhyam api cittaṃ vettī ti jñānagāmbhīryam.
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Does someone going about life this way go about life in something profound?”

“Blessed One, someone going about life this way does not go about life in anything whatsoever,” said Subhūti. “Why not? Because for that person those practices do not arise and are not practiced.”According to Ratnākara (F.160.b), in the question “does someone go about life in something profound,” “something profound” implies an object of the transcendent state of wisdom. In Subhūti’s answer, “those practices” are conventional signs of meditative reference points. “Do not arise” means do not come into being. “Are not practiced” means do not make themselves known. RK: gambhīre caratī ti prajñā­pāramitārthe| samudācārā ālambananimittāni| na pravartante na bhavanti| na samudācaranti na prakhyāntīti caryāgāmbhīryam. This ends the section on the profundity of practice.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, what does he or she go about life in?”
“Blessed One, he or she goes about life in the ultimate reality,” said Subhūti.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? When a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the ultimate reality, does he or she go about life in a conventional sign?”

“Blessed One, definitely not,”According to Ratnākara (F.160.b), the question “does he or she go about in a conventional sign?” means either in a conventional sign of the two (i.e., subject and object) or in a conventional sign of freedom from that duality. The response is “definitely not,” i.e., neither in a conventional sign of duality (i.e., subject and object) nor in a conventional sign of freedom from that duality. RK: nimitte caratī ti dvayanimitte ’dvayanimitte vā| no hī ti na dvayanimitte nādvayanimitte. said Subhūti.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Is any conventional sign unabandoned by him or her?”According to Ratnākara (F.160.b), here “conventional sign” means either a dualistic or nondualistic conventional sign. “Unabandoned” means not cast off. He says it is “definitely not” unabandoned‍—(that is to say, it is a double negative meaning that all conventional signs) are abandoned. This ends the section on the profundity of the nondual. RK: nimittam iti dvayādvayanimittam| avibhāvitam aprahīṇam| no hī ti nāprahīṇam| prahīṇam evety arthaḥ| ity advayagāmbhīryam.
“Blessed One, definitely not,” said Subhūti.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? When a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, has he or she abandoned any conventional sign?”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being does not go about life thinking, ‘While practicing the bodhisattva’s way of going about life, how can I succeed in abandoning conventional signs in this very life?’ Still, if he or she were to succeed in it, he or she would become a disciple who had not accomplished any of the qualities of a buddha.According to Ratnākara (F.160.b), he or she becomes a “śrāvaka,” i.e., not a completely perfect buddha. RK: śrāvako bhaved iti na samyaksambuddhaḥ. Therefore, Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being has skill in means.According to Ratnākara (F.161.a), here he summarizes (the above) with the words from “Therefore…” and what follows in this paragraph. “He or she knows what a characteristic or a conventional sign is” means that he or she perceives all that is comprised by the conventional sign of a thing and the conventional sign of the nature of things. “He or she intensively cultivates” means nothing but repeatedly practices. This ends the section on the profundity of the skill in means. RK: etat tad ityādinopasaṃhāraḥ| yal lakṣaṇaṃ yan nimittam iti yad dharmanimittaṃ yac ca dharmatānimittaṃ tatsarvaṃ jānāti| parijayam abhyāsamātraṃ karoti| ity upāya­kauśala­gāmbhīryam. He or she knows what a conventional sign and a characteristic is, and intensively cultivates the freedom from signs.”According to Ratnākara (F.161.a), among these, the eighth profundity is supreme, whereas the others‍—i.e., the seven types of profundity taught (above)‍—are in the vicinity of awakening and classified as beyond regressing on the path of training (śaikṣa). He will (next) explain the sameness of cyclic existence and nirvāṇa (as it relates to the path of no more training). RK: tatrāṣṭamaṃ gāmbhīryaṃ viprakṛṣṭam| bodhe śeṣāṇi sannikṛṣṭānīty uktavidhaṃ gāmbhīryam| uktaś ca śaikṣau ’vaivartiko gaṇaḥ|| samatā bhavaśāntyoḥ vaktavyā. Note: RK’s comments end by stating that the “signs of nonregressing bodhisattvas” end here and the next Ornament subtopic (IV.ix) on the “sameness of cyclic existence and peace” begins.

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 42 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 52 in the Eighteen Thousand.Then the venerable Śāriputra asked the venerable Subhūti, “Venerable Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being cultivates the three gateways to complete liberation‍—namely, emptiness, freedom from signs, and freedom from inclinations‍—does the transcendent state of wisdom thrive in his or her dreams?”

Subhūti said, “Venerable Śāriputra, when the transcendent state of wisdom thrives for him or her because he or she cultivates it in the daytime, then it also thrives for him or her in a dream.The basic idea here seems to be that a bodhisattva’s daytime-like meditative concentration on the path of cultivation perfumes the sleep-like post-meditation until he or she begins to see cyclic existence and nirvāṇa as the same, not just in meditation but after getting up from it too. That is to say, according to Ratnākara (F.161.a), “dream” refers to the erroneous cognition for someone who has arisen (from meditative concentration into the post-meditation), because it is like a dream. However, “daytime” refers to the accurate cognition that constitutes the path of cultivation, because it is like daytime. When is the one connected to the other? He says that when his or her transcendent state of wisdom thrives due to practicing intensively during the daytime, (then it also thrives for him or her during a dream). RK: vyutthitasya hi jñānaṃ bhrāntaṃ svapnopamatvāt svapnaḥ| bhāvanāmārgas tu samyagjñānaṃ divasopamatvād divasaḥ| tatra kadā yuktaṃ syāt? yadi daivasikād abhyāsātiśayāt svapne ti prajñāpāramitā vivardheta. Note: The translation follows the Tibetan here, which differs only slightly from the Sanskrit edition. How so? Because, Venerable Śāriputra, the Blessed One taught both daytime and dreaming to be indistinguishable. If a bodhisattva great being were to arrive at the transcendent state of wisdom and go about life in that transcendent state of wisdom day in and day out, then because he or she repeatedly practices it, the transcendent state of wisdom would extend also into that bodhisattva great being’s dreams.”According to Ratnākara (F.161.a–b), “repeatedly practicing (ābhyāsataḥ) the transcendent state of wisdom” means practicing at the level of the path of cultivation. This ends the section on the sameness of cyclic existence and nirvāṇa. RK: prajñāpāramitābhyāsata iti bhāvanāmārgābhyāsataḥ| iti saṃsāranirvāṇasamatā.

Śāriputra asked, “But, Venerable Subhūti, what if a man or woman performs either a pure or impure karmic deed in a dream? Does he or she incur or accrue that karmic deed?”According to Ratnākara (F.161.b), when Śāriputra is speaking here, the word “dream” refers to nothing but a dream (i.e., unlike just above where it refers to the post-meditative illusion). RK: śāriputra āhe tyādi| iha svapnaśabdena svapna evocyate. According to Hari (729–30), the question is will he or she “accrue” (ācaya) due to the piling up of karmic events, or “incur” (upacaya) a karmic deed due to its having the capacity to produce results? Śāriptura’s basic qualm is that, just like in a dream, there would be no pure or impure karmic deed even during the daytime, because there would be no preparatory (intention to act) and so on. Hari: svarūpopacayād ācayaḥ. kāryajananasāmarthyad upacayaḥ. tadvat svapnatulyatvena prayogādīnām abhāvād divase ’pi śubhāśubhakarmaṇo ’bhāvaḥ syād iti bhāvaḥ.

Subhūti said, “Venerable Śāriputra, since the Blessed One said that all things are like dreams, that person would not incur or accrue any karmic deed.According to Hari (730), in this sentence he explains that ultimately there is no accruing or accumulating. But at the conventional level, he asserts a relationship between cause and effect (as Vasubandhu’s Twenty Verses says): “When dreaming, the mind is afflicted with sleepiness and hence its result is different.” In other words, Śāriputra’s dream example does not prove anything. Hence, he explains this in the next sentence. Hari: paramārthato naivācayopacayāv ity āha : yathā svapnopamā ityādi. saṃvṛtyā tu karma­phala­sambandhasyābhīṣṭatvān middhenopahataṃ cittaṃ svapne tenāsamaṃ phalam iti dṛṣṭāntāsiddhiḥ syād ity āha: atha punar āyuṣman ityādi. Note: The passage is referring to the Buddha’s knowing, upon awakening, of the destruction of the defilements and worldly influences. However, suppose, Venerable Śāriputra, that after waking from a dream that person were to think about it and develop a particular conception about having killed someone in a dream. Then he or she would incur or accrue the karmic deed of so doing. How, Venerable Śāriputra, would that person think about and develop a particular conception of their having killed someone? Suppose that after committing the murder in the dream, he or she were to be woken up and to think to him- or herself, ‘Hooray, he has been killed! It is great that he has been killed! It is for good reason that he has been killed! And I killed him!’According to Hari (730), in this case, the person thinks, “Hooray (aho), he has been killed! It is great that he has been killed! It is for good reason (suṣṭhu) that he has been killed!” because he or she had a preparatory (intention to act) and so on and harmed body, speech, and mind. Then, by taking (what he or she did in the dream) as applying to himself or herself in all cases, he or she thinks, “I killed him!” and the difference of meaning in this is to be explained (in the next sentence). Hari: tatra prayogādiniṣpādanāt kāyavākcittopaghātād cāho hataḥ sādhu hataḥ suṣṭhu hataḥ. sarva­trātmavyāpāropalambhān mayā hata ity arthabhedo vācyaḥ. Thinking like this while awake, he or she develops a particular conception about having killed someone.”According to Hari (730), the Madhyamaka holds that even though a thing (vastu) in the waking state is similar to a dream in the sense of its verisimilitude, karmic deeds that are virtuous and so on are established for those who have not broken all the bonds of error, because they are fixated on existence and so on. Nevertheless, unclear, nonvirtuous behavior (pratipatti) in a dream brings a reduced result, because its cause is more erroneous due to sleepiness‍—while a clear, unconfused false appearance (pratibhāsa) in the waking state brings a greater result because it lacks a cause that is more erroneous due to sleepiness. Hari: ato madhyamakanayena jāgradavasthāyām api svapnasadṛśe vastuni tadanurūpārthe bhāvādyabhiniveśenākhaṇḍita­sakala­viparyāsa­bandhanānāṃ kuśalādayo vyavasthāpyante. kiṃtu middhādhika­bhrānti­kāraṇatvenāspaṣṭākuśala­pratipattyā svapne nyūnaṃ phalaṃ. jāgradavasthāyāṃ tu middhādikabhrāntikāraṇavigamāt spaṣṭānākulapratibhāsatvenādhikaṃ phalaṃ.

Śāriputra said, “Venerable Subhūti, suppose that a man were to be woken up and to think, ‘Hooray, he has been killed! It is great that he has been killed! It is for good reason that he has been killed! And I killed him!’ Does he incur or accrue a karmic deed? A blessed buddha also develops a particular conception about his own dissolution, so would he incur or accrue a karmic deed too?”According to Ratnākara (F.161.b), in the phrase “a particular conception about his dissolution,” the word “dissolution” means nirvāṇa and “particular conception” means his comprehension of it. RK: kṣayasaṃjñe ti kṣayo nirvāṇaṃ tasya saṃjñā udgrahaḥ.

Subhūti said, “Venerable Śāriputra, definitely not. Why is that? It is because a tathāgata has let go of all thinking and conceptualizing.According to Ratnākara (F.161.b), here the “thinking” concerns real things, whereas the “conceptualizing” is the fabrication of something false. RK: bhutārthakalpanaṃ kalpaḥ| vi tathakalpa naṃ vikalpaḥ. Venerable Śāriputra, he is, as an analogy, just like pure space. Without an objective basis, karmic deeds do not arise. Without an objective basis, a mindset does not arise. That is to say, Venerable Śāriputra, karmic deeds arise only with an objective basis, not without an objective basis. A mindset arises only with an objective basis, not without an objective basis. Venerable Śāriputra, the mind moves toward things seen, heard, thought, and discerned. In those things, some minds apprehend defilement, whereas other minds apprehend purification. It is then because of this, Venerable Śāriputra, that mental activity arises only with an objective basis, not without an objective basis.According to Ratnākara (F.161.b), here the words “mindset,” “mental activity,” “mind,” and “conceptualization” all share a single meaning. RK: cittaṃ cetanā buddhir vikalpaḥ ity eko ’rthaḥ. Karmic deeds arise only with an objective basis, not without an objective basis.”

Śāriputra said, “Venerable Subhūti, the Blessed One described all objective bases as void.According to Ratnākara (F.161.b), “void” refers to something devoid of any inherent nature. RK: viviktānī ti niḥsvabhāvāni. So why, Venerable Subhūti, does mental activity arise only with an objective basis, not without an objective basis?”

Subhūti said, “Venerable Śāriputra, first, an objective basis that is not found is turned into a conventional sign and turned into an objective basis.According to Ratnākara (F.161.b), the deluded mind first makes something nonexistent into a conventional sign, then into objective bases. RK: viplutā hi buddhir avidyamānam eva nimittīkṛtyā rambaṇī karoti. Next, a mental activity arises with that objective basis and not without that objective basis.Here, there is a play on the words “not being found or known” (avidyamāna) and “ignorance or lacking in knowledge” (avidyā) reminiscent of a similar passage in chapter 1 (see 1.46 and n.142). RK: viplutā hi buddhir avidyamānam eva nimittīkṛtyā rambaṇī karoti. Venerable Śāriputra, that mental activity is void and that conventional sign also is void. Likewise, the karmic conditioning caused by ignorance also is void. The consciousness caused by karmic conditioning factors also is void. The same is true up to aging and death caused by birth‍—all are also void.In other words, all twelve links of dependent arising are void. In this very same way, Venerable Śāriputra, all objective bases are void. Mental activity devoid of conventional signs is said to arise on account of worldly conventions.”According to Hari (733), “on account of worldly conventions” means concerning the conventional level. The Sanskrit word iti here implies the words “is said to” (which have been added in the main text above). Hari: lokavyavahāram upādāye ti saṃvṛtim adhikṛtya. utpadyata iti anantaram ucyata iti śeṣaḥ.

Śāriputra said, “Venerable Subhūti, suppose that a bodhisattva great being were to give a gift in a dream and dedicate that gift to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Would such a gift be described as something ‘dedicated’?”

Subhūti said, “Venerable Śāriputra, the bodhisattva great being Maitreya is standing right here. He has been foreordained by the Tathāgata for unsurpassed, perfect awakening and bears direct bodily witness to the facts of the matter. You should ask him and he will respond to the question at hand.”According to Ratnākara (F.161.b), in the phrase “bears direct bodily witness,” the word body refers to the basis (i.e., the container consciousness). By transforming that basis, one becomes a realized being, i.e., direct bodily witness. “This question” refers to the controversial point raised. RK: kāyasākṣī ti kāya āśrayaḥ| tatparāvṛttyā kāyena sākṣ ātkārī kāyasākṣī| enam artham ity etac codyaṃ. According to Hari (734), a bearer of direct bodily witness is a non-returner who has attained cessation, at the end of the path to the state of a worthy one. Similarly, the noble Maitreya is called a bearer of direct bodily witness because he is at the end of the path to buddhahood. “The question at hand” is whether a gift in a dream is (actually) dedicated (to the awakening of all beings, etc.). Hari: kāyasākṣī ti arhattvapratipannako ’nāgāmī nirodhalābhī kāyasākṣī. tadvad buddhatva­pratipanna­katvenārya­maitreyaḥ kāyasākṣī. enam artham iti svapne pariṇāmitaṃ kiṃ tad dānam iti praśnaṃ.

Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the bodhisattva great being Maitreya, “The elder Subhūti has said that the bodhisattva great being Maitreya is here and he will respond to the question at hand. So please, Venerable Unconquerable One, respond to this question.”According to Ratnākara (F.161.b), the “Unconquerable One” is an epithet of Maitreya. RK: ajito maitreya iti paryāyau.

Then the bodhisattva great being Maitreya said to Subhūti, “The elder Subhūti has said that the bodhisattva great being Maitreya is here and he will respond to the question at hand. But, Venerable Subhūti, what is it that has the designation ‘Maitreya’? Will it respond to the question at hand? Or, will its physical forms respond? Will its feelings, its conceptions, its conditioning factors, or perhaps its consciousness respond? Or else will its color or perhaps its shape respond? Or else, will its emptiness of physical forms respond? Or else, will its emptiness of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness respond? Certainly, Subhūti, its emptiness of physical forms has no capacity to respond. Certainly, likewise, its emptiness of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness has no capacity to respond. Furthermore, Venerable Subhūti, I do not identify anything that can respond. Nor do I identify anything to which I can respond. Nor do I identify anything by means of which I can respond. Nor do I identify anything to whom I can respond. Nor do I identify anything foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”According to Ratnākara (F.161.b), the phrase “anything to which I can respond” refers to the question itself; “anything by means of which I can respond” refers to words; and “anything to whom I can respond” refers to Śāriputra himself. RK: visarjayitavya iti praśnaḥ| yena dharmeṇe ti vācā| yasya dharmasye ti śāriputrasya. Note: The sentence “Nor do I identify a thing to whom (yasya dharmasya) I can respond” does not appear in the Sanskrit or Tibetan editions, but appears in Ratnākara’s comments, so it has been included in the translation of the main text.

Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the bodhisattva great being Maitreya, “Venerable Maitreya, I suppose that you have actualized these things in the manner that you verbally describe them?”According to Hari (736), “I suppose” means “if” (i.e., he is wondering whether Maitreya has actualized them). Hari: kaccid iti yadi.

Maitreya said, “Venerable Śāriputra, I have not actualized these things in the manner that I verbally describe them. Nor, Venerable Śāriputra, do I even find, mentally perceive, or identify these things the way that I verbally describe or mentally consider them. Certainly, Venerable Śāriputra, these things cannot be physically encountered, or verbally described, or mentally focused upon. All things are by nature like this, because they lack any inherent nature.”

Then Śāriputra thought to himself, “Oh! This bodhisattva great being Maitreya is someone with profound wisdom. He explicates the transcendent state of wisdom as though he has gone about life in it for a long time.”

Then the Blessed One addressed the venerable Śāriputra as follows: “Śāriputra, why did you think, ‘Oh, the bodhisattva great being Maitreya is someone with profound wisdom?’ Are you revealing that you identify a thing that a worthy one is endowed with?”According to Hari (737), the Blessed One asks “why…” because Śāriputra’s comprehension is only similar to (that of) the disciples. Śāriputra calls it “profound wisdom” because his comprehension of it is equivalent to the merely nonconceptual knowing (that is the scope of a disciple, not of a bodhisattva)‍—so the Blessed One means to say here that Śāriputra should not form such a notion (of Maitreya’s wisdom). To explain this very equivalence, he asks, “Are you revealing that you identity the thing…?” In this sentence, he is asking to be precise: When you comprehend it through your conceptual knowledge, do “you identify”‑‍—i.e., apprehend‍—“the thing,” i.e., the state of a worthy one, which is characterized by the knowledge that (suffering and its cause have been) extinguished and will not re-arise? Hari: śrāvakāṇām īdṛśa evādhigama ity āha : kutas te śāriputre tyādi. nirvikalpa­jñāna­mātreṇādhigamasya tulyatvād gambhīraprajño ’yam iti naivaṃcittam utpādanīyam ity arthaḥ. tulyatvam eva kathayann āha : samanupaśyasi tvam ityādi. taṃ dharmaṃ kṣayānutpādajñānalakṣaṇam arhattvaṃ vikalpajñānenādhigamakāle kiṃ tvaṃ samanupaśyasy upalabhasa iti yāvat.
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Śāriputra.

The Blessed One said, “Likewise, Śāriputra, a bodhisattva great being going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom does not think, ‘This thing has previously been foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening’; ‘This thing is now being foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening’; ‘This thing will soon be foreordained’; or ‘This thing will ever realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’The Sanskrit and Tibetan repetition and word order in this quoted thought have been altered slightly for the sake of clarity in English. Any bodhisattva great being who goes about life in this way is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. And while going about life in this way, he or she is not frightened, not terrified, and not stricken with terror. He or she engages in meditation knowing that he or she possesses a reserve of strength, so it is not the case that he or she will not become completely awakened.According to Ratnākara (F.161.b), “a reserve of strength” means a latent mental impression with the capacity for self-cultivation. RK: balādhānaṃ bhāvanābalavāsanā. If he or she goes about life in this way, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Moreover, if a bodhisattva great being were in a wilderness area infested with carnivorous animals, he or she would not be frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror.According to Hari (738), right after the above, with this sentence, he starts to explain the twofold purification of a buddhafield by separating the world into (first) the inhabitants and (then) the environment. Hari: ity anantaraṃ sattvabhājanalokabhedena dvividhabuddhakṣetraviśuddhiṃ nirdiśann āha: punar aparaṃ śāriputre tyādi. Why not? Because that bodhisattva great being would have completely given up everything for the sake of all sentient beings. He or she would develop the proper mindset, thinking, ‘If carnivorous animals eat me, let me give that gift of myself wholly to them, so that I perfect the transcendent state of generosity and approach unsurpassed, perfect awakening. I will do this, so that when I have realized that unsurpassed, perfect awakening, in my buddhafield there will be no sentient beings born as animals and none known to exist absolutely anywhere in any form or manner. Instead, all beings there will live on divine food.’According to Ratnākara (F.162.a), “all beings there” in that buddhafield “will live on divine food.” He says this in order to exclude carnivorousness and so on. RK: divyopabhoge tyādi| samabuddhakṣetrasattvā iti vartate| rudhirādyāhāravyudāsāya caitad uktam.

“Moreover, if a bodhisattva great being were in a wilderness area infested with thieves, he or she would not be frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror. Why not? Because a bodhisattva great being delights in the roots of virtue by completely giving up all his or her possessions.The translation “the roots of virtue” here follows Tibetan (dge ba’i rtsa ba la) as opposed to the Sanskrit edition’s “virtue” (kuśala). He or she would have offered up even his or her body and completely given up all other requisites or other means of subsistence. Thus, he or she would develop the proper mindset, thinking, ‘If any beings were to steal from me all the requisites and other means of subsistence, let me give that gift of myself wholly to them. If they even were to take my life, I resolve never to develop malevolence, anger, or rage toward them and also never to harm them physically, verbally, or mentally. In this way, at that time, I will perfect the transcendent state of generosity, the transcendent state of ethical discipline, and the transcendent state of tolerant acceptance as I approach unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Also, I will do this and apply myself so that, when I have realized unsurpassed awakening, in my buddhafield there will be no wildernesses infested with thieves and none will be known to exist absolutely anywhere in any form or manner. In this way, I will strive to purify my buddhafield, so that when I have realized unsurpassed awakening, in my buddhafield there will be no defects such as these or others, and none will be known to exist absolutely anywhere in any form or manner.’The above two paragraphs regarding wild animals and thieves concern purification of the world’s inhabitants. The next three paragraphs concern the world’s environment.

“Moreover, if a bodhisattva great being were in a wilderness area that lacked drinkable water, he or she would not be frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror. Why not? Because a bodhisattva great being has the quality of not being terrified or afraid. Hence, he or she would develop the proper mindset, thinking, ‘I should train for the sake of eliminating any thirst that sentient beings may have.’ Also, a bodhisattva great being would not be be frightened, not be terrified, and not be stricken with terror with the idea that if he or she were to die from thirst, his or her next rebirth would be in the hungry ghost realm. Instead, he or she would develop the proper mindset of great compassion, thinking, ‘Alas, these poor sentient beings must have little merit for them to know a world with wilderness areas lacking any drinkable water like these. Again, I will do this and apply myself so that when I have realized unsurpassed awakening, in my buddhafield there will be no wilderness areas lacking drinkable water, and none will be known to exist absolutely anywhere in any form or manner. Furthermore, I will load all beings up with merit, so that they will find drinkable water endowed with the eight qualities.According to Hari (742), they will find drinkable water endowed (upeta) with the eight qualities (aṣṭāṅga), because they get “water that is (1) pleasant-smelling (sugandhi), (2) pleasant tasting (svādu), (3) cool (śītalaṃ), (4) soft (laghu), (5) clear (accha), (6) pure (śuci), and not harmful (7) to the stomach or (8) to the throat to drink it.” Hari: aṣṭāṅgoptapānīyaṃ sugandhi svādu śītalaṃ laghv acchaṃ śuci pātuś ca kukṣikaṇṭhau na bādhata || ity udakalābhād aṣṭāṅgopetapānīyalābhinaḥ. I will apply myself with unwavering heroic perseverance and with respect toward all sentient beings, so that, at that time, I will perfect the transcendent state of heroic perseverance.’

“Moreover, if a bodhisattva great being were in a wilderness area that lacked food, he or she would not be frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror. For this reason, he or she would don the armor, thinking, ‘I will apply myself with unwavering heroic perseverance and purify my own buddhafield so that when I have realized unsurpassed awakening, in my buddhafield there will be no wilderness areas without food like these, and none will be known to exist absolutely anywhere in any form or manner. Those sentient beings will only ever be comfortable, bestowed every delight, and filled with happiness.According to Hari (742), “comfortable,” “bestowed every delight,” and “filled with happiness” are, respectively, lesser, middling, and greater degrees (of happiness). Hari: mṛdumadhyādhimātrabhedena sukhitāḥ sukhasamaṅginaḥ sarvasukhasamarpitāḥ. Also, I will do this so that absolutely anything that sentient beings wish for and absolutely anything that their hearts desire will come to manifest for them. It will be just like, as an analogy, everything manifesting for the gods in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, just by their thinking about it. With such unwavering heroic perseverance, I will apply myself so that everything will manifest and come to be had by sentient beings, just by their thinking about it. Furthermore, I will strive to purify my own mind for the sake of all sentient beings, so that, at that time, I can perfect the transcendent state of meditation to ensure that their spiritual aspirations are fulfilled and no sentient being anywhere ever lacks life’s necessities in any way.’

“Moreover, if a bodhisattva great being were in a wilderness area plagued with disease, he or she would not be frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror. Instead, he or she would reflect upon, contemplate, and consider there to be no thing that is afflicted by disease and no thing that has the name disease. Thus, he or she would consider it to be empty and would be neither frightened by it, nor terrified by it, nor stricken with terror about it.

“Furthermore, such a bodhisattva great being would never develop a mindset that thinks it will take ‘a long time’ to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening‍—nor at such a thought would he or she be frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror. Why not? Because a moment of thought involves a limitation that is a point in time with no beginning, which is to say, it is beyond any point in time. So, that bodhisattva great being would never form the notion of it being difficult, somehow thinking this point in time with no beginning takes so much time or takes such a long time.According to Hari (743), that “moment of thought,” which has not arisen at the ultimate level, lasts a long time at the conventional level. It is “a point in time with no beginning”, i.e., it is beginningless, because it lacks an initial cause. Its endpoint (paryanto bhāgaḥ) is “beyond any point in time” because, at the stage of a buddha, its nature is emptiness. For this reason, a bodhisattva would not be frightened and so on by his or her realization taking a very long time. This is the idea behind him saying the bodhisattva would “never form (utpādayitavyā) the notion of it being difficult.” Hari: yasmād yaś cittakṣaṇas tattvato ’nutpannaḥ saṃvṛtyā saiṣā cirakālavatī prathama­kāraṇarahitatvād anādir apūrvā koṭiḥ. paryanto bhāgo buddhatvāvasthāśūnyatā­svabhāvatvād yad utākoṭis. tasmāc cireṇābhisaṃbodhād uttrāsādi na kartavyaṃ. duṣkarasaṃjñā ca notpādayitavye ty arthaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.162.a), the “limitation” (iyatī) is a measurement circumscribing the “moment of thought.” It is “with no beginning” means it is something in the future. It is “beyond any point in time” because a point in time of a moment without parts does not exist. RK: iyatī ti cittakṣaṇa parimāṇā| apūrve ti bhāvinī| yad utākoṭīr iti na hi niraṃśasya kṣaṇa sya koṭir asti. Note: Ratnākara’s and Hari’s divergent explanations appear to disagree in their interpretation of the Sanskrit word apūrva here. It seems that for Hari, the word apūrva is indicating a “beginningless” point in time, i.e., the beginningless past. Whereas, for Ratnākara, the word is indicating a point in time that is “has not begun,” i.e., “the future” (bhāvinī). Both the available editions in Sanskrit and Tibetan for Ratnākara are corrupt here, but seem to imply that his interpretation of it as “the future” is based on a quote from The Perfection of Wisdom in One Hundred Thousand Lines. The long sūtras have no real equivalent of the present passage at the equivalent point in their structure, and Ratnākara’s mention of the Hundred Thousand is not a verbatim quote, so we have not been able to identify definitively within it the passage to which he is referring, but it is possibly one in chapter 12, 12.201. See Ratnākara (F.162.a): ma byung ba ni sngon med pa ste| shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa stong phrag brgya ba las gsungs so. For inasmuch as this idea is associated with a single moment of thought, that is a point in time with no beginning, which is to say, it is beyond any point in time.According to Ratnākara (F.162.a), “that” which was the thing “with no beginning” (yet) is “a point in time with no beginning” into which he or she will “realize” awakening. This ends the section on the purification of one’s buddhafield. RK: yā se ty apūrvārthābhut (?) āpūrvā koṭīr yatrā bhisaṃbhotsye | iti buddhakṣetrapariśuddhiḥ. In this way, Śāriputra, the bodhisattva great being would never be frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror, thinking he or she will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening ‘in a long time.’To resolve an ambiguity in the Sanskrit, this translation has here followed the Tibetan: shA ri’i bu byang chub sems dpa’ sems dpa’ chen po des ’di ltar dka’ ba’i ’du shes bskyed par mi bya ste | sngon med pa’i mtha’ ma ’di ni mang zhing yun ring ba’o sngon med pa’i mtha’ ma ’di ni sems kyi skad cig ma gcig dang ldan pa ste ’di lta ste mtha’ med pa’o snyam mo. Note: The Tibetan in the Degé Kangyur has gi as a typo for mi. This may have misled Conze’s opposite interpretation which says that a bodhisattva “should think that great and long is this limit which has no beginning, for it is connected with one single thought-moment, in other words, it is the absence of a limit.” But, Śāriputra, if a bodhisattva great being is neither frightened, nor terrified, nor stricken with terror about these dangers or other sources of fear, whether they be seen, heard, considered, or known, then one can be sure, Śāriputra, that such a noble son or daughter is able to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Finally, Śāriputra, in this way the bodhisattva great being would don the great armor, thinking, ‘I will do this and apply myself with unwavering heroic perseverance so that when I have realized unsurpassed awakening, in my buddhafield all sentient beings will be free of all disease, and no disease will be known to exist absolutely anywhere in any form or manner. Also, I will do this so that I can teach what the tathāgatas taught and act in accord with what they taught. Furthermore, I will cultivate the transcendent state of wisdom for the sake of all sentient beings, so that, at that time, I will perfect the transcendent state of wisdom.’ ”

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 43 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 53 in the Eighteen Thousand.Then a certain woman who had come and taken her seat in that assembly stood up from her seat. She put her upper robe over one shoulder, placed her right knee on the ground, and then prostrated to the Blessed One with her hands cupped together in reverence. She then said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, I am not frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror in such places.According to Hari (743), “in such places” means in the places mentioned for purifying one’s buddhafield, because she had the power through her repeated practice. RK: atra sthāna iti abhyāsayogena śakyatvān nirdiṣṭabuddha­kṣetra­pariśodhane. Without being terrified or frightened, I shall teach the Dharma for the sake of sentient beings.”

Then, at that moment, the Blessed One appeared with a golden-colored smile that emanated light to the infinite, limitless world systems. The light shone up as far as the Brahmā world, returned once again, circled the Blessed One to the right three times, and then disappeared into the Blessed One’s forehead. Immediately after the Blessed One appeared with that smile, the woman took yellow flowers and showered them upon the Blessed One and scattered them around. But the yellow flowers remained suspended in midair without touching him.

Then the venerable Ānanda stood up from his seat. He put his upper robe over one shoulder, placed his right knee on the ground, and then prostrated to the Blessed One with his hands cupped together in reverence. After that, he said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas do not smile without a cause or conditions. So, what is the cause and what are the conditions for you smiling?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Ānanda, “Ānanda, this is the woman Gaṅgadevī. In the future she will become the tathāgata named Yellow-Flowered.The translation of the Sanskrit suvarṇa as “yellow” rather than “golden” is, first of all, because suvarṇa often just means “yellow” and, second, because the story (later in the chapter) suggests her flowers were an offering inferior to the blue lotuses (utpala) offered by the Buddha in his previous life. She will be a worthy, well-gone, perfect buddha. She will be perfected in both knowledge and appropriate conduct. She will have the supreme knowledge of the world. As a blessed buddha, she will be a leader among those people to be tamed and will teach gods and humans alike. She will appear in the world during the Star-Like eon and realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Ānanda, the noble lady Gaṅgadevī will leave behind her female form, take on a male form after she has passed away from here, and be reborn in the Abhirati buddhafield world system belonging to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Akṣobhya. Once reborn there in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Akṣobhya, she will engage in spiritual practice. Then, when she passes away from there, she will travel from that buddhafield to the next buddhafield without losing sight of the tathāgata. Again, from that buddhafield to other buddhafields, she will travel without ever being separated from the blessed buddhas. As an analogy, it will be just like a universal emperor traveling from palace to palace throughout his whole life, without the soles of his feet ever stepping on the surface of the earth. Just as that universal emperor, up to his time of death, would pass away without ever stepping upon the earth with the soles of his feet, Ānanda, just so the noble lady Gaṅgadevī too will travel from buddhafield to buddhafield without ever being separated from the blessed buddhas, all the way up until she realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”

Then the venerable Ānanda thought, “This very tathāgata’s assembly must be considered the bodhisattva great beings who will come to be there in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Akṣobhya.”According to Hari (746), Ānanda is astonished at the Blessed One’s predictions that just emerged about her (in this foreordaining of her future awakening). Hence the Blessed One explains more in the next sentence. Hari: vyākṛtānām eva tatrotpādād vismaya ity āha : atha khalv āyuṣmata ityādi.

Then the Blessed One knew in his mind the kind of ruminations that the venerable Ānanda was having and said to him, “Yes, Ānanda, it is true. Ānanda, any bodhisattva great beings who escape the saṃsāric mud and engage in spiritual practice in the buddhafield of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Akṣobhya should be known as close to actualizing awakening.According to Hari (746), “escape the saṃsāric mud” (uttīrṇapaṅkās) due to eliminating the obscuration of the defilements and “close to actualizing awakening” because of being established in realizing the culmination (i.e., the fifth of eight realizations explained in the Ornament). Hari: tatra kleśāvaraṇaprahāṇād uttīrṇapaṅkāḥ. mūrdhābhisamaye ’vasthitatvād bodhipariniṣpattyupagatāḥ. But, Ānanda, this worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Yellow-Flowered will have a community of disciples that no measure could capture. Why not? Ānanda, since there would be no measuring the number of disciples surrounding him, those disciples will instead be reckoned to be immeasurable or innumerable.According to Hari (746), here the Blessed One is also foreordaining (the assembly as) her future disciple community. Hari: śrāvakasaṃgham api vyākurvann āha: tasya khalu punar ityādi. Furthermore, Ānanda, during the time when this buddhafield is present, there will be no wilderness areas infested with carnivorous animals or with thieves. There will be no wilderness areas without drinkable water, without food, or plagued with disease. Ānanda, there will be no unpleasant wilderness areas such as these or others, and none will be known to exist absolutely anywhere in any form or manner. Ānanda, at the time when the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Yellow-Flowered has realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, there will not be any of these types of dangerous and frightening wilderness areas, and none will be known to exist absolutely anywhere in any form or manner.”

At this, the venerable Ānanda asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, in the presence of which tathāgata did the noble lady Gaṅgadevī plant the root of virtue by first developing the proper mindset aimed at unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Ānanda, “Ānanda, the noble lady Gaṅgadevī planted the root of virtue by first developing the proper mindset and dedicated it to unsurpassed, perfect awakening in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Dīpaṃkara.Dīpaṃkara was the buddha in the past under whose guidance the Buddha Śākyamuni, in a distant previous life, first made the vow to attain buddhahood and from whom he received the foreordaining of his future attainment (vyākaraṇa), as recounted in this passage and in many other accounts. In many texts he is counted as the fourth in a lineage of twenty-seven buddhas who preceded Śākyamuni. While praying for unsurpassed, perfect awakening, she showered the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Dīpaṃkara with nothing but yellow flowers. When I myself showered the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Dīpaṃkara with the five blue lotuses, I attained the state of acceptance that all things are free of arising, and after that I was foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Dīpaṃkara. He said, ‘In the future, young man, you will become the tathāgata named Śākyamuṇi. You will be a worthy, well-gone, perfect buddha. You will be perfected in both knowledge and appropriate conduct. You will have supreme knowledge of the world. As a blessed buddha, you will be a leader of persons to be tamed and a teacher to gods and humans alike.’ At the time, the noble lady heard his foreordainment of me and she developed the proper mindset, thinking, ‘Aha! Just as this young man was foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening, so too must I become foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’ And thus, Ānanda, in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Dīpaṃkara, the noble lady came to plant the root of virtue by first developing the proper mindset aimed at unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”

At this, the venerable Ānanda said to the Blessed One, “Ah! Blessed One, it is because she has achieved the preliminary practices and has gone to the very end that the noble lady Gaṅgadevī is foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Ānanda, “Yes, Ānanda, it is true what you say: ‘It is because she has achieved the preliminary practices and has gone to the end that the noble lady Gaṅgadevī is foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’ ”According to Hari (748), it is because this foreordainment of all the aspects (related to her future awakening) is in accord with her original aspirations that (the Blessed One endorses) this statement (of Ānanda’s). In it, “due to having achieved the preliminary practices” means due to having reached the path of seeing; due to having “gone to the very end” means due to her having understood the nature of the path of cultivation. Hari: praṇidhānānurūpam evedaṃ sarvaprakāravyākaraṇam ity āha: kṛtaparikarme tyādi. tatra prāptadarśanamārgatvāt kṛtaparikarmā. viditabhāvanāpathasvabhāvatvāt kṛtaparyantā.
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Noble Lady Gaṅgadevī,” the nineteenth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 20: Examining Skill in MeansAccording to Ratnākara (F.162.b), “Examining Skill in Means” means fully cognizing it. This chapter has that as its central topic. RK: upāyakauśal yānāṃ mīmāṃsā parijñānaṃ tadarthaḥ parivartaḥ.The beginning of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 44 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 54 in the Eighteen Thousand. At the point marked by note 1054, the chapter goes on to include content corresponding to a first part of Chapter 45 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (down to 45.8) and Hundred Thousand, and to Chapter 55 in the Eighteen Thousand (down to 55.9).
div.p: Then the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, how should a bodhisattva great being, who is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, cultivate emptiness? How should he or she engage in the meditative concentration on emptiness?”According to Ratnākara (F.162.b), since the factors in awakening and so on elliptically imply the freedom from signs and the freedom from inclinations only by means of the meditative concentration on emptiness, the word “emptiness” (in the first question) is a synecdoche referring to meditative concentration on emptiness. RK: śūnyatāsamādhinaivānimittāpraṇihitayor bodhipakṣādīnāṃ copalakṣaṇāt| śūnyatāyām iti śūnyatāsamādhau. Note: In other words, Ratnākara is giving a technical reason why emptiness here should be understood as the meditative concentration on emptiness, i.e., one of the three gateways to liberation.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being who goes about life in this transcendent state of wisdom should consider physical forms to be empty. Likewise, he or she should consider feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness to be empty. But he or she should consider this with a mental continuum that is not distracted, so that while considering ‘physical forms,’ he or she would not identify such a true nature of things as the true nature of things.According to Ratnākara (F.162.b), in this sentence “mental continuum” means the unbroken series (of moments of mind). The word “such” refers to the emptiness, which is the “true nature of things,” i.e., the essential nature. The phrase “as a true nature of a things” means as a solid essence or core. RK: santatiḥ pravāhaḥ| kathaṃ pratyavekṣamāṇa ity āha rūpaṃ śunyam itī ti| tām iti śūnyatām| dharmatām iti dharmaprakṛtim| dharmataye ti dharmasāratayā. According to Hari (749), the two repetitions of “true nature of things” both refer to emptiness. The term “physical forms” means real things. The idea is that he or she should consider carefully so that they do not mentally perceive the so-called emptiness of the inherent nature of emptiness. This passage is teaching that, like a magical illusion, emptiness and so on are the objective scope of skill in means. Hari: rūpam iti tāṃ dharmatāṃ dharmataye ti tāṃ dharmatāṃ śūnyatāṃ dharmatayā śūnyatayā rūpaṃ vastv iti pratyavekṣamāṇo yathā na samanupaśyec, chūnyatāṃ śūnyatāsvabhāvenāstīti yathā nopalabheta tathā pratyavekṣitavyam iti yāvat. etad uktaṃ: māyopamaḥ śūnyatādir upāyakauśalaviṣaya iti. And, by not identifying it as the true nature of things, he or she would not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality.”According to Ratnākara (F.162.b), “the pinnacle of reality” is just emptiness when it has become totally detached (from cyclic existence). RK: bhūtakoṭir iti śūnyataiva kaivalyaṃgatā bhūtakoṭiḥ. Note: The bodhisattva does not directly actualize “the pinnacle of reality” because it would lead to the state of a worthy one in the disciples’ nirvāṇa, whereas a bodhisattva chooses to stay in cyclic existence until he or she attains buddhahood, which (unlike mere arhathood) involves the ability to benefit other beings still in cyclic existence. This is discussed in more detail below.

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the Blessed One has said that a bodhisattva great being should not directly actualize emptiness. But how could a bodhisattva great being remaining in that meditative concentration not directly actualize emptiness?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, it is because a bodhisattva great being considers emptiness to be replete with all excellent qualities.According to Hari (750), “replete with all excellent qualities” means it is not lacking in (the transcendent states of) generosity and so forth. Hari: sarvākāravaropetām iti dānādyavikalāṃ. Such a bodhisattva great being does not consider it with the assumption that he or she will directly actualize it. Nor does he or she consider it with the assumption that it is something to be directly actualized. Nor does he or she consider it with the assumption that he or she will get fully aquainted with it or that it is the time for doing so despite its not being the time for directly actualizing it. Rather, the bodhisattva great being tethers his or her mind exclusively to an unfixed meditative object, knowing that he or she will be supported by the transcendent state of wisdom, but does not directly actualize emptiness.According to Ratnākara (F.162.b), (the meditative object is) “unfixed,” i.e., not determined as something to be directly actualized. RK: asamāhita eve ti sākṣātkartavyatayā ’nadhyavasita eva. According to Hari (750), he or she will be “supported by the transcendent state of wisdom,” which consists in great skill in means “but does not directly actualize emptiness.” This means he or she deliberately keeps their mind in the meditative concentration on emptiness, only without being attached to it. Hari: asamāhita eve ti prajñāpāramitā ca mahopāyakauśalātmikā mayā parigṛhītā bhaviṣyati na ca śūnyatā sākṣātkṛte ty abhiprāyād atanniṣṭha eva śūnyatāsamādhau cittaṃ dhārayati. Along the way, that bodhisattva great being does not give up any qualities conducive to awakening. Nor does he or she eliminate worldly influences. Rather, he or she gets fully aquainted with them all.According to Hari (750), the bodhisattva “does not eliminate worldly influences” means does not get rid of worldly influences (that flow away from nirvāṇa toward) the desire realm, because he or she intentionally takes rebirth for the sake of beings. To “get fully acquainted with them” means to destroy the worldly influences through focusing on the object of the meditative concentration on emptiness. Hari: na cāsravakṣayaṃ kāmabhavāsrava prahāṇaṃ saṃcintyasattvārthaṃ pratijanmaparigrahāt karoti. śūnyatāsamādhyālambanād āsravakṣaye ca parijayaṃ karotī ty arthaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.162.b), “along the way” means until he or she has cultivated the qualities of a buddha. RK: atrāntarā iti yāvad buddhadharmā asya na paripakvāḥ. At the time when the bodhisattva great being is abiding in the meditative concentration on emptiness as the gateway to complete liberation, he or she should also abide in the meditative concentration on the freedom from signs, but should not directly actualize the freedom from signs. Why not? Because that bodhisattva great being has procured qualities by way of his or her roots of virtue and considers it a time to ripen those as such, not to directly actualize anything.According to Ratnākara (F.162.b), “as such” means in the stages that were taught. “Procured” means attained. The rest is easy to follow. This ends the section on the preliminary practice of skill in means. RK: evam ity uktena krameṇa| ārūḍha upārjitāḥ| śeṣa subodham| ity upāyakauśalasya prayogaḥ. And so, being someone supported by the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality.The idea here is that the bodhisattva has deliberately chosen, out of compassion, not to attain cessation by directly actualizing emptiness‍—even though it would be possible‍—in order to follow the Mahāyāna path to developing the qualities of buddha for the benefit of others, rather than the disciples’ path leading to nirvāṇa for oneself. The Blessed One gives three extended analogies about such a choice below.

“To illustrate this, Subhūti, suppose that a certain man was extremely courageous, persevering, unwavering, and steadfast.According to Ratnākara (F.162.b), “courageous” means fearless; “persevering” means with fortitude; “steadfast” means firm-minded. RK: śūro nirbhīḥ| vīryam utsāhaḥ| pratiṣṭhānaṃ sthairyam. Also, suppose that he was handsome, charming, extremely attractive, and full of many extremely positive qualities, such as excellent ability, self-restraint, learning, renunciation, and so on. Also, suppose that he was intelligent, articulate, perfectly clear, perfect in behavior, and knew the proper time, place, and circumstances for things.According to Ratnākara (F.163.a), “perfectly clear” means rational. “Perfect in behavior” means he conducts himself perfectly in practice. “Knew the proper time” and so on means he knew what has been taught (by others, such as astrologers and so on) about the “proper time” and so on. RK: pratibhānam abhyūhaḥ| pratipattir anuṣṭhānam| yasmin kālā dau yady uktaṃ tajjñānāt kālā dijñaḥ. Also, suppose that he was supremely masterful in archery and immune to a great number of weapons or attacks.According to Ratnākara (F.163.a), “masterful” means he has attained mastery over his body, speech and mind. His armor and so on are “immune to weapons or attacks.” RK: gatiḥ kāyavākcittakarmaṇyatā| tāṃ gataḥ prāptaḥ| praharaṇāvaraṇaṃ kavacādi. Note: The English translation “weapons or attacks” aims to capture two meanings of the one Sanskrit word praharaṇa following the Tibetan, which appears to do the same. Tib: mtshon cha mang po zlog par gyur pa mtshon cha sra ba zlog par gyur pa. Also, suppose that he had, through his own accomplishment, become extremely skilled in all the arts and had the highest degree of skill in the crafts. Also, suppose that he was mindful, judicious, knowing, considerate, skilled in politics, and learned in all the treatises.According to Ratnākara (F.163.a), “learned in” means expert in. RK: viśāradaḥ paṇḍitaḥ. Also, suppose that he was friendly, wealthy, powerful, free of physical defects, and completely morally strong. Also, suppose that he helped others and was beloved and dear to many people. Also, suppose that he was able to succeed at any task whatsoever that he undertook. Also, suppose that he conducted himself systematically and was extremely successful in all regards. Also, suppose that, while being extremely successful, he shared his wealth with many people, paid tribute to those worthy of tribute, revered those worthy of reverence, respected those worthy of respect, and venerated those worthy of veneration. Subhūti, what do you think? Would such a man be extremely glad, delighted, and full of joy and satisfaction, given all this?”
“Yes, Blessed One. Yes, Sugata,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, suppose further that this extremely fortunate man was traveling and took along his mother, father, son, and daughter. Now suppose that, because of certain conditions and circumstances coming together, he came upon a vast, forested wilderness area that was extremely dangerous, terrifying to the children, and caused them to bristle with fear. Suppose that this man fearlessly assured his mother, father, son, and daughter, saying, ‘Don’t be afraid. Don’t be afraid. I will get us out of this extremely dangerous and frightening, vast, forested wilderness area successfully, safely, and quickly. I will get us to safety very soon.’ But, Subhūti, suppose that, in that vast, forested wilderness area, there were many hostile forces and enemies opposing that man. Subhūti, what do you think? Would such a courageous man‍—who never retreated from hostile forces or enemies opposing him, and who was unwavering, perseverant, steadfast, wise, extremely affectionate, compassionate, courageous, and full of resources‍—abandon his mother, father, son, and daughter? Would he think that he should get only himself out of this extremely dangerous and frightening, vast, forested wilderness area?”

“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti. “Why not? Because, Blessed One, a man with such powerful internal and external resources would never abandon his mother, father, son, and daughter. In that vast, forested wilderness area, many other more courageous, more combat-ready, and noble defenders and counterattackers would stand there by his side and protect him from those hostile forces and enemies. And despite their preying on his weakness and seeking ways to attack him, the hostile forces and enemies would never be able to find any vulnerability to do so. Blessed One, such a capable man would successfully, safely, and quickly be able to get his family, unharmed and uninjured, out of that extremely dangerous and frightening, vast, forested wilderness area, and all the way to their villages, cities, and towns.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “The same is true, Subhūti, in the case of a bodhisattva great being who cares for the welfare of all sentient beings. He or she abides in benevolence, compassion, rejoicing, and impartiality.According to Hari (754), “benevolence, compassion, rejoicing, and impartiality” are meditative concentrations accompanied (by the nature of the five skandhas). They are, respectively, to increase the wish for sentient beings to be joined with happiness, to be separated from suffering, to never be separate from happiness, and to be benefited (impartially). Hari: tatra sattveṣu sukha­saṃyoga­duḥkhaviyoga­sukhāviśleṣahita­karaṇāśaya­samṛddhau saparivārāḥ samādhayo yathākramaṃ maitrīkaruṇā muditopekṣāḥ. He or she is supported by skill in means and the transcendent state of wisdom. He or she dedicates the roots of virtue through the dedication that the perfect Buddha has recommended. But even though such a bodhisattva great being enters the gateways to complete liberation‍—that is to say, emptiness, the freedom from signs, and the freedom from inclinations‍—he or she does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality, in particular, at the levels of the disciples or solitary realizers. Why not? Because he or she has the most powerful and unwavering supporters, namely the skill in means and the transcendent state of wisdom. For this reason, he or she has not abandoned all sentient beings, and hence, he or she has become capable of realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening successfully and safely. Subhūti, at the time when a bodhisattva great being is focused upon the mindset of benevolence toward all sentient beings, he or she embraces them with supreme benevolence. Along the way, a bodhisattva great being overcomes the armies belong to the defilements and māras, overcomes the levels of the disciple and the solitary realizer, and remains in that meditative concentration.According to Ratnākara (F.163.a), since those are enemies, i.e., the hindrances, he or she overcomes them through the four immeasurables. This ends the section on the first skill in means. RK: iti śatrava eva śāstravā (sic!) antarāyāḥ| teṣām atikramaś caturbhir apramāṇair iti prathamam upāyakauśalam. Note: The first part of Ratnākara’s Sanskrit text does not seem to make sense, so this translation follows the Tibetan: ’di ni dgra nyid yin pas na dgra ste bar du gcod pa’o| |de dag las ’das pa ni tshad med pa rnam pa bzhis so| |’di ni thabs la mkhas pa dang bo’o. Subhūti, by not eliminating worldly influences, he or she gets fully aquainted with emptiness in the supreme transcendent state. Subhūti, in that interval of time during which the bodhisattva great being is abiding in the meditative concentration on emptiness as the gateway to complete liberation, the bodhisattva great being does not concurrently abide in the meditative concentration on the freedom from signs. Nor has he or she directly actualized the meditative concentration on the freedom from signs.

“Subhūti, as an analogy, it is just like an extremely large bird flying around in midair that neither lands upon the earth nor perches upon any support and rests there. Like that bird that is just abiding in midair without perching or resting anywhere, Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being abides in the state of emptiness and gets fully aquainted with emptiness. Next, he or she abides in the state of freedom from signs and gets fully aquainted with freedom from signs. Then, he or she abides in the state of freedom from inclinations and gets fully aquainted with freedom from inclinations. However, without having perfected the awakened qualities of a buddha, he or she does not land upon emptiness, freedom from signs, or freedom from inclinations.

“Subhūti, as an analogy, it is just like a powerful, master archer who is well trained and perfectly skilled in archery training. He or she can shoot one arrow upward and then immediately after doing so, shoot other arrows at it to knock back the first arrow and prevent it from falling to the earth. With one arrow after another, he or she can continually keep the first arrow from falling to the earth. And that arrow will not fall to the earth unless he were to wish it, thinking, ‘All right now, let the arrow fall to the earth.’According to Ratnākara (F.163.a), so that the first arrow shot into the sky does not fall for a long time, the force that makes it continue in that state (āvedha) comes from the other arrows. Likewise, so that the first mindset does not directly actualize (the pinnacle of reality) until the qualities of a buddha have ripened, the force that makes it continue in that state (āvedha) comes from other mindsets. Since the force that makes it continue in that state is not overcome, the method functions as the continuative force. RK: ūrdhvakṣiptasya śarasyeva śarāntaraiś ciram apātāya ya āvedhas tadvad ā buddha­dharma­paripākkaṃ sākṣātkaraṇāya cittasya cittāntarair ya āvedhaḥ| tad anatikrameṇa vṛttir yathāvedham upāyaḥ. Note: Ratnākara is ostensibly explaining here how the skill in means‍—as a force that causes the first development of the mindset of awakening to continue until awakening‍—works by preventing a bodhisattva from settling for the lesser nirvāṇa for his or her own sake. Subhūti, in this very way the bodhisattva great being, going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and supported by skill in means, does not directly actualize the supreme pinnacle of reality unless his or her roots of virtue have ripened fully and perfectly into unsurpassed, perfect awakening. It is only when the roots of virtue have ripened fully and perfectly into unsurpassed, perfect awakening that he or she directly actualizes the supreme pinnacle of reality.The term “supreme pinnacle of reality” realized by buddhas seems to be distinguished from the “pinnacle of reality” that is directly experienced by disciples and solitary realizers. Therefore, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being, going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and cultivating it would consider the profound true nature of these things and meditate upon that nature, but would not directly actualize it.”

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being is someone who performs difficult noble deeds. And that bodhisattva great being who performs extremely difficult noble deeds goes about life in emptiness, abides in emptiness, and attains that emptiness as a meditative concentration. But he or she does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality. Blessed One, this is extremely wonderful. Sugata, it is supremely wonderful.”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Yes, Subhūti, yes it is. A bodhisattva great being performs difficult noble deeds. And that bodhisattva great being who performs extremely difficult noble deeds goes about life in emptiness, abides in emptiness, and attains that emptiness as a meditative concentration.According to Ratnākara (F.163.b), here “goes about life” means with tranquility; “abides” with insight; and “attains” with the union of the two. RK: carati śamathena| viharati vipaśyanayā| sam ādhisamāpat tir ubhābhyām. Note: Ratnākara also gives here an untranslatable creative etymology of the word samāpatti suggesting the first syllable sam– stands for samādhi, i.e., sam ādhisamāpat ti. But he or she does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality. Why not? Subhūti, that is because a bodhisattva great being has not abandoned beings. Instead, such a bodhisattva great being holds the distinctive aspiration to free all beings. Such a bodhisattva great being fully accomplishes the proper mindset, thinking, ‘I will not abandon any sentient being. I will free them all.’According to Ratnākara (F.163.b), “fully accomplishes” means realizes. RK: abhinirharatī ti niṣpādayati. In doing so, he or she fully accomplishes emptiness as a gateway to complete liberation through meditative concentration, the freedom from signs as a gateway to complete liberation through meditative concentration, and the freedom from inclinations as a gateway to complete liberation through meditative concentration.According to Ratnākara (F.163.b), (in the Sanskrit compound “gateway to complete liberation through meditative concentration,” we should understand emptiness) as both the “meditative concentration” and the “complete liberation” because it is the door to liberation. RK: abhinirharatī ti niṣpādayati| samādhiś cāsau vimokṣaś ca muktidvāratvāt. If that bodhisattva great being fully accomplishes these in this way, one can be certain that he or she has the skill in means, and does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality until completely perfecting the qualities of a buddha.The last phrase “until completely perfecting the qualities of a buddha” follows the Tibetan edition. There is no comparable phrase in the Sanskrit edition. Why not? Because he or she is preserving the skill in means and simply developing the mindset to not abandon any sentient beings. Thus, by developing this proper mindset together with the skill in means, he or she does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality along the way.According to Ratnākara (F.163.b), in this context “developing the mindset” means fully accomplishing the mindset. He or she “performs difficult noble deeds” by this (skill in means). This method/means is not shared (by others), in the sense that it is not shared by any of the disciples or solitary realizers. RK: cittotpādaś cittābhinirhāraḥ| anenā yaṃ duṣkarakārakaḥ| eṣa ca sarva­śrāvakapratyeka­buddhair asādhāraṇatvād asādhāraṇa upāyaḥ.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being considers or wishes to consider these profound topics‍—namely, emptiness, freedom from signs, and freedom from inclinations as gateways to complete liberation through meditative concentration‍—then he or she thinks, ‘These sentient beings have been going about life for a long time in mental perception because of their conception of a “sentient being.”According to Ratnākara (F.163.b), “sentient being” here means a self (ātma) or a person (puruṣaḥ). RK: sattva ātmā puruṣaḥ. Once I have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, I will teach the Dharma to those sentient beings who erroneously believe in their mental perception, in order to eliminate such an erroneous belief.’ After contemplating this, he or she attains emptiness as a gateway to complete liberation through meditative concentration, but does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality. He or she attains the freedom from signs as a gateway to complete liberation through meditative concentration, but does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality. He or she also attains the freedom from inclinations as a gateway to complete liberation through meditative concentration, but does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality. Thus, a bodhisattva great being who has developed the proper mindset and this skill in means neither directly actualizes the pinnacle of reality along the way, nor loses the meditative concentrations on benevolence, compassion, rejoicing, and impartiality. Why not? Because a bodhisattva great being supported by skill in means thrives to a greater degree with his or her pure qualities. He or she also has sharper faculties, such as faith and so on, and attains the powers, the factors in awakening, and the path.According to Ratnākara (F.163.b), (from the thirty-seven factors in awakening listed, this section is referring to the last four subgroups, namely) the five “faculties” and the five “powers,” which include the same list: “faith” (heroic perseverance, mindfulness, meditative concentration, and wisdom). The “aspects of awakening” are the seven (listed previously) and the “path” is the eightfold one. The belief in a “sentient being” is attachment. Nonattachment is the attainment of emptiness and so on after thinking, “Once I have realized … awakening, I will teach … sentient beings” to help them “eliminate” that. The method/means is not being attached based on an connection with that conception. RK: indriyāṇi pañca śraddhādīni| bal āny api pañcaiva| bodhya ṅgāni sapta| mārgo ’ṣṭāṅgaḥ| sattvadṛṣṭiḥ saktiḥ| tatprahāṇāya sattvānām abhisambuddhaḥ sattvān deśayiṣyāmī ti sañcintya śūnyatā di samāpat tir asaktiḥ| tadyogād asakta upāyaḥ.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being would think, ‘These sentient beings have been going about life for a long time in their mental perception because of their conception of “things.”According to Ratnākara (F.163.b), conceiving of “things” means conceiving of constituents of experience, elements, and so on. This is their “mental perception.” He or she does the same as before to “eliminate” that. Thus, the attainment of emptiness means being free of mental perceptions. The method/means is being free of mental perceptions based on any connection with that conception. RK: dharmasaṃjñā skandhadhātvādisaṃjñā| sa upalambhaḥ | tatprahāṇāye tyādi pūrvavat| evaṃ śūnyatā disamāpat tir anupalambhaḥ| tadyogād anupalambha upāyaḥ. Once I have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, I will teach the Dharma to those sentient beings who erroneously believe in their mental perception, in order to eliminate such an erroneous belief.’ A bodhisattva great being who has developed the proper mindset and this skill in means attains emptiness as a gateway to complete liberation through meditative concentration. But he or she does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality. Also, he or she does not lose the meditative concentration on benevolence, compassion, rejoicing, and impartiality. Why is that? Because the bodhisattva great being supported by skill in means thrives to a greater degree with his or her pure qualities. He or she also has sharper faculties, such as faith and so on, and attains the powers, the aspects of awakening, and the path.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being would think, ‘These sentient beings have been going about life for a long time in a conventional sign because of their conception of a “sign.”According to Ratnākara (F.163.b), the “conception of conventional signs” refers (for example) to “woman,” “man,” “visible form,” or “sound.” To eliminate that, one proceeds as described previously. RK: strī pumān rūpaṃ śabda iti nimittasaṃjñā | tatprahāṇāye tyādi pūrvavat. Once I have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, I will teach the Dharma to those sentient beings who erroneously believe in their mental perception, in order to eliminate such an erroneous belief.’ A bodhisattva great being who has developed the proper mindset and this skill in means attains freedom from signs as a gateway to complete liberation through meditative concentration.RK: evam animittasamāpat tir ānimittam| tadyogād ānimitta upāyaḥ. But he or she does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality. Also, he or she does not lose the meditative concentration on benevolence, compassion, rejoicing, and impartiality. Why not? Because the bodhisattva great being supported by skill in means thrives to a greater degree with his or her pure qualities. He or she also has sharper faculties, such as faith and so on, and attains the powers, the aspects of awakening, and the path.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being would think, ‘These sentient beings have been mistaken for a long time because of their conceptions of “permanence,” “happiness,” “self,” and “purity.” For the sake of helping them to eliminate the mistake of their conceptions of permanence, happiness, self, and purity, I will act now so that, once I have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, I will teach them the Dharma: “All this is impermanent, not permanent. All this is suffering, not happiness. All this lacks a self; it does not have a self.
