All this is impure; it is not pure.” ’ Thus, a bodhisattva great being who has developed the proper mindset and is supported by the previously described skill in means and the transcendent state of wisdom does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality along the way when the qualities of a buddha have not been perfected. In this way, having entered freedom from inclinations as a gateway to complete liberation through meditative concentration, he or she abides in it. But he or she does not directly actualize the pinnacle of reality. Also, he or she does not lose benevolence, compassion, rejoicing, and impartiality. Why not? Because the bodhisattva great being supported by skill in means thrives to a greater degree with his or her pure qualities. He or she also has sharper faculties, such as faith and so on, and attains the powers, the aspects of awakening, and the path.According to Ratnākara (F.164.a), an “inclination” is a desire for some worldly pleasure‍—for instance, to become the universal emperor Śakra, the powerful god. A “mistake” is the root of that desire. For this reason, one “eliminates” the four mistakes as described previously. The meditative concentration on freedom from inclinations is free of inclinations. The method/means is freedom from inclinations due to any connection with that. RK: devaḥ śakraś cakravarttī syām ityādir bhavabhogābhilāṣaḥ praṇihitam| tasya mūlaṃ viparyāsāḥ| tasmāc caturviparyāsaprahāṇāye tyādi pūrvavat| evam apraṇihitasamādhir apraṇihitam tadyogād apraṇihita upāyaḥ.

“Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being also indeed develops the proper mindset, thinking, ‘These sentient beings have been going about life for a long time in a mental perception, and still now go about life in a mental perception.According to Ratnākara (F.164.a), the methods/means of freedom from mental perceptions and so on are taught to be three gateways of complete liberation as the objective scope (of mental concentration). Next (in this paragraph), he explains the additional objective scopes and (and after that) unerroneous nature as the freedom from conditionings and so on. RK: anupalambhādīnām upāyā vimokṣamukhatrayaṃ viṣaya uktaḥ| sāṃpratam eṣām anabhisaṃskārādikam adhikaṃ viṣayam amoghatāṃ ca bravīti. They have been going about life in the conception of a “sign” and still now go about life in a conception of a sign. They have been going about life in an “error” and still now go about life in error. They have been going about life in a conception of “alms” and still now go about life in a conception of alms. They have been going about life in a conception of “nonexistence” and still now go about life in a conception of nonexistence. They have been going about life in erroneous beliefs and still now go about life in erroneous beliefs. I will act so that there will be no such faults, and there will be none known to exist absolutely anywhere in any form or manner.’ In this way, he or she brings all sentient beings to mind. When a bodhisattva great being mindfully brings them to mind and is supported by this skill in means and the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she is considering the true nature of these profound things from the perspectives of emptiness, the freedom from signs, the freedom from inclinations, the freedom from conditioning, the freedom from arising, the freedom from birth, and the freedom from any state of being.According to Ratnākara’s (F.156.a) previous comments in chapter 18 (see n.938), the nature (bhāva) of all things as they appear and the nature of them as they truly are is “emptiness.” It itself is the “freedom from signs” because signs cease in the experience of it. It itself is “freedom from inclinations” because those who experience it have no aspiration for the three realms. It itself is “freedom from conditioning” because those who experience it do not perform karmic deeds that lead to rebirth. It itself is “freedom from arising” because those who experience it do not have arisings due to any karmic deeds. It itself is “freedom from birth” because those who experience it know (that things are) unborn by nature. It itself is “freedom from any state of being” because it is insubstantial. RK: g ambhīram itītyādi| sarvadharmāṇāṃ yathāpratibhāsam bhāvo yathātattvaṃ ca bhāvaḥ śūnyatā| saivānimittaṃ tadvedane nimittanirodhāt| saivāpraṇihitaṃ tadvidāṃ traidhātuke ’praṇidhānāt| saivānabhisaṃskāras teṣāṃ punarbhavāya karmākaraṇāt| saivānutpādas teṣāṃ tena karmaṇā’nutpādāt| saivājātis teṣāṃ prakṛtyajātatājñānāt| saivābhāvo ’dravyatvāt. There is no possibility or chance that a bodhisattva great being with such knowledge would either fall into an unconditioned state or be stuck within the three realms.According to Ratnākara (F.164.a), the phrase “fall into an unconditioned state” means into the disciples’ lesser awakening. The phrase “within the three realms” means in cyclic existence. RK: anabhisaṃskāre pated iti hīnabodhau| traidhātukene ti saṃsāreṇa. It is not possible.

“Thus, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being who wishes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening should ask a second bodhisattva great being, ‘What are the qualities that a bodhisattva great being should acquaint him- or herself with?According to Ratnākara (F.164.a), “a bodhisattva great being who wishes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening should ask (paripraṣṭavya) a second bodhisattva great being” who knows. RK: samyak­sambodhikāmena bodhisattvena vijño bodhisattvaḥ praṣṭavyaḥ. How many sorts of mindsets should a bodhisattva great being fully accomplish so that, while he or she is fully accomplishing them, he or she would not directly actualize emptiness, the freedom from signs, the freedom from inclinations, the freedom from conditioning, the freedom from arising, the freedom from birth, or the freedom from any state of being, but would cultivate the transcendent state of wisdom?’According to Ratnākara (F.164.a), if the question is “For perfect awakening, what should I cultivate and how should I cultivate it?” then one should cultivate the emptiness that has been seen and should actualize the acquaintance with the mother (i.e., the transcendent state of wisdom). In relation to that, the “mindset of not abandoning all sentient beings” and so on (as mentioned below) is the means/method. (Mentioning) this is a mark of an explainer who is beyond regressing. The means is (indirectly expressed) in the mark of the explainer. RK: samyaksambodhaye kiṃ bhāvayeyaṃ kathaṃ ca bhāvayeyam iti| saced ācakṣīta­śūnyatādikam eva bhāvaya tac ca parijayaṃ mātuḥ sākṣātkārṣīḥ| tatropāyaḥ sarva­sattvāparityāgacitt ādir iti| etat tasyākhyātur avaivartikatve liṅgam| tasmāt tasya liṅgam asminn iti talliṅga upāyaḥ. Note: Ratnākara’s clarification here also gives an untranslated grammatical explanation of the Sanskrit adjectival compound. Subhūti, suppose that the second bodhisattva great being, who was asked this question, were to explain, ‘A bodhisattva great being should pay attention to nothing but emptiness. A bodhisattva great being should pay attention to nothing but freedom from signs, nothing but freedom from inclinations, nothing but freedom from conditioning, nothing but freedom from arising, nothing but freedom from birth, and nothing but freedom from any state of being.’ Suppose that the second bodhisattva also failed to point out the development of the proper mindset of not abandoning any sentient beings, or failed to explain the skill in means. Then, Subhūti, one can know that no former worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata has foreordained that bodhisattva great being to be beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. How? Because that bodhisattva great being does not indicate, reveal, point out, or understand the virtue that is distinctive to a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing. And when asked, he or she does not explain it, correctly answer the question, or help others attain the level of a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing.”
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, by what means can one recognize a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, one can recognize a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing by the following means. If a bodhisattva great being‍—whether or not he or she had heard this transcendent state of wisdom‍—were to respond and answer the question the way I described, then he or she should be recognized as a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing.”
Subhūti said, “That explains why, Blessed One, there are many going about life with the aim of awakening, but among those, few would answer the question that way.”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, it explains why there are few bodhisattva great beings foreordained for the stage where their knowing is beyond regressing. But, those who are foreordained will answer the question the way I described. One can recognize those bodhisattva great beings because they have planted and purified the roots of virtue, and none of them can be led astray by the world with its gods, humans, and asuras.This translation has followed the Sanskrit uttapta here, even though it is omitted in the Tibetan: dge ba’i rtsa ba ’bar ba skyed par. Moreover, Subhūti, suppose that, even when dreaming, a bodhisattva great being were to lucidly perceive everything as a dream, without directly actualizing awakening. Then, Subhūti, one should recognize a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing to have this sign of being beyond regressing.According to Ratnākara (F.164.a), that he or she sees while dreaming that everything is like a dream, but does not actualize awakening, is the first mark (i.e., characteristic, of a bodhisattva beyond regressing). RK: svapnāntaragato pi svapnopamān sarvadharmān paśyati na ca sākṣātkarotīti prathamaṃ liṅgam. Note: This is a reference to lucid dreaming that serves an example of how a bodhisattva meditates on emptiness but does not wake up. In the language of this scripture, he or she knows everything is empty, but does not actualize the disciples’ awakening, i.e., a bodhisattva eschews the disciples’ pinnacle of reality in favor of the pinnacle of reality experienced by a buddha.

For the remainder of the chapter from this point on, the content corresponds to the first part Chapter 45 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (down to 45.8) and Hundred Thousand, and to Chapter 55 in the Eighteen Thousand (down to 55.9).“Furthermore, Subhūti, suppose that, even when dreaming, a bodhisattva great being did not develop a mindset to benefit him- or herself, or of yearning for the levels of the disciples or solitary realizers, or for anything within the three realms. Subhūti, one should also recognize a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing to have this sign of being beyond regressing.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, suppose that, even when dreaming, a bodhisattva great being saw him- or herself teaching the Dharma as a worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata, seated in a pavilion surrounded by the saṅgha of monks and attended by the saṅgha of bodhisattvas, at the center of an assembly with persons numbering in the hundreds, thousands, or even quintillions. Subhūti, one should also recognize a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing to have this sign of being beyond regressing.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, suppose that, even when dreaming, a bodhisattva great being rose into the air, taught the Dharma to sentient beings, and perceived him- or herself within a six-foot-wide halo of light, emanating monks who traveled in different directions, performed the activities of a buddha in the different world systems, and taught the Dharma there. Subhūti, one should also recognize a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing to have this sign of being beyond regressing.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, suppose that, even when dreaming, a bodhisattva great being was not frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror when his or her village, city, town, community, or principality was being destroyed. He or she also feels no fear or anxiety when a fire breaks out;Note: The translation has followed the Sanskrit agnidāhe, even though *agnidāhaghate might be suggested by the Tibetan: ’brog me shor ba’i gnod pa byung ngam. when seeing wild carnivorous animals or other very small creatures; when someone is about to cut off his or her head; when encountering any other dangers, threats, pain, or dissatisfaction; or when seeing other sentient beings suffering in extreme fear and anxiety. In this situation, he or she is not frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror. Also, when a bodhisattva great being has awakened and arisen from any dream, he or she thinks, ‘Once I have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, I should teach the Dharma by accurately pointing out that everything in the three realms is like a dream.’ Subhūti, one should also recognize a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing to have this sign of being beyond regressing.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, suppose that, when dreaming, a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing saw sentient beings in the hell realms, and thought, ‘I will act so that once I have realized unsurpassed awakening, in my buddhafield there will not even be any lower realms in any form or manner.’ Subhūti, this complete purification of the lower realms should also be recognized as a sign of a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing.Generally in the Mahāyāna, when buddhas attain awakening they also create around themselves one of three types of buddhafields: pure, impure, and mixed. A buddhafield without any lower realms is considered to be a pure one. The aspiration of a bodhisattva beyond regressing is the thing that “refines” the future buddhafield. For more info, see the entry on pariśuddha­buddha­kṣetra (“purified buddhafield”) in Buswell and Lopez 2014, p. 630. Regarding this, Subhūti, how would one know that once this bodhisattva great being realizes unsurpassed awakening, in his or her buddhafield there will be no lower realms in any form or manner? Subhūti, suppose that when dreaming, a bodhisattva great being saw sentient beings in the hell realm, the hungry ghost realm, or the animal realm, and then regained mindfulness and thought, ‘I will act so that once I have realized unsurpassed awakening, in my buddhafield there will not even be any lower realms in any form or manner.’ In this way, Subhūti, one should recognize this complete purification of the lower realms as a sign of a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing. And, Subhūti, one should also recognize a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing to have this sign of being beyond regressing.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, if a dreaming bodhisattva great being were awakened from a dream where a town or a city is burning, he would recollect it, thinking, ‘The attributes, indications, and signs that I myself have foreseen in my dreams are known to be the attributes, indications, and signs by which a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing should be recognized. Thus, by the power of the truth in this true statement, may the fire burning the town or city subside, be extinguished, and disappear.’ If afterward, Subhuti, the fire burning the town or city subsides, is extinguished, and disappears, then, Subhuti, one should recognize this bodhisattva great being as foreordained by the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas as beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. However, Subhuti, if these do not subside, are not extinguished, and do not disappear, then, Subhuti, one should recognize this bodhisattva great being to be not foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Moreover, Subhuti, if the fire’s burning spreads further and moves to another house or road, from house to house, from road to road, and burns them all‍—that is to say, it does not subside, is not extinguished, and does not disappear‍—then, Subhuti, one should know that, by having rejected the Dharma, this bodhisattva great being has accumulated the karmic deeds leading to inferior wisdom and as a result of that, the ripening of such karmic deeds is causing the bodhisattva great being’s present failure. Hence, it is a remainder from his or her karmic deed of rejecting the Dharma that is being ripened in this way.According to Hari (767), “the karmic deed is ripened” means the karmic deed that is the rejection of the sublime Dharma that leads to his or her present life’s failure is experienced through their mental dissatisfaction due to their failure to perform an act of truth. “As a result of that” means due to having accumulated that karmic deed in a previous life. Hari: karma vipacyata iti saddharma­pratyākhyānaṃ dṛṣṭa­dharma­saṃvartanīyam eva karma satyādhiṣṭhānāniṣpatter daurmanasyādinānubhūyate. tata eve ti janmāntarasaṃgṛhītāt. In sum, Subhūti, these signs are the causes and conditions for a bodhisattva great being to be characterized as beyond regressing. Therefore, Subhūti, one should also recognize such a bodhisattva great being as beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Once again, Subhūti, I will now teach these attributes, indications, and signs by which, all together, a bodhisattva great being can be recognized, as follows. Listen well and pay good attention. I will tell you about them.”
Subhūti said, “Excellent!” Then, he listened to the Blessed One.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, suppose that a man, woman, boy, or girl were taken over or possessed by a malevolent spirit. A bodhisattva great being should approach him or her and perform an act of truth by stating,According to Ratnākara (F.165.a), if someone is “taken over”‍—i.e., controlled “or possessed,” i.e., inhabited “by an malevolent spirit”‍—then the bodhisattva should “perform an act of truth” in response to that. RK: yadi kaścit sattvo ’manuṣyeṇa gṛhīto ’dhiṣṭhitaḥ āviṣṭo vā antaḥpraveśāt| tatra bodhisattvaḥ satyādhiṣṭhānaṃ kuryāt. Note: The notion of “an act of truth” here is that a bodhisattva can perform a miracle merely by stating a true fact, such as that he or she will realize awakening. ‘If it is true that I am foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, then my altruistic disposition to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening is pure. Since I wish to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening; since I have pure attentiveness to that unsurpassed, perfect awakening; and since I have turned away from the mindsets of a disciple and a solitary realizer, I shall realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. It is impossible for me not to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and I will definitely realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. There is nothing that the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who reside, stay, and spend time in immeasurable and innumerable world systems have not known, not seen, not found, not witnessed, and not realized. Since those blessed buddhas are recognizing my altruistic disposition, it must be that I too will awaken. By the power of this truth and my stating it, may you, the malevolent spirit who has taken over or possessed this man, woman, girl, or boy, go away!’ If the malevolent spirit that the bodhisattva great being is addressing like this does not go away, then, Subhūti, one should know that this bodhisattva great being is not foreordained by the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas as beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. However, if the malevolent spirit that the bodhisattva great being is addressing like this does go away, then, Subhūti, one should also recognize this bodhisattva great being as foreordained by the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas to be beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Examining Skill in Means,” the twentieth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 21: Detailing Māra’s ActivitiesAccording to Ratnākara (F.165.b), this is the twenty-first chapter, on Māra’s activities. What is this chapter introducing about them? It is introducing the fact that (a) there are many Māras for a bodhisattva who recently set out on the path, because they can develop a deluded sense of grandeur due to accomplishing results through their (seemingly effortless) performance of acts of truth and so on, and (b) a bodhisattva makes heroic advances (parākramo) against them. RK: ayam ekaviṃśati tamaḥ parivarto mārakarmā ṇām. kas teṣāṃ prastāvaḥ ? nava­yāna­saṃprasthitasya bodhisattvasya satyādhiṣṭhāna­phala­sampādanādinā mithyāmānotpādanāya bahudhā mārāḥ| parākramata ity ayam eṣāṃ prastāvaḥ. Note: Even though the Sanskrit title mārakarmaparivarta is the same as chapter 11, “On Māra’s Activity,” to distinguish the two chapters this chapter has been called “The Chapter Detailing Māra’s Activities” following Ratnākara’s gloss the end of the chapter. RK: mārakarmā ṇām abhidhāyakaḥ parivartas tathoktaḥ.The beginning of this chapter corresponds to a point within Chapter 45 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (starting at 45.9) and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 55 in the Eighteen Thousand (starting at 55.10). The content of the chapter continues to a later point still within the same chapters, 45.22 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and 55.27 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: On that topic, the Blessed One continued, “Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being may say, ‘By the power of the truth in my stating that I have been foreordained by the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, may this malevolent spirit go away!’According to Ratnākara (F.165.b), “on that topic” is referring to the act of truth that was described just before (at the end of the previous chapter where the bodhisattva said, in effect, the same thing). RK: tatre tyanantarokte satyādhiṣṭhāne. But in response, Subhūti, it is the pernicious Māra who will exert himself in making that malevolent spirit go away.According to Ratnākara (F.165.b), he will “exert himself” means he will make an effort to do a (harmful) deed to someone in whom he finds an opportunity. RK: autsukyam iti prāptāvasarasya karmaṇaḥ karaṇārtham utsāhaḥ. Why is that? Because the pernicious Māra will strive extremely strenuously and energetically to get at a bodhisattva great being who has not traveled a long time on the path.According to Ratnākara (F.165.b), “strenuously” refers to the power of his effort and “energetically” refers to the power of his might. RK: balam utsāhaśaktiḥ| tejaḥ prabhāvaśaktiḥ| kathaṃ tejovattaram iti yāvat| tasau matvartha iti bhatvāspadatvaṃ nāstīti bhatvena na bhavitavyam| tadyathā āyuṣmān sarasvān iti| evaṃ tarhi dīptyarthā[F.] dravateḥ śatrantāt tarap| tejasā dīptataraṃ tejovattaram iti. Note: This translation of Ratnākara’s comments essentializes them and omits his Sanskrit etymological explanation of how the word tejovattaram is formed. That is to say, Māra will think how he can make the malevolent spirit go away so that when the malevolent spirit leaves because of Māra’s magical power to influence others, the bodhisattva great being will suppose that his or her own miraculous power is what made the malevolent spirit go away. Moreover, since that bodhisattva great being does not know that Māra’s miraculous power caused the malevolent spirit to go away, he or she will exert him- or herself only minimally going forward. And with his or her exertion, the bodhisattva great being will treat disrespectfully other bodhisattva great beings, be smug to them, be sarcastic toward them, pick at their faults, and criticize them, boasting, ‘I have been foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas.’According to Ratnākara (F.165.b), “with his or her exertion” means with a lofty opinion of him- or herself. In this sentence, to “treat disrespectfully” means to disrespect; being “smug” means smiling to oneself inwardly; being “sarcastic toward them” means doing the opposite with one’s mind; to “pick at their faults” means to highlight their faults; and to “criticize” means to explain their faults. RK: autsukyam iti| ātmani bahumānam| avamaṃsyata ityādi| avamān o ’nādaraḥ| uccagghānam antarhāsaḥ| ullāpan aṃ manasānyatkaraṇam| kutsanaṃ doṣadarśitā| paṃs anaṃ doṣavāditā. As a result, his or her pride will develop and develop, more than even just this much. That pride will increase and increase more. That pride will become stubborn and more stubborn. That pride will be fed and fed more. And it will flare up.According to Ratnākara (F.165.b), “increasing” means causing it to come to the fore; “becoming stubborn” means causing it to be unmovable; “being fed” means fostering; and “flaring up” means getting intense. RK: jananam utpādanam| vardha naṃ puraḥsarīkaraṇam| stambha naṃ sthirīkaraṇam| bṛṃhaṇa paripuṣṭiḥ| utpāda nam uccair nayanam. Out of his or her pride, self-superiority, arrogance, egotism, conceitedness, and deluded sense of grandeur, such a bodhisattva great being will come to distance him- or herself from the state of complete knowing, that is to say, from the unsurpassed knowing of a buddha, the knowing of a self-existent being, the knowing of someone who has complete knowing, and unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.165.b–66.a), it was previously taught that “pride,” in general, is the swelling up of one’s own mindset with a sense of being better than others. But particular types of it are explained here: “pride” means the swelling up compared to someone inferior to him or her; “self-superiority” is compared to someone absolutely equal; “arrogance” is compared to someone more excellent; “conceitedness” is thinking to have attained the state beyond regressing even though one has not attained it; and the “deluded sense of grandeur” is furthermore thinking “I am a possessor of positive qualities” in connection with falsely supposed positive qualities beyond regressing. RK: mānene tyādi| anyebhyo ’dhikatvena cittasyonnatiḥ sāmānyena mānaḥ sa pūrvam uktaḥ| iha tu tadviśeṣā ucyante| yā tasya hīnād unnatiḥ sa mānaḥ | yādṛśād eva so ’timānaḥ | yo punaḥprakṛṣṭāt sa mānātimānaḥ| aprāpte ’vaivartikatve prāptaṃ mayeti so ’bhimānaḥ| yā punar abhūtair avaivartikaguṇair guṇavān asmīti sa mithyāmānaḥ. Note: For similar descriptions, see Vasubandhu’s Pañcaskandhaka, 4.1.23 in Kramer 2014. He or she might see virtuous friends in the form of either beginning or advanced bodhisattva great beings, who have virtuous qualities, have perfected the altruistic disposition, have become skilled in means, and possess qualities beyond regressing. Yet, he or she will feel conceited toward them. And because he or she looks down on them, he or she will not serve these sorts of virtuous friends, wait upon them, attend to them, or ask them questions. Instead, he or she will strengthen his or her ties to Māra, and long for one of the two lower levels, either that of a disciple or a solitary realizer. In this way, Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being has not traveled a long time on the path, has minimal faith, has little learning, has no virtuous friend, is not supported by the transcendent state of wisdom, and lacks the skill in means, the pernicious Māra will use the bodhisattva’s act of truth to create obstacles to his or her unsurpassed, perfect awakening. So, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should recognize this too as an activity of Māra.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, Māra’s activity will also influence a bodhisattva great being by assigning him or her a title. How could Māra’s activity also influence a bodhisattva great being by assigning him or her a title? In this case, Subhūti, the pernicious Māra will attack a bodhisattva great being by assigning him or her a title and corroborating the names to him or her.According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “assigning a title” means labeling (i.e., with a name, title, or epithet). RK: nāmāpadeśaḥ saṃjñākathanam. According to Hari (774), “assigning a title” means saying his or her name/title; “corroborating it with names” means reciting the names of his or her mother and so on (as described below). Hari: nāmāpadeśene ti tannāmakathanena. nāmādhiṣṭhānene ti mātrādināmavyapadeśena. Note: The word nāma (in the Sanskrit compound word nāmāpadeśaḥ) can be translated simply as a “name” but also refers to an epithet, title, or other type of designation. To capture the thrust of the problem described in the passage, it has been translated as “giving a title” (in the sense of a specified title expressing rank, office, or character). To do so, Māra will come in one disguise or another and assign a title to a bodhisattva great being, saying, ‘You have been foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. What is the proof? Here are the names of you,These names of the bodhisattva include past and future. your mother, your father, your brother, your sister, your friends, your advisors, your acquaintances, and your relatives.’ Māra then recites the names stretching back seven generations, saying, ‘Then you were born in this area within this country in this village, city, or town.’ If that bodhisattva great being is naturally simple-minded, Māra will say, ‘In your past lives, too, you were simple-minded.’ If he or she naturally has sharp mental faculties, Māra will say, ‘In your past lives, too, you had sharp mental faculties.’ Then Māra will highlight the particular ascetic quality that a bodhisattva great being is practicing in their present life.According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), the following sentence beginning “an ascetic who dwells in the forest” and so on describes the (fourteen) qualities of a pure ascetic. RK: āraṇyakatvādayo dhutaguṇāḥ. Note: These qualities are practices. In some texts, they number twelve and in others thirteen. In the main text there are fifteen clauses, but the last three were combined into one, because the commentators either do not mention them or suggest they are part of the thirteenth one. The translation of each subsequent ascetic practice has attempted to follow Hari’s or RK’s commentary, but the reader should keep in mind that they are interpreted differently elsewhere; cf. descriptions in other sūtras from dhūtaguṇa in 84000’s cumulative glossary, 84000’s (forthcoming) The Teaching on the Purification Practices from the Path to Liberation (Toh 306), and dhutaṅga in Buswell and Lopez 2014, pp. 255–56. No matter whether that particular bodhisattva is an ascetic who dwells in the forest,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who dwells in the forest” stays only in the forest area more than two miles outside the village. RK: grāmād bahiḥ krośāt param araṇyam| tatraiva sthānāt āraṇyakaḥ. an ascetic who seeks alms,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who seeks alms” eats only what has been offered as alms (into a begging bowl). RK: piṇḍapātasyaiva bhojanāt piṇḍapātikaḥ. an ascetic who wears rags,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who wears rags” is clad only in a collection of rags from a dust-heap. RK: pāṃsukūlasyeva prāvaraṇāt pāṃsukūlikaḥ. an ascetic who never eats later,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who never eats later” only eats around midday. RK: madhyāhnasyopānta eva bhojanāt khalu paścād bhaktikaḥ. an ascetic who uses a single seat,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who uses a single seat” uses only one single seat (to eat meals and so on). RK: ekāsana eva bhuṅkte ekāsanikaḥ. an ascetic who accepts whatever is offered,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who accepts what is offered” always sleeps on whatever (grass mat) is available (where he or she happens to be at dusk). RK: sakṛdāstīrṇe nityaṃ śayanāt yāthāsaṃstarikaḥ. an ascetic who has three robes,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who has three robes” abandons any more than three robes. RK: tricīvarād adhikatyāgāt traicīvarikaḥ. an ascetic who lives in charnel grounds,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who lives in charnel grounds” stays only in the area of the charnel grounds. RK: śmaśānasamīpa eva sthānāt śmāśānikaḥ. an ascetic who lives at the foot of a tree,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who lives at the foot of a tree” stays only at the foot of a tree. RK: vṛkṣamūla eva sthānāt vṛkṣamūlikaḥ. an ascetic who stays upright,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who stays upright” continues through the night (i.e., sleeps) sitting upright. RK: niṣadyayā rātrinayanān naiṣadyikaḥ. an ascetic who lives in the open air,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who lives in the open air” stays only in the open air, i.e., an outdoor space without shelter. RK: acchanno ’vakāśo ’bhyavakāśaḥ| tatraiva sthānāt ābhyavakāśikaḥ. an ascetic who wears felt garments,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who wears felt” wears only felt garments made of wool, hemp, and so on. RK: aurṇakaśānakāder eva prāvaraṇāt nāmantikaḥ. or an ascetic who lives in seclusion, contented with few desires, goes barefoot, and speaks softly with few words,According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), “an ascetic who lives in seclusion, contented with few desires” has “few desires,” i.e., wishes for little; is “content” with just that much; and lives “in seclusion” as a recluse. RK: alpaiṣaṇāt alpecchaḥ| tāvataiva santoṣāt santuṣṭaḥ| ekākitvāt praviviktaḥ. According to Hari (774–75), “an ascetic who lives in seclusion, contented with few desires” lacks any “desire” for more; is “content” with having just that much; lives “in seclusion” in order to be free from offenses (against monastic rules or vows); and due to having few desires, “goes barefoot.” Hari: adhikābhilāṣābhāvād alpecchaḥ. tāvanmātreṇa saṃtoṣāt saṃtuṣṭaḥ. āpattirahitatvena praviviktaḥ. alpecchatvād apagatapādam rakṣaṇaḥ. Note: Three seemingly distinct sentences in the Sanskrit text have been combined into one here because the commentators seem to count them as one. The phrase apagatapādam rakṣaṇaḥ is not in any other list. Although Hari comments on it briefly, RK ignores it. The Tibetan translation (F.211.b) has rkang pa skud pa dang bral ba, which is probably a mistake or hyperedit for rkang pa skung ba dang bral ba or rkang pa skyong ba dang bral ba. The English rendering “goes barefoot” takes the Sanskrit as being equivalent to *apagatapādarakṣaṇaḥ, i.e., one who is free of foot coverings/shoes. Māra will say, ‘In your past lives, too, you practiced this or that ascetic quality. You have been destined for the level of a bodhisattva beyond regressing, and foreordained to unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. What is the proof? Since you are practicing these types of ascetic qualities, it is certain that you practiced the very same ones previously too.’ In this way, by Māra assigning him or her a title and corroborating it with names from the past in connection with his or her present austere practice of ascetic qualities, the bodhisattva great being will become conceited, thinking, ‘Since I do practice these qualities, I must have been foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas.’According to Ratnākara (F.166.a), here “corroborating” means revealing. RK: adhiṣṭhānam āviṣkaraṇam. Then the pernicious Māra will say, ‘You have been foreordained as beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. What is the proof? You practice these sorts of ascetic qualities.’

“Subhūti, the pernicious Māra will occasionally come in the guise of a monk, a nun, a male lay practitioner, a female lay practitioner, a brahmin, a householder, a mother, a father, a brother, a sister, a friend, an advisor, an acquaintance, or a relative. After approaching the bodhisattva great being, Māra will say, ‘You are on the level of a bodhisattva beyond regressing and have been foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening by the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. What is the proof? You practice these sorts of ascetic qualities, which are the qualities of a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing.’ However, Subhūti, that bodhisattva great being will not have any of the qualities of bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing that I have made known, nor will he or she have the attributes, indications, or signs that I have made known. Subhūti, other bodhisattva great beings will recognize this, thinking, ‘Alas! This bodhisattva great being must be under the influence of Māra’s magical power.’ How so? Such a bodhisattva great being does not have the attributes, indications, or signs of those bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing. Rather, Subhūti, he or she will become conceited by Māra’s corroborations with names. After becoming conceited, he or she will be overcome with pride and stubbornness.According to Ratnākara (F.166.a–b), in the phrase “overcome with stubbornness,” the word “stubborn” means lacking devotion to teachers (guru). RK: stambhābhibhūta iti stambho guruṣv agauravam. Because of being overcome by Māra’s magical power to influence others, he or she will treat disrespectfully other bodhisattva great beings, be smug to them, be sarcastic toward them, pick at their faults, and criticize them. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should recognize this too as Māra’s activity through corroborating with names.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being should also understand Māra’s activity by his use of assigning titles as follows. How, Subhūti, should the bodhisattva great being understand Māra’s activity by his use of assigning titles with a prediction of the bodhisattva great being’s future names? Subhūti, in this case the pernicious Māra will come in the guise of a monk and predict to the bodhisattva great being, ‘This will be your name at the time when you realize supreme, perfect awakening.’ And the name that Māra will predict will be precisely one that the bodhisattva great being him- or herself had considered, pondered, and mulled over with the thought, ‘Oh, when I realize supreme, perfect awakening, this is the sort of name I should have.’According to Ratnākara (F.166.b), the latter two terms “pondered” (anuvitarkitam) and “mulled over” provide an explanation of the very first “considered” (anuvartitam<anuvicintitam). He or she “pondered” (anuvitarkitam) means thinking what their name will be, and “mulled over” means thinking this is what my name should be. Māra “predicted” means he declared it. RK: anuvartitam ity anuvicintitam| asyaiva nirdeśa uttarābhyām| anuvitarkitaṃ kiṃ me nāma bhaved iti| anuvicāritam idaṃ me nāma bhaved iti| vyākariṣyatī ty ākhyāsyati Māraḥ. So, regarding Māra’s prediction, a bodhisattva great being who is unwise and lacks the skill in means will think, ‘Oh! This monk has informed me that when I realize supreme, perfect awakening, my name will be the same as the name that I had been considering, pondering, and mulling over.’ Because of that, such a bodhisattva great being compares the name that he or she had already considered with the name expressed by the pernicious Māra, that is to say, by that monk magically created with his demon cohort. He or she then concludes, ‘The name that arose as a thought in my mind is the same name as the one foretold by this monk. The previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas must have foreordained me for unsurpassed, perfect awakening with such a name.’ However, Subhūti, that bodhisattva great being does not have the attributes, indications, or signs of the bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing that I have made known. Without these, that bodhisattva great being will become conceited. After becoming conceited, he or she will treat other bodhisattva great beings disrespectfully, thinking, ‘I have been foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’ Through pride, self-superiority, arrogance, egotism, conceitedness, and a deluded sense of grandeur, he or she distances him- or herself from the state of complete knowing, the unsurpassed knowing of a buddha. Such a bodhisattva great being is not supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and lacks the skill in means. Since he or she is not supported by a virtuous friend, but rather by an unvirtuous friend, such a bodhisattva great being would long for one of the two lesser levels, namely, that of a disciple or a solitary realizer. However, suppose that after spending a very long, long time spinning away in successive rebirths, he or she were to wish to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening based on this very transcendent state of wisdom. Then, suppose that he or she were to get closer to virtuous friends and approach them again and again. Finally, suppose that with the very body that he or she attained in that life, he or she were to condemn his or her own previous development of conceited mindsets, reject them, become disgusted with them, renounce them, and confess them. Even still, such a bodhisattva great being would face difficulty attaining the level of a buddha. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, the underlying condition of conceit as a transgression is so extremely serious for a bodhisattva great being that, Subhūti, it is comparable to the seriousness of the four root transgressions for a monk at the level of a disciple. After a disciple commits any one of these, he is no longer an ascetic, no longer a monk, and no longer among the sons of the Śākya. But this very transgression of developing a proud mindset is more serious than those four transgressions, in that it is an extremely unvirtuous mindset for a bodhisattva great being to have developed through treating others disrespectfully as a result of Māra’s giving him or her a title. One should understand this development of such a mindset to be a more serious transgression than those four transgressions.

“But, Subhūti, setting aside the seriousness of the four root transgressions, Subhūti, the development of this mindset‍—connected with a bodhisattva great being’s pride because of being given a title‍—is even more serious than the five karmic deeds with immediate consequences and it should be understood to be more serious than the five karmic deeds with immediate consequences. Therefore, Subhūti, since the subtlest among Māra’s activities employs the giving of titles, they must be recognized by a bodhisattva great being and should be made known to others too. Once recognized, they should be abandoned.According to Ratnākara (F.166.b), “subtlest” means it is difficult to perceive. RK: sūkṣmasūkṣmāṇī ti durlakṣyāṇi.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, the pernicious Māra will approach a bodhisattva great being to reproach and warn him or her regarding the virtues of seclusion.This next section features a play on two etymologically related Sanskrit terms that are later contrasted to two others. In English, some of the punning and play on the relationship between these pairs of terms and their wider connotations has had to be sacrificed to make the meaning clear. The noun viveka we have translated here as “seclusion,” and the adjective vivikta as “secluded from.” However, the word play depends on the fact that viveka can mean not only “isolated place” or “seclusion,” but also, more broadly, “voidness”‍—i.e., “emptiness”‍—and vivikta can mean “devoid of” or “isolated.” Indeed, everywhere else in this scripture the term viveka, rendered as “void” or “voidness,” is used as an almost perfect synonym of emptiness, while vivikta, for the most part, has been rendered simply as “lacking” or “devoid of.” Hence “living in seclusion” here most literally means living completely isolated from others, but living in voidness also means being in the void state, i.e., realizing emptiness. These two terms are later contrasted with the opposite terms “mingling” (saṃkīrṇa)‍—either in the sense of “mixed up with” (confused or polluted by) or “mingling together with others”‍—and “crowded” (ākīrṇa). Subhūti, how could the pernicious Māra approach to reproach and warn a bodhisattva great being regarding the virtues of empty places? In this case, Subhūti, the pernicious Māra will approach a bodhisattva great being and say, ‘The Tathāgata praises seclusion, saying that one should live in wildernesses, forested areas, mountain caves, charnel grounds, haystacks, and so on.’ However, Subhūti, wildernesses, solitary beds and seats, deserted and remote places, and different forested areas, mountain caves, charnel grounds, haystacks, and so on are not the sort of seclusion I have recommended for bodhisattva great beings.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, what other kinds of seclusion are there for bodhisattva great beings, if not wildernesses, solitary beds and seats, deserted and remote places, and different forested areas, mountain caves, charnel grounds, haystacks, and so on? If they do not live in those kinds of places, what other kinds of seclusion are there for bodhisattva great beings?”

At this, the Blessed One said to Subhūti, “Subhūti, if a bodhisattva great being is secluded from the states of directed mental attention associated with disciples and solitary realizers, then he or she lives in seclusion.According to Ratnākara (F.166.b), living “in seclusion” means apart (from others). RK: vivikto rahitaḥ. This is because even when living around a village,According to Ratnākara (F.166.b), “living around a village” can mean either on the border of a village or in one part of it. RK: grāma syāntaḥ samīpam ekadeśo vā. a bodhisattva great being who is supported by the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means would live in the state of benevolence and compassion for all sentient beings. By living in such a state, he or she lives in utter seclusion. This state, secluded from the states of directed mental attention associated with disciples and solitary realizers, is the state I have recommended for bodhisattva great beings. For a bodhisattva great being to spend his or her time, night and day, in that secluded state is to live in seclusion.

“If a bodhisattva great being lives in such a state when in wildernesses, forested areas, mountain caves, charnel grounds, and solitary beds and seats, then that bodhisattva great being is living in seclusion. But, Subhūti, if that bodhisattva great being is not practicing in the transcendent state of wisdom and is just mingling with the states of directed mental attention associated with disciples and solitary realizers, while living in the sort of seclusion that the pernicious Māra advocates‍—wilderness areas, forested areas, mountain caves, charnel grounds, and solitary beds and seats‍—then that bodhisattva great being is not perfecting the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. Thus, since he or she is living in a mixed-up state and living in a state of directed mental attention that is not completely pure, his or her physical, verbal, and mental karmic deeds do not end up completely purified. Because his or her physical, verbal, and mental karmic deeds do not end up completely purified, such a bodhisattva great being may be disrespectful toward any other bodhisattva great beings who, because they live around a village and live in the state of wisdom, means, and great compassion, have not mingled with the states of directed mental attention associated with disciples and solitary realizers. Even though such a bodhisattva great being might live in wildernesses, his or her physical, verbal, and mental karmic deeds do not end up completely purified, so he or she is just living mixed up, and not living in seclusion. At first he or she treats disrespectfully those other bodhisattva great beings who live around a village, even though they are living in the state of wisdom, means, and great compassion; are practicing completely pure physical, verbal, and mental karmic deeds; are secluded from the states of directed mental attention associated with disciples and solitary realizers; and are not mingling with the states of directed mental attention associated with disciples and solitary realizers. As a consequence of that, he or she will fail to attain the meditative states, the meditative concentrations, the meditative attainments, the complete liberations, and the types of extrasensory knowledge, and will fail to perfect them. Why is that? Because he or she lacks the skill in means.

“Subhūti, suppose that a bodhisattva great being were to live all the time in the middle of a two-hundred-mile-wide wild forest area‍—staying apart from the company of wild beasts or birds, of any small roaming creatures, wild animals, yakṣas, or rakṣasas, and safe from dens of thieves or frightening and dangerous attacks‍—for one year or even a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand, ten million, one billion, ten billion, one trillion, or one quintillion years or more.It is not clear from the Sanskrit text whether the forest area is “two hundred miles wide” (as translated here) or “at a distance of two hundred miles” (locative of distance; cf. Macdonell 203j). Either way, the “two hundred miles” is simply emphasizing the remoteness. Now suppose also that this bodhisattva great being was not aware of the seclusion that I have spoken of. In that case, since such a bodhisattva great being, lacking the skill in means, would be unaware of the secluded state in which a bodhisattva great being sets out with the supreme altruistic disposition or perfects it, he or she would live there as someone who takes the wilderness itself to be supreme. He or she might have depended upon that type of seclusion, relied upon it, dwelled in it, and devoted him- or herself to it.According to Ratnākara (F.166.b), “depended” with moderate habitual fixation; “relied” with medium habitual fixation; “dwelled” with excessive fixation; and “devoted him- or herself” through regularly exalting themselves with it. RK: mṛdunā abhiniveśena niśritaḥ| madhyena ālīnaḥ| adhimātreṇa adhyavasitaḥ| tenaivātmotkarṣaṇāt adhyavasāyam āpannaḥ. Nevertheless, Subhūti, such a bodhisattva great being never brings my heart any pleasure. Why not? Because, Subhūti, while living in that sort of seclusion, that person is not observing the seclusion of a bodhisattva great being as I have explained it. The pernicious Māra might approach him or her, floating in the sky, to commend him or her: ‘Excellent! Excellent, noble child! This is the seclusion of a bodhisattva great being that the Tathāgata explained. Noble child, live in this seclusion and in this way you will quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’ Then, when such a person comes back once again from that empty wilderness to the area of the village, he or she will treat the other bodhisattva great beings disrespectfully, even though they are graceful monks leading a chaste life with spiritual qualities, are not mingling with the states of directed mental attention associated with disciples and solitary realizers, and live with physical, verbal, and mental karmic deeds that will end up completely purified. To those bodhisattva great beings, such a person will say, ‘Alas, you venerable ones are living in a mixed-up state, not living in seclusion.There is a double meaning in the Sanskrit phrase vihārena vihārati, which means abiding either in (a) an abode or dwelling place, or (b) a state or condition. Up until now, it has been translated as “state” since that can more easily stretch to include both meanings. But below it will be translated where necessary as “abode.” Alas, you venerable ones are living in a crowded state, not living in seclusion.’ In this way, he or she will criticize those bodhisattva great beings‍—who are actually living in seclusion‍—for living in a mixed-up state or in a crowded state.According to Ratnākara (F.166.b), the “mixed-up state” refers to (an abode) with householders and monastic renunciants. The “crowded state” means (an abode) crowded with only monastic renunciants. RK: saṃkīrṇo gṛhipravrajitaiḥ| ākīrṇaḥ pravrajitair eva. According to Hari (783), it is a “mixed-up state” (viz. a polluted state) because it involves the states of directed mental attention of disciples and so on. It is a “crowded state” (viz. an abode among the crowds) because it falls outside the dimension of the Mahāyāna. Hari: saṃkīrṇavihāreṇe ti śrāvakādimanaskāropetatvāt. ākīrṇavihāreṇe ti mahāyānād bahirgatavihāratvāt. Note: The commentators diverge on how to interpret the words here beyond the aforementioned play on the words saṃkīrṇa‍—i.e., (a) “mixed together” as in combined and (b) “mixed up with” in the sense of polluted or confused by‍—and ākīrṇavihāra, i.e., (a) an abode “full of people” or (b) abiding in crowds (i.e., filled with common people). Also, he or she will push those living in a mixed-up state to move physically to a secluded place, to develop devotion toward it, and to feel proud about its being worthy of devotion. If you were to ask him or her why, he or she would say, ‘Subhūti, I have been reproached and warned by a superhuman spirit.The “superhuman spirit” refers to Māra who, in disguise, reproached and warned them earlier in the chapter to remain in an empty place. So, this is the place in which I am abiding. What village dweller is a superhuman spirit reproaching? What place in this village is a superhuman spirit warning that village dweller about?’The assumption is that he is a forest dweller, proud about getting advice from spirits. Such a person speaks this way, because he or she treats other persons on the bodhisattva path disrespectfully. Subhūti, one should recognize a person like this to be an outcaste among bodhisattvas, a corrupter of bodhisattvas, a mere likeness of bodhisattvas, a false imitation of bodhisattvas, a poor decoy of bodhisattvas, a thief disguised as an ascetic, a user of persons belonging to the bodhisattva path, and a usurper of the world with its gods.According to Ratnākara (F.166.b–67.a), someone like this is “an outcaste” because others should not live with them; “a corrupter” because they corrupt others with their own faults: “a mere likeness” because they physically look like (bodhisattvas but are not); “a false imitation” because they have a similar name (to bodhisattvas but are not); “a poor decoy “ because their (duck-like) blackness does not resemble the whiteness of those swans (i.e., real bodhisattvas); “a thief” because they use the disguise of ascetics to get hold of the teachings; “a user” because bodhisattvas do not consent to this; and “a usurper of the world with its gods” because they act in a way worthy of veneration but falsely. RK: bodhisattvacaṇḍālaḥ tair asaṃvāsyatvāt| bodhisattvadūṣī svadoṣeṇa teṣāṃ dūṣaṇāt| bodhisattvapratirūpako dehasāmyāt| bodhi­sattvaprativarṇiko nāmasāmyāt| bodhisattvakāraṇḍavakaḥ haṃseṣv iva teṣv akṛṣṇeṣu kṛṣṇatvāt| cauraḥ śramaṇaveṣeṇa dharmagrahaṇāt| cauro bodhisattvānām asammatatvāt| cauraḥ sadevakasya lokasya mithyādakṣiṇīyatvāt. Note: The Sanskrit term caura is rendered here with three different translations based on the three different connotations that Ratnākara highlights. Subhūti, such a person is not worthy of being attended, not worthy of devotion, and not worthy of being served. Why not? Because one must recognize these kinds of persons to have fallen into conceitedness and to corrupt others who have little strength due to not having traveled the path for long. One must recognize a person like this as having impure qualities, no teacher, and ignoble qualities.According to Hari (783), they have “no teacher” because they are bereft of a spiritual friend. Hari: kalyāṇamitravirahād anācāryāḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.167.a), they are “ignoble,” i.e., evil. RK: anāryāḥ pāpāḥ. Note: There are two different Sanskrit manuscript readings attested: (1) having “no teacher” (anācāryāḥ ) in the present Tibetan edition and Hari, and (2) being “ignoble” (anāryāḥ) in the Sanskrit of the sūtra and in Ratnākara. The translation in the main text follows the Tibetan, but both commentators’ comments are reported here for comparison.

“But, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should never attend, be devoted toward, or serve people of that kind, if that bodhisattva great being has the altruistic disposition of wishing to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening to bring about the benefit of all sentient beings without ever abandoning any of them, the state of complete knowing, or unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Instead, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being who stands up for the benefit of all sentient beings should have a mindset concerned at absolutely all times with recognizing these and other acitivities of Māra. In doing so with an unfrightened mindset that is not engrossed in the three worlds, he or she can guide all the sentient beings who have not yet reached the path.This translation has followed the Tibetan yid mi skrag pa (*anuttrastamānasena) rather than the Sanskrit uttrastamānasena (Vaidya and Wogihara). Also, in a similar way, that bodhisattva great being should first of all practice benevolence and compassion. Then, after developing great compassion, he or she should have a mindset that sympathetically rejoices in sentient beings progressing perfectly along the path. Then, with impartiality toward the true nature of things free of mental perception, he or she should develop the proper mindset, thinking, ‘I will act so that there will be no corruptions from Māra’s activities, nor will any even arise in any form or manner. I will train so that if they do arise, they will immediately go away.’ One should understand this as the heroic effort of bodhisattva great beings to truly recognize this for themselves. Subhūti, this all should be recognized by the bodhisattva great being as Māra’s activity in connection with the positive qualities of voidness.”According to Ratnākara (F.167.a), “this all” and so on is just a final summary of the activities of Māra (described in the chapter). RK: idam apī tyādinā paścimamārakarmopasaṃhāraḥ.
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Detailing Māra’s Activities,” the twenty-first chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 22: Virtuous FriendsAccording to Ratnākara (F.168.b), the “Chapter on Virtuous Friends” is a chapter about virtuous friends and so on. RK: kalyāṇamitrādiḥ parivarto kalyāṇamitraparivartaḥ.The beginning of this chapter corresponds to a point within Chapter 45 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (starting at 45.23) and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 55 in the Eighteen Thousand (starting at 55.28). The content of the chapter corresponds to the remainder of those same chapters.
div.p: Then the Blessed One once again addressed Subhūti: “Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being who has set out with the altruistic disposition and wishes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening should attend virtuous friends, be devoted to them, and serve them right from the start.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, if a bodhisattva great being has set out with the altruistic disposition and wishes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, which of the many possible virtuous friends should he or she attend, be devoted to, and serve?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, only the blessed buddhas and the bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing who are skilled in the bodhisattva’s way of going about life can instruct and guide him or her in the transcendent states, and can teach and expound to him or her this transcendent state of wisdom. So, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should understand them to be his or her virtuous friends. But also, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should understand the transcendent state of wisdom, in particular, to be his or her virtuous friend, and, in fact, Subhūti, all six transcendent states to be his or her virtuous friends.According to Ratnākara (F.167.a), “the transcendent state of wisdom, in particular” is highlighted because it is the main one, and “the six transcendent states in fact” since that one comes along with the six. lRK: prajñāpāramitaive ti prādhānyād avadhāraṇam| yathā ca saikā tathā sahitāḥ ṣaṭ. That is to say, precisely these six transcendent states are the teacher.According to Hari (787), the six transcendent states are the “teacher” at the stage of the fruit. Hari: phalāvasthāḥ ṣaṭ pāramitāḥ śāstāḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.167.a), only those who have reached the end are the “teacher,” i.e., the final fruit. RK: tā eva niṣṭhāṃgatāḥ śāstā yaḥ paramaṃ phalam. The six transcendent states are the path.According to Hari (787), they are the “path” at the stage of gaining the sight because they show the path to the realization of the first level (in the warmth phase on the path of preliminary practice). Hari: prathamādhigama­mārga­saṃdarśanāl labdhālokāvasthā mārgah. According to Ratnākara (F.167.a), only prior to that (fruit) are they “the path.” RK: tataḥ pūrvānta eva mārgaḥ. The six transcendent states are the sight.According to Hari (787), they are “sight” at the stage of the ripening of seeing because they offer higher sight (in the peak phase on the path of preliminary practice). Hari: adhikālokarūpatvāt vṛddhālokāvasthā ālokaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.167.a), they alone are also the “sight,” i.e., the spiritual knowing on the delighted phase (i.e., the first level of a bodhisattva). RK: āloko lokottaraṃ jñānaṃ pramuditāyāṃ so pi tā eva. Note: It may not be clear up until this point that Ratnākara is disagreeing with Hari about which stage or phase of the bodhisattva’s path each metaphor or epithet is supposed to refer to. However, Ratnākara is giving a different interpretation of each one all along the way. The six transcendent states are the torch.According to Hari (787), they are the “torch” at the stage of extending (that sight) to a part of the ultimate truth, because they help a bodhisattva enter into one part of the ultimate emptiness of a perceiving subject or perceived object (in the phase of acceptance on the path of preliminary practice). Hari: grāhyagrāhakābhāva­tattvaikadeśa­praviṣṭatvāt tattvārthaika­deśaprasṛtāvasthā ūlkā. According to Ratnākara (F.167.a), they alone are also the “torch” that is the path involving effort on the stainless levels and so on (i.e., the second through seventh impure levels of a bodhisattva). RK: ulkā prayatnavāhī mārgo vimalādibhūmiṣu ṣaṭsu sopi tā eva. The six transcendent states are the radiance.According to Hari (787), they are the “torch” at the stage of meditative concentration that immediately precedes (insight), because they cause a bodhisattva to know reality right afterward (in the pinnacle of worldly things phase on the path of preliminary practice). Hari: anantaratattvajñānodayād ānantaryasamādhyavasthā avabhāsaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.167.a–b), they alone are also the “radiance”‍—i.e., heavenly light‍—that is the path involving no effort on the unmoving levels and so on (i.e., the eighth through tenth pure levels of a bodhisattva). For this reason, they alone are also the Three Jewels, and hence (the next four epithets, namely “shelter”), “refuge,” “ultimate resort,” and “island,” which were explained previously (by Ratnākara in an earlier chapter). RK: avabhāso divasālokaḥ| sa cāyatnavāhī mārgo ’calādiṣu bhūmiṣu so pi tā eva| ataś ca tā eva trīṇi ratnāni| tasmāt tā eva śaraṇaṃ yāvat dīpaḥ| padārthaḥ pūrvavat. The six transcendent states are the shelter.According to Hari (787), they are the “shelter” on the first bodhisattva level because they block out all possible misfortunes. Hari: sarvopadravanivāraṇāt prathamāyāṃ bhūmau trāṇaṃ. The six transcendent states are the refuge.According to Hari (787), they are the “refuge” on the second bodhisattva level because they are the place (of refuge) to go to so that practice with an altruistic disposition is never fruitless. Hari: tadāśayaprayogāvandhyatvapadasthānena dvitīyāyāṃ śaraṇam. The six transcendent states are the sanctuary.According to Hari (787), they are the “sanctuary” on the third bodhisattva level because they are the site of faultless pleasure. Hari: niravadyarativastutvāt tṛtīyāyāṃ layanaṃ. The six transcendent states are the ultimate resort.According to Hari (787), they are the “ultimate resort” on the fourth bodhisattva level because they are the place to go to in order to reach the supreme state of a noble being. Hari: paramāryatvāgamanapadasthānena caturthyāṃ parāyaṇam. The six transcendent states are the island.According to Hari (787), they are the “island” on the fifth bodhisattva level because they are delimited from the three realms. Hari: traidhātukaparicchinnatvāt pañcamyāṃ dvīpaḥ. The six transcendent states are the mother.According to Hari (787), they are the “mother” on the sixth bodhisattva level because their nature is the transcendent state of wisdom. Hari: prajñāpāramitāsvabhāvatvāt ṣaṣṭhyāṃ mātā. According to Ratnākara (F.167.b), they are the “mother” because they carry and feed (bodhisattvas on the noble path). RK: tā eva mātā dhāraṇāt poṣaṇāc ca. The six transcendent states are the father.According to Hari (787), they are the “father” on the seventh bodhisattva level because their nature is the method (i.e., skillful means). Hari: upāyarūpatvāt saptamyāṃ pitā. According to Ratnākara (F.167.b), they are the “father” because they provide the seed (for bodhisattvas). RK: tā eva pitā bijādhānāt. The six transcendent states lead to knowledge and realization, and progress to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Hari (787), they “lead to knowledge” on the eighth bodhisattva level because their nature consists in (pure) aspirations, and they “lead to realization” on the ninth bodhisattva level because they are characterized by the transcendent perfection of strength. They “progress to unsurpassed, perfect awakening” at the tenth bodhisattva level because the transcendent perfection of knowing is unequaled. These are the different meanings (of these epithets). Hari: praṇidhānātmakatvād aṣṭamyāṃ jñānāya, bala­pāramitā­lakṣaṇatvān navamyāṃ bodhāya, jñānapāramitātiriktatvena daśamyām anuttarāyai samyaksambodhaye saṃvartanta ity arthabhedaḥ. According to Ratnākara (F.167.b), they “lead to knowledge,” i.e., the path of seeing (on the first bodhisattva level), and “lead to realization,” i.e., the path of cultivation (on the next nine bodhisattva levels). RK: jñānāyet i darśanāya| bodhāye ti bhāvanāmārgāya. What is the cause for this?According to Ratnākara (F.167.b), “What is the cause for this?” asks the question why the transcendent state of wisdom alone has the qualities described, not the others. What follows is the reply. RK: tat kasya hetor iti kutaḥ prajñāpāramitaiva yathoktaguṇā netarā ity arthaḥ| atra hī tyādinā parihāraḥ. Subhūti, the cause is that the transcendent state of wisdom is consummate within them, that is to say, within these six transcendent states.

“So, Subhūti, those previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who attained complete nirvāṇa had the blessed buddhas’ state of complete knowing that is only born from these, that is to say, from the six transcendent states.According to Ratnākara (F.167.b), he says these sentences in order to explain the fact that the state of complete knowing belonging to the buddhas of the three times issues forth from these (transcendent states). “Born” means accomplished. RK: traiyadhvikabuddhānāṃ ca sarvajñatā tatprasūtaivety ākhyātum āha| yepī tyādi| nirjātā niṣpannā. Also, Subhūti, those future worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening will have the blessed buddhas’ state of complete knowing that is only born from these, that is to say, from the six transcendent states. Also, Subhūti, those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who are now residing, staying, and spending time, and who are realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening, in immeasurable, innumerable, unlimited, inconceivable world systems, they too have the blessed buddhas’ state of complete knowing that is only born from these, that is to say, from the six transcendent states.

“Subhūti, I too am the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata who has, at this moment, realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and indeed my state of complete knowing was only born from these, that is to say, from the six transcendent states.

“How so? Because, Subhūti, the six transcendent states include all the awakened qualities‍—that is, the thirty-seven factors in awakening, the four immeasurable states, the four ways of attracting disciples, and any and every other awakened quality of a buddha.According to Ratnākara (F.167.b), “they include” means they respectively contain. RK: antargatā iti yathāyogam antarbhūtāḥ. They include also the knowing of a buddha, which is to say, the knowing of a self-existent one, the knowing of an inconceivable one, the knowing of an incomparable one, the knowing of an immeasurable one, the knowing of an incalculable one, the knowing of an unequaled one, the knowing of one equal to the unequaled, and the knowing of someone who has complete knowing.

“Therefore, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should understand the virtuous friends to be none other than the six transcendent states. The six transcendent states alone are the teacher. The six transcendent states are the path. The six transcendent states are the sight. The six transcendent states are the torch. The six transcendent states are the radiance. The six transcendent states are the shelter. The six transcendent states are the refuge. The six transcendent states are the sanctuary. The six transcendent states are the ultimate resort. The six transcendent states are the island. The six transcendent states are the mother. The six transcendent states are the father. The six transcendent states lead to knowledge and realization, and progress to attaining unsurpassed, perfect awakening. When a bodhisattva great being trains in the six transcendent states, he or she becomes, in effect, a protector to all sentient beings without any protector.According to Ratnākara (F.167.b), one should connect their becoming “a protector (upakāribhūto) to all sentient beings” with the series of words “the knowing of a buddha” and so on mentioned in the immediately previous paragraph. RK: buddhajñānā dipadāni pūrvam eva vyākhyātāni| sarva bhūtānāṃ upakāribhūto bhavatī ti sambandhaḥ.

“Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being who wishes to train in the six transcendent states should listen to this very transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it. On its meaning, its truth, and its method, he or she should also reflect, meditate, inquire, and fully pursue his or her questions. Why? Because, as foremost among the six transcendent states, the transcendent state of wisdom is the guide, the leader, the beacon, the paragon, the mother who gives birth, and the mother who nurses. Why? Because, without the transcendent state of wisdom, the five other transcendent states would neither be distinguished nor designated as transcendent states. Therefore, Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being who wishes not to be led astray by others should train in this very transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.167.b), since the “transcendent state of wisdom is the leader,” he or she will “not be led astray by others.” Not being led astray is the tenth characteristic thus far. RK: yasmāt iyam eva praṇāyikā tasmāt aparapraṇeyatā ityādi| tasmād aparapraṇeyatā daśamaṃ liṅgam.
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, what is the defining characteristic of the transcendent state of wisdom?”
At this, the Blessed One said to Subhūti, “Subhūti, the defining characteristic of the transcendent state of wisdom is nonattachment.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, in a manner of speaking, could it be said that all things would be observed with the very same defining characteristic of nonattachment that the transcendent state of wisdom is observed with?”

“Yes, Subhūti, yes,” said the Blessed One. “In a manner of speaking, it could be said that all things would be observed with the very same characteristic of nonattachment that the transcendent state of wisdom is observed with. How so? Because, Subhūti, all things are void and empty. Therefore, Subhūti, all things, which are void and empty, are observed with that very same defining characteristic of nonattachment that the transcendent state of wisdom, which is void and empty, is observed with.”According to Hari (790), “the defining characteristic of nonattachment” means it has the nature of freedom from habitual fixation. Hari: asaṅgalakṣaṇe ti anabhiniveśasvabhāvā. According to Ratnākara (F.167.b), a “defining characteristic of nonattachment” means a defining characteristic free of any mental perception, because the defining characteristics of all things are not mentally perceived in that transcendent state of wisdom. The same is also true of physical forms and so on, because they are empty and void. RK: asaṅgalakṣaṇe ty anupalambhalakṣaṇā| sarva­dharma­svalakṣaṇānāṃ tasyām anupalambhāt| tathā rūpādayo pi śūnyatvād viviktatvāt.

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, if all things are void and empty, then, Blessed One, how could someone wisely discern the defilement of sentient beings? How, Blessed One, could someone wisely discern the purification of sentient beings?According to Ratnākara (F.167.b), he raises a controversy with the words “If all things…” If it were not a controversy, it would instead mean “definitely not so!” RK: yadī tyādinā codyaṃ, na codyaṃ na hīty arthaḥ. Also, Blessed One, something void is not defiled and not purified, and, Blessed One, something empty is not defiled and not purified. Moreover, Blessed One, something void and empty cannot realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and, Blessed One, one cannot mentally perceive a thing apart from that voidness and emptiness that has realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, is realizing it, or will come to realize it. So, how do we understand the meaning of what you have described? Please teach us, Blessed One. Please teach us, Sugata.”According to Hari (790), “of what you have described” means the teaching on voidness and emptiness in relation to the nonarising of defilement and so on. Hari: asya bhāṣitasye ti saṃkleśādyanupapattau viviktaśūnyatādeśanāyāḥ.
At this, the Blessed One asked Subhūti, “Subhūti, what do you think? Do sentient beings go about life for a long time in the conceits of ‘me’ and ‘mine’?”
“Yes, Blessed One. Yes, Sugata,” said Subhūti. “Sentient beings go about life for a long time in the conceits of ‘me’ and ‘mine.’ ”
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Are the conceits of ‘me’ and ‘mine’ empty?”
“They are empty, Blessed One. Empty, Sugata,” said Subhūti.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Are sentient beings going around in cyclic existence because of the conceits of ‘me’ and ‘mine’?”
“Yes, Blessed One. Yes, Sugata,” said Subhūti. “Sentient beings are going around in cyclic existence because of the conceits of ‘me’ and ‘mine.’ ”

“So, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One, “someone wisely discerns the defilement of sentient beings as follows: If sentient beings have conceptual grasping and habitual fixation, then there is defilement. But in this case, no one is defiled, and yet, Subhūti, someone does not wisely discern them as having conceits of ‘me’ and ‘mine,’ as long as they have no conceptual grasping or habitual fixation. Likewise, Subhūti, someone wisely discerns the purification of sentient beings as follows: If sentient beings have no conceptual grasping or habitual fixation, then there is purification. But in this case, no one becomes purified. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being, going about life in this way, goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.167.b), it is “conceptual grasping” of a conventional sign. “Habitual fixation” is reifying that (i.e., grasping it as real). So, the defining characteristic of the transcendent state of wisdom and things is nonattachment in this way. RK: udgraho nimittasya| abhiniveśo vastutvena grāhaḥ| tad evam asaṅgalakṣaṇā bhagavatī dharmāś ca. Thus, Subhūti, even though all things are void and empty, he or she wisely discerns the defilement and purification of sentient beings.”According to Hari (790–91), this is what he is saying: All things are (ultimately) empty. But since there is a relationship between cause and effect at the conventional level, defilement appears when there is habitual fixation, and one discerns the purification (of that) when there is no habitual fixation. Hari: etad uktaṃ: śūnyatve ’pi sarvadharmāṇāṃ saṃvṛtyā karmaphalasambandhasya vidyamānatvād yathā ’bhiniveśas tathā saṃkleśo, yathā cānabhiniveśas tathā vyavadānaṃ ca prajñāyata iti.

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, it is wonderful that, even though all things are void and empty, he or she wisely discerns the defilement and purification of sentient beings. It is wonderful that a bodhisattva great being, going about life in this way, goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. This is because a bodhisattva great being who goes about life like this does not go about life in physical forms, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being going about life in this way cannot be conquered by anyone in the world with its gods, humans, and asuras. Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being going about life in this way surpasses the way of going about life for persons belonging to the paths of the disciples or of the solitary realizers, and will attain a state that is unsurpassable. How so? Because, Blessed One, the state of a buddha, a tathāgata, a self-existent being, and someone who has complete knowing is unsurpassable. Also, Blessed One, if a bodhisattva great being were to spend his or her time, night and day, abiding in the state of attentiveness associated with the transcendent state of wisdom, then he or she would approach unsurpassed, perfect awakening and quickly realize it.”

“Yes, Subhūti, yes,” said the Blessed One. “A bodhisattva great being who goes about life in this way does not go about life in physical forms. Likewise, he or she does not go about life in feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being going about life in this way cannot be conquered by the world with its gods, humans, and asuras.According to Ratnākara (F.168.a), “cannot be conquered” means cannot be led astray. RK: anavamardanīya ity asaṃhāryaḥ. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being going about life in this way surpasses the way of going about life for persons belonging to the paths of the disciples or of the solitary realizers, and he or she will attain a state that is unsurpassable. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, the state of a buddha, a tathāgata, a self-existent being, and one with complete knowing is unsurpassable. Also, Subhūti, if a bodhisattva great being were to spend his or her time, night and day, abiding in the state of attentiveness associated with the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she would approach unsurpassed, perfect awakening and quickly realize it.

“Subhūti, suppose every sentient being on the Jambu continent were to obtain a human being’s physical body all at once. Suppose that after obtaining a human body, each were to develop the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Suppose that after developing the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening, each were to remain in it throughout their lives.
