Suppose that while remaining in it throughout their lives, each were to pay tribute to all the tathāgatas, revere them, respect them, venerate them, honor them, and tend to them throughout their lives. Also, suppose that each were to give gifts in the same way to all sentient beings. Finally, suppose each were to dedicate those gifts to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.168.a), “dedicate those gifts” that they gave to sentient beings and the buddhas “to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.” RK: tad dānam iti yad buddhebhyo sarvasattvebhyaḥ. Subhūti, what do you think? Based on this, would the bodhisattva great being earn much merit?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, that noble son or daughter who abides in the attentive mental states associated with the transcendent state of wisdom for even so much as a single day would earn an even greater amount of merit than this. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, the more that a bodhisattva great being abides in the attentive mental states associated with the transcendent state of wisdom, night and day, the more he or she becomes worthy of veneration by sentient beings.According to Ratnākara (F.168.a), he or she “abides in the attentive mental states” focused on increasing it. RK: manasikāraiḥ viharatī ti vivṛddhisaṃgṛhītaiḥ. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, the mindset associated with benevolence that a bodhisattva great being has is not like that of anyone else, except for the blessed buddhas. Why is that?According to Ratnākara (F.168.a), “why is that” asks the question why the buddhas are excluded. RK: tat teṣāṃ sthāpanaṃ kasya hetoḥ? Because, Subhūti, the tathāgatas have no counterpart. Subhūti, the tathāgatas are incomparable. Subhūti, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas possess inconceivable qualities.

“So, how does a noble son or daughter produce that much merit? Through the kind of wisdom that a bodhisattva great being possesses, he or she sees all sentient beings as though they are on the way to their slaughter, and at that very moment he or she is gripped by great compassion.According to Ratnākara (F.168.a), “on the way to their slaughter” means on the way to the slaughterhouse. RK: vadhyagatān ive ti vadhyasthānagatān iva. Beholding them with his or her divine eye, such a bodhisattva great being sees immeasurable, innumerable, unlimited, and illimitable sentient beings whose karmic deeds lead to the immediate consequences of obtaining inauspicious births, being destroyed, not finding the path, and getting caught up in the web of erroneous beliefs. He or she also sees others who have obtained auspicious births wasting their good fortune.According to Ratnākara (F.168.a), “wasting their good fortune” as though they had never obtained it. RK: virāgayata ity aprāptavataḥ. According to Hari (795), “wasting their good fortune” means losing the human state and so on through doing harmful deeds. Hari: kṣaṇāṃś ca virāgayata iti pāpānuṣṭhānena manuṣyādibhāvān nāśayataḥ. At these moments, a bodhisattva great being feels extremely moved. Throbbing with deep benevolence and great compassion, he or she focuses mentally upon sentient beings, thinking, ‘I must become a protector for all these sentient beings. I must liberate all these sentient beings from their suffering.’ Yet, such a bodhisattva great being does not live embroiled with this conventional sign or any other, because, Subhūti, he or she also is filled with the great light of wisdom to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening.The translation follows the Sanskrit edition rather than the Tibetan, which differs slightly here by including an extra, redundant description of the bodhisattva as “being wise.”

“Subhūti, those bodhisattva great beings who abide in such a state accept that they are worthy of veneration by the whole world, but do not turn away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Since their mindset is perfectly established in this transcendent state of wisdom, those bodhisattva great beings purify the gifts and devotional offerings of those donors and patrons, on whose behalf they enjoy the robes, alms, accommodation, preventive medicines, and other requisites, while approaching the state of complete knowing. Therefore, Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being should bring to mind the attentive mental states associated with the transcendent state of wisdom if he or she wishes to unerringly enjoy the alms of the realm, wishes to expound the path to all sentient beings, wishes to help their radiance expand, wishes to liberate all sentient beings in cyclic existence from that cyclic existence, and wishes to purify the way they see. So, if a bodhisattva great being wishes to abide in such attentiveness, then he or she should bring to mind these types of attentiveness associated with the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Hari (795), “such attentiveness” means the attentiveness that has the (just mentioned) nature of pointing out the path to sentient beings and so on. Hari: anena manasikāreṇe ti sattvānāṃ mārgopadeśādisvabhāvena. Why is that? It is because any bodhisattva great being who wishes to bring these to mind should be attentive to nothing but this.According to Hari (795), “attentive to nothing but this” means that when a bodhisattva is focusing on the mother transcendent state of wisdom with its great skill in means, he or she is only being attentive to the benefit of beings. Hari: sa eve ti mahopāyakauśalāyā mātur ālambane sa eva sattvopakāramanaskāraḥ. He or she should not leave room for any other attentiveness that lacks the transcendent state of wisdom. And a bodhisattva great being should act so that he or she can spend night and day in the attentive mental states associated with the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Hari (795), can “spend,” i.e., pass his or her time, “in the attentivenesses,” whose nature is benevolence, compassion, and so on. Hari: yathāyam iti maitryādisvabhāvo manaskāraḥ. kṣapayed ity atikrāmayet.

“Subhūti, as an analogy, it is just as if a certain man, knowledgeable about jewels and their different varieties, were to obtain a valuable jewel he had not obtained before. Upon obtaining that jewel, he would be filled with extremely immense joy and delight. But if his jewel were to disappear, then because of that he would be filled with great suffering and despair. With his attentiveness only connected to the valuable jewel, he would keep on thinking, ‘Oh, I have become disconnected from such a valuable jewel.’ Indeed, he would never forget such a valuable jewel until he could regain either that very same valuable jewel or an equivalent one with the same positive qualities. Subhūti, in this very same way, a bodhisattva great being who has lost the valuable jewel of the transcendent state of wisdom‍—just like someone with a valuable jewel who has lost that valuable jewel‍—might lose attentiveness to the transcendent state of wisdom. But he or she would perceive its value and would seek this transcendent state of wisdom scripture. And, with his or her mind set on the state of complete knowing, he or she would never lack attentiveness to the transcendent state of wisdom until he or she were to find either that very same one or a different one.According to Hari (795), “until he or she were to find either that very same one or a different one” means “the same” transcendent state of wisdom or “another one,” such as the King of Samādhis Sūtra and so on, where (a definitive explanation of) the true nature of things can be found. Some scholars say that the phrase “same one or a different one” depends (rather on a distinction between) book copies. Hari: yāvat sā vānyā vā pratilabdhā bhavatīti sā vā prajñāpāramitānyā vā samādhi­rājādi­sūtrānta­dharmatāprāptā bhavati. pustakāpekṣayā sā vānyā ve ti kecit. According to Ratnākara (F.168.a), “he or she would seek” the transcendent state of wisdom scripture “with a mind set on never lacking…” The phrase “same one or a different one” is based on a difference of book copies. RK: avirahitasarvajñatācittena| avirahitāt pūrvaṃ manasikāra śabdaḥ kvacit paṭhyate| sā vānyā ve ti pustakabhedāt. Note: Ratnākara also gives here a technical note that some versions of the scripture read “never lacking attentiveness” (as the modern Sanskrit edition does, whereas others do not). The whole time he or she would never lack the attentive mental states associated with the obtainment of the valuable jewel of the transcendent state of wisdom, or lack the attentive mental states associated with the obtainment of the valuable jewel of the state of complete knowing.”

Subhūti said, “But, Blessed One, the Blessed One has taught that all things and all attentive mental states lack inherent nature and are empty. So, Blessed One, how could a bodhisattva great being never lack the attentive mental states associated with the transcendent state of wisdom and associated with the state of complete knowing?”

At this, the Blessed One said to Subhūti, “Subhūti, if a bodhisattva great being pays attention to all things lacking in inherent nature and being empty of inherent nature, then, by paying attention like this, he or she never lacks the attentive mental states associated with the transcendent state of wisdom or the attentive mental states associated with the state of complete knowing. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, the transcendent state of wisdom is empty and never increases or decreases.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, if the transcendent state of wisdom is empty and never increases or decreases, then, Blessed One, how could a bodhisattva great being come to attain awakening without the transcendent state of wisdom increasing? And, how could he or she realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”

At this, the Blessed One said to Subhūti, “Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom neither increases nor decreases. A bodhisattva great being is empty and never increases or decreases, in just the same way that the transcendent state of wisdom is empty and never increases or decreases. Hence, a bodhisattva great being comes to attain awakening and realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening because that transcendent state of wisdom is empty and never increases or decreases, in just the same way that a bodhisattva great being is empty and never increases or decreases. Subhūti, if, when this is being taught, a bodhisattva great being is not frightened, terrified, stricken with terror, or disheartened, then, Subhūti, one can be certain that this bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.”
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, does the transcendent state of wisdom go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom?”
“Subhūti, not at all,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, does the emptiness of the transcendent state of wisdom go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom?”
“Subhūti, not at all,” said the Blessed One.

Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, other than the emptiness of the transcendent state of wisdom, is there any thing that goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom that is mentally perceived?”
“Subhūti, not at all,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, does emptiness go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom?”
“Subhūti, not at all,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, is there any thing in emptiness that goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom that is mentally perceived?”
“Subhūti, not at all,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, does emptiness go about life in emptiness?”
“Subhūti, not at all,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, do physical forms go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom?”
“Subhūti, not at all,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, do feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom?”
“Subhūti, not at all,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, apart from physical forms, is there any thing that goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom that is mentally perceived?”
“Subhūti, not at all,” said the Blessed One.

Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, apart from feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness, is there any thing that goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom that is mentally perceived?”
“Subhūti, not at all,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti asked, “Then, Blessed One, how could a bodhisattva great being go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom?”
At this, the Blessed One asked the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, do you identify a thing that goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom?”
“Blessed One, not at all,” said Subhūti.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, do you identify that transcendent state of wisdom in which a bodhisattva great being goes about life?”
“Blessed One, not at all,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Do you identify any thing that is free from mental perception? Has that very thing arisen, will it arise, or is it arising? Has it ceased, will it cease, or is it ceasing?”
“Blessed One, not at all,” said Subhūti.

The Blessed One said, “Thus, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being has this kind of acceptance that all things are free of arising. And, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being with this kind of acceptance is foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Subhūti, that bodhisattva great being is treading the path to a tathāgata’s grounds for self-confidence. By going about life, striving, and making efforts in this way, it is impossible that such a bodhisattva great being would not arrive at the unsurpassed knowing of a buddha, the knowing of someone who has complete knowing, or the knowing of the great caravan leader.”
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, is that true nature of all things being free of arising something foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”
“Subhūti, not at all,” said the Blessed One.
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, how could there be foreordaining for unsurpassed, perfect awakening for such a thing?”
The Blessed One asked in return, “Subhūti, do you identify any thing that possesses foreordaining for unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”

“Blessed One, not at all,” said Subhūti. “I do not identify a thing that has been foreordained, will be foreordained, or is being foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Blessed One, I also do not identify a thing that is awakened, that should be awakened, or that is awakening. Why is that? Blessed One, since no things are being mentally perceived, I do not have the thought, ‘This thing is awakened,’ or ‘This thing should be awakened,’ or ‘This thing is awakening.’ ”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Virtuous Friends,” the twenty-second chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 23: ŚakraAccording to Ratnākara (F.168.b), the “Śakra chapter” means the chapter spoken by him. RK: śakreṇoktaḥ parivartaḥ śakraparivartaḥ. Ratnākara gives practically no comments on this chapter.The content of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 46 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (46.1–10) and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 56 in the Eighteen Thousand (56.1–10).
div.p: At that time, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, had alighted and taken his seat in that very assembly. Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, this transcendent state of wisdom is profound. Blessed One, this transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to see and difficult to comprehend.”

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “True, Kauśika. It is true. Kauśika, this transcendent state of wisdom is profound. Alas, Kauśika, this transcendent state of wisdom is difficult to see and difficult to comprehend. This transcendent state of wisdom is profound just as space is profound. It is difficult to see, because it is void. It is difficult to comprehend, because it is empty.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, if sentient beings will listen to this profound transcendent state of wisdom, and after listening to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, then their root of virtue will not be trivial!”

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “True, Kauśika. It is true. Kauśika, if sentient beings will listen to this profound transcendent state of wisdom, and after listening to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, then their root of virtue will not be trivial. Kauśika, if as many sentient beings as there are now on the Jambu continent were all endowed with the ten virtuous ways of acting, then what do you think, Kauśika? Based on this, would those sentient beings earn much merit?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” said Śakra.

The Blessed One said, “Yet, Kauśika, more merit than that would be earned by a noble son or daughter who listens to this transcendent state of wisdom, and after listening to it, takes it in, keeps it in mind, chants it, masters it, passes it on, teaches it, expounds it, explains it, recites it, and writes it out. Any noble son or daughter who will listen to this profound transcendent state of wisdom, and after listening to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, will earn more merit than this. Kauśika, the amount of merit derived from the ethical discipline of all the past sentient beings on the Jambu continent does not come close in worth to even a fraction of this amount of merit. It does not come close in worth to one hundredth, one thousandth, one hundred thousandth, one ten millionth, one billionth, one ten billionth, one trillionth, or even one ten trillionth of it. Nor is any calculation, any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison adequate for comparing them. Any noble son or daughter who will listen to this profound transcendent state of wisdom, and after listening to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out, will be endowed with such a root of virtue.”

Then another monk said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, you are surpassed by any noble son or daughter who will listen to this profound transcendent state of wisdom, and after listening to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the monk, “I am surpassed by any noble son or daughter who has developed the mindset even just once. What more is there to say about someone who will listen to this profound transcendent state of wisdom, and after listening to it, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out. What more is there to say about those who, after having taken it in, kept it in mind, chanted it, mastered it, passed it on, taught it, expounded it, explained it, recited it, and written it out, will train to attain the actual nature, progress to attain the actual nature, and engage in meditation to attain the actual nature? Such a bodhisattva great being surpasses the whole world with its gods, humans, and asuras.Here, the plural “those bodhisattvas” has been made into the singular “such a bodhisattva great being” throughout Śakra’s speech in order to make the contrast between subject and object pronouns clearer. Interestingly, he started and ended speaking of a bodhisattva in the singular but somehow changed to plural midway.

“Such a bodhisattva great being not only surpasses the whole world with its gods, humans, and asuras, but also surpasses all the stream enterers, once-returners, non-returners, worthy ones, and solitary realizers. Such a bodhisattva great being not only surpasses the stream enterers, once-returners, non-returners, worthy ones, and solitary realizers, but also surpasses all those who are extremely generous patrons, yet lack the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means.

“Such a bodhisattva great being not only surpasses the extremely generous patrons who lack the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means, but also surpasses all those with pure ethical discipline, who possess a great amount of ethical discipline that is unbroken, flawless, stainless, perfect, purified, and faultless, yet lack the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means.

“Such a bodhisattva great being not only surpasses those who possess a great amount of ethical discipline that is unbroken, flawless, stainless, perfect, purified, and faultless, but also surpasses all those perfectly equipped with tolerant acceptance and patience, who possess mindsets free of any hostility within and do not become vengeful even when their house has been burned down, yet lack the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means.

“Such a bodhisattva great being not only surpasses those perfectly equipped with tolerant acceptance and patience, who possess mindsets free of any hostility, but also surpasses all those who have applied themselves with heroic perseverance, without shirking their duty, without laziness, and without shrinking away from accomplishing positive physical, verbal, and mental karmic deeds, yet lack the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means.

“Such a bodhisattva great being not only surpasses those who have applied themselves with heroic perseverance without shirking their duty, without laziness, and without having shrunk away from the accomplishment of positive physical, verbal, and mental karmic deeds, but also surpasses all those pleased and delighted with meditative states, who possess strength and power in the meditative states, are stationed in meditative states, and have mastered the meditative states, yet lack the transcendent state of wisdom and skill in means.

“Indeed, such a bodhisattva great being surpasses the world with its gods, humans, and asuras simply by going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom as explicated. He or she also surpasses those without skill in means who belong to the paths of the disciples and solitary realizers. Such a bodhisattva great being is not surpassed by any of them. Why is that? It is because a bodhisattva great being, who goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and pursues it as explicated, positions him- or herself to not cause any interruption to the lineage of those who have complete knowing. Such a bodhisattva great being keeps him- or herself not far from the Tathāgata. By conducting him- or herself in this manner, it will not be long before this bodhisattva great being reaches the epicenter of awakening. By training in this way, this bodhisattva great being will lift sentient beings out of the mud of the defilements that they are stuck in. In doing so, he or she will be engaged in the bodhisattva’s training, not the trainings of the disciples or solitary realizers. Moreover, the Four Great Kings, who are the guardians of the human world, will approach a bodhisattva great being training in this way and say, ‘Noble child! Train immediately in the bodhisattva’s way of going about life. Train with ease. These are the four begging bowls that you should receive once you are seated at the epicenter of awakening after realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’ Not only would those Four Great Kings, who are the guardians of the human world, approach a bodhisattva great being training in the transcendent state of wisdom the way it is explicated, but also I myself, Blessed One, will approach that bodhisattva great being; needless to say, those other gods will do the same. And the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas will even constantly bring to mind that bodhisattva great being. Going about life in this way in the transcendent state of wisdom, this bodhisattva great being will encounter absolutely no worldly sufferings or attacks by others in any form or manner. Blessed One, this bodhisattva great being going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom will have this benefit even in this present life.”
Then the venerable Ānanda thought, “Is Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, speaking through his own illumination or through the miraculous power of the Buddha?”

Then, through the miraculous power of the Buddha, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, knew in his mind what kind of mental rumination the venerable Ānanda was having, and said to the venerable Ānanda, “Noble Ānanda, one should know this to be the miraculous power of the Buddha. Noble Ānanda, one should know this also to be the blessings of the Buddha. I myself am powerless to talk about such a bodhisattva great being.”

Then the Blessed One said to the venerable Ānanda, “So it is, Ānanda. It is just as Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, has said. As Śakra said, it is due to the miraculous power of the Buddha and due to the blessings of the Buddha.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Śakra,” the twenty-third chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 24: ConceitednessThis chapter, the “Chapter on Conceitedness,” is the chapter that is synecdochic for (upalakṣitaḥ) a bodhisattva’s “conceitedness,” which is the cause of many setbacks to becoming (a bodhisattva) beyond regressing. RK: yo ’yam avinivartanīyo ’bhimāno bodhisattvasya bahvanarthahetus tadupalakṣitaḥ parivarto ’bhimānaparivartaḥ. Note: Ratnākara gives very few comments on this chapter.The beginning of this chapter corresponds to a point within Chapter 46 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (46.10) and Hundred Thousand, and within Chapter 56 in the Eighteen Thousand (56.11), and its content corresponds to the remainder of those chapters.
div.p: Then, once again, the Blessed One addressed the venerable Ānanda: “Ānanda, whenever a bodhisattva great being is training in the transcendent state of wisdom, engaging in meditation in it, or cultivating it, Ānanda, at that time, all the pernicious māras in the billionfold, great order world system fall into a state of uncertainty as to whether the bodhisattva great being will actualize the pinnacle of reality at the levels of the disciples or solitary realizers, or whether he or she will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Moreover, Ānanda, whenever a bodhisattva great being is abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom, at that time the pernicious māras feel deep pangs of grief. And, Ānanda, whenever a bodhisattva great being is training in the transcendent state of wisdom, engaging in meditation in it, or cultivating it, those pernicious māras who harm bodhisattva great beings stir up fear by launching attacks of fiery meteors in all directions or by creating the appearance that the horizon itself is on fire. These māras do so because they think that if they can cause any bodhisattva great being to become deflated or to bristle with fear, it will at least destroy the arising of one mindset aimed at unsurpassed, perfect awakening. To this end, Ānanda, the pernicious Māra does not attack every bodhisattva great being with the deliberate intention to harm him or her. Rather, he attacks just some bodhisattva great beings, not others.”
Ānanda asked, “Blessed One, what sort of bodhisattva great being does the pernicious Māra attack with the deliberate intention to harm him or her?”

The Blessed One said, “Ānanda, if any bodhisattva great being, when he or she was previously discussing the transcendent state of wisdom, failed to developed a mindset with firm conviction in it, then the pernicious Māra would attack that bodhisattva great being with the deliberate intention to harm him or her, and find any vulnerability.

“Furthermore, Ānanda, if any bodhisattva great being has become uncertain when the profound transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, and is vacillating about whether this is or is not the transcendent state of wisdom, then the pernicious Māra attacks that bodhisattva great being with the deliberate intention to harm him or her, and finds any vulnerability.

“Furthermore, Ānanda, suppose that a bodhisattva great being lacks a virtuous friend or has been taken in by an unvirtuous friend, and does not listen to the extremely profound points when the profound transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed. By not listening, he or she does not understand. By not understanding, he or she does not ask the question about how one should cultivate the transcendent state of wisdom. Then, Ānanda, the pernicious Māra also attacks that bodhisattva great being, with the deliberate intention to harm him or her, and finds any vulnerability.

“Furthermore, Ānanda, suppose that a bodhisattva great being has relied on someone who cleaves to views that are against the genuine Dharma, thinking, ‘He is my friend who never steers me wrong in any worldly affairs. I know many bodhisattva great beings and other friends. But none of them help me fulfill my wishes. Whereas, this friend that I have found is an example worth imitating.Cf. MW pratirūpa, model for imitation, exemplary in conduct, worthy of imitation. He will help me fulfill my wishes.’There is the diminutive or pejorative –ka in the term sahāyaka (i.e., sahāya+ ka) that is difficult to translate. One might freely translate this diminutive or pejorative aspect sarcastically as “my personal friend” since he or she might not call their own friend “my little friend” or “my despicable friend.” Cf. BHSD sahāyaka, which cites a parallel passage from the Eight Thousand (that differs slightly) and translates it “he is my despicable companion, and I have many others too, but they do not fulfil my purpose; however, here I have got a suitable companion.” See also Conze 1958, p. 168: “I am his adherent, and in all things he does not abandon me. [418] There are many other bodhisattvas whom I might adhere to, but they do not suit me. I have taken this one as my fitting companion and he will suit me.” Ānanda, the pernicious Māra also attacks that bodhisattva great being, with the deliberate intention to harm him or her, and finds any vulnerability.

“Furthermore, Ānanda, suppose that, when the profound transcendent state of wisdom is being discussed, a bodhisattva great being were to say to another bodhisattva, ‘Profound? This transcendent state of wisdom? What is the use of your listening to this? There is no applying oneself to it in the way that the Tathāgata taught in other sūtras, and I myself do not find any bottom to it or enjoyment in it.According to Ratnākara (F.169.a), the meaning here is “In it, there is no way to apply oneself as the Tathāgata taught in other sūtras. (I myself do not find any) ‘bottom to it,’ i.e., firm basis in it, or ‘enjoyment in it,’ ” i.e., appeal in it. RK: yathānyasūtreṣu tathāgatabhāṣitaṃ yujyamānaṃ evam atra nāstīty arthaḥ| gādhaḥ pratiṣṭhā| āsvādo ruciḥ. According to Hari (814), “there is no applying oneself to it” means that there is nothing to strive for that is explained in this scripture in the way that is explained in other scriptures. So, since what is described is impossible to realize by means of the knowledge from study and contemplation, I cannot obtain, respectively, any “bottom to it” or “enjoyment” from that. Hari: na hy evam atra yujyamānam iti yathānyeṣu sūtrānteṣu bhāṣitaṃ tathā naivātra ghaṭamānavastu nirdiṣṭam, ato ’sya bhāṣitasya śrutacintāmayajñānena boddhum aśakyatvād yathākramam āgādham āsvādaṃ ca na prāpnuyām ity arthaḥ. So what is the use of your listening to that or writing that out?’ In this way, this bodhisattva great being causes other bodhisattva great beings to avoid it. Ānanda, the pernicious Māra also, with the deliberate intention to harm, attacks that bodhisattva great being, and finds any vulnerability.

“Furthermore, Ānanda, suppose that a bodhisattva great being criticizes other bodhisattvas, saying that he or she is abiding in a void state, whereas others are not abiding in a void state, and their voidness cannot be found anywhere. Ānanda, at the time when this happens, the pernicious Māra is pleased, cheerful, gladdened, delighted, and full of joy and satisfaction. His excited mindset is thrilled, overjoyed, and totally delighted. Why is that? Becausee he thinks that such a bodhisattva great being is getting further away from awakening.

“Furthermore, Ānanda, suppose that Māra mentions a bodhisattva great being’s former names and lineage, and praises his or her practice of ascetic qualities, and, as a result, he or she criticizes other graceful bodhisattvas with spiritual qualities.According to Ratnākara (F.169.a), this is just like before (i.e., it is a reference to the first part of chapter 21, “Describing Māra’s Activities,” 21.2; where) Māra announces a bodhisattva great being’s names and lineage, and praises his or her practice of ascetic qualities. RK: nāmagotragrahaṇaṃ dhutaguṇakīrtanaṃ ca pūrvavat. But he or she lacks those qualities, attributes, indications, and signs‍—in fact, he or she lacks any qualities of a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing who is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Since he or she lacks these qualities beyond regressing, he or she gives rise to emotional defilements. In particular, he or she brags about him- or herself and disparages others, saying, ‘I am someone in whom such qualities appear, but those bodhisattvas are not.’The qualities here are those of a bodhisattva beyond regressing. Note: The translation of this sentiment is free. The Sanskrit idiom might be expressed more literally as “they are not similar to those qualities that I am similar to.” The Skt reads na khalv ete teṣu dharmeṣu saṃdṛśyante, yatrāhaṃ saṃdṛśya iti. When this happens, the pernicious Māra thinks, ‘The realms superintended by Māra will not be empty. They will be overcrowded. The great hell, hungry ghost, animal, and asura realms will all be overcrowded hordes.’According to Ratnākara (F.169.a), “overcrowded” means abundantly furnished with “hordes,” i.e., heaps, (of beings). RK: utsadā upacitāḥ kāyā rāśayaḥ. And, the pernicious Māra will magically influence others so that the bodhisattva great being who acts this way will be smothered with donations and tributes, and whatever they say will be accepted. Since what they say will be accepted, they will gather a following of many people. And a great multitude will think that they should listen to them and have faith in them. After seeing and hearing them speak, those people will fall into imitating him or her. Those who are imitating what is seen and heard will not train to attain the actual nature, not progress to attain the actual nature, and not engage in meditation to attain the actual nature. By not training to attain the actual nature, not progressing to attain the actual nature, and not engaging in meditation to attain the actual nature, their defilement will increase. With their mindstreams deluded, every physical, verbal, or mental action that they undertake will lead them further toward unadvised, undesired, unpleasing, and joyless states.According to Hari (814), some scholars say that he uses these four terms “unadvised, undesired, unpleasing, and joyless” because they (respectively) lead to (rebirth in) the realms of hell, animals, hungry ghosts, and asuras. Hari: aniṣṭatvāye tyādipadacatuṣṭayaṃ narakatiryak­pretāsura­gati­saṃvartanīyatvād iti kecit. Thus, the great hells will become extremely overcrowded. The great hell, hungry ghost, animal, and asura realms will all be overcrowded hordes. The realms superintended by Māra will be extremely overcrowded. Moreover, Ānanda, upon seeing his purpose fulfilled, the pernicious Māra becomes pleased, cheerful, gladdened, delighted, and full of joy and satisfaction.

“Furthermore, Ānanda, whenever such a bodhisattva great being fights with a person on the disciple path, quarrels with them, argues with them, scolds them, criticizes them, hurts them, or develops a grudge against them, the pernicious Māra thinks, ‘Oh good, this noble son or daughter is distancing him- or herself from the state of complete knowing. He or she will be kept far away from the state of complete knowing.’ But if such a bodhisattva great being fights with another person on the bodhisattva path, quarrels with them, argues with them, scolds them, criticizes them, hurts them, or develops a grudge against them, then the pernicious Māra becomes even more pleased, cheerful, gladdened, delighted, and full of joy and satisfaction about this. And he thinks, ‘Both these bodhisattvas will be kept extremely far away from the state of complete knowing.’

“Furthermore, Ānanda, suppose that a foreordained bodhisattva great being were to fight with an unforeordained bodhisattva great being, quarrel with them, argue with them, scold them, criticize them, hurt them, develop a grudge against them, or develop a hostile mindset toward them. Assuming that such a bodhisattva great being had not completely abandoned the state of complete knowing, he or she would need to don the armor for as many eons as there are moments in the arising of that mindset.”According to Hari (814–15), regarding his “donning the armor,” the idea is that assuming he or she did not completely abandon the state of complete knowing, then they will need to practice heroic perseverance for a duration of eons delimited by the time length in that “mindset” in order to eliminate the harm of such flare ups, such as fighting and so on. Hari: saṃnāhaḥ saṃnahya iti yadi sarva­jñatāparityaktā tadā kalahādi­samuttha­pāpāpanayanārthaṃ cittotpāde saṃkhyāvacchinna­kalpa­pramāṇaṃ vīryaṃ karaṇīyam ity arthaḥ.

At this, the venerable Ānanda asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, is there some remedy for the arising of such mindsets, or would he or she need to don the armor for as many eons as there are moments in the arising of that mindset?”According to Hari (815), the meaning of Ānanda’s question is whether a “remedy” is possible given the gravity of the harmful deed (i.e., the offense). Hari: gurutaratvāt pāpasya niḥsaraṇam asambhāvayan praśnārtham āha : asti bhagavann ityādi.

The Blessed One said, “Ānanda, the Dharma that I have taught for persons on the disciple path, the path of a solitary realizer, and the bodhisattva path includes a remedy.According to Hari (815), he says this in order to communicate the following general principle: “There is a (remedial) quality that works to counteract absolutely everything that forms a part of any possible behavior.” Hari: sambhavapratipattipakṣatvena sarveṣāṃ eva sampratikarmako dharma iti vyāptam āvedayann āha : saniḥsaraṇa ityādi. Among these, Ānanda, for a person on the bodhisattva path who fights with another person on the bodhisattva path, quarrels with them, argues with them, scolds them, criticizes them, hurts them, or develops a grudge against them, I have not included a remedy, if afterward such a bodhisattva great being does not confess or practice restraint, while he or she just carries on with the negative propensity and remains bound up in it. Ānanda, it is inevitable that such a person would need to don the armor again and again for eons equal to that duration. However, Ānanda, I have included a remedy for any person on the bodhisattva path who, after fighting with another person on the bodhisattva path, quarreling with them, arguing with them, scolding them, criticizing them, hurting them, or developing a grudge against them, does confess and subsequently practices restraint by developing the proper mindset.According to Hari (815), he or she “confesses”‍—i.e., confesses the harm he or she has done‍—“and subsequently practices restraint” by taking a vow not to do it (again). Hari: kṛtapāpadeśanān na deśayati. akaraṇasaṃvarākaraṇān nāyatyāṃ saṃvarāya pratipadyate. In this, he or she would think, ‘I should be helping all sentient beings defuse, reflect upon, and pacify their arguments, disputes, and conflicts. Yet I myself am just quarreling! When I myself lash back against what is said to me, it is a loss for me, not a gain.According to Hari (815), he or she should develop that mindset that “it is a loss for me”‍—meaning “when I myself lash back against (pratijalpāmi) what is said to me,” these fights and so on are the “loss for me,” because they are not praiseworthy. Hari: durlabdhā iti yo ’ham jalpite sati parasmin pratijalpāmy amī kalahādayo ’praśastatvena durlabdhā ity evaṃ cittam utpādayatīty arthaḥ. I should be like a bridge for all sentient beings, but I still say to others, “The same to you!” and give a harsh or rough reply.Conze 1958, p. 170: “When I should be to all beings a bridge across the sea of birth-and-death, I nevertheless say to another, (‘the same to you’), or return a harsh and rough answer.” I should never say even this. Toward fights, quarrels, and arguments, I should be like a dimwit or like a person who cannot hear or speak.This translation “cannot hear or speak” (Sanskrit eḍamūka; Pāli eḷamuga) follows Cone 2010, Part I, p. 549 eḷamuga, which settles the dispute over whether the term means MW “deaf and dumb” or simply BHSD “dumb like a sheep.” Cone shows these two interpretations to be simply two of the three usages of the term. When hearing someone else speaking harsh, offensive, or bad words to me, I should not develop a hostile mindset. Concerning others, it is not good or proper of me to conceive of someone else’s various faults. I think it is also not proper of me to even consider others’ faults worth listening to. Why not? Because I should not spoil my altruistic disposition, because of which I should be helping all sentient beings become happy with every source of happiness. And, after I have realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, I should bring them to complete nirvāṇa. However, I am actually hurting them! I should not be hurtful even toward others who have greatly offended me, yet I become agitated. This I should not do. I should make a heroic effort to be firm in this resolution. Even when my life is being threatened, I should neither create any disturbance nor allow furrowed brows to appear on my face.’ Ānanda, this is the remedy that I teach for such a bodhisattva great being. Also, Ānanda, a bodhisattva great being would be restrained this way toward a person on the disciple path, such that he or she would not get stirred up in the presence of any sentient being but would be restrained just this way toward all sentient beings. And how would the bodhisattva great being be restrained toward other persons belonging to the bodhisattva path? Ānanda, the bodhisattva great being would be restrained toward others in the same way he or she would be toward a teacher, thinking, ‘These bodhisattva great beings are my teachers. Remember, these bodhisattva great beings have ascended in the same vehicle that I have.The exclamation bata has been translated as “remember” in this context, since he or she is talking to themselves. Remember, they have climbed up the same path that I have. Remember, they have had the same intentions that I have. Remember, they have set out on the same journey that I have. I should train in what they train in, and I should train in the exact same manner that they train.Conze: “Wherein they should be trained, that is the method by which I should be trained.” But, if any of them will abide in a completely mixed-up state, then I will not abide in a completely mixed-up state.The commentators do not explain the term “mixed-up (vyavakīrṇa) state” here, but it is a reference to chapter 22 on voidness where a bodhisattva great being must not get “mixed up with” (viz. polluted or confused by) the dualistic, object-oriented attentiveness associated with the paths of a disciple or solitary realizer. See notes n.1094 and n.1100. Yet, if they will abide in a completely unmixed-up state with their attentivenesses connected to the state of complete knowing, then I will train in the same way.’ Ānanda, the bodhisattva great beings who are training in the state of complete knowing in this way do not have obstacles to unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Conceitedness,” the twenty-fourth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 25: The TrainingThis chapter corresponds to the whole of Chapter 47 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 57 in the Eighteen Thousand, though with certain differences in how the points it contains are discussed.According to Ratnākara (F.170.a), the “Chapter on the Training” is the chapter that articulates the training. RK: śikṣāyā vācakaḥ parivartaḥ śikṣāparivartaḥ.
div.p: Then the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, when a bodhisattva great being trains in the state of complete knowing, what is he or she training in?”

The Blessed One said, “When, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being is training in dissipation, then he or she trains in the state of complete knowing, and, similarly, in the freedom from arising, in the freedom from cessation, in the freedom from birth, in the freedom from any state of being, in voidness, in the freedom from passion, in space, and in the ultimate realm of things. If, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being is training in nirvāṇa, then he or she trains in the state of complete knowing.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, why does a bodhisattva great being training in dissipation train in the state of complete knowing? Similarly, why does a bodhisattva great being, training in the freedom from arising, in the freedom from cessation, in the freedom from birth, in the freedom from any state of being, in voidness, in the freedom from passion, in space, in the ultimate realm of things, and in nirvāṇa, train in the state of complete knowing?”

At this, the Blessed One said to Subhūti, “Subhūti, you asked the following: ‘Why does a bodhisattva great being training in dissipation train in the state of complete knowing? Similarly, why does a bodhisattva great being, training in the freedom from arising, in the freedom from cessation, in the freedom from birth, in the freedom from any state of being, in voidness, in the freedom from passion, in space, in the ultimate realm of things, and in nirvāṇa, train in the state of complete knowing?’ Subhūti, since the actual nature of a tathāgata is what makes the Tathāgata a tathāgata, what do you think? Does it dissipate?”

“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti. “Why not? Because, Blessed One, dissipation dissipates. Blessed One, it is because dissipation is undissipating.”According to Ratnākara (F.169.a), (in other words) the actual nature of a tathāgata, due to which there is a tathāgata, does not dissipate, because dissipation is undissipating. RK: yā hi tathāgatasya tathatā yayā sau tathāgataḥ na sā kṣīyate ’kṣayatvāt kṣayasya.

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Does the actual nature of a tathāgata‍—that which makes the Tathāgata a tathāgata‍—arise or cease? Is it born? Does it become anything? Does it disappear? Does it become void? Is it passionate or dispassionate? Does it become space? Does it become a thing?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.
The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, since the actual nature of a tathāgata is what makes the Tathāgata a tathāgata, what do you think? Does it attain complete nirvāṇa?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.

“Therefore, Subhūti,” said the Blessed One, “a bodhisattva great being training in this way trains with the conviction that the actual nature does not dissipate. A bodhisattva great being training in this way trains in the state of complete knowing. A bodhisattva great being training in this way trains in the transcendent state of wisdom and trains in the level of a buddha, the powers, the grounds for self-confidence, all the qualities of a buddha, and the knowing of someone who has complete knowing.According to Ratnākara (F.169.a–b), he explains the path with the phrase “a bodhisattva great being training in this way trains in the transcendent state of wisdom.” He explains the fruit with the phrase “train in the level of a buddha” (and so on). RK: evaṃ śikṣamāṇaḥ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ śikṣata ity anena mārgam āha|| buddhabhūmau śikṣata ity anena phalam āha. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way will reach the perfection of all trainings. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way cannot be overcome by Māra or his demon cohort. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way will quickly come to have the qualities beyond regressing. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way will quickly come to be seated at the epicenter of awakening. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way will quickly be going about life in his or her own field.Neither commentator explains precisely what this “field” (gocara) refers to. As in many other texts, there are two ways in which it can be understood: either as the field of knowing (i.e., what a Buddha sees), or as the field of activity (i.e., where a Buddha does what he does). Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way trains in the qualities that make him or her a protector: great benevolence, great compassion, great rejoicing, and great impartiality. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way trains to set in motion the wheel of Dharma with its three sections containing twelve aspects.Note that, as before in chapter 7, this phrase is not referring to the “three turnings” of the Dharma Wheel. For details see n.493 and The Sūtra of the Wheel of Dharma, 1.13. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way trains with the conviction that he or she will not leave the masses of sentient beings wanting.We have followed the Tib. and emended sarvadhātu (Vaidya, Wogihara) to sattvadhātu. Cf. BHSD dhātu 6, sattvadhātu, masses of beings. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way trains so that there is no interruption in the Tathāgata’s lineage. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way trains with the conviction that he or she will open the door to the deathless dimension.According to Ratnākara (F.169.b), “deathless dimension” means the dimension of liberation. RK: amṛtadhātur mokṣadhātuḥ.

“Subhūti, an inferior sentient being is not able to engage in such a noble training, because a weak person cannot train in it. Why not? Subhūti, those who train in this training are the strongest among all sentient beings, Subhūti, because they wish to become the protectors of all sentient beings.According to Ratnākara (F.169.b), “wish to become the protectors” means wish to achieve the state of a protector. RK: nāthakāmā iti nāthatvakāmāḥ. Subhūti, those who train in it are wishing to obtain the highest state among all sentient beings.According to Ratnākara (F.169.b), “the highest state” means the most excellent state. RK: abhyudgatate ty utkṛṣṭatā| Thus, Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being who trains like this is not reborn in the hells, animal births, the hungry ghost domain, or the asura assemblies. He or she is not reborn in the borderlands, the families of outcastes, or the families of fowlers, hunters, fishermen, sheep butchers, or similar kinds of low-class families such as those involved in ignoble karmic deeds.According to Ratnākara (F.169.b), “fowlers” kill birds; “hunters” kill wild animals; “fishermen” catch fish; and “sheep butchers” kill sheep. RK: śakunīn ghnanti śākunikāḥ| niṣādā mṛgayavaḥ| dhīvarāḥ kaivartāḥ| urabhrā meṣāḥ| tān ghnantīti aurabhrikāḥ. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way does not become blind, deaf, one-eyed, deformed, or hunchbacked.According to Ratnākara (F.169.b), the “blind” lack proper organs to see; the “deaf” lack proper organs to hear; the “one-eyed” have a single eye; the “deformed” have crooked limbs; and the “hunchbacked” have broken (or collapsed) backs. RK: andho ’cakṣuḥ| badhiro ’śrotraḥ| kāṇa ekākṣaḥ| kuṇṭho vakrabāhuḥ| kubjo bhagnapṛṣṭhaḥ. He or she does not come to have crippled hands, knock knees, paralysis, or a dim wit.According to Ratnākara (F.169.b), “crippled hands” means deformed hands; “knock knees” means knees that hit each other; “paralysis” means impaired movement; and “dim wit” means lacking in mental clarity. RK: kuṇiḥ karavikalaḥ| laḍgaḥ saṃhatajānuḥ| khañjo vikalagatiḥ| jaḍo niṣpratibhaḥ. He or she does not become unable to walk steadily, speak clearly, or hear well. He or she does not have disabled limbs, missing limbs, or disfigured limbs.According to Ratnākara (F.169.b), “unable to walk steadily” means having unstable feet; “unable to speak clearly” means having a dysfunctional tongue; “unable to hear well” means hearing (only) loud words; “disabled limbs” means faulty limbs; “missing limbs” means lost limbs; and “disfigured limbs” means limbs that do not look human. RK: lolo ’sthirapadaḥ| lallo ’karmaṇyajihvaḥ| kalla uccaiḥ śabdaśrāvī| hīnāṅgo ninditāṅgaḥ| vikalāṅga ūnāṅgaḥ| vikṛtāṅgo manuṣyavisadṛśāṅgaḥ. He or she does not become weak, ugly, or physically deformed. He or she does not come to have inferior mental faculties or to lack any faculties‍—that is to say, he or she comes to have completely perfect faculties and a resonant voice. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way does not become a killer, a taker of what is not given, one who engages in improper sexual conduct, a liar, a slanderer, a speaker of harsh words, an incoherent gossip, someone who is covetous, someone with a malevolent mindset, or a holder of erroneous beliefs. He or she does not earn his or her living though any improper means of livelihood. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way is not reborn among the gods with long lives, does not adhere to faulty ethical disciplines, does not adhere to nonexistent principles, and is not reborn by force of his or her meditative attainments. Why not? Because a bodhisattva great being is endowed with the skill in means that he or she has, and hence he or she is not reborn among the gods with long lives. If you ask, Subhūti, what does such a bodhisattva great being’s skill in means consist in, to be precise, it is just this transcendent state of wisdom. That is to say, the manner of having the skill in means by which a bodhisattva great being who attains the meditative states is not reborn by force of those meditative states is in the transcendent state of wisdom itself, and it is thus that he or she engages in meditative concentration with skill in means.The idea here is that a bodhisattva attains the meditative states that normally lead to rebirth in realms of gods with long lives, rather than to liberation. But since he or she practices the meditative concentration in “this skill in means,” i.e., the transcendent state of wisdom, such a bodhisattva is not involuntarily reborn in those god realms by virtue of having practiced those meditative states. Rather, a bodhisattva can deliberately choose his or her place of rebirth. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way brings about the complete purity of the powers, the complete purity of the grounds for self-confidence, and the complete purity of all the qualities of a buddha, and subsequently attains them all.”According to Ratnākara (F.169.b), he or she “brings about,” i.e., causes them to ripen, and “subsequently attains them all,” i.e., attains the complete purity of the powers and so on. RK: nigacchatī ti niṣpādayati| tām anuprāpnotī ti bal ādipariśuddhim.

The venerable Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, since all things are completely pure by their very nature, Blessed One, what thing could possess the complete purity of the powers, the grounds for self-confidence, and all the qualities of a buddha, which this bodhisattva great being approaches and subsequently attains?”According to Hari (822), Subhūti asks this question because accomplishing the purity of the powers and so on is impossible due to the natural purity (of all things). Hari: asti hi tasye tyādi. prakṛtipari­śuddhitvena balādiviśuddhyādhigamo nopapadyata ity āha : yadā Bhagavann ityādi.

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “True, Subhūti. True. Why is that? It is because all things are completely pure by their very nature.According to Hari (822), the Blessed One answers “True” and so on (to Subhūti’s rhetorical question), because there is accomplishment at the conventional level. Yet what he is saying is that although all things are pure by nature, sentient beings do not know they are naturally pure. So, they must meditate upon things being like an illusion in order to give rise to the knowing of how things truly are. By doing so, they attain, at the conventional level, the complete purity of the powers and so on. Hari: samvṛtyadhigamād āha : evam etad ityādi. pūrvavat tat kasya hetor ity āśaṅkyāha: sarvadharmā hī tyādi. etad uktaṃ : prakṛtipariśuddhatve ’pi sarvadharmāṇāṃ svabhāva­śuddha­dharmāpari­jñānavatāṃ sattvānāṃ tathābhūta­jñānotpādanārtha­māyopama­dharma­bhāvanayāsaṃsīdanatvena samvṛtyā balādipariśuddhiṃ prāpnotīti. Thus, Subhūti, for a bodhisattva great being training in the transcendent state of wisdom, the transcendent state of wisdom is the state of not being disheartened or deflated about all things being completely pure by nature. Subhūti, immature, ordinary persons, who do not know these things, do not know the true nature of things and do not see it. Subhūti, it is for the sake of those sentient beings that bodhisattva great beings are exerting themselves, undertaking heroic perseverance, and train in this training with the conviction, ‘We are the ones who will bring those sentient beings, who do not know such things, to know them. We are the ones who will bring those sentient beings, who do not see this way, to come to see them.’ Those bodhisattva great beings who train with such a conviction attain the powers, the grounds for self-confidence, and all the qualities of a buddha. Subhūti, the bodhisattva great beings who train in this training wisely discern, just as they are, the mental wanderings and impulses of other persons and beings. By wisely discerning these just as they are, they perfect their knowledge of the mental wanderings of others.

“Subhūti, it is just as there are only a few areas on this earth without rock, where gold and silver appear.“Without rock” suggests a place where these metals are found in streams or on the ground. Note: The translation “gold” follows Ratnākara (F.169.b), who explains that the combination of Sanskrit words‍—i.e., suvarṇaṃ and jātarūpaṃ and so on‍—forms a pleonasm (i.e., more words than necessary to describe the one thing “gold”). RK: suvarṇajātarūpā dayaḥ gobalīvardanyāyena. However, there are many areas on this earth where there are saline deserts, wastelands, different grasses, craggy fissures, and thorny bushes. Likewise, Subhūti, there are only a few bodhisattva great beings, among the multitude of sentient beings, who train in this training of the state of complete knowing, namely, in the training of the transcendent state of wisdom. But there are many sentient being among the multitude who train in the training of a disciple or solitary realizer.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, just as there are only a few sentient beings, among the multitude of sentient beings, who turn toward performing the karmic deeds conducive toward ruling as a universal emperor, there are, however, many sentient beings among the multitude who turn toward performing the karmic deeds conducive toward becoming a regional king. Likewise, Subhūti, there are only a few bodhisattva great beings, among the multitude of sentient beings, who have climbed up the path‍—specifically, the path of the transcendent state of wisdom‍—with the conviction that they will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. But there are many sentient beings among the multitude who have climbed up the path of a disciple or solitary realizer.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, just as there are only a few sentient beings observed among the multitude of sentient beings who turn toward performing the karmic deeds conducive toward becoming Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, there are, however, many sentient beings among the multitude who turn toward performing the karmic deeds conducive toward the world of the gods. Likewise, Subhūti, there are only a few bodhisattva great beings, among the multitude of sentient beings, who train in this training of the transcendent state of wisdom. However, there are many sentient beings, among the multitude of sentient beings, who train in the training of a disciple or solitary realizer.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, just as there are only a few sentient beings among the multitude who turn toward performing the karmic deeds conducive toward becoming Brahmā, there are, however, many sentient beings among the multitude who turn toward performing the karmic deeds conducive toward belonging in Brahmā’s retinue. Likewise, Subhūti, there are only a few bodhisattva great beings among the multitude who are beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. However, there are many sentient beings among the multitude who turn away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Therefore, Subhūti, there are only a few bodhisattva great beings, among the multitude of sentient beings, who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. And, Subhūti, among those, there are even fewer who progress along the path toward the actual nature. And, Subhūti, among those progressing along the path toward the actual nature, there are even fewer who engage in meditation in the transcendent state of wisdom. And, Subhūti, among those who engage in meditation in the transcendent state of wisdom, there are even fewer who are beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. So, Subhūti, those bodhisattva great beings who wish to be counted among the fewest among the few bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing should train in this very transcendent state of wisdom and engage in meditation in it.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being training in the transcendent state of wisdom in this way does not develop a mindset associated with mental obstructions, a mindset associated with doubt, a mindset associated with envy or jealousy, a mindset associated with unethical conduct, a mindset associated with malevolence, a mindset associated with laziness, a mindset associated with distraction, or a mindset associated with being unwise.According to Ratnākara (F.170.a), “mental obstructions” refers to the attachment, hatred, and confusion that block the growth of virtuous fruits; “doubt” refers to being of two minds about the Three Jewels; and “envy” means being angry about another’s success. The rest of the terms here are forces counterproductive to the six transcendent states. RK: kuśala­sasyapraroha­virodhino rāgadveṣamohāḥ khilāḥ| vicikitsā satyaratneṣu vimatiḥ| īrṣyā parasampattau vyāroṣaḥ| śeṣāḥ ṣaṭ pāramitāvipakṣāḥ. For in this very way, Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being trains in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she incorporates all the transcendent states, takes in all the transcendent states, pursues all the transcendent states, and includes all the transcendent states.

“Subhūti, just as the erroneous beliefs about the transitory collection include sixty-two erroneous beliefs,The sixty-two erroneous beliefs are listed in the Dīgha Nikāya’s Brahmajāla-sutta, its Dīrghāgama equivalent in Chinese, the Tibetan tshangs pa’i dra ba’i mdo, (Toh 352), and other parallel texts. Bhikkhu Anālayo’s comparative study can be found in Anālayo 2010. likewise, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in the transcendent state of wisdom includes all the transcendent states.

“Subhūti, just as the sustenance of a person’s vital faculty includes all his or her faculties,The sustenance of the “vital faculty” is the thing that keeps a being alive. likewise, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in the transcendent state of wisdom includes all the virtuous indications.

“Subhūti, just as when a person’s vital faculty has ceased, all the faculties cease, likewise, Subhūti, when the ignorance of a bodhisattva great being training in the transcendent state of wisdom has ceased, then all his or her unvirtuous indications cease, and all the transcendent states opposite of those are embraced.According to Ratnākara (F.170.a), “ignorance” is the opposite of the transcendent state of wisdom. RK: ajñāna iti prajñāpāramitāvipakṣe. Therefore, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being who wishes to embrace all the transcendent states should train in the transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in the transcendent state of wisdom trains in being the foremost among sentient beings. Why is that? Because he or she becomes foremost in merit.According to Ratnākara (F.170.a), “foremost in merit” means foremost due to his or her merits. RK: puṇyāgraḥ puṇyair agraḥ. So, Subhūti, what do you think? If one takes together all the sentient beings in a billionfold, great order world system, are they numerous?”
Subhūti said, “Blessed One, the sentient beings on the Jambu continent alone are numerous, so what more can be said about those sentient beings in a billionfold, great order world system?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, suppose that a bodhisattva great being were to continue throughout his or her whole life to take care of all sentient beings by providing them with robes, alms, accommodation, preventive medicines and remedies, other requisites, and all the means for happiness. Then, what do you think, Subhūti? Would he or she earn much merit based on this?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” said Subhūti.

“Subhūti, based on this, any bodhisattva great being who cultivates this transcendent state of wisdom for even just a mere finger snap would earn more merit,” said the Blessed One. “Why is that? Subhūti, it is because in this way the transcendent state of wisdom is extremely effective for the bodhisattva great being to bring forth unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Therefore, a bodhisattva great being should train in the transcendent state of wisdom if he or she wishes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening; wishes to become unsurpassed among all sentient beings; wishes to be a protector for all sentient beings without a protector; wishes to reach the domain of a buddha; wishes to pursue the great majesty of a buddha; wishes to manifest the playful display of a buddha; wishes to roar the lion’s roar of a buddha; wishes to obtain the attainments of a buddha; and wishes to give Dharma teachings to the billionfold, great order world system.According to Ratnākara (F.170.a), “domain of a buddha” means the totality of a buddha’s qualities; a buddha’s “great majesty” means the state of having authoritative power; a buddha’s “playful display” is through his performing miraculous feats; a buddha’s roaring the “lion’s roar” is through his miraculous guidance of others (that instantly destroys their vices); and the “attainments” of buddhas are to be practiced by both types of noble saṅghas. RK: buddhaviṣayo buddha­dharmasākalyam| vaśavartī vṛṣabhiḥ| tadbhāvastattā| vikrīḍitaṃ ṛddhiprātihāryeṇa| siṃhanāda nadanam anuśāsanī­prātihāryeṇa| buddhānāṃ sampattir ubhayāryasaṃghaiś caryam. Subhūti, I do not see any attainment that a bodhisattva great being training in the transcendent state of wisdom is not training in.”
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, does a bodhisattva great being train even in the attainment of a disciple?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being trains even in the attainment of a disciple. However, that bodhisattva great being trains with the notion that he or she will not come to abide in the attainment of the disciple, or at least, he or she does not train thinking that he or she will come to have the attainment. Such a bodhisattva is aware of the qualities of a disciple but does not get stuck in them. For this reason, he or she fully understands those qualities, does not reject them, and trains with the notion that he or she should teach and elucidate them to others.According to Ratnākara (F.170.a), he or she “does not reject them” through not being committed to those qualities. RK: na ca prativahati teṣv anavasthānāt. Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being training in this way becomes worthy of veneration by the world with its gods, humans, and asuras. Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being training in this way surpasses all other venerable ones among the disciples or solitary realizers, and he or she approaches the state of complete knowing. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being training in this way does not give up the transcendent state of wisdom, goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, and is never lacking the abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being going about life in this way should be known as someone who does not waste the teachings. Such a bodhisattva great being distances him- or herself from the levels of a disciple and solitary realizer, but approaches unsurpassed, perfect awakening. However, a bodhisattva great being might have the conception, ‘This transcendent state of wisdom will develop the state of complete knowing.’ In that case, he or she does not go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. On the other hand, if he or she does not even conceive of that transcendent state of wisdom by thinking, ‘This is the transcendent state of wisdom,’ ‘This has the transcendent state of wisdom,’ or ‘This will develop the state of complete knowing,’ then this bodhisattva great being does not conceive of it or identify it even as such. So, if the bodhisattva great being goes about life in this way, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Training,” the twenty-fifth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 26: The Magical IllusionAccording to Ratnākara (F.175.a), “Chapter on the Magical Illusion” is a synecdochic chapter title for the magical illusion-like mind. RK: māyopama cittopalakṣitaḥ parivarto māyopama parivartaḥ.The beginning of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 48 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 58 in the Eighteen Thousand, and its content corresponds to the whole of those chapters.
div.p: Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, thought to himself, “If a bodhisattva great being surpasses all sentient beings even just by going about his or her life, then how much more so after he or she realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.170.b), regarding the phrase “even just by going about his or her life,” one may ask in what does he or she go about life? He or she goes about life in increasing their practice of generosity and so forth. RK: carann apīti kva caran ? vivṛddhau| kiṃ caran? dānādīn. If those sentient beings whose mindset takes a step toward the state of complete knowing profit greatly and live their lives well, then how much more so once they develop the mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.170.b), their “mindset takes a step toward” means it grows more clear. They “develop the mindset” when they think, “Ah! I should definitely realize this transcendent state of wisdom!” RK: cittaṃ krāmatī ti gacchatīti prasīdatīty arthaḥ| cittam utpāditam ity aho batāham enām adhigaccheyam iti. Such sentient beings, who are the strongest among sentient beings, are enviable, since they will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, magically created heavenly māndārava flowers, cupped the flowers together with his hands folded in reverence, then showered them down upon the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata, and spoke these words: “Those persons on the bodhisattva path who have developed the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening think, ‘We will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. After doing so, all sentient beings adrift in the ocean of cyclic existence we will deliver to the smooth shore at the other side.’According to Ratnākara (F.170.b), “adrift” means floating (in that ocean); to the “smooth”‍—i.e., faultless‍—“shore at the other side” means to nirvāṇa. RK: uhyamānān iti hriyamānān| same yukte nirdoṣe| pārime tīra iti nirvāṇe. According to Hari (830), “adrift” means pushed along (in that ocean). It is “smooth” because it has no faults or unevenness. The “shore at the other side” means nirvāṇa, i.e., buddhahood. Hari: uhyamānān iti preryamāṇān. sarvadoṣavaiṣamyābhāvāt same. pārime tīre nirvāṇe buddhatva iti yāvat. May they be successful in this!According to Ratnākara (F.170.b), the sentence “may they be successful in this” should be connected with the other development of the proper mindset (just stated). RK: samṛdhyantāṃ teṣām iti cittotpādā iti pareṇa sambandhaḥ. May they come to fulfill these very qualities of a buddha that their development of the proper mindset desired, fully considered, and fully embraced!According to Ratnākara (F.170.b), “desired” means held dear; “fully considered” means developed again and again; “embraced” means by means of not letting it go. RK: abhīpsitāḥ prītikaratvāt| paricintitāḥ punaḥ punar utpādanāt| parigṛhītā aparityāgayogena. According to Hari (830), regarding the three verbs “desired,” “fully considered,” and “embraced,” some scholars say they refer, respectively, to obtaining success in benefiting oneself, others, and both together. Other scholars say these words refer, respectively, to actualizing the three types of complete knowing. Hari: svaparobhayārtha­sampatsampādakatvena yathākramam abhīpsitāḥ paricintitāḥ parigṛhītā iti kecit. tri­sarva­jñatāniṣpādakatvenety anye. May they fulfill these very same qualities of the state of complete knowing. May they fulfill these very same qualities of a self-existent being. May they fulfill these very qualities that cannot be led astray.According to Ratnākara (F.170.b), the “qualities of a buddha” means the qualities of a buddha in the sense of a completely knowing being. The first wish is the general statement. But the detailed explanation of that statement is in the three wishes based on his complete knowing, i.e., his being completely aware. That is to say, he has complete knowing because of his “state of complete knowing,” his “self-existent being,” and “not being led astray.” Among these, the “state of complete knowing,” in this context, means his perfect knowing of all things in all their aspects. His “self-existent being” means for him those things are absolutely self-arising, i.e., to his mind alone, and hence it means he has perfect control (over them). For him, these things are the ones included in his being “self-existent.” He “cannot be led astray” because he does not get carried away‍—meaning that he is free of all obscurations and their latent impressions. “May they fulfill these” means all his qualities included in his not being led astray‍—all this describes their fulfillment. RK: buddhadharmāṇām iti buddhaḥ sarvajñaḥ| tasya dharmāṇām ity ayam uddeśaḥ| tasya nirdeśas tribhiḥ sarvajñatvaiḥ sarvajñabhāvaiḥ yataḥ sa sarvajño bhavati sarvajñatā ca svayambhūtvaṃ ca asaṃhāryatā ca| tatra sarvākāra­sarva­dharma­samyagjñānam iha sarvajñatā| svayam eva cittād eva bhavanty asya dharmā iti svayaṃbhūḥ| sarva­dharma­vaśavartītyarthaḥ| tasya dharmāḥ svayaṃbhūtvena ye saṃgṛhītāḥ| nāsya saṃhāram astīti asaṃhāryaḥ| savāsanasarvāvaraṇanirmukta ity arthaḥ| tasya dharmā ye ’saṃhāryatvena saṃgṛhītās teṣāṃ sarveṣāṃ paripūraṇāya bhavantv iti iyatā paripūrir uktā. According to Hari (830), the first words, “qualities of a buddha,” are the general statement. The following three sentences are explained according to the divisions, such the state of complete knowing and so on, referring to (a) the qualities connected with complete knowing, (b) the qualities of self-existent being, and (c) the qualities of not being led astray. So, in this way, he explains the supreme fulfillment of all the qualities of the three types of the state of complete knowing. Hari: buddhadharmāṇām ity uddeśapadaṃ. sarva­jñatāprati­saṃyukta­dharmāṇāṃ svayambhūdharmāṇām asaṃhāryadharmāṇām iti sarvākārajñatādibhedena nirdiṣṭaṃ. tad evaṃ trisarvajñatādharmāṇām anuttarā paripūriḥ kathitā syāt. Note: All commentators agree that the term “state of complete knowing” generally (in this scripture) can refer to the knowing achieved by a disciple, solitary realizer, or buddha. Here, Ratnākara seems to suggest that all three qualities (described in the three different sentences) are exclusive to a buddha. Hari seems to suggest that they are the qualities of each kind of awakening, and that a buddha (cumulatively) possesses them. Blessed One, I do not develop even a single mindset imagining that those extremely compassionate bodhisattva great beings might turn away from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Blessed One, I do not develop even a single mindset imagining that those persons on the bodhisattva path who have set out for unsurpassed, perfect awakening might turn away from it. Rather, those bodhisattva great beings who see these sufferings in the realm of cyclic existence will give rise to an even greater aspiration for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Why is that? It is because those who have developed such mindsets very compassionately feel sympathy for the world with its gods, humans, and asuras, and wish for its benefit and welfare. They continue to develop that mindset, thinking, ‘Having escaped, how indeed should we help sentient beings escape? Having been liberated, how indeed should we help liberate them? Having found relief, how indeed should we help them find relief? Having attained complete nirvāṇa, how indeed should we help them attain complete nirvāṇa?’ Blessed One, if a noble son or daughter rejoices that those bodhisattva great beings who have first set out upon the path have developed the proper mindset; if he or she rejoices that those beyond regressing possess the qualities beyond regressing; and if he or she rejoices that those bound for one more birth possess the qualities of being bound for one more birth, then, Blessed One, how much merit would that noble son or daughter earn?”

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, by comparing Meru, the king of mountains, with the tip of a piece of straw, one might still be able to grasp its size. But, Kauśika, one would never be able to grasp the amount of merit in the development of the mindset associated with a noble son or daughter’s rejoicing for those bodhisattva great beings.According to Ratnākara (F.171.a), the stabilization of the “mindset” (from the Ornament V.iii) is to be explained here, i.e., the meditative concentration (associated with rejoicing). RK: citta sya saṃsthitiḥ| sā vaktavyā| samādhir ity arthaḥ. Note: It is not uncommon for early texts to use the word “mindset” (citta) as a synonym for meditative concentration (samādhi). Ratnākara goes on to explain how the Ornament uses that term in describing this point.
