“Kauśika, by comparing a world system with four great continents with the tip of a piece of straw, one might still be able to grasp its size. But, Kauśika, one would never be able to grasp the amount of merit in the development of the mindset associated with a noble son or daughter’s rejoicing for those bodhisattva great beings.

“Kauśika, by comparing a thousandfold, lesser order world system with the tip of a piece of straw, one might still be able to grasp its size. But, Kauśika, one would never be able to grasp the amount of merit in the development of the mindset associated with a noble son or daughter’s rejoicing for those bodhisattva great beings.

“Kauśika, by comparing a millionfold, medium order world system with the tip of a piece of straw, one might still be able to grasp its size. But, Kauśika, one would never be able to grasp the amount of merit in the development of the mindset associated with a noble son or daughter’s rejoicing for bodhisattva great beings.

“Kauśika, by comparing a billionfold, great order world system with the tip of a piece of straw, one might still be able to grasp its size. But, Kauśika, one would never be able to grasp the amount of merit in the development of the mindset associated with a noble son or daughter’s rejoicing for those bodhisattva great beings.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, there is immeasurable merit in developing the mindset associated with rejoicing for bodhisattva great beings, beginning from their first development of the proper mindset up to their having realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening. But if sentient beings do not listen to this fact, do not understand it, do not see it, and do not keep that rejoicing in mind, then those sentient beings are under Māra’s magical influence.According to Ratnākara (F.171.a–b), he says this sentence so that beings make their rejoicing complete by the absence of all negative attitudes, and to chastise those who are do not do so. “Under Māra’s magical influence” means the extremely pernicious Māra has sapped their energy. They do not “listen” to it based on the words; “understand it” based on the meaning; or “see it” based on respect. They “do not keep it to mind” means they do not turn themselves directly toward (rejoicing). RK: anumodanāyāḥ paripūr aṇārthaṃ tām akurvatāṃ ca nindārtham āha| evam ityādi| mārādhiṣṭhitā iti māreṇa mahāpāpena nirutsāhīkṛtāḥ| na śṛṇvanti śabdataḥ| na jānanty arthataḥ| na paśyanty ādarataḥ| na samanvāharantī ti nābhimukhīkurvanti. Blessed One, by not rejoicing in those bodhisattva great beings’ development of the proper mindset, these sentient beings will become members of Māra’s demon cohort. Blessed One, by not rejoicing in those bodhisattva great beings’ development of the proper mindset, these sentient beings will also fall into Māra’s realms.According to Ratnākara (F.171.b), “members of Māra’s demon cohort” are beings in his cohort, because, in a very similar way, they create obstacles to the great benefit of beings. They “fall into Māra’s realms” means they become nothing but Māras, because they are absolutely the same as he is. RK: | mārapakṣe bhavāḥ mārapākṣikāḥ | tadvad eva mahataḥ sattvārthasya vighātakaraṇāt| mārabhavanebhyaś cyutā iti mārā eva atyantaṃ tatsādharmyāt. Why is that? Because, Blessed One, when they dedicate such developments of the proper mindset to unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and rejoice in these developments of the proper mindset, they accomplish these developments of the proper mindset to destroy Māra’s realms. Blessed One, one should rejoice that those bodhisattva great beings who have developed the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening have developed this mindset. Blessed One, one should rejoice in the development of this mindset by those noble sons and daughters who have not abandoned the Tathāgata, the Dharma, or the Saṅgha.”

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “True, Kauśika. True. Kauśika, one should rejoice in the development of this mindset by those noble sons and daughters who have not abandoned the Tathāgata, the Dharma, or the Saṅgha. Kauśika, whether on the bodhisattva path, the path of a solitary realizer, or the disciple path, any noble sons or daughters who rejoice in the development of such a mindset will quickly please the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, rather than displease them.”

At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “So they will, Blessed One. So they will. Whether on the bodhisattva path, the path of a solitary realizer, or the disciple path, any noble sons or daughters who rejoice in the development of such a mindset will quickly please the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, rather than displease them. Wherever these noble sons and daughters are reborn, they will be paid tribute to, revered, respected, venerated, honored, and cared for as a result of these roots of virtue from their having developed the mindset associated with rejoicing. They will not see unpleasant sights. They will not hear unpleasant sounds. They will not smell unpleasant scents. They will not taste unpleasant flavors. They will not touch unpleasant tactile objects. They can also anticipate being born in the heaven realm rather than being reborn in the lower realms. Why is that? It is because when those noble sons or daughters first develop their aspiration and then rejoice in the developments of the proper mindset of persons on the bodhisattva path to awakening, they are rejoicing in the roots of virtue of immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings that lead all those beings to happiness. As their developments of the proper mindset grow, they will bring forth unsurpassed, perfect awakening. And after they realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, they will help immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings to attain complete nirvāṇa.”

“True, Kauśika. True,” said the Blessed One. “It is just the way you have verbally articulated it through the miraculous power of the Tathāgata. Kauśika, when any noble son or daughter rejoices in the developments of the proper mindset by persons on the bodhisattva path, then, by doing so in this manner, he or she not only rejoices in the roots of virtue of immeasurable and innumerable sentient beings, but also plants them and accomplishes them.”According to Ratnākara (F.171.b), he or she “not only rejoices” in the first development of the mindset and so on, “but also plants them” (avaropitāni) in his or her own mindstream “and accomplishes them.” After rejoicing, one should automatically dedicate that. One should recognize these things to be done as coming from the chapter on rejoicing and dedication. The long Transcendent State of Wisdom scriptures say (about this): “After rejoicing in those roots of virtue, one should dedicate them to unsurpassed, perfect awakening. One should dedicate (that rejoicing mindset) as though there is no mindset that goes about in a mindset or something other than a mindset” and so on (see, for example, the Twenty-Five Thousand, 48.8). So, the (rejoicing mindset that is) the root of virtue is a bodhisattva’s reference point for meditative concentration, while the dedicating (mindset) is his or her meditative perspective (on that). The nature is the continuity of the “mindset” (i.e., the connection between the two mindsets of rejoicing and dedicating). Where is that continuity? (He says) it is “not in the mindset” because it has the characteristic of a grasping subject. It is “not somewhere other than” that, i.e., in any other thing. How is it then? The continuity of a mindset is in the nondual nature of all things. RK: yair apī tyādinā prathamacittotpād ikānām api na kevalam anumoditāni svasantāne cāvaropitāny abhinirhṛtāni ca bhavanti| anumodya cāvaśyaṃ pariṇāmanā kartavyā| yathā ca te kartavye tathā ’numodanāpariṇāmanāparivartād veditavyam| yathoktaṃ mahatyor bhagavatyoḥ ‍—tāni kuśalamūlāni anumodyānuttarāyāṃ samyaksambodhau pariṇāmayitavyāni| tathā ca pariṇāmayitavyāni yathā na cittaṃ citte carati na cānyatra cittāt” ityādi| tad evam anumodanāsahagataṃ kuśalamūlam asya samādher ālambanaṃ samyaksambodhau pariṇāmanam ākāraḥ| cittasaṃsthitiḥ svabhāvaḥ| kva saṃsthitiḥ ? na citte grāhakalakṣaṇena na cānyatre ti na sarvadharmeṣu| kiṃ tarhi ? sarvadharmatathatāyām advayāyām iti cittasaṃsthitiḥ. Note: For those who are interested to know, it may be worth mentioning that Hari and Ratnākara begin here to associate this section, and the whole of the next chapter, with thirty-six conceptualizations (vikalpa) subdivided into four main groups: (1–2) the conceptualizations about grasped objects, respectively, of worldly engagement (pravṛtti) and spiritual disengagement (nivṛtti), and (3–4) the conceptualizations about grasping subjects that are, respectively, really existent (dravyasat) and nominally existent (prajñaptisat). Each of these four has nine kinds of conceptualizations (9 x 4 = 36). The commentators draw these thirty-six from the descriptions in the Ornament (5.5–5.23) and connect those to the main text. The thirty-six conceptualizations form part of the fifth of the Ornament’s eight topics, the culminations, and fall in that fifth topic’s fifth subtopic, the culmination of the path of seeing (see introduction). It would require too much additional explanation to include all Ratnakāra and Hari’s comments and clarify them fully. But some of those comments will be translated in the footnotes going forward just to give readers a basic sense of how Ratnākara and Hari read the main text. While reading the comments on each conceptualization below and in the next chapter, the reader must keep in mind that each successive conceptualization is more subtle and represents some progress, but also something that must be eliminated by the next antidote. Hari connects the thirty-six conceptualizations to the text in a quite different way, but his extensive comments are not translated here because they are easily available in Sparham 2011 (vol. 4), pp. 173–200. Finally, it should be kept in mind that most traditional Buddhist readers would not expect to fully understand the comments by Ratnākara and Hari without a great deal of further study. Note that the conceptualizations mentioned here and in the notes that follow can be found set out‍—using the term “discriminations”‍—as subheadings in Conze’s 1975 translation of the long sūtras, pp. 460–80. See also Brunnhölzl vol. 1 pp. 58–9, and vol. 2 pp. 80–84 and 190–200. The first set of nine conceptualizations, about the grasped objects of worldly engagement (see above) is covered in the Twenty-Five Thousand, 48.8–27.
Subhūti asked, “But, Blessed One, how can a mindset that is like a magical illusion realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening?”

At this, the Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Do you identify a mindset that is like a magical illusion?”According to Ratnākara (F.172.b), a “magical illusion” refers to an elephant and so on that is conjured up by a magician. A similar but a distinct thing is “like a magical illusion,” which means that what appears is not there as it seems (iha). A mindset is like that, because it has the characteristic of a grasping subject but a grasping subject does not exist. For a grasping subject is not logical when the grasped object does not exist. In response to the question why the grasped object does not exist, if an (external) thing were illuminated by knowing, it would have the quality of being a grasped object. But (such an external thing) is not illuminated, because it is contradictory for (a thing that is) not illuminating to be illuminated (i.e., made to appear as a mental image in the mind). But if that thing is illumination itself, (then there is no contradiction). RK: māyākāranirmitaṃ gajādikaṃ māyā| tadvad anyad api yat khyāti nehāsti tat māyopamam| tathśā ca cittam| grāhakalakṣaṇatvāt grāhakasya cāsattvāt| na hy asati grāhye grāhakaṃ yuktam| grāhyābhāvaḥ katham iti cet| yadi jñānenārthaḥ prakāśyeta syād grāhyatvam| na ca prakāśyeta| aprakāśasya prakāśyavirodhāt| athārthaḥ prakāśātmaiva. Note: Here, Ratnākara is first explaining that a mindset is an illusion, because it appears to have the characteristic of a grasping subject, but cannot be directly observed to exist as such. Next he is suggesting that the mind’s illumination of anything other than itself is illogical, and hence, the “mindset” that is said here to be like an illusion refers only to the grasping subject that is imagined, not to the real mind that consists in nothing but illumination.
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti.
At this, the Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? Do you identify a magical illusion?”
“Definitely not, Blessed One,” said Subhūti. “I do not identify a mindset that is like a magical illusion, nor do I identify a magical illusion.”

The Blessed One asked, “Subhūti, what do you think? When you do not identify an illusion-like mindset or a magical illusion, do you identify that thing that realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening as something other than a magical illusion or other than an illusion-like mindset?”According to Ratnākara (F.172.b), “that thing” refers to a self. RK: taṃ dharmam ity ātmānam.

“Definitely not, Blessed One,”According to Ratnākara (F.172.b), “definitely not” because it is absolutely nonexistent‍—this is the idea. RK: no hīdam iti tasyātyantam asattvād iti bhāvaḥ. said Subhūti. “I do not identify that thing that realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening as something other than a magical illusion or other than an illusion-like mindset. Blessed One, since I myself am not identifying that thing that realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening as something other than a magical illusion or other than an illusion-like mindset, what thing could I point to as being ‘there’ or ‘not there’?According to Ratnākara (F.172.b), it cannot be specified (nirdeśaṃ nopaiti) as “there or not there” because it cannot be logically connected either with proving it to be (truly) nonexistent or disproving it to be a form that is (simply) not recognized‍—this is the idea. RK: astīti nāstīti vā nirdeśaṃ nopaiti| asato ’vijñātarūpasya vidhipratiṣedhābhyām asambandhād iti bhāvaḥ. Also, it cannot be specified whether an absolutely void thing is ‘there’ or ‘not there,’ or whether an absolutely void thing realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Why is that? Because, Blessed One, a thing that cannot be found to exist cannot realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. So, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is absolutely void, and that thing that is absolutely void cannot be cultivated, nor can it either produce or accomplish anything.According to Ratnākara (F.172.b), it does not “produce”‍—i.e., achieve‍—or “accomplish”‍—i.e., actualize‍—“anything” such as perfect awakening and so on, because a nonexistent thing cannot do anything at all. RK: nāpyasāv ityādi| kasyacid dharmasye ti samyaksambodhyādeḥ| āvāhaka āhārakaḥ| nirvāhako niṣpādakaḥ| asato ’kiñcitkaratvāt. Blessed One, how could a bodhisattva great being realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening as a result of a transcendent state of wisdom that is absolutely void? Blessed One, even unsurpassed, perfect awakening is absolutely void. And, Blessed One, if even the transcendent state of wisdom is absolutely void, and if even unsurpassed, perfect awakening is absolutely void, then, Blessed One, how could a void be realized by a void?”According to Ratnākara (F.173.a), “awakening is absolutely void,” as is “the transcendent state of wisdom.” So, his question, “How could a void realize…,” means it actually could not, because the means and the goal are absolutely nonexistent‍—this is the basic idea. RK: anuttarāpī tyādi| atyantaviviktā prajñāpāramitā vat| kathaṃ bhavatī ti naiva bhavati| atyantāsatoḥ sādhyasādhanatvāyogād iti bhāvaḥ.

At this, the Blessed One said to Subhūti, “Excellent, Subhūti. Excellent. You are right, Subhūti. You are right. The transcendent state of wisdom is absolutely void, and unsurpassed, perfect awakening is absolutely void. Subhūti, it is only because the transcendent state of wisdom is absolutely void that unsurpassed, perfect awakening is realized as absolutely void.According to Ratnākara (F.173.a), “it is only because” means because the fruit resembles the cause. RK: yata eva ata eve ti| hetvanurūpatvāt phalasyety arthaḥ. Subhūti, if a bodhisattva great being conceives of the notion, ‘The transcendent state of wisdom is absolutely void,’ then it would not be the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.173.a–b), with this sentence the Blessed One helps to perfect the cause, because if one “conceives” that way, then it would be a conceptualization, not “the transcendent state of wisdom.” RK: sa ced ityādinā hetuṃ paripūrayati| yadi hi saṃjānīte tadā vikalpaḥ syāt na prajñāpāramitā. Thus, Subhūti, it is certain both that unsurpassed, perfect awakening is realized depending upon the transcendent state of wisdom, and also, Subhūti, that unsurpassed, perfect awakening is not realized depending upon the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Hari (839), “thus” means just like its conventional nature, which holds up only when unscrutinized. Then he explains the absolute nature in the second half of the sentence, beginning “also not realized.” Hari: evam avicāraikaramyatvena samvṛtirūpatayeti yāvat. atha eva paramārtham adhikṛtyāha: nāpi subhūte prajñāpāramitām ityādi. According to Ratnākara (F.173.b), the word “thus” begins a brief summary. It is “also not realized” because realization is not conceptualizing‍—this is the basic idea. RK: evam ityādinopasaṃhāraḥ| nāpy abhisaṃbudhyata ity abhisaṃbodher avikalpanād iti bhāvaḥ. Nevertheless, although voidness is not realized by voidness, a bodhisattva great being does realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, but not without depending upon the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Hari (839), the Blessed One says “a bodhisattva great being does realize” and so on as a summary at the conventional level. Hari: saṃvṛtyopasaṃharann āha : abhisambudhyate ce tyadi. According to Ratnākara (F.173.b), “a bodhisattva great being does realize” because these three (i.e., the bodhisattva, the transcendent state of wisdom, and awakening) do not exist in their imagined nature, but they do exist in the nature of representations (vijñapti), which is empty‍—this is the idea. These (three) are (or can be called) conventional because they are similar in how they appear (as illusions). But their ultimate reality is emptiness. RK: abhisabudhyate ce tyādi| parikalpitena hi rūpeṇa trayam idaṃ nāsti, tacchūnyena tu vijñaptirūpeṇāstīti bhāvaḥ| tathāpi saṃvṛttir eṣāṃ yathāprati­bhāsa­samatvāt| śūnyatā tu paramārthaḥ.

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, as I understand the point of what the Blessed One has just taught, Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being is going about life in the profound point.”According to Hari (839), the point referred to as “what he has just taught” is that it is both not realized (ultimately) and not realized (conventionally). Hari: bhāṣitasye ti nābhisambudhyate ’bhisambudhyate cety asya. According to Ratnākara (F.173.b), he or she “is going about life in the profound point” because in this “point” he or she cannot go about life with other (points). RK: gambhīre ’rthe caratī ti yato ’sminn arthe na śakyate ’nyaiś caritum. Note: The translation of artha as “point” in this context is to capture this word’s semantic range from “meaning” to “object” to “purpose” to “goal”‍—all of which seem intended in this passage.

“Yes, Subhūti, that is true,” said the Blessed One. “A bodhisattva great being is going about life in the profound point. Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being performs difficult noble deeds, goes about life in the profound point, and does not actualize that point at the level of a disciple or a solitary realizer.”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, as I understand the point of what the Blessed One has just said, there is no bodhisattva great being performing difficult noble deeds.According to Ratnākara (F.173.b), (he or she is actually) “performing difficult noble deeds,” for even the Blessed One, who would be capable of actualizing (fully the point just discussed), does not actualize it out of his care for sentient beings. “The point of what you just said” means the intended meaning (behind it). So the idea (behind saying that he or she is “not performing difficult noble deeds”) is simply that what others find difficult, a bodhisattva finds easy. Why easy? Because of his or her skill in means, i.e., the means that simply consist in the transcendent state of wisdom. RK: duṣkarakāraka iti yataḥ sa bhagavān sākṣātkartuṃ śakto pi na sākṣātkaroti sattvāvekṣayā| bhāṣitasyārtham iti ābhiprāyikam artham| duṣkaram anyeṣāṃ sukaraṃ bodhisattvasyeti bhāvaḥ| kutaḥ sukaram ? upāyakuśalatvāt| sa copāyaḥ prajñāpāramitaiva. Why not? Because, Blessed One, one cannot mentally perceive any thing that actualizes something, any thing that is actualized by it, or any thing that serves as a means to actualize that.According to Ratnākara (F.173.b), “any thing that actualizes” refers to a bodhisattva; “any thing that is actualized” refers to a profound point; and “any thing that serves as a means to actualize” refers to what is called the transcendent state of wisdom. RK: ya iti bodhisattvaḥ| ya iti gambhīro ’rthaḥ| yene ti prajñāpāramitākhyena. Blessed One, when this is described, if a bodhisattva great being does not become disheartened, deflated, depressed, discouraged, frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.173.a–74.a), now, for a bodhisattva “going about life (carati) in the transcendent state of wisdom,” he explains the extent to which he or she goes about in it. In this sentence, “not disheartened” means not divided (about it). This is the general statement. The detailed explanation of that is in the next six descriptions: “not deflated,” i.e., not having a mediocre delight in that transcendent state of wisdom; “not depressed,” i.e., not having weak delight in it; “not discouraged,” i.e., not lacking any delight in it; “not frightened,” i.e., not wishing to abandon it; “not terrified,” i.e., not abandoning it; or “not stricken with terror,” i.e., not abandoning it continuously. So, by refuting these six types of branches (of not being disheartened) in this way, it is implied that whoever absolutely delights in being face to face with that transcendent state of wisdom, “he or she goes about” in it. RK: ath āsyāṃ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ carataḥ sā caryā kiyatā bhavatīty ata āha| sa ced ityādi| na saṃsīdatī ti na bhajyate| ayam uddeśaḥ| asya nirdeśaḥ ṣoḍhā| nāvalīyata iti nāsyāṃ madhyapremā| na saṃlīyata iti nāsyāṃ mṛdupremā| na vipṛṣṭhībhavatī ti nāpremā| nottrasyatī ti naināṃ tyaktukāmaḥ| na saṃtrasyatī ti na tyajati| na saṃtrāsam āpadyata iti na prabandhena tyajati| tad evaṃ ṣaḍvidhabhaṅga­pratiṣedhād etad gamyate yo syām abhimukhībhūtāyām atyantaṃ prīyate so ’syāṃ caratī ti. If he or she does not identify the conception, ‘I am going about my life,’ then he or she is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. If such a bodhisattva great being does not identify the conception, ‘I am close to unsurpassed, perfect awakening,’ then in this way, too, he or she is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. If such a bodhisattva great being does not have the conception, ‘I am far from the level of a disciple or the level of a solitary realizer,’ then in this way, too, he or she is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.174.a), with these two sentences‍—namely, “I am close” or “I am far”‍—he expresses (the conceptions on) one side and the opposite side. In this section, there are nine points (that are conceptualized), such as an inherent nature and so on, because they can be taken in by bodhisattvas in (each) part of the path. Conceptualizations about these nine are confusion or inaccurate knowing (concerning the grasped object of worldly engagement). Among these nine, the first conceptualization is about inherent nature. Such an inherent nature involves its own intention, i.e., the bodhicitta (conception): “I should attain unsurpassed, perfect awakening through this path.” In this, there is conceptualization. There is also unknowing and unconcentrated knowing about the emptiness of the self, awakening, and the transcendent state of wisdom. And there is the conceit of those three (i.e., self, awakening, etc.) in the concentrated knowing: “I am going about” and “I am close,” i.e., my great awakening (is close) or “I am far,” i.e., from the lesser awakening (of the disciples’ path). He explains the antidote to this (trio of conceptualizations) starting from Subhūti’s question, “How could a mindset that is like a magical illusion…” After that, the beginning of the series of analogies marks the end of the section on the first conceptualization about inherent nature. (In the section following this are the other eight conceptualizations). RK: aparam āha sa ced ityādinā| aparam āha| āsanne tyādinā| aparam āha dūrīkṛte tyādinā| iha svabhāvādayo navārthāḥ| pravṛttipakṣe bodhisattvair upādeyatvāt| teṣu vikalpaḥ sammoho ’samyagjñānam| tatrādyaḥ svabhāvavikalpaḥ| svabhāvaḥ svābhiprāyaḥ| aham anuttarāṃ samyaksambodhim adhigaccheyaṃ tanmārgeṇeti| bodhicittam ity arthaḥ| tasmin vikalpaḥ | ātmano bodheḥ prajñāpāramitāyāś ca śūnyatāyāṃ yad ajñānam asamāhitajñānaṃ ca| samāhitajñāne ca tisro manyanā| ahaṃ carāmi āsannā me mahābodhir ahaṃ vā dūre hīnabodheḥ syām iti| asya pratipakṣaḥ kathaṃ bhagavan māyopamaṃ cittam ity ata ārabhya tadyathāpīt y ataḥ prāk| iti svabhāvavikalpaḥ.

“Blessed One, it is just, for instance, like space, which does not have the conception, ‘I am close to something’ or ‘I am far from something.’ Why not? Because, Blessed One, space is free of conceptualization. Blessed One, in this very way, that bodhisattva great being going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom does not have the conception, ‘I am close to unsurpassed, perfect awakening’ or ‘I am far from the level of a disciple or the level of a solitary realizer.’ Why not? Because, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is beyond conceptualization.According to Ratnākara (F.174.a), the confusion is that my great awakening “is close” or the lesser awakening is “far” because I belong to the spiritual family (gotra) of a buddha. The antidote to this confusion is “the transcendent state of wisdom that is free of conceptualization” of such belonging to the spiritual family, because it is similar to space. This ends the (second) conceptualization about belonging to the spiritual family. RK: tadyathāpītyādi| āsannā me mahābodhir dūre hīnabodhī yato haṃ buddhagotra iti sammohaḥ| asya pratipakṣaḥ| ākāśa samatvād gotrasya tadvad avikalpā prajñāpāramite ti gotravikalpaḥ.

“Blessed One, it is just, for instance, like the fact that an illusory person does not have the conception, ‘I am close to the magical illusionist, but I am far from that crowd that has assembled.’According to Ratnākara (F.174.a), the confusion is that my great awakening “is close” or the lesser awakening is “far” due to my conduct of progressing along the path. The antidote to this is “the transcendent state of wisdom that is free of conceptualization” because the conduct is like that of an illusory person. This ends the (third) conceptualization about conduct along the path. RK: tadyathāpī tyādi| asti me pratipatti­samudāgamaḥ| tata āsannā me mahābodhir dūre hīnabodhī iti saṃmohaḥ| tasya pratipakṣaḥ samudāgamasya māyāpuruṣo pamatvād avikalpā prajñāpāramite ti pratipatsamudāgama­vikalpaḥ. Why not? Because, Blessed One, an illusory person is free of conceptualization. Blessed One, in this very way a bodhisattva great being going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom does not think, ‘I am close to unsurpassed, perfect awakening’ or ‘I am far from the level of a disciple or the level of a solitary realizer.’ Why not? Because, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is free of conceptualization.

“Blessed One, it is just, for instance, like the fact that a reflection does not have the conception, ‘I am close to that object, because of which the reflection arises, but I am far from those who do not come close here, in the mirror or on the surface of water.’According to Ratnākara (F.174.b), an appearance is a “reflection.” The knowing of it is due to the appearing of a false appearance in knowing, rather than (due to the appearing) of an object. The object of knowing is a cognitive error that is produced by me. Due to this, there is the confusion that my great awakening “is close” or the lesser awakening is “far”. The antidote to this confusion is “the transcendent state of wisdom that is free of conceptualization,” which does not get stuck in something delightful or not delightful. This ends the (fourth) conceptualization of the cognitive error of an object. RK: tadyathāpī tyādi| pratibhāsaḥ pratibimbaḥ| jñānapratibhāsasya pratibhāsatvenaiva yadjñānaṃ na tv ālambanasya ca| seha jñānasyālambanā bhrāntiḥ| sā mama jātā| tata āsannā me mahābodhir dūre hīnabodhī iti saṃmohaḥ| tasya pratipakṣaḥ pratibimbavad avikalpā prajñāpāramite ti ālambanābhrānti­vikalpaḥ. Why not? Because, Blessed One, a reflection is free of being conceptualized [as such]. Blessed One, in this very way it does not occur to a bodhisattva great being going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, ‘I am close to unsurpassed, perfect awakening’ or ‘I am far from the level of a disciple or the level of a solitary realizer.’ Why not? Because, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is free of conceptualization.

“Blessed One, it is just, for instance, like the fact that a delightful thing or an undelightful thing is not found in the Tathāgata. Why not? Because, Blessed One, the Tathāgata has exhausted all fabrications and conceptualizations. Blessed One, in this very way something delightful or not delightful is not found in the Tathāgata.According to Ratnākara (F.174.b), the confusion is that an antidote is a destroyer and a counterproductive force is what is to be destroyed. The antidote to this confusion is just as the “delightful and undelightful” do not belong to a tathāgata, just so he or she “goes about in the transcendent state of wisdom that is free of conceptualization” because the conduct is like that of an illusory person. This ends the (fifth) conceptualization of an antidote and a counterproductive force. RK: tadyathāpī tyādi| pratipakṣo hantā vipakṣo hantavya iti saṃmohaḥ| asya pratipakṣo yathā tathāgatasya priyāpriya u na stas tadvad avikalpāyāṃ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ carato pī ti pratipakṣavipakṣavikalpaḥ. Why not? Because, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is free of conceptualization, since, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom has exhausted all fabrications and conceptualizations exactly as the Tathāgata has exhausted all fabrications and conceptualizations.According to Ratnākara (F.174.b), following the second “Why not?” in this paragraph, the “fabrication” is due to imagining something unreal and the “conceptualization” is due to fabricating a lack of that fabrication. As those two are “exhausted,” he or she “goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom that is free of conceptualization” of a tathāgata. The confusion of one’s own attainment is the conceptualization of a lack of a fabrication in that. The antidote to that confusion is “the transcendent state of wisdom that has exhausted all fabrications and conceptualizations.” This ends the (sixth) conceptualization of one’s own attainment. RK: yathaiva hī tyādi abhūtakalpanāt kalpaḥ| tasya vigamakalpanād vikalpaḥ| tau prahīṇau yathā tathāgatasya tadvad avikalpāyāṃ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ carato pi| tatra kalpavigamo vikalpaḥ svādhigamasaṃmohaḥ| tasya pratipakṣaḥ kalpa vikalpaprahīṇā prajñāpāramite ti svādhigamavikalpaḥ.

“Blessed One, it is just, for instance, like the fact that a miraculous creation created by the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata does not have the conception, ‘I am far from the level of a disciple or the level of a solitary realizer’ or ‘I am close to unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’According to Ratnākara (F.174.b–75.a), due to thinking “I am cultivating the transcendent state of wisdom,” there is the confusion about an agent (in the conception), that my great awakening “is close” or the lesser awakening is “far.” The antidote to this confusion is “the transcendent state of wisdom that is free of conceptualization,” which is exactly like a miraculous creation of an agent. This ends the (seventh) conceptualization about an agent. RK: tadyathāpī tyādi| prajñāpāramitām ahaṃ bhāvayāmi| tata āsannā me mahābodhir dūre hīnabodhī iti kartari sammohaḥ| tasya pratipakṣaḥ| kartus tathāgatanirmito pamatattadvad eva kartari nirvikalpā prajñāpāramite ti kartari vikalpaḥ. Why not? Because, Blessed One, a miraculous creation is free of conceptualization. Blessed One, in this very way a bodhisattva great being going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom does not think, ‘I am close to unsurpassed, perfect awakening’ or ‘I am far from the level of a disciple or the level of a solitary realizer.’ Why not? Because, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is free of conceptualization.

“Blessed One, it is just, for instance, like when a tathāgata’s miraculous creation has been created to do a task and it does that task, but that miraculous creation is free of conceptualization.According to Ratnākara (F.175.a), a “tathāgata’s miraculous creation has been created” for a purpose (kṛtyasya) “and it does that” but does not conceptualize that activity. Likewise, a bodhisattva cultivates the transcendent state of wisdom but the antidote is that he or she does not conceptualize it. This is (eighth) conceptualization about the fruits of activities, i.e., the conceptualization of (an agent involved in) awakening. RK: tadyathāpī tyādi| sa ca tathāgatanirmito yadarthaṃ nirmitas tacca kṛtyaṃ karoti| na ca tāṃ kriyāṃ vikalpayati| tadvadbodhisattvaḥ prajñāpāramitāṃ bhāvayati na ca vikalpayatīti pratipakṣaḥ| iti bodhivikalpanaṃ kriyāphalavikalpaḥ. Why? Because a miraculous creation is free of conceptualization. Blessed One, in this very way a bodhisattva great being cultivates the transcendent state of wisdom for the sake of a task and he or she does that task, but the transcendent state of wisdom is free of conceptualization. Why? Because, Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is free of conceptualization.

“Blessed One, it is just, for instance, like the fact that a skilled carpenter or an apprentice carpenter might have made a wooden man or woman and connected it to strings. It might do the task for which it was made, but that slapped-together wood is free of conceptualization.According to Ratnākara (F.175.a), a carpenter creates a “wooden (dārumayī) man connected to strings” for a purpose (kṛtyasya) “and it does that” but does not conceptualize anything. Likewise, a bodhisattva aims for awakening and does not attain it, but the antidote is that he or she does not conceptualize it. This ends the ( ninth) conceptualization about the fruits of activities as a conceptualization of awakening. RK: tadyathāpītyādi [ā parivartā]ntāt| śilpinā yadarthaṃ dāruyantramayaḥ pumānkṛtaḥ sa tadarthaṃ karoti na vikalpayati| tadvad bodhisattvo bodhaye, na tāṃ bodhiṃ prāpsyati na ca tāṃ vikalpayatīti pratipakṣaḥ| iti bodhivikalpanaṃ kriyāphalavikalpaḥ. Why? Because slapped-together wood is free of conceptualization. Blessed One, in this very way a bodhisattva great being cultivates the transcendent state of wisdom for the sake of a task and does that task, but the transcendent state of wisdom is free of conceptualization. Why? Because, Blessed One, this transcendent state of wisdom is free of any conceptualization at all.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Magical Illusion,” the twenty-sixth in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 27: The EssenceThe beginning of this chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 49 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 59 in the Eighteen Thousand, and the content of the whole of those chapters is included. In addition, at the point marked by note 1254, the chapter covers the initial passages of Chapter 50 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 60 in the Eighteen Thousand.According to Ratnākara (F.181.a), the “Chapter on the Essence” is the chapter that is the essence and so on. RK: sārā diḥ parivartaḥ sāraparivartaḥ.
div.p: Then the venerable Śāriputra said to the venerable Subhūti, “Ah! Venerable Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being who goes about life in this transcendent state of wisdom goes about life in the essence.”According to Ratnākara (F.175.a–b), the first (of the four groups of nine) conceptualizations about a (nominally existent) grasped object were explained at the end of the previous “Chapter on the Magical Illusion” (see n.1203.) The next three groups of conceptualizations will be explained in this chapter. Among the four groups, the second group (of nine) conceptualizations is about a grasped object (of spiritual disengagement), as explained in the Ornament. (This second group of nine conceptualizations is covered in the passage of the Twenty-Five Thousand at 53.1–26.) When bodhisattvas eliminate the faults of (these nine conceptualizations)‍—namely the deficiencies for realization and so on‍—then the basis for a partial cessation comes about due to the faults’ absence, and (the bodhisattvas further eliminate) nine conceptualizations that occur in the minds of disciples, since they have not eliminated those (yet). The interjection “ah!” concerns “the essence” in the sense of joy. The excellent fruit is perfect awakening, the essence (sāra) is what flows from that (anusāra). The bodhisattva goes about life based upon that essence. Śāriputra explains what that is as “this transcendent state of wisdom”‍—which in this context is described as the knowing of the absence of the two kinds of identity (personal identity and the identity of things in general). A bodhisattva “going about life” in that transcendent state of wisdom is not totally anxious (udvega) about cyclic existence and also not totally desirous (utkaṇṭhā) about nirvāṇa, because he or she is free of any mental perception of either of these. For this reason, the antidote (to the next conceptualizations) is that he or she should (aim further to) realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. If he or she does not, then (by practicing the other) five transcendent states, he or she would either fall back into cyclic existence, or else, know (only) of the absence of personal identity in (a disciple-like) nirvāṇa. For this very reason, any bodhisattva lacking the transcendent state of wisdom does not earn the name “bodhisattva”‍—just as the perfectly anointed son of a universal emperor is not dubbed a universal emperor without the seven precious things (that a emperor must possess). Thinking like this is the (first) conceptualization about the deficiencies for realization. RK: māyopamaparivartasyānte prathamo grāhyavikalpa uktaḥ| itare trayo vikalpā iha sāraparivart e vaktavyāḥ| tatra dvitīyaṃ grāhyavikalpam adhikṛtya śāstram|…adhigamanyūnatā dau doṣe bodhisattvapraheye [’]bhāvān nivṛtti­pakṣādhiṣṭhāno navavidhaś ca śrāvakādīnāṃ manobhavas tair aheyatvāt|…sāre batāyam iti bata śabdo harṣe| utkṛṣṭaṃ phalaṃ samyaksaṃbodhiḥ| so ’nusāraḥ| tannimittaṃ bodhisattvaś carati| katama ity āha| ya ityādi| prajñāpāramitā cātra ubhayanairātmyajñānam ucyate| tasyāṃ carataḥ saṃsāre nātyantam udvego nirvāṇe nātyantam utkaṇṭhā| ubhayor anupalambhatvāt| tato ’yam anuttarāṃ samyaksambodhim adhigacched iti pratipakṣaḥ| anyathā tu saṃsāre vā patet pañcabhiḥ pāramitābhiḥ| nirvāṇe vā pudgalanairātmyajñānāt| ata eva ca vinā prajñāpāramitayā bodhisattvo bodhisattvākhyāṃ na labhate| tadyathā samyagabhiṣikto pi cakravartinaḥ putraś cakravartiśabdaṃ vinā saptabhī ratnair ity adhi­gamanyūnatāvikalpaḥ. Note: In the Sanskrit reported here, the first ellipsis marks where Ratnākara cites the Ornament (5.10–5.12), listing the nine conceptualizations about the grasped object of spiritual disengagement. The second ellipsis marks where Ratnākara makes a technical comment on the structure of all that is said here at the start, previous to the Buddha’s first comment.

At this, the venerable Subhūti said to the venerable Śāriputra, “Ah! Venerable Śāriputra, any bodhisattva great being who goes about life in this transcendent state of wisdom goes about life in the essenceless.”According to Ratnākara (F.175.b), (Śāriputra has called it) the “essence” (sāra, a noun etymologically connected with) approaching (saraṇam, in the sense that) the object of an action (karman) is the thing (i.e. the essence being approached). An approachable quality is a state easy to overcome, so (Subhūti calls it) the opposite, the “essenceless” indicating a state difficult to overcome. Hence a bodhisattva who goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom is able to protect (the other five) transcendent states. The transcendent state of wisdom, as an analogy, is like an armed guard for women walking along a dangerous city street. Hence it is the antidote (to conceptualization). This (second) conceptualization is about the absence of any protection, i.e. thinking that the (practices of the five) transcendent states would be easy to overcome if the opposite were true (i.e., if the transcendent state of wisdom were not there to protect them). RK: saraṇaṃ sāraḥ, karma dharmaḥ| gamyatā sudharṣaṇatety arthaḥ| viparyayād asāro durddharṣaṇatā| ataḥ pāramitānāṃ durddharṣaṇatve sa carati yaś carati prajñāpāramitāyām| sā hi tāsāṃ parigrahasamarthā| tadyathā strīṇāṃ sadhūrtake nagaramārge śastrapāṇipuruṣa iti pratipakṣaḥ| anyathā sudharṣaṇāḥ syur iti parigrahābhāvavikalpaḥ. Note: Ratnākara’s initial etymology about approaching, or essence, is difficult to translate because he is stretching it in order to explain how it is that the first two statements of this chapter concern the first two of the nine conceptualizations taught in the corresponding section of the Ornament.Although the Sanskrit editions suggest that Subhūti repeats Śāriputra’s exclamation exactly (which would in itself be unusual in the context of the exchanges between the two interlocuotors), the Add.866 manuscript and Haribhadra’s lemma both read asāra (“essenceless”) in Subhūti’s response in place of Śāriputra’s sāra. This is corroborated by the Tibetan in all Kangyurs, and indeed by the equivalent passages in the three long Transcendent State of Wisdom sūtras, too. The passage that follows in the long sūtras, absent here, is a discussion of the “essencelessness” of all the dharmas (see the Eighteen Thousand, 59.1–2 ff. and Twenty-Five Thousand, 49.1-2 ff.).

Then many thousands of gods of the desire realm had the thought, “We should pay homage to those sentient beings who have accomplished the mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening.According to Ratnākara (F.175.b), they “should be paid homage” due to their cumulative progress, their independence, and their not turning back from their stated intention (i.e., the three objects of the third through fifth conceptualizations). Hence, they are explaining what the sentient beings “have accomplished” in terms of their progress (pratipatti) with the five transcendent states. RK: namaskartavyās ta iti kutaḥ? pratipattisākalyād aparādhīnatvād uddeśānivṛtteś ca| ata evāhuḥ yair ityādi| sarvam abhinirhṛtānī ti pratipattitaḥ pañcabhiḥ pāramitābhiḥ| ihety ubhaya­nairātmyavedinyām| Those who go about life in such a profound transcendent state of wisdom never actualize the pinnacle of reality at the level of a disciple or a solitary realizer, because they go about life that way.According to Ratnākara (F.175.b), “In such” a transcendent state of wisdom means in the one that knows the absence of the two (kinds of) identity. For this very reason, such a transcendent state of wisdom is “profound”‍—and considering this is the antidote. The (third) conceptualization is about their lack of progress, thinking that without going about in that transcendent state of wisdom, bodhisattvas would lack progress, so they need to fight well against the Māras, like a warrior uses unbroken armor against opponents. Just as a universal emperor, due to his great might, is the chief of his subordinate regional governors, just so the transcendent state of wisdom is chief because of being free of mentally perceiving cyclic existence or nirvāṇa. Since those bodhisattvas, due to (practicing) the transcendent states, do not fall into cyclic existence, they likewise “never actualize the pinnacle of reality” by force of their knowing of the absence of personal identity (as the disciples and solitary realizers do). But the (fourth) conceptualization is about intending to proceed based on other (guides, i.e., not the transcendent state of wisdom), thinking that if the transcendent state of wisdom were not chief, he or she would (just as soon) actualize the pinnacle of reality as the lesser awakening (of the disciple path). RK: ata eva gambhīrāyām iti pratipakṣaḥ| tasyām acaratāṃ pratipattivaikalyaṃ syāt| tatas te mārādibhiḥ suyodhanāḥ syuḥ| akṣatakavacā iva yodhāḥ pratiyodhair iti prati­pattivaikalya­vikalpaḥ| bhūtakoṭir ityādi| yathā hi cakravartī pradhānaṃ mahāprabhāvatvāt koṭṭarājānaḥ tadanuvartinas tathaiva prajñāpāramitā pradhānaṃ tayā saṃsāranirvāṇayor anupalambhāt| yathā te pāramitābhiḥ saṃsāre na pātyante tathā pudgala­nairātmya­jñānavaśena bhūtakoṭiṃ na sākṣātkurvanti| yadi tu prajñāpāramitā pradhānaṃ na syāt tadā hīnabodhiṃ bhūtakoṭiṃ sākṣātkuryur eveti para­pratyayagāmitva­vikalpaḥ. For this very reason, one can know that these bodhisattva great beings are going about life in the true nature of things, but they do not actualize that true nature of things.”According to Ratnākara (F.175.b), regarding this sentence, if one were to ask why not, it is because of the bodhisattva’s not turning back from his or her stated intention (uddeśa; ched du bya ba). The stated intention is his or her desire to reach perfect awakening. Why does he or she not turn back from that? A bodhisattva (at this point of the journey) does not turn back from that before reaching the transcendent state of wisdom, because he or she (has been) going about life in it for that very purpose. The same is true of the others, because he or she does not turn back also from those that have merged with that transcendent state of wisdom. It is like, for example, all the small rivers merging with a large river and then going only to the ocean without turning back. This (fifth) conceptualization is about not turning back from one’s stated intention. Although there should be a total of nine conceptualizations here (in this scripture), the others‍—concerning one’s own benefit in the long Transcendent State of Wisdom scriptures‍—must be supplied from there (since this scripture only hints at them with a few words). RK: tam evāhur ya ityādinā na sākṣātkurvantī ti| kutaḥ? uddeśānivṛtteḥ| uddeśaḥ samyaksaṃbodhau lipsā| kutas tasmād anivṛttiḥ ? yato na tāvat prajñāpāramitā tato nivartate, tadartham eva tasyāṃ caraṇāt| nāpītarāḥ| tadanupraviṣṭānāṃ tāsām apy anivṛtteḥ| tadyathā sarvāḥ kunadyo mahānadīm anupraviśya samudram eva gacchanti na nivartata ity uddeśanivṛttikalpaḥ|| navāpyete vikalpā mahatyor bhagavatyo svārthasampattim adhikṛtya yojitāḥ. Note: Since the sixth and seventh conceptualizations have two aspects‍—i.e., for one’s own and others’ benefit‍—Ratnākara proceeds to explain the sixth and seventh conceptualizations first in terms of one’s own benefit using passages from The Perfection of Wisdom in Twenty-Five Thousand Lines, then returns to explain these same two conceptualizations in terms of the benefit of others according to this Eight Thousand scripture. The sixth and seventh conceptualizations are covered, in the Twenty-Five Thousand, by the passage 48.24–25. We note here again, as in note 1198, that the the conceptualizations mentioned here and in the notes above and below can be found set out‍—using the term “discriminations”‍—as subheadings in Conze’s 1975 translation of the long sūtras, pp. 460–80, and in Brunnhölzl vol. 1 pp. 58–9, and vol. 2 pp. 80–84 and 190–200.

Then the venerable Subhūti knew in his mind the mental ruminations of the many thousands of gods of the desire realm, and said to them, “Gods, a bodhisattva great being has no difficulty that is because of not actualizing the pinnacle of reality. Rather, the only difficulty that a bodhisattva great being has‍—which is an extreme difficulty‍—is that he or she dons the armor in order to help immeasurable, innumerable, and measureless sentient beings attain complete nirvāṇa, but he or she finds there to be absolutely no sentient beings.According to Ratnākara (F.175.b), in this Eight Thousand scripture, Subhūti addresses the two conceptualizations in relation to the benefit of others, in this passage here. To explain those (two conceptualizations, the first is) that to “don” (the armor) demonstrates what is difficult, because of the impossibility of bringing about the benefit of endless sentient beings. Nevertheless, the antidote is to consider that a bodhisattva should do exactly that (i.e., don the armor) because, without his or her being greatly disposed (toward their benefit) in this way, he or she would not be able to bring benefit to themselves or to others. But this (sixth) conceptualization is about limiting (oneself), thinking that one should put on the armor (only) for the sake of taming oneself because one cannot (really) do anything for the sake of all beings. RK: asyāṃ tu bhagavatyāṃ parārthasannāham adhikṛtya dvāv imau vikalpau subhūtir āhe tyādinā| atha khalv ityādinā tatra sannahyanta ity anena duṣkaratvaṃ darśitam anantānāṃ sattvānām arthasya kartum aśakyatvāt| tathāpy evam eva sannāhaḥ kartavya īdṛśīṃ mahāśayatām antareṇa svaparārthayoḥ kartum aśakyatvād iti pratipakṣaḥ| sarvasattvānām arthasya kenacid akaraṇād ātmavineyānām arthāya sannāhaḥ kartavya iti prādeśikatvavikalpaḥ. Since he or she does not find those sentient beings thanks to their voidness, that bodhisattva great being also does not mentally perceive them. Thus, the bodhisattva great being finds absolutely no disciples to be tamed, even though he or she sets out to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening with the conception, ‘I will tame sentient beings.’According to Ratnākara (F.176.b–77.a), with this following passage he intends to express the sameness, which is antidote to the (seventh) conceptualization about (results) being varied. The phrase “disciples to be tamed” means those beings who are tamable. Despite the problem (described in the first sentence), the next sentence explains what the (actual) difficulty is. RK: nānātvavikalpapratipakṣaḥ samatā| tām eva vivakṣuḥ paramaduṣkara tvam āha te ce tyādinā| vainayikā iti vinayārhāḥ sattvāḥ| tathāpi kasya duṣkaratety ata āha| evaṃ ce tyādi. Note: Ratnākara says the seventh conceptualization begins here, but describes various pieces gradually over the next fifteen sentences. Gods, one should think of those sentient beings to be tamed as being like space. Why is that? Because, gods, one should understand sentient beings to be void in the same way that space is void.According to Ratnākara (F.177.a), he says “space” and so on to strengthen the point with a simile. “Why is that?” asks the question why these are similar. His response comes in the following sentences. RK: dṛṣṭāntena dṛḍhīkartum āha| ākāśam ityādi| tatkasya hetor iti kutaḥ sādharmyād ity arthaḥ| ata uttaraṃ ākāśe tyādi. For this very reason, gods, when a bodhisattva great being performs such difficult noble deeds, he or she equips him- or herself with the great armor in order to benefit sentient beings, who are neither being found nor mentally perceived.According to Ratnākara (F.177.a), “for this very reason” introduces a summary (of the difficulty). RK: anene ty upasaṃhāraḥ. And, gods, anyone who considers themselves to have donned the great armor for the benefit of sentient beings should also consider thenselves to be in a struggle with space, because the armor that he or she has donned for the benefit of sentient beings is also just that.According to Ratnākara (F.177.a), he says this sentence to further strength his point. The meaning is that sentient beings are like space. Why is there a struggle? He explains in the following clause connected through the Sanskrit word ca in the sense of a reason (and translated “because”). “Struggle” means conflict. The conflict is that he or she wishes to tame (existent) beings but they resist (being tamed) due to their not being existent. RK: punar dṛḍhīkartum āha| ākāśene tyādi| ākāśopamaiḥ sattvair ity arthaḥ| kuto vivāda ityāha| ayaṃ ce tyādi veditavyāntam| ca śabdo hetau| vivādaḥ kalahaḥ| sattvān ayaṃ vinetukāmas te cāsattvāt pariharantīti kalahaḥ. Note: Ratnākara’s catchy last comment is playing on the word sattva, which means both “sentient being” and “being existent.” It has been taught by the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata that a bodhisattva great being’s freedom from any mental perception of sentient beings is the absolute nature of everything. So this freedom from any mental perception should also be understood as the voidness of sentient beings. And the voidness of bodhisattvas should simply be understood as the voidness of the disciples to be tamed.According to Ratnākara (F.177.a), regarding “the disciples to be tamed” and so on, since there are no disciples to be tamed, there is likewise also no one taming them. So, due to the nonexistence of both tamer and tamed, the (bodhisattva undertakes deeds that are) the most extremely difficult. In this way, bodhisattvas should see the sameness of all beings and themselves, since this would enable them to work for the benefit of themselves and others. But he says the next sentence to address how one can be aware while going about life in this. RK: vainayike tyādi| yathā ca vineyā na santi tathā vinayitāpi| tata ubhayāsattayā sutarāṃ paramaduṣkaratā| evam iyaṃ bodhisattvena sarvasattvātmasamatā draṣṭavyā yataḥ śaknuyāt svaparārthau kartum| sa punar asyāṃ caran kathaṃ jñeya ityāha. Note: In line with Ratnākara’s comments about this line, the translation “voidness of bodhisattvas” is based on the Tibetan byang chub sems dpa’ dben par (*bodhisattvaviviktatā), instead of on the Sanskrit edition’s typo “voidness of beings” (sattvaviviktatā). If a bodhisattva great being is not disheartened by what is being taught in this way, then, gods, one can know that this bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.177.a), in this sentence “not disheartened” means not afflicted. This means he or she is extremely cheerful. RK: sa ced ityādi| na saṃsīdatī ti na khidyate sutarāṃ prīyata ity arthaḥ. How so?According to Ratnākara (F.177.a), “How so?” asks the question what the cause of his or her delight (i.e., being the opposite of disheartened) is in going about life in this transcendent state of wisdom. This question implies that there is no cause because the bodhisattva sees the absence of any thing’s identity. The response (to this question) is the following sentence. RK: tatkasya hetor iti| mattasya prajñāpāramitāyāṃ caraṇaṃ kasya hetor naiva kasyacit| dharmanairātmyādarśanād iti bhāvaḥ| ata uttaraṃ sattvaviviktataye tyādi. Because in this way the voidness of physical forms should be known as the voidness of sentient beings. Likewise, the voidness of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness should be known as the voidness of sentient beings. In fact, everything up to the voidness of all things should be known as the voidness of sentient beings.According to Ratnākara (F.177.a), “In fact…” indicates a summary here. The antidote with all the details (described above) is that the “voidness of all things” is the same. The (seventh) conceptualization about varied results is thinking there are various beings and various dharmas, and hence the transcendent state of wisdom that has a single flavor does not exist. RK: evam ityādinā draṣṭavyāntenopasaṃhāraḥ| viviktatā hi sarvadharmā ṇāṃ samateti vistareṇa pratipakṣaḥ| nānā sattvā nānā dharmās tato nāsty ekarasā prajñāpāramiteti nānātvavikalpaḥ. In this way, gods, a bodhisattva great being should see the voidness of all things. And thus, gods, a bodhisattva great being is not disheartened when the voidness of all things is being described.According to Ratnākara (F.177.a), “is being described” means taught just now by me. The bodhisattva’s not being disheartened is connected to when the “voidness of all things” is taught, i.e., at that time he or she is going about life (in that understanding). (And when doing so), a bodhisattva is not confused about going or staying‍—thinking “Where should I go?” or “Where should I stay?”‍—because, by knowing the sameness of all things, he or she is unconfused about (there being) two transcendent states of wisdom. That is to say, a bodhisattva would (always just) go where awakening is, and stay in awakening. Going and staying like this involves the independent establishment of cause and result of the very same transcendent state of wisdom. So, where the transcendent state of wisdom goes or stays is exactly the same place where the other five transcendent states‍—who are dedicated to Her‍—go or stay. It is like, for example, wherever the precious wheel of a universal emperor goes or stays, all his armed guards go there too‍—considering this is the antidote. But the (eighth) conceptualization is about the confusion of going or staying, i.e., the uncertainty about going or staying due to the fact that the five transcendent states are not discerned and the sixth does not have any appearance. RK: evaṃ devaputrā ityādi| evaṃ bhāṣyamāṇāyām iti| evam idānīṃ mayā deśyamānāyāṃ sarva­dharma­viviktatāyām iti caryākāle deśyamānāyāṃ| bodhisattvo na saṃsīdatī ti sambandhaḥ| na saṃsīdatī ti sthānaprasthānayor na muhyati| kena gantavyaṃ kva vā sthātavyam iti| tayaiva sarva­dharma­samatāsaṃvedinyā prajñāpāramitayor asaṃmohāt| tathāhi| yena bodhis tena gantavyaṃ bodhau sthātavyam| te ca gatisthitī tasyā eva svādhīne hetuphalāvasthe| tato yena sā gacchati yatra vā tiṣṭhati tatpariṇāmitā api pañca pāramitās tenaiva gacchanti tatraiva tiṣṭhanti| tadyathā cakravartinaś cakraratnaṃ yena gacchati yatra vā tiṣṭhati sarvo balakāyas tenaiva gacchati tatraiva tiṣṭhatīti pratipakṣaḥ| avijñāḥ pañcapāramitāḥ ṣaṣṭhī nirābhāsā| tataḥ sthānaprasthānayor aniścayaḥ sthāna­prasthānasammoha­vikalpaḥ. Since the bodhisattva great being is not disheartened, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.177.b), it is, for example, like the precious wheel of a universal emperor that goes at the front while the armed guards follow behind‍—considering this is the antidote. The (ninth) conceptualization is about proceeding backward, thinking that the five transcendent states proceed ahead on account of their having a noble objective, while the sixth one goes behind, on account of its subtlety. This ends the section that teaches the second group of nine conceptualizations about the grasped objects (of spiritual disengagement). RK: tadyathā cakravartinaś cakraratnam agrato gacchati paścād balakāya iti pratipakṣaḥ| udāraviṣayatvāt pañcapāramitāḥ prāk pravartante| sūkṣmaviṣayatvāt pṛṣṭhataḥ ṣaṣṭhīti pṛṣṭhato gamanavikalpaḥ|| ity ukto dvitīyo grāhyavikalpo navavidhaḥ.

Then the Blessed One, who was already aware of this exchange, asked the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, what is the reason that a bodhisattva great being is not disheartened when the voidness of all things is being taught?”

Subhūti said, “Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being is not disheartened because of voidness. For this reason, Blessed One, when the voidness of all things is being taught, he or she is not disheartened, nor is a thing disheartened.According to Ratnākara (F.177.b), the next nine kinds (of conceptualizations, those belonging to the third group of 36 as explained in note 1198) are taught in the long Transcendent State of Wisdom scriptures (see below). But here in the Eight Thousand, he explains (only) generally the nine conceptualizations about (really existent) grasping subjects together with their antidotes in the short section before the word “nor.” It says “already aware” because there is nothing that the Blessed One is not aware of. “A bodhisattva great being” refers here to the form of a really existent person according to worldly convention. The conceptualization about a really existent grasping subject concerns the cause of why he or she is “not disheartened.” The antidote to this is the freedom from mental perception. He explains this with the words “because of voidness,” i.e., because that does not exist. Since he himself is nonexistent, why be “disheartened”? RK: amī nava prabhedā mahatyor bhagavatyor uktāḥ| asyāṃ tu sāmānyena dravyasadgrāhakavikalpaṃ sapratipakṣam āha| atha khalv ityādinā nāpī tyataḥ prāk| jānanneve ti na hi bhagavataḥ kiñcid ajñātam asti| bodhisattvo mahāsattva iti lokaprasiddhito dravyasatpudgalarūpaḥ, sa kena kāraṇena na saṃsīdatī ti dravyasadgrāhaka­vikalpaḥ| tasya pratipakṣo ’nupalambhaḥ| tam evāha| viviktatvādityā dinā| asattvād ity arthaḥ| sa hi svayam asat kutaḥ saṃsīdatī ti. Note: This brief section includes all nine nominally existent grasping subjects, which Ratnākara comments about in reference to the longer Transcendent State of Wisdom scriptures. Those comments are not included here, since they do not help readers understand this specific text. This group of nine conceptualizations is covered in the Twenty-Five Thousand, 53.12–26. Why is that? Because, Blessed One, a bodhisattva great being does not mentally perceive a thing able to be disheartened.According to Ratnākara (F.177.b), the next nine kinds (the fourth group) of (thirty-six) conceptualizations (as explained in note 1198) are taught in the long Transcendent State of Wisdom scriptures (in the Twenty-Five Thousand, they are covered in the passage 53.55–68). But here in the Eight Thousand, beginning after the word “nor,” he explains (only) generally the nine conceptualizations about (nominally existent) grasping subjects together with their antidotes. The words “a thing” refer to the constituents of experience that are the scope for the designation of a self. He is not disheartened because he himself does not exist. RK: ete ’pi na va prabhedāḥ mahatyor bhagavatyor uktāḥ| asyāṃ tu sāmānyenaiva prajñapti­saṃgrāhaka­vikalpaṃ sapratipakṣam āha| nāpī tyādinā api tu khalv ity ataḥ prāk| kaścid dharma iti ātmaprajñaptiviṣayaḥ skandhādiḥ| na saṃsīdatī ti svayam asattvāt. Nor, Blessed One, does he or she mentally perceive a thing by which any thing could be disheartened.”According to Ratnākara (F.177.b), “a thing by which” refers to a mindset by which; “any thing” refers to a bodhisattva. RK: ata evāha| tatkasye tyādi| kaścid dharma iti skandhādi cittaṃ vā| so pī tyādi| yena dharmeṇe ti cittena yo dharma iti bodhisattvaḥ.

“Yes, Subhūti, that is true,”According to Ratnākara (F.177.b), “true” and so on indicates his acceptance of this. This much teaches the antidote: Don’t be a really existent being! This is (connected with) the fourth conceptualization about lumping together designations, thinking that the constituents of experience and so on are the objective scope of ego (ahaṃkāra) or the mindset is the bodhisattva. Likewise, (with the word “true) the Blessed One teaches the transcendent state of wisdom to be the antidote to (all) four kinds of conceptualizations. RK: evam etad ityādir abhyupagamaḥ| iyatā pratipakṣa uktaḥ| mā bhūd dravyasat sattvaḥ| ahaṃkāraviṣayaḥ skandhādiś cittaṃ vā bodhisattva iti prajñapti­saṃgrāhaka­vikalpaś caturthaḥ|| evaṃ bhagavatā catur­vidhāvikalpa­prati­pakṣa­bhūtā bhagavatī vistareṇa deśitā. Note: Although Hari finds a few places below to connect with the two kinds of conceptualizations about the grasping subject (the third and fourth groups of nine), Ratnākara explains them only in relation to the long Transcendent State of Wisdom scriptures. Ratnākara’s comments have not been translated here, because they concern the Ornament of Clear Realization rather than this text per se. said the Blessed One. “Nevertheless, when this is being described, taught, explicated, or expounded in this way, if a bodhisattva great being is not disheartened, dejected, sunk in despondency, deflated, or depressed, and if his or her mental attitude does not become discouraged, shattered, frightened, terrified, or stricken with terror, then he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.177.b), he explains here how to know a bodhisattva. When it is “described” based on the text; “taught” based on the meaning; “explicated” based on explaining it entirely; and “expounded” based on telling the secret meaning, then a bodhisattva great being is not “disheartened,” meaning slowed down. Nor is he or she “dejected” due to fatigue; “sunk in despondency” due to constant fatigue; “deflated” due a mindset with a sinking feeling; “depressed” due to that sinking feeling being constant; “discouraged” due to turning away; “shattered” due to not facing it; “frightened” due to facing toward the fear; “terrified” because they are completely afraid; or “stricken with terror” in constant fear. One can be certain that “he or she goes about life in this transcendent state of wisdom” because a bodhisattva not going about in it would be automatically disheartened and so on. RK: bodhisattvas tu tasyāṃ caran yathā jñeyas tad āha| api tu khalv ityādi| bhāṣyamāṇe granthataḥ| deśyamāne arthataḥ| nirdeśyamāne nirviśeṣaṃ kathanāt| upadiśyamāne rahasyakathanāt| na saṃsīdati na mandībhavati| na viṣīdati khedāt| na viṣādam āpadyate santatakhedāt| nāvalīyate cittanamanāt| na saṃlīyate santataṃ tannamanāt| vipṛṣṭhaṃ vaimukhyāt| bhagnapṛṣṭhaṃ punar asāṃmukhyāt| nottrasyati trāsonmukhatvāt| na saṃtrasyati samyak trāsāt| nainam āpadyate sātatyena| tadā veditavyaṃ caraty asyāṃ prajñāpāramitāyām iti| asyām acarataḥ saṃsīdanādīnām avaśyaṃ bhāvāt.

“So it is, Blessed One. So it is, Sugata,” replied Subhūti. “If a bodhisattva great being goes about life like this, he or she goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. All the gods‍—including Indra, Brahmā, the earth lords, and Īśāna‍—and all the sages, together with their male and female followers, will pay homage from a distance with their hands cupped in respect to any bodhisattva great being going about life in this way.”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, not only will all the gods‍—including Indra, Brahmā, the earth lords, and Īśāna‍—and all the sages, together with their male and female followers, pay homage from a distance with their hands cupped in respect to any bodhisattva great being going about life in this way. But also, Subhūti, the gods of the Brahmakāyika, Brahmapurohita, and Brahmapārṣadyā heavens, and the gods of the heavens of Mahābrahma, Parīttābha, Apramāṇābha, Ābhāsvara, Parīttaśubha, Apramāṇaśubha, Śubhakṛtsna, Anabhraka, Puṇyaprasava, Bṛhatphala, Asaṃjñisattva, Avṛha, Atapa, Sudṛśa, Sudarśana, and Akaniṣṭha‍, all will pay homage from a distance with their hands cupped in respect to the bodhisattva great being going about life in this way. Also, Subhūti, those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who presently reside, stay, and spend time as blessed buddhas in immeasurable and innumerable world systems will look with their buddha eyes upon the bodhisattva great being going about life in this way. They will offer their support and keep in mind any bodhisattva great being going about life in this way.According to Ratnākara (F.180.a), beginning with this sentence, he expresses abundant praises for someone “going about life” in this way. They will “support” a bodhisattva through a particular pledge (of their resources) and “keep in mind” through remembering him or her. These (words) should also be the blessed buddhas teaching him or her the Dharma (mentioned below). RK: evam ityādinā| asyāṃ carato ’nuśaṃsātiśayān āha| anugṛṇhanti viśeṣādhānataḥ| samanvāharanti smaraṇataḥ| te ’pi tasya buddhā bhagavanto dharma deśayantī ti sambandhaḥ. Nonetheless, Subhūti, one should remember that when a bodhisattva great being beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening is supported and kept in mind by the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, then neither Māra nor anyone else can create obstacles for him or her. Why is that? Subhūti, suppose that all the sentient beings in a billionfold, great order world system were to become pernicious Māras. And, Subhūti, suppose also that each pernicious Māra magically created the same number of armies of Māras. Subhūti, even these pernicious Māras would not be able to create an obstacle to the unsurpassed, perfect awakening for a bodhisattva great being who goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, because he or she is kept in mind by these buddhas. So, Subhūti, set aside all the sentient beings in one billionfold, great order world system being pernicious Māras. Suppose that all the sentient beings in all the billionfold, great order world systems equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges were to become Māras. Even this number of Māras would not be able to create any obstacle to the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of a bodhisattva great being who goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, because he or she is kept in mind by the buddhas.

“Subhūti, there are two qualities that make a bodhisattva great being unassailable by the pernicious Māra or his demon cohort. What are these two? To be precise, these qualities are that he or she does not abandon any sentient beings, and that he or she regards all things from the perspective of emptiness. Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being endowed with these two qualities is unassailable by the pernicious Māra or his demon cohort.

“Subhūti, there are two other qualities that make a bodhisattva great being unassailable by the pernicious Māra or his demon cohort. What are these two? To be precise, these qualities are that a bodhisattva great being does as he or she says, and that he or she is kept in mind by the blessed buddhas. Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being endowed with these two other qualities is unassailable by the pernicious Māra or his demon cohort.

“Subhūti, even the gods will think that they should get closer to a bodhisattva great being going about life in this way. When they do, they will want to ask questions, fully pursue their questions with him or her, and serve him or her. They will also encourage that bodhisattva great being, saying, ‘Noble son or daughter, you will quickly realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Therefore, noble son or daughter, you should abide in this very state, this transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? Because, noble son or daughter, if you abide in this very state, you will become a protector for those sentient beings without a protector; a shelter for those without shelter; a refuge for those without a refuge; a sanctuary for those without a sanctuary; an ultimate resort for those without an ultimate resort; an island for those without an island; a light for those in darkness; a leader for those without a leader; a resting place for those without rest; and a reference point and guide along the path for those without any reference point who have lost their way.’ Saying things like this, those gods will encourage him or her. For what reason? It is because the blessed buddhas presently residing, staying, and spending time in immeasurable and innumerable world systems, surrounded by their saṅgha of monks and attended by their community of bodhisattvas, will teach the Dharma to such a bodhisattva great being abiding in this transcendent state of wisdom. They will also utter proclamations by announcing the names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms of those bodhisattva great beings who abide in the transcendent state of wisdom and go about life with these kinds of positive qualities, namely, the positive qualities of abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.180.a), “names” means titles (or epithets); “familial lineages” means having descended from Garga and so on; “powers” means physical and intellectual abilities; “good qualities” means other qualities remaining (i.e., unmentioned); and “forms” is about one’s physical appearance. When “announcing” all these, the nature of them will be excellent beyond excellent. He will “utter,” i.e., express, verses to inspire others about their qualities. In the following, he describes whose qualities. RK: nāma cetyādi| nāma saṃjñā| gotraṃ gārgyādi| balaṃ kāyabalaṃ buddhibalaṃ ca| varṇaḥ pariśiṣṭā guṇāḥ| rūpaṃ varṇasaṃsthāne| etāni parikīrtayamānāḥ prakarṣeṇa prakarṣarūpam| udānam iti guṇaharṣodbhavāṃ gāthāṃ udānayanty udāharanti| kasya guṇā ityāha tasyetyādi.

“Subhūti, it is just as, for example, the way that I presently teach the Dharma and utter proclamations, announcing the names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms of the bodhisattva great being Ratnaketu, the bodhisattva great being Śikhin, and of other bodhisattva great beings who are presently going about their lives engaged in spiritual practice in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Akṣobhya. Likewise, Subhūti, those blessed buddhas are also now teaching the Dharma and uttering proclamations by announcing the names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms of those bodhisattva great beings in my buddhafield who are presently going about their lives engaged in spiritual practice and abiding in this transcendent state of wisdom.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, are those blessed buddhas teaching the Dharma and uttering proclamations by announcing the names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms of absolutely all bodhisattva great beings?”

The Blessed One said, “No, Subhūti, those blessed buddhas are not teaching the Dharma and uttering proclamations by announcing the names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms of all bodhisattva great beings. Rather, those blessed buddhas are teaching the Dharma and uttering proclamations by announcing the names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms of only those bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing and devoid of all attachments.”RK: sarvasaṃgāḥ sarve ’bhūtopalambhāḥ.

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, aside from any bodhisattva great being beyond regressing, are there any other bodhisattva great beings to whom those blessed buddhas teach the Dharma and about whom they utter proclamations by announcing their names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, aside from the bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing, the blessed buddhas are teaching the Dharma to those persons on the bodhisattva path with certain abilities from helpful external factors.The technical term translated here as “abilities from helpful external factors” (pratipakṣabalin; gnyen po’i stobs) concerns certain bodhisattvas who gain the specific “ability” to travel to buddhafields to hear the teachings and so on “from helpful external factors” like spiritual friends, worldly psychic abilities, or magical powers. These bodhisattvas are not realized themselves, and hence do not have their own power‍—which automatically comes with realization‍—to travel to buddhafields without the help of external factors. This term is related to “power of applying the antidotes” (gnyen po kun tu spyod pa’i stobs) and explains why, in general, one can experience healing benefits from applying certain practices as antidotes to one’s unhealthy mindsets. The blessed buddhas are also uttering proclamations by announcing their names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms. But, who are they? Subhūti, they are persons on the bodhisattva path presently performing the practices of a bodhisattva and abiding by training in the bodhisattva’s way of going about life in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Akṣobhya. Subhūti, they are also other persons on the bodhisattva path presently performing the practices of a bodhisattva and abiding by training in the bodhisattva’s way of going about life in the presence of the bodhisattva Ratnaketu. So, aside from those bodhisattva great beings beyond regressing, the blessed buddhas are teaching them the Dharma and uttering proclamations by announcing their names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms.According to Hari (856), in the first clarifying sentence, the Blessed One mentions “the buddhafield of Akṣobhya” because fully purified mindstreams very often appear in his buddhafield. He also specifies other persons in the second sentence, where the mention of “the bodhisattva Ratnaketu” should be understood as a synecdoche for all bodhisattvas of a similar type to him. The general meaning of this paragraph is as follows: Aside from the bodhisattvas beyond regressing, there are (other) bodhisattvas living in the buddhafields of Akṣobhya and so on, whose names and so on are being announced. These bodhisattvas have gained this magical ability due to their parting with the conceptualizations that obstruct traveling anywhere they wish. This mastery is gained, somehow or other, previous to the Unmoving level (i.e., the eighth bhūmi where bodhisattvas can travel anywhere) through the power of external aids like, for instance, a particular virtuous friend and so on. Hari: pratipādayann āha: ya etarhītyādi. akṣobhyasye ti vacanaṃ bāhulyena tadbuddhakṣetre pariśuddha­saṃtatīnām utpādāt. anyān apy atideśayann āha : ye ’pi te subūta ityādi. ratna­ketu­bodhi­sattva­grahaṇaṃ tat­samānajātīya­bodhi­sattvopalakṣaṇa­paraṃ jñeyaṃ. prati­viśiṣṭakalyāṇa­mitrādi­pari­graha­balenācalāyā bhūmer api prāk kathaṃcid yathecchagamana-vyāghātavikalpavirahād ṛddhivaśitāṃ prāptā ye ’kṣobhyādibuddhakṣetre bodhisattvā viharanti te ’vinivartanīyān sthāpayitvā nāmādikīrtanaviṣayā vidyanta ity arthaḥ. Note: For the sake of clarity, the translation eliminates one long sentence repeating the qualification about buddhas teaching and uttering proclamations, etc.

“Also, beyond just those, Subhūti, there are bodhisattva great beings going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom who, despite not yet having reached the stage of accepting that all things are free of arising, have the firm conviction that all things are free of arising. There are also bodhisattva great beings who, despite not having advanced to the level beyond regressing where they attain mastery over everything, have firm conviction in all things subsiding.According to Hari (857), based on their motivation to follow their stated intention to reach the state that is foremost for any sentient being and so on, they “have firm conviction”‍—i.e., they keep in mind that all things are “free of arising” or “subsiding” (i.e., being empty).
