But they have “not yet” achieved the very advanced “stage of accepting all things to be free of arising” or the “level beyond regressing where they attain mastery.” This demonstrates their absence of conceptualization in exiting cyclic existence (to various buddhafields) based on a stated intention (uddeśa). Hari: adhimuñcantī ti sarvasattvāgratādyuddeśagamanābhiprāyeṇānutpattikāḥ. śāntā iti vā sarva dharmān manasikurvanti. na ca tāvat prakarṣavatīm anutpattika­dharmakṣāntim avinivartanīya­vaśitāprāptiṃ cādhigatā bhavantīty evam uddeśāniryāṇa­vikalpābhāvo darśitaḥ. Subhūti, the blessed buddhas also are uttering proclamations by announcing the names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms of those bodhisattva great beings abiding even in these states.

“However, Subhūti, those blessed buddhas are teaching the Dharma just to those who have eschewed the levels of disciples and solitary realizers in favor of only attaining the level of a buddha, so that they too will be foreordained for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Those blessed buddhas are also uttering proclamations by announcing the names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms of those bodhisattva great beings. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, the blessed buddhas are teaching the Dharma and uttering proclamations by announcing the names, familial lineages, powers, good qualities, and forms of those bodhisattvas going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom in that way.

“Furthermore, Subhūti, once those bodhisattva great beings listen to this profound transcendent state of wisdom being taught, they will have firm conviction and not become confused, worried, or doubtful. They will listen fully with firm conviction, thinking, ‘It is exactly the way the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata is teaching.’ Also, they will develop the proper mindset, thinking, ‘We should listen fully to this transcendent state of wisdom in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Akṣobhya.’ Then, they will listen to this profound transcendent state of wisdom while going about life engaged in spiritual practice in the buddhafield of Akṣobhya, surrounded by persons on the bodhisattva path, and they will have firm conviction in it. Because of their firm conviction in this transcendent state of wisdom, those bodhisattva great beings will also have firm conviction in the way that the Tathāgata taught it, and they will reside in the state beyond regressing. Subhūti, I am saying that even just listening to the transcendent state of wisdom is extremely helpful—how much more so is having firm conviction in it! With such firm conviction, those bodhisattva great beings will adhere to the actual nature and progress along the path toward the actual nature. After adhering to the actual nature and progressing along the path toward it, they will come to reside in that actual nature. After residing in the actual nature and the state of complete knowing, they will teach the Dharma.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, if one does not mentally perceive any thing other than the actual nature, then, Blessed One, what thing will reside in the actual nature? What is it that will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening? And what is it that will teach this Dharma?”According to Ratnākara (F.180.b), “one does not mentally perceive any thing” because various types of imagined things do not appear. He says “other than the actual nature” because only that very actual nature makes itself known. (tathatāvinirmukto), RK: tathatāvinirmukta iti| tathatāmātrasyeva prakhyānāt| parikalpitasya nānāvidhasyāpratibhāsanāt| na kaścid dharma upalabhyate.

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, what you asked is this: ‘If one does not mentally perceive any thing other than the actual nature, then what thing will reside in the actual nature? What is it that will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening? And what is it that will teach this Dharma?’ Subhūti, apart from the actual nature, one mentally perceives no other thing that will reside in the actual nature. First of all, Subhūti, since one does not mentally perceive the actual nature itself, how much less so someone residing in that actual nature. Next, Subhūti, the actual nature does not realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Subhūti, one does not even mentally perceive any thing that has realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening, is realizing it, or will realize it. Finally, Subhūti, the actual nature does not teach the Dharma. Subhūti, one does not mentally perceive even a thing that could teach it.”

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, the transcendent state of wisdom is profound. Blessed One, the bodhisattva great being performs difficult noble deeds wishing for unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Why is that? Because, Blessed One, no thing, in fact, resides in the actual nature, nor does any thing realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, nor does anyone teach the Dharma. And yet, a bodhisattva great being does not become deflated, worried, or confused about this.”

Then the venerable Subhūti said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, what you said is that the bodhisattva great beings who perform difficult noble deeds are not worried or confused when these profound teachings are being taught. Since all things are empty, Kauśika, who is it that becomes worried or confused about them?”

Śakra said, “Anything whatsoever that the noble Subhūti explicates is about emptiness. He never gets caught up in anything.According to Ratnākara (F.180.b), “caught up” means distressed by. RK: sajjatī ti vihanyata ity arthaḥ. It is just, as an analogy, like an arrow shot into midair; it does not get caught up in anything. In this very same way, the noble Subhūti’s explication of the Dharma does not get caught up in anything.”

From this point in the chapter, the content corresponds to the initial passages of Chapter 50 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (50.1–5) and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 60 in the Eighteen Thousand (60.1–7).Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the Blessed One, “Blessed One, in light of the way the noble Subhūti is speaking and explicating, I hope that I may become someone who presents what has been taught by the Tathāgata and speaks the truth, and that I may reveal whatever is conducive to the Dharma.”According to Ratnākara (F.180.b–81.a), “in the light of” means in consideration of; “speaks the truth” means speaks correctly. “I explain,” i.e., communicate, “whatever is conducive,” i.e., a path that is conducive. “The Dharma” here means the nirvāṇa of a buddha (rather than that of a worthy one). RK: ārabhye ti adhikṛtya| dharmavādī ti yuktivādī| dharmasye ti buddhanirvāṇasya| anudharmam anukūlaṃ mārgaḥ| vyākurvan ācakṣāṇaḥ| vyākaromy ācakṣe.

At this, the Blessed One said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, “Kauśika, what you are saying is so. It is so, Kauśika, it is so. You are presenting what has been taught by the Tathāgata and speaking the truth. You are revealing what is conducive to the Dharma. Why is that? It is because anything that the noble Subhūti illuminates, he illuminates about emptiness. Why is that? Because, Kauśika, the elder Subhūti neither sees nor identifies even so much as the transcendent state of wisdom‍—how much less so one who goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom? He does not mentally perceive even so much as awakening‍—how much less so one who will realize awakening? He does not mentally perceive even so much as the state of complete knowing‍—how much less so one who will attain the state of complete knowing? He does not mentally perceive even so much as the actual nature‍—how much less so one who will become a tathāgata? He does not mentally perceive even so much as the freedom from arising‍—how much less so one who will actualize freedom from arising? He does not mentally perceive even so much as a bodhisattva‍—how much less so one who will realize awakening? He does not mentally perceive even so much as the powers‍—how much less so one who will become equipped with the powers? He does not mentally perceive even so much as the states of self-confidence‍—how much less so one who will become self-confident? He does not mentally perceive even so much as the Dharma‍—how much less so one who will teach the Dharma? This is because, Kauśika, the elder Subhūti is abiding in the state void of all things and free of mental conceptions about all things.According to Ratnākara (F.181.a), it is the “state”‍—i.e., meditative concentration‍—on all things being “void,” i.e., voidness or emptiness. And it is the “state”‍—i.e., meditative concentration‍—on all things being “free from mental perception.” RK: sarvadharmā ṇāṃ viviktaṃ vivekaḥ śūnyatā| tasmin vihāraḥ samādhiḥ| sarvadharmā ṇāṃ anupalambhaḥ| tasmin vihāraḥ. But, Kauśika, the elder Subhūti’s state devoid of all things and free of mental conceptions of them does not come close in worth to one hundredth, one thousandth, one hundred thousandth, one ten millionth, one billionth, one ten billionth, one trillionth, or even one ten trillionth, Kauśika, of the state of a bodhisattva great being going about life and abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.181.a), here the Blessed One is demonstrating that a bodhisattva’s state is far superior to Subhūti’s. RK: yaḥ khalu punar ityādinā subhūter vihārād bodhisattvavihārasyotkṛṣtatvaṃ darśayati. Nor would any calculation, any fraction, any quantification, any metaphor, any analogy, any equivalence, or any comparison even be adequate for comparing them. This is because, leaving aside the Tathāgata, Kauśika, the state of a bodhisattva great being going about life and abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom surpasses all other states.According to Ratnākara (F.181.a), here “going about life” means going about in the actualization of the meditative concentration, and “abiding” means in the meditative concentration actualized. The rest is easy to understand, whereas what was explained right before was difficult to understand. RK: carataḥ samādhiniṣpattaye| viharato niṣpannena samādhinā| śeṣaṃ subodham| durbodhaṃ tu prāg eva vyākhyātam. Kauśika, this state is called the foremost, the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled. This state of a bodhisattva great being going about life and abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom surpasses the states of the disciples and solitary realizers. Therefore, Kauśika, any son or daughter who wishes to reach the foremost, the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled should abide in this state of the bodhisattva great being going about life and abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Essence,” the twenty-seventh chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 28: AvakīrṇakusumaAccording to Ratnākara (F.193.b), in this chapter the bodhisattvas named Avakīrṇakusuma are foreordained for awakening. So “The Chapter on Avakīrṇakusuma” (lit. “Showered Blossoms”) is a synecdochic title for those (bodhisattvas who are foreordained in the chapter receiving this name). RK: avakīrṇakusuma nāmāno bodhisattvā iha parivarte bodhau vyākṛtāḥ| atas tair upalakṣitaḥ parivartas tat parivarta ḥ. Note: The meaning of the proper name Avakīrṇakusuma is central to understanding the chapter and also the name is applied within the chapter to multiple individuals, almost as an epithet.The beginning of this chapter corresponds to a point within Chapter 50 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (50.6) and Hundred Thousand, and in Chapter 60 in the Eighteen Thousand, (60.8) and its content corresponds to the remainder of those chapters. In addition, from the point marked by note 1286, its content corresponds to that of Chapter 51 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 61 in the Eighteen Thousand.
div.p: Then, at that time, one of the gods together with the gods from the Heaven of the Thirty-Three gathered a magnificent array of heavenly māndārava flowers and then approached the Blessed One.Here the text literally states “māndārava flowers and large (or great) māndārava flowers” (māndāravair mahāmāndāravaiś ca). But since the idea is that these flowers from the heavenly realms are rained down as blessings by gods, like small and large snowflakes, these two terms have been translated throughout as a “magnificent array of māndārava flowers” or just “māndārava flowers.” At that same time, six hundred monks had arrived at that very assembly and taken their seats. They stood up from their seats, put their upper robes over one shoulder, placed their right knees on the earth, and then prostrated to the Blessed One with their hands cupped together in reverence. Through the Buddha’s miraculous power, those monks’ hands, outstretched and cupped together in reverence, were completely filled with the magificent heavenly māndārava flower blossoms. The monks showered and scattered magnificent heavenly māndārava flower blossoms upon the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata and spoke these words: “Blessed One, we will go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Blessed One, we will abide in this supreme state, the transcendent state of wisdom.”According to Ratnākara (F.181.a), the two immediately preceding chapters‍—i.e., “Chapter on Magical Illusion” and “Chapter on the Essence”‍—taught (a) the conceptualizations to be abandoned through seeing (as opposed to through cultivation) and (b) the fact that abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom on the path of seeing and so on automatically leads to awakening. Now this chapter teaches, firstly, the three causes that lead a bodhisattva to being (awake) and, subsequently, the defining characteristics of awakening. RK: māyopamasāraparivartābhyāṃ darśanaheyā vikalpā uktāḥ| darśanamārgādeś ca prajñāpāramitāvihārasyāvaśyakaṃ bodhiprāpakatvam uktam| tatas tasya tathābhāve kāraṇatrayam anena parivartena prathamaṃ vakṣyati paścād bodhilakṣaṇam. Note: Ratnākara continues here explaining the Ornament’s introduction of the three causes: (1) foreordainment that points others to the awakening, (2) the entrustment (of the scripture) to noble Ānanda in order to serve as a cause for awakening in future times, and (3) the defining characteristic of having an abundance of merit. These three causes are the overall outline for the first part of the chapter.

Then, at that moment, the Blessed One manifested a smile. But, as is the true nature of blessed buddhas when they manifest a smile, a multicolored variety of light rays‍—blue, yellow, red, white, bright red, clear, silver, and gold‍—emerged from the mouth of the Blessed One and radiated to infinite and limitless world systems. After having ascended as far as the Brahmā world, the light rays returned once again, circled to the right three times around the Blessed One, and then disappeared into the Blessed One’s forehead.

Then the venerable Ānanda stood up from his seat, put his upper robe over one shoulder, placed his right knee on the earth, prostrated to the Blessed One with his hands cupped together in reverence, and said, “Blessed One, given that worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas do not manifest smiles without cause or conditions, what is the cause and what are the conditions for your manifesting this smile?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Ānanda, “Ānanda, in a future time, during the Star-Like eon, these six hundred monks will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and once awakened, will teach the Dharma. And they all will have the same name, Avakīrṇakusuma.According to Ratnākara (F.181.b), all the monks are to be foreordained (as future buddhas) with the name Avakīrṇakusuma, which means “Showered Blossoms.” That is because the flowers brought by the gods were “showered” (on the Blessed One) by those (monks) to be foreordained. As a result, the (foreordainment) procedure and radiation of light rays took place, and the Blessed One, once asked by Ānanda, foreordained them. (Hence, they will be called Showered Blossoms because they showered the blossoms as an offering to the Blessed One). Their foreordainment is characterized as being for a particular world system elsewhere, because (the name of) the world system is not specified. RK: avakīrṇakusumanāmāno vyākartavyāḥ| tato devaiḥ puṣpam ānītaṃ vyākartavyair avakīrṇaṃ tato raśmīnāṃ niścārapraveśau| ānanda pṛṣṭena bhagavatā teṣāṃ vyākaraṇam| tat teṣāṃ vyākaraṇam anyasmin viśiṣṭe lokadhātāv iti lakṣyate lokadhātor anirdeśāt. As worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas, they will be teachers in the world. Also, Ānanda, all the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas with the name Avakīrṇakusuma will have the same saṅgha of disciples. And all the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas with the name Avakīrṇakusuma will have the exact same twenty-thousand-eon lifespan, and the extensive teachings given by each and every one of them will be received far and wide among gods and humans, so that the genuine Dharma of absolutely all of them will remain for an equal time period‍—each for exactly twenty thousand eons.Hari gives an extensive explanation of the cosmological reason why their lives and teachings are limited to the twenty-thousand-year time span. For those who are interested, see Sparham vol. 4, p. 202. Also, a shower of flowers with five-colored blossoms will appear in all the villages, cities, towns, communities, kingdoms, and principalities, from which they all depart for the ascetic life and then set in motion the Dharma wheel, and also in those places where they subsequently enter, abide, or depart whenever they are entering, abiding, or departing there.

“Therefore, Ānanda, those bodhisattva great beings who wish to abide in this final state should abide in the transcendent state of wisdom. That is to say, Ānanda, those who wish to abide in the state of a tathāgata should abide in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.181.b), following that (foreordainment), the word “therefore” begins a brief summary that marks the end of the first cause of attaining awakening by means of abiding in the accomplished state. RK: tadanu tasmāt tarhī tyādinā upasaṃhāro vihartavyā ntaḥ| iti prakṛtena vihāreṇa bodhiprāpteḥ prathamaṃ kāraṇam. For, Ānanda, one can conclude that to be born here among humans, those bodhisattva great beings who will go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom will have died in their previous life either in the human realm or else in the Tuṣita heaven. Why there? Because this transcendent state of wisdom will spread widely among humans and among Tuṣita gods. Ānanda, if bodhisattva great beings go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, or even just write it out‍—and in doing so help other bodhisattva great beings see it clearly, help them take on the practice of it, help them be enthusiastic about it, and help them rejoice in it‍—then one should know that these bodhisattva great beings are watched over by the tathāgatas. Ānanda, one should know that those bodhisattva great beings have planted the roots of virtue in the presence of worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. That is to say, there should be no doubt that any bodhisattva great beings who train in this transcendent state of wisdom without being stricken with fear have planted the roots of virtue to train in this transcendent state of wisdom not merely in the presence of disciples and solitary realizers, but rather in the presence of worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. Ānanda, if those bodhisattva great beings take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, write it out, and follow it, based on its meaning, contents, and methods, then one can conclude that they must previously have been face to face with the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. Also, if these bodhisattva great beings never criticize this transcendent state of wisdom, reject it, resent it, suppress it, repudiate it, dismiss it, or wish to obstruct it, then one should know that they must have performed some service to the former conquerors.According to Ratnākara (F.181.b), he is basically describing the (seven) positive qualities of those who listen to it but do not dismiss it. Among these, “criticize it” means mentally blaming it; “reject it” means mentally renouncing it; “resent it” means being angry toward it; “suppress it” means placing it somewhere hidden; “repudiate it” means alleging it to be essenceless; “dismiss it” means making false refutations of it; and “wish to obstruct it” means negligence toward it. RK: tato ye cainām ityādinā veditavyā ntena ye py enāṃ śrutvā na pratikṣipanti teṣāṃ guṇam āha| atra pratikrośa ṇaṃ mānasī nindā| prativaha naṃ manasā tyāgaḥ| pratikop anaṃ tasmai kopaḥ| pratisaṃhara ṇaṃ guptau sthāpanam| pratiṣedha naṃ niḥsāratākhyāpanam| pratikṣep o ’sadbhūtabādhakābhidhānam| tadavyavasāyaḥ saptamaḥ.

“Beyond even that, Ānanda, if bodhisattva great beings do not renounce their aspiration to attain unsurpassed, perfect awakening, then accordingly the roots of virtue that they planted in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas will not ripen to bring such bodhisattva great beings to the state of a disciple or the state of a solitary realizer, but instead, for the most part, Ānanda, to their observantly going about their lives in the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.181.b), in the passage up to this point, he has been explaining the two causes for falling to the disciples’ path, namely renouncing either the aspiration and so on, or the “observance” of them. RK: tataḥ kiñcāpī ty ādinā caritavate ty etadantena hīnayānāpatane kāraṇadvayam āha| praṇidhānā dinā visaṃvād itāṃ kṛtajñatāṃ ce ti| tad evaṃ ye hi kecid ityādinā vistareṇopodghātaṃ kṛtvā tasmāt tarhī tyādinā parīndanām āha| parīndanā samarpaṇā saivānubandhinī anuparīndanā.

“Therefore, Ānanda, I am entrusting this transcendent state of wisdom to you, and I am transmitting it to you in the form of syllables, to ensure, to a greater degree, that it be taken in, kept in mind, chanted, mastered, and passed on so that it does not disappear for a long time.According to Ratnākara (F.181.b–82.a), after having fully introduced (the first cause of the three mentioned above), he now explains (the second, namely) “entrusting you,” which means assigning (him the responsibility to remember the scripture), and “transmitting it to you,” which just means entrusting it in perpetuity. “In the form of syllables” means the grouping together of syllables. What is the purpose (for this entrustment)? He says “to ensure that it be taken in” and so on. How is he entrusting Ānanda with this responsibility? He is saying you must do this so that it does not disappear. RK: tad evaṃ ye hi kecid ityādinā vistareṇopodghātaṃ kṛtvā tasmāt tarhī tyādinā parīndanām āha| parīndanā samarpaṇā saivānubandhinī anuparīndanā | akṣarasannipātād iti| akṣarasannipāto vyañjanakāyaḥ| tamadhikṛtyeti lyap lope pañcamī| kim artham ity āha| udgrahaṇāye tyādi| kathaṃ parīndanety ata āha| yatheyaṃ nāntarddhīyete ti| yatheyam antarhitā na syāt tathā tvayā karaṇīyam ity arthaḥ. Note: Ratnākara’s comments also give a grammatical justification for why the Sanskrit form saṃnipātād is in the ablative case here. It has not been translated here since it requires too much explanation and only pertains to Sanskritists. Ānanda, if‍—excluding the transcendent state of wisdom‍—you were to take in all the Dharma teachings that you have personally been taught by me, and subsequently lose them, set them aside, and forget them, then the offense you would commit against me would not be so serious.According to Ratnākara (F.182.a), from “if…” above down to the words “brings them forth” he gives a second introductory illustration of the point, and then a second entrustment of responsibility beginning “therefore” and so on. Within this section, the word “subsequently” means later; “lose it” means cause oneself to be deprived of it; “set it aside” means quickly lose track of it; and “forget it” means (simply) because you cannot remember. RK: ata ūrdhvaṃ sa ced ityādinā āhārike ty etadantena dvitīyam upodghātaṃ kṛtvā tasmāt tarhī tyādinā dvitīyā parīndanā| atra punar eve ti paścāt| nāśayer iti hārayeḥ| śīghram ananusaraṇāt utsṛjeḥ | smartum aśakyatvāt vismareḥ. However, Ānanda, if you lose, set aside, or forget even a word or phrase connected with the transcendent state of wisdom, then you would be committing a serious offense against me, and it would not please my heart.According to Ratnākara (F.182.a), to “please” (my heart) means to win favor or to satisfy me. RK: ārādhana m ārāgaṇaṃ ca toṣaṇam.

“Also, Ānanda, if you were to take in the transcendent state of wisdom, and subsequently lose it, set it aside, or forget it, not pay tribute to it, not revere it, not respect it, not venerate it, not honor it, and not tend to it, then you would not be paying tribute to the past, present, and future blessed buddhas, not be revering them, not be respecting them, not be venerating them, not be honoring them, and not be tending to them. And, if you were to take in the transcendent state of wisdom, and subsequently lose it, set it aside, or forget it, then you would be committing a serious offense against me, and you would bring displeasure to my heart. Why is that? Ānanda, the Tathāgata has stated, ‘The transcendent state of wisdom is the mother of the past, present, and future worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. She engenders them, gives them birth, and brings them forth.’According to Ratnākara (F.182.a), their “mother” because she carries the seed (of them within); “engenders them” because she nourishes the seed (of them); and “gives them birth” because of causing them to appear. RK: mātā bījadhāraṇāt| jananī bījapuṣṭeḥ| janayitrī utpādanāt.

“Therefore, Ānanda, I am entrusting this transcendent state of wisdom to you, and transmitting it to you in the form of syllables to ensure, to a greater degree, that it does not disappear. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be taken in. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be kept in mind. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be chanted. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be mastered. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be passed on. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be taught. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be expounded. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be explained. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be recited. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be written out. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be cultivated. Ānanda, you should make sure this transcendent state of wisdom is well attended to, well kept in mind, well mastered, and well passed on. It should be well pronounced and taken in with clearly enunciated vowels, consonants, and words.According to Ratnākara (F.182.a), here “well pronounced” means read aloud clearly. RK: atra suniruktā supaṭhitā. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, conforming with the true nature of things, ‘it becomes the truth body of the past, present, and future worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas.’According to Ratnākara (F.182.a), here the word “truth” (in “truth body” or “body of truth”‍—dharmakāya) refers to this very transcendent state of wisdom. That is those buddhas’ body (i.e., the scripture embodies the buddhas and the realization is their body). RK: dharmakāyate ti prajñāpāramitaiva dharmaḥ kāya eṣāṃ. Note: There is a play on the Sanskrit words dharmatā (viz. “true nature of things” vs. the nature of the Dharma teachings) and dharmakāya (viz. “truth body”, “embodiment of truth,” or body of Dharma teachings). In a different play on the word dharmakāya elsewhere (see 4.2 and n.339-n.342), he said, “The blessed buddhas are the bodies of the truth. But, monks, do not see this real body as that body. Instead, monks, see me through actualizing the body of truth (dharmakāya).”

“Ānanda, just as you think, out of your appreciation, devotion, goodness, and your wish for my welfare, to serve me, give me offerings, and keep me in mind, since I am the Tathāgata who is residing, existing, and abiding here presently, just so, out of your appreciation, devotion, helpfulness, and contentment, you should take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, write it out, cultivate it, pay tribute to it, revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and care for it.According to Ratnākara (F.182.a), “serve” means attend closely; “give offerings” (refers to offerings) such as flowers; “keep in mind” (refers to keeping in mind) the teaching and so on; “helpfulness” means longing to (earn) merit; “contentment” means being happy with that alone; and “goodness” means virtues and so on. RK: tatra kartavyam upasthānādi dātavyaṃ puṣpādi samanvāhartavyaṃ deśanādi| kalyāṇata iti puṇyakāmatayā| sparśavihārata iti tenaiva sukhībhāvāt| guṇavattaye ti kalyāṇādinā. Note: The translation “my welfare” follows Hari’s suggestion. Hari (872): tayāhitaiṣitaye ti tathāgatahitaiṣitayā. In this way, Ānanda, you venerate me and the bodhisattva great beings, and you also engender appreciation, trust, and devotion for the past, present, and future blessed buddhas.

“Ānanda, if you feel affection for me, cherish me, and do not forsake me as the Tathāgata, then may you feel affection for this transcendent state of wisdom, cherish it, and not forsake it, so that not even one word of it is lost or disappears. I could speak highly of the entrustment of the transcendent state of wisdom for a long time‍—one eon, several eons, hundreds of eons, thousands of eons, hundreds of thousands of eons, millions of eons, billions of eons, trillions of eons, or even more than that. But, in brief, Ānanda, the transcendent state of wisdom is your teacher in the same way that I am your teacher.According to Ratnākara (F.182.a), “speak highly of,” i.e., praise it for “an eon” or “more than that” if one were to want the details. Nevertheless, he speaks of it “in brief.” RK: bhāṣeyam iti vadeyam| RK: kalpaṃ vā yāvat tato vā upari yadi vistara iṣṭaḥ syāt| kin tarhi saṃkṣepeṇānanda bhāṣeyam| yādṛśa ityādi.According to Ratnākara (F.182.a), he gives an introduction in the passage beginning with the words “should take in” (three paragraphs) above and ending with this sentence where he states that the transcendent state of wisdom is like the “teacher.” The transcendent state of wisdom is the teacher of the world with its gods, humans, and asuras in the same way that the blessed buddhas are teachers of the world with its gods, humans, and asuras.

“Therefore, Ānanda, as this transcendent state of wisdom is unlimited, I am entrusting and transmitting this transcendent state of wisdom to you with an unlimited assignment to bring welfare and happiness to the world with its gods, humans, and asuras. Ānanda, if anyone does not forsake the Tathāgata, does not forsake the Dharma, does not forsake the Saṅgha, and does not forsake the awakening of the past, present, and future blessed buddhas, then let him or her also not forsake the transcendent state of wisdom! This is our admonition.

“Also, Ānanda, if anyone were to take in this transcendent state of wisdom, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, and recite it, write it out, and cultivate it, then he or she would support the awakening of the past, future, and present blessed buddhas. Ānanda, this is because anyone who supports this transcendent state of wisdom during its degeneration supports the awakening of the past, present, and future blessed buddhas. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, the awakening of the blessed buddhas is born from the transcendent state of wisdom.
“Ānanda, the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of those blessed buddhas who were the past worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas was born from nothing but the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Ānanda, the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of those blessed buddhas who will be the future worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas will be born from nothing but the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Ānanda, the unsurpassed, perfect awakening of those blessed buddhas who are the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas presently residing, existing, and abiding in immeasurable, innumerable world systems is born from nothing but the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Therefore, Ānanda, any bodhisattva great being who wishes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening and wishes to train in the six transcendent states should listen to this very transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, and write it out. He or she should train in and engage in meditation in this very transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom is the mother who engenders and gives birth to bodhisattva great beings.

“Furthermore, Ānanda, all those bodhisattva great beings who have gone forth, are going forth, or will go beyond cyclic existence to unsurpassed, perfect awakening train in the six transcendent states and become trained in them, Ānanda, thanks to the transcendent state of wisdom. They too are all born from the six transcendent states, Ānanda, thanks to only this transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, all the transcendent states born from the transcendent state of wisdom bring forth unsurpassed, perfect awakening.

“Therefore, Ānanda, I am entrusting this transcendent state of wisdom to you and transmitting it to you, to an even greater degree, a second and a third time, so that it does not disappear. Ānanda, it is because this, the transcendent state of wisdom, is the inexhaustible Dharma treasury of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, all the Dharma that the previous blessed buddhas taught to sentient beings stuck in beginningless and endless cyclic existence has come from this very Dharma treasury, the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Also, Ānanda, the future blessed buddhas who will realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening and then teach the Dharma to sentient beings stuck in boundless cyclic existence will arise from this very Dharma treasury, the transcendent state of wisdom.

“Also, Ānanda, the present blessed buddhas who are residing, existing, and abiding in immeasurable, innumerable world systems, while teaching the Dharma, are manifesting from this very Dharma treasury, the transcendent state of wisdom. Therefore, Ānanda, this Dharma treasury, the treasury of the transcendent state of wisdom, is inexhaustible.The next few paragraphs below, ending with the introduction of Akṣobhya, do not exist in either Mitra, Wogihara, or Vaidya’s edition. But the missing Sanskrit folio was later located by Conze and is translated here. Karashima, seemingly unaware of Conze’s 1952 edition of the missing folio, produced a new edition based on an (inferior) manuscript. Conze’s is to be preferred.

“Suppose you were to teach the Dharma at the disciple level to persons on the disciple path. Even if, by your doing so, all the sentient beings in a billionfold, great order world system were to actualize the state of a worthy one, then, you, as a disciple of mine, still would not have accomplished the task of a disciple through teaching the Dharma by continuing to turn the Dharma wheel that I set in motion. But, Ānanda, suppose you were to teach and elucidate for a bodhisattva great being even just one word of Dharma associated with the transcendent state of wisdom. Then it would please me that you, as a disciple, were teaching the Dharma by continuing to turn the Dharma wheel that I set in motion. But that would not be the case with the former Dharma teaching that helped all those sentient beings in the billionfold, great order world system to attain the state of a worthy one. Ānanda, what do you think? Would the things that all those worthy ones are producing‍—namely, the merit associated with generosity, the merit associated with ethical discipline, and the merit associated with cultivation‍—amount to much merit?”
“A great amount, Blessed One. A great amount, Sugata,” said Ānanda.

The Blessed One said, “Ānanda, compared to that, a person on the disciple path who teaches bodhisattva great beings the Dharma associated with the transcendent state of wisdom would earn much more merit. For this reason, Ānanda, if a bodhisattva great being were to teach another bodhisattva great being the Dharma associated with the transcendent state of wisdom even just for one day, he or she would earn much more merit. Ānanda, never mind one day, even just for one breakfast period. Ānanda, never mind one breakfast period, even just for thirty minutes to an hour. Ānanda, never mind thirty minutes to an hour, even just for one minute. Ānanda, never mind one minute, even just for one second. Ānanda, never mind one second, even just for one instant.The translation of time lengths is approximate, since the time units from ancient India do not necessarily correspond to our measurements, and different commentators disagree on how many of each unit makes another unit. For scholars interested, Ratnākara’s (F.182.b) comments on these time units are as follows: atra adhvaparyantaḥ kṣaṇaḥ| viṃśaṃ kṣaṇaśataṃ tatkṣaṇaḥ| tatkṣaṇāḥ ṣaṣṭir lavaḥ| lavās triṃśanmuhūrtaḥ| nālikā ghaṭikā. According to Apte, a muhūrtaḥ is one minute, a lavaḥ one second. No dictionary explains nālikā but Ratnākara’s gloss of it is one ghaṭikā, which according to Apte is 24 minutes. If a bodhisattva great being were to teach another bodhisattva great being a Dharma word associated with the transcendent state of wisdom‍—be it just for one minute, one second, or one instant‍—he or she would earn much more merit, because, Ānanda, this gift of the Dharma to the bodhisattva great being surpasses the roots of virtue belonging to all persons on the paths of disciples and solitary realizers. When, Ānanda, a bodhisattva great being has such a root of virtue and keeps it in mind, there is no possibility or chance that he or she would regress from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Such a thing could never occur.”

Then, at that time, the Blessed One created a magical display such that, through the miraculous power of the Buddha, everyone there‍—including the four assemblies consisting of the monks, nuns, male lay devotees, and female lay devotees, along with the gods, nāgas, benevolent spirits, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, and other nonhumans‍—came to see the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Akṣobhya teaching the Dharma surrounded by a retinue of monks, and flanked by a gathering of bodhisattvas. In his deep, ocean-like assembly, he was attended by bodhisattva great beings endowed with inconceivable positive qualities and encircled by all the worthy ones.According to Ratnākara (F.182.b), both the appearance and disappearance of the Blessed Akṣobhya with his buddhafield and assembly is an illustration (for teaching a point). In this illustration, this place is “ocean-like” because of its expansiveness. “Deep” refers to the accomplishment of meditation. The state of “Akṣobhya” means the accomplishment of wisdom. RK: tato ’tha khalu bhagavān ityādinā akṣobhyasya bhagavataḥ sabuddhakṣetrasya sapariṣadaḥ sandarśanam antardhāyanaṃ ca dṛṣṭāntaḥ| atra sāgaropam atvam ativistīrṇatvāt| gambhīratvaṃ dhyānasampadā| akṣobhyatvaṃ prajñāsampadā. Those worthy ones had eliminated the worldly influences. They had no emotional defilements. They had become self-controlled. They had utterly liberated their mindset. They had utterly liberated their discernment. They were noble. They were exemplary. They had completed the work to be done. They had accomplished the tasks. They had laid down their burdens. They had attained the benefits for themselves. They had completely cut the ties to worldly existence. They had the mindset utterly liberated through accurate understanding. And they had succeeded in reaching the supreme perfection of mastery over all their mental faculties.

Then the Blessed One retracted the magical display he had created. After he had retracted it, the blessed, worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Akṣobhya was no longer visible. His buddhafield, his bodhisattva great beings, and his great disciples all no longer appeared to the eyes of the four assemblies, or the gods, nāgas, benevolent spirits, gandharvas, asuras, garuḍas, kinnaras, mahoragas, humans, or other nonhumans. Why? Because the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata had retracted his magical display, so everything ceased to appear anymore to any of their eyes.

Then the Blessed One addressed the venerable Ānanda: “Ānanda, such is the case with all things.According to Ratnākara (F.182.b), to connect his point to the illustration (i.e., making Akṣobhya appear and disappear), the Blessed One says “such is the case with all things.” RK: dārṣṭāntike yojayitum āha| evam ānanda sarvadharmā ityādi. They do not appear to the eye. Things do not appear to things. No things see things. No things know things. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, nothing is a knower, a seer, or has the capacity to do anything. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, all things are actionless in the same way that space is actionless.This translation follows the Tibetan, rather than the Sanskrit edition, which has the word “ungraspable” (agrahya) floating in this sentence, but unmentioned by any commentator. All things are inconceivable, just as an illusory person is. There is not anything that is experiencing, due to there not being an existent thing.According to Hari (876), this is saying that an imagined (kalpita) thing is “not a knower” because it is “actionless in the same way that space is actionless.” A dependent (paratantra) thing is “not a seer” because it is “inconceivable just as an illusory person is.” An actualized (pariniṣpanna) thing is “has no capacity to do anything” because it is not an experiencer, since there is not an existent thing. Hari: etad uktaṃ bhavati : kalpito dharmo ’jānakaḥ yasmād ākāśanirīhakatayā nirīhakaḥ. tathā paratantro ’paśyakaḥ yato māyāpuruṣopamatvenā cintyaḥ. pariniṣpanno ’pi na kāryasamartho yasmād asadbhāvatvenāvedaka iti. Ānanda, by going about life in this way, a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. He or she does not habitually fixate on any thing. Ānanda, by training in this way, a bodhisattva great being trains in the transcendent state of wisdom. Any bodhisattva great being who wishes to obtain the great awakening, the supreme perfection of all trainings, should train in the transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, among all the trainings, this training is called the foremost, the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled, in terms of its providing protection to those without a protector, its bringing benefit to the whole world, its bringing happiness to the whole world, its being recommended by the Buddha, and its being praised by the Buddha. Ānanda, because the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas have trained in this transcendent state of wisdom and have remained in this training, they could, with a single big toe, lift up this entire billionfold, great order world system and let it drop again, without them even thinking that they had ‘lifted up’ this billionfold, great order world system or ‘let it drop.’ Why is that? Because the transcendent state of wisdom is endowed with immeasurable and innumerable positive qualities. Ānanda, the blessed buddhas have trained in the training of this transcendent state of wisdom and they have attained freedom from attachment toward all things past, present, and future.According to Ratnākara (F.182.b), in this context “freedom from attachment” means either having no mental perceptions, or knowing that is free of any obstruction. RK: asaṅgatām iti nirupalambhatām apratihatajñānatāṃ vā. See n.555. So, Ānanda, however many trainings there are in the past, present, and future, Ānanda, among them all, it is this very training in the transcendent state of wisdom that is called the foremost, the best, the most distinguished, the most eminent, the preeminent, the most prominent, the excellent, the unexcelled, the most excellent, the unequaled, and the equal to the unequaled. For, Ānanda, the transcendent state of wisdom is immeasureable; Ānanda, the transcendent state of wisdom is inexhaustible; and, Ānanda, the transcendent state of wisdom is limitless. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, the transcendent state of wisdom is a state free of existence.According to Hari (877), because it is “a state free of existence” means because there is no dissipation of it. Hari: asattvād iti. kṣayābhāvād ity arthaḥ. It is because, Ānanda, anyone who might think he or she can grasp the measure, dissipation, or limit of the transcendent state of wisdom would be thinking he or she can grasp the measure, dissipation, or limit of space. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, the transcendent state of wisdom is immeasurable; Ānanda, the transcendent state of wisdom is inexhaustible; and, Ānanda, the transcendent state of wisdom is limitless. Never, Ānanda, have I described a measure, dissipation, or limit of the transcendent state of wisdom. But certainly, Ānanda, any grouping of names, words, or syllables is bound up with measures. And yet, Ānanda, the transcendent state of wisdom is not bound up with measures.According to Ratnākara (F.182.b), “measure” refers to a limitation in terms of spatial extension; “dissipation” refers to a limitation in terms of time; “limit” refers to a limitation in terms of number; “bound up with measures” means accurately defined or delimited by any measure. “Because it is immeasurable” means because it is free of mental perception. “Dissipation” is a depletion, while a complete dissipation is an absence. (So its inexhaustibility means it cannot even be depleted, let alone become completely absent). RK: pramāṇaṃ vaipulyaniyamaḥ| kṣayaḥ kālaniyamaḥ| paryantaḥ saṃkhyāniyamaḥ| pramāṇabaddhe ti pramāṇaparicchinnā| aprameyatvād ity anupalambhāt| kṣayo ’pacayaḥ| parikṣayo ’bhāvaḥ. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, the grouping of names, words, and syllables here is not the transcendent state of wisdom. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom is not subject to being measured. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom is unlimited.”
Ānanda asked, “But, Blessed One, what is the reason that the Blessed One has not described the measure of the transcendent state of wisdom?”

The Blessed One said, “Ānanda, the Tathāgata has not explicated the measure of the transcendent state of wisdom because it is inexhaustible. Ānanda, the Tathāgata has not described the measure of the transcendent state of wisdom because of its voidness. Ānanda, one cannot mentally perceive the voidness of a void thing, so how could one possibly measure it? Ānanda, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas in the past have manifested from this very transcendent state of wisdom, Ānanda, but this transcendent state of wisdom has not been depleted or completely exhausted. Also, Ānanda, the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas in the future will manifest from this very transcendent state of wisdom, Ānanda, but this transcendent state of wisdom will not be depleted or completely exhausted. Also, Ānanda, those worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas who are presently residing, existing, and abiding in immeasurable, innumerable world systems are manifesting from this very transcendent state of wisdom, Ānanda, but this transcendent state of wisdom is not depleted or completely exhausted. Also, Ānanda, I am presently the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened Tathāgata. I too am a manifestation of this very transcendent state of wisdom, Ānanda, but this transcendent state of wisdom is not depleted or completely exhausted. Why is that? Because, Ānanda, anyone who thinks that the transcendent state of wisdom can be exhausted should think space can be exhausted. That, Ānanda, is why this transcendent state of wisdom is inexhaustible.”

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 51 in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and of Chapter 61 in the Eighteen Thousand.Then the venerable Subhūti thought to himself, “This point that the Tathāgata is teaching is profound. I should certainly ask the Tathāgata about it.”
Then the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, is the transcendent state of wisdom inexhaustible?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, this transcendent state of wisdom is inexhaustible, specifically as inexhaustible as space, because it is free of the arising of all things.”According to Ratnākara (F.183.a), why is it “as inexhaustible as space”? He says “because it is free of the arising of all things.” There are no things that have arisen, so the transcendent state of wisdom, which is characterized as free of mentally perceiving those things, is also “as inexhaustible as space.” RK: ākāśākṣayatvād iti ākāśavad akṣayatvāt| kuta ity āha sarvadharmānutpādata iti| anutpannā sarvadharmāḥ| atas teṣām anupalambhalakṣaṇā prajñāpāramitāpy ākāśa vad akṣaye ti.
Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, how should one realize the transcendent state of wisdom?”

The Blessed One said, “Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being should realize this transcendent state of wisdom through the inexhaustibility of physical forms.According to Ratnākara (F.183.a), he or she “should realize” the transcendent state of wisdom means he or she should make it be directly perceived (within his or her experience through reflexive awareness). “Through the inexhaustibility of physical forms” and so on means based on their nonarising. The same is true for ignorance and so on. RK: abhinirhartavye ti pratyakṣīkartavyā| rūpā dīnām akṣayatvene ti teṣām anutpādataḥ| evam avidyā dīnām. Likewise, the bodhisattva great being should realize this transcendent state of wisdom through the inexhaustibility of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness. In this way, Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being should realize this transcendent state of wisdom.

“Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being should realize this transcendent state of wisdom through the inexhaustibility of ignorance. Likewise, the bodhisattva great being should realize this transcendent state of wisdom through the inexhaustibility of karmic conditioning factors, through the inexhaustibility of consciousness, through the inexhaustibility of mentality and physical form, through the inexhaustibility of the six bases, through the inexhaustibility of contact, through the inexhaustibility of craving, through the inexhaustibility of clinging, through the inexhaustibility of becoming, through the inexhaustibility of birth, through the inexhaustibility of aging, and through the inexhaustibility of dying. The bodhisattva great being should realize this transcendent state of wisdom through the inexhaustibility of the mental disturbances of grief, lamentation, suffering, and despair.

“Subhūti, that is how a bodhisattva great being surveys dependent arising freed from the two extremes. Subhūti, by surveying it in this way, a bodhisattva great being surveys dependent arising as lacking either beginning, middle, or end. Subhūti, what a bodhisattva great being surveys while seated at the epicenter of awakening is something unique.According to Ratnākara (F.183.a), “two extremes” refers to (eternalistic) creation and (nihilistic) destruction. The “middle” refers to the continuity of what has arisen. “Unique” means not in common with others (like the disciples and solitary realizers). RK: antadvayam utpattivināśau madhyam utpannasya sthitiḥ| āveṇiko ’nyair asādhāraṇaḥ. Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being surveys dependent arising in this way, he or she attains the knowing of someone who has complete knowing. Subhūti, this is because when a bodhisattva great being goes about life and surveys dependent arising in terms of realizing the inexhaustible, he or she will come to abide not on the levels of disciples or solitary realizers, but in the state of complete knowing.

“Subhūti, there are some bodhisattva great beings who might regress from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. For instance, as a result of not depending upon the proper attentiveness and skill in means, they may not know how a bodhisattva great being should realize the transcendent state of wisdom through realizing the inexhaustible, or how a bodhisattva great being should survey dependent arising in the transcendent state of wisdom through realizing the inexhaustible. Subhūti, all those bodhisattva great beings who have previously regressed from unsurpassed, perfect awakening, are now regressing from it, or will regress from it will do so as a result of not depending upon the proper attentiveness and skill in means. Subhūti, all those bodhisattva great beings who have not regressed from unsurpassed, perfect awakening, are not regressing from it, or will not regress from it do so as a result of depending upon this transcendent state of wisdom. Thus, a bodhisattva great being going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom should realize the transcendent state of wisdom through realizing the inexhaustible, and should survey dependent arising in the transcendent state of wisdom through realizing the inexhaustible in this way.

“But that said, Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being surveys dependent arising in this way, he or she does not identify any thing that arises without a cause. He or she also does not identify any thing that is permanent, fixed, lasting, or qualitatively unchanging. He or she also does not identify any thing that is an agent or an experiencer.According to Ratnākara (F.183.a), he or she does not identify “any thing” included within the (five) constituents of experience. Why not? Because of their dependent arising. “Permanent” means it has no beginning or end; “fixed” means sturdy; “lasting” means endless. And he or she does not identify an “agent or an experiencer” because there is no impeller or grasper. The text should be easy to understand from here to the next occurrence of the phrase “unsurpassed awakening.” RK: na ca kiñcid dharmam iti skandhaiḥ saṃgṛhītam| kutaḥ ? pratītyotpādadarśanāt| nityam ity anādinidhanam| dhruvam iti sthiram| śāśvatam ity anidhanam| kārakaṃ vedakaṃ ve ti| īhatur gṛhītuś cābhāvāt. Note: Following this comment, Ratnākara focuses for the rest of the chapter on the many topics in the corresponding verses from the Ornament. So he says little directly about what follows from here. Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being realizes this transcendent state of wisdom through realizing the inexhaustible, and goes about life in this transcendent state of wisdom, that is how he or she surveys dependent arising. Subhūti, at the time when a bodhisattva great being realizes this transcendent state of wisdom through realizing the inexhaustible and surveys dependent arising, Subhūti, that bodhisattva great being does not identify physical forms. He or she also does not identify feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness. He or she also does not identify ignorance, the karmic conditioning factors, consciousness, mentality and physical form, the six bases, contact, craving, clinging, becoming, birth, aging, or dying. He or she also does not identify the mental disturbances of grief, lamentation, suffering, or despair. He or she also does not identify this buddhafield or another buddhafield, or even a thing through which this or another buddhafield might be identified. Subhūti, this is the transcendent state of wisdom for bodhisattva great beings.

“Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, then the pernicious Māra is overcome with pangs of utter grief. As an analogy, it is just like a person who is overcome with pangs of utter grief when his mother and father are dying. In the very same way, Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, then the pernicious Māra is overcome with pangs of utter grief.”

Subhūti asked, “Blessed One, is just a single pernicious Māra overcome with pangs of utter grief? Or are many pernicious Māras overcome with pangs of utter grief? Or else, are all the pernicious Māras in the billionfold, great order world system overcome with pangs of utter grief?”

At this, the Blessed One said to Subhūti, “Subhūti, at the time when a bodhisattva great being is abiding in the transcendent state of wisdom, all the pernicious Māras in the billionfold, great order world system are overcome with pangs of utter grief and do not enjoy sitting on their own thrones. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being abides in the transcendent state of wisdom, no one in the world with its gods, humans, and asuras can find any vulnerability in such a bodhisattva great being, or gain any foothold to take possession of him or her, so that in doing so they can harm the bodhisattva great being and cause him or her to regress from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Therefore, Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being who wishes to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening should go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she perfects the cultivation of the transcendent states of generosity, ethical discipline, tolerant acceptance, heroic perseverance, and meditation.From this point in the chapter, which corresponds to a point within Chapter 51 in the Twenty-Five Thousand (51.13) and Hundred Thousand, and within Chapter 61 in the Eighteen Thousand (61.13), it becomes difficult to discern the parallels between the Eight Thousand and the long sūtras. The latter embark on an extensive explanation structured according to the six transcendent states that fills several of their subsequent chapters.

“That is to say, Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, he or she perfects the cultivation of all six transcendent states and perfects the cultivation of all the skills in means. When a bodhisattva great being goes about life in this transcendent state of wisdom, he or she discerns all the activities of Māra that might arise immediately as they are arising, and in so doing, dismisses them. So, Subhūti, any bodhisattva great being wishing to gain all the skills in means should go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and should cultivate the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.189.b), he says “wishing to gain all the skills in means” and so forth. Here, the “means” (upāya)-‍—in the literal sense that it brings one closer (upeyate)‍—is the state of complete knowing. “Skill” (in those means) refers to the removal of incorrect perceptions. RK: sarvopāyakauśalyāni parigrahītukāmene tyādi| upeyata ity upāyaḥ sarvākārajñatā| tasmiṃ kauśalyāni vipratipattīnām apohanāni. Subhūti, at the time when a bodhisattva great being is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and realizing the transcendent state of wisdom, then at that time, Subhūti, the bodhisattva great being should keep in mind not merely those blessed buddhas presently residing, existing, and abiding in immeasurable, innumerable world systems, but also their state of complete knowing, or, more precisely, the transcendent state of wisdom, because they are born from this alone. After keeping this in mind, the bodhisattva great being should develop the proper mindset repeatedly, thinking, ‘I will attain the qualities that have been attained by the blessed buddhas.’ Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being is going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom in this way, he or she should develop this mindset and should realize it throughout the day, even just for a mere finger snap. Even if other bodhisattva great beings who cling to mental perceptions were to give gifts for eons equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges, any bodhisattva great being who realizes this transcendent state of wisdom throughout the day, even just for a mere finger snap, would earn much more merit than those other bodhisattva great beings who cling to mental perceptions. Such a bodhisattva great being will come to abide in the state beyond regressing. One should also know that such a bodhisattva great being who goes about life in this transcendent state of wisdom and develops this mindset throughout the day, even just for a mere finger snap, will be kept in mind by the tathāgatas. What more can be said about someone who functions within this mindset on a daily basis? Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being is kept in mind by the tathāgatas, what future birth should he or she look forward to? Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being kept in mind by the tathāgatas should look forward to nowhere other than unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Yet, since it is not possible for them to be reborn in the lower realms, one would expect them to be born in a heaven realm‍—that is to say, born into buddhafields that never lack tathāgatas so that he or she will never be separate from the tathāgatas, and can also help other sentient beings ripen. Subhūti, when a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom, realizes the transcendent state of wisdom, and develops this mindset too throughout the day, even just for a mere finger snap, such are the qualities and advantages he or she possesses. What more can be said about someone who functions within this mindset on a daily basis? Subhūti, it is just like, for example, the bodhisattva great being called Gandhahastin who is presently going about his life engaged in spiritual practice in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Akṣobhya.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Avakīrṇakusuma,” the twenty-eighth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 29: The ApproachAccording to Ratnākara, the “Chapter on the Approach” (writing where his comments on this chapter end, F.220.b) is the chapter articulating the approach (i.e., how to “comprehend,” “pursue,” “follow,” and “conform to” the transcendent state of wisdom). RK: anugama sya vācakaḥ parivarto ’nugamaparivartaḥ. Note: The translation “approach” (anugama; rjes su rig pa) aims to capture the range of meaning of the Sanskrit word anugama, which mainly means “following” (in all physical and intellectual senses of the word), “comprehending,” and “conforming to.” Ratnākara glosses the term differently to make clear which sense is intended in that sentence; e.g., one should “approach” means (1) one should know (anugantavye ti veditavyā), (2) one should follow (anugantavye ti anusartavyā), and (3) one should listen by means of the grouping together of syllables (anugamiṣyatī ti vyañjanakāyena śroṣyati).As mentioned in note n.1296, from a point near the close of the previous chapter it is no longer easy to point to parallels between the Eight Thousand and the long sūtras. Essentially, the last three of the topics of the Ornament are covered in the Eight Thousand only by tersely expressed and schematic lists of points, as in the present chapter, while in the long sūtras they are set out and described at great length. Thus for Chapters 52–72 of the Twenty-Five Thousand and Hundred Thousand, and for Chapters 62–83 in the Eighteen Thousand, there are no passages in the Eight Thousand that correspond directly. At the end of the text, however, the narrative of Sadāprarudita and Dharmodgata, and the entrusting of the text to Ānanda, have close parallels in the Twenty-Five Thousand and the Eighteen Thousand, as detailed in notes n.1339, n.1397, and n.1438.
div.p: “Furthermore, Subhūti, a bodhisattva great being should approach the transcendent state of wisdom in this way.According to Ratnākara (F.193.b–94.a), the phrase “in this way” refers to the next three sentences that will be explained. The phrase “transcendent state of wisdom” here refers to the progressive trainings (on a bodhisattva’s path of cultivation). The phrase “should approach” means should be aware of. RK: evam iti vakṣyamāṇena vākyatrayeṇa| prajñāpāramite ty anupūrvakriyā| anugantavye ti veditavyā. According to Hari (893), the Blessed One has (just) described the path of cultivation. Now he will explain the four types of conceptualizations of grasped objects and grasping subjects to be eliminated on that (path of cultivation) through the antidotes to the counterproductive forces. To introduce those (in terms of their progressive realization), he says “furthermore” and so on in this sentence. Hari: bhāvanāmārgam evam abhidhāya tatra praheyaś caturvidho ’grāhyagrāhaka vikalpaḥ vipakṣa­prati­pakṣaprati­pādanatvena vaktavya ity upodghātayann. Note: Both commentators agree that this chapter concerns the “progressive realization” (anupūrvābhisamaya) of the transcendent state of wisdom (i.e., the sixth of the eight main topics in the Ornament). In this topic, the Buddha outlines the way that a bodhisattva progressively moves along the stages of the path of cultivation. However, in the long Transcendent State of Wisdom sūtras, the four sets of nine conceptualizations eliminated on the path of cultivation, covered in the Twenty-Five Thousand, for example, in 53.69–198, are counted according to Conze 1975 (pp. 505–523) within the fifth topic, the “culminations.” Indeed the last three of the eight topics (VI–VIII, see Synopsis of the Text According to the) are covered in a considerably more expanded way in the long sūtras than in the present text, in which they are all included in this chapter and the next. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through being free from attachment to all things.According to Ratnākara (F.194.a), first the phrase “all things” refers to the six transcendent states, because these alone constitute the path to awakening and the word “thing” (dharma) here describes the path. So “being free from attachment to all things” (i.e., to all transcendent states) means having neither attachment to them nor having any obtacles (in practicing them). Through (freedom from attachment), the bodhisattvas “approach (i.e., are aware of) the transcendent state of wisdom” in each progressive stage to be practiced, beginning with the first arising of the proper mindset and so on and ending with causing beings to ripen. RK: tatrādau sarvadharmāḥ ṣaṭpāramitāḥ| tāsām eva bodhimārgatvāt| dharmaśabdasya ceha mārgavācitvāt| sarvadharmā ṇām asaṅgatā niḥsaṅgatā nirvighnatā| bodhisattvair anukramakaraṇīyeṣu prathamacittotpād ādiṣu sattvaparimocanānteṣu tayā sā ’nugantavyā. According to Hari (893), among the (four types of conceptualizations, see preceding note), the Blessed One explains the meaning of the first‍—namely, the first nine types of conceptualizations of grasped objects‍—with the words “one should approach the transcendent state of wisdom by being free from attachment to all things.” That is to say, in order to eliminate the conceptualization of “things” altogether, a bodhisattva should cultivate the path of cultivation through being free of any habitual fixation toward anything. In the context of this section on the (thirty-six) conceptualizations (to be eliminated on the path of cultivation), one should understand the meaning of the “transcendent state of wisdom” to be synonymous with the meaning of “the path of cultivation.” Hari: tatra ca prathamo grāhyavikalpo viṣayabhedān navadheti prathamavikalpārtham āha : sarvadharmāsaṅgataḥ prajñā­pāramitānugantavye ti. dharma saṃkṣepa­vikalpaprahāṇārthaṃ sarva­dharmānabhiniveśād bhāvanāmārgo bhāvayitavyaḥ. evam uttaratra vikalpādhikāre prajñāpāramitārtho bhāvanāmārgārtha ity agantavyam. Note: The idea is that each moment of the transcendent state of wisdom along the path of cultivation is a distinct instance of a similar seeing of emptiness that counteracts a particular conceptualization to be eliminated progressively along that path. So, in that sense, each moment of the “transcendent state of wisdom” constitutes another progressive (and cumulative) step of the path of cultivation itself. So, Hari is recommending here that we understand each mention of “transcendent state of wisdom” in each sentence (throughout the chapter) as a different step along the path of cultivation. And Ratnākara is clarifying that the “approach” to each step is to be aware of what each sentence describes. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through not differentiating all things.According to Ratnākara (F.194.a), here “all things” refers to the six objects of recollection (i.e., the Buddha, Dharma, Saṅgha, ethical discipline, nonattachment, and gods). How could these be “things”? Because they are maintained within the mind. Furthermore, even merit is called a “thing” (by people) in the world, and these comprise the merit that consists in cultivation. “Not differentiating” all things means that they are (seen as) the same, not different, because their inherent nature is nonexistent. A bodhisattva should “approach” that (i.e., be aware of it) as mentioned before. RK: sarva­dharmāsambhedataḥ prajñāpāramitā ’nugantavye ti| sarvadharmāḥ ṣaḍanusmṛtayaḥ| tāḥ kathaṃ dharmāḥ ? cittadhāraṇāt| api ca puṇyam api loke dharma ucyate| tāś ca bhāvanāmayaṃ puṇyam| sarvadharmāṇām asaṃbhedato ’bhinnatā samatā| abhāvasvabhāvatvāt| tayā ’nugantavye ti pūrvavat. Note: Ratnākara’s immediately subsequent commentary goes on to explain how on the path of cultivation, a bodhisattva recollects the six objects of recollection “without being attentive” (amanasīkāra) to any object separate from a subject grasping it. Hari’s commentary in this chapter‍—which is uncontradicted by Ratnākara‍—connects each sentence with the antidotes to the thirty-six conceptualizations to be eliminated on the path of cultivation. These thirty-six conceptualizations are different from those that are to be eliminated on the path of seeing (as discussed in previous chapters, see n.1203; see also n.1300). Hari’s commentary will not be translated here. For those interested, see Sparham vol. 4, pp. 223–40. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through the nonoccurence of all things.According to Ratnākara (F.195.b), a “thing” is defined as that which bears its own particular characteristic, so here “all things” refers to the constituents of experience, elements, bases, aids to awakening, transcendent states, and so on, all the way up to the state of knowing of all aspects. That one should be aware of their “nonoccurrence” refers to the progressive training to be aware of their nonoccurrence, i.e., their inherent nature being not existent. To explain, Ratnākara quotes (though it is not clear from where), “They have no inherent nature. The inherent nature that they have is a lack of inherent nature. So, a bodhisattva training in the transcendent state of wisdom develops the first proper mindset, then develops firm conviction that all things have the inherent nature of being not existent, and then cultivates the four applications of mindfulness, the four correct exertions, and so on, up to the state of knowing of all aspects. Then, having realized that the nonexistent inherent nature of all things, he or she sets in motion the Dharma wheel and liberates beings from cyclic existence through the three vehicles.” This is the thirteenth progressive training in all things having the inherent nature of nonexistence. RK: sarva­dharmāsambhavataḥ prajñā­pāramitā’nugantavye ti| svalakṣaṇadhāraṇāt dharmāḥ| skandhā dhātava āyatanāni bodhipakṣāḥ pāramitā yāvat sarvākārajñateti sarvadharmāḥ| teṣām asaṃbhavata ity asaṃbhavajñānato ’nupūrvakriyā veditavyā| asaṃbhavo ’bhāvasvabhāvatā| tathā hi‍— “teṣāṃ nāsti svabhāvaḥ| yasya svabhāvo nāsti so ’svabhāvaḥ| sa prajñāpāramitāyāṃ caran prathamacittotpādam upādāya sarvadharmāṇām abhāvasvabhāvatām adhimucya catvāri smṛtyupasthānāni catvāri samyakprahāṇāni yāvat sarvākārajñatāṃ bhāvayati| so ’bhāvasvabhāvān sarvadharmān abhisaṃbudhya dharmacakraṃ pravartayati| tribhiś ca yānaiḥ sattvān saṃsārāt parimocayati” iti dharmāṇām abhāvasvabhāvatayā trayodaśyānu­pūrva­kriyāḥ. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things are equal to the unchanging.According to Ratnākara (F.195.b–96.a), the objections to the previous sentence were uttered by themselves (in a previous chapter), but the responses are (now) to be explained. The basic objection is this: If a perfection whose inherent nature is nonexistent is to be cultivated, then cultivating it would be fruitless, like cultivating the horns of a horse (which do not exist). He rebuts that here: “All things are equal to the unchanging,” i.e., to the ultimate realm of things (dharmadhātu), and not (equal to) mere nonexistence. The ultimate realm of things being taken as a meditative object is the cause of all the noble qualities. That is why it is called the ultimate realm of things (viz. the absolute source of qualities/things). So, how could that be fruitless? RK: ita ūrdhvaṃ codyāni svayam ūhyāni parihārās tu vaktavyāḥ| yady abhāvasvabhāvāḥ pāramitā bhāvyante tadā tadbhāvanā niṣphalā turagaviṣāṇabhāvanāvad iti codyam| parihāram āha| sarvadharmā nirvikārasamā itī tyādi| nirvikāro dharmadhātus tena samāḥ sarvadharmā nābhāvamātreṇa| dharmadhātuś cālambyamāna āryadharmāṇāṃ hetur bhavati| ata eva dharmadhātur ity ucyate| tatkuto vaiphalyam? Note: Ratnākara begins to explain each step along the path more specifically according to his own viewpoint by rebutting opponent objections to his school’s explanation of how the path actually works. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through wisely knowing the freedom from self and the freedom from representations.According to Ratnākara (F.196.a), the freedom from the form of “a self” because a self has no characteristics and from the form of “representations” because there is no grasped object or grasping subject. “Wisely knowing” means perceiving through a valid means of cognition, because knowing a “self” would be knowing it to be a lasting, single essence, but the constituents of experience and so on are not like that. Hence, they are proven to be “free of self.” And since there is no grasped object or grasping subject they are also proven, based on that alone, to be free of representational form, because (a representation) does not have the characteristic of a grasping subject. Due to the absence of a grasping subject, there is also nothing grasped. So, the nonexistence of all things is proven. RK: anātmavijñaptita ity ātmavilakṣaṇatvād anātmā kāreṇa grāhyagrāhakavaidhuryād avijñapty ākāreṇa ca| prajñānubodhanata iti pramāṇena pratīteḥ| ātmā hi nityaikarūpo jñātā na ca skandhādaya ithambhūtāḥ| tasmād anātmatvena siddhāḥ| grāhyagrāhakavaidhuryāc ca| ata eva cāvijñaptyākāreṇāpi siddhāḥ| grāhakalakṣaṇatvāt tasyāḥ| grāhakābhāvād grāhyam api nāstīti siddhaḥ sarvadharmāṇām abhāvaḥ. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things are talked about by way of a mere name or conventional expression. But a conventional expression neither arises anywhere, nor arises from anywhere, and also the conventional expression is not a thing. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things are not a conventional expression or utterance, and not expressed conventionally or uttered.According to Ratnākara (F.196.a–b), the objection here is this: Those physical forms themselves are what is expressed by the words “physical forms” and so on, and the expression of those physical forms by those words exists in the world as conventional expressions or agreements among listeners. So, how could all things be nonexistent? The rebuttal is this sentence. A “mere name” means a name is empty of any referent (i.e., a particular thing it refers to). “All things are talked about by way of” that (mere name), because the referent of those (words) is a mind’s representational form, which is neither perceived as external (to mind), nor (does it arise) from advancing toward something external. Also, there is no external referent, because even without an external thing, the referent arises from the word. Someone might object, “But the referent of the word is mind in the form of something external.” (To this we respond that) the mental formations are not the referent as a physical form, since they are fundamentally distinct things, or else there could be no contradiction between the two, and having a mind would mean having a referent. Therefore, due to the absence of any referent, he says that such a thing is “talked about by way of a mere name or conventional expression.” Someone might object, “Conventional expressions exist to that extent.” The Blessed One rebuts this with the next sentence. They do not “arise anywhere” because of the nonexistence of any referent. They do not “arise from anywhere” because of the nonexistence of a thing that expresses it. Also, it is “not a thing”‍—i.e., not something thing physical, verbal, or mental‍—because the expresser and what is expressed do not exist. The next sentence says “all things” and so on to summarize this. Those things are “not a conventional expression or utterance” because they to not exist in these (expressions or utterances), and for this reason, too, they are “not uttered.” RK: rūpādiśabdair ye ’bhilapyante ta eva rūpādayaḥ| asti ca loke tais teṣām abhilāpaḥ| śrotṝṇāṃ vyavahāraḥ saṃvādaś ca| tatkuto ’bhāvaḥ sarvadharmāṇām iti codyam| parihāram āha| nāmamātreṇe tyādinā| arthaśūnyaṃ nāma nāmamātram| tena te sarvadharmā abhilapyante| na hi buddhyākāra eṣām artho bāhyatvena pratiyate, bāhye ca pravṛtteḥ| nāpi bāhya evārthaḥ, asaty api bāhye śabdād arthagateḥ| buddhir eva bāhyarūpeṇa śabdārtha iti cet| na caitasyā artho rūpaṃ, atyantabhedāt| abhede vā na visaṃvādaḥ syāt| buddhimantaś cārthavantā bhaveyuḥ| tasmād arthābhāvān nāmamātreṇa vyavahāramātreṇābhilapyante| vyavahāras tāvad astīti ced āha| vyavahāraś cetyādi| na kvacid ity arthābhāvat| na kutaścid iti vācakābhāvāt| nāpi kaścid iti na vāciko na ca kāyiko nāpi mānasaḥ| vācyavācakayor abhāvāt| sarvadharmā ity ādinopasaṃhāraḥ| avyavahārā avyāhārā iti teṣu tayor abhāvāt| ata eva cāvyāhṛtāḥ. Note: This philosophical discussion of the lack of any true relationship between words and their referents is common to all Mahāyāna schools.
