But Ratnākara’s explanation (in this context) is emphasizing his particular Yogācāra viewpoint of things’ lack of externality. Hari and scholars of other schools, of course, explain this differently. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through the lack of any valid means of cognizing anything. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through the lack of any valid means of cognizing physical forms. Likewise, he or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through the lack of any valid means of cognizing feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness.According to Ratnākara (F.196.b), the objection here is, “Since physical forms are established by means of valid cognition, their emptiness should also be established by means of valid cognition. Hence, the transcendent state of wisdom should be established by means of valid cognition, since it has that (emptiness) as its object. But this is not asserted by you.” The Blessed One rebuts this with the next sentences: “through the lack of any valid means of cognizing anything” because there is no valid means of cognition. To explain: There is no valid means of cognizing anything because it is characterized by nonexistence, just like space, which is characterized as a lack of physical forms, and hence there is no valid means of cognizing its emptiness, because there is nothing that can delimit that. Therefore, the transcendent state of wisdom is like that too, because it cannot be separated from its object (i.e., emptiness). RK: nanu rūpādīnāṃ pramāṇavattvāt tacchūnyatā api pramāṇavatya iti tadālambanā prajñāpāramitā pramāṇavatī syāt| na ceṣyata iti codyam| parihāram āha| sarvadharmāṇāṃ apramāṇata iti| apramāṇatvāt| tathā hi sarvadharmāṇāṃ nāsti pramāṇam abhāvalakṣaṇatvāt| ākāśasyeva rūpā bhāvalakṣaṇasya, tatas tacchūnyatānām api nāsti pramāṇam avacchedakābhāvāt| tataḥ prajñāpāramitāyā apy ālambanābhedāt. Note: Ratnākara’s explanation (in this context) is emphasizing his particular Yogācāra viewpoint on the means of valid cognition. Hari and scholars of other schools, of course, explain this differently. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through the freedom from signs of all things.According to Ratnākara (F.196.b-97.a), the objection is this: So if physical forms and so on do not exist, then what is appearing as physical forms and so on? The conventional signs of things are not the reality of things, because they lack the nature of being either the same or different, and something without the nature of shining forth cannot be connected to illumination. But the conventional signs of the things are the representational forms of the cognitive errors of things, since those (cognitive errors) that are shining forth, due to having the nature of illumination, are appearing with false forms, because nonerroneous (natures) are characterized as appearing as something false. This may be so, but it does not prove the transcendent state of wisdom. That must be nonerroneous, while a mind with forms would just be erroneous. On the other hand, the mind cannot be without representational forms as they appear even in sleep. The Blessed One rebuts this with the next sentence, “through the freedom from signs of all things” and so on. The freedom of signs in all things comes from the disappearance of signs, and hence is a state of knowing that does not have appearances, i.e., representational forms. Based on that, one should recognize the transcendent state of wisdom‍—the basic idea is like in the attainment of the formless realm due to the abandonment of all forms. RK: yadi tarhi rūpādayo na santi kimiti nīlādayaḥ prakhyānti| dharmanimittāny etāni na dharmavastūni| ekānekasvabhāvavirahāt, aprakāśātmanaḥ prakāśāyogāc ca| dharmanimittāni tu dharmabhrāntīnām ākārāḥ| tā hi prakāśarūpatvāt prakāśamānās tair asadbhir api rūpaiḥ prakhyānti| asatkhyātilakṣaṇatvād abhrāntīnām| yady eva, na siddhayati prajñāpāramitā| sā khalv abhrāntā sākārā tu buddhir bhrāntaiva| na ca nirākārā buddhiḥ svapne py anubhūyata iti codyam| parihāram āha| sarvadharmānimittata ityādinā| sarvadharme ṣv ānimittaṃ nimittāstamayāj jñānasya nirābhāsatā nirākāratā| tataḥ prajñāpāramitā veditavyā| ākāraparivarjanād ārūpyasamāpattivad iti bhāvaḥ. Note: Ratnākara’s explanation (in this context) is emphasizing his particular Yogācāra viewpoint on the relationship between conventional signs, error, and the unerroneous knowing. Hari and scholars of other schools, of course, explain this differently. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through penetrating all things.According to Ratnākara (F.197.a), the objection is this: This is the perfect attentiveness of the ultimate realm of things based on eliminating signs, but not the transcendent state of wisdom. The Blessed One rebuts this with the next sentence, “through penetrating all things” and so on. The penetrating insight (prativedha) is taught here with the word “penetrating” (nirvedha). The penetration of all things is the actualization of knowing that has penetrating insight into their actualized nature (pariniṣpanna). Based on that (penetration), the transcendent state of wisdom is (known) through that characteristic. This is the meaning here. RK: nanv ayaṃ vikalpanimittānāṃ parivarjanād dharmadhātoḥ samyaṅmanasikāro na punaḥ prajñāpāramiteti codyam| parihāram āha sarvadharmanirvedhata ityādi| nirvedhaśabdenātra prativedha uktaḥ| sarvadharmāṇāṃ pariniṣpannena rūpeṇa prativedho jñānasākṣātkriyā sa teṣāṃ nirvedhaḥ| ta ta iti tena lakṣaṇena prajñāpāramitā ity arthaḥ. Note: Ratnākara’s explanation (in this context) is emphasizing his particular Yogācāra viewpoint on the fact that conventional signs disappear (rather than being merely seen through). Hari and scholars of other schools, of course, explain this differently. Ratnākara continues to make lengthy comments in opposition to his opponent’s “objections” for all subsequent sentences. But only his rebuttals or responses on difficult points will be translated below. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through the natural purity of all things.According to Ratnākara (F.197.b), “the natural purity of all things” means the purity belonging to the nature of all things. He or she “should approach,” i.e., should accomplish, “the transcendent state of wisdom” with that (purity) as his or her aim. RK: sarva­dharma­prakṛtipariśuddhita ityādi| sarvadharmā ṇāṃ prakṛtis tasyāḥ pariśuddhis tatas tena prayojanena prajñāpāramitā ’nugantavyā| anuṣṭhātavyety arthaḥ. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through not speaking of any things.According to Ratnākara (F.197.b), he says “through not speaking of all things” and so on, because all things are beyond words. That is, a word does not make their inherent nature shine forth by pointing toward it alone, and hence he or she must “approach the transcendent state of wisdom” to see the ultimate truth (of all things). RK: sarvadharmāvacanata ityādinā| sarvadharmāṇām avacanāt| vācā svarūpam aprakāśya kevalaṃ sūcanāt| paramārthadarśanāya prajñāpāramitā ’ nugantavyā. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through the noncessation of all things being equal to relinquishing them.According to Ratnākara (F.198.a), he or she should “approach the transcendent state of wisdom through the noncessation of all things” because there is no destruction of things free of worldly influences (anāśrava; zag med). What is their noncessation like? It is “equal to relinquishing” them, because relinquishing them according to the explanation (of the transcendent state of wisdom) is no different. RK: sarvadharmāṇām anirodhata iti| anāśravāṇāṃ dharmāṇām akṣayāt prajñāpāramitā ’nugantavyā| katham anirodhaḥ? prahāṇasamatayā| nirdeśatvena prahāṇād aviśeṣāt. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through the attainment of nirvāṇa of all things being the same in their actual nature.According to Ratnākara (F.198.a), he or she should “approach the transcendent state of wisdom through attaining the nirvāṇa” of all defiling things. “Being the same in their actual nature” means knowing that they are just the way their nature is. RK: sarvadharmāṇām iti sāṃkleśikānāṃ sarvadharmāṇāṃ nirvāṇa prāptitaḥ prajñāpāramitā ’nugantavyā| yathā teṣāṃ tathatā tathaiva ta iti jñānaṃ tathatāsamatā. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things neither come nor go, and are neither knowing nor born, because of their absolute freedom from birth.According to Ratnākara (F.198.a), the next sentence addresses how there is sameness with the actual nature. All things do not “come” from the future time and do not “go” to the past time, (a) because that from which it can come or toward which it can go does not exist, (b) because there is no “knowing” time in mere reflexive awareness, and (c) because coming and going are preceded by knowing. Therefore, they are not “born due to their absolute freedom from birth.” RK: tayā kathaṃ tathatāsamatety āha| sarvadharmā ityādi| nāgacchanty anāgatād adhvanaḥ, na gacchanty atītam adhvānaṃ, yata āgantavyaṃ yatra ca gantavyaṃ tayor asattvāt| yataś ca ajānānāḥ svasaṃvinmātre ’dhvanām ajñānāt| jñānapūrvakatvāc ca gatyāgamanayoḥ| tasmāt ajātā atyantājātita iti. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through not seeing a ‘self’ or an ‘other.’According to Ratnākara (F.198.a), not seeing “a ‘self’ or an ‘other’ ” because of not seeing a difference in that he or she sees all beings as the same as themselves. RK: As is stated in The Teaching of Vimalakīrti (7.11), “The buddha-qualities…grow in those who, after having produced a Sumeru-like mountain of egoistic views, conceive the spirit of enlightenment, from which all the buddha-qualities then arise.” RK: ātmā para iti yo bhedaḥ tasyādarśanāt sarvasattvānām ātmasamatādarśanād ity arthaḥ| tathā coktam āryavimalakīrtinirdeśe “sumerusamāṃ satkāyadṛṣṭim utpādya bodhicittam utpādyate| tataḥ sarvadharmā virohanti” iti. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things are noble worthy ones insofar as they are pure by nature.According to Ratnākara (F.198.b), above, after the word “ocean,” the Blessed One explains‍— as an antidote to conceiving (of the bodhisattva’s path) as “difficult”‍—the ways that things should be carefully considered. Among those ways, he says this first (among the sentences below that are related) to uproot the conception of difficulty sentient beings have regarding the state of a worthy one. In this context, the usage of the conventional expression “all things” is (limited to) sentient beings. They are “noble worthy ones since they are pure by nature,” i.e., due to their merely having the nature of all things. RK: ita ūrdhvaṃ duṣkara­saṃjñāpratipakṣeṇa yathā yathā pravekṣaṇīyaṃ tad āha samudraśabdād arvāk| tatrādau sattvānām arhattvadiṣu duṣkara­saṃjñānirāsārtham āha| sarvadharmāḥ sattvavyahāraviṣayāḥ| āryārhantaḥ yataḥ prakṛtipariśuddha| dharmatāmātratvāt. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things have laid down their burdens since they are not imposed upon by burdens.According to Ratnākara (F.198.b), “since they are not imposed upon by burdens” means because existence lacks an inherent nature. RK: bhārānāropaṇataye ti niḥsvabhāvatvāt bhāvasya. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through the lack of place or location of all things. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, physical forms have no place and no location, given the inherent nature of their essential nature.According to Ratnākara (F.198.b), he says this sentence in order to uproot the conception of it being “difficult” to travel to the buddhafields of the ten directions for the sake of taming beings. “Physical forms have no place, given the inherent nature of their essential nature” because that essential nature does not have a place‍—insofar as it is not a thing that disintegrates‍—nor is it a “location” because, like space, it has no parts. RK: sattvavinayāya daśadigbuddha­kṣetra­gamanena duṣkara­saṃjñānirāsāyāha| sarvadharmāṇāṃ adeśāpradeśata iti| adeśatvāc ca prakṛtisvabhāvata iti| rūpā dīnāṃ prakṛtis tasyāḥ svabhāvataḥ| na hi tasyā deśo ’patanadharmatvāt| nāpi pradeśo niraṃśatvād ākāśavat. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through being delighted in the cessation of all things. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through neither liking nor disliking.According to Ratnākara (F.198.b-99.a), in regard to taming bodhisattvas, he says “through neither liking nor disliking.” Here, the word “liking” means total excitement about nirvāṇa. Since bodhisattvas lack that, they do not have a “liking.” The word “disliking” means total depression about cyclic existence. Since bodhisattvas lack that, they do not have a “disliking.” The transcendent state of wisdom is (approached) through neither of these. RK: bodhisattvavinayanam adhikṛtyāha| aratyaviratita iti| ratir atyantam utkaṇṭhā nirvāṇe| tadabhāvo bodhisattvānāṃ aratiḥ| viratir atyantam udvegaḥ saṃsāre| tadabhāvo bodhisattvānām avirati| tābhyāṃ prajñāpāramitā. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom through neither passion nor dispassion.According to Ratnākara (F.199.a), “neither passion nor dispassion” and so on is something to carefully consider in order to renounce (the world further). RK: araktāviraktataye tyādinā vairāgyāya pratyavekṣā. Why is that? Because, Subhūti, physical forms are neither impassioned nor unimpassioned by a nature with any reality, insofar as physical forms are pure by nature. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are neither impassioned nor unimpassioned by a nature with any reality, insofar as they are pure by nature. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things are detached since they are devoid of either attachment or freedom from attachment.According to Ratnākara (F.199.a), “all things are detached” means not habitually fixated upon. They are “devoid of either attachment or freedom from attachment” means devoid of attachment or its antidote, because neither of these is mentally perceived. This is something to carefully consider in order to eliminate conceptualization. RK: sarvadharmā asaktā ity anabhiniviṣṭāḥ| saṅgāsaṅgā saṅgatatpratipakṣau| tadvigatās tayor anupalambhād iti vikalpaprahāṇāya pratyavekṣā. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things are awakening through becoming aware of the knowing of a buddha.According to Ratnākara (F.199.a), here “all things” means all the (true) paths, i.e., the seven realizations (among the eight described in the Ornament). These are (called) “awakening” because they procure (unsurpassed, perfect) awakening. For this very reason, he says “since they are awakening through becoming aware of the knowing of a buddha.” The awakening, literally, is “becoming aware of,” i.e., procuring (the knowing of a buddha). This is the careful consideration aimed an awakening. RK: sarvadharmāḥ sarvamārgāḥ saptābhisamayāḥ| ta eva bodhiḥ| bodhiprāpakatvāt| ata evāha| bodhiḥ| buddhajñānāvabodhanataye ti| avabodhana prāpaṇa iti bodhiḥ| sulabhāvabodhanāya pratyavekṣā. Note: There are generally three types of “awakening” (bodhi) connected with disciples, solitary realizers, and buddhas. But a bodhisattva eschews the first two awakenings for the awakening of a buddha. Since the bodhisattvas on the ten levels have wisdom equal to or greater than the disciples and solitary realizers, Ratnākara is (in a manner of speaking) explaining that bodhisattvas are awakening, i.e., in the process of awakening. To allow for a distinction between the process of awakening and its end result, the Sanskrit words bodhi and avabodhana (which are etymologically related) are translated respectively as “awakening” and “becoming aware.” He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom as all things being empty, free from signs, and free from inclinations.According to Ratnākara (F.199.a), “all things,” due to bearing their own inherent characteristic, are “empty, free from signs, and free from inclinations.” They are “empty” because, in being nothing but consciousness, they are devoid of a (separate) object; they are “free from signs” because all conventional signs disappear (in that consciousness upon awakening); and they are “free from inclinations” because the inclinations of the three realms are absent in their body consisting of error. These three provide an abbreviated summary of the path. RK: sarvadharmāḥ svalakṣaṇadhāraṇāt| te śūnyānimittāpraṇihitā vijñāna­mātratvenārthavirahāt sarvanimittāstamayāt| bhrāntiśarīre traidhātu­kapraṇidhānābhāvāc ca yathākramam| iti mārgasaṃkṣepaḥ. Note: Ratnākara is unique in saying that bodhisattvas deliberately aim upon awakening to manifest a body consisting of error (i.e., the two form bodies) for the sake of beings. It is a little like a dream body deliberately manifested for others. No other Buddhist scholar agrees with this view. For more on error within Ratnākara’s philosophical system, see Seton 2022. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things are medicine, since they are preceded by benevolence.According to Ratnākara (F.199.a), here “all things” refers to all the bodhisattva paths. They are “medicine” in the sense of curing the severe disease of longing for the disciples’ path. He explains why that is by saying “since they are preceded by benevolence,” i.e., because “benevolence” is a precursor of those paths. RK: sarvadharmāḥ sarva­bodhisattva­mārgāḥ| bhaiṣajyam iti hīnayāna­spṛhāmahāvyādhiharāḥ| kuta ityāha| maitrīpūrvaṅgamataye ti| yato maitrī teṣāṃ puraḥsarī. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things are due to abiding in benevelonce, compassion, rejoicing, and impartiality.According to Ratnākara (F.199.b), furthermore, they are abiding in “benevolence” (and so on), because they are abiding in the four immeasurable states. RK: api ca| sarva caturbhir apramāṇaiḥ saha viharaṇāt matrīvihāriṇaḥ. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things are like Brahmā, since faults do not arise in them because of their freedom from the arising of all faults.According to Ratnākara (F.199.b), “like Brahma” means equal in terms of his purity. Very similarly, the general point is “faults do not arise in them” and the specific explanation is “due to their freedom from the arising of all faults.” Here “all faults” refers to the opposites of (the four immeasurables of) benevolence and so on‍—respectively, malevolence, harming, joylessness, and the pair of attachment and aversion. RK: brahmabhūtā iti brahmaṇā tulyāḥ| tadvad eva doṣānutpādanād ity uddeśaḥ| sarvadoṣānutpādanata iti nirdeśaḥ| sarvadoṣā maitryādīnāṃ vipakṣo yathākramaṃ vyāpādo vihiṃsā aratiḥ pratighānunayau ca. Note: Brahman, brahma, or brahmā can refer to the being or to purity, etc., and has been translated different ways in the text and Ratnākara’s comments to bring out the meaning. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom comprehending that all things are due to the freedom from inclination and freedom from revulsion.According to Ratnākara (F.199.b), “due to the freedom from inclination” means due to lacking excitement about nirvāṇa, and “due to the freedom from revulsion” means due to total depression in cyclic existence. This ends the thirteen kinds of progressive realizations in an uninterrupted series (i.e., the sixth of eight topics in the Ornament). Next he explains the realization in a single moment (i.e., the seventh topic of the Ornament) RK: apraṇihitata iti nirvānotkaṇṭhāvirahāt| apratihatita iti saṃsāre atyantānudvegāt| iti trayodaśavidho ’nupūrvābhisamayaḥ sānuṣaṅgaḥ|| ekakṣaṇābhisamayo vaktavyāḥ.

“He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom through it being as limitless as the ocean.According to Ratnākara (F.199.b), here, both the transcendent state of wisdom‍—due to its being a support (to the bodhisattva)‍—and the state of knowing all aspects‍—due to its never lacking the (proper) attentiveness‍—are referred to as (the single term) “the transcendent state of wisdom,” denoting the (ocean-like) deep and vast single moment of the mindset of generosity and so on. Its “limitlessness” is the state of attracting the qualities free of worldly influences (anāsrava). He or she “should approach” that, i.e., be aware of that, because their mutual connection (i.e., between qualities and the bodhisattva’s limitless transcendent state of wisdom) was practiced for a long time. He explains how that is so, with the phrase “through it being as limitless as the ocean.” The limitlessness of the deep and vast “ocean” is the thing that attracts all the rivers inclined toward it, all at once, because they merge with one another. The phrase “as limitless as the ocean” is a simile meaning that the transcendent state of wisdom is like that. This sentence corresponds to the realization of all the qualities free of worldly influence in one single moment (as described in the Ornament). RK: atra prajñāpāramitā parigṛhītatvāt, sarvākārajñatā manasikārā­virahitatvāc ca| gambhīrodāra ekaiko dānādicittakṣaṇaḥ prajñāpāramitety ucyate| tasyāparyantatā anāśravadharmākarṣakatvam| teṣām anyonyasambandhasya cirapariśīlitatvāt anugantavyā veditavyā| atha mity āha| samudrāparyantataye ti| gambhīrodārasya samudrasyāparyantatā sakṛd aparyantanimnagā­jalākarṣakatvam| tāsāṃ parasparasaṃsargāt| tayā samudrāparyantatayā upamayā| tadvad ity arthaḥ| iti sarvānāśravadharmaikacittakṣaṇābhisamayaḥ. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom through it being as limitless as the sky.According to Ratnākara (F.200.a), here the “transcendent state of wisdom” is at the stage with the qualities of maturation and all the positive qualities (whether with or without worldly influences). For this very reason, “the limitlessness” of those is the single flavor of existence and nonexistence within those, and also the noneffort (toward them) because that is born from the maturation (of those qualities). He explains how this is so with the illustrating example “as limitless as the sky,” i.e., space, because its single flavor with regard to both physical substances and their nonexistence is limitless and effortless, because it does not stir (in either case). Thus, the limitlessness of both is similar. This is the second point (in the seventh topic of the Ornament): the realization of the qualities of maturation and the positive qualities in a single moment of a mindset. RK: prajñāpāramitā yāḥ vipākadharmatāvasthā sarvaśuklamayī ca| ata evāparyantatā| teṣu sarveṣu bhāvābhāvaikarasā| nirābhogaś ca vipākajatvāt| tasyā aparyantatā veditavyā | katham ityāha| gaganāparyantatayā dṛṣṭānteneti| gaganam ākāśam| tac ca rūpidravyābhāvaikarasam aparyantaṃ nirābhogaṃ ca parispandābhāvāt| ata ubhayor api aparyantatā sadṛśīti vipākajasarvaśukladharmaikacittakṣaṇābhisamayo dvitīyaḥ. He or she should approach the multifaceted nature of the transcendent state of wisdom through its nature being as multifaceted as Mount Meru.According to Ratnākara (F.201.a), here, since there is a lack of characteristics in all things, which are like a magical illusion, the “transcendent state of wisdom” refers to knowing them with their single flavor. Hence, even though the transcendent state of wisdom has that single flavor, one should know that “multifaceted nature” with the illusory appearances of all things through the illustrating example of the multifaceted nature of “Mount Meru.” For just as space has no form, it appears as multifaceted as Mount Meru, because on the four sides of Meru it appears with all the colors. So, this is the third point (in the seventh topic of the Ornament): the realization in a single moment of a mindset with all qualities belonging to it being free of characteristics. RK: māyopameṣu sarvadharmeṣv alakṣaṇatvena yad ekarasaṃ jñānaṃ seha prajñāpāramitā| atas tasyā ekarasatve pi māyopamaiḥ sarva­dharma­pratibhāsair yā vicitratā sā meruvicitratayā dṛṣṭāntena veditavyā| yathā hy ākāśasya nīrūpatvād asatī meruṇā vicitratā ’vabhāti| merupārśvānāṃ caturvarṇānāṃ dikṣu tatsarvavarṇatayā prakhyānāt| tathā tasyā ity alakṣaṇa­sarva­dharmaika­citta­kṣaṇābhisamaya­stṛtīyaḥ. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of physical forms. Likewise, he or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness.According to Ratnākara (F.201.b), here the “transcendent state of wisdom” refers to the fourth realization in a single moment. That is “limitless” because this realization knows the pure ultimate reality of all things like the pure, unadulterated sky. So, one “should approach,” i.e., know, that transcendent state of wisdom. He explains how so with the limitlessness of all “physical forms” and so on. How are they limitless? Because the dualistic appearances disappear and it is only as long as there is duality that there is a limit due to be circumscribed (by that duality). RK: iha caturtha ekakṣaṇābhisamayaḥ prajñāpāramitā | asyā aparyantatā| sarva­dharma­paramārthasya kevalasya kevalavimalanabhani­bhasya tena saṃvedanāt| sā veditavyā| katham ityāha| rūpā dīnām aparyantaye ti| kathaṃ teṣām aparyantatā ? dvaya­prati­bhāsāstamayāt| yāvat khalu dvayaṃ tāvat paryantaḥ parimitatvāt. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as illuminating limitlessly like the sun’s halo of rays.According to Ratnākara (F.202.b), absolutely everything is a pure crystal devoid of faults such as fear and so on. Among (crystals), any others beside the sun and moon are metaphors for the liberation body of the disciples and solitary realizers. The sun and moon are also pure crystals, yet their superiority is that they produce rays of light pervading all directions. So, these two are for comparing (the disciple and solitary realizer liberation bodies) to the liberation body of the buddhas that gives rise to infinite knowing and infinite objects of knowing. Hence, it is called the body of qualities (dharmakāya), because it is the basis for infinite qualities. So, here, the “transcendent state of wisdom” refers to the infinite knowing born from the purity of the ultimate realm of things (dharmadhātu). He or she should know “the limitlessness” of objects of knowing belonging to “the transcendent state of wisdom” through the example of it “illuminating; limitlessly like the sun’s halo of rays.” To explain, as the perfect purity of the buddha qualities, the ultimate realm of things is like the sun. The wisdom born from it is the “transcendent state of wisdom,” which is like the sun’s halo of rays. Its limitless illumination is like the sun’s rays illuminating limitlessly. As the perfect purity of the buddhas is due to this illumination, it is the natural body (svābhāvikakāya) and the power of its knowing is demonstrated with respect to objects of knowing everywhere. RK: sarva eva trāsādidoṣarahitāḥ sphāṭikāḥ śuddhāḥ| tatra candrasūryābhyām anye śrāvakapratyekabuddhānāṃ vimuktikāyasyopamāḥ| sūryācandram asāv api śuddhasphāṭikau| tayor etad adhikaṃ yat tau daśadigvyāpiraśmijālaṃ janayataḥ| tatas tāv anantajñeyaviṣayam anantaṃ jñānam utpādayato buddhānāṃ vimuktikāyasyopamāne| ata evāsau dharmakāya ity abhidhīyate| anantadharmāśrayatvāt| tad iha dharmadhātuviśuddhijam anantaṃ jñānaṃ prajñāpāramitā| tasyā aparyanta jñeyāvabhāsanatā veditavyā sūrya­raśmi­maṇḍalāparyantāvabhāsanatayā dṛṣṭāntena| tathā hi buddhadharmāṇāṃ suviśuddho dharmadhātuḥ sūryavat| tajjā prajñā prajñapāramitā sūryaraśmimaṇḍalavat| tayā yad aparyantāvabhāsanaṃ tad ravi­raśmi­maṇḍalenāparyantāvabhāsanavat| tad anena buddhānāṃ suviśuddho dharmadhātuḥ svābhāvikaḥ kāyas tasya ca sarvatra jñeye jñānavaśitā darśitā. Note: Ratnākara, like other Yogācāra scholars, takes “natural body” (svābhāvikakāya) to be a synonym of the truth body (dharmakāya), which is the source of all qualities, and hence can be understood to mean “body of qualities” (dharmakāya) in certain contexts. Hari explains the natural body (svābhāvikakāya) and truth body (dharmakāya) as two separate bodies, meaning that there are four bodies, not three as Ratnākara is explaining here. For more on the Indian controversy around the number of bodies intended here, see Makransky 1997. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of all sounds.According to Ratnākara (F.203.a), much about the enjoyment body is to be explained (in relation to this next sentence). Regarding the enjoyment in it, he says this sentence. Here, the “transcendent state of wisdom” refers to the realization (saṃbodha) of the supreme Mahāyāna Dharma, which belongs to both the teacher teaching it and the bodhisattvas listening to it. That realization itself is the enjoyment, because it is the thing causing enjoyment. However, the main “enjoyment” (in the enjoyment body) is born from the “transcendent state of wisdom” as the experience of the enjoyable feeling (sukha) of pleasure, joy, and delight characterized by mild, medium, and exceeding mental satisfaction. Hence, one should know “the limitlessness” of that “transcendent state of wisdom.” The Blessed One explains how this is so through the phrase “due to the limitlessness of all sounds” in the sense of it being a cause (hetau tṛtīyā). The “limitless sounds” of the teachings refers to both what is generating the sound and the sound generated, which, respectively, belong to the two realizations (of the tathāgata and of his retinue of bodhisattvas). What are those “all” like? They are all varied, because they appear in and through all representational forms (ākāra) in conformity with the minds of disciples. How are they “limitless”? They are “limitless” because the teaching by that endless body is unlimited. Also, the realization of the tathāgata is “limitless.” RK: sāmbhogikasya kāyasya bahu vaktavyam| tatra sambhogam adhikṛtyāha| sarvaśabdāparyantatayā prajñāpāramitāparyāntatānugantavye ti śāstur deśayataḥ śrṛṇvatāṃ ca bodhisattvānāṃ yaḥ parama­mahā­yāna­dharmasaṃbodhaḥ sā prajñāpāramitā| sa eva sambhogaḥ sambhogakāraṇatvāt| yaḥ punas tajjānāṃ mṛduma­dhyādhimātra­saumanasya­lakṣaṇānāṃ ratiprītiprāmodyānāṃ sukhasya cānubhavaḥ sa mukhyaḥ sambhogaḥ| tasyāḥ prajñāpāramitāyā aparyantatā veditavyā| katham ity āha| sarva­śabdhāparyāntataye ti| hetau tṛtīyā| yatas tayoḥ sambodhayor yathākramam aparyantā deśanāḥ śabdajanyā janakāś ca te kathaṃ sarve ? nānā vineyānurūpaiḥ sarvair ākāraiḥ prathanāt| katham aparyantā ḥ ? anidhanena tena kāyena deśanāyā niravadhikatvāt| tāthāgatasyāpi sambodhasyāparyantatā. Note: Ratnākara explains the next eight sentences as corresponding to numerous different qualities of the enjoyment body, including the thirty-two major and eighty minor marks, and many others. His explanation of these goes on for many, many pages and will not be translated here. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of the attainment of the buddha’s qualities. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of the accumulation of merit and knowledge by all the masses of sentient beings. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of the earth element. Likewise, he or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of the water element, the fire element, the wind element, the space element, and the consciousness element.

“He or she should approach the nature beyond measure of the transcendent state of wisdom as the nature beyond measure of gathering virtuous and unvirtuous qualities. He or she should approach the nature beyond measure of the transcendent state of wisdom as the nature beyond measure of gathering all the qualities. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as having attained the limitlessness of all the qualities and meditative concentrations. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of all the qualities of a buddha. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of all things.According to Ratnākara (F.211.a–b), “all things” here means all those things comprised of the body or speech of beings, either moving or stationary, on the earth, in the water, or in the atmosphere that are emanated (nirmita) in infinite world systems by the Blessed One (as an emanation body) in order to tame beings. One should know “the limitlessness” of the Blessed One’s “wisdom from the limitlessness” of those physical and verbal things. RK: sarva­dharmāparyantatayā prajñā­pāramitā’paryantatā’nugantavye ti| yāvantaḥ sthala­jalāntarikṣa­carāṇāṃ sthāvarajaṅgamānāṃ sattvānāṃ kāyavāksaṃgṛhītā dharmāḥ sattvānāṃ vinayanāya bhagavatā ananteṣu lokadhātuṣu nirmitāḥ te sarvadharmās teṣām aparyantatayā bhagavataḥ prajñāyā aparyantatā veditavyā. Note: Ratnākara’s explains that an emanation body (nairmāṇikakāya) is not a body separate from the enjoyment body, and hence does not have its own mind. It is just the way that the enjoyment body appears to sentient beings. Needless to say, Ratnākara’s view of this differs greatly from Hari’s and other scholars. The next few sentences correspond to the qualities of the emanation body. These comments will not be translated here. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of emptiness. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of mindsets and the components of mindsets. He or she should approach the limitlessness of the transcendent state of wisdom as the limitlessness of mental wanderings. He or she should approach the transcendent state of wisdom as unlimited just as virtuous and unvirtuous qualities are unlimited. He or she should approach the roaring of the transcendent state of wisdom as the roaring of a lion’s roar.According to Ratnākara (212.a), a buddha’s three bodies have been taught. Now his enlightened activity should be taught. The condensed statement of it is when he says, “He or she should approach the roaring (nadanatā) of the transcendent state of wisdom as the roaring of a lion’s roar.” The lion is the supreme among animals. Due to his being like that, the Buddha is called the supreme among persons or (figuratively) a lion among persons. But here, due to his having reached the highest excellence, the Blessed One is called a lion in the sense of being supreme in comparison to all persons, including gods, men, and so on. His Dharma teachings are called the lion’s roar because they completely fulfill all aspirations and they are frightening to all creatures promulgating false doctrines, like the gem (known as) the lion’s banner. The “roaring” of a lion means his teachings. One should “approach” that wisdom of the Blessed One as (like a lion’s roar) means that one should know it as the perfection (of wisdom), because it has transcended to the other shore. But the “roaring of the lion” is teaching his enlightened activity of twenty-seven types. RK: uktās trayaḥ kāyāḥ| kāritram idānīṃ vaktavyam| tat samāsata āha| siṃhanādanadanatayā prajñā­pāramitānugantavye ti| mṛgāṇām uttamaḥ siṃhaḥ| tatsādharmyāt puruṣottamāḥ puruṣasiṃhā ity ucyante| iha tu prakarṣagater deva­manuṣyādi­sarva­puruṣottamas tathāgataḥ siṃha ity ucyate| tasya dharmaḥ sarvāśāparipūraṇatayā sarva­para­pravādimṛgabhīṣaṇatayā ca siṃhanāda ity ucyate| siṃhadhvajavat| tasya nadano deśakaḥ| tadbhāvas tattā| tayā bhagavataḥ prajñā gantavyā pāragamanāt pāramitā veditavyety arthaḥ| sa punaḥ siṃhanādanadanaḥ saptaviṃśatividhasya kāritradeśanā. Note: Ratnākara comments at length on the enlightened activity. Those comments will not be translated here. He or she should approach the inviolability of the transcendent state of wisdom as the unassailability of all things.According to Ratnākara (F.219.a), to demonstrate the pervasiveness of the Buddha’s enlightened activity, he says this sentence. Enlightened activity is behavior, and behavior is a “thing” according to the saying “a method, a thing with its behavior, and a nature are called a thing.” So “all things” refers to the twenty-seven types of enlightened activities. Their “unassailability” means their unscathability. It functions uninterruptedly to the limits of space as a vessel with all aspects for sentient beings located in all the realms within the ten directions. One should know that unassailability to be the same as the “unassailability of the transcendent state of wisdom”‍—referring here to the mother of the activities belonging to the Blessed One. This ends the section teaching the activity of the truth body. RK: kāritrasyaiva vyāpitvaṃ darśayitum āha| sarvadharmākopyatayā prajñā­pāramitākopyatā’nugantavye ti| kāritram ācāraḥ| ācāro dharmaḥ| “nyāyo hi sācāro dharmaḥ svabhāvaś ca dharmākhyaḥ” iti vacanāt| sarvo dharmaḥ saptaviṃśatividhaṃ kāritram| tasyākopyatā avadhyatā| daśadiksarva­loka­dhātusthiteṣu sarvasattveṣu sarvair ākāraiḥ yathā bhājanam avicchedena yāvad ākāśaṃ pravṛttiḥ| tayā tajjanikayā bhagavataḥ prajñāpāramitāyā akopyatā tādṛśy eva veditavyā| ity uktaṃ dharmakāyasya kāritram. Why is that?
“It is because physical forms are like the ocean. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like the ocean.
“Physical forms are like the sky. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like the sky.
“Physical forms are like the multifaceted Mount Meru. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like the multifaceted Mount Meru.
“Physical forms are like the limitless. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like the limitless.
“Physical forms are like the development of the sun’s halo of rays. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like the development of the sun’s halo of rays.
“Physical forms are as limitless as all sounds. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are as limitless as all sounds.

“Physical forms are as limitless as all the masses of sentient beings. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are as limitless as all the masses of sentient beings.This item in the series is present in the Sanskrit of Wogihara’s edition (pp. 911–2), but is missing in Vaidya’s edition and in the Tibetan. Note that it would appear to be additional to the series if the series here is compared to the matching individual elements in the preceding paragraphs, where even in Wogihara’s edition nothing resembling it is present.

“Physical forms are as limitless as the attainment of all the qualities of a buddha. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are as limitless as the attainment of all the qualities of a buddha.

“Physical forms are as limitless as the accumulation of merit and knowledge by all the masses of sentient beings. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are as limitless as the accumulation of merit and knowledge by all the masses of sentient beings.
“Physical forms are like earth. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like earth.
“Physical forms are like water. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like water.
“Physical forms are like fire. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like fire.
“Physical forms are like wind. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like wind.
“Physical forms are like space. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like space.
“Physical forms are like consciousness. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like consciousness.

“Physical forms are devoid of gathering virtuous and unvirtuous qualities. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are devoid of gathering virtuous and unvirtuous qualities.
“Physical forms are devoid of gathering all the qualities. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are devoid of gathering all the qualities.

“Physical forms are as limitless as all the qualities and meditative concentrations. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are as limitless as all the qualities and meditative concentrations.

“Physical forms are void as the inherent nature of physical forms, the actual nature of physical forms, and the qualities of a buddha. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are void as the inherent nature of consciousness, the actual nature of consciousness, and the qualities of a buddha.
“Physical forms are the limitless true nature of all things. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are the limitless true nature of all things.
“Physical forms are emptiness, the limitless true nature. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are emptiness, the limitless true nature.

“Physical forms are the limitlessness of mindsets and the components of mindsets. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are the limitlessness of mindsets and the components of mindsets.
“Physical forms are the mental wanderings. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are the mental wanderings.

“Physical forms are the virtuous and unvirtuous, up to the freedom from mental perception. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are the virtuous and unvirtuous, up to the freedom from mental perception.
“Physical forms are like the lion’s roar. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are like the lion’s roar.
“Physical forms are inviolable. Likewise, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are inviolable.
“A bodhisattva great being should approach the transcendent state of wisdom in this very way.

“Subhūti, if the bodhisattva great being will approach, explore, get into, become aware of, contemplate, weigh, and investigate this transcendent state of wisdom and cultivate it with attentiveness free from all self-deception and dishonesty; with attentiveness free from all conceit; with attentiveness free from self-superiority; with attentiveness free from all laziness; with attentiveness free from disparaging others; with attentiveness free from conceptions of oneself; with attentiveness free from conceptions of sentient beings; with attentiveness free from donations, tributes, and celebrity; with attentiveness free from the five obstructions; with attentiveness free from jealousy and envy; and with attentiveness free from all wavering, then it will not be difficult for him or her to attain the complete perfection of all the qualities, the complete perfection of the buddhafield, and the unsurpassed qualities of a buddha.”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Approach,” the twenty-ninth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 30: SadāpraruditaThis chapter corresponds in content to the first part of the narrative of Sadāprarudita’s quest to receive the transcendent state of wisdom teachings from Dharmodgata, as recounted in Chapters 73 and 74 of the Twenty-Five Thousand, and Chapter 85 of the Eighteen Thousand (the Hundred Thousand as witnessed in the Sanskrit and in the Tibetan of most Kangyurs does not include the narrative). The accounts in the different sūtras are similar in content, but with some significant differences. Generally speaking, the versions in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Eighteen Thousand are more similar to each other than either of them is to the version here in the Eight Thousand. There are differences of content and order in the Tibetan to an extent that may reflect different Sanskrit source texts, rather than simply representing independently made translations into Tibetan or resulting from divergences in successive layers of editing and revision.According to Ratnākara (F.224.b), the “Chapter on Sadāprarudita” is the chapter characterized by (the bodhisattva named) Sadāprarudita (“Always Crying”). RK: sadāprarudito palakṣitaḥ parivartas tatparivartaḥ. Note: As will be seen in the story, the bodhisattva Sadāprarudita always cries when overwhelmed with positive emotions, not worldly sadness or grief.
div.p: [The Blessed One continued,] “Furthermore, Subhūti, there is a bodhisattva great being, Sadāprarudita, presently going about his life engaged in spiritual practice in the presence of the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgata Bhīṣma­garjita­nirghoṣasvara.According to Ratnākara (F.221.a), the transcendent state of wisdom has been explained through the eight realizations (outlined in the Ornament). One must understand what has not (yet) been said about the way it is. So, wishing to explain this, the Blessed One said “furthermore” and adds the following (three chapters, 30 to 32). RK: aṣṭābhir abhisamayair bhagavatī nirdiṣṭā| sā yathā veditavyā tan noktam| atas tad bhagavān vivakṣur āha punaraparam ityādi. Note: The translation of the name Bhīṣma­garjita­nirghoṣasvara would be something like “Irresistible Thundering Melodious Voice,” based on the Tibetan mi bzad for bhīṣma. A bodhisattva great being should seek the transcendent state of wisdom in the same way that Sadāprarudita sought it.”
At this, the venerable Subhūti asked the Blessed One, “Blessed One, how did the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita seek the transcendent state of wisdom?”

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Subhūti, “Subhūti, in former times, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita sought the transcendent state of wisdom without concern for his physical well-being, without regard for his life, and without attachment to donations, tributes, or celebrity. While seeking the transcendent state of wisdom, he went to stay in the wilderness, and heard a voice from the sky saying, ‘Noble son, go to the east! There you will learn the transcendent state of wisdom.In this chapter, the term “Transcendent State of Wisdom” often refers to the scripture in the form of a book. Hence, many times the forms of the Sanskrit verb √śru are translated as, for instance, “learned” (śruta) rather than “heard” or “listened to,” because (a) the transcendent state of wisdom cannot be “heard” from a book, as it is presented in this chapter, and (b) the entire scripture also talks about learning it by taking it in, memorizing it, and so forth as the preliminary step toward realizing the actual transcendent state of wisdom. As you go, do not pay attention to physical fatigue. Do not pay attention to whether you feel drowsy or sleepy. Do not pay attention to whether you have food or water. Do not pay attention to whether it is night or day. Do not pay attention to whether it is cold or hot.According to Ratnākara (F.221.a), the prohibition of paying attention “to physical fatigue” signifies an exceedingly heroic deed. RK: kāyaklamathādimanasikārā ṇāṃ pratiṣedho vīryātiśayārthaḥ. Do not let your mind fixate upon anything inside or outside. Noble son, do not go looking left or right. Do not go looking either south, east, west, north, up, down, or in any direction in between. Noble son, as you go, do not proceed on the basis of a self or the transitory collection. Do not proceed on the basis of physical forms, feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, or consciousness, since one who proceeds on such a basis moves backward.According to Ratnākara (F.221.a), the sentences beginning with “Noble son, as you go…” are prohibiting attentiveness to unreal things. The term satkāya, “transitory collection,” is etymologically derived from the verb √sad, meaning something is perishing (translated here as “transitory”), while the word kāya is translated “collection” because it is the heaps (of the constituents of experience). The constituents of experience such as physical form and so on are impermanent; hence, it is called the transitory collection. One assigns the name “person” to this transitory collection. So, when it says “not on the basis of a self or the transitory collection,” it means not based on your having the nature of a person with either real existence or nominal existence, because neither of these (types of existence) is mentally perceived. When he says “on the basis of physical forms” and so on, it refers to the constituents of experience, namely physical forms and so on, because these things too are not mentally perceived. One who “proceeds on such a basis” means one who proceeds on the basis of these things; “moves backward” means turns away due to projecting (existence) onto those. The rest here can be easily understood (without commentary). RK: tathā ca kulaputre tyādinā atattvamanasikārāṇāṃ pratiṣedhaḥ| nātmato na satkāyata iti| sīdatīti sat| rāśitvāt kāyaḥ| anityo rūpādirāśiḥ satkāya ity ucyate| satkāye pudgalaḥ prajñapyate| ataś ca nātmato na satkāyataś cohī ti| na dravyasatā pudgalarūpeṇa nāpi prajñaptisatā| ubhayor anupalambhād ity arthaḥ| na rūpataś ce tyādi| nāpi rūpādibhiḥ skandhaiḥ| dharmāṇām apy anupalabdher iti bhāvaḥ| ataś calatī ti ebhiś calati| eṣāṃ samāropeṇa vitiṣṭhata iti nivartate| śeṣaṃ sugamam. See n.341 for another etymology and meaning of satkāya. From what does one move backward? One moves backward from the qualities of a buddha. Furthermore, anyone who moves backward from the qualities of a buddha goes about life in cyclic existence. Anyone who goes about life in cyclic existence does not go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and does not reach the transcendent state of wisdom.’

“At this, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita said to the voice, ‘I will do exactly as you say. Why will I do that? Because I wish to provide a light for all sentient beings and to develop the qualities of a buddha.’According to Ratnākara (F.221.b), “light” means to teach the path of liberation. “To develop” means he wishes to give rise perfectly (to those qualities) himself. RK: ālokam iti mokṣamārgasya deśanām| samudānetukāma iti| ātmani samyag utpādayitukāmaḥ.
“At this, the voice said to the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, ‘Excellent, noble son. Excellent, Sadāprarudita.’

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita listened for words once again and heard this: ‘Noble son, once you develop firm conviction that all things are emptiness, free from signs, and free from inclinations, you should seek the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.221.b), having “firm conviction” means determining a fact to be absolutely true.” RK: adhimuktim iti| evam evaitad ity avadhāraṇam. Also, you should give up conventional signs, give up existence, and give up the belief in a sentient being. Noble son, you should give up unvirtuous friends and instead attend to virtuous friends, be devoted to them, and serve them along with anyone teaching the Dharma that all things are empty, free from signs, free from inclinations, free from arising, free from birth, free from cessation, and free from any state of being.According to Ratnākara (F.221.b), regarding things being “empty, free from signs, free from inclinations,” they are “empty” because an intentional thing does not exist the way it falsely appears; “free from signs” because the false appearance, which is an erroneous conventional sign, ceases naturally; and “free from inclinations” because there is no inclination toward anything in the three realms that is seen to be a mere cognitive error. Also, there is no arising, birth, or cessation (aniruddha) of something without an inherent nature. Something without any state of being has no arising or cessation. Therefore, a virtuous friend is someone who teaches the freedom from arising, birth, cessation, or any state of being. Also, the reverse of that is implicitly suggested here, namely that an unvirtuous friend does not teach these things. RK: śūnyānimittāpraṇihiteṣu| yathāpratibhāsam arthābhāvāc chūnyeṣu| tasya ca pratibhāsasya bhrāntinimittasya prakṛtinirodhād animitteṣu| ataś ca bhrāntimātratayā dṛṣṭe traidhātuke praṇidhānābhāvād apraṇihiteṣu| … niḥsvabhāvasya notpādo na jātir na nirodhaḥ| yasya notpādanirodhau so ’bhāvaḥ| tasmād anutpādā ajātā anirodhā abhāvāḥ sarvadharmā ity evaṃ yāni dharmaṃ deśayanti tāni kalyāṇamitrāṇi| arthād gamyate tadviparītaṃ dharma yāni deśayanti tāni pāpamitrāṇi. Note: Ratnākara also says here, “In some manuscripts, the word śūnyatā appears (instead of śūnya), but in these cases the -tā suffix is pleonastic.” RK: śūnyatā śabdaḥ kvacit paṭhyate| tatra svārthe tal. Noble son, when you progress along the path in this way, it will not be long before you will get to learn the transcendent state of wisdom either in a book or physically with a monk teaching the Dharma. Noble son, with whomever you happen to learn the transcendent state of wisdom, you should conceive of him or her as a teacher, and with appreciation and gratitude think, “This is the virtuous friend with whom I am learning this transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.221.b), “appreciation” is based on knowing what has been learned and “gratitude” is based knowing the fruits of what has been learned. RK: kṛtajña śrutajñānāt| kṛtavedī śrutaphalajñānāt. By listening to this person, I will quickly reach the state beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and I will be close to the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas. I will be reborn in a buddhafield and never be without a tathāgata. I will completely leave behind inauspicious birth and gain the good fortune of auspicious rebirth.” Noble son, by weighing such advantages, you should conceive of the monk preaching the Dharma as your teacher. In doing so, you should follow that monk preaching the Dharma not with a mindset attached to worldly enjoyments but with a mindset longing for the Dharma and devoted to it.According to Ratnākara (F.221.b–22.a), “worldly enjoyments” means food, drink, clothing and so on. Being “attached to” those means desiring them. RK: lokāmiṣaṃ annapānavastrādi| tena pratisaṃyuktā tadabhilāṣiṇī. You must recognize the activities of Māra, because, noble son, the pernicious Māra himself imitates a bodhisattva great being preaching the Dharma in order to partake of visible forms, sounds, scents, flavors, and physical feelings, to enjoy them, and to be involved with them.According to Ratnākara (F.222.a), the three verbs “partakes of” and so on mean to consume. RK: sevitum ityādeḥ padatrayasya paribhoktum ity arthaḥ. However, noble son, you should not develop a mindset of distrust toward a monk preaching the Dharma, if he or she has overcome these things, and yet, out of skill in means, partakes of them, enjoys them, and is involved with them. Instead, you should develop the proper mindset, thinking, “I do not know the skill in means that this Dharma preacher wisely understands. The Dharma preacher partakes of these things, enjoys them, and is involved with them in order to tame sentient beings and support their roots of virtue, because bodhisattva great beings do not have any attachment to anything, nor do they possess sense objects.”According to Ratnākara (F.222.a), “He or she has overcome” means he or she is free from vices. “In order to tame sentient beings” means in order to protect their minds; “support” means assist; “attachment” means craving; and “sense objects” means mental perceptions (of them). RK: abhibhūye ti nirdoṣīkṛtya| sattvavinayene ti sattvānāṃ cittarakṣārtham| parigraham upādāye ti parigrahārtham| saṅgas tṛṣṇā| ārambaṇam upalambhaḥ. Note: In place of “sense objects” (ārambaṇam), the Tibetan text reads “obscurations” (sgrib pa). Although the Tibetan makes good sense, this English translation has followed the Sanskrit edition to align with the comments of both Ratnākara and Hari. Also, at that time, noble son, you should investigate the true nature of of things. Noble son, what is the true nature of things? It is, to be specific, that all things are free of emotional defilement and free of purification.According to Ratnākara (F.222.a), “true nature” refers to the ontological analysis (of them). RK: bhūtanaya paramārthaprabhedaḥ. Why is that? Because all things are empty of inherent nature. All things are free of any sentient being, soul, personality, animating spirit, or person. They are like a magical illusion, a dream, an echo, or a reflection.According to Ratnākara (F.222.a), the first sentence, “free of any sentient being” and so on, explains the freedom from the identity of a person. The second sentence, “like a magical illusion” and so on, explains the freedom from the identity of things. RK: niḥsattvā dipadaiḥ pudgalanairātmyam āha| māyopamā dipadair dharmanairātmyam. Noble son, if you investigate the true nature of all things and follow a Dharma preacher, it will not be long before you go completely beyond cyclic existence in the transcendent state of wisdom. Also, noble son, you should keep in mind another activity of Māra. That is to say, noble son, while you are seeking the transcendent state of wisdom, if the Dharma preacher rebukes you or does not keep you in mind, then, noble son, you should not talk back but rather follow that monk preaching the Dharma with nothing but reverence for the Dharma and without your mind becoming depressed.’According to Ratnākara (F.222.a), “talk back” means reply (with resistance); not “depressed” means not distressed. RK: prativāṇiḥ prativacanam| anirviṇṇam akhinnam.

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita accepted this guidance from the voice and headed in the eastern direction.According to Ratnākara (F.222.a), “guidance” means instructions. RK: anuśāsanī upadeśaḥ. Not long after setting forth, it occurred to him that he did not ask the voice how far he should go. Then, right there in that spot where he stood, he began crying, weeping, grieving, and lamenting, thinking, ‘Until I hear the transcendent state of wisdom, I will spend day and night‍—whether it be two, three, four, five, six, or seven days and nights‍—in this very location.According to Ratnākara (F.222.a), “crying” means with tears falling; “weeping” means with miserable whimpering; “grieving” means longingly; and “lamenting” means wailing. RK: rudan aśrupātena| krandan dīnasvaraiḥ| śocan utkaṇṭhamānaḥ| paridevamāno vilapan. I will not pay attention to physical fatigue. I will not pay attention to whether I feel drowsy or sleepy. I will not pay attention to whether I have food or water. I will not pay attention to whether it is night or day. I will not pay attention to whether it is cold or hot.’ Subhūti, it was, as an analogy, just like a man filled with suffering and despair at the death of his only son. That man’s attentiveness would not be occupied with anything other than his grief for his son. Rather, he would be occupied with nothing but attentiveness to his only son. In this very same way, Subhūti, the attentiveness of the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita was occupied with nothing else but repeating to himself, ‘When will I ever get to learn the transcendent state of wisdom?’

“Then, Subhūti, after the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita had pined sadly away like that, the form of a tathāgata appear right in front of him and commended him: ‘Excellent, noble son. It is excellent that you have uttered these words. You are now seeking the transcendent state of wisdom the way that the former worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas previously sought it, when they were going about life engaged in spiritual practice. Thus, noble son, just follow [the guidance] and go east with nothing but this heroic perseverance, this courage, this longing, and this aspiration! Noble son, in one thousand two hundred milesThe text reads pañcabhir yojanaśatair, “five hundred yojanas.” There are different opinions about how far a yojana is. This translation follows the idea that a yojana is 2.5 miles. This means that 500 yojana is roughly 1,250 miles. Cf. MW yojana, where a yojana is 9 miles, meaning that 500 yojana is roughly 4,500 miles. from here, there is a city called Gandhavatī built out of the seven precious substances. The city is thirty miles long and thirty miles wide, surrounded with seven barrier walls, seven moats, and seven rows of palm trees. The city is thriving, successful, and safe. It has excellent provisions and is filled with many people and animals.According to Ratnākara (F.222.a), “thriving” with extensive wealth; “successful” with beautiful houses, gardens, and so on; “safe” means not susceptible to attack; “has excellent provisions” because food and drink are easy to come by; “filled with” means full of; and “many” means different types. RK: ṛddhā dhanasampattyā| sphītā gṛhodyānādiśobhayā| kṣemā nirupadravatvāt| subhikṣā sulabhānnapānatvāt| ākīrṇo vistīrṇaḥ| bahu nānāvidho. It has five separate one-hundred-aisle indoor bazaars, beautiful to behold like a brightly colored painting. It is well organized with a fixed order of streets for people, carriages, and vehicles, not overflowing, but harmonious for all.According to Ratnākara (F.222.b), “indoor bazaar” means an indoor place to buy and sell. In it are “one hundred aisles.” There are “five” of those “separate,” i.e., completely separated from one another, since their aisles go up to the end of a barrier on both sides. “Brightly colored” (as if) by “painting” because they are marvelous. They are “like” they are, because they are fit for a large fort town. “Not overflowing” with those means not thronged with those things. The “streets” means long carriage roads that are places for “people, carriages, and vehicles” to move about together. The streets have a “fixed order” (sthāpita<sthāpana). It is “well organized” i.e., well arranged, with those. There, the “people” such as pedestrians and so on; “the carriages,” i.e., wagons; and the “vehicles,” i.e., chariots, are “harmonious for all” because there are no potholes and there is equal (room) for one another. RK: antara syāntare āpaṇaḥ krayavikrayasthānam| tasmin vithīśatāni| taiḥ pañcabhiḥ nirviddhā niḥśeṣaṃ viddhā| vithī nām āprakārāntam ubhayato gamanāt| ālekhy ena vicitrāṇi ca tāni citrāṇi cādbhūtatvāt| sadṛśāni ca purānurūpatvāt| taiḥ anutpīḍam asaṃbādhaṃ janayugyayān ānāṃ saṃkramasthānāni saṃbhūyagamanasthānāni vistīrṇarathyāḥ| teṣāṃ sthāpitāni sthāpanāni| bhāve ktaḥ| taiḥ sumāpitā suracitā| tatra janaḥ padikādilokāḥ| yugyāni vāhanāni| yānāni rathāḥ| anantaratvāt parasparatulyatvāc ca samasamaiḥ.

“ ‘Surrounding the city are ramparts made of the seven precious substances. Also, those ramparts‍—made of the seven precious substances‍—have large parapets made of gold from the River Jambu that rise above them.According to Ratnākara (F.222.b), “battlements” (khoḍakaśīrṣāṇi; mda’ yab) are (the parts of the rampart at) the top of the steps (krama; ka gzhu). “Rise above” means extending upward due to sticking out somewhat from the broad rampart. “Large” means high and wide. RK: khoḍakaśīrṣāṇi kramaśīrṣāṇi| upodgatānī ti prākārād ativistīrṇāt kiñcinnirgatyodgatāni| pramāṇavantī ti pṛthūni tuṅgāni ca. Note: The Tibetan translation of pramāṇavantī as mtho zhing tshad dang ldan pa for pramāṇavanti here (F.263.b) suggests that the Tibetan translation of the Eight Thousand was indeed edited or revised following Ratnākara’s commentary, among others. The Tibetan translators’ colophon (see Translators’ colophon from the Tibetan and introduction) sets out several stages in which this may have been the case. See also n.16. On top of all the battlements, fruit trees grow, laden with various types of marvelous fruits made of seven precious substances. A string made of seven precious substances is connected from each tree on the battlements to the other trees on the next battlement, enveloping the entire city in a net with small golden bells attached, so that a sweet, charming, and pleasant sound emerges from the small bells when gently shaken by the wind.According to Ratnākara (F.222.b), (the strings) are coming out “from each tree on the battlements” to the “other trees” in a “connected” manner. “Entire” means that everything is within it. The “net with small bells fastened to it” is hanging down like many strings of pearls fastened in a square (crisscrossing way) with other strings of pearls connected to the trees on the battlements of the four city walls. RK: sarvataś ca khoḍakavṛkṣān nirgatā vṛkṣāntaram| yathābhavati tath āvasaktam| sarvam asyām astīti sarvāvatī| kiṅkiṇījālene ti catuṣprākāra­khoḍakavṛkṣāvasakta­maṇi­sūtra-catuṣṭayāvabaddhāneka­maṇisūtra­valambinā. As an analogy, it is just like the sweet, charming, and pleasant sound that emerges from the five musical instruments played by skilled singers when singing together.According to Ratnākara (F.222.b), it is “sweet” because it is pleasant to hear. “Charming” means captivating. The “five types of musical instruments” refers to lutes, flutes, and so on. RK: valguḥ| śrotrasukhatvāt| rañjanīyo manoharatvāt| pañcāṅgāni vīṇāvaṃśādīni. In the same way, the sweet, charming, and pleasant sound emerges from the small bells being shaken by the wind, and with that sound, the city’s inhabitants are entertained and delighted, and amuse themselves.According to Ratnākara (F.222.b), “entertained” through the ears; “delighted” mentally; and “amuse themselves” with physical and verbal activities. RK: krīḍanti śrotreṇa, ramante manasā, paricārayanti kāyavākparispandaiḥ.

“ ‘All around the city are moats completely full of water that is not too cold or too hot, and that flows along harmoniously.According to Ratnākara (F.222.b), “moats” are ditches. These are endowed with a water current in “harmony” with the wind and so on. RK: parikhāḥ khātayaḥ| anusārivārivāhinya iti vātādyanusāri ṇā jalapravāhe ṇa yuktāḥ. In that water, manifesting from the ripening of previous karmic deeds by the city’s inhabitants, there are beautiful, brightly colored boats made of the seven precious substances. The city’s inhabitants aboard them are entertained and delighted, and amuse themselves. The water itself is completely covered with blue lotuses, pink lotuses, white water lilies, white lotuses, and the most excellent fragrantly scented blossoms‍—there is no type of blossom anywhere in the billionfold, great order world system that is lacking there!According to Ratnākara (F.222.b), “pink lotuses” are reddish kamalas and “white lotuses” are white padmas. The “most excellent” means the most distinguished. The word “fragrant” is a synonym of sweet-smelling. RK: padmaṃ raktakamalam| puṇḍarīkaṃ sitapadmam| abhijātābhijātar iti praṇītapraṇītaiḥ| sugandha śabdaḥ surabhiparyāyaḥ. Note: Dictionaries are not univocal on the color and type of flowers represented by the Sanskrit words here, so this translation follows Ratnākara.

“ ‘Surrounding the city, there are five hundred gardens. They all are made of the seven precious substances, brightly colored and beautiful, and, in each of those gardens, there are twenty-five hundred lotus pools. The size of each of the pools is a half mile around. In all those lotus pools appear blue lotuses, pink lotuses, white water lilies, and white lotuses that cover the water. All the blue lotuses, pink lotuses, white water lilies, and white lotuses are fragrant, with a circumference the size of a cart wheel. Some are blue, blue tinted, blueish, or seemingly blue. Some are yellow, yellow tinted, yellowish, or seemingly yellow. Some are red, red tinted, reddish, or seemingly red. Some are white, white tinted, whitish, or seemingly white. All the lotus pools resonate with the calls of geese, cranes, ducks, curlews, and brahminy ducks. All the gardens are public property, not private, and are manifesting from the ripening of previous karmic deeds by the city’s inhabitants, who, for a long time, obviously have gone about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and, for a long time with the proper mindset of listening to the sugatas, have approached with respect for the guidance of the buddhas and had firm conviction in their profound Dharma teachings.According to Ratnākara (F.223.a), “the guidance of the buddhas” refers to the transcendent state of wisdom. “Respect” means with extraordinarily clear trust in it. RK: buddhanetrī prajñāpāramitā| tasyā citrīkāraḥ paraḥ prasādaḥ.

“ ‘At the intersection in the center of the city of Gandhavatī, noble son, lies the estate of the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata. It is two miles in circumference, made of the seven precious substances, brightly colored, beautiful, and surrounded with seven walls and seven rows of palm trees. Within that estate, encircling the house, are four gardens for enjoyment and for leisure.According to Ratnākara (F.223.a), “for enjoyment” of fruits, flowers, and so on, “and for leisure,” meaning entirely for enjoying by being in the forest and so on. RK: upabhogaparibhogāye ty upabhogaḥ phalapuṣpādīnām| paribhogaḥ sākalyena bhogo vanavihārādinā. The gardens are called Always Delighted, Sorrowless, Sorrow Free, and Brightly Colored Blossoms. In each garden, there are eight lotus pools, namely, Good Lotus Pool, Best Lotus Pool, Joyful Lotus Pool, Most Joyful Lotus Pool, Patient Lotus Pool, Most Patient Lotus Pool, Temperate Lotus Pool, and The Never Flowing Away Lotus Pool. The first side of each lotus pool is gold, the second silver, the third beryl, and the fourth crystal. The bottom of each pool consists of quartz with golden sand spread over it. In each one of the lotus pools, there are eight staircases with each step adorned by various brightly colored precious substances. In all the spaces between the staircase steps there appears a plantain tree made of gold from the River Jambu. The water in all the lotus pools is blanketed with various blue lotuses, red lotuses, white water lilies, and pink lotuses, and resonates with the calls of geese, cranes, ducks, curlews, and brahminy ducks. All around the lotus pools there are various flowering trees. When shaken by the wind, the flowers in these flowering trees float downward into the lotus pools, so that in all the lotus pools the water has a sandalwood scent, color, taste, and feel.

“ ‘In that estate, the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata is surrounded by a retinue with sixty-eight thousand women. Being endowed with the five types of objects of sensual desire and enjoying them, he is entertained and delighted, and amuses himself.According to Ratnākara (F.223.a), “endowed” means united with the sense objects of pleasure on that occasion. “Enjoying” means being connected with enjoyments that please all the senses. He is “entertained” with words; “delighted” mentally; and “amuses himself” physically. RK: samarpitaḥ saṅgamitaḥ| tair avakāśaguṇaiḥ| sarvendriyāṇām anugrahaḥ samanvaṅgaḥ| tadyogāt samanvaṅgī| krīḍati vācā| ramate manasā| paricārayati kāyena. All the other people‍—both the men and women who are inhabitants in that city‍—also are endowed with the five types of objects of sensual desire and enjoy them. They are constantly joyful, entertained, and delighted, and amusing themselves in the gardens and among the lotus pools. But it is only for so long that the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata is entertained, delighted, and amuses himself with his retinue; afterward, he teaches the transcendent state of wisdom during the three daily time periods.According to Ratnākara (F.223.a), “for so long” means for a limited time. “Afterward” means after being entertained and so on. The “three daily time periods” are the morning, midday, and evening. RK: tāvat kālam iti parimitakālam| tata iti krīḍādikālād ūrdhvam| trikālam iti prāṇtamadhyāntāparānteṣu. The inhabitants of the city of Gandhavatī prepare a throne for the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata in the city’s central square, with a base of gold, silver, beryl, and crystal.Cf. BHSD prajñapayanti, “arrange, provide a seat.” Cf. BHSD pādakaṃ, “foot of a seat.” It is covered with a cotton cushion, then a wool coverlet, next a cloth on top, and finally muslin fabric from Kāśi, to the height of six hundred feet.According to Ratnākara (F.223.a), “muslin fabric from Kāśi” could be either from Vārānasī or from a heaven (presumably associated with Kāśi). RK: kāśikavastraṃ vārāṇaseyakaṃ divyaṃ vā. High above the throne in the sky, the inhabitants together carefully hold up a cloth canopy adorned with pearls, evenly so that it is well formed. How is that possible? Because their various karmic ripenings are extremely well formed.According to Ratnākara (F.223.a), they hold it up “evenly”‍—not in an uneven manner using long posts. “Together” means in unison. “Carefully” means with attentiveness. He explains how it is possible (that they can do all this) with the words “because” and so on. RK: samaṃ yathā bhavati na viṣamaṃ tathā dhārayanti pramāṇavadbhiḥ stambhaiḥ| sahitāḥ samagrāḥ| niratāḥ sāvadhānāḥ| katham ity āha| kim ayaṃ saṃsthita iti| kuta ityāha| susaṃsthite tyādi.

“ ‘All around the area of the throne, they strew, spread, and scatter five-colored blossoms.According to Ratnākara (F.223.b), “strew” them right in front, “spread” them around the three sides, but “scatter” them even around the fourth (i.e., the back side). RK: abhyavakiranti purastāt| prakaranti triṣu pārśveṣu| saṃpravikiranti punaś caturṣv api pārśveṣu. And, out of their pure motivation and devotion to the Dharma of the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, they painstakingly ensure that the area is scented with various perfumes and incenses. Then, once seated on that throne, Dharmodgata teaches the transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.223.b), regarding their “pure motivation,” the purity concerns their faith and aspirations for the teachings. Their “devotion” to the teachings is because of their virtuous friend. RK: dharmāśayaviśuddhye ti dharme yā teṣāṃ śraddhācchandayoḥ śuddhiḥ| dharmāya gauravaṃ kalyāṇamitratvāt.

“ ‘Noble son, those sentient beings listen to the transcendent state of wisdom in the direct presence of the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata with devotion to the Dharma, with readiness for the qualities, with trust in the trustworthy, and giving rise to faith.According to Ratnākara (F.223.b), their “readiness” means their being a good vessel. RK: sanniśrayatā bhājanatā. Among both gods and humans, many beings‍—hundreds, thousands, hundreds of thousands of living beings‍—come together there to listen to it. With the proper attentiveness, some of them explain it, some recite it, some write it out, and some follow along. But all those sentient beings have the qualities of not falling into lower births, and are beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Noble son, go to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata! From him you will hear the transcendent state of wisdom, because for a long time, noble son, he has been your virtuous friend, helping you to see clearly, to take in the practice, to be enthusiastic, and to rejoice about unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Noble son, he also previously sought the transcendent state of wisdom just as you are seeking it now. So, noble son, go there, keeping this in mind without interruption day or night, and it will not be long before you will hear the transcendent state of wisdom!’

“Then, after hearing this, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita felt pleased, cheerful, gladdened, delighted, and filled with joy and satisfaction.According to Ratnākara (F.223.b), “pleased” (tuṣṭa) means content, “cheerful” means one’s upper body is uplifted because it is content; “gladdened” is because of having that single content feeling in mind; “delighted” is because being content increases; and “filled with joy and satisfaction” means filled with the trust that comes along with being content. RK: tuṣṭa iti prītaḥ| udagra iti tayā prītyā unnatapūrvakāyaḥ| tadekatānatvāt āttamanaskaḥ| prīter utkarṣāt pramuditaḥ| prīti sahagataḥ prasādaḥ prītisaumanasyam| tajjātam asyeti tathoktaḥ. It was just like, as an analogy, for a man pierced by an arrow with poison. Such a man would not pay attention to anything else. Instead, he would think, ‘When will I reach a doctor to extract this arrow? Who will get this arrow out of me? Who will rescue me from this pain?’ In the very same way, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita did not pay attention to any other duty. Instead, he thought, ‘When will I see that noble son? Who will help me hear the transcendent state of wisdom? After hearing that Dharma, when will I give up paying attention to my mental perceptions?’

“Just then, as he stood right there in that very location, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita could hear the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata teaching the transcendent state of wisdom. While listening to him, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita suddenly conceived of all things as being unfounded and found himself directly in front of the gateways to multiple meditative concentrations.According to Ratnākara (F.223.b), he “suddenly conceived of all things as being unfounded” refers to his meditatively concentrated conception: “Just as the appearance of Dharmodgata teaching me right now‍—together with its causal basis‍—is unfounded, just so all things are not founded on the law of causality.” The second phrase in the sentence, “found himself” and so on, explains what occurred due to the power of his conception. The “meditative concentrations” (that follow below) are themselves “gateways” because they are the great means (of the Great Vehicle). RK: sarvadharmeṣv aniśritasaṃjñām iti| yathaitarhi me dharmodgatadeśanayā pratibhāsaḥ sanidānam anāśritaḥ| evaṃ hetuniyamam anāśritāḥ sarvadharmā ity enāṃ saṃjñām atyantasamāhitām| asyāḥ saṃjñāyāḥ prabhāvāt kim abhūd ity āha| tasye tyādi| samādh aya eva mukhāni mahopāyatvāt. Note: Hari gives a long explanation of the context for the subsequent fifty meditative concentrations. In that, he suggests that Sadāprarudita experiences all these profound meditative concentrations at the level of the path of preparation, based on visionary instructions he receives. Cf. Sparham, vol. 4, pp. 277–79. To be specific, he experienced the meditative concentration called focused on surveying the inherent nature of all things;According to Ratnākara (F.223.b), “surveying” means observing “the inherent nature of all things,” i.e., their individual characteristics. RK: sarvadharmā ṇāṃ svabhāvāḥ svalakṣaṇāni| teṣāṃ vyavalokano draṣṭā. Note: A meditative concentration is essentially a deeply focused mindset whose meditative object is framed by a particular, profound conception.
