In all the subsequent meditative concentrations, the translation adds “focused on” to the name of the meditative concentration and uses substantives (instead of awkward adjectival constructions) to avoid creating confusing and unmemorable names in English. the meditative concentration called focused on the inherent nature of all things not being mentally perceived;According to Ratnākara (F.223.b), “not mentally perceiving” them means certainty in their nonexistence. RK: teṣāṃ anupalabdhir asattāniścayaḥ. the meditative concentration called focused on the knowing of all things disappearing; the meditative concentration called focused on the nondifference of all things;According to Ratnākara (F.223.b–24.a), the “nondifference,” i.e., sameness “of all things,” is because their individual characteristics do not exist. The “meditative concentration” has that (nondifference) as its meditative object. And based on that sameness, which is the true nature, he sees all things as unchanging‍—hence the next one (right after this) is called focused on seeing of all things as unchanging. One should understand all the subsequent meditative concentrations like these two. (As it will say at the end of the long list), he is dwelling successively “in” all “these meditative concentrations.” RK: sarvadharmā ṇāṃ nirnānātvaṃ samatā svalakṣaṇānām abhāvāt| tadālambanaḥ samādhis tathoktaḥ| sā ca samatā tathatā| tayā nirvikārān sarvadharmān paśyatīti sarvadhamanirvikāradarśī| evaṃ yāvatsarvatathāgatadarśī samādhir veditavyaḥ| eṣu samādhiṣv iti krameṇa sarveṣu sthitaḥ san. Note: Ratnākara does not explain each one of the following meditative concentrations. But the idea is that they are successive and each one forms the basis for the next. the meditative concentration called focused on seeing all things as unchanging; the meditative concentration called focused on the illuminator of all things; the meditative concentration called focused on the removal of the darkness of all things; the meditative concentration called focused on the destruction of the knowing of all things; the meditative concentration called focused on the shaking up of all things; the meditative concentration called focused on the shower of flower blossoms; the meditative concentration called focused on the bringing forth of the individuality of all things; the meditative concentration called focused on abandoning illusion; the meditative concentration called focused on producing reflections in the dimension of a mirror; the meditative concentration called focused on bringing forth the cries of all sentient beings; the meditative concentration called focused on removing dust; the meditative concentration called focused on delighting all sentient beings; the meditative concentration called focused on the approach of skill in the language of all sentient beings; the meditative concentration called focused on the production of various utterances, words, and syllables; the meditative concentration called focused on the unimpeded; the meditative concentration called focused on the natural lack of conventions; the meditative concentration called focused on the attainment of liberation free from obstructions; the meditative concentration called focused on the royal approach; the meditative concentration called focused on terms, etymologies, words, and syllables; the meditative concentration called focused on examining all things; the meditative concentration called focused on leaving behind the objective scope of all things; the meditative concentration called focused on the limit of freedom from obstructions of all things; the meditative concentration called focused on being equal to the sky; the meditative concentration called focused on the vajra-like; the meditative concentration called focused on the king of nearby forms; the meditative concentration called focused on the king free of enemies; the meditative concentration called focused on gaining victory; the meditative concentration called focused on not averting the eyes; the meditative concentration called focused on certainty about the ultimate realm of things; the meditative concentration called focused on going beyond the ultimate realm of things; the meditative concentration called focused on providing inspiration; the meditative concentration called focused on the roaring of a lion; the meditative concentration called focused on surpassing all sentient beings; the meditative concentration called focused on the absence of dust; the meditative concentration called focused on the undefiled; the meditative concentration called focused on the array of lotuses; the meditative concentration called focused on cutting off doubts; the meditative concentration called focused on pursuing the essence in everything; the meditative concentration called focused on rising above all things; the meditative concentration called focused on the attainment of extrasensory knowledge, strength, and self-confidence; the meditative concentration called focused on penetrating all things; the meditative concentration called focused on the seal of all things not existing; the meditative concentration called focused on the ocean of all things not existing; the meditative concentration called focused on seeing no distinction in all things; the meditative concentration called focused on abandoning the jungle of all made-up beliefs; the meditative concentration called focused on the removal of darkness; the meditative concentration called focused on leaving behind the conventional signs of all things; the meditative concentration called focused on liberation from all attachments; the meditative concentration called focused on leaving behind all laziness; the meditative concentration called focused on the sun of the profound Dharma; the meditative concentration called focused on equality with Mount Meru; the meditative concentration called focused on undistractability; the meditative concentration called focused on bringing destruction to Māra’s circle; the meditative concentration called focused on not habitually fixating upon anything in the three worlds; the meditative concentration called focused on emanating light rays; the meditative concentration called focused on seeing the tathāgata; and the meditative concentration called focused on seeing all the tathāgatas.

“As he dwelled in these meditative concentrations, he saw immeasurable, innumerable blessed buddhas in the world systems of the ten directions, elucidating this very transcendent state of wisdom to bodhisattva great beings.According to Ratnākara (F.224.a), “ten directions” means the worlds in the ten directions that all world systems have. In those, he “saw immeasurable, innumerable blessed buddhas.” To this explanation, you might object, “The presence of a tathāgata in the same period of time is not multiple, because only one is able to bring about the benefit of all sentient beings.” We respond, “There is always only one buddha, because only he is able to ascend to infinite life. So why are we not asserting (what you want)?” You might respond, “Because others also (must be able to) come into being by force of causes.” We respond, “Then (there is no problem); it is (also) only by force of causes that infinite tathāgatas are simultaneously manifesting in infinite world systems. So what is the contradiction? The great charioteers have convincingly established that just as two universal emperors cannot coexist at the same time in a single world system with four continents, likewise two tathāgatas cannot arise at the same time in a billionfold world system.” RK: daśadiśo ’sminn iti daśadiglokaḥ sarvalokadhātava ity arthaḥ| tatra buddhān bhagavato ’prameyāsaṃkhyeyān paśyati sma| naikasmin kāle ’nekas tathāgata ekasyaiva sarvasattvārthakriyāsu śakter iti cet| nityam eka eva buddhas tasyaivānantam āyur adhiṣṭhātuṃ śakter iti kin neṣyate ? hetubalād anye pi bhavantīti cet| hetubalād eva tarhi yugapad ananteṣu lokadhātuṣv anantās tathāgatā jāyanta iti ko virodhaḥ ? yathā buddhau cakravartinau sakṛd ekatra caturdvīpake lokadhātau notpadyete niḥsapatnakarmakāritvāt tathā tathāgatau trisāhasre lokadhātāv iti mahārathaiḥ kṣuṇṇam etat. Those tathāgatas commended and assured him, and said, ‘Noble son, we ourselves went about our lives in the bodhisattva’s way of going about life. We also searched all over for this very transcendent state of wisdom. While searching all over for it, we too attained these very meditative concentrations that you have just now attained. After attaining these meditative concentrations, we broke through to understanding and became established in the transcendent state of wisdom with respect to a buddha’s qualities beyond regressing. Surveying the essential nature or inherent nature of these very meditative concentrations, we do not identify a thing that goes into them or comes out of them, nor do we identify anyone going about life for the sake of awakening or anyone realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Noble son, this transcendent state of wisdom is not a state of developing conceits about any thing. By remaining in such a state without developing conceits, we obtained bodies with this golden color. We also attained the thirty-two principal characteristics of a great person, the eighty subsidiary characteristics, and a halo with a six-foot radius, the inconceivable and unsurpassed knowing of a buddha, the wisdom of a buddha, the unsurpassed meditative concentration of a buddha, and the perfection of the excellent qualities of a buddha. Since the tathāgatas themselves cannot grasp the magnitude of such qualities or demonstrate the limit to the perfection of such qualities, how much less able would a disciple or solitary realizer be? Therefore, noble son, you should develop reverence for these very qualities by longing and aspiring more intensely for them. Noble son, when you are longing and aspiring, it will not be difficult to reach unsurpassed, perfect awakening. Noble son, you should develop intense devotion to virtuous friends, appreciation for them, and trust in them, because a bodhisattva great being supported by a virtuous friend quickly realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening.’
“Then, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita asked those tathāgatas, ‘Who is my virtuous friend?’

“They replied, ‘Noble son, for a long time the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata has been helping you ripen toward unsurpassed, perfect awakening and supporting you. He has also been helping you obtain the training in the transcendent state of wisdom, the skill in means, and the qualities of a buddha. Noble son, he is your supporter and your virtuous friend. You should pay tribute to him with appreciation and gratitude and keep in mind what he has done for you.According to Ratnākara (F.224.a), “what he has done for you” means exactly what he did previously. You “should keep in mind” means you should not forget! RK: tat kṛtam iti| yad eva tena purvakṛtam| dhārayitavyaṃ cetasi na vismartavyam. Noble son, suppose that for one eon‍—or two, three, one hundred, or even one thousand eons‍—you were to carry around the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata on top of your head, like a turban, so that you could provide him all sentient beings’ pleasant provisions, and offer him as many visible forms, sounds, scents, tastes, and tangibles as there are in a billionfold, great order world system.According to Ratnākara (F.224.a), here a “turban” just means a fabric wrap, i.e., head cloth. RK: celaṃ vastram| tasya uṇḍukaḥ| śiraśāṭaka ity arthaḥ. Still, noble son, you would not yet have repaid what that noble son has done for you. Why is that? Because, noble son, through the miraculous power of that noble son, you have come to obtain these sorts of meditative concentrations, to hear the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means, and to obtain the transcendent state of wisdom.’

This point in the chapter corresponds to the beginning of Chapter 74 in the Twenty-Five Thousand. The corresponding narrative in the Eighteen Thousand has no chapter break at this point.“Then, after those tathāgatas encouraged the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, they disappeared and the noble son emerged from his meditative concentrations. After emerging, he thought, ‘Where did those tathāgatas come from and where did those tathāgatas go to?’ Not seeing those tathāgatas, he was overcome with sorrow and yearning.According to Ratnākara (F.224.a), “sorrow” means grief and “yearning” means an uneasy feeling that fuels one’s fire. RK: utkaṇṭhā śokaḥ| paritasanaṃ duḥkhāsikā kāṣṭhāgatā. He thought, ‘The noble bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata has obtained the power of memory. He has the five types of extrasensory knowledge. He has performed his service for the former conquerors. And he is the perfect support and virtuous friend to me. Over a long period of time he has brought me benefit. So, since the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata is certain to be a benefit, I should keep going toward him, approach him, and ask him, “Where did the tathāgatas come from and where have the tathāgatas gone to?” ’

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita kindled appreciation for the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, trust in him, devotion to him, and awe toward him.According to Ratnākara (F.224.a), “appreciation” means affection for; “trust” means not being mentally muddled about the qualities he possesses; “awe” means amazement at those qualities; and “devotion” means venerating. RK: prītiḥ prema| guṇavatsu cetaso ’kāluṣyaṃ prasādaḥ| tadguṇeṣu vismayaḥ citrīkāraḥ| abhyarhaṇaṃ gauravam. After kindling these, he wondered, ‘When I approach the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, how much tribute can I pay to him? Since I am poor, I do not have any fabric, precious material, gold, jewels, pearls, beryl, mother of pearl, coral, silver, flower blossoms, incense, perfume, wreaths, fragrant oil, aromatic powder, parasols, banners, bells, flags, or the like. How can I pay tribute and show reverence to him? It would not be proper of me if I were to approach the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata this way, poor as I am, with nothing precious or delightful to present.’

“With these types of feelings of devoted attentiveness, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita continued on his journey and gradually arrived at the next city. When he reached the central market, it occurred to him, ‘Why not sell my physical body? I could use the proceeds to pay tribute to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata. Certainly, throughout my long time in this unlimited cycle of existence, thousands of my bodies have been broken, destroyed, enslaved, and sold. Again and again I must have experienced unlimited sufferings in the hell realms because of lust and motivated by sense pleasures, but never has it been for the sake of paying tribute to such qualities or sentient beings.’
“So, while the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita was in the central market, with a raised voice he repeatedly shouted the words, ‘Who wants a man? Who wants a man? Who wants to buy a man?’

“At that moment, the pernicious Māra thought, ‘This bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita must really want the Dharma, if he is going to sell himself to pay tribute to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata and request the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means. If he gets to ask how a bodhisattva great being goes about life in the transcendent state of wisdom to quickly actualize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, then he will become an ocean of learning. In doing so, he will become unassailable by any māra or demon cohort. Then he will reach the perfection of all the positive qualities and benefit numerous sentient beings. Finally, once he realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening, he will help those beings and others to get beyond my reach. So I must create obstacles for him.’

“Then the pernicious Māra created a diversion so that the brahmins and householders would not hear the voice of the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita shouting, ‘Who wants a man? Who wants a man? Who wants to buy a man?’According to Ratnākara (F.224.b), “created a diversion” means he caused them to be alarmed (by something to capture their attention). RK: paryutthāpayām āse ti vyagrīkṛtavān.

“Then, when the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita did not find anyone to buy him, he went into a corner and cried. Letting the tears roll out, he lamented, ‘Woe is me! How difficult it is to get any wealth! We beings cannot even find anyone to buy our bodies! If only we could sell them and pay tribute to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata!’

“Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, had the thought, ‘Perhaps I should test the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita to see whether he indeed has gained the altruistic disposition to give up his body out of his wish for the Dharma.’ At that, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, miraculously disguised himself as a young brahmin man, went over to Sadāprarudita, and asked, ‘Noble son, why are you in such a state with your mind so utterly distressed and full of sorrow, letting the tears pour out in this way?’
“Sadāprarudita replied, ‘Brahmin, I wish to sell myself, but I cannot find anyone to buy my body.’
“Śakra, in the form of the brahmin man, asked him, ‘Noble son, for what purpose do you wish to sell yourself?’

“Sadāprarudita replied, ‘Brahmin, out of my wish for the Dharma, I decided to sell myself so that I can make an offering for the teachings. I wish to pay tribute to the noble bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata. As I was not finding a buyer for my body, I thought, “Oh, I must have such little merit‍—even though I wish to make an offering for the teachings and pay tribute to the noble bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, I cannot find anyone to buy my body!” ’

“Then the brahmin said to the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, ‘Noble son, I have no need for a man. But my father must make a sacrifice. For that, a man’s heart, blood, and bone marrow is required. Can you offer me those for sale?’

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita thought to himself, ‘How extremely easy this would be for me to get what I need! Since I have found this brahmin to buy my heart, blood, and bone marrow, I now see how this body of mine can help perfect the transcendent state of wisdom, the skill in means, and the qualities of a buddha.’ With a joyful heart, a ready mind, and a cheerful attitude, he said to the brahmin, ‘Brahmin, I will give you absolutely whatever you want from this body.’According to Ratnākara (F.224.b), “a ready mind” is a mind fit to do whatever it might be, due to that very joy. Since this joy increases twofold from having that ready mind, he has a “cheerful attitude.” RK: kalyacitta iti tenaiva harṣeṇa karmaṇyacittaḥ| tataś ca dviguṇībhūtaharṣatvāt pramuditacittaḥ.
“The brahmin asked him, ‘Noble son, what should I give you in payment for it?’
“Sadāprarudita replied, ‘Brahmin, give me whatever you can spare.’

“With that, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita grabbed a sharp knife, pierced his right arm, and let the blood spurt out. Next, he pierced his right thigh and scraped off the flesh. He then started toward the base of a wall in order to break open his bones against it.

“At the same time, a wealthy merchant’s daughter, who had gone up onto the roof of her mansion, saw the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita pierce his arm, let the blood spurt out, and scrape off his flesh. As he went to the base of the wall in order to break open his bones, she thought, ‘Why indeed is that noble son inflicting such torture upon his very own self? I must go to that noble son and find out.’

“So the merchant’s daughter approached the place where the bodhisattva great being was. After approaching, she said to the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, ‘Noble son, why are you inflicting the kind of torture upon yourself that will kill you? What will you do with this blood? What will you do with the bone marrow?’
“Sadāprarudita replied, ‘Young lady, I will sell them to this brahmin, so that I can venerate the transcendent state of wisdom and pay tribute to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata.’

“Then the young lady said to the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, ‘Noble son, with such a wish to sell your own heart, blood, and bone marrow, and pay tribute to that noble son, what kind or special type of positive qualities do you hope to actualize?’RK: guṇajātiḥ manuṣyasāmānyo guṇaḥ| viśeṣas tu manuṣyātikrānto guṇaḥ.

“Sadāprarudita said to the young lady, ‘Young lady, that noble son will teach us beings the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means so that we can train in those. When we do, we will become a refuge to all sentient beings. After realizing unsurpassed, perfect awakening, we will obtain a golden body. We will attain the thirty-two principal characteristics of a great person, the eighty subsidiary characteristics, and a halo with a six-foot radius and infinite light rays. We will obtain great benevolence, compassion, rejoicing, and impartiality. We will obtain the four grounds for self-confidence, the four types of experiential knowledge, the eighteen distinctive qualities of a buddha, and the five types of extrasensory knowledge. We will obtain inconceivable purity in meditative concentration, inconceivable purity in wisdom, and the ten powers of a tathāgata. We will realize the unsurpassed knowing of a buddha, and once we come to possess the jewel of Dharma, we will share it with all sentient beings.’

“Then the young lady said to the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, ‘Noble son, it is wonderful that you proclaim such noble and excellent qualities. Noble son, for the sake of even one quality like this, anyone would be able to give up all their bodies even for eons equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges. How much more so to give up one body for the sake of so many qualities! The qualities that you have proclaimed are noble and excellent. To me, they are appealing and seem worthwhile.According to Ratnākara (F.224.b), they “seem worthwhile” because they are appealing as something worthy of her taking action for them herself. RK: kṣamante ce ti svayaṃ kartavyatayāpi rocanāt. But please do not inflict this kind of torture upon yourself! I will give you whatever you need‍—whether it be gold, jewels, pearls, silver, beryl, special coral, red sunstone, crystal, flower blossoms, incense, perfume, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, fabrics, parasols, banners, bells, flags, or lamps‍—so that you can pay tribute to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata. And I will go to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata together with you and I too will plant the roots of virtue right along with you, specifically to obtain these kinds of qualities.’

“Then the powerful lord Śakra let his brahmin disguise fade away. Now in his own body, he stood before the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita and said, ‘Excellent, noble son. It is excellent that you have such firm resolve. By seeking the Dharma in this way, all the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas also realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening. They obtained the jewel of Dharma by going about their lives in the way that a bodhisattva goes about life, and by requesting the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means. Noble son, I have no need for your heart, blood, or bone marrow. It was only because of my wish to test you that I came here. Now, noble son, make a wish. However great your wish is, I will grant it.’
“Sadāprarudita said to him, ‘Śakra, please give me the unsurpassed qualities of a buddha.’

“The Lord of Gods said, ‘Noble son, I am not the sovereign in that domain.According to Ratnākara (F.224.b), to be “sovereign” means to have power (in that domain). RK: viṣayite ti śaktiḥ. It is rather the blessed buddhas who are the sovereigns in such domains. Make a different wish.’

“Sadāprarudita said, ‘O Lord of Gods, do not worry about restoring this body of mine to its proper condition.According to Ratnākara (F.224.b), Sadāprarudita says “do not worry,” i.e., be concerned, because he knows Śakra’s intention (behind offering the boon). RK: viditābhiprāya āha| alpotsuka ityādi| utsuka autsukyam. Lord of Gods, I shall just perform an act of truth by myself. By the power of the truth in my stating that the worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas have foreordained me to be beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening‍—and as I am known by the sincerity of my altruistic disposition‍—let this body of mine now become exactly as it was before!’

“At that very moment, that very second, and that very instant, through the buddhas’ miraculous power and the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita’s purely altruistic disposition, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita’s body came to be exactly the way it was before, in its healthy and uninjured state.According to Ratnākara (F.224.b), the “moment,” “second,” and “instant” all refer to the same thing, but due to the (three) different types of faith among listeners, all three are mentioned. RK: yat tat kṣaṇaṃ lavam api tat| tan muhūrtam api tat| trayāṇām upādānaṃ tu śrotṛṇāṃ śraddhabhedāt.

“At that, both the powerful lord Śakra and the pernicious Māra, with the shine of their boldness dimmed and unable to keep up with the superior shine of the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita’s boldness, disappeared right on the spot.According to Ratnākara (F.224.b), the overview is “the shine of their boldness dimmed” and the more detailed explanation follows. Take “superior” to be the cause (of their inability) “to keep up,” i.e., to catch. RK: niṣpratibhāna ity uddeśaḥ| sadāpraruditasye tyādi nirdeśaḥ| uttara iti uttaranimittam| apratipadyamāno ’labhamānaḥ. Note: The translation of Ratnākara’s comments could not be translated literally here because they were already incorporated into the main text with a different syntax.

“Then the merchant’s daughter said to the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, ‘Noble son, come, let’s go to my house. I will have my parents give you the riches with which you will venerate the transcendent state of wisdom and will pay tribute to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, namely, out of your wish for the Dharma.’

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita went together with the merchant’s daughter to her house. When they arrived, Sadāprarudita stayed at the front door, while the merchant’s daughter went inside the house and said to her mother and father, ‘Mother and Father, please give me precious metals, gold, precious substances, jewels, fabrics, flower blossoms, incense, perfume, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, cloth parasols, banners, bells, flags, and different kinds of heavenly musical instruments. Also, please grant me leave along with the five hundred young ladies that you gave me as my servants. Together with the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, I will go to be in the presence of the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata and venerate him. He will teach us the Dharma, and through this we will obtain the qualities of a buddha.’
“Then the young lady’s parents said to their daughter, ‘But, Daughter, who is this bodhisattva great being named Sadāprarudita? Where is he located now?’

“The young lady said, ‘The noble son is standing near the front door to this very house of ours. Because of his altruistic disposition, he has set out to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening; specifically, he wishes to have all the awakened qualities in order to liberate all the unlimited sentient beings from suffering in cyclic existence. He had decided to sell himself, so that he could venerate the transcendent state of wisdom, and pay tribute to the noble bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata. But he could not find anyone to buy his body. Not finding a buyer, he suffered with his mind dejected and distressed. As he stood there pondering it, his tears began to roll out.

“ ‘Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, miraculously took on the form of a young brahmin man and asked, “Why are you standing there pondering, suffering, with your mind dejected and distressed, just letting your tears roll out?”
“ ‘Sadāprarudita replied, “I wish to sell myself, but I cannot find a buyer.”
“ ‘In the form of the brahmin, Śakra said to him, “But, noble son, for what purpose do you wish to sell yourself?”

“ ‘Sadāprarudita said, “I wish to venerate the transcendent state of wisdom and to pay tribute to the noble bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata. Specifically, my wish to obtain the Dharma is connected with obtaining the qualities of a buddha.”

“ ‘In the form of the brahmin, Śakra said to him, “Noble son, I have no need for your body. But my father will be making a sacrifice on my behalf. For that, a man’s heart, blood, and bone marrow are required.”

“ ‘Then the noble son’s mind ceased to be dejected, and he said, “I will give it to you.” At that, he grabbed a sharp knife, pierced his right arm, and let the blood spurt out. Then, he pierced his right thigh and scraped off the flesh. Next, he started toward the base of a wall in order to break open the bone against it. As he stood to one side to break it open, he said, “Here, I will give it to you.”

“ ‘At that time, I was on the roof of our mansion. From above, I saw the noble son’s blood gushing and I thought, “Why indeed is this man inflicting such torture upon himself?” So I went to him to find out: “Noble son, your blood is gushing. What purpose could there be for doing such harm to your body?”
“ ‘Then he replied, “Young lady, I am going to give my blood, heart, and bone marrow to this brahmin. Why is that? Because I am poor and have no other wealth.”
“ ‘I asked him, “But what are you going to do with that wealth?”
“ ‘He replied, “Because of my wish for the Dharma, specifically I will use it to venerate the transcendent state of wisdom and pay tribute to the noble bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata.”
“ ‘I asked, “But, noble son, what sort or special type of quality will come from such an offering?”
“ ‘Then, after he described to me the inconceivable and immeasurable qualities of a buddha and elucidated them, he said, “These are the kinds of qualities that will come from that offering.”

“ ‘Once I heard about the inconceivable qualities of a buddha, I felt great joy and delight, and thought, “It is amazing that this noble son is performing such difficult deeds. He wishes so intensely for the Dharma that he courageously bears such a painful condition of his own body. Certainly, out of his wish for Dharma, this noble son has given himself entirely over to it. With our extensive and vast wealth, why should we not venerate the Dharma and make aspirations ourselves for such conditions?”According to Ratnākara (F.224.b), it was “extensive” because it was of many kinds, and it was “vast” because in each of those kinds, it seemed endless. “Wealth” means riches. RK: prabhūtāḥ prakārabāhulyāt| vipulāḥ pratiprakāram ānantyāt| bhogā arthāḥ. So, I myself told this noble son, “Please, please do not do such deeds, as they will kill you. I will donate the very extensive wealth that you seek for paying tribute and showing reverence to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata. Also, I will go together with you to where the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata is and venerate that noble son. And we will accomplish these kinds of qualities that you proclaimed, namely, the unsurpassed qualities of a buddha.” So, Mother and Father, please grant me this! Please give me an extensive amount of wealth. Together with this noble son, I will use it to go and venerate the noble bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata.’

“Then the young lady’s parents responded to their daughter, ‘Daughter, it is amazing what you are reporting about how difficult the noble son’s circumstances have been. Those qualities, for which he courageously bears such difficult circumstances, are absolutely inconceivable, the most excellent in the whole world, and certainly conducive to the happiness of all sentient beings.According to Ratnākara (F.224.b), “most excellent in the whole world” means they surpass the entire world. RK: sarvalokaviśiṣṭā iti sarvalokātikrāntāḥ. Daughter, we will grant this to you. Make room for us too. We will join you in going to see the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, to pay homage to him, and to serve him.’

“Then, once the young lady was aware that her own mother and father would set out with her to venerate and pay tribute to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, she said, ‘Mother and Father, let us do as you say. I will not get in the way of anyone on the side of virtue.’

“After saying this, the merchant’s daughter began setting out to venerate and pay tribute to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata. First, the merchant’s daughter readied five hundred carriages and got the five hundred young ladies to prepare. After they were prepared, she gathered various types of brightly colored blossoms, various colors of fabric, flowers, incenses, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, cloth parasols, banners, bells, flags, various brightly colored precious materials and various brightly colored flowers made of precious materials, and great quantities of dry edibles, soft foods, and tasty treats.

“Then the merchant’s daughter and the bodhisattva great being boarded a single carriage surrounded by the five hundred young ladies in their carriages. Then, placing their carriage at the forefront preceded by her parents, they traveled east with this great retinue. In due course while traveling, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita saw from afar the beautiful, brightly colored city Gandhavatī. It was made of seven precious substances, surrounded by seven barrier walls, with seven ornamental arches, seven moats, and seven rows of palm trees. It was thirty miles long and thirty miles wide. The city was thriving, successful, and safe. It had excellent provisions and was filled with many people and animals. It had five separate one-hundred-aisle indoor bazaars that were beautiful to behold like a brightly colored painting. It was well organized with a fixed order of streets for people, carriages, and vehicles, not overflowing, but harmonious for all.

“At the central intersection of the city, surrounded by an assembly of many hundreds, thousands, and hundreds of thousands, Sadāprarudita saw the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata on his Dharma throne at the front, teaching the Dharma. Just by seeing him, Sadāprarudita felt the kind of happiness that a monk feels when he has attained the first meditative attainment with one-pointed attentiveness. But immediately after seeing him, Sadāprarudita thought, ‘It is not appropriate for me to approach the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata in a carriage. Therefore, I will get down from this carriage here.’ So, he got down from that carriage. Likewise, the merchant’s daughter and the five hundred young ladies got down from their carriages. From there, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita at the front, together with the merchant’s daughter and surrounded by the five hundred young ladies, walked toward the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata with their array of unlimited devotional offerings.

“Now, at the time, the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata had had a peaked temple built out of the seven precious substances to house the transcendent state of wisdom scripture. The peaked temple was decorated with red sandalwood and covered over with pearls. At its four corners were placed precious jewels to function as lamps. Four incense holders made of silver hung in the four directions, where pure, black aloeswood perfumed the air specifically to venerate the transcendent state of wisdom. In the middle of the peaked temple, on an arranged throne made of the seven precious substances, inside a large chest made of four precious substances, there had been placed the scripture of the transcendent state of wisdom, written in melted beryl on golden plates. Beyond that, the peaked temple was adorned with a variety of brightly colored silk scarves hanging all around.

“As they approached, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita and the merchant’s daughter, at the forefront of the five hundred young ladies, saw the peaked temple enveloped in a limitless array of devotional offerings. And as they heard the music of heavenly instruments, they also saw heavenly māndārava flowers, heavenly aromatic powders of sandalwood, heavenly gold powders, and heavenly silver powders being rained down upon the peaked temple, showered upon it, and scattered around it by many thousands of gods there, including Śakra, powerful lord of the gods.

“Upon seeing and hearing all this, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, ‘Lord of Gods, why are you raining down, showering, and scattering around upon that peaked temple heavenly māndārava flowers, heavenly aromatic powders of sandalwood, heavenly gold powders, and heavenly silver powders? And why are these heavenly musical instruments being played by the gods in the upper heavens?’

“At this, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, ‘Noble son, do you not know? It is because this transcendent state of wisdom is the mother and the guide of bodhisattva great beings. Bodhisattva great beings who train in it quickly accomplish the approach of the perfection of all positive qualities, the qualities of a buddha, and the state of complete knowing.’

“At this, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita said to Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, ‘Lord of Gods, where is this transcendent state of wisdom, the mother and the guide of bodhisattva great beings?’

“Śakra said, ‘Noble son, the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata has placed it in the middle of this peaked temple, written with melted beryl on golden plates and sealed with seven seals. Showing it to you is not an easy thing for us to do.’

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, and the merchant’s daughter leading the five hundred young ladies, as a group, venerated the transcendent state of wisdom with their gathered flower blossoms, garlands, precious fabrics, incense, perfume, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, cloth parasols, banners, bells, flags, and flowers made of gold and silver. One after another, they set out each portion of those offerings, specifically to pay tribute to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata. Then, after venerating the transcendent state of wisdom with their gathered flower blossoms, garlands, precious fabrics, incense, perfume, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, cloth parasols, banners, bells, flags, and flowers made of gold and silver, to the sound of music from the heavenly instruments, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, and the merchant’s daughter leading the five hundred young ladies, as a group, approached the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, purely to make an offering for the Dharma, and they sprinkled upon him, rained upon him, and scattered around him the flower blossoms, incense, perfume, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, cloth parasols, banners, bells, flags, sandalwood powders, and flowers made of gold and silver, to the sound of music from the heavenly instruments.

“But those flower blossoms formed a peaked temple in the air above the head of the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, and those variously colored flowers and the flowers made of gold and silver came to rest like a celestial palace in the sky. Likewise, the cloth and precious fabrics settled in the atmosphere like a temple hall in the clouds made of precious substances. The bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, along with the merchant’s daughter leading the five hundred young ladies, saw the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata’s display of miraculous magic power. Upon seeing it, they thought, ‘It is amazing. How great is the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata’s magic power! How great is his miraculous power! How great is his powerful energy! If this noble son can perform such magical feats from just practicing the bodhisattva’s way of going about life alone, then what will happen once he has realized unsurpassed, perfect awakening?’

“Then the merchant’s daughter and the five hundred young ladies that she led, all as a group, felt longing for the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata because of their altruistic disposition, and they developed the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening, saying, ‘By these roots of virtue, may we become worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas in the future! May we go about life in the bodhisattva’s way of going about life and attain these very qualities that the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata is attaining. Likewise, may we pay tribute and show reverence to the transcendent state of wisdom in the same way that this bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata pays tribute and shows reverence to it. May we also elucidate it to the multitude of people in the same way that this bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata elucidates it. So may we become endowed with the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means, and accomplish them, in the same way that this bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata has become endowed with the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means, and accomplished them.’

“Then, after making the devotional offerings to the transcendent state of wisdom, and after paying tribute to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, along with the merchant’s daughter leading the five hundred young ladies, made obeisance to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata’s feet with their heads, and devotedly and considerately cupped their hands together and stood to one side. After doing so, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita said to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, ‘Noble son, I am here because I went to the wilderness seeking the transcendent state of wisdom, and heard a voice that said for me to go to the east, since there I could hear the transcendent state of wisdom.

“ ‘After listening carefully to that voice, I myself set out in the eastern direction. Then it occurred to me that I had listened carefully to that voice, but failed to ask how far I should go or in whose presence I would hear or obtain the transcendent state of wisdom. I became extremely dejected about it and overcome with sorrow and yearning. I spent seven days and nights in that very location, full of sorrow. I could not eat, and paid attention only to the transcendent state of wisdom, wondering, since I did not ask the voice how far east I needed to go and how I would find the transcendent state of wisdom in order to listen to it. Then suddenly, right in front of me, the body of a tathāgata appeared and said, “Noble son, go one thousand two hundred miles due east from here where there is a city called Gandhavatī. There you will see the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata teaching and elucidating the transcendent state of wisdom.” Then I felt great excitement, joy, and delight. And as that great excitement, joy, and delight suffused me, I did not move from that very location, but could hear you teaching the transcendent state of wisdom. As I listened, numerous gateways to meditative concentration manifested before me, and the blessed buddhas residing in the world systems of the ten directions encouraged me and commended me, saying, “Excellent, noble son. Excellent. These meditative concentrations are born from the transcendent state of wisdom. We too previously abided in these and then accomplished all the qualities of a buddha.” After helping me to see clearly, to take in the practice, to be enthusiastic, and to rejoice well and properly, then those tathāgatas disappeared.

“ ‘Once I emerged from meditative concentration, I thought, “From where indeed did those tathāgatas come? And, where do those tathāgatas go?” Then, it occurred to me, “The noble bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata has obtained the power of memory. He has the five types of extrasensory knowledge. He has devotedly served the former conquerors. He has planted the roots of virtue. And he is well trained in the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means. He will explain this topic to me, as it is, and tell me where the tatahāgatas have come from and where they have gone to.” After listening to the voice of that tathāgata’s body, I set out toward the east as instructed. As I was arriving, I saw from far away the Noble One teaching the Dharma. Right upon seeing you, I felt a happiness like that of a monk upon reaching the first meditative attainment with single-pointed attentiveness. So, noble son, I now am asking you: Where have the tatahāgatas come from? Where have they gone to? Noble son, please teach me the coming and going of those tathāgatas, so that we may know the coming and going of those tathāgatas, and never be separated from seeing those tathāgatas.’ ”
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Sadāprarudita,” the thirtieth chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 31: DharmodgataAccording to Ratnākara (F.228.b–29.a), the “Chapter on Dharmodgata” is the chapter characterized by the bodhisattva Dharmodgata (“Elevated by Dharma”). RK: dharmodgate na lakṣitaḥ parivartas tatparivartaḥ.This chapter corresponds in content to the second part of the narrative of Sadāprarudita’s quest to receive the transcendent state of wisdom teachings from Dharmodgata, as recounted in Chapter 75 of the Twenty-Five Thousand, and in Chapter 86 of the Eighteen Thousand (the Hundred Thousand, as witnessed in the Sanskrit and in the Tibetan of most Kangyurs, does not include the narrative). As noted above (n.1339), the accounts in the different sūtras are similar in content, but with some significant differences. Generally speaking, the versions in the Twenty-Five Thousand and Eighteen Thousand are more similar to each other than either of them is to the version here in the Eight Thousand.
div.p: “At this, the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata said to the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, ‘Noble son, the tathāgatas definitely do not come from anywhere or go to anywhere. That is because the actual nature does not move and that which is the actual nature is the Tathāgata.According to Ratnākara (F.224.b–25.a), (in logical terms) “the tathāgatas do not come or go” is the thesis. “Since the actual nature does not move” is the reason. One might then ask what the tathāgatas (i.e., tathā+āgata, literally “those who have come to the way things are”) have come to (āgatam). So he pronounces the subsequent sentences. These responses also should be considered logically admissible reasons, and all these reasons involve different synonyms of emptiness based on differences in conventional signs. RK: na khalv ityādi pratijñā| acalitā hi tathate ti hetuḥ| tathāgatānāṃ kim āgatam iti ced āha| yā ce tyādi| evamuttare ’pi hetavaḥ sasamarthanā veditavyāḥ| sarve caite hetavaḥ śūnyatāparyāyā nimittabhedāt tu bhedaḥ.
“ ‘It is because, noble son, the freedom from arising neither comes nor goes, and that which is the freedom from arising is the Tathāgata.
“ ‘It is because, noble son, one does not accurately discern either a coming or a going of the pinnacle of reality, and that which is the pinnacle of reality is the Tathāgata.
“ ‘It is because, noble son, one does not accurately discern either a coming or a going of emptiness, and that which is emptiness is the Tathāgata.
“ ‘It is because, noble son, one does not accurately discern either a coming or a going of “something as it really is,” and that which is “something as it really is” is the Tathāgata.
“ ‘It is because, noble son, one does not accurately discern either a coming or a going of freedom from passion, and that which is freedom from passion is the Tathāgata.
“ ‘It is because, noble son, one does not accurately discern either a coming or a going of cessation, and that which is cessation is the Tathāgata.

“ ‘It is because, noble son, one does not accurately discern either a coming or a going of the space element, and that which is the space element is the Tathāgata.According to Ratnākara (F.225.a), the “space element” is just the actual nature because it is the same as space. RK: ākāśadhātur ākāśasādharmyāt tathataiva.

“ ‘Noble son, the Tathāgata is not anywhere other than these things. Noble son, the actual nature of these very things, the actual nature of all things, and the actual nature of the Tathāgata are just the same single actual nature.According to Ratnākara (F.225.a), the first of the two sentences here is a response to someone who might say that the the Tathāgata is the body shining forth with the thirty-two principal characteristics of a great person and the eighty subsidiary characteristics, but is not the actual natures (of those things) and so on. The basic idea behind this sentence is that the Tathāgata is just the absolutely pure actual nature, because (the Tathāgata’s natural body), by definition, is the body that is the nature of those things. So, after explaining that the Tathāgata is not different from these actual natures, he adds the second of the two sentences here to show that they have absolutely no difference. In this sentence, the “actual nature” refers to the emptiness of the imagined nature. The actual nature “of these very things” means the actual nature of the actual natures (of the abovementioned things). The next phrase, “of all things,” refers to the constituents of experience, the elements, and so on that belong to a tathāgata. The “same single actual nature” gives the general thesis, for which the explanation comes in the next sentence (see next note). RK: nanu dvātriṃśanmahāpuruṣalakṣaṇāśīty-anuvyañjanavirājitavigrahas tathāgato na punas tathatādaya ity ata āha| na hi kulaputrānyatre tyādi| tathataiva suviśuddhā tathāgata iti bhāvaḥ| sa hi teṣāṃ svābhāvikaḥ kāya iti nyāyaḥ| evaṃ tathatāditathāgatayor abhedam uktvā ’tyantābhedaṃ darśayitum āha| yā ce tyādi| eṣām eva dharmāṇām iti tathatādīnāṃ tathate ti parikalpitarūpaśūnyatā| yā ca sarvadharmāṇām iti skandhadhātvādīnāṃ yā ca tathāgatasya| ekaive tyādir uddeśaḥ. Noble son, one cannot divide that actual nature into two. And, noble son, there is only one actual nature, not two or three actual natures. Noble son, since the actual nature is not an existent thing, it eludes quantifications.According to Ratnākara (F.225.a), the “same single actual nature” (see preceding note) is the general thesis, whereas here “only one nature” and so on is the explanation (of that thesis). He explains the reason (for this thesis) with the words “since the actual nature is not an existent thing.” The idea (behind it not being an existent thing) is that they are not real substances, but not that they are a nonexistent thing that accords with characteristics. Nevertheless, the coming and going of the tathāgatas is not real because they do not exist as they are imagined. But since the imagination of existence is due to an error of conception, he goes on to explain this fact by means of the examples (below). RK: ekaive tyādir uddeśaḥ| punar ekaive tyādi nirdeśaḥ| hetum āha| yadutāsattvād iti| adravyatvān na punar yathālakṣaṇam abhāvād iti bhāvaḥ| punas tathāgatānām agatigaty or abhāvaṃ yathākalpanam asattvāt| punas tathāgatānām agatigaty or abhāvaṃ yathākalpanam asattvāt| bhāvakalpanā tu saṃjñāviparyāsād iti dṛṣṭāntaiḥ pratipādayitum āha| tadyathāpī tyādi. Note: Ratnākara is explaining that the actual nature is “not an existent thing” in the same way that, for instance, a mirage might be said to not be an existent thing, because it has no real water substance in it, but not because the characteristics of water don’t appear to be there. In other words, the water is not found to exist as imagined when mistaking the mirage for a real body of water. Noble son, as an analogy, it is just like a man distressed by the summer heat experienced in the last month of summer. At midday, he might see an undulating mirage and rush toward it, thinking, “I see water here. I see something to drink.” Noble son, what do you think? Where did that water come from? Where does that water go to? To the eastern ocean, the southern ocean, the western ocean, or the northern ocean?’

“Sadāprarudita said, ‘Noble sir, one does not observe any water in the mirage, how much less so does one accurately discern its coming or going? But, noble son, a man distressed by the summer heat, who is an immature or unwise type of person, sees the mirage and develops the conception of “water” about something that has no water. However, water, as an essence, is not found in that mirage.’

“ ‘That is correct, noble son. That is correct,’ said Dharmodgata. ‘Similarly, noble son, some people who habitually fixate upon the form or voice of the Tathāgata imagine that the Tathāgata comes and goes. All those who imagine the Tathāgata to come and go, however, are liable to being called immature and unwise types of persons, because, noble son, they are just like a man who conceives of water where there is no water. Why is it like that? It is because a tathāgata should not be seen as his body with physical form. The tathāgatas are the bodies of the truth. And, noble son, just as the true nature of things neither comes nor goes, just so, noble son, the tathāgatas neither come nor go.According to Ratnākara (F.225.b), here one should understand the term “bodies of the truth” (dharmakāya) to mean the bodies of the true natures of things (dharmatākāya). RK: dharmākāyā iti dharma tākāyāḥ. Note: In other contexts where appropriate, Ratnākara will want us to understanding the same term dharmakāya to mean the “body of qualities.”

“ ‘Noble son, as an analogy, it is just like the bodies conjured up by a magical illusionist‍—such as bodies of an elephant, a horse, a carriage, or a pedestrian‍—that neither come nor go. Just so, the tathāgatas neither come nor go.

“ ‘Noble son, it is also, as an analogy, just like a sleeping man who, in the midst of a dream, sees one tathāgata or more‍—whether it be two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, one hundred, or one thousand tathāgatas. But when he wakes up, he does not see even one tathāgata. Noble son, what do you think? Where did that tathāgata come from? Where has that tathāgata gone to?’

“Sadāprarudita said, ‘Noble son, he accurately discerns no actuality in his dream, since that dream is a false representation.’According to Ratnākara (F.225.b), here the word “actuality” means reality. The dream is a “false representation” means it is deceptive because it seems like reality. For this very reason, he speaks of it as “not real” below. RK: pariniṣpattir iti bhūtatvam| muṣāvādas tatsādharmyān vañcaka ity arthaḥ| ata evāha abhūta iti.

“Dharmodgata said, ‘Noble son, the Blessed One taught that all things are like a dream. Noble son, certain people do not accurately discern all things as they are, like the dream the Tathāgata taught them to be. So they habitually fixate on a tathāgata as a body with names or a body with physical form, and hence imagine the tathāgatas to come and go. But since all who imagine the tathāgatas to come and go are not accurately discerning the true nature of things, they are immature types of ordinary people who have gone, are going, and will continue to go to the six realms of cyclic existence. They all are far from the transcendent state of wisdom. They all are far from the indications of a buddha. But definitely, noble son, those people who do accurately discern all things as they are, like the dream the Tathāgata taught them to be, do not imagine any things to come and go, or to arise and cease. Rather, they accurately discern the Tathāgata as the true nature. Also, those who accurately discern the Tathāgata as the true nature do not imagine the tathāgatas to come and go. Furthermore, when they accurately discern that true nature of the Tathāgata, they are going about life close to unsurpassed, perfect awakening‍—they are going about life in the transcendent state of wisdom. Moreover, when these disciples of the Blessed One partake of the alms of the kingdom, it is not in vain since they are worthy of being venerated by such people.

“ ‘Noble son, as an analogy, it is just like the jewels in the ocean. They do not come from any direction, not from the east, the south, the west, the north, below, or above. Instead, jewels that arise in the ocean are caused by the roots of virtue, not uncaused. They are a dependent arising that depends upon causes, conditions, and reasons. When these causes, conditions, and reasons cease, those jewels do not get transferred to any of the ten directions. In other words, when the conditions are present, the jewels appear, but when the conditions are not present, the jewels do not appear.According to Ratnākara (F.225.b), they do not “appear” from the ocean means they are not mentally perceived. RK: prabhāvyante upalabhyante. Likewise, noble son, the physical actualization of the tathāgatas does not come from anywhere in the world’s ten directions, nor does it go to anywhere in the world’s ten directions.According to Ratnākara (F.225.b), the “physical actualization” includes (a buddha’s) final birth in existence (prior to awakening). It does “not go anywhere” at the time of passing into nirvāṇa. RK: kāyapariniṣpattiḥ caramabhavasaṃgṛhītā| nāpi kvacid gacchatī ti parinirvāṇakāle. Nor do the blessed buddhas have a body that is uncaused. That body is actualized through previous deeds, dependent upon causes and conditions, arisen for a reason, and arisen from the ripening of previous karmic deeds. It does not exist anywhere in the world’s ten directions.According to Ratnākara (F.225.b), it “does not exist anywhere” means it does not go to any place after passing into nirvāṇa. RK: na kvacid astī ti parinirvāya yatra gacchati. Rather, when the requisite conditions are present, the physical actualization comes to be present. But when those requisite conditions are not present, one does not accurately discern any physical actualization.

“ ‘Noble son, it is just like, as an analogy, the sound emerging from a lute, which does not come from anywhere, and also when it is ceasing does not go or get transferred to anywhere. It arises, in dependence upon the totality of causes and conditions, as something that is dependent upon causes and conditions. To explain, that sound‍—which is dependent upon causes and conditions‍—emanates from a lute in dependence upon its wooden body, in dependence upon its leather covering, in dependence upon its strings, in dependence upon its cross bar, in dependence upon its bridge, in dependence upon its bow, and in dependence upon the effort of the person generating it. But that sound does not emanate from the wooden body. It does not emanate from the leather covering. It does not emanate from the strings. It does not emanate from the bridge. It does not emanate from the bow. It does not emanate from the effort of a person generating it. Nonetheless, one accurately discerns that sound through all those conditions coming together. Similarly, when the sound is ceasing, it does not go to anywhere.

“ ‘Noble son, it is exactly the same with the physical actualization of a blessed buddha. It is dependent upon causes and conditions, and is actualized because of many roots of virtue converging. The manifestation of a buddha’s body is not due to a single cause, a single condition, or a single root of virtue. Nor is it something uncaused.According to Ratnākara (F.225.b), here, the word “cause” means the substantive cause, whereas “conditions” means the contributing causes. Of these, some scholars say the substantive cause (of sound) here refers to the subtle atomic particles (in general), whereas other scholars say it refers to only the subtle atomic particle of “wind-energy.” With respect to this (lute), the substantive cause is the (sound board) covered by the strings in the lower part of a lute, as the strings are secondary for its resounding. The “sound does not emanate” from beginning to end, (which includes) the sound in the middle, as the referent with a logical connection to everything (else). RK: hetur upādānakāraṇaṃ| pratyayāḥ sahakāriṇaḥ| tatra śabdasyopādānaṃ śabdaparamāṇava ity eke| vāyuparamāṇava ity apare| tatra upadhānī tantrī veṣṭanaṃ ca kulikā| upavāṇyo ’pradhānaṃ tantrayaḥ| ādyantayoḥ na śabdo niścaratī ti vacanaṃ madhye sarvatrasambandhanārtham. As it has arisen in relation to the totality of causes and conditions, it does not come from any particular place. When that totality of causes and conditions is not there, it does not go to any particular place. Noble son, it is in this way that you should regard the tathāgatas to come and go, and also approach only the true nature of all things. Noble son, if you accurately discern the tathāgatas and all things to not have arisen or ceased in this way, then you will be assured of unsurpassed, perfect awakening, and you will, for sure, go about life in the transcendent state of wisdom and in the skill in means.’

“When this explication of the tathāgatas’ not coming and not going was being explicated, the earth quaked. There were six changes and eighteen great signs as everything in the billionfold, great order world system shook, shuddered, and juddered. Then, everything rocked, reeled, and tottered. Then, everything creaked, crackled, and cracked. Then, everything rattled, clattered, and crashed. Then, everything lurched, careened, and convulsed. Finally, everything rumbled, roared, and thundered.See n.870. All realms superintended by Māra became agitated and obscured.According to Ratnākara (225.b), here the word “agitated” means stirred up; “obscured” means lost in darkness. RK: saṃkṣobhitāni saṃpracalitāni| jihmībhūtāni naṣṭacchāyāni. All the grass, the bushes, and the trees in the billionfold, great order world system bowed in the direction of the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata. Flowers blossomed out of season. Above, in the sky, a great downpour of flower blossoms rained down everywhere. And upon the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, and the Four Great Kings rained down, showered, and scattered around heavenly māndārava flowers, aromatic powders of sandalwood, gold powders, and silver powders. At the same time, they uttered the words, ‘Excellent, noble son. Excellent. Through your miraculous power, noble son, today we have heard you teach this discourse born from the ultimate reality that is counterintuitive to the whole conventional world. In it, there is no ground for any sentient beings dwelling on any erroneous beliefs about the transitory collection or habitually fixating upon any erroneous beliefs in the unreal.’

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita said to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, ‘Noble son, what is the cause for this? What is the condition for the manifestation in the world of this great earthquake?’

“The bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata said, ‘Noble son, as a result of your requesting, and as a result of my giving, an explication of the tathāgatas’ not coming and not going, eight thousand beings attained the acceptance that all things are free of arising; eighty quintillion beings developed the proper mindset for unsurpassed, perfect awakening; and sixty-four thousand living beings had their Dharma eyes completely purified so that they became free of their dust and dirt.’

“The bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita felt a supreme, expansive joy and delight, thinking, ‘The fact that my request for this transcendent state of wisdom and Dharmodgata’s explication of the tathāgatas as neither coming nor going has brought so much benefit to sentient beings is to me the supreme positive gain. May this very virtue just obtained lead us to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening and not lead me to greater doubt regarding unsurpassed, perfect awakening, so that without any doubt I will become a worthy, perfectly awakened buddha.’

“By virtue of his feeling such pure joy and delight, Sadāprarudita began floating upward into the sky, reaching the height of seven palm trees. Once he came to a stop there at the height of seven palm trees, he thought, ‘While floating here now, how can I pay tribute to the bodhisattva great being?’

“Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, saw that the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita was floating, in his own mind knew his thoughts, and offered him heavenly māndārava flowers, saying, ‘Noble son, please pay tribute to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata with these heavenly flowers. Indeed, noble son, we gods should pay tribute to him for supporting you, since, noble son, it was by your grace today that many thousands of beings have been benefited. Noble son, it is difficult for such sentient beings to find someone who courageously bears the great burden for the sake of sentient beings for immeasurable and innumerable eons in the way that you are exerting yourself to do.’

“The bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita accepted the heavenly māndārava flowers from Śakra, powerful lord of the gods. He rained them down upon, showered them down upon, and scattered them around the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata. Next, he lay his own body prostrate before the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, and said, ‘Noble son, from this day forward I present myself as an offering to you so that I may attend you and physically serve you.’According to Ratnākara (225.b), he “lay his own body prostrate before Dharmodgata” means he fell to the ground with his whole body (lying prostrate) in front of him. RK: svakena kāyena dharmodgatam abhicchādayati sme ti tasyāgrataḥ sarvakāyena bhūmau patati smety arthaḥ. Having offered himself, he cupped his hands together in respect, then stood before the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata.

“Then the merchant’s daughter and the five hundred young ladies said to the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, ‘Noble son, we too present the offering of ourselves to you. With this root of virtue, may we attain the very same indications of a buddha. May we also pay tribute and show reverence to the blessed buddhas and bodhisattvas again and again, right along with you. And may we remain close to you.’

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita said to the merchant’s daughter and the five hundred young ladies, ‘If you young ladies are following my altruistic disposition, and with that altruistic disposition presenting yourselves to me as an offering, then I accept you.’
“The merchant’s daughter said, ‘We are following your altruistic disposition, and with that disposition presenting ourselves to you as an offering. Do with us as you wish.’

“Then, with all the ornaments, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita adorned the merchant’s daughter along with the five hundred young ladies, decorated the five hundred carriages, and presented them all as an offering to the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata so that they might attend and physically serve him, saying, ‘Noble son, for your thorough enjoyment, I present these servants to you as an offering, and I present these five hundred carriages to you as an offering.’

Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, commended the noble son: ‘Excellent, noble son. Excellent. A bodhisattva great being should completely renounce all his belongings. With such a mindset of complete renunciation, a bodhisattva great being quickly realizes unsurpassed, perfect awakening. After venerating the Dharma teacher in this way, a bodhisattva great being becomes able to hear the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means. Noble son, when all the past worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas had earlier been going about life how bodhisattvas go about life, they remained in renunciation and attained unsurpassed, perfect awakening through asking questions about the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means.’

“The bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata accepted the merchant’s daughter, along with the five hundred young ladies and the five hundred carriages, so that the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita’s virtue would be perfectly fulfilled. After accepting them, the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata got up and entered his house as it was time for the sun to set.

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita thought, ‘It would not be appropriate for me, since I came with the wish for Dharma, to sit down or go to sleep. Therefore, I shall remain here and spend my time only in the two physical positions of standing and walking until the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata emerges from his own house specifically to elucidate the Dharma.

“The bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata then immersed himself in a single state of meditative concentration that lasted seven years. He was in the state of immeasurable and innumerable thousands of meditative concentrations belonging to the bodhisattvas born from the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means. For seven years also, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita spent his time in those same two physical positions and never became drowsy or sleepy. Also, for seven years, he never had a lustful thought, a malevolent thought, a violent thought, a yearning for food, or any mental excitement. Instead, he thought, ‘Whenever it is that the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata comes out from this meditative concentration, we should prepare the Dharma throne so that the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata will sit on it to teach the Dharma. In the location where the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata will elucidate the transcendent state of wisdom and the skill in means, we should sprinkle water on it well, clean it well, and strew various flower blossoms about.’ The merchant’s daughter with the five hundred young ladies also imitated the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, spending their time in those same two physical positions and following all his activities.

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita heard a celestial voice say, ‘Seven days from now, the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata will come out from his meditative concentration.According to Ratnākara (F.225.b), “celestial” means arising from the sky. RK: divyam ity ākāśabhavam. After coming out, he will sit in the center of the city and teach the Dharma.’

“After hearing the celestial voice, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita became pleased, cheerful, gladdened, delighted, and full of joy and satisfaction. Together with the merchant’s daughter leading the five hundred young ladies, they purified the teaching area and prepared a Dharma throne made of the seven precious substances. From his body, he took off his own upper robe and arranged it over the throne. Then the merchant’s daughter led the five hundred young ladies to take off their own upper robes and arrange them over the throne, thinking, ‘The bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata will sit here and teach the Dharma.’ After covering the throne in this way, all those young ladies were pleased, cheerful, gladdened, delighted, and full of joy and satisfaction.

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita wished to sprinkle water on the teaching area. But despite searching everywhere, he found no water that he could use to sprinkle on the dust. Indeed, the pernicious Māra had dissolved all the water, hoping that the proper mindset of the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita would be disturbed by not finding any water, or while feeling suffering and despair, his mind would become sidetracked. Also, as a result, his roots of virtue would disappear, or his devotional offerings would lack the requisite luster.

“But the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita thought to himself, ‘What if I were to pierce my own body? If so, I could sprinkle my blood on this teaching area. Why is that? The dust in this teaching area has been kicked up, but no speck of dust from the ground should be allowed to fall upon the bodhisattva great being’s body. Aside from that, what else can be done with such a body, whose nature is to inevitably break down? It would be best to let it perish through such an activity, rather than through an activity lacking such potential benefits. Moreover, thanks to lust and motivated by the sense pleasures, many thousands of my bodies have been broken down again and again from one cyclic existence to another, but never in such a situation to apprehend the genuine Dharma. So, if it is to be broken down again, let it be broken down in such conditions in line with my aspirations!’

After the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita carefully considered it in this way, he grabbed a sharp knife, pierced his body all over, and sprinkled his own blood over the entire teaching area.According to Ratnākara (F.225.b), what he “carefully considered,” i.e., reflected about, was (mainly) the lack of water to sprinkle on the dusty earth. RK: pṛthivī sekavaimukhyam iti pratisaṃkhyāye ti evaṃ nirūpya. The merchant’s daughter, leading the five hundred young ladies, imitated the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita. They all grabbed sharp knives, pierced their own bodies, and sprinkled their own blood over the entire teaching area. During this, the minds of the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita and the young ladies never became sidetracked at any moment, such that the pernicious Māra might find any vulnerability to create obstacles for their roots of virtue.

“Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, thought this: ‘It is wonderful how much this bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita wishes for the Dharma, how firm his resolve is, how well he has donned the great armor, and how indifferent he is toward his body, life, and sense comforts. That is to say, how wonderful that he has set out to realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening with the altruistic disposition, thinking, “After I realize unsurpassed, perfect awakening, I will liberate all sentient beings from unlimited suffering in cyclic existence.” ’ Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, magically transformed all the blood into heavenly sandalwood-scented water so that everywhere in the whole area‍—within a two-hundred-fifty-mile radius‍—was filled with the inconceivable and supremely sublime scent of heavenly sandalwood.

“Then, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, ‘Excellent, noble son. Excellent. Noble son, your inconceivable heroic perseverance is excellent. Your longing and striving for the Dharma is unsurpassed. And, noble son, the previous worthy, perfectly and completely awakened tathāgatas all attained unsurpassed, perfect awakening through the very same kind of altruistic disposition, heroic perseverance, and longing for the Dharma.’

“Then the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita thought this: ‘I have prepared the Dharma throne for the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata, and also have sprinkled down and cleansed this teaching area. But where indeed can I find flower blossoms to spread over this area and strew them upon the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata when he is teaching the Dharma on the Dharma throne?’

“Then Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, said to the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita, ‘Noble son, take these heavenly māndārava flowers. Spread them over the area and strew them upon the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata when he is teaching the Dharma on the Dharma throne.’ Then Śakra presented him thousands of heavenly loads of heavenly māndārava flowers.
“The bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita accepted the flowers from him in order to spread them and other flowers over the whole area and strew them upon the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata.

“When the seven years ended, the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata emerged from his meditative concentration, went to the Dharma throne that had been prepared, and sat down upon it. Then, in front of the assembly of many hundreds of thousands surrounding him, he taught the transcendent state of wisdom. Just by seeing the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata once again, Sadāprarudita felt the kind of happiness that a monk feels when he has attained the first meditative attainment through one-pointed attentiveness.

“Upon that occasion, the bodhisattva great being Dharmodgata gave a teaching on the transcendent state of wisdom. Specifically, he said,According to Ratnākara (F.226.b), the word “specifically” is highlighting the particular details of what was signified generically (only in the previous sentence)‍—in this case, it means he teaches (the transcendent state of wisdom) through (the following) thirty aspects. For this very reason, we should read (below) the thirty meditative concentrations that arise for the bodhisattva Sadāprarudita with these thirty aspects to have arisen for him by force of listening to (the teaching on) the transcendent state of wisdom. RK: yaduta śabdaḥ sāmānyenoddiṣṭasya viśeṣaṃ dyotayati| ebhis triṃśatā ākārair ity arthaḥ| ata evāsyāḥ śrutabalenotpannā| ebhir eva triṃśatākāraiḥ sadāpraruditasya bodhisattvasyotpannās triṃśatsamādhayaḥ paṭhiṣyante. ‘One should know the following:According to Ratnākara (F.228.b), “one should know” is connected to all the following statements, because they (must be) lit up within (one’s experience, not merely intellectual). RK: veditavye ty ante yat paṭhyate tat­sarva­dharma­samatādivākyeṣu sarveṣu sambadhyate ’ntardīpakatvāt. Note: Due to Sanskrit word order, “one should know” (veditavya) appears in the text at the end of the series of statements that follow. But in English word order, Ratnākara’s comments can be understood best here. The transcendent state of wisdom is a sameness, just as all things are a sameness.According to Ratnākara (F.226.b), the “sameness of the transcendent state of wisdom” is with respect to all things. Why is that? It says “since all things are a sameness,” i.e., since the “transcendent state of wisdom” is the absolute dimension of all things as its objective scope, and since that is the same with respect to all things. RK: prajñāpāramitā yāḥ samatā sarvadharmeṣu| kutaḥ? sarvadharmasamatayā| sā hi dharmadhātvālambanā| sa ca samānaḥ sarvadharmeṣu. The transcendent state of wisdom is void, just as all things are void.According to Ratnākara (F.226.b), since all things are “void” of their own individual characteristics, the transcendent state of wisdom also is void of the appearance of any individual characteristics. RK: yataḥ sarvadharmāḥ svalakṣaṇair viviktāḥ| ataḥ sāpi viviktā svalakṣaṇapratibhāsaiḥ. The transcendent state of wisdom is not moving, just as all things are not moving.According to Ratnākara (F.226.b), “since all things are not moving”‍—i.e., because they are void‍—the transcendent state of wisdom also is not moving because it is void. RK: yataḥ sarvadharmā na vivekāc calanti| tataḥ sāpi na vivekāc calati. The transcendent state of wisdom is free of creating conceits, just as all things are free of creating conceits.According to Ratnākara (F.226.b), “all things are free of creating conceits” about anything because they lack any inherent nature. Therefore, the transcendent state of wisdom also is free of creating conceits. RK: amananā hi sarvadharmāḥ| na te kiñcin manyante niḥsvabhāvatvāt| tataḥ sāpy amananā. The transcendent state of wisdom is not stopped, just as all things are not stopped.According to Ratnākara (F.226.b), “all things” do not move because they are void, and hence “are not stopped” by anything at all.
