Nevertheless, things are, by nature, immovable in the void, and hence the transcendent state of wisdom is immovable too. RK: na hi vivekāc calanti sarvadharmāḥ kenāpi stambhitāḥ| kiṃ tarhi prakṛtyaiva viveke sthirās tataḥ prajñāpāramitāpi sthirā. The transcendent state of wisdom has a single flavor, just as all things have a single flavor.According to Ratnākara (F.226.b), “all things have a single flavor” since all things have the single flavor of the actual nature, because they lack their own individual characteristics. Similarly, the transcendent state of wisdom also has a single flavor because individual characteristics do not appear (in it). RK: sarvadharm āṇām ekarasatayā tathataikarasā hi sarvadharmāḥ svalakṣaṇānām abhāvāt| tadvat prajñāpāramitāpi svalakṣaṇānām apratibhāsāt. The transcendent state of wisdom has no limits, just as all things have no limits.According to Ratnākara (F.226.b), to explain, the two “limits” are the beginning point and ending point, and all things have neither of those two limits, because the past and future do not exist. Therefore, “all things have no limits,” and likewise the transcendent state of wisdom does not have them either. RK: tathā hi dvau paryantau pūrvā ca koṭir aparā ca| tau ca na staḥ sarvadharmāṇām| atītānagatayor asattvāt| tasmād aparyantāḥ sarvadharmāḥ| tadvat prajñāpāramitāpi. The transcendent state of wisdom is free of arising, just as all things are free of arising.According to Ratnākara (F.226.b), to explain, “all things” have no inherent nature. Hence, like the horns of a mule, they do not arise. Thus, they are “free of arising,” and likewise the transcendent state of wisdom is too. RK: tathā hi niḥsvabhāvāḥ sarvadharmāḥ| tasmān notpadyante kharaviṣāṇavat| tasmād anutpādāḥ| tadvat prajñāpāramitāpi. The transcendent state of wisdom is free of ceasing, just as all things are free of ceasing.According to Ratnākara (F.226.b), “all things are free of ceasing” means they do not cease due to their nonexistence (in the first place), like the horns of a mule. Likewise, the transcendent state of wisdom does not cease either. RK: sarvadharmā ṇām anirodha tayā| na hy amī nirudhyante ’sattvāt kharaviṣāṇavat| tadvat prajñāpāramitāpi.

“ ‘The transcendent state of wisdom is limitless, just as the sky is limitless.According to Ratnākara (F.226.b), the “sky is limitless” because it has no limit in the ten directions or the three times. The same is true of the transcendent state of wisdom, because, in being connected to the ten directions and three times, she reveals all things to be the actual nature alone. RK: gaganāparyantatayā| tathā hi gaganasya nāsti paryanto daśasu dikṣu triṣu cādhveṣu| evaṃ prajñāpāramitāyāḥ| yā daśadiktraiyadhvikā sarva­dharma­tathatāmātraprathanāt. The transcendent state of wisdom is limitless, just as the ocean is limitless.According to Ratnākara (F.226.b–27.a), in all the world systems of the ten directions, just as the sky has no end, how much more so for the oceans in those endless world systems, thus proving the limitlessness of the ocean. The same is true for the transcendent state of wisdom. To explain: The actual nature of the oceans, all things, and the transcendent state of wisdom are all the same single actual nature. And the transcendent state of wisdom has that actual nature as its objective scope. So this proves that the transcendent state of wisdom is limitless in the way that the ocean is limitless. RK: samudrāparyantatayā prajñāpāramitāparyantate ti| yathā hy ākāśasya nāsty antas tathaiva daśadiglokadhātūnāṃ kim punar ananteṣu lokadhātuṣu samudrāṇām| tataḥ siddhā samudrāparyantatā| tadvat prajñāpāramitāyāḥ| tathā hi yā teṣāṃ samudrāṇāṃ tathatā yā ca sarvadharmāṇāṃ yā ca prajñāpāramitāyāḥ, ekaivaiṣā tathatā| tathatālambanā ca prajñāpāramitā| tasmāt samudrāparyantatayā prajñā­pāramitāparyantateti siddham. The transcendent state of wisdom is multifaceted, just as Mount Meru is multifaceted.According to Ratnākara (F.227.a), since space by nature has a single flavor, it is the dominant (condition for) the multifaceted sides of Mount Meru. Hence, just as it appears in a multifaceted way, just so the actual nature and the transcendent state of wisdom‍—which has the actual nature as its objective scope‍—have only a single flavor and are not (themselves) multifaceted. However, the thing that is imagining the unreal appears multifaceted in the knowing of (things) being like a dream and so on. Therefore, the knowing of (things) being like a dream or like an illusion is at the conventional level. This is taught to introduce beings to the ultimate knowing free of appearances. Nonconceptualization‍—which is the state after (the meditation’s) freedom from appearance‍—refers to the certainty about what was experienced (just previously in the meditative state free of appearance). Therefore it is proved that the transcendent state of wisdom (in the post-meditative worldly state) “is multifaceted just as Mount Meru is multifaceted.” RK: yathā hy ekarasam ākāśaṃ prakṛtyā tathāpi merupārśvānāṃ vicitrāṇām ādhipatyāt tadvad eva vicitraṃ khyāti| evam ekarasaiva tathatā na vicitrā| tathatālambanā ca prajñāpāramitā| yas tu svapnopamādijñāne vicitrapratibhāsaḥ so ’bhūtaparikalpadharmaḥ| tasmāt svapnopama­māyopamādi­jñānaṃ saṃvṛtiḥ| nirābhāse yā ca parimārthike jñāne sattvanām avatāraṇāya nirdiśyate, nirābhāsapṛṣṭhabhāvī cāvikalpo ’yaṃ tadanubhūtaniścayārthaḥ| tasmāt siddham idaṃ meruvicitratayā prajñāpāramitāvicitrate ti. Note: The translation of Sanskrit vicitra as “multifaceted” has been used throughout these comments because Mount Meru is the point of comparison. Ratnākara takes the word more broadly here to be meaning “variety” and “diversity” of appearances. In his philosophical system, this diversity of appearances is a conventional reality that appears only out of cognitive error and conceptualization. The transcendent state of wisdom does not fabricate, just as the sky does not fabricate.According to Ratnākara (F.227.a–b), “the transcendent state of wisdom does not fabricate” anything because it has transcended conceptualization (i.e., fabrication). As an example, “the sky does not fabricate” because it is inanimate (and hence cannot fabricate, i.e., conceptualize). You might say, “Fine, but if the transcendent state of wisdom is not animate (ajaḍa), then why does it not conceptualize?” It is taught in this scripture that conceptualization is an error, but the transcendent state of wisdom is error-free. Thus, it is proven that the transcendent state of wisdom does not conceptualize. RK: prajñāpāramitā yā yākalpanatā na sā kiñcid vikalpayati vikalpapāragatatvāt| tadyathā gaganaṃ na kiñcid vikalpayati jaḍatvāt| ajaḍā prajñāpāramitā kin na vikalpayatīti cet| uktam atra| vikalpaś ca bhrāntir abhrāntā ca sā| tasmān na vikalpayatīti siddham.

“ ‘The transcendent state of wisdom is limitless, just as physical forms are limitless. Likewise, the transcendent state of wisdom is limitless, just as feelings, conceptions, conditioning factors, and consciousness are limitless.

“ ‘The transcendent state of wisdom is limitless, just as the earth element is limitless. Likewise, the transcendent state of wisdom is limitless, just as the water element, the fire element, the wind element, and the space element are limitless. The transcendent state of wisdom is limitless, just as the element of consciousness is limitless.According to Ratnākara (F.227.b), since space is limitless, the constituents of experience are too, because where there is no physical form, there automatically can be no darkness or illumination (of that form). Likewise, since it appears in relation to the actual nature of the qualities belonging to those (physical forms and so on), the “transcendent state of wisdom is limitless.” Since we explained that the transcendent state of wisdom has no limit just as the ocean has none, we should explain it, due to the endlessness of beings, as also the limitlessness of feelings, conceptions, consciousness, the earth element, the water element, the fire element, the air element, the space element, and the consciousness element. One might ask, “What is the difference (in this list here) between the limitlessness of consciousness as a constituent of experience and as an element? The two terms “constituent of experience” (skandha) and “element” (dhātu) are applied (contextually and thus frame consciousness differently). RK: rūpāparyantatayā prajñāpāramitāparyantate ti| yathā hy ākāśam aparyantaṃ tathārūpaskandho ’pi| yatrānyad rūpaṃ nāsti tatrāvaśyaṃ tamaḥ prakāśo vāsti| tadvat tadīyatathatāpratibhāsinī prajñāpāramitā py aparyantatā| yathaiva samudrāparyantatayā prajñāpāramitāparyantatā ’smābhir vyākhyātā tathaiva sattvānām ānantyāt vedanāparyantatayāpi saṃskāraparyantatayāpi vijñānāparyantatayāpi pṛthivīdhātvaparyantatayāpi apdhātvaparyantatayāpi tejodhātvaparyantatayāpi vāyudhātvaparyantatayāpi ākāśadhātvaparyantatayāpi vijñāna­dhātvaparyantatayāpi vyākhyātavyā| atha yā vijñāna­skandhāparyantatayā yā ca vijñānadhātvaparyantatayā tayoḥ ko viśeṣaḥ ? skandhadhātuśabdābhyāṃ prayogakṛtaḥ.

“ ‘The transcendent state of wisdom is a sameness, just as the vajra-like Dharma is a sameness.According to Ratnākara (F.227.b–28.a), here, due to bearing (the quality of) a mindset, the (vajra-like) “dharma” refers to the (vajra-like) meditative concentration. The transcendent state of wisdom’s “sameness” is due to that vajra-like meditative concentration’s “sameness” with regard to all things. RK: cittadhāraṇād dharmaḥ samādhiḥ| tasya yā sarvadharmeṣu samatā tayopamānena prajñāpāramitā yāḥ samatā. The transcendent state of wisdom is free of differentiation, just as all things are free of differentiation.According to Ratnākara (F.228.a), “all things are free of differentiation” means they are not a distinction, because the differentiations lack individual characteristics. Likewise, the transcendent state of wisdom also is not a distinction, because it has no (differentiations) in terms of a particular nature being experienced. RK: sarvadharmeṣu sarva­dharmā­sambhedanatayā prajñā­pāramitā­sambhedanat eti sarvadharmāṇām asambhedo abhedaḥ| bhedakānāṃ svalakṣaṇānām abhāvāt| tadvat prajñāpāramitāyā api na bhedaḥ| samvedyamānenaiva rūpeṇa teṣāṃ tasyām abhāvāt. The transcendent state of wisdom is free of mental perception, just as all things are free of mental perception.According to Ratnākara (F.228.a), “all things” do not mentally perceive anything (a) because physical form is inanimate, (b) because all other things are connected to a subject grasping (that physical form) in relation to oneself or other, and (c) because, as the subject grasping does not exist, those things cannot be mentally perceiving (anything other than it). Likewise, the “transcendent state of wisdom” too neither mentally perceives nor is mentally perceived. Therefore, things and the transcendent state of wisdom are both free of mental perception. RK: sarvadharmā na kiñcid upalabhante| rūpasya jaḍatvāt| śeṣāṇām apy ātmani paratra vā grāhakatvāyogāt| nāpy upalabhyante grāhakābhāvāt| tadvat prajñāpāramitā pi nopalabhate nāpyupalabhyate| tasmāt teṣāṃ tasyāś cānupalabdhitā. The transcendent state of wisdom is the same as the freedom from clarification, just as all things are the same as the freedom from clarification.According to Ratnākara (F.228.a), “clarifying” means showing to others through words. A clarification is not found to exist for any things. Therefore, those things are the “same as the freedom from clarification.” Likewise, the transcendent state of wisdom is the “same as the freedom from clarification” with regard to all objective scopes. RK: prajñā­pāramitāvibhāvanāsamat eti| vibhāvanāṃ parebhyo vācā deśanā| sā sarvadharmāṇāṃ na vidyate tasmād avibhāvanayā samatā teṣām| tadvat sarvālambaneṣu prajñāpāramitāyā avibhāvanāsamatā. Note: This translation “freedom from clarification” (avibhāvanā) is based on Ratnākara’s comments. The Sanskrit edition of Mitra (copied in Wogihara and Vaidya) has an error, reading abhibhāvanā. Hari does not comment on the term, but while Ratnākara’s comments confirm his reading of avibhāvanā, his comments in Tibetan translation are confusing: the translation has him using rnam par ’jig pa med pa in his explanation, but its apparent opposite rnam par ’jig pa when citing the words of the scripture. It is the latter reading that conforms to the wording of the scripture in all Kangyurs‍—both here and in the list of meditative concentrations that follows. The Sanskrit term vibhāvana has a complex range of meanings, and for more on the difficulties associated with the Tibetan interpretations of this term, see glossary, and also n.267 in the Twenty-Five Thousand.

“ ‘The transcendent state of wisdom has no function, just as all things have no function.According to Ratnākara (F.228.a), in this context “function” refers to operation. “All things” do not operate. How then could anything arise from anywhere? It arises from mere conditions, to the extent that the law of causality is, “When one thing is present, another thing comes to be. When one thing is absent, another thing ceases to be.” Therefore, things are free of functioning, and the transcendent state of wisdom is too. RK: ceṣṭā īhāvyāpāraḥ| avyāpāraḥ sarvadharmāḥ| kathaṃ tarhi kaścit kutaścid utpadyate ? idaṃ pratyayamātreṇa| asmin satīdaṃ bhavati, asati na bhavatītyetāvatā hetuphalabhāvaḥ| tasmād amī niśceṣṭāḥ tadvat prajñāpāramitāpi. The transcendent state of wisdom is inconceivable, just as all things are inconceivable.’According to Ratnākara (F.228.b), “things” are never signified to others since words have no (real) scope (as explained previously). So, they themselves cannot be conceived of or described, since speech and concept have a single referent (namely, a generic mental image, not a particular one). Similarly, the “transcendent state of wisdom is inconceivable.” RK: yathā hi dharmāḥ parebhyo na deśyante nirviṣayatvād vācaḥ| tathā svayam api na cintyante na nirūpyante vāgvikalpayor ekārthatvāt| tadvat prajñāpāramitā py acintyā.

“Now, at that time, while the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita was still sitting there, he attained the king of meditative concentrations known as focused on all things as a sameness.According to Ratnākara (F.228.b), “now” means at the end of this teaching. “While he was still sitting” means he had not yet arisen from the seat where he was been listening. “There” means (in) the completed general teaching and its explanation. “At that time” means, in brief, those stages and aspects with which the transcendent state of wisdom was taught arose for him as the thirty meditative concentrations (described below). RK: atha khalv ityādi| athe ti deśanāparisamāptau| tathāniṣaṇṇasyaive ti śrutāsanād anutthitasya| tathe ty uddeśaḥ kṛtaḥ| tasyaiva nirdeśaḥ| tasyāṃ velāyām iti| yenaiva krameṇa yair evākāraiḥ prajñāpāramitā deśitā tenaiva krameṇa tair evākārais triṃśatsamādhaya utpannā iti samudāyārthaḥ. After that, he attained the meditative concentration called focused on all things being free from conceit; the meditative concentration called focused on all things as not stopped; the meditative concentration called focused on all things as having a single flavor; the meditative concentration called focused on all things as limitless; the meditative concentration called focused on all things as free from arising; the meditative concentration called focused on all things as free from ceasing; the meditative concentration called focused on limitlessness like the sky; the meditative concentration called focused on limitlessness like the ocean; the meditative concentration called focused on the multifaceted like Meru; the meditative concentration called focused on the unfabricated like the sky; the meditative concentration called focused on limitlessness like physical forms; the meditative concentration called focused on limitlessness like feelings, conceptions, conditioning forces, and consciousness; the meditative concentration called focused on limitlessness like the earth element; the meditative concentration called focused on limitlessness like the water element, the fire element, the wind element, and the space element; the meditative concentration called focused on being like a vajra; the meditative concentration called focused on all things free of differentiation; the meditative concentration called focused on all things being free of mental perception; the meditative concentration called focused on all things as the same as freedom from clarification; and the meditative concentration called focused on all things as inconceivable. These meditative concentrations were the main ones among the six million meditative concentrations that the bodhisattva great being attained.”According to Ratnākara (F.228.b), the meditative concentrations are called what they are called because their meditative perspectives (ākāra) are “all things as a sameness” and so on. How are these perspectives? Because one is aware of things through these modes (of seeing). As it is said, “A meditative perspective is of the modes of being aware of things. This is the defining characteristic.” Then what is the meditative reference point (ālambana)? It is merely each thing on its own account. Alternatively, (one can also understand the names of the meditative concentrations), such as all things as a sameness and so on, themselves as the meditative reference points, because the meditative concentrations are what take those meditative objects as their reference point. That is to say, a meditative object is a meditative support. So (in this way of understanding it), the meditative perspective would be the freedom from conventional signs and so on (on which each one is focused). These thirty “meditative concentrations are the main ones.” How could they be “main”? Because these are indicative of all the others, i.e., the (six) million meditative concentrations (that he experienced). RK: atha khalv ityādi| athe ti deśanāparisamāptau| tathāniṣaṇṇasyaive ti śrutāsanād anutthitasya| tathe ty uddeśaḥ kṛtaḥ| tasyaiva nirdeśaḥ| tasyāṃ velāyām iti| yenaiva krameṇa yair evākāraiḥ prajñāpāramitā deśitā tenaiva krameṇa tair evākārais triṃśatsamādhaya utpannā iti samudāyārthaḥ| tatra sarvadharmasamatā dyākāratvāt samādhayas tathā vyapadiśyante| katham amī ākārāḥ ? ebhiḥ prakārair vastujñānāt| yathoktam‍—vastujñānaprakārāṇām ākāra iti lakṣaṇam|iti| kiṃ tarhy ālambanam ? tad eva vastu yathāsvam| athavā sarvadharmasamatā dir evālambanam| tadālambanatvāt samādhayas tathoktāḥ| vastūny adhiṣṭhāni| ākāro yathāsvaṃ nirnimittatādiḥ| evaṃ pramukhānī ti triṃśatsamādhipramukhāni| katham eṣāṃ pramukhatvam ? ebhir avaśeṣāṇām ākṣepāt śatasahasrāṇī ti lakṣāṇi.
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “Dharmodgata,” the thirty-first chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Chapter 32: The EntrustmentAccording to Ratnākara (F.230.a), the “Chapter of Entrustment” is the chapter that expresses the entrustment. RK: parīndanā bhidhāyī parivartaḥ parīndanāparivartaḥ.This chapter corresponds to Chapter 76 of the Twenty-Five Thousand, and Chapter 87 of the Eighteen Thousand. The Hundred Thousand as witnessed in the Sanskrit and in the Tibetan of most Kangyurs does not include corresponding content.
div.p: “Subhūti, at the same time that the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita attained those six million meditative concentrations, he saw blessed buddhas, equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges, in all the ten directions‍—in the east, south, west, north, between, above, and below‍—within the billionfold, great order world systems equal in number to the grains of sand in the River Ganges. Each buddha was surrounded by his saṅgha of monks and attended by those with the positive qualities of bodhisattvas. Each was teaching this very transcendent state of wisdom with these very methods, these very words, and these very syllables.According to Ratnākara (F.229.a), the Blessed One says “at the same that he attained” and so on to describe the benefits of what arose at the time of those meditative concentrations that he attained. For this reason, the words “at that time” mean to express the simultaneity with when he attained them. “These very methods” refers to the previously taught topics, i.e., the eight clear realizations. “These words” refers to the state of complete knowing of all aspects and so on. RK: sahapratilabdhānām ityādi| anena labdhānāṃ samādhīnāṃ tatkālajam anuśaṃsam āha| ata eva saha śabdaḥ samānakālatārthaḥ| pratilabdhānām iti pratilabdheṣu satsu| ebhir eva nayer iti yathoktavastubhir aṣṭābhir abhisamayaiḥ| ebhir eva nāmabhir iti sarvākārajñatādibhiḥ. Each was doing exactly what I am doing right now, surrounded by this saṅgha of monks and attended by this community of bodhisattvas, and teaching this very transcendent state of wisdom with these very methods, these very words, and these very syllables. Through seeing these, the bodhisattva great being Sadāprarudita came to possess inconceivably great learning and ocean-like knowledge. In all his births, he was never separate from the buddhas and was born only where he would be face to face with the blessed buddhas. He was never separate from the blessed buddhas, even in the midst of sleep.According to Ratnākara (F.229.a), “in all his births, he was never separate from the buddhas” is the general overview. The detailed explanation of it comes in the following passage up to “in the midst of sleep.” RK: sarvāsu ca jātiṣu na jātu buddhavirahito ’bhūd ity uddeśaḥ| asya nirdeśa uttaro granthaḥ svapnāntaragatopī ti yāvat. He completely left behind inauspicious birth and gained the good fortune of auspicious births.”

Then the Blessed One said to the venerable Ānanda, “Ānanda, you should understand also through this Dharma teaching how this transcendent state of wisdom brings forth the knowing of someone who has complete knowing.According to Ratnākara (F.229.a), this sentence is the introduction to the entrustment. “Also through this Dharma teaching” means also through the story of Sadāprarudita. RK: tatra khalv ityādir upodghātaḥ parīndanāyāḥ| tad anenāpi paryāyeṇai ti sadāpraruditavṛttāntenāpi. Therefore, Ānanda, any bodhisattva great being who wishes to obtain the knowing of someone who has complete knowing should go about life in this transcendent state of wisdom. Ānanda, this transcendent state of wisdom should be listened to, taken in, kept in mind, chanted, mastered, passed on, taught, expounded, explained, recited, and written out. Through the Tathāgata’s blessings, it should be made into a high-quality book, nicely written out with extremely clear letters. Then one should pay tribute to it‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, cloth, musical instruments, parasols, fabrics, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and care for it.According to Ratnākara (F.229.a), what is the purpose for the words “through the Tathāgata’s blessings”? It is to show that due to its great value and the many obstacles (that come with that value), one cannot even write out the blessed transcendent state of wisdom without the blessings of the buddhas. RK: atra ca tathāgatādhiṣṭhānene ti vacanaṃ kim artham ? mahārthatvād bhagavatī bahvantarāyā ca vinā buddhādhiṣṭhānena likhitum api na śakyeti pradarśanārtham. Ānanda, this is our admonition to you.According to Ratnākara (F.229.a), “this is our admonition to you” means this is our entrustment to you, or in other words you should act to ensure that studying the scripture and so on is not interrupted. RK: iyam asmākam anuśāsanī tīyam asmākaṃ parīndanā| yathā asyāḥ śravaṇādikaṃ na vicchidyate tathā tvayā kartavyam ity arthaḥ. Why is that?According to Ratnākara (F.229.a), “Why is that?” asks what is the purpose for this entrusting. The purpose is then explained in the following sentence. RK: tatkasya hetor iti| tatparīndanaṃ kena prayojanenety arthaḥ| ata āha| atra hī tyādi. It is because one actualizes the knowing of someone who has complete knowing in this transcendent state of wisdom. Ānanda, what do you think? Is the Tathāgata your teacher?”According to Ratnākara (F.229.a–b), the introduction of the second entrustment begins with this question. RK: tatkim ityādinā dvītīyaparīndanāyā upodghātaḥ.
“Blessed One, the Tathāgata is my teacher. Sugata, the Tathāgata is my teacher,” said Ānanda.

At this, the Blessed One said to the venerable Ānanda, “Ānanda, you have served me with benevolent and agreeable acts of body, acts of speech, and acts of mind. Therefore, Ānanda, just as you have acted with appreciation, trust, and devotion toward this assemblage belonging to me while it is presently residing, existing, and remaining here, exactly so, Ānanda, you should act toward this transcendent state of wisdom.According to Ratnākara (F.229.b), “this assemblage belonging to me” means my body. RK: asmin mama samucchaya iti mama śarīre. I am entrusting you this transcendent state of wisdom and transmitting it to you a second time, and also a third time, to ensure that it does not disappear, since no other person besides you would be able to do so.According to Ratnākara (F.229.b), “exactly so” means “with appreciation” and so on. With these words, he gives two entrustments, since he says “a second time, and also a third time.” The words “I entrust” means I am giving it to you; “I transmit” means I am giving it to you with our minds in agreement. Why is he entrusting it? He says “to ensure that …” and so on. The “other persons” refers to those without his devotion. RK: tathaiva iti| etat premā dikam| etenopodghātena dve parīndane| yata āha dvir api trir apī ti| parīndāmī ti samarpayāmi| anuparīndamī ty ānukūlyaṃ samarpayāmi| kathaṃ parīndanety ata āha yatheyam ityādi| anyaḥ puruṣa ity abhaktaḥ. As long as this transcendent state of wisdom is circulating among beings in the world, Ānanda, one should understand the Tathāgata to be remaining, one should understand the Tathāgata to be teaching the Dharma, and one should understand those sentient beings to never be separate from seeing the Buddha, from hearing the Dharma, or from being near the Saṅgha. Also, Ānanda, one should understand those sentient beings to be living in the presence of the Tathāgata whenever they listen to this transcendent state of wisdom, take it in, keep it in mind, chant it, master it, pass it on, teach it, expound it, explain it, recite it, write it out, pay tribute to it‍—with flowers, incense, perfumes, wreaths, fragrant oils, aromatic powders, cloth, parasols, banners, bells, flags, garlands of lights all around, and a wide variety of other devotional offerings to venerate it‍—revere it, respect it, venerate it, honor it, and tend to it.”

As the Blessed One taught this with delight in his heart, they all‍—the bodhisattva great beings headed by Maitreya, the venerable Subhūti, the venerable Śariputra, the venerable Ānanda, Śakra, powerful lord of the gods, and the world with its gods, humans, asuras, garuḍas, and gandharvas‍—rejoiced in what the Blessed One had taught.According to Ratnākara (F.229.b), to highlight the end of the blessed Transcendent State of Wisdom, the reciter of this scripture says “as the Blessed One taught this” and so on. “This” refers to the Transcendent State of Wisdom scripture that he has just taught. The “Blessed One” means the teacher. The tathāgatas delight in benefiting others. The teaching is for the benefit of others and brings benefit to others. So, the reciter of the scripture says “with delight in his heart,” i.e., he is pleased with having taught the unexcelled Dharma teaching. How much more are the listeners made pleased? He says they “rejoiced in what the Blessed One had taught,” i.e., one after another they rejoiced in the deed performed. The same (rejoicing) can generally be applied to desire at any time or to happiness at any time. That is to say, when one is in happiness, then taking that very (happiness) as an object is a deed (to be rejoiced in or dedicated). But this deed is “what the Blessed One had taught,” i.e., this very teaching. But who were the listeners? The reciter says “they all” and so on. Here, “the world” refers to the multitude of beings (in it). Also, since the listeners and Rājagṛha are mentioned, one might assume the beings to be just a group of “humans” alone. But to counter that assumption, he distinguishes “gods, humans, asuras, garuḍas, and gandharvas.” So one should include them‍—i.e., the bodhisattvas and the gods and so on‍—as those in “the world.” RK: bhagavatyāḥ parisamāptim udyotayan saṅgītikāra āha| idam avocad ityādi| idam iti prajñāpāramitāsūtram| avocad iti bhāṣitavān| bhagavān iti śāstā| parārtharasikās tathāgatāḥ| parārthaṃś ca parārthakriyā bhagavatyā deśanā| tata āha| āttamanā iti| nir­uttara­dharmadeśanayā prītaḥ sann ity arthaḥ| śrotāraḥ kimu kurvann ityāha| bhagavato bhāṣitam abhyanandann iti| abhipūrvo nandiḥ sakarmakaḥ| sa kadācidabhilāṣe vartate| kadācitprītau| yadā prītau tadā tadālambanam evāsya karma bhavati| tat punaḥ karma bhagavato bhāṣitam| eṣaiva deśanā| ke punas te śrotāra ity āha| te cetyādi| lokaḥ sattvasamūhaḥ| sa ca śrotṝṇāṃ rājagṛhasya ca prakṛtatvān manuṣya samūha eva kevalo gamyeta| tathā mā bhūd iti viśeṣaṇaṃ kriyate| sadevamānuṣāsuragandharvaś ceti| te ca bodhisattvādayaḥ sadevādiś ca loka iti samuccayaḥ.
div.trailer: This ends the chapter “The Entrustment,” the thirty-second chapter in “The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines.”

div.head: Colophon
Colophon from the Sanskrit
div.p: This ends The Noble Transcendent State of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines. She is the one who engenders all the tathāgatas; the mother of the bodhisattvas, the solitary realizers, and the disciples; the seal of the Dharma; the torch of the Dharma; the drum of the Dharma; the wreath of pearls inconceivable and amazing to behold; venerated by the world with its gods, humans, asuras, garuḍas, and gandharvas; the inexhaustible Dharma, a repository of Dharma jewels; and the cause of all happiness.

“Of those things produced by a cause, the Tathāgata has taught their cause and cessation. He who so explains is the Great Mendicant.”As many readers will recognize, this is a translation of the famous formula ye dharmā hetuprabhāvā hetusteṣāṃ tathāgato hyavadat/ teṣāṃ ca yo nirodha evaṃvādī mahāśramaṇaḥ first pronounced by the disciple Asvajit, as a summary of the Buddha’s teaching, to Śāriputra before the latter first met the Buddha (see The Chapter on Going Forth, 1.292) sometimes called “the essence of dependent arising” (rten ’brel snying po). The formula in Sanskrit and Pali has acquired the status of a dhāraṇī, and is ubiquitous in Buddhist Asia as a seal at the end of texts, rolled into scrolls in stūpas, or used in rituals (sometimes with oṁ at the beginning and svāhā at the end).

div.head: Translators’ colophon from the Tibetan
div.p: Translated, edited, and verified by the Indian preceptors Śākyasena and Jñānasiddhi, the chief editor and translator Bandé Dharmatāśīla, and others. Subsequently, on the orders of the sublime lord ruler of Tibet, Pel Lhatsenpo Tashi Lhadetsen,dpal lha btsan po bkra shis lha sde btsan‍—the name of a tenth-century ruler in Western Tibet, either a predecessor or successor of Lha Lama Yeshé Ö. See van der Kuijp 1992. the Indian preceptor Subhāṣita and the translator, the monk Rinchen Zangpo, harmonized it with the commentary. Subsequently again, the Indian preceptor, the great paṇḍita Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna, and the chief editor-translator, the monk Rinchen Zangpo, harmonized it with a commentary from Magadha, emending, editing, and establishing it. Still later, at a time when the great paṇḍita Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna and the translator Drom Gyalwai Jungné were both expounding The Eight Thousand at Nyethang Namoché in the Lower Kyichu Valley, they verified one approximate passage.Tentative translation of che long zhig gtan la phab. Afterward, at the temple of Radreng, the translator Drom Gyalwai Jungné verified it twice by comparing it with three Indian sūtra manuscripts. Later still, the same translator expounded and verified some minor points. In a later period the great translator and Śākya bhikṣu Loden Sherab collected a number of manuscripts from Kashmir and Magadha and verified the text.

note.p: The eastern buddhafield of the Buddha Akṣobhya.

term.hi: nyon mongs pa med pa la gnas pa

note.p: See n.100.
The third of four stages or experiences of breaking through on the path of preliminary practice.
Fifth and highest of the five pure abodes, meaning “Highest.”
The buddha who presides over the buddhafield of Abhirati.
The name of one of the four gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.

Eleventh of the thirteen levels of the god realm of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Cloudless.” In some versions of the longer Prajñāpāramitā sūtras, which list sixteen realms instead of thirteen, this god realm has the name Parīttabṛhat (Tib. chung che).

term.hi: dran pa nye bar gzhag pa

note.p: The placing of attention, especially on four particular objects: body, feelings, mind, and phenomena. Classified among the dharmas of the path, and figuring among the thirty-seven factors in awakening. See n.218.
Ninth of the thirteen god realms of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Immeasurable Virtue.”
Sixth of the thirteen god realms of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Immeasurable Radiance.”

A god realm of form listed in this and other texts between the thirteenth heaven of the form realm, Bṛhatphala, and the five pure abodes of the form realm, known collectively as Śuddhāvāsa. Its name means “Being Without Conception.”

gloss.term: ascetic who accepts whatever is offered

note.p: One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.

gloss.term: ascetic who dwells in the forest

note.p: One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.
One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.

gloss.term: ascetic who lives at the foot of a tree

note.p: One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.

gloss.term: ascetic who lives in charnel grounds

note.p: One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.

gloss.term: ascetic who lives in seclusion, contented with few desires

term.hi: ’dod pa nyung zhing chog shes pa rab tu dben pa

note.p: One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.

gloss.term: ascetic who lives in the open air

note.p: One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.
One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.
One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.
One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.

gloss.term: ascetic who uses a single seat

note.p: One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.
One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.
One of the kinds of ascetic practice, usually numbered as thirteen altogether, that were optionally followed by certain renunciant monks. See n.1076 and following notes.
See n.219.
Second of the five pure abodes, meaning “Painless.”

The great Bengali master (982–1054) and Vikramaśīla teacher who spent the last twelve years of his life teaching in Tibet and overseeing the translation of many important texts, including his own works, near the beginning of the later translation period. Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna was his ordination name, but in Tibet he was often known by the soubriquet Atiśa, or simply as Jowo Jé (jo bo rje, “the great lord”).

term.hi: ’jig rten gyi zang zing dang ldan pa
pha rol gyi gnod pa byed

note.p: A defining characteristic, like, for instance, the dewlap of a cow. See n.881.
The name that six hundred future buddhas will all take after attaining awakening, as foreordained by the Buddha in chapter 21.
Second lowest of the five pure abodes, meaning “Slightest.”
See n.337 and n.257.
The name of one of eight lotus pools in each of the gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.

Describes a stage on the path of bodhisattvas (or bodhisattvas who have reached that stage) at which their realization is such that henceforth nothing can ever make them turn back from progressing toward the full awakening of a buddha.

gloss.term: beyond regressing from unsurpassed, perfect awakening

term.hi: bla na med pa yang dag par rdzogs pa’i byang chub las phyir mi ldog pa

note.p: Describes a stage on the path of bodhisattvas (or bodhisattvas who have reached that stage) at which their realization is such that henceforth nothing can ever make them turn back from continuing to make progress toward the full awakening of a buddha. For the sake of brevity our English rendering follows the Sanskrit and Tibetan, but it should not be understood as implying that such bodhisattvas have already attained the full awakening of a buddha.

term.hi: sgra dbyangs mi zad par sgrogs pa

note.p: “Irresistible Thundering Melodious Voice,” the name of the tathāgata in whose buddhafield the bodhisattva Sadāprarudita is said by the Buddha Śākyamuni to be presently residing after attaining his quest for the teachings, as described in the exemplary narrative of the final chapters of this sūtra, found also in the Twenty-Five Thousand but not in the other long Perfection of Wisdom sūtras.
The king of Magadha, centered on the city of Rājgṛha, and an early royal patron and disciple of the Buddha.
The honorific term applied to the Buddha Śākyamuni and other buddhas in numerous Buddhist texts. See n.67note 65 for Ratnākaraśānti’s explanation of its meanings.

Most specifically in this text, the term refers to the ten or eleven serial levels or bhūmis of which the progression of bodhisattvas toward awakening is comprised from the path of seeing onward, each defined in terms of qualities possessed and obscurations eliminated (see n.104,n.109, and n.379). Often also used in this text to refer to the level of bodhisattvas in general, or to more generic stages in the sequence of levels, such as the “level of nonregressing” or the “impure levels.”

term.hi: byang chub sems dpa’i skyon med pa

note.p: For the play on the two meanings of the Sanskrit term, see n.879note 872.
See n.279.
First and lowest of the thirteen god realms of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Stratum of Brahmā.”
Second of the thirteen god realms of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Brahmā Priest.”
Third of the thirteen god realms of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Retinue of Brahmā.” In some texts it is a synonym of Mahābrahma.
The principal god of the realm of form.

term.hi: rtogs par khong du chud par gyur

note.p: The name of one of the four gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.
Thirteenth and highest of the thirteen god realms of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Great Fruition.”

term.hi: yongs su mya ngan las ’das pa

note.p: The final passing of a buddha into nirvāṇa, often left untranslated as parinirvāṇa. See n.230.
An epithet used to refer to a buddha.

gloss.term: constituents underlying the clinging to existence

term.hi: nye bar len pa’i phung po
byang chub sems dpa’ sun ’byin pa

gloss.term: cut the ties to worldly existence

term.hi: srid par kun tu sbyor ba yongs su zad pa
rjes su yi rang ba yongs su bsngo ba
bdud kyi ris kyi lha dag

note.p: An eighth- to ninth-century Tibetan translator, reviser, and editor.

The bodhisattva from whom Sadāparudita seeks the teaching on the perfection of wisdom in the exemplary narrative of the final chapters of this sūtra, found also in the Twenty-Five Thousand but not in the other long Perfection of Wisdom sūtras.

A “disciple” refers to someone who “listens” to the teachings of the Buddha and “causes others to listen” to those teachings, i.e., by repeating them. The word śrāvaka is taken as a causative construction in the second case.

term.hi: sangs rgyas kyi rnam par rol ba

note.p: An important scholar-teacher (1003–1064) and disciple of Atiśa.
The capital city in the royal palace of which the Buddha Śākyamuni, in a distant previous life as a bodhisattva, studied under the Buddha Dīpaṃkara.

Dīpaṃkara was the buddha in the past under whose guidance the Buddha Śākyamuni, in a distant previous life, first made the vow to attain buddhahood and from whom he received the foreordaining of his future attainment (vyākaraṇa). In many texts he is counted as the fourth in a lineage of twenty-seven buddhas who preceded Śākyamuni.

gloss.term: eighteen distinctive qualities of a buddha

term.hi: sangs rgyas kyi chos ma ’dres pa bco brgyad

note.p: See n.634 and n.634627.
Eighteen aspects of the earthquake that accompany a tathāgata’s realization of perfect awakening. Note that the Tibetan term is “eight great signs.” See also n.870note 863.
See n.70.
Used specifically in the context of “enclosing” relics in a stūpa
Meditation (yoga) here is glossed as “meditative concentration” (samādhi).

term.hi: mi mnyam pa dang mnyam pa
sangs rgyas kyi yon tan phun sum tshogs pa

note.p: See n.74.
See n.483.

term.hi: byang chub kyi phyogs kyi chos

note.p: See n.219.
See n.483.

gloss.term: five karmic deeds that ripen immediately

term.hi: mtshams ma mchis pa lnga’i las

note.p: See n.516.

gloss.term: focused on what is beyond measure

term.hi: gzugs med pa’i snyoms par ’jug pa

note.p: Also called the “Four Guardians of the World,” the four kings who represent the lowest level of gods of the desire realm, located on the four facets of Mount Meru, each ruling over a different category of nonhuman beings. They are Vaiśravaṇa (also called Kubera), Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Virūḍhaka, and Virūpākṣa.
See notes n.489, n.490, and n.632.

term.hi: so so yang dag par rig pa bzhi

note.p: See n.557.
Generosity, kind words, meaningful actions, and practicing what one preaches.
See n.187 and n.188.

gloss.term: free of any state of being

term.hi: rnam par rtog pa med pa
kun nas nyon mongs pa ma mchis pa
ngo bo nyid ma mchis pa
mtshams sbyor ba ma mchis pa
phyin ci ma log pa de bzhin nyid

note.p: The Sanskrit vibhāvanā has a range of meanings; cf. Monier-Williams: “vi-°bhāvanā f. (in rhet.) description of effects the causes of which are left to be conjectured (or accord. to some, ‘description by negatives, bringing out the qualities of any object more clearly than by positive description’),” as does its negative form avibhāvanā, cf. Monier-Williams: “a-vibhāvanā, f. or a-vibhāvana, am, n. (rt. bhū), non-perception, non-discrimination, imperceptibility, disappearance.” In this text it has been translated following Ratnākaraśānti’s commentary (see n.267), but an additional complication comes from the difference between the Sanskrit and the different renderings in Tibetan found in this and other texts. The Tibetan renderings follow other shades of meaning of vibhāvanā, “dissolution,” “reduction,” “[analysis by] breaking down.” See also n.1432 in the Twenty-Five Thousand, where in this context of the Sadāprarudita-Dharmodgata narrative it is translated as “irreducibility.”

gloss.term: fruitional state of a worthy one

term.hi: dgra bcom pa nyid kyi ’bras bu

note.p: See notes n.1094and n.1100.
The name (meaning “Musth Elephant”) of a bodhisattva in the buddhafield of Abhirati.

The city in which the estate of the bodhisattva Dharmodgata is located, as described in the exemplary narrative recounting Sadāprarudita’s quest for the perfection of wisdom that forms the final chapters of this sūtra, found also in the Twenty-Five Thousand but not in the other long Perfection of Wisdom sūtras.

A woman whose name, meaning “goddess of the Ganges,” appears as the title of chapter 19, towards the end of which she makes a powerful statement and some offerings of flowers. Her future awakening is foreordained by the Buddha, who states that she first gave rise to the mindset of awakening, like him, in the presence of the Buddha Dīpaṃkara.

term.hi: yongs su sbyong bar byed pa

note.p: Translations of the Sanskrit verb √car sometimes suggest that bodhisattvas should “practice” the perfection of wisdom, but the English verb “to practice” wrongly implies that some mental effort, special focus, or activity is always required to exercise the perfection of wisdom. That bodhisattvas should “go about life” means that they should do whatever they do in life (i.e., sit, walk, eat, sleep, and so on) in the perfection of wisdom. See also notes n.52 and n.1217.
The name of one of eight lotus pools in each of the gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.

term.hi: dal ba phun sum tshogs pa

note.p: An epithet very frequently appended to “bodhisattva” to signify that he or she has a magnanimous (mahat) attitude with strong (mahat) renunciation and great (mahat) realization. Other glosses (see n.390note 383) explain that a “great being” is a bodhisattva replete with means, or one with a particular resolve.

term.hi: bsod nams ma lags pa’i phung po chen po

note.p: See n.71.
See n.76.

term.hi: sum cu rtsa gsum gyi ris

note.p: The second from lowest god realm of desire, presided over by the thirty-three gods of whom Śakra is the chief.
To make visible, in this text usually in the sense of how mind or awareness functions, but also in the sense of physical light.

This term (in its verbal and noun forms) is used in this translation in the sense of illuminating or elucidating by speaking, i.e., by expressing something in speech with all the eloquence of a confident knowledge of what is being illuminated, hence the Tibetan rendering spobs pa (“courage,” “audacity”). The Sanskrit term has connotations of brilliance, clarity, and intelligence as well as audacity. See n.84< and n.326.

term.hi: byang chub sems dpa’ bcos bu
dge ba’i rtsa ba yongs su ma smin pa
srog gi bar chad du ’gyur ba
mi zad pa la mi zad pa nyid
yang dag pa nyid rjes su rtogs pas

note.p: This translation is applied when -bhūta is used at end of a compound.

gloss.term: in the form of a book

term.hi: phan tshun tshogs pa dang mi mthun par

note.p: An artificial mark, like, for instance, the bell on a cow. See n.881note 874.

The god who presides over the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, and appears in many Buddhist texts, frequently referred to using the epithet Śakra and addressed by his personal name Kauśika, as both protector and disciple of the Buddha. In the Perfection of Wisdom sūtras he is an important interlocutor.

The southern continent of the four continents that, arranged around the central Mount Meru, are said to comprise our world that is one of the innumerable first order worlds in the thousandfold (second order) and billionfold (third order) universe.
The name of one of eight lotus pools in each of the gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.
An eighth-century Indian paṇḍita who participated in translation from Sanskrit into Tibetan.
Indian Buddhist scholar (ca. 740–795) who taught at Nālandā and accompanied Śāntarakṣita (725–788) to Tibet at the request of Tri Songdetsen.

The personal name of Śakra, the god who presides over the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, and appears in many Buddhist texts, sometimes referred to using the epithet Indra, as both protector and disciple of the Buddha. In the Perfection of Wisdom sūtras he is an important interlocutor.

The term used in the long Perfection of Wisdom sūtras to denote the knowing of a buddha. It includes the thirty-nine aspects of dwelling as a buddha together with thirty-seven aspects of the fruition of a disciple and thirty-four of the fruition of a bodhisattva to make one hundred and ten aspects in all. See notes n.626, n.631, and n.637; also Brunnhölzl 2011, vol. 2, pp. 296–97.

term.hi: lam gyi rnam pa shes pa nyid

note.p: The “complete knowing” characteristic of bodhisattvas, who must know all the paths to awakening as well as their own. It is the second of the eight topics of the Ornament, and a key term in the long Perfection of Wisdom sūtras. While it is not found as such in this sūtra, according to the commentaries it is to be understood in certain specific instances of the term “the state of complete knowing” (q.v.). See n.65, and also n.637 which mentions thirty-four aspects.

gloss.term: knowing of someone who has complete knowing

term.hi: thams cad mkhyen pa’i ye shes

note.p: One of the four main castes in the Indian social order, the one to which kings, rulers, and warriors belong.

term.hi: grub pa dang bde ba med

note.p: One of the ruling clans of the Vṛji Republic, centered on the important city of Vaiśālī.

term.hi: byang chub sems dpa’i gzugs brnyan
bris te glegs bam du chud par byas

note.p: Note that the Tibetan would be rendered “write it and put it into book form.”
Fourth of the thirteen god realms of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Great Brahmā.”
An arhat disciple of the Buddha Śākyamuni, from Śrāvastī. He had the moniker “foremost in experiential knowledge.” Also spelled (Mahā-) Kauṣṭhila.

term.hi: kA t+yA ya na chen po

note.p: An arhat disciple of the Buddha Śākyamuni, from Avanti. He had the moniker “foremost in exegesis.”
An arhat disciple of the Buddha Śākyamuni, from Magadha. he had the moniker “foremost in the practice of ascetic qualities.”
A bodhisattva disciple of the Buddha Śākyamuni, destined to become the next buddha, and an interlocutor in the Perfection of Wisdom sūtras.
Essentially a deeply focused mindset whose meditative object is framed by a particular, profound conception. On the individual meditative concentrations in this scripture, see notes n.1377 and n.1379

In this scripture wherever the term “mindset” (citta) appears alone (i.e., not in a compound like bodhicitta), it may refer to the mindset of awakening, or to a proper mindset directly related to it, or to an improper mindset antithetical to it, or to the immediate content of any of these three senses of the word mindset. See the introduction, i.92.

Although the Sanskrit word citta (in the compound bodhicitta) has both cognitive and affective aspects difficult to render accurately in a single English word, the term mindset has been adopted in the sense of a complex mental state constantly arising in each moment, shaped by particular past actions that affect the way one sees, thinks, feels, interprets, and acts moment to moment. See the introduction, i.92.
The term is used in two senses, “mixed up with” (confused or polluted by) and “mixed together with others.” See notes n.1094 and n.1100.
The name of one of eight lotus pools in each of the gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.
The name of one of eight lotus pools in each of the gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.
Kāśi is usually understood as the city of the Kāśi people, Vārāṇasī, known for its fine textiles.

A god of the desire realm who, in Buddhist scriptures, attempts to prevent the Buddha and others from attaining awakening, often by creating discord and arousing various gross or subtle afflictions. His name means “death.” He and his attendants (māras) personify the external and internal obstacles to awakening. See notes n.153and n.261.
The god realms presided over by Māra.

term.hi: ngo bo nyid med pa’i rang bzhin

note.p: The name of one of eight lotus pools in each of the gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.

term.hi: mthar gyis gnas pa’i snyoms par ’jug pa dgu

note.p: See n.628.
Fifth god realm of desire, meaning “Delighting in Emanation.”
See n.229.
An unconditioned space beyond the three conditioned realms of existence in which worthy ones are said to abide. See n.229.
See n.73.

term.hi: mu stegs can gzhan kun tu rgyu

note.p: See also “free of attachment” and “unattached.”

gloss.term: not be left to go astray

term.hi: ther zug tu gnas pa ma mchis pa
gnas pa dang bral ba ma lags pa
yang dag par rjes su ma mthong na
yang dag pa ma yin pa
kun tu skrag pa dang ldan par mi ’gyur

note.p: Usually translated as “at peace” or “quelled,” but here most often referring to the cessation or absence of something. See n.433 and n.609.

term.hi: byang chub sems dpa’i theg pa can
bye ba khrag khrig brgya stong
byang chub sems dpa’ gdol pa

note.p: See n.279.
See n.279.
Sixth god realm of desire, meaning “Mastery over Transformations.”
Eighth of the thirteen god realms of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Little Virtue.”
Fifth of the thirteen god realms of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Little Radiance.”
Fourth of the five paths.
Fifth of the five paths.
Second of the five paths.
Third of the five paths.
The term may encompass the first four of the five paths, as well as the first seven of the eight stages that culminate in the state of a worthy one.
The name of one of eight lotus pools in each of the gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.
The second of four stages or experiences of breaking through on the path of preliminary practice.

term.hi: bden pa’i byin gyi rlabs bya

note.p: See n.279.
See n.279.
The fourth of four stages or experiences of breaking through on the path of preliminary practice.
See also n.1076.
Literally “Lord of Beings,” an epithet designating a god of the Brahmā realm but also referring to Śiva, Viṣṇu, and others.
King of the powerful kingdom of Kośala, centered on the city of Śrāvasti. He was a contemporary of the Buddha Śākyamuni, becoming his disciple and patron.
See n.1024.

gloss.term: principal characteristics of a great person

term.hi: rnyed pa legs par rnyed pa

note.p: The twelfth of the thirteen levels of the god realm of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Increasing Merit.” In some versions of the longer Prajñāpāramitā sūtras, which list sixteen realms instead of thirteen, this god realm has the name Apramāṇabṛhat (Tib. tshad med che ba).

Of the several arhat disciples of the Buddha with the name Pūrṇa, this is Pūrṇa Maitrāyaṇīputra, Pūrṇa the son of Maitrāyaṇī. He is a Śākyan who became an arhat under the guidance of his uncle Ājñātakauṇḍinya before meeting the Buddha (his mother Maitrāyaṇī was Ājñātakauṇḍinya’s sister). He was considered “foremost in teaching.” See the 84000 Knowledge Base article “Disciples Named Pūrṇa.”
A bodhisattva named in this scripture as presently living in the buddhafield of the Buddha Akṣobhya.
For an explanation of the term satkāya in this sense, used in such contexts as that of the Buddha’s body, as at 4.2, compared to the more usual “transitory collection,” see n.341.

term.hi: mi zad pa mngon par bsgrub pa

note.p: Sanskrit syntax uses the noun “refinement,” often translated as “purification.”

term.hi: rnam pa thams cad kyi mchog dang ldan pa
zha lu lo tsA ba rin chen chos skyong bzang po

note.p: Scholar and translator also known as Zhalu Lotsawa (1441–1527). Renowned particularly for his expertise in Sanskrit. See bio at Treasury of Lives.
Capital of the kingdom of Magadha, near which is situated Vulture Peak, the setting for the Perfection of Wisdom teachings.

The bodhisttava protagonist of the final chapters of this scripture, whose indefatigable quest for the perfection of wisdom forms an exemplary narrative found also in the Twenty-Five Thousand but not in the other long Perfection of Wisdom sūtras.
Often also translated in this text as “devoid.” See also “voidness,” “void,” “seclusion,” and n.1094.

term.hi: rnam par grol ba’i ye shes gzigs pa

note.p: The direct witnessing of the distinctive knowing of liberation. One of the five “masses” (skandha) particular to bodhisattvas. See notes n.69 and n.399.
An inherent quality, like, for instance, the whiteness of a (white) cow. See n.881note 874.
The movements and sounds of the earthquakes that accompany a tathāgata’s realization of perfect awakening. See n.870.
Someone who has realized an awakening comparable to that of an arhat but without hearing the teachings of a buddha, on his or her own in isolation, and does not teach a path to others.
The name of one of the four gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.
The name of one of the four gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.
Also translated according to context as “ultimate realm of things.” See introduction, “.”
A tenth-century Indian paṇḍita who participated in translation from Sanskrit into Tibetan.

A disciple of the Buddha whose uniquely important role in the Perfection of Wisdom sūtras is to express and draw out the Buddha’s teaching on the perfection of wisdom, at the Buddha’s instigation, for the benefit of the audience of bodhisattvas although he himself is not among their number. Hari’s gloss: “He whose mental gift (bhūtiḥ<sampatti) is brilliance is (su<śobhanā) foremost among those dwelling without conflict.” See also n.100, and for more on Subhūti see the introduction to the Twenty-Five Thousand, i.78–90.

term.hi: sems rgya chen po ma yin pa

note.p: Fourth of the five pure abodes, meaning “Extreme Insight.” Also the name of the principal city of the Heaven of the Thirty-Three.
The name of the assembly hall in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three.
Third of the five pure abodes, meaning “Attractive.”
See n.307.

A tathāgata is a buddha, one who has come from or gone to (āgata or gata) the actual nature (tathā), often etymologized as one who has “come to the way things are.” Ratnākaraśānti also glosses a tathāgata as one who has clarified the actual way things are (see n.255).
The name of one of eight lotus pools in each of the gardens of Dharmodgata’s estate in Gandhavatī.
See n.124note 122.

term.hi: dge ba bcu’i las kyi lam
bsod nams bya ba’i dngos po
dngos po dang dngos po med pa
byang chub kyi phyogs kyi chos sum cu rtsa bdun

note.p: See n.219.

The mistaken focus of an important category of false views regarding there being a “self” that is related in various ways to the “collection” that is made up of the “transitory” constituents of experience or “constituents underlying the clinging to existence” (skandhas). See also n.1343.

Tuṣita, literally “Joyous” or “Contented,” is the fourth of the six heavens of the desire realm (kāmadhātu). This god realm is where all future buddhas are said to dwell before taking on their final rebirth prior to awakening. There, the Buddha Śākyamuni lived his preceding life as the bodhisattva Śvetaketu. When departing to take birth in this world, he appointed the bodhisattva Maitreya, who will be the next buddha of this eon, as his Dharma regent in Tuṣita.

As in other texts, mentions are made of five or six types. The five are (1) the knowledge of various types of magical powers, (2) the divine ear faculty, (3) the knowledge of others’ mindsets, (4) the knowledge that remembers occurrences in previous lives, (5) the divine eye faculty, all these first five being considered “mundane” (laukika) achievements resulting from the fourth dhyāna; they are therefore common to non-Buddhist practitioners. The sixth is (6) knowledge of the exhaustion of worldly influences (āsravakṣaya), which is a supramundane (lokottara) achievement resulting from vipaśyana. For explanations of the first five, see n.382note 375.
See also “free of attachment” and “nonattachment.”

gloss.term: under the influence of Māra’s magical power

term.hi: bdud kyi byin gyis brlabs pa
gzhan ma yin pa’i de bzhin nyid
ma log pa’i de bzhin nyid

note.p: The Tibetan translates both apramāṇa and aparimāṇa as tshad med pa.

term.hi: rnam par rgyal ba’i khang pa

note.p: The palace of Śakra (Indra) in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three.
An important bodhisattva, originally a fierce yakṣa, responsible for the protection of the Buddha’s person, and of the Dharma teachings in general.
A term of address for monks, meaning “blessed in life.” “fortunate,” “possessed of life’s (joy).” See n.81.

term.hi: dge ba dang mi dge ba’i chos

note.p: Often meaning the state of being isolated, solitary, or secluded, but also “lacking (something),” and almost synonymous with “emptiness.” See also “void” and “secluded,” translations of the related term viveka (dben pa) and n.1094.

term.hi: bya rgod kyi phung po’i ri

note.p: The craggy hilltop outside Rajgṛha where the Buddha gave many teachings including the Perfection of Wisdom.
The first of four stages or experiences of breaking through on the path of preliminary practice.
Ratnākara’s gloss suggests that pada here means a “step” along the path rather than the Tibetan translation as “word” (tshig).

term.hi: phung po lhag ma med pa

note.p: See n.69.

A “worthy one” (arhat) refers to a disciple who has reached the final stage of a disciple’s realization. Indian scholars gave six or more etymologies for arhat. This translation follows the etymology “someone who is worthy,” i.e., worthy of the generosity (niṇḍārha) of others and worthy of the veneration (pūjārha) of others. Note that Tibetan translators follow a different one: “someone who has eliminated the enemies” (ari+hatavān), i.e., the emotional defilements. See n.68.
The name of the future tathāgata that the woman Gaṅgadevī is foreordained to become by the Buddha, in the narrative found in all the long Perfection of Wisdom scriptures.
Third god realm of desire, meaning “strifeless.”
Seventh of the thirteen god realms of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Inner Radiance.”

The Buddha’s cousin, attendant, and disciple to whom the task of memorizing and upholding his discourses was given. He is often mentioned as not having attained the level of arhat at the time teachings were given since he did so only after the Buddha’s parinirvāṇa on the eve of the first council.
“Powerful One,” an epithet that may refer to Śiva, Viṣṇu, Maheśvara, or other gods.

An epithet of Indra, the god who presides over the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, and appears in many Buddhist texts, usually referred to using this name and addressed by his personal name Kauśika, as both protector and disciple of the Buddha. In the Perfection of Wisdom sūtras he is an important interlocutor.
A non-Buddhist wandering ascetic who converts to Buddhism and in the Perfection of Wisdom sūtras exemplifies one who attains understanding through faith.
Tenth of the thirteen god realms of form that correspond to the four meditative concentrations, meaning “Most Extensive Virtue.”
The five pure abodes. See n.206.
The Buddha’s own clan.
An eighth-century Indian paṇḍita who participated in translation from Sanskrit into Tibetan.

One of the Buddha’s closest and most able śrāvaka disciples, who had the moniker “foremost in learning”; also known as Śāradvatīputra. While in some Mahāyāna sūtras he is almost ridiculed in his role as an interlocutor representing the limited view of the disciples’ awakening, in the Perfection of Wisdom sūtras he is treated with respect but nevertheless expresses an understanding less vast than that of Subhūti, also a disciple.
